D: Original: Nicomachean Ethics - Aristotle (W.D. Ross translation)
D: Refashioned: Nicomachean Ethics - Aristotle - Refashioned

D: Original: Every art and every inquiry, and similarly every action and pursuit, is thought to aim at some good; and for this reason the good has rightly been declared to be that at which all things aim. But a certain difference is found among ends; some are activities, others are products apart from the activities that produce them. Where there are ends apart from the actions, it is the nature of the products to be better than the activities. Now, as there are many actions, arts, and sciences, their ends also are many; the end of the medical art is health, that of shipbuilding a vessel, that of strategy victory, that of economics wealth. But where such arts fall under a single capacity- as bridle-making and the other arts concerned with the equipment of horses fall under the art of riding, and this and every military action under strategy, in the same way other arts fall under yet others- in all of these the ends of the master arts are to be preferred to all the subordinate ends; for it is for the sake of the former that the latter are pursued. It makes no difference whether the activities themselves are the ends of the actions, or something else apart from the activities, as in the case of the sciences just mentioned.
D: Refashioned: Every craft and every systematic inquiry, and likewise every action and deliberate pursuit, is thought to aim at some good. This is why the good has rightly been described as that at which all things aim. However, there is a notable difference among ends: some ends are the activities themselves, while others are products that exist apart from the activities that produce them. Where the ends are products distinct from the activities, these products are by nature better than the activities that produce them. Now, since there are many actions, crafts, and sciences, their ends are correspondingly many: the end of medicine is health, of shipbuilding a vessel, of military strategy victory, of household management wealth. But wherever such crafts fall under a single overarching discipline -- as bridle-making and the other crafts concerned with equipping horses fall under horsemanship, and horsemanship together with every military endeavor falls under strategy, and in the same way other crafts fall under yet other master disciplines -- in all such cases the ends of the master disciplines are to be preferred over the ends of the subordinate ones, since the subordinate ends are pursued for the sake of the master ends. It makes no difference whether the activities themselves are the ends of the actions, or whether some further product beyond the activities is the end, as in the crafts just mentioned.
D: Elegant: Every craft, inquiry, action, and pursuit aims at some good -- which is why the good has been defined as that at which all things aim. Ends differ, however: some are the activities themselves, others are products beyond them, and products are superior to the activities that produce them. Since crafts are organized hierarchically, with subordinate disciplines serving master ones, the ends of master disciplines are always to be preferred, for it is for their sake that the subordinate ends are pursued.
D: Summary: All human activities aim at some good, but goods are organized hierarchically. Subordinate crafts serve higher ones, and the ends of the higher crafts are always more important than those beneath them.
D: Distilled: Every purposeful activity aims at some good, and goods are arranged in hierarchies where lesser ends serve greater ones.
Notes: This is the famous opening line of the Nicomachean Ethics, establishing teleology (purpose-directedness) as fundamental to understanding human action. The Greek word 'telos' (end/purpose) is central throughout. -- The hierarchical ordering of crafts anticipates the argument in 1.2 that politics is the 'master art.' -- The distinction between activities that are their own ends (energeiai) and those producing separate products (poieseis) recurs in Book VI and in the Metaphysics (Theta 6). -- This passage parallels the opening of the Eudemian Ethics (I.1).

D: Original: If, then, there is some end of the things we do, which we desire for its own sake (everything else being desired for the sake of this), and if we do not choose everything for the sake of something else (for at that rate the process would go on to infinity, so that our desire would be empty and vain), clearly this must be the good and the chief good. Will not the knowledge of it, then, have a great influence on life? Shall we not, like archers who have a mark to aim at, be more likely to hit upon what is right? If so, we must try, in outline at least, to determine what it is, and of which of the sciences or capacities it is the object. It would seem to belong to the most authoritative art and that which is most truly the master art. And politics appears to be of this nature; for it is this that ordains which of the sciences should be studied in a state, and which each class of citizens should learn and up to what point they should learn them; and we see even the most highly esteemed of capacities to fall under this, e.g. strategy, economics, rhetoric; now, since politics uses the rest of the sciences, and since, again, it legislates as to what we are to do and what we are to abstain from, the end of this science must include those of the others, so that this end must be the good for man. For even if the end is the same for a single man and for a state, that of the state seems at all events something greater and more complete whether to attain or to preserve; though it is worth while to attain the end merely for one man, it is finer and more godlike to attain it for a nation or for city-states. These, then, are the ends at which our inquiry aims, since it is political science, in one sense of that term.
D: Refashioned:
If, then, there is some end of everything we do that we desire for its own sake -- with everything else being desired for the sake of this -- and if we do not choose everything for the sake of something further (for then the chain would go on to infinity, making all desire empty and pointless), then clearly this ultimate end must be the good, indeed the chief good. Would not knowledge of this chief good have a profound influence on how we live? Would we not, like archers who have a target to aim at, be more likely to hit upon what is right? If so, we must try, at least in outline, to determine what this chief good is, and which science or discipline has it as its object.

It would seem to belong to the most authoritative and most truly master discipline. And political science appears to be of this nature; for it is political science that determines which disciplines should be studied in a city, which ones each class of citizens should learn, and to what extent. We see that even the most highly valued disciplines -- such as military strategy, household management, and rhetoric -- fall under its authority. Since political science makes use of all the other sciences and since it legislates about what we should do and what we should refrain from, its end must encompass the ends of all the others, so that this end must be the good for humankind. For even if the good is the same for a single individual and for a city-state, that of the city-state is clearly something greater and more complete, whether to achieve or to preserve. While it is worthwhile to achieve the good even for one person alone, it is nobler and more divine to achieve it for a nation or for city-states. These, then, are the aims of our inquiry, since it belongs to political science in one sense of that term.
D: Elegant: If there is an ultimate end we desire for its own sake -- everything else being desired for the sake of it -- then this must be the chief good, and knowing it would guide our lives as a target guides an archer. This chief good belongs to the most authoritative discipline, which is political science, since it governs all other sciences and legislates what citizens should do. Its end encompasses every other end, making it the good for humankind. And while achieving this good for one person is worthwhile, achieving it for a city-state is nobler and more divine.
D: Summary: There must be an ultimate good that all other goods serve, or else desire would regress infinitely and become pointless. This chief good falls under political science, the master discipline that governs all others and legislates for the city-state. Achieving the good for a whole community is nobler than achieving it for an individual.
D: Distilled: The chief good -- the ultimate aim of all human action -- belongs to political science, which governs all other disciplines and pursues the good of the whole community.
Notes: The 'archer' analogy is one of Aristotle's most famous images and recurs in ethical literature for centuries. -- The identification of ethics with political science reflects the Greek understanding that individual flourishing is inseparable from civic life; see also Politics I.2 (1253a), where man is called a 'political animal.' -- The infinite regress argument is a standard Aristotelian move; compare Metaphysics Alpha 2 (994a). -- The claim that the communal good is 'more divine' (theioteron) connects to Aristotle's teleological theology.

D: Original: Our discussion will be adequate if it has as much clearness as the subject-matter admits of, for precision is not to be sought for alike in all discussions, any more than in all the products of the crafts. Now fine and just actions, which political science investigates, admit of much variety and fluctuation of opinion, so that they may be thought to exist only by convention, and not by nature. And goods also give rise to a similar fluctuation because they bring harm to many people; for before now men have been undone by reason of their wealth, and others by reason of their courage. We must be content, then, in speaking of such subjects and with such premisses to indicate the truth roughly and in outline, and in speaking about things which are only for the most part true and with premisses of the same kind to reach conclusions that are no better. In the same spirit, therefore, should each type of statement be received; for it is the mark of an educated man to look for precision in each class of things just so far as the nature of the subject admits; it is evidently equally foolish to accept probable reasoning from a mathematician and to demand from a rhetorician scientific proofs.
D: Refashioned: Our discussion will be adequate if it achieves as much clarity as the subject matter allows, for the same degree of precision should not be expected in every inquiry, any more than in every product of craftsmanship. Now the subjects that political science investigates -- noble and just actions -- involve so much variety and inconsistency that they may seem to exist merely by convention rather than by nature. Even goods themselves produce a similar uncertainty, because they sometimes bring harm: people have been ruined by their wealth, and others by their courage. We must therefore be content, when speaking about such subjects and reasoning from such starting points, to indicate the truth roughly and in outline. When we speak about things that hold only for the most part, using premises of the same kind, we should expect conclusions of the same character. In this same spirit each of our statements should be received, for it is the mark of an educated person to seek only as much precision in each subject as the nature of that subject permits. It is equally foolish to accept merely probable reasoning from a mathematician and to demand rigorous proofs from an orator.
D: Elegant: Our discussion need only be as precise as ethics allows, for noble and just actions involve too much variation for mathematical exactness. Goods themselves are uncertain -- wealth and courage can bring ruin. We must therefore reason roughly, from premises that hold for the most part, and accept conclusions of the same kind. The mark of an educated person is knowing how much precision each subject permits: it is as foolish to accept mere probability from a mathematician as to demand strict proof from an orator.
D: Summary: Ethics cannot achieve mathematical precision because its subject matter -- noble and just actions -- is inherently variable. An educated person recognizes that different fields require different standards of exactness and does not demand more rigor than the subject allows.
D: Distilled: The mark of an educated mind is knowing how much precision each subject permits, and ethics by nature allows only rough generalization.
Notes: This methodological passage is one of the most cited in Aristotle's corpus. The principle of 'precision appropriate to the subject' (to akribes) is fundamental to Aristotle's epistemology. -- The claim that moral truths hold 'for the most part' (hos epi to polu) rather than universally is central to Aristotle's practical philosophy and distinguishes it from his theoretical sciences. -- The convention-vs-nature debate (nomos vs. physis) was a major theme among the Sophists and in Plato's dialogues (Republic, Gorgias). -- This passage influenced Thomas Aquinas's treatment of moral reasoning in the Summa Theologiae (I-II, q.94, a.4).

D: Original:
Now each man judges well the things he knows, and of these he is a good judge. And so the man who has been educated in a subject is a good judge of that subject, and the man who has received an all-round education is a good judge in general. Hence a young man is not a proper hearer of lectures on political science; for he is inexperienced in the actions that occur in life, but its discussions start from these and are about these; and, further, since he tends to follow his passions, his study will be vain and unprofitable, because the end aimed at is not knowledge but action. And it makes no difference whether he is young in years or youthful in character; the defect does not depend on time, but on his living, and pursuing each successive object, as passion directs. For to such persons, as to the incontinent, knowledge brings no profit; but to those who desire and act in accordance with a rational principle knowledge about such matters will be of great benefit.

These remarks about the student, the sort of treatment to be expected, and the purpose of the inquiry, may be taken as our preface.
D: Refashioned:
Each person judges well about the things they know, and in those domains they are a good judge. Someone educated in a particular subject judges that subject well; someone who has received an all-round education judges well in general. This is why a young person is not a suitable student of political science: they lack experience in the actions of life, and the discussions of this subject begin from and concern such actions. Furthermore, since young people tend to be led by their passions, their study will be empty and unprofitable, because the aim of this inquiry is not mere knowledge but action. And it makes no difference whether someone is young in years or youthful in character; the deficiency does not depend on age but on living and pursuing each successive goal as passion dictates. For such people, like those who lack self-control, knowledge brings no benefit. But for those who form their desires and act in accordance with a rational principle, knowledge about these matters will be enormously valuable.

Let these remarks about the student, the kind of treatment to expect, and the purpose of this inquiry serve as our preface.
D: Elegant: One judges well only what one knows. A young person is therefore an unsuitable student of political science, lacking the life experience from which ethical discussions begin. Moreover, since the young are driven by passion and this inquiry aims at action rather than mere knowledge, their study will be fruitless. The deficiency is not about age but about character: anyone who lives at the mercy of passion gains nothing from ethical knowledge. Only those who desire and act according to rational principle will benefit.
D: Summary: Ethical study requires life experience and emotional maturity, not just intellectual ability. Since ethics aims at action rather than mere knowledge, those who are governed by passion -- whether young in years or in character -- will gain nothing from it.
D: Distilled: Ethics requires not just intellectual capacity but life experience and emotional maturity, since its aim is right action, not mere knowledge.
Notes: The distinction between knowledge (gnosis) and action (praxis) as the aim of inquiry is fundamental to Aristotle's division of sciences into theoretical, practical, and productive (Metaphysics VI.1). -- The 'incontinent' (akratikos) person -- who knows the good but fails to do it -- is treated at length in Book VII. -- The claim that character rather than age determines readiness for ethical study challenges mere seniority as a qualification for wisdom. -- This passage establishes that the Nicomachean Ethics is a practical work: its purpose is to make us good, not merely to understand goodness.

D: Original: Let us resume our inquiry and state, in view of the fact that all knowledge and every pursuit aims at some good, what it is that we say political science aims at and what is the highest of all goods achievable by action. Verbally there is very general agreement; for both the general run of men and people of superior refinement say that it is happiness, and identify living well and doing well with being happy; but with regard to what happiness is they differ, and the many do not give the same account as the wise. For the former think it is some plain and obvious thing, like pleasure, wealth, or honour; they differ, however, from one another- and often even the same man identifies it with different things, with health when he is ill, with wealth when he is poor; but, conscious of their ignorance, they admire those who proclaim some great ideal that is above their comprehension. Now some thought that apart from these many goods there is another which is self-subsistent and causes the goodness of all these as well. To examine all the opinions that have been held were perhaps somewhat fruitless; enough to examine those that are most prevalent or that seem to be arguable.
D: Refashioned: Let us resume our inquiry and state, given that all knowledge and every pursuit aims at some good, what it is that political science aims at and what is the highest good achievable through action. On the verbal level there is very broad agreement: both ordinary people and people of refinement say it is happiness, and they identify living well and doing well with being happy. But when it comes to what happiness actually is, they disagree, and ordinary people do not give the same answer as the wise. Ordinary people think happiness is something plain and obvious -- like pleasure, wealth, or honor -- and they disagree among themselves. Often even the same person identifies it with different things: with health when sick, with wealth when poor. Aware of their own ignorance, they admire those who proclaim some grand ideal beyond their comprehension. Some thinkers, moreover, believed that apart from these many particular goods there exists another good that is self-subsistent and is the cause of the goodness in all of them. To examine every opinion that has been held would be rather pointless; it is enough to examine those that are most widely held or that seem to have some rational basis.
D: Elegant: Everyone agrees verbally that the highest good is happiness -- living well and doing well. But they disagree about what happiness actually is. Ordinary people identify it with pleasure, wealth, or honor, and even the same person shifts between these depending on circumstance. Some philosophers have posited a self-subsistent Good that causes goodness in all particular goods. Rather than examining every opinion, it suffices to consider those most widely held or most rationally defensible.
D: Summary: While everyone agrees that the highest good is happiness, people disagree profoundly about what happiness consists in. Ordinary people equate it with pleasure, wealth, or honor, while some philosophers posit an abstract Form of the Good. Aristotle proposes to examine only the most prevalent and reasonable views.
D: Distilled: Everyone calls the highest good 'happiness,' but they disagree fundamentally about what happiness actually is.
Notes: This begins Aristotle's dialectical method (endoxa): surveying common and expert opinions before working toward the truth. -- The 'self-subsistent good' refers to Plato's Form of the Good (Republic VI, 508e-509b), which Aristotle critiques at length in 1.6 (1.9-1.12). -- The identification of happiness (eudaimonia) with 'living well and doing well' (eu zen kai eu prattein) reveals that eudaimonia is not a feeling but an activity. -- This passage sets up the survey of candidate lives (pleasure, politics, contemplation) in 1.5 (1.7-1.8).

D: Original:
Let us not fail to notice, however, that there is a difference between arguments from and those to the first principles. For Plato, too, was right in raising this question and asking, as he used to do, 'are we on the way from or to the first principles?' There is a difference, as there is in a race-course between the course from the judges to the turning-point and the way back. For, while we must begin with what is known, things are objects of knowledge in two senses- some to us, some without qualification. Presumably, then, we must begin with things known to us. Hence any one who is to listen intelligently to lectures about what is noble and just, and generally, about the subjects of political science must have been brought up in good habits. For the fact is the starting-point, and if this is sufficiently plain to him, he will not at the start need the reason as well; and the man who has been well brought up has or can easily get startingpoints. And as for him who neither has nor can get them, let him hear the words of Hesiod:

Far best is he who knows all things himself; Good, he that hearkens when men counsel right; But he who neither knows, nor lays to heart Another's wisdom, is a useless wight.
D: Elegant: We must distinguish between reasoning from first principles and reasoning toward them -- as Plato rightly noted. Since we must begin with what is known to us rather than what is known in the absolute sense, a student of ethics must already have been brought up in good habits. The fact is our starting point; if it is sufficiently clear, there is no initial need for the reason behind it. Those who lack good upbringing and cannot acquire these starting points should heed Hesiod: best is he who understands on his own; good is he who listens to wise counsel; useless is he who does neither.
D: Summary: Ethical inquiry must begin with what is already known to us through experience, not with abstract first principles. This is why good moral upbringing is essential: it provides the factual starting points from which ethical reasoning proceeds.
D: Distilled: Good moral upbringing provides the experiential starting points without which ethical reasoning cannot even begin.
Notes: Plato's question about direction of inquiry appears in Republic VII (the 'Allegory of the Cave' and the 'Divided Line'). -- The distinction between 'known to us' (gnorimon hemin) and 'known without qualification' (gnorimon haplos) is fundamental to Aristotle's epistemology; see also Physics I.1 and Posterior Analytics I.2. -- The Hesiod quotation is from Works and Days, lines 293-297. Hesiod (c. 700 BCE) was one of the earliest Greek poets, a foundational figure alongside Homer. -- The emphasis on habituation (ethismos) as prior to rational understanding anticipates Book II's account of how virtue is acquired.

D: Original: Let us, however, resume our discussion from the point at which we digressed. To judge from the lives that men lead, most men, and men of the most vulgar type, seem (not without some ground) to identify the good, or happiness, with pleasure; which is the reason why they love the life of enjoyment. For there are, we may say, three prominent types of life- that just mentioned, the political, and thirdly the contemplative life. Now the mass of mankind are evidently quite slavish in their tastes, preferring a life suitable to beasts, but they get some ground for their view from the fact that many of those in high places share the tastes of Sardanapallus. A consideration of the prominent types of life shows that people of superior refinement and of active disposition identify happiness with honour; for this is, roughly speaking, the end of the political life. But it seems too superficial to be what we are looking for, since it is thought to depend on those who bestow honour rather than on him who receives it, but the good we divine to be something proper to a man and not easily taken from him. Further, men seem to pursue honour in order that they may be assured of their goodness; at least it is by men of practical wisdom that they seek to be honoured, and among those who know them, and on the ground of their virtue; clearly, then, according to them, at any rate, virtue is better. And perhaps one might even suppose this to be, rather than honour, the end of the political life. But even this appears somewhat incomplete; for possession of virtue seems actually compatible with being asleep, or with lifelong inactivity, and, further, with the greatest sufferings and misfortunes; but a man who was living so no one would call happy, unless he were maintaining a thesis at all costs. But enough of this; for the subject has been sufficiently treated even in the current discussions. Third comes the contemplative life, which we shall consider later.
D: Refashioned:
Let us, however, resume our discussion from where we digressed. Judging from the lives people actually lead, most people -- including those of the most ordinary sort -- seem, not without some justification, to identify the good, or happiness, with pleasure. This is why they love the life of enjoyment. There are, broadly speaking, three prominent types of life: the life of enjoyment, the political life, and the contemplative life. The masses are plainly slavish in their tastes, preferring a life fit for cattle, but they gain some support for their view from the fact that many people in positions of power share the tastes of Sardanapallus.

A consideration of the prominent types of life shows that people of superior refinement and active temperament identify happiness with honor, for this is, roughly speaking, the goal of the political life. But honor seems too superficial to be what we are looking for, since it is thought to depend on those who bestow it rather than on the person who receives it, whereas the good, we sense, should be something that belongs to a person and is not easily taken away. Moreover, people seem to pursue honor in order to be reassured of their own goodness: they seek to be honored by people of practical wisdom, among those who know them, and on the basis of their virtue. Clearly, then, according to these people at least, virtue is superior to honor. And perhaps one might suppose that virtue, rather than honor, is the end of the political life. But even this seems somewhat incomplete, for it appears possible to possess virtue while being asleep, or while being inactive throughout one's entire life, and further, while suffering the greatest misfortunes. No one would call a person living this way happy, unless they were defending a thesis at any cost. But enough of this, for the subject has been sufficiently discussed.

Third comes the contemplative life, which we will consider later.
D: Elegant: Most people identify happiness with pleasure and prefer the life of enjoyment -- a slavish preference shared even by the powerful. Those of superior refinement identify happiness with honor, the goal of the political life. But honor depends on others, not on oneself, and the good should be something one's own. People pursue honor as confirmation of their virtue, suggesting virtue is superior to honor. Yet even virtue seems incomplete: one can possess it while asleep, inactive, or suffering greatly, and no one would call such a person happy. The contemplative life remains to be considered.
D: Summary: Aristotle surveys three candidate lives: the life of pleasure (rejected as slavish), the political life aimed at honor (rejected because honor depends on others and points toward virtue), and the contemplative life (deferred). Even virtue alone is insufficient for happiness, since one can possess virtue while inactive or suffering.
D: Distilled: Neither pleasure, honor, nor the mere possession of virtue can be happiness, since each fails to account for what we truly mean by living well.
Notes: Sardanapallus (Ashurbanipal) was a legendary Assyrian king proverbial among the Greeks for luxury and self-indulgence; the epitaph attributed to him was 'Eat, drink, and play, for everything else is not worth this.' -- The three lives (bios apolaustikos, bios politikos, bios theoretikos) form a framework that structures the entire Ethics, with the contemplative life treated in Book X.6-8. -- The observation that one can possess virtue while asleep introduces the crucial distinction between hexis (stable disposition/state) and energeia (activity), which becomes central to the function argument in 1.7 (1.17-1.18). -- The political life devoted to honor connects to the discussion of megalopsychia (greatness of soul) in Book IV.3.

D: Original: The life of money-making is one undertaken under compulsion, and wealth is evidently not the good we are seeking; for it is merely useful and for the sake of something else. And so one might rather take the aforenamed objects to be ends; for they are loved for themselves. But it is evident that not even these are ends; yet many arguments have been thrown away in support of them. Let us leave this subject, then.
D: Refashioned: The life of money-making is one pursued under compulsion, and wealth is clearly not the good we are seeking, for it is merely useful -- valuable only for the sake of something else. One might sooner regard the previously mentioned objects -- pleasure, honor, virtue -- as ends, since they are valued for their own sake. But it is evident that not even these are the ultimate end, despite the many arguments that have been advanced in their support. Let us set this subject aside, then.
D: Elegant: Wealth is plainly not the highest good, since it is merely useful -- valued only for the sake of something else. The money-making life is pursued under compulsion, not freely chosen. Even pleasure, honor, and virtue, though valued for themselves, are not the ultimate end.
D: Summary: Wealth cannot be the highest good because it is always pursued as a means to something else, never as an end in itself. The money-making life is compulsory rather than freely chosen.
D: Distilled: Wealth is always a means to something else and therefore cannot be the highest good.
Notes: The dismissal of wealth as merely instrumental (chresimon) reflects Aristotle's broader teleological framework: the good must be complete and self-sufficient, not a mere tool. -- This brief passage completes the survey of lives begun in 1.7. -- The theme of wealth as instrumental returns in Politics I.8-10, where Aristotle distinguishes natural household management from unnatural money-making (chrematistike). -- The 'compulsion' (anagke) of money-making contrasts with the freedom (eleutheria) that characterizes the best human life.

D: Original: We had perhaps better consider the universal good and discuss thoroughly what is meant by it, although such an inquiry is made an uphill one by the fact that the Forms have been introduced by friends of our own. Yet it would perhaps be thought to be better, indeed to be our duty, for the sake of maintaining the truth even to destroy what touches us closely, especially as we are philosophers or lovers of wisdom; for, while both are dear, piety requires us to honour truth above our friends.
D: Refashioned: It would perhaps be better to consider the universal good and discuss thoroughly what is meant by it, although this inquiry is made difficult by the fact that the theory of Forms was introduced by people dear to us. Yet it would seem to be our duty -- indeed it is required of us -- for the sake of maintaining the truth, to criticize even what touches us closely, especially since we are philosophers, that is, lovers of wisdom. For while both truth and friends are dear, reverence demands that we honor truth above our friends.
D: Elegant: We should examine the universal good, even though the theory of Forms was introduced by our friends. Yet as philosophers -- lovers of wisdom -- we must honor truth above friendship.
D: Summary: Aristotle announces he must critique the Platonic theory of a universal Form of the Good, even though Plato was his teacher. He asserts that truth must take precedence over personal loyalty.
D: Distilled: Truth must be honored above friendship, even when criticizing the ideas of those we love.
Notes: The 'friends' are Plato and the Platonists; Aristotle studied at Plato's Academy for nearly twenty years (c. 367-347 BCE). -- The famous principle 'amicus Plato, sed magis amica veritas' (Plato is dear, but truth is dearer) derives from this passage, though the Latin formulation is medieval. -- This passage begins Aristotle's extended critique of Plato's Form of the Good (1.9-1.12), which is one of his most sustained engagements with Platonic metaphysics in the Ethics. -- The critique of the Forms also appears in Metaphysics I.9, VII.13-16, and XIII-XIV.

D: Original: The men who introduced this doctrine did not posit Ideas of classes within which they recognized priority and posteriority (which is the reason why they did not maintain the existence of an Idea embracing all numbers); but the term 'good' is used both in the category of substance and in that of quality and in that of relation, and that which is per se, i.e. substance, is prior in nature to the relative (for the latter is like an off shoot and accident of being); so that there could not be a common Idea set over all these goods. Further, since 'good' has as many senses as 'being' (for it is predicated both in the category of substance, as of God and of reason, and in quality, i.e. of the virtues, and in quantity, i.e. of that which is moderate, and in relation, i.e. of the useful, and in time, i.e. of the right opportunity, and in place, i.e. of the right locality and the like), clearly it cannot be something universally present in all cases and single; for then it could not have been predicated in all the categories but in one only. Further, since of the things answering to one Idea there is one science, there would have been one science of all the goods; but as it is there are many sciences even of the things that fall under one category, e.g. of opportunity, for opportunity in war is studied by strategics and in disease by medicine, and the moderate in food is studied by medicine and in exercise by the science of gymnastics. And one might ask the question, what in the world they mean by 'a thing itself', is (as is the case) in 'man himself' and in a particular man the account of man is one and the same. For in so far as they are man, they will in no respect differ; and if this is so, neither will 'good itself' and particular goods, in so far as they are good. But again it will not be good any the more for being eternal, since that which lasts long is no whiter than that which perishes in a day. The Pythagoreans seem to give a more plausible account of the good, when they place the one in the column of goods; and it is they that Speusippus seems to have followed.
D: Refashioned:
The thinkers who introduced the theory of Forms did not posit Forms for classes within which they recognized priority and posteriority (which is why they did not maintain the existence of a Form embracing all numbers). But the term 'good' is used in the category of substance, in that of quality, and in that of relation; and substance -- that which exists in its own right -- is by nature prior to the relative (for the relative is like an offshoot or accident of being). So there could not be a single common Form set over all these goods.

Furthermore, since 'good' has as many senses as 'being' -- for it is used in the category of substance (as of God and of reason), in quality (as of the virtues), in quantity (as of the moderate amount), in relation (as of the useful), in time (as of the right opportunity), and in place (as of the right location), and so on -- it clearly cannot be something universally present in all cases as a single thing, for then it would not be found in all the categories but in only one.

Furthermore, since there would be a single science of all the things falling under one Form, there would have to be one science of all goods. But in fact there are many sciences even of goods falling under a single category -- for example, the right opportunity in war is studied by military strategy, in disease by medicine; and the moderate amount in food is studied by medicine, in exercise by the science of gymnastics.

One might also ask what the Platonists mean by 'a thing itself,' since in 'man himself' and in a particular man the account of what it is to be a man is one and the same. Insofar as they are both man, they will in no respect differ; and if so, neither will 'the good itself' and particular goods differ insofar as they are good. Nor will the good be any more truly good for being eternal, since something that lasts a long time is no whiter than something that lasts only a day.

The Pythagoreans seem to give a more plausible account of the good by placing 'the one' in the column of goods; and it is them that Speusippus appears to have followed.
D: Elegant: The Platonists did not posit Forms for series with priority and posteriority, yet 'good' is used across multiple categories -- substance, quality, relation, quantity, time, place -- so no single Form can unify them all. Moreover, if one Form covered all goods, there would be one science of them, yet in fact there are many. The 'thing itself' adds nothing: man-himself and a particular man share the same definition of man, so the good-itself and particular goods do not differ as goods. Nor does eternal duration make something more good, just as lasting longer does not make something whiter. The Pythagoreans offer a more plausible account by placing 'the one' in the column of goods.
D: Summary: Aristotle raises multiple objections to Plato's Form of the Good: 'good' is used across different categories (substance, quality, relation, etc.) so no single Form can encompass it; there is no single science of all goods; the Form of the Good adds nothing to particular goods; and eternal existence does not make something better.
D: Distilled: There can be no single universal Form of the Good because 'good' is used in irreducibly different senses across different categories of being.
Notes: The Platonic doctrine of Forms (eidoi/ideai) is most fully presented in the Republic, Phaedo, and Symposium. -- The argument from categories draws on Aristotle's own Categories, where he identifies substance, quantity, quality, relation, place, time, etc. -- Speusippus (c. 407-339 BCE) was Plato's nephew and successor as head of the Academy. -- The Pythagoreans maintained a 'Table of Opposites' with columns of good and bad, where the One, Limit, and Odd were on the good side; see Metaphysics I.5 (986a). -- The argument that eternal duration does not add to goodness targets the Platonic assumption that Forms, being eternal, are superior to their temporal instances.

D: Original: But let us discuss these matters elsewhere; an objection to what we have said, however, may be discerned in the fact that the Platonists have not been speaking about all goods, and that the goods that are pursued and loved for themselves are called good by reference to a single Form, while those which tend to produce or to preserve these somehow or to prevent their contraries are called so by reference to these, and in a secondary sense. Clearly, then, goods must be spoken of in two ways, and some must be good in themselves, the others by reason of these. Let us separate, then, things good in themselves from things useful, and consider whether the former are called good by reference to a single Idea. What sort of goods would one call good in themselves? Is it those that are pursued even when isolated from others, such as intelligence, sight, and certain pleasures and honours? Certainly, if we pursue these also for the sake of something else, yet one would place them among things good in themselves. Or is nothing other than the Idea of good good in itself? In that case the Form will be empty. But if the things we have named are also things good in themselves, the account of the good will have to appear as something identical in them all, as that of whiteness is identical in snow and in white lead. But of honour, wisdom, and pleasure, just in respect of their goodness, the accounts are distinct and diverse. The good, therefore, is not some common element answering to one Idea.
D: Refashioned:
But let us discuss these matters elsewhere. An objection to what we have said, however, can be identified: the Platonists have not been speaking about all goods. Rather, goods that are pursued and valued for their own sake are called good by reference to a single Form, while those that tend to produce, preserve, or protect these goods are called good in a secondary sense, by reference to the first kind.

Clearly, then, goods must be spoken of in two ways: some are good in themselves, others are good because of these. Let us separate things good in themselves from things merely useful and consider whether the former are called good by reference to a single Form. What sorts of things would count as good in themselves? Those that are pursued even when isolated from any further benefit -- such as intelligence, sight, certain pleasures, and honors? These, even if we also pursue them for the sake of something else, would still be placed among things good in themselves. Or is nothing other than the Form of Good itself good in its own right? In that case the Form would be empty of content.

But if the things we named are also good in themselves, then the account of goodness will have to appear as something identical in all of them, just as the account of whiteness is identical in snow and in white lead. Yet the accounts of honor, wisdom, and pleasure, precisely with respect to their goodness, are distinct and diverse. The good, therefore, is not some single common element corresponding to one Form.
D: Elegant: The Platonists might reply that only goods pursued for their own sake fall under a single Form, while instrumental goods are called good in a secondary sense. But even among intrinsic goods -- intelligence, sight, pleasure, honor -- the accounts of their goodness are distinct and diverse, unlike whiteness which is the same in snow and white lead. Therefore goodness is not a single common element answering to one Form.
D: Summary: Even if we limit the discussion to things good in themselves rather than merely useful goods, their goodness turns out to be diverse. Intelligence, sight, pleasure, and honor are each good in different ways, so there is no single account of goodness that unifies them under one Form.
D: Distilled: Even things that are good in themselves are good in irreducibly different ways, so no single Form of the Good can account for them all.
Notes: This continues the critique of Plato's Form of the Good begun in 1.9. -- The analogy with whiteness in snow and white lead (ceruse) illustrates what would have to be the case if the Form theory were correct: goodness would need a single, uniform account like whiteness. -- The distinction between intrinsic goods (pursued for their own sake) and instrumental goods (pursued for the sake of something else) becomes central to Aristotle's own positive account of happiness in 1.7 (1.14-1.16). -- This argument influenced later debates about the univocity vs. equivocity of 'good,' particularly in medieval Scholastic philosophy.

D: Original: But what then do we mean by the good? It is surely not like the things that only chance to have the same name. Are goods one, then, by being derived from one good or by all contributing to one good, or are they rather one by analogy? Certainly as sight is in the body, so is reason in the soul, and so on in other cases. But perhaps these subjects had better be dismissed for the present; for perfect precision about them would be more appropriate to another branch of philosophy. And similarly with regard to the Idea; even if there is some one good which is universally predicable of goods or is capable of separate and independent existence, clearly it could not be achieved or attained by man; but we are now seeking something attainable. Perhaps, however, some one might think it worth while to recognize this with a view to the goods that are attainable and achievable; for having this as a sort of pattern we shall know better the goods that are good for us, and if we know them shall attain them. This argument has some plausibility, but seems to clash with the procedure of the sciences; for all of these, though they aim at some good and seek to supply the deficiency of it, leave on one side the knowledge of the good. Yet that all the exponents of the arts should be ignorant of, and should not even seek, so great an aid is not probable. It is hard, too, to see how a weaver or a carpenter will be benefited in regard to his own craft by knowing this 'good itself', or how the man who has viewed the Idea itself will be a better doctor or general thereby. For a doctor seems not even to study health in this way, but the health of man, or perhaps rather the health of a particular man; it is individuals that he is healing. But enough of these topics.
D: Refashioned:
But then what do we mean by 'good'? Surely goods are not like things that merely happen to share the same name by coincidence. Are goods one by being derived from one source, or by all contributing to one end, or rather by analogy? For just as sight is to the body, so reason is to the soul, and so on in other cases.

But perhaps these questions had better be set aside for now, since precise treatment of them would be more appropriate to another branch of philosophy. The same goes for the Form of the Good: even if there is some single good that is universally applicable to all goods, or that exists separately and independently, it clearly could not be achieved or attained by human beings, and what we are now seeking is something attainable.

Perhaps, however, someone might think it worthwhile to know this universal good as a kind of pattern, reasoning that by having it we would better know what goods are good for us, and knowing them, would attain them. This argument has some plausibility, but it seems to conflict with how the actual sciences proceed. For all the sciences, though they aim at some good and seek to supply what is lacking, set aside knowledge of the universal Good. Yet it is unlikely that all practitioners of the arts would be ignorant of, and not even seek, so powerful an aid.

It is hard to see, moreover, how a weaver or a carpenter would be helped in their own craft by knowing 'the good itself,' or how someone who has contemplated the Form would become a better doctor or general. For a doctor does not study health in the abstract but the health of human beings, or rather the health of a particular patient -- it is individuals that a doctor heals. But enough of these topics.
D: Elegant: What do we mean by 'good'? Perhaps goods are unified by analogy -- as sight is in the body, so reason is in the soul. But even if a universal Good exists, it is unattainable by humans, and we seek something achievable. Knowing the Form of the Good as a pattern might seem useful, but the actual sciences all proceed without it. A doctor does not study health in the abstract but treats particular patients. Knowledge of 'the good itself' would not make a better weaver, carpenter, or general.
D: Summary: Even if a universal Form of the Good existed, it would be unattainable by humans and practically useless. The actual sciences and crafts all function without reference to it. A doctor treats particular patients, not abstract health -- practical knowledge, not knowledge of universals, is what makes practitioners effective.
D: Distilled: Even if a universal Good existed, it would be unattainable and practically useless, since every craft and science deals with particular goods, not abstract ones.
Notes: This concludes Aristotle's critique of the Platonic Form of the Good (1.9-1.12). -- The suggestion that goods are related 'by analogy' (kat' analogian) rather than by a single Form anticipates the medieval doctrine of the 'analogy of being' (analogia entis), developed especially by Aquinas. -- The 'another branch of philosophy' likely refers to metaphysics or first philosophy. -- The practical argument -- that knowledge of the Form does not help the craftsman -- is characteristically Aristotelian: theory must serve practice. -- This passage influenced the development of particularism in modern virtue ethics, especially in the work of John McDowell and Martha Nussbaum.

D: Original: Let us again return to the good we are seeking, and ask what it can be. It seems different in different actions and arts; it is different in medicine, in strategy, and in the other arts likewise. What then is the good of each? Surely that for whose sake everything else is done. In medicine this is health, in strategy victory, in architecture a house, in any other sphere something else, and in every action and pursuit the end; for it is for the sake of this that all men do whatever else they do. Therefore, if there is an end for all that we do, this will be the good achievable by action, and if there are more than one, these will be the goods achievable by action.
D: Refashioned: Let us return once more to the good we are seeking and ask what it might be. It appears to be different in different actions and crafts: it is one thing in medicine, another in strategy, another in each of the other crafts. What, then, is the good of each? Surely it is that for the sake of which everything else in that domain is done. In medicine this is health, in strategy victory, in architecture a house, and in every other sphere something else. In every action and pursuit, the end is the good, for it is for the sake of this end that people do everything else they do. Therefore, if there is a single end for all that we do, this will be the good achievable by action; and if there are more than one, these will be the goods achievable by action.
D: Elegant: The good differs across different actions and crafts: health in medicine, victory in strategy, a house in architecture. In each case the good is that for the sake of which everything else is done. If there is one ultimate end of all action, this is the good achievable by action; if there are several, these are the goods.
D: Summary: The good varies across different domains -- it is health for medicine, victory for strategy. In each case, the good is the end for whose sake everything else is done. If all actions converge on a single ultimate end, that is the good achievable by action.
D: Distilled: The good in any domain is the end for whose sake everything else is done, and if there is one ultimate end of all action, that is the highest good.
Notes: This passage marks the transition from Aristotle's critique of Plato's Form of the Good to his own positive account. -- The teleological framework (everything aims at an end) was established in 1.1 and is now applied to identify the supreme good. -- The argument will narrow in 1.14 to show that among ends, some are more final than others, with happiness as the most final. -- The method of starting from particular cases and generalizing is characteristic of Aristotle's empirical approach; contrast Plato's top-down deduction from the Form of the Good.

D: Original: So the argument has by a different course reached the same point; but we must try to state this even more clearly. Since there are evidently more than one end, and we choose some of these (e.g. wealth, flutes, and in general instruments) for the sake of something else, clearly not all ends are final ends; but the chief good is evidently something final. Therefore, if there is only one final end, this will be what we are seeking, and if there are more than one, the most final of these will be what we are seeking. Now we call that which is in itself worthy of pursuit more final than that which is worthy of pursuit for the sake of something else, and that which is never desirable for the sake of something else more final than the things that are desirable both in themselves and for the sake of that other thing, and therefore we call final without qualification that which is always desirable in itself and never for the sake of something else.
D: Refashioned:
So the argument, by a different route, has arrived at the same point. But we must try to state this even more clearly. Since there are evidently more ends than one, and we choose some of them -- such as wealth, flutes, and instruments generally -- for the sake of something else, clearly not all ends are ultimate ends. But the chief good is evidently something ultimate. Therefore, if there is only one ultimate end, this will be what we are seeking; and if there are more than one, the most ultimate among them will be what we are seeking.

Now we call that which is worthy of pursuit for its own sake more ultimate than that which is pursued for the sake of something else. And we call that which is never chosen for the sake of anything else more ultimate than things that are chosen both for themselves and for the sake of that other thing. Therefore, what we call ultimate without qualification is that which is always chosen for its own sake and never for the sake of something else.
D: Elegant: Not all ends are ultimate -- we choose some, like wealth and tools, only for the sake of further ends. The chief good must be something truly ultimate. What is pursued for its own sake is more ultimate than what is pursued for the sake of something else, and what is never chosen as a means to anything further is ultimate without qualification.
D: Summary: Since some ends are pursued only as means to further ends, not all ends are equally final. The chief good must be the most final end -- the one that is always chosen for its own sake and never as a means to anything else.
D: Distilled: The chief good is that which is always chosen for its own sake and never as a means to something else.
Notes: The concept of 'finality' (teleiotaton) introduces a ranking among ends that is crucial to identifying happiness as the chief good in the next passage. -- The examples of wealth and flutes as intermediate ends (chosen for the sake of something else) recall the earlier discussion of instrumental goods. -- This passage establishes the criterion of finality (teleiotes) that, together with self-sufficiency (autarkeia) in 1.16, forms Aristotle's formal definition of the highest good. -- The argument structure -- moving from multiple ends to a single supreme end -- mirrors the hierarchical structure of crafts established in 1.1.

D: Original: Now such a thing happiness, above all else, is held to be; for this we choose always for self and never for the sake of something else, but honour, pleasure, reason, and every virtue we choose indeed for themselves (for if nothing resulted from them we should still choose each of them), but we choose them also for the sake of happiness, judging that by means of them we shall be happy. Happiness, on the other hand, no one chooses for the sake of these, nor, in general, for anything other than itself.
D: Refashioned: Now happiness, above all else, seems to be of this ultimate character. For we always choose happiness for its own sake and never for the sake of something else. Honor, pleasure, reason, and every virtue we do indeed choose for themselves -- we would choose each of them even if nothing further resulted from them -- but we also choose them for the sake of happiness, believing that through them we will be happy. Happiness, on the other hand, no one chooses for the sake of these things, nor for the sake of anything else at all.
D: Elegant: Happiness fits this criterion perfectly: we always choose it for itself, never as a means to something else. Honor, pleasure, reason, and virtue we choose both for themselves and for the sake of happiness -- but no one chooses happiness for the sake of these or anything else.
D: Summary: Happiness uniquely satisfies the criterion of finality. While we choose things like honor, pleasure, and virtue both for themselves and as means to happiness, we never choose happiness as a means to anything further.
D: Distilled: Happiness alone is chosen purely for its own sake and never as a means to anything else.
Notes: This passage applies the criterion of finality established in 1.14 to identify happiness (eudaimonia) as the chief good. -- The claim that we choose virtue 'for itself' but also 'for the sake of happiness' raises a question about whether virtue is intrinsically or instrumentally valuable; Aristotle's answer (both) is developed throughout the Ethics. -- The list of goods chosen for themselves (honor, pleasure, reason, virtue) corresponds to the candidate lives surveyed in 1.5-1.8. -- This passage is crucial for understanding that eudaimonia for Aristotle is not a subjective feeling but the most complete and self-sufficient activity.

D: Original: From the point of view of self-sufficiency the same result seems to follow; for the final good is thought to be self-sufficient. Now by self-sufficient we do not mean that which is sufficient for a man by himself, for one who lives a solitary life, but also for parents, children, wife, and in general for his friends and fellow citizens, since man is born for citizenship. But some limit must be set to this; for if we extend our requirement to ancestors and descendants and friends' friends we are in for an infinite series. Let us examine this question, however, on another occasion; the self-sufficient we now define as that which when isolated makes life desirable and lacking in nothing; and such we think happiness to be; and further we think it most desirable of all things, without being counted as one good thing among others- if it were so counted it would clearly be made more desirable by the addition of even the least of goods; for that which is added becomes an excess of goods, and of goods the greater is always more desirable. Happiness, then, is something final and self-sufficient, and is the end of action.
D: Refashioned:
The same conclusion seems to follow from the point of view of self-sufficiency, for the ultimate good is thought to be self-sufficient. By 'self-sufficient' we do not mean what suffices for a person living in isolation, but what suffices also for parents, children, spouse, and in general for friends and fellow citizens, since the human being is by nature a social and political creature. But some limit must be set to this, for if we extend the requirement to ancestors and descendants and friends of friends, we would be launched into an infinite series. Let us set this question aside for now.

We define self-sufficiency as that which, taken by itself, makes life choiceworthy and lacking in nothing. This, we hold, is what happiness is. Furthermore, we think happiness is the most choiceworthy of all things, but not as one good counted among others. If it were merely one good among many, it would obviously be made more choiceworthy by the addition of even the smallest further good, since what is added creates a surplus of goods, and the greater amount of good is always more choiceworthy. Happiness, then, is something ultimate and self-sufficient, and is the end of action.
D: Elegant: The chief good must also be self-sufficient -- not for a solitary individual, but for someone living with family, friends, and fellow citizens, since humans are by nature political beings. Self-sufficiency means making life choiceworthy and complete in itself. Happiness meets this criterion, and it is not merely one good among others; if it were, adding any small good would make the combination more choiceworthy. Happiness is therefore ultimate, self-sufficient, and the end of action.
D: Summary: Happiness is self-sufficient: it makes life fully choiceworthy on its own, needing nothing added. This self-sufficiency is understood not in terms of solitary living but in the context of family, friends, and civic life. Happiness is not one good among many but stands as the complete and final end of all action.
D: Distilled: Happiness is the self-sufficient end that makes life complete, understood not in isolation but within the full context of human social life.
Notes: The claim that humans are 'by nature political' (politikon zoon) is stated more fully in Politics I.2 (1253a2-3): 'Man is by nature a political animal.' -- The self-sufficiency (autarkeia) criterion complements the finality criterion from 1.14-1.15. Together they provide Aristotle's formal characterization of happiness. -- The argument that happiness is not 'one good among others' but rather an all-encompassing good anticipates the question of whether happiness is an 'inclusive' or 'dominant' end -- a major debate in Aristotelian scholarship (Ackrill vs. Kraut). -- The mention of the infinite regress concerning ancestors and descendants connects to the discussion in 1.10-1.11 about whether the fortunes of descendants affect one's happiness.

D: Original: Presumably, however, to say that happiness is the chief good seems a platitude, and a clearer account of what it is still desired. This might perhaps be given, if we could first ascertain the function of man. For just as for a flute-player, a sculptor, or an artist, and, in general, for all things that have a function or activity, the good and the 'well' is thought to reside in the function, so would it seem to be for man, if he has a function. Have the carpenter, then, and the tanner certain functions or activities, and has man none? Is he born without a function? Or as eye, hand, foot, and in general each of the parts evidently has a function, may one lay it down that man similarly has a function apart from all these? What then can this be? Life seems to be common even to plants, but we are seeking what is peculiar to man. Let us exclude, therefore, the life of nutrition and growth. Next there would be a life of perception, but it also seems to be common even to the horse, the ox, and every animal. There remains, then, an active life of the element that has a rational principle; of this, one part has such a principle in the sense of being obedient to one, the other in the sense of possessing one and exercising thought. And, as 'life of the rational element' also has two meanings, we must state that life in the sense of activity is what we mean; for this seems to be the more proper sense of the term.
D: Refashioned:
However, to say that happiness is the chief good is presumably a platitude, and a clearer account of what it is remains to be given. This might be achieved if we could first determine the function of a human being. For just as the good and the 'doing well' for a flute-player, a sculptor, or any craftsperson -- and in general for anything that has a function or characteristic activity -- is thought to reside in that function, so it would seem to be for a human being, if indeed a human being has a function.

Do the carpenter and the tanner have functions and activities, but the human being as such has none? Is a human being born without a function? Or, just as the eye, the hand, the foot, and each of the bodily parts evidently has a function, may we also attribute a function to the human being as a whole, over and above all these particular functions?

What, then, could this function be? Life -- in the sense of nutrition and growth -- seems to be shared even with plants, but we are seeking what is distinctive to human beings. Let us therefore set aside the life of nutrition and growth. Next would come the life of sense-perception, but this too appears to be shared with horses, cattle, and every animal. What remains, then, is an active life of the element that possesses reason. This rational element has two aspects: one that is rational in the sense of being obedient to reason, the other in the sense of possessing reason and actively thinking. And since 'the life of the rational element' can be understood in two ways, we must specify that we mean life in the sense of activity, for this seems to be the more proper sense of the term.
D: Elegant: Saying happiness is the chief good is a platitude -- we need to determine what it consists in. This becomes possible if we identify the distinctive function of a human being. The good for anything with a function resides in performing that function well. Since nutrition belongs to plants and perception to animals, the distinctively human function must be an active life of the rational element -- not merely possessing reason but actively exercising it.
D: Summary: To give real content to 'happiness,' Aristotle introduces the function argument: every being with a characteristic function achieves its good by performing that function well. The distinctively human function is rational activity -- not the life of nutrition shared with plants or perception shared with animals, but the active exercise of reason.
D: Distilled: The distinctively human function is the active exercise of reason, and human good must therefore consist in performing this function well.
Notes: The 'function argument' (ergon argument) is one of the most important and debated passages in the entire Ethics. -- The Greek word ergon means function, characteristic work, or activity; it carries teleological weight in Aristotle's biology and metaphysics. -- The elimination of nutrition and perception to arrive at reason as distinctive parallels De Anima II.2-3, where Aristotle distinguishes nutritive, perceptive, and rational soul. -- The distinction between possessing reason and exercising it (hexis vs. energeia) was prefigured in the critique of virtue-as-mere-possession in 1.7. -- The function argument has been criticized by many modern philosophers (e.g., J.L. Austin, Bernard Williams) who question whether humans have a function in the way that tools and organs do.

D: Original: Now if the function of man is an activity of soul which follows or implies a rational principle, and if we say 'so-and-so-and 'a good so-and-so' have a function which is the same in kind, e.g. a lyre, and a good lyre-player, and so without qualification in all cases, eminence in respect of goodness being idded to the name of the function (for the function of a lyre-player is to play the lyre, and that of a good lyre-player is to do so well): if this is the case, and we state the function of man to be a certain kind of life, and this to be an activity or actions of the soul implying a rational principle, and the function of a good man to be the good and noble performance of these, and if any action is well performed when it is performed in accordance with the appropriate excellence: if this is the case, human good turns out to be activity of soul in accordance with virtue, and if there are more than one virtue, in accordance with the best and most complete.
D: Refashioned: Now if the function of a human being is an activity of the soul that expresses or involves a rational principle, and if the function of a given kind of person and of a good person of that kind are the same in type -- as the function of a lyre-player and a good lyre-player are the same in type, the difference being that excellence is added to the function (for the function of a lyre-player is to play the lyre, and of a good lyre-player to play it well) -- and if this holds without qualification in every case, with superiority in excellence being added to the function: then, if we identify the function of a human being as a certain kind of life, namely an activity or set of actions of the soul that express a rational principle, and the function of a good human being as performing these well and nobly; and if any action is well performed when it is performed in accordance with the appropriate excellence: then it follows that the human good is activity of the soul in accordance with virtue -- and if there are multiple virtues, in accordance with the best and most complete.
D: Elegant: If the human function is rational activity of the soul, and a good person performs this function excellently -- just as a good lyre-player plays the lyre well -- then the human good is activity of the soul in accordance with virtue. If there are multiple virtues, it is activity in accordance with the best and most complete virtue.
D: Summary: Since the human function is rational activity, and excellence means performing one's function well, the human good turns out to be activity of the soul in accordance with virtue -- or if there are several virtues, in accordance with the best and most complete one.
D: Distilled: Human good is activity of the soul in accordance with the best and most complete virtue.
Notes: This is Aristotle's formal definition of happiness (eudaimonia): 'activity of soul in accordance with virtue, and if there are more than one virtue, in accordance with the best and most complete.' -- The phrase 'best and most complete' (ariste kai teleiotate) raises the question of whether happiness requires all the virtues or only the highest one (contemplation). This debate runs through the entire Ethics and is addressed in Book X.7-8. -- 'Virtue' (arete) here means excellence of any kind, not merely moral goodness. -- The lyre-player analogy illustrates the principle that the good of any kind X is determined by what a good X does. -- This passage, together with 1.17, forms the core of the function argument, which Thomas Aquinas adopted and Christianized in the Summa Theologiae.

D: Original:
But we must add 'in a complete life.' For one swallow does not make a summer, nor does one day; and so too one day, or a short time, does not make a man blessed and happy.

Let this serve as an outline of the good; for we must presumably first sketch it roughly, and then later fill in the details. But it would seem that any one is capable of carrying on and articulating what has once been well outlined, and that time is a good discoverer or partner in such a work; to which facts the advances of the arts are due; for any one can add what is lacking. And we must also remember what has been said before, and not look for precision in all things alike, but in each class of things such precision as accords with the subject-matter, and so much as is appropriate to the inquiry. For a carpenter and a geometer investigate the right angle in different ways; the former does so in so far as the right angle is useful for his work, while the latter inquires what it is or what sort of thing it is; for he is a spectator of the truth. We must act in the same way, then, in all other matters as well, that our main task may not be subordinated to minor questions. Nor must we demand the cause in all matters alike; it is enough in some cases that the fact be well established, as in the case of the first principles; the fact is the primary thing or first principle. Now of first principles we see some by induction, some by perception, some by a certain habituation, and others too in other ways. But each set of principles we must try to investigate in the natural way, and we must take pains to state them definitely, since they have a great influence on what follows. For the beginning is thought to be more than half of the whole, and many of the questions we ask are cleared up by it.
D: Refashioned:
But we must add the qualification 'in a complete life.' For one swallow does not make a summer, nor does one day; and similarly, one day or a brief period does not make a person blessed and happy.

Let this serve as an outline of the good, for presumably one should first sketch it roughly and fill in the details later. It would seem that anyone is capable of carrying forward and articulating what has been well outlined, and that time is a good discoverer and collaborator in such work -- which is how advances in the arts and sciences have come about, since anyone can add what is lacking.

We must also keep in mind what was said earlier: we should not demand the same precision in every subject, but in each case only as much as the subject matter allows and as much as is appropriate to the inquiry. A carpenter and a geometer investigate the right angle in different ways: the carpenter only insofar as it is useful for the work, while the geometer inquires into what it is or what kind of thing it is, since the geometer is a seeker of truth. We must proceed in the same way in all other areas, so that secondary matters do not overshadow the main task.

Nor should we demand an explanation of the 'why' in every case. In some cases it is enough to establish the 'that' clearly, as with first principles. The fact is itself the starting point, the first principle. Some first principles we grasp by induction, some by perception, some by a kind of habituation, and others in yet other ways. We must try to investigate each set of principles in the way natural to it, and take care to define them well, since they have a great influence on everything that follows. For the starting point is thought to be more than half of the whole, and many of the questions we ask are answered once the starting point is established.
D: Elegant: We must add: 'in a complete life.' One swallow does not make a summer, nor does one day of good action make a person happy. Let this serve as an outline to be filled in later. We must also remember not to demand equal precision in every subject: a carpenter and a geometer study the right angle differently. Nor must we always demand the 'why'; sometimes establishing the 'that' suffices, as with first principles, which we grasp through induction, perception, or habituation. First principles matter enormously, for the starting point is more than half the whole.
D: Summary: Happiness requires a complete life, not just a moment of virtue. This outline of the good will be refined later. Different subjects require different levels of precision, and not everything needs a 'why' -- sometimes establishing the fact is enough. First principles, grasped through induction, perception, or habituation, are enormously important because they determine everything that follows.
D: Distilled: Happiness requires not a single good day but a complete life of virtuous activity, and establishing sound first principles is more than half the work of any inquiry.
Notes: The famous proverb 'one swallow does not make a summer' (mia chelidon ear ou poiei) remains in common use today. -- The qualification 'in a complete life' (en bioi teleioi) raises difficult questions about whether happiness can be attributed to someone before death, which Aristotle addresses in 1.10 (1.29-1.35). -- The distinction between the 'that' (to hoti) and the 'why' (to dioti) is fundamental to Aristotle's epistemology; see Posterior Analytics I.13. -- The claim that the beginning is 'more than half the whole' is attributed to various sources and became proverbial in Greek. -- The methodological remarks here echo those in 1.3 about appropriate precision.

D: Original: We must consider it, however, in the light not only of our conclusion and our premisses, but also of what is commonly said about it; for with a true view all the data harmonize, but with a false one the facts soon clash. Now goods have been divided into three classes, and some are described as external, others as relating to soul or to body; we call those that relate to soul most properly and truly goods, and psychical actions and activities we class as relating to soul. Therefore our account must be sound, at least according to this view, which is an old one and agreed on by philosophers. It is correct also in that we identify the end with certain actions and activities; for thus it falls among goods of the soul and not among external goods. Another belief which harmonizes with our account is that the happy man lives well and does well; for we have practically defined happiness as a sort of good life and good action. The characteristics that are looked for in happiness seem also, all of them, to belong to what we have defined happiness as being. For some identify happiness with virtue, some with practical wisdom, others with a kind of philosophic wisdom, others with these, or one of these, accompanied by pleasure or not without pleasure; while others include also external prosperity. Now some of these views have been held by many men and men of old, others by a few eminent persons; and it is not probable that either of these should be entirely mistaken, but rather that they should be right in at least some one respect or even in most respects.
D: Refashioned:
We must, however, consider our definition not only in light of our conclusion and premises, but also in light of what is commonly said about happiness, since all the data harmonize with a true account, while a false one quickly clashes with the facts.

Now goods have been divided into three classes: external goods, goods of the body, and goods of the soul. Of these, we call goods of the soul the most properly and truly goods, and we classify actions and activities of the soul among goods of the soul. Therefore our account must be sound, at least according to this long-established view accepted by philosophers. It is also correct insofar as we identify the end with certain actions and activities, for this places the end among goods of the soul rather than external goods.

Another widely held belief that harmonizes with our account is that the happy person lives well and does well, for we have essentially defined happiness as a kind of living well and acting well.

Moreover, the characteristics that various thinkers look for in happiness all seem to belong to what we have defined it as. Some identify happiness with virtue, others with practical wisdom, others with a kind of philosophical wisdom, others with these accompanied by pleasure or at least not without pleasure, and still others include external prosperity. Some of these views have been held by many people over a long period, others by a few distinguished thinkers. It is unlikely that any of these groups is entirely wrong; rather, they are probably right in at least one respect, or even in most respects.
D: Elegant: A true account of happiness should harmonize with common beliefs. The traditional division of goods into external, bodily, and psychical -- with psychical being highest -- confirms our definition, since we placed happiness among activities of the soul. The common belief that the happy person lives well and acts well also agrees.
