Furthermore, the various views that happiness involves virtue, practical wisdom, philosophical wisdom, pleasure, or external prosperity all capture some aspect of what we have defined happiness to be.
D: Summary: Aristotle tests his definition of happiness against common opinions and philosophical tradition. The classification of goods into external, bodily, and psychical (with psychical highest) supports his account. So does the common equation of happiness with living and acting well. The various candidate definitions -- virtue, wisdom, pleasure, prosperity -- each capture some part of the truth.
D: Distilled: A correct definition of happiness should harmonize with the best common opinions, and Aristotle's account -- activity of the soul in accordance with virtue -- does precisely this.
Notes: The tripartite division of goods into external, bodily, and psychical was traditional in Greek philosophy; see also Plato's Laws V (743e). -- The dialectical method of testing a philosophical thesis against reputable opinions (endoxa) is described in Topics I.1 and is Aristotle's primary method in the Ethics. -- The phrase 'living well and doing well' (eu zen kai eu prattein) confirms the active nature of eudaimonia. -- The various identifications of happiness (with virtue, wisdom, pleasure, etc.) correspond to the views surveyed in 1.4-1.8, showing that Aristotle's definition synthesizes rather than simply rejects competing views.

D: Original: With those who identify happiness with virtue or some one virtue our account is in harmony; for to virtue belongs virtuous activity. But it makes, perhaps, no small difference whether we place the chief good in possession or in use, in state of mind or in activity. For the state of mind may exist without producing any good result, as in a man who is asleep or in some other way quite inactive, but the activity cannot; for one who has the activity will of necessity be acting, and acting well. And as in the Olympic Games it is not the most beautiful and the strongest that are crowned but those who compete (for it is some of these that are victorious), so those who act win, and rightly win, the noble and good things in life.
D: Refashioned:
Our account is in harmony with those who identify happiness with virtue or with some particular virtue, for virtuous activity belongs to virtue. But it makes no small difference whether we locate the chief good in the possession of virtue or in its exercise -- in the state of mind or in the activity. For a state of mind can exist without producing any good result, as in someone who is asleep or otherwise completely inactive. But this cannot be the case with activity: one who is engaged in activity will necessarily be acting, and acting well.

Just as in the Olympic Games it is not the most beautiful and strongest who are crowned, but those who actually compete (for it is among the competitors that the victors are found), so too in life: it is those who act rightly who win the noble and good things.
D: Elegant: Our account agrees with those who identify happiness with virtue, but with a crucial distinction: happiness lies not in merely possessing virtue but in exercising it. A state can exist without producing any result -- a virtuous person can be asleep. But activity necessarily involves acting, and acting well. As at the Olympic Games, crowns go not to the strongest but to those who compete; so in life, noble prizes go to those who act.
D: Summary: Happiness is not the mere possession of virtue but its active exercise. Just as Olympic crowns go to those who compete rather than to the strongest bystanders, the good things in life go to those who act virtuously, not to those who merely have the capacity for virtue.
D: Distilled: Happiness lies not in possessing virtue but in actively exercising it, just as Olympic victory belongs to those who compete, not merely to the strongest.
Notes: The Olympic Games analogy is one of Aristotle's most vivid illustrations. The ancient Olympic Games (held at Olympia every four years from 776 BCE) were the most prestigious athletic competition in Greece. -- The distinction between possession (hexis) and exercise (energeia/chresis) is fundamental to Aristotle's ethics and was foreshadowed in the critique of the merely virtuous but inactive person in 1.7. -- This passage reinforces that eudaimonia is an activity (energeia), not a state (hexis) -- a point with major implications for the entire ethical theory. -- Compare Metaphysics Theta 6-8 on the priority of actuality over potentiality.

D: Original: Their life is also in itself pleasant. For pleasure is a state of soul, and to each man that which he is said to be a lover of is pleasant; e.g. not only is a horse pleasant to the lover of horses, and a spectacle to the lover of sights, but also in the same way just acts are pleasant to the lover of justice and in general virtuous acts to the lover of virtue. Now for most men their pleasures are in conflict with one another because these are not by nature pleasant, but the lovers of what is noble find pleasant the things that are by nature pleasant; and virtuous actions are such, so that these are pleasant for such men as well as in their own nature. Their life, therefore, has no further need of pleasure as a sort of adventitious charm, but has its pleasure in itself. For, besides what we have said, the man who does not rejoice in noble actions is not even good; since no one would call a man just who did not enjoy acting justly, nor any man liberal who did not enjoy liberal actions; and similarly in all other cases. If this is so, virtuous actions must be in themselves pleasant. But they are also good and noble, and have each of these attributes in the highest degree, since the good man judges well about these attributes; his judgement is such as we have described. Happiness then is the best, noblest, and most pleasant thing in the world, and these attributes are not severed as in the inscription at Delos-
D: Refashioned:
The life of those who act virtuously is also pleasant in itself. For pleasure is a condition of the soul, and to each person that which they are said to love is pleasant: a horse is pleasant to the lover of horses, a spectacle to the lover of sights, and in the same way just actions are pleasant to the lover of justice, and virtuous actions generally to the lover of virtue.

For most people, their pleasures conflict with one another because these pleasures are not pleasant by nature. But for lovers of what is noble, the things that are naturally pleasant are what they find pleasant, and virtuous actions are of this kind. These actions are therefore pleasant both for such people and in their own nature. The life of the virtuous, then, has no need of pleasure as an added ornament but contains pleasure within itself.

Besides what we have said, a person who does not take delight in noble actions is not even good. No one would call a person just who did not enjoy acting justly, or generous who did not enjoy generous actions, and so on in every other case. If this is so, then virtuous actions must be pleasant in themselves. But they are also good and noble, and they possess each of these qualities to the highest degree, since the good person judges these matters correctly, and their judgment accords with what we have described.

Happiness, then, is the best, the noblest, and the most pleasant thing, and these qualities are not separated as in the inscription at Delos --
D: Elegant: Virtuous activity is inherently pleasant. Each person finds pleasure in what they love, and the lover of virtue finds pleasure in virtuous acts. Unlike the conflicting pleasures of most people, the pleasures of the virtuous are naturally pleasant. Someone who takes no delight in noble action is not truly good -- no one is just who does not enjoy acting justly. Virtuous actions are therefore simultaneously good, noble, and pleasant to the highest degree. Happiness unites these qualities that the Delian inscription separates.
D: Summary: Virtuous activity is inherently pleasant because people take pleasure in what they love, and lovers of virtue love virtuous action. Unlike ordinary pleasures that conflict with each other, the pleasure of virtue is natural and stable. Happiness thus unifies goodness, nobility, and pleasure -- qualities that popular opinion wrongly separates.
D: Distilled: Virtuous activity is inherently pleasant because those who truly love the good find their deepest pleasure in noble action itself.
Notes: The claim that virtuous action is pleasant 'in itself' (kath' hauto) is important: Aristotle is not saying that virtue is pleasant as a means to something else, but that the activity itself is enjoyable for the virtuous person. -- This anticipates the extended treatment of pleasure in Books VII.11-14 and X.1-5. -- The discussion of natural vs. non-natural pleasures connects to the account of 'pleasure proper to each activity' in Book X.5. -- The 'inscription at Delos' is quoted in the next passage (1.23). Delos was a sacred island in the Aegean, birthplace of Apollo and Artemis, and site of a major Greek temple and sanctuary.

D: Original:
Most noble is that which is justest, and best is health;

But pleasantest is it to win what we love.
D: Refashioned:
'Most noble is that which is most just, and best is health;
but most pleasant is it to win what we love.'
D: Elegant: 'Noblest is justice, best is health, most pleasant is winning what we love.'
D: Summary: This inscription from the temple at Delos separates justice, health, and pleasure as three distinct goods -- a separation that Aristotle's account of happiness overcomes.
D: Distilled: The Delian inscription separates nobility, health, and pleasure, but happiness unites them all.
Notes: This inscription was reportedly carved at the temple of Artemis (or Apollo) on the island of Delos. -- Aristotle quotes it to show that popular thought separates qualities that his account of happiness unifies: the good, the noble, and the pleasant all belong to virtuous activity. -- The inscription is also quoted in the Eudemian Ethics I.1 (1214a5). -- Delos was one of the most important religious sanctuaries in ancient Greece and the center of the Delian League, an Athenian-led alliance.

D: Original:
For all these properties belong to the best activities; and these, or one- the best- of these, we identify with happiness.

Yet evidently, as we said, it needs the external goods as well; for it is impossible, or not easy, to do noble acts without the proper equipment. In many actions we use friends and riches and political power as instruments; and there are some things the lack of which takes the lustre from happiness, as good birth, goodly children, beauty; for the man who is very ugly in appearance or ill-born or solitary and childless is not very likely to be happy, and perhaps a man would be still less likely if he had thoroughly bad children or friends or had lost good children or friends by death. As we said, then, happiness seems to need this sort of prosperity in addition; for which reason some identify happiness with good fortune, though others identify it with virtue.
D: Refashioned:
For all these qualities -- goodness, nobility, and pleasure -- belong to the best activities, and these activities, or the single best among them, we identify with happiness.

Yet happiness evidently also requires external goods, as we said; for it is impossible, or at least not easy, to perform noble actions without the proper resources. In many actions we use friends, wealth, and political power as instruments. And there are certain things whose absence diminishes happiness -- things like good birth, fine children, and physical beauty. For a person who is extremely ugly, of low birth, solitary, or childless is not very likely to be happy, and perhaps even less so if they had utterly bad children or friends, or had lost good ones through death.

As we said, then, happiness seems to require this kind of external prosperity in addition to virtuous activity. This is why some people identify happiness with good fortune, while others identify it with virtue.
D: Elegant: Goodness, nobility, and pleasure all belong to the best activities, which we identify with happiness. Yet happiness also requires external goods: noble action needs resources, friends, wealth, and political power as instruments. The absence of good birth, children, or beauty diminishes happiness. This is why some identify happiness with fortune and others with virtue.
D: Summary: While happiness is primarily virtuous activity, it also requires external goods like friends, wealth, beauty, and good birth. Noble actions are difficult or impossible without proper resources. This dual requirement explains why some identify happiness with fortune and others with virtue.
D: Distilled: Happiness is primarily virtuous activity but also requires external goods, since noble actions are difficult without resources, friends, and favorable circumstances.
Notes: The role of external goods (ta ektos agatha) in happiness is one of the most debated topics in Aristotelian ethics. Aristotle never resolves the tension between his claim that happiness is self-sufficient and his admission that it requires external conditions. -- This passage qualifies the strong claims of the function argument by reintroducing the role of fortune (tuche). -- The examples of diminishing conditions (ugliness, low birth, childlessness) reflect the social values of 4th-century Athens. -- The tension between virtue and fortune as sources of happiness is explored further in 1.9-1.10 (1.25-1.35) and recurs in Book X. -- Stoic philosophers later rejected this aspect of Aristotle's view, arguing that virtue alone is sufficient for happiness.

D: Original: For this reason also the question is asked, whether happiness is to be acquired by learning or by habituation or some other sort of training, or comes in virtue of some divine providence or again by chance. Now if there is any gift of the gods to men, it is reasonable that happiness should be god-given, and most surely god-given of all human things inasmuch as it is the best. But this question would perhaps be more appropriate to another inquiry; happiness seems, however, even if it is not god-sent but comes as a result of virtue and some process of learning or training, to be among the most godlike things; for that which is the prize and end of virtue seems to be the best thing in the world, and something godlike and blessed.
D: Refashioned:
This is also why the question is raised whether happiness is acquired through learning, through habituation, through some other kind of training, or whether it comes as a gift of divine providence or even by chance.

Now if there is any gift of the gods to human beings, it is reasonable that happiness should be god-given, and most surely god-given of all human things, since it is the best. But this question would perhaps be more appropriate to another inquiry.

Happiness seems, however -- even if it is not sent by the gods but comes through virtue and some process of learning or training -- to be among the most divine things. For that which is the prize and end of virtue seems to be the best thing in the world, and something divine and blessed.
D: Elegant: Is happiness acquired through learning, habituation, training, divine gift, or chance? If the gods give anything to humans, happiness would be the most likely gift, being the best of human things. Yet even if happiness comes through virtue and learning rather than divine gift, it remains among the most divine things -- for the prize and end of virtue is the best and most blessed thing in the world.
D: Summary: Whether happiness comes from divine gift, learning, or training, it is among the most divine of human goods. Even if it results from virtue and effort rather than from the gods, the prize and end of virtue is the noblest and most blessed thing.
D: Distilled: Whether divinely given or earned through virtue and effort, happiness is the most divine and blessed of all human achievements.
Notes: The question of whether happiness is god-given or humanly achievable engages a long Greek debate about the role of divine favor (theia moira) in human life; see Plato's Meno (99e-100a) on virtue as a divine gift. -- Aristotle's preference for happiness as something achievable through effort reflects his broader commitment to human agency and practical wisdom. -- The 'other inquiry' likely refers to theology or metaphysics. -- The characterization of happiness as 'divine and blessed' (theion kai makarion) uses language typically reserved for the gods, suggesting that happiness brings humans closest to the divine condition -- a theme developed in Book X.7-8.

D: Original: It will also on this view be very generally shared; for all who are not maimed as regards their potentiality for virtue may win it by a certain kind of study and care. But if it is better to be happy thus than by chance, it is reasonable that the facts should be so, since everything that depends on the action of nature is by nature as good as it can be, and similarly everything that depends on art or any rational cause, and especially if it depends on the best of all causes. To entrust to chance what is greatest and most noble would be a very defective arrangement.
D: Refashioned: On this view, happiness would also be widely shared, for all who are not impaired in their capacity for virtue can win it through a certain kind of study and care. And if it is better to become happy through effort than by chance, it is reasonable to expect that this is how things actually are, since everything that depends on the workings of nature is by nature as good as it can be, and the same holds for everything that depends on craft or any rational cause -- especially when it depends on the best of all causes. To entrust the greatest and noblest thing to chance would be a deeply defective arrangement.
D: Elegant: If happiness comes through effort, it would be widely accessible to all whose capacity for virtue is not impaired. And it is reasonable that happiness should come through effort rather than chance, since nature and craft always produce the best results they can -- and to leave the greatest human good to mere chance would be a deeply defective arrangement.
D: Summary: If happiness is achievable through effort and learning rather than chance, it would be accessible to nearly everyone with the capacity for virtue. Nature and rational causes produce the best outcomes possible, so it would be unreasonable for the greatest human good to depend on luck.
D: Distilled: Happiness should be achievable through effort rather than left to chance, because nature produces the best results it can, and the greatest good should not depend on luck.
Notes: The argument that nature does nothing in vain and always aims at the best possible outcome is a fundamental principle of Aristotle's natural philosophy; see Physics II.8 and Parts of Animals I.1. -- The claim that happiness is 'widely shared' (koinon) reflects Aristotle's relatively democratic view of human potential, though he does restrict it to those not 'maimed' in their capacity for virtue. -- This passage implicitly argues against the view (associated with popular Greek religion) that happiness depends primarily on divine favor or luck. -- The emphasis on effort (epimeleia) and study (mathesis) connects to the practical orientation of the Ethics.

D: Original: The answer to the question we are asking is plain also from the definition of happiness; for it has been said to be a virtuous activity of soul, of a certain kind. Of the remaining goods, some must necessarily pre-exist as conditions of happiness, and others are naturally co-operative and useful as instruments. And this will be found to agree with what we said at the outset; for we stated the end of political science to be the best end, and political science spends most of its pains on making the citizens to be of a certain character, viz. good and capable of noble acts.
D: Refashioned:
The answer to the question we have been asking is also clear from our definition of happiness, for we have said it is a virtuous activity of the soul of a certain kind. Of the remaining goods, some must necessarily be present as preconditions of happiness, while others are naturally cooperative and useful as instruments.

This also agrees with what we said at the outset: we stated that the end of political science is the best end, and political science devotes most of its effort to making citizens of a certain character -- namely, good people capable of noble actions.
D: Elegant: Our definition confirms the answer: happiness is virtuous activity of the soul. Other goods serve either as preconditions or as useful instruments. This aligns with our opening claim that political science, which aims at the best end, devotes itself primarily to making citizens good and capable of noble action.
D: Summary: The definition of happiness as virtuous soul-activity clarifies the role of other goods: some are preconditions, others are instruments. This confirms that political science, which aims at the highest good, rightly focuses on cultivating good character in citizens.
D: Distilled: Happiness is virtuous activity of the soul, and other goods serve either as necessary preconditions or as helpful instruments for this activity.
Notes: This passage ties together the function argument (1.17-1.18), the role of external goods (1.24), and the opening claim about political science (1.2). -- The distinction between preconditions and instruments of happiness corresponds roughly to necessary conditions and contributing causes. -- The connection to political science reinforces Aristotle's view that ethics and politics are inseparable; see Politics VII.1-3 for an extended discussion of the best life in relation to the best political community. -- The claim that political science 'makes citizens good' is central to Aristotle's understanding of legislation and education as the primary tools of political life.

D: Original: It is natural, then, that we call neither ox nor horse nor any other of the animals happy; for none of them is capable of sharing in such activity. For this reason also a boy is not happy; for he is not yet capable of such acts, owing to his age; and boys who are called happy are being congratulated by reason of the hopes we have for them. For there is required, as we said, not only complete virtue but also a complete life, since many changes occur in life, and all manner of chances, and the most prosperous may fall into great misfortunes in old age, as is told of Priam in the Trojan Cycle; and one who has experienced such chances and has ended wretchedly no one calls happy.
D: Refashioned:
It is natural, then, that we do not call an ox, a horse, or any other animal happy, since none of them is capable of sharing in the kind of activity we have described. For this same reason, a child is not happy either, for a child is not yet capable of such actions due to their age. When children are called happy, it is because of the hopes we have for them.

For happiness requires, as we have said, both complete virtue and a complete life. Many changes and all sorts of fortunes occur in the course of a life, and the most prosperous person may fall into great misfortunes in old age, as the stories tell of Priam in the Trojan cycle. No one calls happy a person who has experienced such fortunes and come to a wretched end.
D: Elegant: We do not call animals happy because they cannot engage in rational virtuous activity. Nor are children happy, since they are not yet capable of such activity; when called happy, it is only in hope. Happiness requires both complete virtue and a complete life, for fortune can reverse even the most prosperous existence -- as happened to Priam, who fell from greatness to misery.
D: Summary: Animals and children cannot be happy in the proper sense because they lack the capacity for mature rational activity. Happiness requires both complete virtue and a complete life, since even the most fortunate person can meet with catastrophe, as the story of Priam illustrates.
D: Distilled: Happiness requires both the full development of virtue and a complete life, since fortune can reverse prosperity at any stage.
Notes: Priam was the legendary king of Troy, who was wealthy and powerful but lost his sons (including Hector) and his city in the Trojan War, and was killed by Achilles' son Neoptolemus. His story was told in Homer's Iliad and in the poems of the Trojan Cycle. -- The exclusion of animals from happiness follows from the function argument: only beings with rational capacity can perform the distinctively human function. -- The exclusion of children is not about their moral worth but about their developmental stage; compare Politics VII.13 on education. -- The requirement of a 'complete life' (bios teleios) raises the 'Priam problem' that dominates the next several sections (1.29-1.35) and connects to Solon's famous admonition.

D: Original: Must no one at all, then, be called happy while he lives; must we, as Solon says, see the end? Even if we are to lay down this doctrine, is it also the case that a man is happy when he is dead? Or is not this quite absurd, especially for us who say that happiness is an activity? But if we do not call the dead man happy, and if Solon does not mean this, but that one can then safely call a man blessed as being at last beyond evils and misfortunes, this also affords matter for discussion; for both evil and good are thought to exist for a dead man, as much as for one who is alive but not aware of them; e.g. honours and dishonours and the good or bad fortunes of children and in general of descendants. And this also presents a problem; for though a man has lived happily up to old age and has had a death worthy of his life, many reverses may befall his descendants- some of them may be good and attain the life they deserve, while with others the opposite may be the case; and clearly too the degrees of relationship between them and their ancestors may vary indefinitely. It would be odd, then, if the dead man were to share in these changes and become at one time happy, at another wretched; while it would also be odd if the fortunes of the descendants did not for some time have some effect on the happiness of their ancestors.
D: Refashioned:
Must no one, then, be called happy while they are still alive? Must we, as Solon advised, wait to see the end? Even if we accept this principle, is the person actually happy when they are dead? Or is that not quite absurd, especially for us who define happiness as an activity?

But if we do not call the dead person happy, and if Solon's point is rather that one can only then safely call a person blessed as being finally beyond evils and misfortunes, this too raises problems. For both good and evil are thought to affect a dead person just as much as one who is alive but unaware of them -- consider honors and dishonors, and the good or bad fortunes of children and descendants.

This raises a further difficulty. Suppose a person has lived happily into old age and died a death worthy of their life: many misfortunes may still befall their descendants -- some may be good and enjoy the life they deserve, others may experience the opposite. And clearly, the degrees of relationship between these descendants and their ancestor may vary enormously. It would be strange if the dead person's happiness shifted with each of these changes, becoming happy at one time and wretched at another. But it would also be strange if the fortunes of descendants had no effect at all on their ancestors, even for some period of time.
D: Elegant: Must we follow Solon and call no one happy while alive? But calling the dead happy seems absurd if happiness is an activity. Perhaps Solon means only that the dead are beyond misfortune -- yet both good and evil seem to affect the dead, through honors and the fortunes of descendants. It would be strange if a dead person's happiness fluctuated with every descendant's fortune, yet equally strange if descendants' lives had no effect at all.
D: Summary: Solon's advice to call no one happy until death raises paradoxes. If happiness is an activity, the dead cannot be happy. But if the dead are beyond misfortune, we still face the puzzle that descendants' fortunes seem to affect them. Aristotle finds it equally problematic to say the dead person's happiness fluctuates with every change in their descendants' lives and to say it is entirely unaffected.
D: Distilled: The question of whether happiness can only be judged after death raises deep puzzles about whether the dead can be affected by the fortunes of their descendants.
Notes: Solon (c. 640-558 BCE) was an Athenian statesman, lawgiver, and one of the 'Seven Sages' of Greece. His famous exchange with Croesus, king of Lydia, is reported by Herodotus (Histories I.30-33): Solon refused to call Croesus happy, saying 'Count no man happy until he is dead.' -- The puzzle about whether the dead can be affected by the living connects to Greek beliefs about the afterlife and ancestral honor. -- This passage begins an extended discussion (1.29-1.35) of the relationship between happiness, fortune, and the completeness of a life. -- The problem anticipates modern discussions of posthumous harm and whether the dead can be wronged.

D: Original: But we must return to our first difficulty; for perhaps by a consideration of it our present problem might be solved. Now if we must see the end and only then call a man happy, not as being happy but as having been so before, surely this is a paradox, that when he is happy the attribute that belongs to him is not to be truly predicated of him because we do not wish to call living men happy, on account of the changes that may befall them, and because we have assumed happiness to be something permanent and by no means easily changed, while a single man may suffer many turns of fortune's wheel. For clearly if we were to keep pace with his fortunes, we should often call the same man happy and again wretched, making the happy man out to be chameleon and insecurely based. Or is this keeping pace with his fortunes quite wrong? Success or failure in life does not depend on these, but human life, as we said, needs these as mere additions, while virtuous activities or their opposites are what constitute happiness or the reverse.
D: Refashioned:
But we must return to our original difficulty, for considering it may help solve our present problem. If we must see the end and only then call a person happy -- not as being happy now, but as having been happy before -- surely this is a paradox. When the person is happy, we cannot truly say so, because we do not wish to call the living happy on account of the changes that may befall them, and because we have assumed happiness to be something permanent and not easily altered. Yet a single individual may experience many turns of fortune's wheel.

For clearly, if we were to follow the fluctuations of someone's fortune, we would often call the same person happy and then wretched again, making the happy person out to be a kind of chameleon, resting on no stable foundation.

Or is tracking fortunes entirely the wrong approach? Success or failure in life does not depend on fortune, but, as we said, human life requires some favorable fortune as an addition. What truly constitutes happiness or its opposite is virtuous activity or its absence.
D: Elegant: If we can only call someone happy after death -- not as being happy but as having been so -- this is paradoxical: we cannot affirm happiness when it exists. Following fortune's fluctuations would make the happy person a chameleon on unstable ground. But perhaps tracking fortune is the wrong approach entirely. Happiness does not depend on fortune but on virtuous activity; external fortune is merely an addition to what truly constitutes happiness.
D: Summary: Calling someone happy only after death creates a paradox: we cannot affirm their happiness while it exists. But tracking fortune's fluctuations would make happiness unstable and chameleon-like. The solution is that happiness depends fundamentally on virtuous activity, not on fortune, which is merely a supplement.
D: Distilled: Happiness depends not on fortune's fluctuations but on the stability of virtuous activity, which is the true foundation of a good life.
Notes: The 'chameleon' metaphor vividly captures the instability that would result from tying happiness to fortune. -- This passage resolves the Solon problem by distinguishing what constitutes happiness (virtuous activity) from what supplements it (fortune). -- The claim that virtuous activities 'constitute' happiness while external goods are 'additions' parallels the distinction between formal and material causes in Aristotle's metaphysics. -- This resolution anticipates the Stoic position that virtue is sufficient for happiness, though Aristotle does not go that far -- he maintains that external goods are genuinely needed, just not as the primary determinant.

D: Original: The question we have now discussed confirms our definition. For no function of man has so much permanence as virtuous activities (these are thought to be more durable even than knowledge of the sciences), and of these themselves the most valuable are more durable because those who are happy spend their life most readily and most continuously in these; for this seems to be the reason why we do not forget them. The attribute in question, then, will belong to the happy man, and he will be happy throughout his life; for always, or by preference to everything else, he will be engaged in virtuous action and contemplation, and he will bear the chances of life most nobly and altogether decorously, if he is 'truly good' and 'foursquare beyond reproach'.
D: Refashioned:
The question we have just discussed confirms our definition. For no human activity has as much permanence as virtuous activities -- they are thought to be more enduring even than scientific knowledge. And among virtuous activities themselves, the most valuable are the most enduring, because happy people spend their lives most readily and most continuously engaged in these. This seems to be the reason we do not forget them.

The quality we seek, then, will belong to the happy person, and they will be happy throughout their life. For always, or at least in preference to everything else, they will be engaged in virtuous action and contemplation, and they will bear the fortunes of life most nobly and with complete dignity -- if they are truly good and 'foursquare beyond reproach.'
D: Elegant: Virtuous activities have greater permanence than any other human pursuit, even scientific knowledge. The most valuable among them are the most enduring, and happy people devote themselves to these most continuously. The happy person will therefore be happy throughout life, always engaged in virtuous action and contemplation, bearing fortune nobly -- truly good and 'foursquare beyond reproach.'
D: Summary: Virtuous activities are the most permanent of all human pursuits, more enduring even than scientific knowledge. Because the happy person devotes their life to these activities continuously, their happiness is stable and they bear misfortune with nobility and grace.
D: Distilled: Virtuous activity is the most enduring thing in human life, which is why the truly happy person remains happy throughout life, bearing fortune with noble steadfastness.
Notes: The phrase 'foursquare beyond reproach' (tetragonos aneu psogou) is from the poet Simonides of Ceos (c. 556-468 BCE), quoted in Plato's Protagoras (339b). The image of a foursquare (cubic) man suggests stability and solidity of character. -- The claim that virtuous activities are more enduring than scientific knowledge is striking and reflects Aristotle's view that practical wisdom is deeply embedded in character. -- The mention of contemplation (theoria) alongside virtuous action anticipates the discussion of the contemplative life in Book X.7-8. -- This passage resolves the stability problem: happiness, grounded in virtue, is not subject to fortune's volatility.

D: Original: Now many events happen by chance, and events differing in importance; small pieces of good fortune or of its opposite clearly do not weigh down the scales of life one way or the other, but a multitude of great events if they turn out well will make life happier (for not only are they themselves such as to add beauty to life, but the way a man deals with them may be noble and good), while if they turn out ill they crush and maim happiness; for they both bring pain with them and hinder many activities. Yet even in these nobility shines through, when a man bears with resignation many great misfortunes, not through insensibility to pain but through nobility and greatness of soul.
D: Refashioned:
Now many things happen by chance, and these events vary in significance. Small pieces of good fortune or bad fortune clearly do not tip the scales of life one way or the other. But many great events, if they turn out well, will make life happier -- for they are themselves the kind of thing that adds beauty to life, and the way a person handles them can be noble and good. If they turn out badly, however, they crush and damage happiness, for they bring pain and hinder many activities.

Yet even in the midst of such adversity, nobility shines through when a person bears many great misfortunes with composure -- not from insensitivity to pain, but from nobility and greatness of soul.
D: Elegant: Small fortunes do not determine the quality of a life, but many great events do matter: good fortune adds beauty to life, and handling it well reveals nobility, while great misfortune brings pain and hinders virtuous activity. Yet even amid adversity, true nobility shines through -- not from insensitivity to pain but from greatness of soul.
D: Summary: Minor fortunes do not affect happiness much, but major ones do: great good fortune enhances life, while great misfortune causes pain and hinders virtuous activity. Yet even under severe adversity, a person of noble character bears suffering with grace, revealing greatness of soul.
D: Distilled: Great misfortunes genuinely damage happiness by causing pain and hindering activity, yet true nobility of character shines through even in adversity.
Notes: The concept of 'greatness of soul' (megalopsychia) is treated at length in Book IV.3, where Aristotle describes the megalopsychos as someone who considers themselves worthy of great things and actually is. -- The acknowledgment that great misfortune can 'crush and maim' happiness qualifies the stability thesis of 1.31 and shows Aristotle's realism about the human condition. -- The distinction between insensitivity (anaisthesia) and noble endurance reflects Aristotle's general view that virtue is a mean between extremes. -- This passage influenced the Stoic concept of apatheia (freedom from destructive passion), though the Stoics took the idea further than Aristotle would.

D: Original: If activities are, as we said, what gives life its character, no happy man can become miserable; for he will never do the acts that are hateful and mean. For the man who is truly good and wise, we think, bears all the chances life becomingly and always makes the best of circumstances, as a good general makes the best military use of the army at his command and a good shoemaker makes the best shoes out of the hides that are given him; and so with all other craftsmen. And if this is the case, the happy man can never become miserable; though he will not reach blessedness, if he meet with fortunes like those of Priam.
D: Refashioned:
If activities are, as we said, what determine the character of a life, then no happy person can become truly miserable, for they will never perform hateful and base actions. The person who is truly good and wise, we believe, bears all of life's fortunes with grace and always makes the best of circumstances -- just as a good general makes the best military use of the army at their command, and a good shoemaker makes the best shoes from the hides they are given, and so with all other craftspeople.

If this is the case, the happy person can never become truly miserable -- though they will not reach full blessedness if they encounter fortunes like those of Priam.
D: Elegant: If activities determine life's character, the happy person can never become truly miserable, since they will never do base things. The truly good and wise person always makes the best of circumstances -- as a good general uses the army at hand, and a good shoemaker makes the best shoes from available hides. The happy person cannot become miserable, though Priam-like fortunes will prevent full blessedness.
D: Summary: Since activities define the character of a life, a truly good person will never become miserable because they will never act basely. Like skilled craftspeople who make the best of whatever materials they have, the virtuous person always handles circumstances with wisdom and grace -- though extreme misfortune like Priam's can prevent full blessedness.
D: Distilled: The truly virtuous person can never become miserable because they will always act nobly with whatever circumstances provide, like a craftsman making the best of available materials.
Notes: The craftsman analogies (general, shoemaker) illustrate practical wisdom (phronesis): the ability to deliberate well about what is achievable given one's circumstances. This is treated fully in Book VI. -- The distinction between not being miserable and not reaching full blessedness (makariotes) introduces a middle ground that avoids both the claim that the virtuous are always happy and the claim that fortune can destroy happiness. -- The return to Priam's example (cf. 1.28) provides continuity to the discussion. -- This passage reflects Aristotle's characteristic balance between idealism (virtue is paramount) and realism (fortune matters).

D: Original: Nor, again, is he many-coloured and changeable; for neither will he be moved from his happy state easily or by any ordinary misadventures, but only by many great ones, nor, if he has had many great misadventures, will he recover his happiness in a short time, but if at all, only in a long and complete one in which he has attained many splendid successes.
D: Refashioned: Nor, again, is the happy person unstable and changeable. They will not be easily dislodged from their happy state by ordinary misfortunes, but only by many great ones. And if they have suffered many great misfortunes, they will not recover their happiness quickly, but only -- if at all -- over a long and complete period in which they have achieved many splendid successes.
D: Elegant: The happy person is not easily dislodged from happiness by ordinary misfortunes, but only by many great ones. Recovery from such calamity, if possible at all, requires a long period of renewed splendid achievement.
D: Summary: The truly happy person is stable: ordinary misfortunes cannot shake their happiness, only many great ones can. Recovery from such devastating losses requires a long period of renewed virtuous achievement.
D: Distilled: True happiness is resilient against ordinary misfortune and can only be shaken by many great calamities, with recovery requiring sustained virtuous achievement.
Notes: This brief passage completes the account of happiness and fortune begun in 1.29. -- The emphasis on stability (bebaios) of happiness contrasts with the 'chameleon' image from 1.30. -- The requirement of a 'long and complete' period for recovery recalls the 'complete life' requirement from 1.19. -- The passage implicitly distinguishes between the loss of happiness (which requires many great misfortunes) and the impossibility of full blessedness (Priam's case in 1.33), suggesting degrees of well-being.

D: Original: When then should we not say that he is happy who is active in accordance with complete virtue and is sufficiently equipped with external goods, not for some chance period but throughout a complete life? Or must we add 'and who is destined to live thus and die as befits his life'? Certainly the future is obscure to us, while happiness, we claim, is an end and something in every way final. If so, we shall call happy those among living men in whom these conditions are, and are to be, fulfilled- but happy men. So much for these questions.
D: Refashioned:
Why then should we not say that the happy person is one who is active in accordance with complete virtue and is adequately equipped with external goods, not for some chance period but throughout a complete life? Or must we add: 'and who is destined to live this way and die as befits such a life'? Certainly the future is unknown to us, while happiness, we maintain, is an end and something completely final in every respect.

If so, we shall call those among the living happy in whom these conditions are, and are to be, fulfilled -- but happy as human beings can be happy. So much for these questions.
D: Elegant: The happy person is one who is active in accordance with complete virtue, adequately equipped with external goods, throughout a complete life. Perhaps we should add: 'and destined to live and die accordingly' -- though the future is unknowable. We call such living persons happy -- happy, that is, as human beings can be.
D: Summary: Aristotle concludes that we can call someone happy who actively exercises complete virtue with sufficient external goods throughout a complete life. The qualification 'as human beings can be' acknowledges that full certainty about happiness is impossible given the unknowability of the future.
D: Distilled: A person is happy who exercises complete virtue with adequate external goods throughout a whole life -- happy, that is, to the extent that human beings can be.
Notes: This passage provides Aristotle's final, qualified definition of happiness in Book I: virtuous activity + external goods + complete life. -- The qualification 'happy as human beings' (makarious d' anthropous) suggests that perfect blessedness (makariotes) belongs only to the gods, while humans can achieve a human-scale happiness. This distinction is developed in Book X.8 (1178b). -- The phrase 'destined to live thus and die as befits' acknowledges the Solon problem (1.29) without fully resolving it. -- This summary passage effectively closes the extended discussion of happiness and fortune (1.29-1.35) before Aristotle moves to related questions.

D: Original: That the fortunes of descendants and of all a man's friends should not affect his happiness at all seems a very unfriendly doctrine, and one opposed to the opinions men hold; but since the events that happen are numerous and admit of all sorts of difference, and some come more near to us and others less so, it seems a long- nay, an infinite- task to discuss each in detail; a general outline will perhaps suffice. If, then, as some of a man's own misadventures have a certain weight and influence on life while others are, as it were, lighter, so too there are differences among the misadventures of our friends taken as a whole, and it makes a difference whether the various suffering befall the living or the dead (much more even than whether lawless and terrible deeds are presupposed in a tragedy or done on the stage), this difference also must be taken into account; or rather, perhaps, the fact that doubt is felt whether the dead share in any good or evil. For it seems, from these considerations, that even if anything whether good or evil penetrates to them, it must be something weak and negligible, either in itself or for them, or if not, at least it must be such in degree and kind as not to make happy those who are not happy nor to take away their blessedness from those who are. The good or bad fortunes of friends, then, seem to have some effects on the dead, but effects of such a kind and degree as neither to make the happy unhappy nor to produce any other change of the kind.
D: Refashioned:
That the fortunes of descendants and of all one's friends should have no effect on one's happiness at all seems an unfriendly doctrine, and one contrary to common opinion. But since the events that occur are numerous and differ greatly in kind -- some affecting us more closely, others less -- it would be a long, indeed an infinite, task to discuss each case in detail. A general outline will perhaps suffice.

If, then, just as some of a person's own misfortunes carry significant weight for their life while others are comparatively trivial, so too there are differences among the misfortunes of friends considered as a whole; and if it makes a difference whether the various sufferings befall the living or the dead (even more than whether terrible crimes in a tragedy are presupposed in the background or enacted on stage) -- then this difference must be taken into account. Or rather, perhaps we should focus on the fact that real doubt exists about whether the dead share in any good or evil at all.

For it seems, from these considerations, that even if some good or evil does reach the dead, it must be something weak and negligible -- either in itself or for them. Or if not negligible in itself, it must be of such a kind and degree as not to make the unhappy happy or to take blessedness from the blessed. The good or bad fortunes of friends, then, appear to have some effect on the dead, but of a kind and degree that neither makes the happy unhappy nor produces any comparable transformation.
D: Elegant: It seems unfriendly to deny that descendants' fortunes affect one's happiness, yet detailed analysis of every case would be endless. Just as some personal misfortunes matter more than others, so do those of friends -- and it matters greatly whether they befall the living or the dead. Even if something reaches the dead, it must be too weak to make the unhappy happy or to strip blessedness from the blessed. Friends' fortunes may touch the dead slightly, but not enough to transform their fundamental condition.
D: Summary: While it seems harsh to say that descendants' fortunes have no effect on a dead person's happiness, any such effect must be minimal. Just as personal misfortunes vary in weight, the fortunes of friends and descendants may reach the dead, but never enough to transform happiness into misery or vice versa.
D: Distilled: The fortunes of descendants may faintly touch the dead, but never enough to transform their happiness or misery.
Notes: The tragedy analogy -- events presupposed vs. enacted on stage -- refers to the convention in Greek tragedy (especially Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides) that acts of violence typically occurred offstage and were reported by messengers. -- The discussion of whether the dead are affected by the living connects to Greek religious practices of ancestor worship and tomb offerings. -- This passage attempts to navigate between two extremes: total indifference of the dead to the living (which seems cold) and complete sensitivity (which would make happiness impossibly unstable). -- The topic is unique to the Nicomachean Ethics and does not appear in the Eudemian Ethics.

D: Original: These questions having been definitely answered, let us consider whether happiness is among the things that are praised or rather among the things that are prized; for clearly it is not to be placed among potentialities. Everything that is praised seems to be praised because it is of a certain kind and is related somehow to something else; for we praise the just or brave man and in general both the good man and virtue itself because of the actions and functions involved, and we praise the strong man, the good runner, and so on, because he is of a certain kind and is related in a certain way to something good and important. This is clear also from the praises of the gods; for it seems absurd that the gods should be referred to our standard, but this is done because praise involves a reference, to something else. But if if praise is for things such as we have described, clearly what applies to the best things is not praise, but something greater and better, as is indeed obvious; for what we do to the gods and the most godlike of men is to call them blessed and happy. And so too with good things; no one praises happiness as he does justice, but rather calls it blessed, as being something more divine and better.
D: Refashioned:
These questions having been settled, let us consider whether happiness belongs among the things that are praised or rather among the things that are honored and prized -- for clearly it is not to be counted among mere potentialities.

Everything that is praised seems to be praised because it is of a certain character and stands in a certain relation to something else. For we praise the just person, the courageous person, and generally both the good person and virtue itself because of the actions and functions involved. We praise the strong person, the fast runner, and so on because they are of a certain character and stand in a certain relation to something good and important. This is also clear from the praises addressed to the gods, for it seems absurd to refer the gods to our standard -- yet this is what praise does, since praise involves comparison to something else.

But if praise is for things of this kind, then clearly what applies to the highest things is not praise but something greater and better, as is indeed obvious. What we give to the gods and to the most godlike of human beings is not praise but the title of blessed and happy. The same applies to goods: no one praises happiness the way they praise justice; rather, they call it blessed, as being something more divine and better.
D: Elegant: Is happiness something praised or something prized? Praise is given for being of a certain character in relation to something else -- we praise the just or brave for their actions. Even praising the gods seems absurd, since praise implies comparison. What applies to the highest things is not praise but something greater: we call the gods and the most godlike humans blessed and happy. No one praises happiness -- they call it blessed, as something more divine.
D: Summary: Happiness is not something to be praised (like a virtue or skill, which is praised by comparison) but something to be honored as blessed. Praise implies reference to a standard, which is why praising the gods seems absurd. Happiness, like divinity, transcends the category of praise and belongs to the category of the prized and blessed.
D: Distilled: Happiness is not praised by comparison like a virtue or skill but honored as blessed, belonging to a higher category beyond praise.
Notes: The distinction between praise (epainos) and honor/prize (timios) draws on Greek cultural practices: praise was given for specific excellences, while honor and reverence were given to what was supreme. -- The argument that praising the gods is absurd because it subjects them to human standards reflects Aristotle's theology, where God is the unmoved mover beyond comparison (Metaphysics XII). -- This passage connects to the discussion of honor in 1.7, where honor was rejected as happiness because it depends on others' judgment. -- The argument is paralleled in the Eudemian Ethics I.12.

D: Original: Eudoxus also seems to have been right in his method of advocating the supremacy of pleasure; he thought that the fact that, though a good, it is not praised indicated it to be better than the things that are praised, and that this is what God and the good are; for by reference to these all other things are judged. Praise is appropriate to virtue, for as a result of virtue men tend to do noble deeds, but encomia are bestowed on acts, whether of the body or of the soul. But perhaps nicety in these matters is more proper to those who have made a study of encomia; to us it is clear from what has been said that happiness is among the things that are prized and perfect. It seems to be so also from the fact that it is a first principle; for it is for the sake of this that we all do all that we do, and the first principle and cause of goods is, we claim, something prized and divine.
D: Refashioned:
Eudoxus too seems to have been right in his method of arguing for the supremacy of pleasure: he thought that the fact that pleasure, though a good, is not praised indicated that it is superior to the things that are praised, and that this is what God and the good are -- for it is by reference to these that all other things are judged.

Praise is appropriate to virtue, since virtue disposes people to perform noble actions, while formal tributes are bestowed on accomplished deeds, whether of the body or of the soul. But perhaps precise distinctions in these matters are more properly the concern of those who have made a specialized study of such tributes.

For us, it is clear from what has been said that happiness belongs among the things that are honored and perfect. This seems to be confirmed also by the fact that happiness is a first principle, for it is for the sake of happiness that we do everything else we do; and the first principle and cause of goods is, we maintain, something honored and divine.
D: Elegant: Eudoxus argued that pleasure's not being praised shows it is superior to praised things, like God and the good by which all else is judged. Praise belongs to virtue, which produces noble deeds, while formal tributes honor accomplished acts. For our purposes, happiness clearly belongs among things honored and perfect -- it is a first principle, the cause for whose sake we do everything else, and such a principle is something honored and divine.
D: Summary: Eudoxus's argument that pleasure transcends praise supports the broader point that happiness is not merely praised but honored as something divine. Happiness is a first principle -- the ultimate cause for whose sake all else is done -- and such a principle deserves not praise but reverence.
D: Distilled: Happiness is a first principle and ultimate cause of all goods, and as such it belongs not among things praised but among things honored as divine.
Notes: Eudoxus of Cnidus (c. 390-337 BCE) was a mathematician, astronomer, and philosopher who studied at Plato's Academy. His hedonist arguments are discussed more fully in Book X.2. -- The distinction between praise (epainos) and encomium (enkomion) was a formal one in Greek rhetoric: epainos was general commendation, enkomion was formal tribute to specific achievements. -- The identification of happiness as a 'first principle' (arche) and 'cause' (aition) elevates it from a practical goal to a metaphysical principle, connecting ethics to Aristotle's broader philosophy. -- This passage closes the discussion of praise vs. honor (1.37-1.38) and transitions to the final topic of Book I: the nature of virtue.

D: Original: Since happiness is an activity of soul in accordance with perfect virtue, we must consider the nature of virtue; for perhaps we shall thus see better the nature of happiness. The true student of politics, too, is thought to have studied virtue above all things; for he wishes to make his fellow citizens good and obedient to the laws. As an example of this we have the lawgivers of the Cretans and the Spartans, and any others of the kind that there may have been. And if this inquiry belongs to political science, clearly the pursuit of it will be in accordance with our original plan. But clearly the virtue we must study is human virtue; for the good we were seeking was human good and the happiness human happiness. By human virtue we mean not that of the body but that of the soul; and happiness also we call an activity of soul. But if this is so, clearly the student of politics must know somehow the facts about soul, as the man who is to heal the eyes or the body as a whole must know about the eyes or the body; and all the more since politics is more prized and better than medicine; but even among doctors the best educated spend much labour on acquiring knowledge of the body. The student of politics, then, must study the soul, and must study it with these objects in view, and do so just to the extent which is sufficient for the questions we are discussing; for further precision is perhaps something more laborious than our purposes require.
D: Refashioned:
Since happiness is an activity of the soul in accordance with perfect virtue, we must consider the nature of virtue, for perhaps we will thereby see the nature of happiness more clearly. The true student of politics, too, is thought to have studied virtue above all else, for the political leader wishes to make fellow citizens good and obedient to the laws. We see examples of this in the lawgivers of Crete and Sparta, and in any others of the kind that there may have been. And if this inquiry into virtue belongs to political science, then pursuing it will clearly be in keeping with our original plan.

But clearly the virtue we must study is human virtue, since the good we were seeking was human good and the happiness was human happiness. By human virtue we mean not virtue of the body but virtue of the soul, and happiness too we define as an activity of the soul.

If this is so, the student of politics must clearly have some knowledge of the soul, just as the person who is to heal the eyes or the body as a whole must know about the eyes or the body. This is all the more true since politics is more esteemed and better than medicine; and even among doctors, the best educated devote much effort to understanding the body. The student of politics, then, must study the soul -- but with these practical objectives in view, and only to the extent that is adequate for our present purposes, since a more exhaustive treatment would be more laborious than our inquiry requires.
D: Elegant: Since happiness is activity of the soul in accordance with perfect virtue, we must examine virtue to understand happiness. The student of politics studies virtue above all because the political leader aims to make citizens good. The virtue in question is human virtue -- virtue of the soul, not the body -- since happiness is an activity of the soul. The student of politics must therefore study the soul, as a doctor must study the body, but only so far as our practical purposes require.
D: Summary: Understanding happiness requires understanding virtue, since happiness is virtuous soul-activity. The student of politics studies virtue because the political leader's goal is to make citizens good. This requires some knowledge of the soul, just as medicine requires knowledge of the body, though only as much as the practical inquiry demands.
D: Distilled: Since happiness is virtuous activity of the soul, understanding it requires studying virtue, and studying virtue requires some knowledge of the soul.
Notes: This passage transitions from the account of happiness (chapters 1-12) to the account of virtue (chapter 13), which will be the subject of Books II-VI. -- The lawgivers of Crete and Sparta were famous in antiquity for their comprehensive systems of civic education; Sparta's was attributed to Lycurgus, Crete's to Minos. Plato discusses both in the Laws. -- The connection between politics and virtue-education is central to Aristotle's Politics, especially Books VII-VIII. -- The claim that politics is 'more esteemed and better than medicine' reflects the Greek ranking of sciences, with practical/political wisdom at the summit of practical knowledge (see Book VI). -- The soul (psyche) as the subject of study connects to Aristotle's De Anima, though he here explicitly limits the investigation to what ethics requires.

D: Original: Some things are said about it, adequately enough, even in the discussions outside our school, and we must use these; e.g. that one element in the soul is irrational and one has a rational principle. Whether these are separated as the parts of the body or of anything divisible are, or are distinct by definition but by nature inseparable, like convex and concave in the circumference of a circle, does not affect the present question.
D: Refashioned: Some things about the soul are said adequately enough even in the discussions outside our school, and we should make use of them. For example, it is said that one element of the soul is irrational and another has a rational principle. Whether these are separated like the parts of a body or of any divisible thing, or whether they are distinct in definition but inseparable by nature -- like the convex and concave in the circumference of a circle -- does not matter for our present purposes.
D: Elegant: It is commonly held that the soul has both a rational and an irrational element. Whether these are physically separable or merely conceptually distinct -- like convex and concave in a circle's circumference -- does not matter for our purposes.
D: Summary: The soul has a rational and an irrational element. Whether these are physically separate parts or merely conceptually distinguishable does not affect the ethical inquiry at hand.
D: Distilled: The soul contains both rational and irrational elements, and their exact metaphysical relationship need not concern us for ethical purposes.
Notes: The 'discussions outside our school' (exoterikoi logoi) likely refers to popular philosophical works circulating outside the Lyceum, possibly including Aristotle's own published dialogues (now lost) or Platonic works. -- The convex/concave analogy illustrates Aristotle's frequent point that conceptual distinctions do not always map onto real separations. -- The bipartite division of the soul (rational/irrational) simplifies Plato's tripartite division (reason, spirit, appetite) from Republic IV. -- De Anima II-III provides Aristotle's more detailed and technical account of the soul's faculties.

D: Original: Of the irrational element one division seems to be widely distributed, and vegetative in its nature, I mean that which causes nutrition and growth; for it is this kind of power of the soul that one must assign to all nurslings and to embryos, and this same power to fullgrown creatures; this is more reasonable than to assign some different power to them. Now the excellence of this seems to be common to all species and not specifically human; for this part or faculty seems to function most in sleep, while goodness and badness are least manifest in sleep (whence comes the saying that the happy are not better off than the wretched for half their lives; and this happens naturally enough, since sleep is an inactivity of the soul in that respect in which it is called good or bad), unless perhaps to a small extent some of the movements actually penetrate to the soul, and in this respect the dreams of good men are better than those of ordinary people. Enough of this subject, however; let us leave the nutritive faculty alone, since it has by its nature no share in human excellence.
D: Refashioned:
Of the irrational element, one part seems to be widely shared across living things and vegetative in nature: I mean the part that is responsible for nutrition and growth. For this kind of power of the soul must be attributed to all things that take nourishment -- to embryos as well as to fully developed creatures. It is more reasonable to assign the same power to all of them than to suppose they have some different one.

Now the excellence of this part seems to be common to all species and not specifically human. This part or faculty seems to function most during sleep, and goodness and badness are least apparent when we sleep -- which is why the saying goes that the happy are no better off than the wretched for half their lives. This happens naturally enough, since sleep is a cessation of the soul's activity in the very respect in which the soul is called good or bad -- unless perhaps to a small extent some of our impulses penetrate through during sleep, which is why the dreams of good people are better than those of ordinary people.

But enough of this subject. Let us leave the nutritive faculty aside, since it has by nature no share in human excellence.
D: Elegant: The vegetative part of the soul -- responsible for nutrition and growth -- is shared by all living things and is not specifically human. It functions most during sleep, when goodness and badness are least manifest, which is why it is said the happy are no better than the wretched for half their lives. Since this faculty has no share in human excellence, it can be set aside.
D: Summary: The nutritive/vegetative part of the soul, responsible for growth and nourishment, is shared by all living things and operates most during sleep. Since goodness and badness are irrelevant during sleep, this part of the soul plays no role in human excellence and can be excluded from ethical inquiry.
D: Distilled: The vegetative soul, shared by all living things and most active during sleep, has no role in human excellence.
Notes: The classification of soul-parts (nutritive, perceptive, rational) follows De Anima II.2-4 closely. -- The observation about dreams is interesting: Aristotle suggests a limited connection between moral character and dream content, a topic he explores further in the short treatise On Dreams (De Insomniis). -- The proverb that 'the happy are no better than the wretched for half their lives' was apparently common; it highlights the connection between happiness and waking activity. -- The exclusion of the nutritive soul from ethics parallels its exclusion in the function argument (1.17).

D: Original: There seems to be also another irrational element in the soul-one which in a sense, however, shares in a rational principle. For we praise the rational principle of the continent man and of the incontinent, and the part of their soul that has such a principle, since it urges them aright and towards the best objects; but there is found in them also another element naturally opposed to the rational principle, which fights against and resists that principle. For exactly as paralysed limbs when we intend to move them to the right turn on the contrary to the left, so is it with the soul; the impulses of incontinent people move in contrary directions. But while in the body we see that which moves astray, in the soul we do not. No doubt, however, we must none the less suppose that in the soul too there is something contrary to the rational principle, resisting and opposing it. In what sense it is distinct from the other elements does not concern us. Now even this seems to have a share in a rational principle, as we said; at any rate in the continent man it obeys the rational principle and presumably in the temperate and brave man it is still more obedient; for in him it speaks, on all matters, with the same voice as the rational principle.
D: Refashioned:
There seems to be another irrational element in the soul as well -- one that, in a sense, however, shares in a rational principle. For we praise the rational principle both in the person with self-control and in the person without it, and we praise the part of their soul that possesses such a principle, since it urges them in the right direction, toward the best objects. But there is found in them also another element, naturally opposed to the rational principle, which fights against and resists it.

For just as paralyzed limbs, when we intend to move them to the right, turn instead to the left, so it is with the soul: the impulses of people who lack self-control carry them in the opposite direction from what reason prescribes. But while in the body we can see the part that goes astray, in the soul we cannot see it directly. Nonetheless, we must suppose that in the soul too there is something that opposes the rational principle, resisting and going against it. In what precise sense this element is distinct from the others does not concern us here.

Now even this element seems to share in a rational principle to some degree, as we said. At any rate, in the person with self-control it obeys the rational principle. And presumably in the temperate and courageous person it is still more obedient, for in such a person it speaks on all matters with the same voice as the rational principle.
D: Elegant: Another irrational element in the soul partly shares in reason. We see this in the conflict between reason and desire: in those lacking self-control, impulses oppose what reason prescribes, like paralyzed limbs that move the wrong way. Yet this desiring element can listen to reason -- in the self-controlled person it obeys reason, and in the truly temperate and courageous person it speaks with the same voice as reason on every matter.
D: Summary: Beyond the vegetative soul, there is another irrational element -- appetite or desire -- that can oppose reason, as seen in people who lack self-control. Yet this element can also listen to and obey reason. In the self-controlled it obeys reluctantly; in the truly virtuous it harmonizes completely with reason.
D: Distilled: The desiring part of the soul is irrational yet capable of listening to reason -- opposing it in the undisciplined but harmonizing with it in the truly virtuous.
Notes: The distinction between the self-controlled (enkratic) person -- who has bad desires but overcomes them through reason -- and the truly virtuous person -- whose desires naturally align with reason -- is crucial to Aristotle's moral psychology and is treated at length in Book VII. -- The paralyzed limb analogy illustrates moral conflict vividly: the body can visibly malfunction, while the soul's 'malfunction' is invisible but real. -- This account of the soul's internal conflict can be compared to Plato's chariot allegory in the Phaedrus (246a-254e). -- The idea that virtue involves harmony between reason and desire, not merely reason's domination, distinguishes Aristotle's view from Kant's later emphasis on duty against inclination.

D: Original: Therefore the irrational element also appears to be two-fold. For the vegetative element in no way shares in a rational principle, but the appetitive and in general the desiring element in a sense shares in it, in so far as it listens to and obeys it; this is the sense in which we speak of 'taking account' of one's father or one's friends, not that in which we speak of 'accounting for a mathematical property. That the irrational element is in some sense persuaded by a rational principle is indicated also by the giving of advice and by all reproof and exhortation. And if this element also must be said to have a rational principle, that which has a rational principle (as well as that which has not) will be twofold, one subdivision having it in the strict sense and in itself, and the other having a tendency to obey as one does one's father.
D: Refashioned:
The irrational element of the soul therefore appears to be twofold. The vegetative element shares in no way in a rational principle. But the appetitive element -- and the desiring element in general -- shares in a rational principle in a certain sense, insofar as it listens to and obeys it. This is the sense in which we speak of 'paying heed' to one's father or friends -- not the sense in which we speak of 'giving an account' of a mathematical property.

That the irrational element is in some way persuaded by a rational principle is also shown by the practice of giving advice and by all reproof and exhortation. And if this element must also be said to possess a rational principle, then the element that has a rational principle will itself be twofold: one part having it in the strict sense and in itself, the other having it in the sense of being disposed to listen, as one listens to one's father.
D: Elegant: The irrational soul is twofold: the vegetative shares nothing with reason, while the appetitive shares in reason insofar as it listens and obeys -- as one 'heeds' a father, not as one 'accounts for' a mathematical truth. That desire can be persuaded by reason is shown by the existence of advice, reproof, and exhortation. So the rational element is also twofold: one part possesses reason in itself, the other merely tends to obey it.
D: Summary: The irrational soul has two parts: the vegetative (entirely non-rational) and the appetitive (capable of listening to reason). The appetitive element's responsiveness to reason is shown by the fact that advice and exhortation work. This means the rational element of the soul is also twofold: one part possesses reason directly, the other obeys it as a child obeys a parent.
D: Distilled: The appetitive soul is irrational yet responsive to reason, creating a twofold rationality: reason proper and the capacity to be guided by reason.
Notes: The wordplay on 'logos' is important: 'having logos' can mean either 'possessing reason' or 'listening to reason/heeding.' Aristotle exploits this ambiguity to show that the appetitive soul 'has logos' in the second sense. -- The father analogy (echoing the opening of the Politics on household authority) suggests that the relationship between reason and appetite is one of legitimate authority, not tyrannical domination. -- The observation that advice and exhortation presuppose appetite's responsiveness to reason is a practical argument with significant implications: moral education is possible precisely because desire can be shaped by rational persuasion. -- This twofold division of rationality maps directly onto the division of virtues in 1.44.

D: Original: Virtue too is distinguished into kinds in accordance with this difference; for we say that some of the virtues are intellectual and others moral, philosophic wisdom and understanding and practical wisdom being intellectual, liberality and temperance moral. For in speaking about a man's character we do not say that he is wise or has understanding but that he is good-tempered or temperate; yet we praise the wise man also with respect to his state of mind; and of states of mind we call those which merit praise virtues.
D: Refashioned: Virtue, too, is divided into kinds in accordance with this distinction. Some virtues we call intellectual and others moral. Philosophical wisdom, understanding, and practical wisdom are intellectual virtues; generosity and temperance are moral virtues. When speaking of someone's character, we do not say that they are wise or have understanding, but that they are good-tempered or temperate. Yet we also praise the wise person with respect to their state of mind; and those states of mind that merit praise are what we call virtues.
D: Elegant: Virtue divides accordingly into intellectual and moral. Philosophical wisdom, understanding, and practical wisdom are intellectual virtues; generosity and temperance are moral. Character descriptions concern moral virtue -- we call someone temperate, not wise. Yet we also praise the wise person's state of mind, and praiseworthy states of mind are what we call virtues.
D: Summary: The twofold division of the soul's rational element yields a corresponding division of virtue: intellectual virtues (like philosophical wisdom and practical wisdom) and moral virtues (like generosity and temperance). Character descriptions concern moral virtues, though intellectual virtues are also praiseworthy.
D: Distilled: Virtue divides into intellectual virtues of the mind and moral virtues of character, corresponding to the two ways the soul can be rational.
