Notes: This passage provides the structural framework for the rest of the Ethics: moral virtues are treated in Books II-V, intellectual virtues in Book VI. -- The Greek word for moral virtue (ethike arete) derives from 'ethos' (character/habit), while intellectual virtue (dianoetike arete) derives from 'dianoia' (thought). -- Philosophical wisdom (sophia), understanding (sunesis), and practical wisdom (phronesis) are the key intellectual virtues analyzed in Book VI. -- Generosity (eleutheriotis) is treated in Book IV.1, temperance (sophrosune) in Book III.10-12. -- This final passage of Book I serves as a bridge to Book II, which opens with the distinction between intellectual virtues (acquired through teaching) and moral virtues (acquired through habituation). -- The overall structure of Book I thus moves from the highest good (happiness) to its components (virtue) to the division of virtue, setting the agenda for the remainder of the Ethics.

D: Original: Virtue, then, being of two kinds, intellectual and moral, intellectual virtue in the main owes both its birth and its growth to teaching (for which reason it requires experience and time), while moral virtue comes about as a result of habit, whence also its name (ethike) is one that is formed by a slight variation from the word ethos (habit). From this it is also plain that none of the moral virtues arises in us by nature; for nothing that exists by nature can form a habit contrary to its nature. For instance the stone which by nature moves downwards cannot be habituated to move upwards, not even if one tries to train it by throwing it up ten thousand times; nor can fire be habituated to move downwards, nor can anything else that by nature behaves in one way be trained to behave in another. Neither by nature, then, nor contrary to nature do the virtues arise in us; rather we are adapted by nature to receive them, and are made perfect by habit.
D: Refashioned: Virtue comes in two kinds: intellectual and moral. Intellectual virtue largely originates and develops through teaching -- which is exactly why it demands experience and time. Moral virtue, on the other hand, comes about through habit. Indeed, the very name for moral virtue in Greek (ethike) is derived, with only a slight variation, from the word for habit (ethos). This also makes it clear that none of the moral virtues develop in us simply by nature. Nothing that exists by nature can be trained into the opposite behavior. A stone, for instance, naturally falls downward and cannot be habituated to move upward -- not even if you threw it into the air ten thousand times. Nor can fire be trained to move downward, nor can anything else that naturally behaves one way be conditioned to behave differently. The virtues, then, arise in us neither by nature nor contrary to nature. Rather, we are naturally equipped to receive them, and we bring them to completion through habit.
D: Elegant: Virtue is twofold: intellectual and moral. Intellectual virtue grows mainly through teaching and requires time, while moral virtue develops through habit -- its very name (ethike) derives from the word for habit (ethos). No moral virtue arises in us by nature, since nothing natural can be trained into its opposite: a stone cannot learn to rise, nor fire to fall. Yet neither do the virtues arise contrary to nature. We are naturally constituted to receive them, but we perfect them through habit.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes intellectual virtue, which comes from teaching, from moral virtue, which develops through habit. Moral virtue is neither innate nor contrary to our nature -- we have a natural capacity for it, but it must be cultivated through practice.
D: Distilled: Moral virtue is not inborn but developed through habitual practice, for which nature gives us only the capacity.
Notes: The etymological connection between ethike (moral virtue) and ethos (habit) is central to Aristotle's argument and reflects his method of drawing philosophical insight from linguistic usage. -- The stone analogy illustrates Aristotle's physics, where natural objects have inherent tendencies (e.g., earth moves downward, fire upward) as discussed in Physics II and De Caelo. -- This passage implicitly challenges Plato's doctrine of innate knowledge (anamnesis) from the Meno, where virtue is recollected rather than acquired. -- The distinction between intellectual and moral virtue is foundational to the Ethics and recurs throughout, especially in Book VI's treatment of intellectual virtues.

D: Original: Again, of all the things that come to us by nature we first acquire the potentiality and later exhibit the activity (this is plain in the case of the senses; for it was not by often seeing or often hearing that we got these senses, but on the contrary we had them before we used them, and did not come to have them by using them); but the virtues we get by first exercising them, as also happens in the case of the arts as well. For the things we have to learn before we can do them, we learn by doing them, e.g. men become builders by building and lyreplayers by playing the lyre; so too we become just by doing just acts, temperate by doing temperate acts, brave by doing brave acts.
D: Refashioned: Furthermore, everything that comes to us by nature follows this pattern: we first possess the capacity and only later exercise it in activity. This is obvious with the senses -- we did not acquire sight or hearing by repeatedly seeing and hearing. On the contrary, we already had these senses before we used them; we did not develop them through use. But the virtues work the opposite way: we acquire them by first putting them into practice, just as we do with the crafts. We learn things by doing them -- people become builders by building and lyre-players by playing the lyre. In the same way, we become just by performing just actions, temperate by performing temperate actions, and brave by performing brave actions.
D: Elegant: Natural capacities like the senses exist in us before we use them -- we do not gain sight by seeing. Virtues work in reverse: we acquire them only through practice. Just as we become builders by building and musicians by playing, we become just by acting justly, temperate by acting temperately, and brave by acting bravely.
D: Summary: Unlike natural faculties such as sight and hearing, which we possess before using them, virtues are acquired through practice. We become virtuous by performing virtuous actions, just as craftsmen develop their skills by exercising them.
D: Distilled: We acquire virtues by exercising them -- we become just by doing just things, brave by doing brave things.
Notes: The potentiality/actuality (dynamis/energeia) distinction is a cornerstone of Aristotle's Metaphysics (Book IX). -- The craft analogy (techne) is one Aristotle uses frequently but will later qualify in 2.4 (thought 15), where he distinguishes the conditions for virtue from those for craft expertise. -- This learning-by-doing principle has been enormously influential in educational theory, anticipating pragmatist philosophy (Dewey) and contemporary research on deliberate practice.

D: Original:
This is confirmed by what happens in states; for legislators make the citizens good by forming habits in them, and this is the wish of every legislator, and those who do not effect it miss their mark, and it is in this that a good constitution differs from a bad one.

Again, it is from the same causes and by the same means that every virtue is both produced and destroyed, and similarly every art; for it is from playing the lyre that both good and bad lyre-players are produced. And the corresponding statement is true of builders and of all the rest; men will be good or bad builders as a result of building well or badly. For if this were not so, there would have been no need of a teacher, but all men would have been born good or bad at their craft. This, then, is the case with the virtues also; by doing the acts that we do in our transactions with other men we become just or unjust, and by doing the acts that we do in the presence of danger, and being habituated to feel fear or confidence, we become brave or cowardly. The same is true of appetites and feelings of anger; some men become temperate and good-tempered, others self-indulgent and irascible, by behaving in one way or the other in the appropriate circumstances. Thus, in one word, states of character arise out of like activities. This is why the activities we exhibit must be of a certain kind; it is because the states of character correspond to the differences between these. It makes no small difference, then, whether we form habits of one kind or of another from our very youth; it makes a very great difference, or rather all the difference.
D: Refashioned:
This is confirmed by what happens in political communities. Legislators make citizens good by shaping their habits -- this is the aim of every legislator, and those who fail at it miss their mark entirely. This is precisely what distinguishes a good constitution from a bad one.

Moreover, every virtue is both produced and destroyed by the same causes and the same means, just as every craft is. Both good and bad lyre-players are produced by playing the lyre. The same applies to builders and all other craftsmen: people become good or bad builders depending on whether they build well or badly. If this were not the case, there would be no need for teachers, since everyone would simply be born good or bad at their craft. The same holds true for the virtues. Through our dealings with other people we become either just or unjust. Through our behavior in the face of danger -- by becoming habituated to feel either fear or confidence -- we become either brave or cowardly. The same applies to our appetites and our anger: some people become temperate and even-tempered, others self-indulgent and hot-headed, depending on how they conduct themselves in the relevant situations. In a word, states of character arise from corresponding activities. This is why the activities we engage in must be of a certain quality, since our character traits correspond to the differences in these activities. It makes no small difference whether we develop one set of habits or another from our earliest youth -- it makes an enormous difference, or rather, it makes all the difference.
D: Elegant: Political life confirms this: legislators make citizens good by forming their habits, and a good constitution differs from a bad one precisely in its success at this. Every virtue, like every craft, is both produced and destroyed by the same activities -- good and bad lyre-players alike are made by playing. Through our actions with others we become just or unjust; through our responses to danger, brave or cowardly; through our handling of desire and anger, temperate or self-indulgent. Character arises from repeated activity. Therefore, the habits we form from youth onward make all the difference.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that virtues are produced and destroyed by the same kinds of activities, just as good and bad craftsmen are made by practicing their craft well or poorly. Legislators confirm this by aiming to shape citizens' habits. The habits formed from youth are therefore of supreme importance.
D: Distilled: Character is shaped by repeated action, and the habits formed from youth make all the difference between virtue and vice.
Notes: The political dimension of moral education is developed extensively in Politics VII-VIII, where Aristotle discusses how the ideal state should structure education. -- The claim that legislators aim to make citizens good connects the Ethics to the Politics; Aristotle ends the Ethics (X.9) by turning to political science as the means to make people virtuous. -- The emphasis on youth education echoes Plato's Republic (Books II-III) and Laws, though Aristotle grounds the argument in habituation rather than Platonic metaphysics.

D: Original: Since, then, the present inquiry does not aim at theoretical knowledge like the others (for we are inquiring not in order to know what virtue is, but in order to become good, since otherwise our inquiry would have been of no use), we must examine the nature of actions, namely how we ought to do them; for these determine also the nature of the states of character that are produced, as we have said. Now, that we must act according to the right rule is a common principle and must be assumed-it will be discussed later, i.e. both what the right rule is, and how it is related to the other virtues. But this must be agreed upon beforehand, that the whole account of matters of conduct must be given in outline and not precisely, as we said at the very beginning that the accounts we demand must be in accordance with the subject-matter; matters concerned with conduct and questions of what is good for us have no fixity, any more than matters of health. The general account being of this nature, the account of particular cases is yet more lacking in exactness; for they do not fall under any art or precept but the agents themselves must in each case consider what is appropriate to the occasion, as happens also in the art of medicine or of navigation.
D: Refashioned: Since our present inquiry does not aim at theoretical knowledge the way other inquiries do -- we are not investigating simply to understand what virtue is, but in order to become good, since otherwise the inquiry would be useless -- we need to examine how we ought to act. For our actions determine the character traits that develop in us, as we have already said. Now, the principle that we must act in accordance with right reason is widely accepted and may be taken as a starting point -- we will discuss later what right reason is and how it relates to the other virtues. But one thing must be agreed upon in advance: any account of matters of conduct can only be given in outline, not with precision. We made this point at the very beginning: the degree of exactness we should expect must match the subject matter. Questions about conduct and about what is good for us have no fixed answers, any more than questions about health do. And if the general account lacks precision, the account of particular cases is even less exact, since these do not fall under any established art or set of rules. The people acting must judge for themselves what the situation requires, just as in the practice of medicine or navigation.
D: Elegant: Our inquiry aims not at theoretical knowledge of virtue but at becoming good -- otherwise it would be pointless. We must therefore examine how to act, since actions shape character. Acting according to right reason is our guiding principle, to be elaborated later. But we must accept in advance that ethics allows only outline accuracy, not precision: matters of conduct have no fixity, like matters of health. Particular cases are even less exact, falling under no fixed rules -- agents must judge each situation themselves, as in medicine or navigation.
D: Summary: Aristotle stresses that ethics is a practical inquiry aimed at becoming good, not just understanding virtue theoretically. He warns that ethical reasoning can only provide general guidelines, not precise rules, since particular situations require individual judgment -- much like medicine or navigation.
D: Distilled: Ethics aims at becoming good, not just knowing what goodness is, and its guidance can only be approximate since every situation demands individual judgment.
Notes: The contrast between theoretical and practical knowledge is fundamental to Aristotle's philosophy; see Metaphysics VI.1 and Ethics VI on the distinction between episteme (scientific knowledge) and phronesis (practical wisdom). -- The reference to 'right rule' (orthos logos) anticipates the detailed discussion of practical wisdom (phronesis) in Book VI. -- The comparison to medicine recurs throughout the Ethics and reflects Aristotle's own background (his father Nicomachus was a physician). -- The methodological point about precision matching subject matter was first stated in I.3.

D: Original: But though our present account is of this nature we must give what help we can. First, then, let us consider this, that it is the nature of such things to be destroyed by defect and excess, as we see in the case of strength and of health (for to gain light on things imperceptible we must use the evidence of sensible things); both excessive and defective exercise destroys the strength, and similarly drink or food which is above or below a certain amount destroys the health, while that which is proportionate both produces and increases and preserves it. So too is it, then, in the case of temperance and courage and the other virtues. For the man who flies from and fears everything and does not stand his ground against anything becomes a coward, and the man who fears nothing at all but goes to meet every danger becomes rash; and similarly the man who indulges in every pleasure and abstains from none becomes self-indulgent, while the man who shuns every pleasure, as boors do, becomes in a way insensible; temperance and courage, then, are destroyed by excess and defect, and preserved by the mean.
D: Refashioned: Even though our account is necessarily imprecise, we must still offer what guidance we can. First, let us consider that things of this kind are naturally destroyed by excess and deficiency, as we can see in the case of physical strength and health. (To shed light on what is hard to perceive directly, we must rely on evidence from what is observable.) Both too much exercise and too little destroy physical strength, just as food and drink above or below a certain amount destroy health, while the right proportion produces, increases, and preserves it. The same holds true for temperance, courage, and the other virtues. The person who runs from everything, fears everything, and never stands firm becomes a coward. The person who fears nothing whatsoever and rushes into every danger becomes reckless. Similarly, the person who indulges in every pleasure and abstains from none becomes self-indulgent, while the person who avoids every pleasure, like those who are crude and unrefined, becomes in a way insensible. Temperance and courage, then, are destroyed by excess and deficiency, and preserved by the mean.
D: Elegant: Excess and deficiency destroy virtue, just as they destroy physical strength and health -- too much or too little exercise ruins the body, while the right amount preserves it. Whoever flees everything becomes a coward; whoever fears nothing becomes reckless. Whoever indulges in every pleasure becomes self-indulgent; whoever shuns all pleasure becomes insensible. Temperance and courage are preserved by the mean and destroyed by extremes.
D: Summary: Aristotle introduces the doctrine of the mean by analogy with physical health: just as too much or too little food and exercise destroy the body, excess and deficiency in our responses to fear and pleasure destroy the virtues of courage and temperance. The balanced middle state preserves them.
D: Distilled: Virtues like courage and temperance are destroyed by excess and deficiency and preserved by hitting the right balance -- the mean.
Notes: This is the first explicit statement of the doctrine of the mean (mesotes), which becomes the central concept of Book II. -- The health analogy reflects the strong influence of Greek medical theory (especially Hippocratic medicine) on Aristotle's ethics, where balance (isonomia) among bodily elements was considered the basis of health. -- The term 'boors' (agroikoi) for those insensible to pleasure recurs in Book IV's discussion of temperance.

D: Original: But not only are the sources and causes of their origination and growth the same as those of their destruction, but also the sphere of their actualization will be the same; for this is also true of the things which are more evident to sense, e.g. of strength; it is produced by taking much food and undergoing much exertion, and it is the strong man that will be most able to do these things. So too is it with the virtues; by abstaining from pleasures we become temperate, and it is when we have become so that we are most able to abstain from them; and similarly too in the case of courage; for by being habituated to despise things that are terrible and to stand our ground against them we become brave, and it is when we have become so that we shall be most able to stand our ground against them.
D: Refashioned: Not only do the sources of a virtue's development and destruction operate through the same activities, but the sphere in which the virtue is exercised is also the same. This is clearly true in more observable cases, such as physical strength: strength is built by consuming substantial food and undertaking strenuous exertion, and it is the strong person who is best able to do these very things. The same applies to the virtues. By abstaining from pleasures, we become temperate, and once we have become temperate, we are best able to abstain from them. Likewise with courage: by becoming habituated to face frightening things and to hold our ground, we become brave, and once we have become brave, we are best able to stand firm.
D: Elegant: The activities that produce a virtue are the same ones the virtue enables us to perform best. Strength is built through exertion, and the strong person is most capable of exertion. Likewise, we become temperate by practicing restraint, and once temperate, we are most capable of restraint. We become brave by facing danger, and once brave, we face danger most effectively.
D: Summary: Aristotle observes a virtuous circle: the same activities that develop a virtue are the ones that the virtue then enables us to perform best. Practicing restraint builds temperance, and temperance makes restraint easier; facing danger builds courage, and courage makes us better at facing danger.
D: Distilled: Virtue creates a reinforcing cycle: the practice that builds it is the same activity the virtue then empowers us to perform best.
Notes: This circular relationship between virtuous activity and virtuous disposition is a distinctive feature of Aristotle's moral psychology. -- The idea that virtues are self-reinforcing connects to the concept of hexis (stable disposition) developed more fully in the Categories (ch. 8) and Metaphysics V.20. -- This reciprocal structure poses a well-known philosophical puzzle: how does one begin to practice virtue without already possessing it? Aristotle addresses this partly through the role of early habituation and education.

D: Original: We must take as a sign of states of character the pleasure or pain that ensues on acts; for the man who abstains from bodily pleasures and delights in this very fact is temperate, while the man who is annoyed at it is self-indulgent, and he who stands his ground against things that are terrible and delights in this or at least is not pained is brave, while the man who is pained is a coward. For moral excellence is concerned with pleasures and pains; it is on account of the pleasure that we do bad things, and on account of the pain that we abstain from noble ones. Hence we ought to have been brought up in a particular way from our very youth, as Plato says, so as both to delight in and to be pained by the things that we ought; for this is the right education.
D: Refashioned: We should treat the pleasure or pain that follows our actions as an indicator of our character. The person who abstains from bodily pleasures and takes delight in doing so is temperate, while the person who finds abstinence annoying is self-indulgent. The person who faces frightening situations and enjoys it -- or at least is not distressed -- is brave, while the person who is distressed by doing so is a coward. Moral virtue is fundamentally concerned with pleasures and pains. It is because of pleasure that we do bad things, and because of pain that we hold back from noble ones. This is why we ought to be raised in a particular way from our earliest youth, as Plato says -- so that we come to feel delight and pain in response to the right things. This is what proper education really is.
D: Elegant: Pleasure and pain following action reveal character: the temperate person delights in abstaining from excess, the brave person is undistressed by danger. Moral virtue is fundamentally about pleasures and pains -- pleasure drives us toward bad actions, pain holds us back from noble ones. Therefore, as Plato said, proper education from youth must train us to feel delight and pain in response to the right things.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that our emotional responses to our own actions -- whether we feel pleasure or pain -- reveal our true character. Since pleasure and pain drive all moral behavior, proper education must train us from childhood to take pleasure in what is good and feel pain at what is bad.
D: Distilled: The pleasure or pain we feel in acting reveals our character, and right education trains us to delight in what is truly good.
Notes: The reference is to Plato's Laws (653a-c), where Plato argues that the first task of education is to train children's pleasures and pains. -- This passage establishes the central role of pleasure and pain in Aristotle's moral psychology, which will be explored more fully in Books VII and X. -- The idea that virtue involves enjoying virtuous action (not merely performing it grudgingly) is distinctively Aristotelian and distinguishes his view from Kantian ethics, where acting from duty rather than inclination is paramount.

D: Original: Again, if the virtues are concerned with actions and passions, and every passion and every action is accompanied by pleasure and pain, for this reason also virtue will be concerned with pleasures and pains. This is indicated also by the fact that punishment is inflicted by these means; for it is a kind of cure, and it is the nature of cures to be effected by contraries.
D: Refashioned: Here is another reason virtue is concerned with pleasures and pains: if the virtues deal with actions and emotions, and every emotion and every action is accompanied by pleasure and pain, then for this very reason virtue must be concerned with pleasures and pains. This is also confirmed by the fact that punishments work through pleasure and pain -- punishment is a kind of corrective treatment, and it is in the nature of corrective treatments to work through opposites.
D: Elegant: Since every action and emotion involves pleasure or pain, and virtues are concerned with actions and emotions, virtue necessarily involves pleasures and pains. Punishment confirms this: it works as a cure through contraries -- applying pain to correct pleasure-driven wrongdoing.
D: Summary: Aristotle offers a further argument that virtue involves pleasure and pain: since all actions and emotions are accompanied by pleasure and pain, and virtue governs actions and emotions, it necessarily governs pleasure and pain. Punishment, which corrects behavior through opposing stimuli, confirms this.
D: Distilled: Virtue necessarily concerns pleasure and pain because every action and emotion it governs is accompanied by one or both.
Notes: The principle that cures work through contraries (allopathic medicine) comes from Hippocratic medical theory and is discussed in Aristotle's own biological works. -- This brief argument is one of several converging lines of evidence Aristotle marshals in chapter 3 to establish that virtue is fundamentally about pleasure and pain.

D: Original: Again, as we said but lately, every state of soul has a nature relative to and concerned with the kind of things by which it tends to be made worse or better; but it is by reason of pleasures and pains that men become bad, by pursuing and avoiding these- either the pleasures and pains they ought not or when they ought not or as they ought not, or by going wrong in one of the other similar ways that may be distinguished. Hence men even define the virtues as certain states of impassivity and rest; not well, however, because they speak absolutely, and do not say 'as one ought' and 'as one ought not' and 'when one ought or ought not', and the other things that may be added. We assume, then, that this kind of excellence tends to do what is best with regard to pleasures and pains, and vice does the contrary.
D: Refashioned: Furthermore, as we noted earlier, every state of the soul is defined by its relationship to the things that tend to make it better or worse. People become bad through pleasures and pains -- by pursuing and avoiding the wrong ones, or at the wrong times, or in the wrong ways, or by going wrong in any of the other ways we might distinguish. This is why some thinkers have even defined the virtues as states of being unaffected and at rest. But this definition is inadequate, because they state it in absolute terms without adding the necessary qualifications: 'as one ought,' 'as one ought not,' 'when one ought or ought not,' and the other relevant conditions. We may take it as established, then, that virtue is the kind of excellence that disposes us to act in the best way with regard to pleasures and pains, while vice does the opposite.
D: Elegant: Every state of soul is shaped by what improves or worsens it, and people become bad through mishandling pleasures and pains -- pursuing or avoiding the wrong ones, at the wrong times, in the wrong ways. Some define virtue as a state of impassivity, but this is too crude: the crucial qualifications are 'as one ought,' 'when one ought,' and 'in the way one ought.' Virtue, then, is the excellence of responding rightly to pleasures and pains; vice is its opposite.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that character is corrupted by pursuing the wrong pleasures and pains in the wrong ways. He criticizes definitions of virtue as mere impassivity, insisting on the crucial qualifications: the right objects, at the right time, in the right way. Virtue is the disposition to handle pleasure and pain well.
D: Distilled: Virtue is not the absence of feeling but the excellence of responding to pleasure and pain in the right way, at the right time, toward the right objects.
Notes: The 'some' who define virtue as apatheia (impassivity or freedom from passions) likely refers to early Cynics or proto-Stoic thinkers. The Stoics would later develop apatheia as a central ethical ideal, in explicit contrast to Aristotle's doctrine of the mean. -- The qualification formula ('the right time, the right object, the right way') becomes a signature Aristotelian formulation, appearing most famously in the definition of virtue at 2.6 (thought 23). -- Cross-reference to the earlier methodological remark at 2.3 (thought 9).

D: Original: The following facts also may show us that virtue and vice are concerned with these same things. There being three objects of choice and three of avoidance, the noble, the advantageous, the pleasant, and their contraries, the base, the injurious, the painful, about all of these the good man tends to go right and the bad man to go wrong, and especially about pleasure; for this is common to the animals, and also it accompanies all objects of choice; for even the noble and the advantageous appear pleasant.
D: Refashioned: The following observations further demonstrate that virtue and vice are concerned with pleasure and pain. There are three objects of choice -- the noble, the advantageous, and the pleasant -- and three corresponding objects of avoidance -- the base, the harmful, and the painful. The good person tends to get things right with respect to all of these, while the bad person tends to go wrong, especially regarding pleasure. For pleasure is something we share with all animals, and it accompanies every object of choice -- even what is noble and what is advantageous appear pleasant to us.
D: Elegant: Three things attract choice -- the noble, the advantageous, and the pleasant -- and three repel it: the base, the harmful, and the painful. The good person responds rightly to all of these, the bad person wrongly, but especially regarding pleasure, which is universal among animals and accompanies all objects of choice, since even the noble and advantageous appear pleasant.
D: Summary: Aristotle identifies three objects of choice (the noble, advantageous, and pleasant) and argues that virtue involves responding correctly to all three. Pleasure is especially important because it is shared with all animals and accompanies every object of choice.
D: Distilled: Pleasure is the most critical domain of virtue because it is universal, shared with all animals, and colors every choice we make.
Notes: The tripartite classification of objects of choice (noble/kalon, advantageous/sympheron, pleasant/hedys) recurs throughout the Ethics, notably in the discussion of friendship (Books VIII-IX), where friendships are classified according to these three grounds. -- The observation that even the noble appears pleasant connects to Aristotle's view that the virtuous person takes pleasure in virtuous action (I.8).

D: Original: Again, it has grown up with us all from our infancy; this is why it is difficult to rub off this passion, engrained as it is in our life. And we measure even our actions, some of us more and others less, by the rule of pleasure and pain. For this reason, then, our whole inquiry must be about these; for to feel delight and pain rightly or wrongly has no small effect on our actions.
D: Refashioned: Moreover, the experience of pleasure has been with every one of us since infancy, which is why this feeling is so difficult to remove -- it is deeply embedded in our lives. And we measure our actions, some of us more and others less, by the standard of pleasure and pain. For this reason, our entire inquiry must concern itself with these, since feeling delight and pain in the right or wrong way has no small effect on our actions.
D: Elegant: Pleasure has shaped us since infancy and is ingrained in our lives, making it difficult to dislodge. We measure our actions by pleasure and pain, some more than others. Because responding rightly or wrongly to pleasure and pain profoundly affects our conduct, our whole inquiry must concern itself with these.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that pleasure is deeply ingrained from infancy and functions as the measure by which we guide our actions. Since our responses to pleasure and pain powerfully shape our conduct, the study of virtue must centrally address them.
D: Distilled: Pleasure has shaped us since infancy and serves as the measure by which we judge our actions, making it central to the study of virtue.
Notes: The claim that pleasure is ingrained from infancy anticipates modern developmental psychology's emphasis on early emotional conditioning. -- This passage supports Aristotle's broader project of integrating pleasure into a theory of the good life rather than treating it as inherently suspect, as some interpretations of Plato suggest.

D: Original:
Again, it is harder to fight with pleasure than with anger, to use Heraclitus' phrase', but both art and virtue are always concerned with what is harder; for even the good is better when it is harder. Therefore for this reason also the whole concern both of virtue and of political science is with pleasures and pains; for the man who uses these well will be good, he who uses them badly bad.

That virtue, then, is concerned with pleasures and pains, and that by the acts from which it arises it is both increased and, if they are done differently, destroyed, and that the acts from which it arose are those in which it actualizes itself- let this be taken as said.
D: Refashioned:
Furthermore, as Heraclitus says, it is harder to fight against pleasure than against anger -- and both craft and virtue are always concerned with what is more difficult, since achievement in what is harder is a greater good. For this reason too, the entire concern of both virtue and political science is with pleasures and pains: the person who handles them well will be good, and the one who handles them badly will be bad.

Let us take it as established, then, that virtue is concerned with pleasures and pains; that the actions from which it arises also increase it -- or, if done differently, destroy it; and that the actions from which it arose are the very same ones in which it expresses itself.
D: Elegant: As Heraclitus observed, fighting pleasure is harder than fighting anger, and virtue always concerns itself with what is harder, since harder achievements are greater goods. Therefore both virtue and political science center on pleasures and pains: whoever manages them well is good, whoever manages them badly is bad. In sum: virtue concerns pleasures and pains, is built and destroyed by the same activities, and expresses itself in those very activities.
D: Summary: Aristotle concludes his argument that virtue centers on pleasure and pain by citing Heraclitus: since resisting pleasure is harder than resisting anger, and virtue deals with what is difficult, pleasure is virtue's primary domain. He summarizes the chapter's key findings about how virtue develops, can be destroyed, and manifests.
D: Distilled: Mastering pleasure is the hardest and most important challenge for virtue, since whoever manages pleasure and pain well will be good.
Notes: The Heraclitus quotation (DK B85) -- 'It is hard to fight against anger (thymos), for whatever it wishes, it buys at the price of soul (psyche)' -- is adapted by Aristotle, who substitutes pleasure for the original reference. The exact original context is debated among scholars. -- The summary passage that closes this section uses the technique of ring composition, a common rhetorical device in Greek prose. -- The link between virtue and political science anticipates the discussion in Book X.9 and the transition to the Politics.

D: Original: The question might be asked,; what we mean by saying that we must become just by doing just acts, and temperate by doing temperate acts; for if men do just and temperate acts, they are already just and temperate, exactly as, if they do what is in accordance with the laws of grammar and of music, they are grammarians and musicians.
D: Refashioned: Someone might raise a question about our claim that we must become just by doing just acts and temperate by doing temperate acts. For if people are already performing just and temperate actions, are they not already just and temperate? After all, if someone does things that accord with the rules of grammar and music, they are already a grammarian or musician.
D: Elegant: An objection arises: if we become just by doing just acts, then those who already perform just acts must already be just -- just as those who follow grammatical rules are already grammarians.
D: Summary: Aristotle raises a natural objection to his theory: if virtue comes from doing virtuous acts, then anyone performing virtuous acts must already be virtuous -- which seems circular. He compares this to grammar, where following grammatical rules apparently makes one a grammarian.
D: Distilled: If we become virtuous by acting virtuously, the puzzle arises: must we not already be virtuous to perform virtuous acts?
Notes: This is a classic instance of Aristotle's dialectical method: he raises an objection against his own position in order to refine it. -- The puzzle echoes the Meno paradox about learning (Meno 80d-e): how can you search for something you do not already know? -- Aristotle will resolve this over the next several sections (2.14-2.17).

D: Original: Or is this not true even of the arts? It is possible to do something that is in accordance with the laws of grammar, either by chance or at the suggestion of another. A man will be a grammarian, then, only when he has both done something grammatical and done it grammatically; and this means doing it in accordance with the grammatical knowledge in himself.
D: Refashioned: But is this really true, even in the case of the crafts? It is perfectly possible to do something that accords with grammatical rules either by chance or by following someone else's instructions. A person is only a true grammarian when they have both produced something grammatically correct and done so from their own grammatical knowledge -- that is, when the action flows from the knowledge they possess within themselves.
D: Elegant: Even in the crafts, the objection fails. Someone might produce grammatically correct results by chance or by following instructions. A true grammarian does grammatical things from grammatical knowledge within himself -- the source of the action matters, not just its external form.
D: Summary: Aristotle begins resolving the objection by noting that even in crafts like grammar, producing correct results by chance or by following directions does not make someone a grammarian. True expertise requires that the correct action flows from the person's own knowledge.
D: Distilled: Doing the right thing by accident or instruction does not make you an expert -- the action must flow from genuine knowledge within you.
Notes: The distinction between acting correctly by accident and acting from genuine knowledge anticipates the more detailed threefold condition for virtue in the next passage (2.15). -- This reflects Aristotle's broader philosophical concern with internal states and causes, not just external outcomes -- a theme running through his metaphysics and philosophy of action.

D: Original: Again, the case of the arts and that of the virtues are not similar; for the products of the arts have their goodness in themselves, so that it is enough that they should have a certain character, but if the acts that are in accordance with the virtues have themselves a certain character it does not follow that they are done justly or temperately. The agent also must be in a certain condition when he does them; in the first place he must have knowledge, secondly he must choose the acts, and choose them for their own sakes, and thirdly his action must proceed from a firm and unchangeable character. These are not reckoned in as conditions of the possession of the arts, except the bare knowledge; but as a condition of the possession of the virtues knowledge has little or no weight, while the other conditions count not for a little but for everything, i.e. the very conditions which result from often doing just and temperate acts.
D: Refashioned: Moreover, the case of the crafts and that of the virtues are not truly parallel. The products of crafts contain their goodness within themselves -- it is enough that the product has the right qualities. But with actions that accord with the virtues, the quality of the action alone does not make them just or temperate. The person performing the action must also meet certain conditions. First, they must act with knowledge. Second, they must choose the actions deliberately, and choose them for their own sake. Third, the actions must flow from a firm and stable character. None of these conditions -- apart from bare knowledge -- are required for mastery of a craft. But for the possession of virtue, knowledge counts for little or nothing, while the other two conditions -- deliberate choice and stable character -- count for nearly everything. And these are precisely the conditions that develop from repeatedly doing just and temperate acts.
D: Elegant: Crafts and virtues differ fundamentally. A craft product is good if it has the right qualities -- the maker's inner state is irrelevant. But virtuous action requires three conditions in the agent: knowledge of what one is doing, deliberate choice of the action for its own sake, and action flowing from a firm and stable character. For crafts, only knowledge matters. For virtue, knowledge counts for little -- deliberate choice and stable character count for everything, and these develop from repeated practice.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes virtue from craft expertise. While a craft only requires that its product be good, virtue demands three things of the agent: knowledge, deliberate choice for the action's own sake, and a stable character. Unlike craft knowledge, these deeper conditions develop only through repeated virtuous practice.
D: Distilled: Virtue requires more than right action -- it demands knowledge, deliberate choice for the right reasons, and a firmly established character, all built through practice.
Notes: The three conditions for virtue -- knowledge, choice (prohairesis), and stable character (hexis) -- constitute one of the most important passages in the Ethics. Prohairesis (deliberate choice) becomes a key concept explored in detail in Book III.2-3. -- The claim that knowledge 'has little or no weight' for virtue may seem surprising but reflects Aristotle's anti-Socratic position: against Socrates' view (in Protagoras and Meno) that virtue is knowledge, Aristotle insists that character disposition and deliberate choice matter far more. -- The notion of hexis (stable disposition) is drawn from Aristotle's Categories (ch. 8) and distinguishes virtue from mere momentary right action.

D: Original: Actions, then, are called just and temperate when they are such as the just or the temperate man would do; but it is not the man who does these that is just and temperate, but the man who also does them as just and temperate men do them. It is well said, then, that it is by doing just acts that the just man is produced, and by doing temperate acts the temperate man; without doing these no one would have even a prospect of becoming good.
D: Refashioned: Actions are called just and temperate when they are the kind of actions a just or temperate person would perform. But the person performing them is not truly just or temperate merely for doing these things -- they must also do them in the way that just and temperate people do them. It is well said, then, that the just person is produced by doing just acts, and the temperate person by doing temperate acts. Without performing these actions, no one would have even the prospect of becoming good.
D: Elegant: Actions are called just or temperate when they match what the just or temperate person would do. But the agent is truly virtuous only when performing them as the virtuous person would -- with the right knowledge, choice, and character. The just person is made by doing just acts; without such practice, no one could ever become good.
D: Summary: Aristotle concludes that while virtuous actions are defined by what a virtuous person would do, truly being virtuous requires performing them in the right way -- with proper knowledge, deliberate choice, and stable character. Nevertheless, doing these acts is the only path to becoming good.
D: Distilled: A person becomes virtuous not merely by doing the right things, but by doing them in the right way -- yet without doing them, no one can ever become good.
Notes: The appeal to what the virtuous person 'would do' introduces the role of the phronimos (person of practical wisdom) as the standard of right action, a concept fully developed in Book VI. -- This passage resolves the circularity objection from 2.13: the initial practice need not be fully virtuous to begin the developmental process, but it must progressively approach the manner in which the truly virtuous person acts.

D: Original: But most people do not do these, but take refuge in theory and think they are being philosophers and will become good in this way, behaving somewhat like patients who listen attentively to their doctors, but do none of the things they are ordered to do. As the latter will not be made well in body by such a course of treatment, the former will not be made well in soul by such a course of philosophy.
D: Refashioned: But most people do not actually do these things. Instead, they take refuge in theory and believe they are practicing philosophy and that this will make them good. They behave rather like patients who listen carefully to everything their doctors say but then follow none of their instructions. Just as such patients will never heal their bodies through that approach, these people will never improve their souls through that kind of philosophy.
D: Elegant: Most people retreat into theory, imagining that philosophical understanding alone will make them good. They are like patients who listen attentively to their doctors but follow none of the prescribed treatment. Such patients will not heal their bodies, and such philosophers will not improve their souls.
D: Summary: Aristotle criticizes those who substitute theoretical study of virtue for actual practice. He compares them to patients who listen to their doctors' advice but never follow it -- their intellectual understanding alone will never make them good.
D: Distilled: Studying virtue without practicing it is like listening to your doctor's advice without following it -- understanding alone cannot make you good.
Notes: This is one of Aristotle's most memorable analogies and reinforces the practical orientation of the Ethics as stated in 2.4. -- The critique may be directed partly at certain followers of Plato's Academy who emphasized theoretical contemplation of the Forms over practical moral development. -- The medical analogy continues the recurrent comparison between ethics and medicine that runs throughout the Ethics, reflecting the ancient view that philosophy is therapy for the soul.

D: Original: Next we must consider what virtue is. Since things that are found in the soul are of three kinds- passions, faculties, states of character, virtue must be one of these. By passions I mean appetite, anger, fear, confidence, envy, joy, friendly feeling, hatred, longing, emulation, pity, and in general the feelings that are accompanied by pleasure or pain; by faculties the things in virtue of which we are said to be capable of feeling these, e.g. of becoming angry or being pained or feeling pity; by states of character the things in virtue of which we stand well or badly with reference to the passions, e.g. with reference to anger we stand badly if we feel it violently or too weakly, and well if we feel it moderately; and similarly with reference to the other passions.
D: Refashioned: Next, we must consider what virtue actually is. The things found in the soul fall into three categories: passions, faculties, and states of character. Virtue must be one of these. By passions, I mean things like appetite, anger, fear, confidence, envy, joy, friendly feeling, hatred, longing, competitiveness, pity -- and in general, any feeling accompanied by pleasure or pain. By faculties, I mean the capacities that make us able to experience these passions -- our capacity for becoming angry, feeling pain, or feeling pity, for example. By states of character, I mean the dispositions by which we are well or poorly oriented toward the passions. For instance, regarding anger, we are poorly disposed if we feel it too intensely or too weakly, and well disposed if we feel it in a moderate way. The same applies to the other passions.
D: Elegant: The soul contains three things: passions (feelings like anger, fear, joy, and pity that involve pleasure or pain), faculties (our capacities for experiencing these feelings), and states of character (the dispositions by which we relate well or poorly to our passions -- feeling anger too intensely, too weakly, or moderately). Virtue must belong to one of these categories.
D: Summary: Aristotle begins his analysis of what virtue is by distinguishing three elements of the soul: passions (emotions like anger and fear), faculties (capacities for feeling emotions), and states of character (our settled dispositions toward emotions). Virtue, he says, must fall into one of these categories.
D: Distilled: The soul contains passions, faculties, and states of character -- and virtue must be one of these three.
Notes: This tripartite classification of psychic elements is Aristotle's own framework, distinct from Plato's tripartite division of the soul (reason, spirit, appetite) in Republic IV. -- The list of passions (pathe) given here is not exhaustive but representative; compare the more systematic treatment in Rhetoric II.1-11. -- The concept of hexis (state of character/stable disposition) draws on the Categories (ch. 8) and becomes the genus of Aristotle's definition of virtue.

D: Original: Now neither the virtues nor the vices are passions, because we are not called good or bad on the ground of our passions, but are so called on the ground of our virtues and our vices, and because we are neither praised nor blamed for our passions (for the man who feels fear or anger is not praised, nor is the man who simply feels anger blamed, but the man who feels it in a certain way), but for our virtues and our vices we are praised or blamed.
D: Refashioned: Now, the virtues and vices are not passions. The reason is that we are not called good or bad on the basis of our passions, but we are called good or bad on the basis of our virtues and vices. Furthermore, we are neither praised nor blamed for our passions -- a person is not praised simply for feeling fear or anger, nor blamed simply for feeling anger, but rather for feeling it in a particular way. However, we are praised or blamed for our virtues and vices.
D: Elegant: Virtues and vices are not passions. We are called good or bad because of our virtues and vices, not our passions. We are neither praised nor blamed for simply feeling fear or anger, but for how we feel them. Yet we are praised and blamed for our virtues and vices.
D: Summary: Aristotle eliminates passions as the category of virtue. We are not judged good or bad for merely having feelings like anger or fear, but for how we experience them. Since we are praised and blamed for virtues and vices but not for bare passions, virtue cannot be a passion.
D: Distilled: Virtue is not a passion, because we are judged good or bad by our virtues and vices, not by merely having feelings.
Notes: The argument from praise and blame reflects Aristotle's method of using common moral judgments (endoxa) as data for ethical theory. -- This passage contributes to the process of elimination that will conclude with virtue being identified as a state of character (hexis) in 2.20. -- The distinction between feeling an emotion and feeling it 'in a certain way' connects to the doctrine of the mean developed in 2.6.

D: Original:
Again, we feel anger and fear without choice, but the virtues are modes of choice or involve choice. Further, in respect of the passions we are said to be moved, but in respect of the virtues and the vices we are said not to be moved but to be disposed in a particular way.

For these reasons also they are not faculties; for we are neither called good nor bad, nor praised nor blamed, for the simple capacity of feeling the passions; again, we have the faculties by nature, but we are not made good or bad by nature; we have spoken of this before. If, then, the virtues are neither passions nor faculties, all that remains is that they should be states of character.

Thus we have stated what virtue is in respect of its genus.
D: Refashioned:
Furthermore, we experience anger and fear without choosing to, whereas the virtues involve deliberate choice or at least require it. Also, with respect to the passions, we are said to be moved, but with respect to the virtues and vices, we are said not to be moved but to be disposed in a certain way.

For these same reasons, the virtues are not faculties either. We are not called good or bad, nor praised or blamed, simply for having the capacity to feel emotions. Moreover, we possess our faculties by nature, but we do not become good or bad by nature -- a point we discussed earlier. If, then, the virtues are neither passions nor faculties, the only remaining possibility is that they are states of character.

We have now established what virtue is with respect to its general category.
D: Elegant: Passions arise without choice; virtues involve choice. Passions move us; virtues dispose us. Nor are virtues faculties: we are not judged good or bad for the mere capacity to feel, and faculties are natural while virtue is not inborn. Since virtues are neither passions nor faculties, they must be states of character. This establishes the genus of virtue.
D: Summary: Aristotle completes his elimination argument: virtues cannot be passions (which arise involuntarily) or faculties (which are natural and morally neutral). By elimination, virtues must be states of character -- stable dispositions that involve choice and for which we are rightly praised or blamed.
D: Distilled: Virtue is a state of character -- a stable disposition involving choice -- since it is neither an involuntary passion nor a natural faculty.
Notes: The identification of virtue as a hexis (state of character/disposition) is a foundational result that feeds into the formal definition of virtue in 2.6 (thought 25). -- The role of choice (prohairesis) in distinguishing virtue from passion is developed more fully in Book III. -- The argument by elimination (neither passion nor faculty, therefore state of character) is a standard form of Aristotelian dialectical reasoning. -- The reference to the earlier discussion of nature is to 2.1 (thought 1).

D: Original: We must, however, not only describe virtue as a state of character, but also say what sort of state it is. We may remark, then, that every virtue or excellence both brings into good condition the thing of which it is the excellence and makes the work of that thing be done well; e.g. the excellence of the eye makes both the eye and its work good; for it is by the excellence of the eye that we see well. Similarly the excellence of the horse makes a horse both good in itself and good at running and at carrying its rider and at awaiting the attack of the enemy. Therefore, if this is true in every case, the virtue of man also will be the state of character which makes a man good and which makes him do his own work well.
D: Refashioned: However, we need to do more than simply classify virtue as a state of character -- we must also specify what kind of state it is. Let us begin with this observation: every excellence or virtue both puts its possessor in good condition and enables it to perform its function well. For example, the excellence of the eye makes both the eye itself good and its function -- seeing -- work well. Similarly, the excellence of a horse makes the horse good in itself and good at running, carrying its rider, and standing firm against the enemy. If this holds true in every case, then the virtue of a human being will likewise be the state of character that makes a person good and enables them to perform their proper function well.
D: Elegant: We must specify not just that virtue is a state of character, but what kind. Every excellence both perfects its possessor and enables it to function well: the excellence of the eye makes it good at seeing, the excellence of a horse makes it good at running and carrying its rider. Human virtue, likewise, is the state that makes a person good and enables them to fulfill their function well.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that every excellence serves two purposes: it perfects the thing that has it and enables that thing to perform its function well. Just as the excellence of an eye makes it see well and the excellence of a horse makes it run well, human virtue is the state that makes a person good and enables them to live well.
D: Distilled: Virtue is the excellence that both makes a person good and enables them to fulfill their human function well.
Notes: The 'function argument' (ergon argument) was introduced in I.7, where Aristotle argued that the human good (eudaimonia) consists in the excellent performance of our characteristic function -- rational activity. This passage applies that framework to virtue. -- The horse example is characteristic of Aristotle's tendency to draw from everyday Greek life (cavalry was central to Greek warfare). -- The connection between arete (excellence/virtue) and ergon (function/work) reflects a deeply teleological worldview elaborated in Physics II and Parts of Animals I.

D: Original: How this is to happen we have stated already, but it will be made plain also by the following consideration of the specific nature of virtue. In everything that is continuous and divisible it is possible to take more, less, or an equal amount, and that either in terms of the thing itself or relatively to us; and the equal is an intermediate between excess and defect. By the intermediate in the object I mean that which is equidistant from each of the extremes, which is one and the same for all men; by the intermediate relatively to us that which is neither too much nor too little- and this is not one, nor the same for all. For instance, if ten is many and two is few, six is the intermediate, taken in terms of the object; for it exceeds and is exceeded by an equal amount; this is intermediate according to arithmetical proportion. But the intermediate relatively to us is not to be taken so; if ten pounds are too much for a particular person to eat and two too little, it does not follow that the trainer will order six pounds; for this also is perhaps too much for the person who is to take it, or too little- too little for Milo, too much for the beginner in athletic exercises. The same is true of running and wrestling. Thus a master of any art avoids excess and defect, but seeks the intermediate and chooses this- the intermediate not in the object but relatively to us.
D: Refashioned: How this happens we have already discussed, but it will also become clear from the following analysis of virtue's specific nature. In everything continuous and divisible, one can take more, less, or an equal amount -- and this can be measured either in terms of the thing itself or relative to us. The equal amount is an intermediate between excess and deficiency. By 'intermediate in terms of the object,' I mean what is equidistant from each extreme -- a single, fixed point that is the same for everyone. By 'intermediate relative to us,' I mean what is neither too much nor too little for a particular person -- and this is not a single fixed point, nor the same for everyone. For example, if ten is many and two is few, then six is the intermediate in terms of the object, since it exceeds and is exceeded by an equal amount. This is the intermediate according to arithmetic. But the intermediate relative to us cannot be determined this way. If ten pounds of food is too much for a particular person and two pounds too little, it does not follow that the trainer should prescribe six pounds. Six might still be too much or too little for the person in question -- too little for Milo, too much for a beginner in athletic training. The same applies to running and wrestling. In this way, every master of a craft avoids excess and deficiency and seeks the intermediate -- not the intermediate in terms of the object, but the intermediate relative to us.
D: Elegant: In anything continuous and divisible, there is more, less, and the equal -- measured either objectively or relative to us. The objective intermediate (e.g., six between two and ten) is the same for all. But the intermediate relative to us varies by person: if ten pounds of food is too much and two too little, the right amount is not necessarily six -- it might be too little for Milo the wrestler and too much for a beginner. Every skilled practitioner avoids excess and deficiency, seeking not the objective midpoint but the intermediate relative to the individual.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes two kinds of intermediate: the objective mathematical midpoint (the same for everyone) and the intermediate relative to the individual (which varies by person and circumstance). Virtue aims at the latter, not the former -- just as a trainer prescribes different diets for different athletes.
D: Distilled: The virtuous mean is not a fixed mathematical midpoint but a balance calibrated to the individual -- what is right for one person may be wrong for another.
Notes: Milo of Croton was a legendary wrestler of the 6th century BCE, famous for his extraordinary strength and appetite. He reportedly carried a bull on his shoulders and ate enormous quantities of food. -- The distinction between the objective mean and the mean relative to us is crucial for understanding the doctrine of the mean: virtue does not prescribe a one-size-fits-all formula. -- The arithmetic proportion mentioned here contrasts with the geometric proportion Aristotle uses for distributive justice in Book V.3.

D: Original: If it is thus, then, that every art does its work well- by looking to the intermediate and judgling its works by this standard (so that we often say of good works of art that it is not possible either to take away or to add anything, implying that excess and defect destroy the goodness of works of art, while the mean preserves it; and good artists, as we say, look to this in their work), and if, further, virtue is more exact and better than any art, as nature also is, then virtue must have the quality of aiming at the intermediate. I mean moral virtue; for it is this that is concerned with passions and actions, and in these there is excess, defect, and the intermediate. For instance, both fear and confidence and appetite and anger and pity and in general pleasure and pain may be felt both too much and too little, and in both cases not well; but to feel them at the right times, with reference to the right objects, towards the right people, with the right motive, and in the right way, is what is both intermediate and best, and this is characteristic of virtue. Similarly with regard to actions also there is excess, defect, and the intermediate. Now virtue is concerned with passions and actions, in which excess is a form of failure, and so is defect, while the intermediate is praised and is a form of success; and being praised and being successful are both characteristics of virtue. Therefore virtue is a kind of mean, since, as we have seen, it aims at what is intermediate.
D: Refashioned: If this is how every craft performs its work well -- by looking to the intermediate and judging its products by this standard (which is why we often say of fine works of art that nothing could be added or taken away, implying that excess and deficiency ruin quality while the mean preserves it, and good artists keep this in mind as they work) -- and if, further, virtue is more precise and superior to any craft, just as nature is, then virtue must possess the quality of aiming at the intermediate. I mean moral virtue specifically, since it deals with passions and actions, and in these there is excess, deficiency, and the intermediate. For example, fear, confidence, appetite, anger, pity, and pleasure and pain in general can all be felt too much or too little -- and in both cases, not well. But to feel them at the right times, about the right things, toward the right people, for the right reasons, and in the right way -- that is the intermediate and the best response, and this is what characterizes virtue. Similarly, with actions too there is excess, deficiency, and the intermediate. Now, virtue is concerned with passions and actions, where excess is a form of failure and deficiency is also a form of failure, while the intermediate is praised and constitutes success. Being praised and being successful are both marks of virtue. Therefore, virtue is a kind of mean, since it aims at what is intermediate.
D: Elegant: Every craft achieves excellence by aiming at the intermediate -- we say of great works that nothing could be added or removed, since excess and deficiency destroy quality while the mean preserves it. Since virtue surpasses any craft in precision, it too must aim at the intermediate. Moral virtue concerns passions and actions, where both excess and deficiency are failures. To feel fear, anger, pleasure, or pity at the right times, toward the right objects, for the right reasons, and in the right way -- this is the intermediate, the best, and the characteristic mark of virtue. Virtue is therefore a kind of mean.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that just as great craftsmanship hits the perfect balance where nothing can be added or removed, moral virtue aims at an intermediate in passions and actions. Feeling emotions at the right time, toward the right objects, for the right reasons, and in the right way is what constitutes this virtuous mean.
D: Distilled: Virtue aims at the intermediate in feelings and actions -- the right response, to the right things, at the right time, in the right way.
Notes: The aesthetic principle that perfection admits neither addition nor subtraction echoes a dictum attributed to the sculptor Polyclitus and discussed in later aesthetic theory. -- The formula 'at the right times, with reference to the right objects, towards the right people, with the right motive, and in the right way' is the fullest statement of the multi-dimensional character of the mean. -- The claim that virtue is 'more exact and better than any art' elevates moral excellence above technical skill, a theme that connects to the discussion in 2.4 (thought 15) about how virtue differs from craft.

D: Original: Again, it is possible to fail in many ways (for evil belongs to the class of the unlimited, as the Pythagoreans conjectured, and good to that of the limited), while to succeed is possible only in one way (for which reason also one is easy and the other difficult- to miss the mark easy, to hit it difficult); for these reasons also, then, excess and defect are characteristic of vice, and the mean of virtue;
D: Refashioned: Furthermore, it is possible to go wrong in many ways -- as the Pythagoreans conjectured, evil belongs to the class of the unlimited, while good belongs to the class of the limited. But there is only one way to succeed. This is why missing the mark is easy while hitting it is difficult. For these reasons too, excess and deficiency are characteristic of vice, while the mean is characteristic of virtue.
D: Elegant: There are countless ways to fail but only one way to succeed -- evil belongs to the unlimited, good to the limited, as the Pythagoreans held. Missing the mark is easy; hitting it is hard. Excess and deficiency therefore characterize vice; the mean characterizes virtue.
D: Summary: Aristotle draws on Pythagorean philosophy to support the doctrine of the mean: since there are infinite ways to go wrong but only one way to get things right, failure (vice) is easy and success (virtue) is difficult. This is why vice involves excess or deficiency while virtue hits the mean.
D: Distilled: There are countless ways to go wrong but only one way to get it right, which is why virtue -- hitting the mean -- is rare and difficult.
Notes: The Pythagoreans organized reality into pairs of opposites (Table of Contraries), including limited/unlimited, good/evil, one/many. Aristotle discusses their system in Metaphysics I.5. -- The archery metaphor implicit in 'missing the mark' (hamartia) is significant: hamartia later becomes the key term in Aristotle's Poetics (ch. 13) for the tragic hero's error. -- This passage influenced the later philosophical and theological idea that evil is a privation or deviation from the good (Augustine, Thomas Aquinas).

D: Original:
For men are good in but one way, but bad in many.

Virtue, then, is a state of character concerned with choice, lying in a mean, i.e. the mean relative to us, this being determined by a rational principle, and by that principle by which the man of practical wisdom would determine it. Now it is a mean between two vices, that which depends on excess and that which depends on defect; and again it is a mean because the vices respectively fall short of or exceed what is right in both passions and actions, while virtue both finds and chooses that which is intermediate. Hence in respect of its substance and the definition which states its essence virtue is a mean, with regard to what is best and right an extreme.
D: Refashioned:
People are good in only one way but bad in many.

Virtue, then, is a state of character concerned with choice, lying in a mean -- the mean relative to us -- as determined by reason and by the standard that a person of practical wisdom would use. It is a mean between two vices: one involving excess and the other involving deficiency. It is also a mean in the sense that vices either fall short of or exceed what is right in both passions and actions, while virtue finds and chooses the intermediate. Thus, in terms of its essential nature and the definition that states what it is, virtue is a mean. But with regard to what is best and right, it is an extreme.
D: Elegant: People are good in one way, bad in many. Virtue is a state of character involving choice, lying in the mean relative to us, determined by reason as a person of practical wisdom would determine it. It is a mean between the vice of excess and the vice of deficiency -- finding and choosing the intermediate in passions and actions. In substance, virtue is a mean; in terms of excellence and rightness, it is an extreme.
D: Summary: Aristotle provides his formal definition of virtue: a stable state of character involving deliberate choice, lying in a mean relative to the individual, as determined by practical wisdom. While virtue is a mean between the vices of excess and deficiency in its substance, it represents the highest point of excellence -- an extreme -- in terms of what is best and right.
D: Distilled: Virtue is a deliberate disposition to choose the mean between excess and deficiency as practical wisdom determines -- a mean in substance, but the peak of excellence.
Notes: This is the famous formal definition of virtue (arete), one of the most cited passages in the history of philosophy. -- The reference to the phronimos (person of practical wisdom) as the standard of right action is central: virtue cannot be reduced to a formula but requires the judgment of the practically wise person, discussed in Book VI. -- The paradox that virtue is both a mean and an extreme resolves what might seem a contradiction: virtue is moderate in the sense of avoiding excess and deficiency, but supreme in the sense of being the best possible response. -- The definition parallels the Eudemian Ethics II.3, 1220b35-1221a12.

D: Original: But not every action nor every passion admits of a mean; for some have names that already imply badness, e.g. spite, shamelessness, envy, and in the case of actions adultery, theft, murder; for all of these and suchlike things imply by their names that they are themselves bad, and not the excesses or deficiencies of them. It is not possible, then, ever to be right with regard to them; one must always be wrong. Nor does goodness or badness with regard to such things depend on committing adultery with the right woman, at the right time, and in the right way, but simply to do any of them is to go wrong. It would be equally absurd, then, to expect that in unjust, cowardly, and voluptuous action there should be a mean, an excess, and a deficiency; for at that rate there would be a mean of excess and of deficiency, an excess of excess, and a deficiency of deficiency. But as there is no excess and deficiency of temperance and courage because what is intermediate is in a sense an extreme, so too of the actions we have mentioned there is no mean nor any excess and deficiency, but however they are done they are wrong; for in general there is neither a mean of excess and deficiency, nor excess and deficiency of a mean.
D: Refashioned: However, not every action or every passion admits of a mean. Some are named in ways that already imply badness -- for example, spite, shamelessness, and envy among the passions, and adultery, theft, and murder among actions. All of these and similar things are called bad in themselves, not merely when taken to excess or done in deficiency. It is never possible to be right with regard to them; one can only be wrong. Nor does the rightness or wrongness of such things depend on doing them with the right person, at the right time, or in the right way -- simply performing any of them is to do wrong. It would be equally absurd to look for a mean, an excess, and a deficiency in unjust, cowardly, or self-indulgent action, since that would imply a mean of excess, an excess of excess, and a deficiency of deficiency. Just as there is no excess or deficiency of temperance and courage -- because the intermediate is, in a sense, already an extreme -- so too there is no mean in these inherently bad actions and passions, nor any excess or deficiency of them. However they are performed, they are wrong. In general, there is neither a mean of excess and deficiency, nor an excess and deficiency of a mean.
D: Elegant: Not every action or passion admits of a mean. Some -- like spite, shamelessness, envy, adultery, theft, murder -- are intrinsically bad, not bad merely in excess or deficiency. One cannot commit them rightly: there is no 'right person' for adultery, no 'right time' for murder. Looking for a mean of unjust or cowardly action would absurdly produce a mean of excess and an excess of excess. Just as temperance and courage have no excess or deficiency (being themselves the right extreme), inherently bad actions have no mean. However they are done, they are wrong.
D: Summary: Aristotle qualifies the doctrine of the mean: certain actions and emotions are inherently bad, not bad merely in excess. There is no 'right way' to commit murder, adultery, or theft, and no mean to be found in them. The doctrine of the mean applies only to actions and passions that are not intrinsically wrong.
D: Distilled: Some actions -- like murder, theft, and adultery -- are inherently wrong and admit no mean; the doctrine of the mean applies only where the kind of action is not itself evil.
Notes: This qualification is philosophically crucial, as it addresses the most common misunderstanding of the doctrine of the mean -- that it recommends moderation in everything. Aristotle explicitly denies this. -- The examples of intrinsically wrong actions anticipate discussions of absolute moral prohibitions in later ethical traditions, including natural law theory (Aquinas). -- The logical point about a 'mean of excess' and 'excess of excess' demonstrates Aristotle's analytical precision in preventing infinite regress in the application of his doctrine.

D: Original: We must, however, not only make this general statement, but also apply it to the individual facts. For among statements about conduct those which are general apply more widely, but those which are particular are more genuine, since conduct has to do with individual cases, and our statements must harmonize with the facts in these cases. We may take these cases from our table. With regard to feelings of fear and confidence courage is the mean; of the people who exceed, he who exceeds in fearlessness has no name (many of the states have no name), while the man who exceeds in confidence is rash, and he who exceeds in fear and falls short in confidence is a coward. With regard to pleasures and pains- not all of them, and not so much with regard to the pains- the mean is temperance, the excess self-indulgence. Persons deficient with regard to the pleasures are not often found; hence such persons also have received no name. But let us call them 'insensible'.
D: Refashioned: We must not only state this general principle but also apply it to specific cases.
