In discussions of conduct, general statements may apply more broadly, but particular applications are more genuine, since conduct deals with individual cases and our theories must align with the facts in these cases. Let us draw our examples from the traditional table of virtues. Regarding feelings of fear and confidence, courage is the mean. Among those who go to extremes, the person who is excessively fearless has no established name (many of these states lack names), while the person who is excessively confident is rash, and the person who is excessive in fear and deficient in confidence is a coward. Regarding pleasures and pains -- though not all of them, and the connection to pains is less direct -- the mean is temperance, and the excess is self-indulgence. People who are deficient regarding pleasures are rare, which is why no name has been established for them. Let us call them 'insensible.'
D: Elegant: General principles must be applied to particulars, since conduct deals with individual cases. For fear and confidence, the mean is courage; excess in confidence is rashness, excess in fear is cowardice (excessive fearlessness lacks a name). For pleasures and pains, the mean is temperance, the excess self-indulgence. Deficiency in pleasure is so rare it has no name -- we might call it insensibility.
D: Summary: Aristotle begins mapping specific virtues onto his framework of the mean. Courage is the mean between cowardice and rashness regarding fear and confidence. Temperance is the mean between self-indulgence and insensibility regarding pleasure. He notes that some extremes are so rare they lack established names.
D: Distilled: Courage is the mean between cowardice and rashness; temperance is the mean between self-indulgence and insensibility -- though some extremes are so rare they lack names.
Notes: The 'table' Aristotle references may have been an actual chart used in his lectures at the Lyceum. -- The observation that many states lack names is methodologically important: Aristotle's scheme is not merely describing existing moral vocabulary but imposing a theoretical framework that reveals gaps in ordinary language. -- Courage is discussed in detail in Book III.6-9, temperance in III.10-12. -- The namelessness of certain extremes becomes a recurring theme in this chapter.

D: Original: With regard to giving and taking of money the mean is liberality, the excess and the defect prodigality and meanness. In these actions people exceed and fall short in contrary ways; the prodigal exceeds in spending and falls short in taking, while the mean man exceeds in taking and falls short in spending. (At present we are giving a mere outline or summary, and are satisfied with this; later these states will be more exactly determined.) With regard to money there are also other dispositions- a mean, magnificence (for the magnificent man differs from the liberal man; the former deals with large sums, the latter with small ones), an excess, tastelessness and vulgarity, and a deficiency, niggardliness; these differ from the states opposed to liberality, and the mode of their difference will be stated later. With regard to honour and dishonour the mean is proper pride, the excess is known as a sort of 'empty vanity', and the deficiency is undue humility; and as we said liberality was related to magnificence, differing from it by dealing with small sums, so there is a state similarly related to proper pride, being concerned with small honours while that is concerned with great. For it is possible to desire honour as one ought, and more than one ought, and less, and the man who exceeds in his desires is called ambitious, the man who falls short unambitious, while the intermediate person has no name. The dispositions also are nameless, except that that of the ambitious man is called ambition. Hence the people who are at the extremes lay claim to the middle place; and we ourselves sometimes call the intermediate person ambitious and sometimes unambitious, and sometimes praise the ambitious man and sometimes the unambitious. The reason of our doing this will be stated in what follows; but now let us speak of the remaining states according to the method which has been indicated.
D: Refashioned: Regarding giving and taking money, the mean is generosity, while the excess and deficiency are extravagance and stinginess. In these, people go to opposite extremes in contrary ways: the extravagant person spends too much and takes too little, while the stingy person takes too much and spends too little. (For now, we are giving only a rough outline and are satisfied with this; later these states will be examined more precisely.) Regarding money, there are also other dispositions. There is a mean called magnificence (the magnificent person differs from the generous person in dealing with large sums rather than small ones), with an excess of tastelessness and vulgarity, and a deficiency of miserliness. These differ from the states opposed to generosity, and how they differ will be explained later. Regarding honor and dishonor, the mean is proper pride; the excess is a kind of empty vanity, and the deficiency is excessive humility. And just as we said generosity is related to magnificence -- differing by dealing with small sums rather than large -- there is a similar state related to proper pride, concerned with small honors rather than great ones. It is possible to desire honor as one ought, or more than one ought, or less. The person who desires it excessively is called ambitious, the one who desires it too little unambitious, while the person in the middle has no name. The dispositions themselves are also nameless, except that the ambitious person's disposition is called ambition. As a result, people at the extremes each claim the middle ground, and we ourselves sometimes call the intermediate person ambitious and sometimes unambitious, sometimes praising the ambitious person and sometimes the unambitious one. The reasons for this will be explained later; but for now, let us discuss the remaining states according to the method we have been using.
D: Elegant: For money, the mean is generosity (excess: extravagance; deficiency: stinginess). Magnificence is a related mean for large-scale expenditure (excess: vulgarity; deficiency: miserliness). For honor, the mean is proper pride (excess: vanity; deficiency: excessive humility). A parallel virtue handles small honors, but it and its possessor have no name -- we sometimes call the middle person ambitious, sometimes unambitious, and praise both extremes at different times, since people at the extremes each claim the center.
D: Summary: Aristotle maps several more virtues as means: generosity (between extravagance and stinginess), magnificence (for large-scale spending), and proper pride (between vanity and excessive humility). He notes a parallel unnamed virtue concerning small honors and observes that everyday language is inconsistent, sometimes praising and sometimes blaming the same disposition.
D: Distilled: Generosity, magnificence, and proper pride are each means between excess and deficiency -- and ordinary language sometimes fails to name or consistently evaluate the middle states.
Notes: Generosity (eleutheriotēs) is discussed in Book IV.1, magnificence (megaloprepeia) in IV.2, and proper pride or magnanimity (megalopsychia) in IV.3 -- one of the most discussed and controversial passages in the Ethics. -- The observation that common language lacks names for some virtues shows Aristotle's awareness that ordinary moral vocabulary is incomplete and sometimes misleading. -- The ambiguity around 'ambition' (philotimia) -- sometimes praised, sometimes blamed -- illustrates how the mean shifts depending on context, a point that resists rigid codification.

D: Original: With regard to anger also there is an excess, a deficiency, and a mean. Although they can scarcely be said to have names, yet since we call the intermediate person good-tempered let us call the mean good temper; of the persons at the extremes let the one who exceeds be called irascible, and his vice irascibility, and the man who falls short an inirascible sort of person, and the deficiency inirascibility.
D: Refashioned: Regarding anger, there is also an excess, a deficiency, and a mean. Although these states can hardly be said to have proper names, since we call the intermediate person even-tempered, let us call the mean even-temperedness. Of the people at the extremes, let us call the one who is excessive in anger hot-tempered and the vice hot-temperedness, and the one who falls short unflappable, with the deficiency being unflappability.
D: Elegant: Anger too has a mean, excess, and deficiency. The mean is even-temperedness; the excess is hot-temperedness; the deficiency -- being too slow to anger -- we might call unflappability. These states barely have established names.
D: Summary: Aristotle identifies even-temperedness as the mean regarding anger, with hot-temperedness as the excess and an unnamed deficiency (excessive lack of anger). He notes these states barely have established names and proposes terminology.
D: Distilled: Even-temperedness is the mean regarding anger, lying between the excess of hot-temperedness and the deficiency of being too slow to anger.
Notes: Good temper (praotes, sometimes translated 'mildness' or 'gentleness') is discussed in detail in Book IV.5. -- Aristotle's recognition that deficiency in anger can be a vice is notable: being completely unangered by injustice is itself a failing. This counters any interpretation of Aristotle as recommending mere passivity. -- The persistent namelessness of virtuous and vicious states is a recurrent observation in this chapter, highlighting the gap between theory and ordinary language.

D: Original: There are also three other means, which have a certain likeness to one another, but differ from one another: for they are all concerned with intercourse in words and actions, but differ in that one is concerned with truth in this sphere, the other two with pleasantness; and of this one kind is exhibited in giving amusement, the other in all the circumstances of life. We must therefore speak of these too, that we may the better see that in all things the mean is praise-worthy, and the extremes neither praiseworthy nor right, but worthy of blame. Now most of these states also have no names, but we must try, as in the other cases, to invent names ourselves so that we may be clear and easy to follow. With regard to truth, then, the intermediate is a truthful sort of person and the mean may be called truthfulness, while the pretence which exaggerates is boastfulness and the person characterized by it a boaster, and that which understates is mock modesty and the person characterized by it mock-modest. With regard to pleasantness in the giving of amusement the intermediate person is ready-witted and the disposition ready wit, the excess is buffoonery and the person characterized by it a buffoon, while the man who falls short is a sort of boor and his state is boorishness. With regard to the remaining kind of pleasantness, that which is exhibited in life in general, the man who is pleasant in the right way is friendly and the mean is friendliness, while the man who exceeds is an obsequious person if he has no end in view, a flatterer if he is aiming at his own advantage, and the man who falls short and is unpleasant in all circumstances is a quarrelsome and surly sort of person.
D: Refashioned: There are also three other means that resemble each other in some ways but differ in others. All three concern social interaction in words and actions, but they differ in their specific domains: one deals with truth in social life, while the other two deal with pleasantness -- one in the context of amusement, the other in all the general circumstances of life. We must discuss these as well, so that we can see more clearly that the mean is praiseworthy in all things and the extremes are neither praiseworthy nor right, but blameworthy. Most of these states also lack established names, but we must try, as in the other cases, to coin names for clarity. Regarding truth, the intermediate person is truthful and the mean may be called truthfulness. The exaggeration is boastfulness (and its possessor a boaster), while the understatement is self-deprecation (and its possessor self-deprecating). Regarding pleasantness in amusement, the intermediate person is witty and the disposition is wit. The excess is buffoonery (and its possessor a buffoon), while the person who falls short is a kind of boor and the state is boorishness. Regarding the remaining kind of pleasantness -- that which shows itself in life generally -- the person who is pleasant in the right way is friendly and the mean is friendliness. The person who goes too far is obsequious if doing so without ulterior motive, or a flatterer if aiming at personal advantage. The person who falls short and is unpleasant in all situations is quarrelsome and surly.
D: Elegant: Three more means concern social life: truthfulness (between boastfulness and self-deprecation), wit (between buffoonery and boorishness), and friendliness (between obsequiousness or flattery and surliness). All involve conduct in words and actions -- one regarding truth, two regarding pleasantness (in amusement and in life generally). Most of these states lack established names, but the pattern holds: the mean is praiseworthy, the extremes blameworthy.
D: Summary: Aristotle identifies three social virtues as means: truthfulness (between boasting and self-deprecation), wit (between buffoonery and boorishness), and friendliness (between obsequiousness and surliness). All concern how we interact with others, whether regarding truth or pleasantness.
D: Distilled: Truthfulness, wit, and friendliness are three social virtues, each a mean between excess and deficiency in how we present ourselves and relate to others.
Notes: Truthfulness (aletheia in social contexts) is discussed in Book IV.7, wit (eutrapelia) in IV.8, and friendliness (philia in the dispositional sense, distinct from the deeper friendship of Books VIII-IX) in IV.6. -- The distinction between the obsequious person and the flatterer (who acts from self-interest) shows Aristotle's attention to motives, not just outward behavior. -- The term eutrapelia (wit/ready-wittedness) was later given a negative connotation by some Christian moralists (Ephesians 5:4), showing how Aristotelian virtues were sometimes reinterpreted in later traditions.

D: Original: There are also means in the passions and concerned with the passions; since shame is not a virtue, and yet praise is extended to the modest man. For even in these matters one man is said to be intermediate, and another to exceed, as for instance the bashful man who is ashamed of everything; while he who falls short or is not ashamed of anything at all is shameless, and the intermediate person is modest. Righteous indignation is a mean between envy and spite, and these states are concerned with the pain and pleasure that are felt at the fortunes of our neighbours; the man who is characterized by righteous indignation is pained at undeserved good fortune, the envious man, going beyond him, is pained at all good fortune, and the spiteful man falls so far short of being pained that he even rejoices. But these states there will be an opportunity of describing elsewhere; with regard to justice, since it has not one simple meaning, we shall, after describing the other states, distinguish its two kinds and say how each of them is a mean; and similarly we shall treat also of the rational virtues.
D: Refashioned: There are also means in the domain of emotions. While shame is not a virtue, praise is nonetheless extended to the modest person. Even here, one person is said to occupy the intermediate position while another goes to excess -- like the bashful person who feels shame about everything -- while the person who falls short, feeling no shame at all, is shameless; the intermediate person is modest. Righteous indignation is a mean between envy and spite. These states concern the pain and pleasure we feel at our neighbors' fortunes: the person characterized by righteous indignation is pained by undeserved good fortune; the envious person goes further, being pained by all good fortune; and the spiteful person falls so far short of feeling pain that they actually rejoice in others' misfortune. But there will be an occasion to describe these states elsewhere. As for justice, since it does not have a single simple meaning, we will distinguish its two kinds after discussing the other states and explain how each is a mean. We will treat the intellectual virtues in the same way.
D: Elegant: Some means concern passions rather than actions. Modesty is a mean regarding shame -- between the bashful person (ashamed of everything) and the shameless person (ashamed of nothing), though it is not strictly a virtue. Righteous indignation is a mean between envy and spite: the righteous person is pained by undeserved good fortune, the envious by all good fortune, and the spiteful person rejoices in others' misfortune. Justice and the intellectual virtues, having complex structures, will be treated separately.
D: Summary: Aristotle discusses means in the emotional domain: modesty (though not a full virtue) is the mean between bashfulness and shamelessness, and righteous indignation is the mean between envy and spite. He defers detailed treatment of justice and the intellectual virtues to later books.
D: Distilled: Modesty and righteous indignation are emotional means -- and justice and the intellectual virtues, being more complex, require separate treatment.
Notes: Shame (aidos) is treated in Book IV.9, where Aristotle explains why it is a quasi-virtue appropriate mainly for the young rather than a full virtue. -- Righteous indignation (nemesis) is discussed more fully in Rhetoric II.9. -- The two kinds of justice (distributive and corrective) are the subject of Book V, one of the longest and most technical books of the Ethics. -- The intellectual virtues (sophia, phronesis, nous, episteme, techne) are treated in Book VI. -- 'Spite' (epichairekakia) -- rejoicing in others' misfortune -- is the concept later captured by the German term Schadenfreude.

D: Original: There are three kinds of disposition, then, two of them vices, involving excess and deficiency respectively, and one a virtue, viz. the mean, and all are in a sense opposed to all; for the extreme states are contrary both to the intermediate state and to each other, and the intermediate to the extremes; as the equal is greater relatively to the less, less relatively to the greater, so the middle states are excessive relatively to the deficiencies, deficient relatively to the excesses, both in passions and in actions. For the brave man appears rash relatively to the coward, and cowardly relatively to the rash man; and similarly the temperate man appears self-indulgent relatively to the insensible man, insensible relatively to the self-indulgent, and the liberal man prodigal relatively to the mean man, mean relatively to the prodigal. Hence also the people at the extremes push the intermediate man each over to the other, and the brave man is called rash by the coward, cowardly by the rash man, and correspondingly in the other cases.
D: Refashioned: There are, then, three kinds of disposition: two of them are vices (one involving excess and the other deficiency), and one is the virtue, namely the mean. All three stand in a kind of opposition to each other. The extreme states are contrary both to the intermediate state and to each other, and the intermediate is contrary to both extremes. Just as what is equal appears greater compared to what is less and smaller compared to what is greater, so the middle states appear excessive relative to the deficiencies and deficient relative to the excesses, both in passions and in actions. The brave person appears rash compared to the coward and cowardly compared to the rash person. Similarly, the temperate person appears self-indulgent compared to the insensible person and insensible compared to the self-indulgent one, and the generous person appears extravagant compared to the stingy person and stingy compared to the extravagant one. This is why people at the extremes each push the person in the middle toward the other extreme: the coward calls the brave person rash, and the rash person calls the brave person a coward, and so on in the other cases.
D: Elegant: Three dispositions exist: two vices (excess and deficiency) and one virtue (the mean), all mutually opposed. The mean appears excessive to the deficient and deficient to the excessive, just as equality seems large beside the small and small beside the large. The brave person looks rash to the coward and cowardly to the rash. Each extreme pushes the person in the middle toward the opposite extreme.
D: Summary: Aristotle explains how the three dispositions -- excess, deficiency, and the mean -- relate to each other. The mean appears as an extreme from each side: the brave person looks reckless to the coward and cowardly to the reckless person. People at each extreme try to push the middle person toward the opposite pole.
D: Distilled: The virtuous mean appears excessive to one extreme and deficient to the other, which is why people at each extreme accuse the balanced person of the opposite fault.
Notes: This analysis of the relational nature of virtue explains common moral disagreements: opposing parties each see the moderate person as biased toward the other side. -- The observation has practical implications for moral education and self-assessment that Aristotle draws out in Book II.9. -- The logical structure here -- the intermediate being 'greater than the less and less than the greater' -- echoes the mathematical treatment of proportionality in Book V's discussion of justice.

D: Original: These states being thus opposed to one another, the greatest contrariety is that of the extremes to each other, rather than to the intermediate; for these are further from each other than from the intermediate, as the great is further from the small and the small from the great than both are from the equal. Again, to the intermediate some extremes show a certain likeness, as that of rashness to courage and that of prodigality to liberality; but the extremes show the greatest unlikeness to each other; now contraries are defined as the things that are furthest from each other, so that things that are further apart are more contrary.
D: Refashioned: Given that these states oppose one another in these ways, the greatest opposition exists between the two extremes rather than between either extreme and the intermediate. The extremes are further from each other than either is from the intermediate, just as the great is further from the small and the small from the great than either is from what is equal. Furthermore, some extremes bear a certain resemblance to the intermediate -- rashness to courage, for instance, or extravagance to generosity -- but the two extremes show the greatest dissimilarity to each other. Since contraries are defined as the things most distant from one another, the things that are further apart are more contrary.
D: Elegant: The greatest opposition lies between the two extremes, not between either extreme and the mean. The extremes are further from each other than from the mean, just as the very large and very small are further from each other than from the equal. Some extremes even resemble the mean -- rashness resembles courage, extravagance resembles generosity -- but the extremes are most unlike each other. Since contraries are what is most distant, the extremes are the truest contraries.
D: Summary: The two extremes of vice are more opposed to each other than either is to the virtuous mean. Some extremes even resemble the mean (e.g., rashness looks like courage), while the two extremes are maximally unlike each other.
D: Distilled: The two vicious extremes are more opposed to each other than either is to the virtue between them, and some extremes can even be mistaken for the mean.
Notes: The observation that some extremes resemble the mean has practical importance: it explains why vices are sometimes mistaken for virtues (e.g., rashness for courage, prodigality for generosity). -- The definition of contraries as 'things furthest from each other' draws on Aristotle's logical works, particularly Categories 6 and Metaphysics V.10. -- This analysis helps explain why moral disagreement is so persistent: genuine vice can masquerade as virtue when it resembles one side of the mean.

D: Original: To the mean in some cases the deficiency, in some the excess is more opposed; e.g. it is not rashness, which is an excess, but cowardice, which is a deficiency, that is more opposed to courage, and not insensibility, which is a deficiency, but self-indulgence, which is an excess, that is more opposed to temperance. This happens from two reasons, one being drawn from the thing itself; for because one extreme is nearer and liker to the intermediate, we oppose not this but rather its contrary to the intermediate. E.g. since rashness is thought liker and nearer to courage, and cowardice more unlike, we oppose rather the latter to courage; for things that are further from the intermediate are thought more contrary to it. This, then, is one cause, drawn from the thing itself; another is drawn from ourselves; for the things to which we ourselves more naturally tend seem more contrary to the intermediate. For instance, we ourselves tend more naturally to pleasures, and hence are more easily carried away towards self-indulgence than towards propriety. We describe as contrary to the mean, then, rather the directions in which we more often go to great lengths; and therefore self-indulgence, which is an excess, is the more contrary to temperance.
D: Refashioned: In some cases, the deficiency is more opposed to the mean; in others, the excess is. For example, it is not rashness (the excess) but cowardice (the deficiency) that is more opposed to courage. And it is not insensibility (the deficiency) but self-indulgence (the excess) that is more opposed to temperance. This happens for two reasons. The first comes from the nature of the thing itself: because one extreme is closer to and more similar to the intermediate, we tend to oppose the other, more distant extreme to the mean. For instance, since rashness is considered more similar to courage than cowardice is, we treat cowardice as the greater opposite of courage, because what is further from the intermediate seems more contrary to it. This is one reason, drawn from the thing itself. The second reason comes from ourselves: the things toward which we naturally tend more strongly seem more contrary to the intermediate. For instance, we naturally incline more toward pleasures, which makes us more easily drawn toward self-indulgence than toward restraint. We therefore describe as more contrary to the mean the direction in which we are more likely to go to excess. This is why self-indulgence, which is an excess, is more opposed to temperance than insensibility is.
D: Elegant: Sometimes deficiency opposes the mean more, sometimes excess does. Cowardice (deficiency) is more contrary to courage than rashness (excess), yet self-indulgence (excess) is more contrary to temperance than insensibility (deficiency). Two reasons explain this. First, from the thing itself: whichever extreme is closer to the mean seems less opposed to it, so we contrast the more distant one. Second, from ourselves: we naturally tend more toward certain extremes (e.g., toward pleasure and self-indulgence), making those seem more contrary to virtue.
D: Summary: Aristotle explains why one extreme is sometimes more opposed to the mean than the other. Two factors determine this: which extreme is objectively more unlike the mean, and which extreme we naturally tend toward more strongly. Cowardice is more opposed to courage (being more unlike it), while self-indulgence is more opposed to temperance (because we naturally incline toward pleasure).
D: Distilled: Which extreme more opposes a virtue depends on two things: which is objectively more unlike the mean, and which direction our natural inclinations pull us toward most strongly.
Notes: This passage provides practical diagnostic criteria for moral self-assessment: we should be especially vigilant against the extreme toward which we naturally incline. -- The two explanatory factors (objective similarity and subjective tendency) show the sophistication of Aristotle's moral psychology. -- The principle that natural inclination toward pleasure makes self-indulgence a greater threat than insensibility anticipates the practical advice in Book II.9 about counteracting our natural biases. -- This analysis influenced Thomas Aquinas's discussion of the 'greater sin' in the Summa Theologiae (I-II, Q.64, a.4).

D: Original: That moral virtue is a mean, then, and in what sense it is so, and that it is a mean between two vices, the one involving excess, the other deficiency, and that it is such because its character is to aim at what is intermediate in passions and in actions, has been sufficiently stated. Hence also it is no easy task to be good. For in everything it is no easy task to find the middle, e.g. to find the middle of a circle is not for every one but for him who knows; so, too, any one can get angry- that is easy- or give or spend money; but to do this to the right person, to the right extent, at the right time, with the right motive, and in the right way, that is not for every one, nor is it easy; wherefore goodness is both rare and laudable and noble.
D: Refashioned: That moral virtue is a mean, then, and in what sense it is so -- that it is a mean between two vices, one involving excess and the other deficiency, and that it has this character because it aims at what is intermediate in passions and actions -- has been sufficiently established. This is also why being good is no easy task. In every domain, finding the middle is difficult: finding the center of a circle, for instance, is not something just anyone can do -- it requires someone with knowledge. In the same way, anyone can get angry, or give or spend money -- that is easy. But doing these things to the right person, to the right degree, at the right time, for the right reason, and in the right way -- that is not something just anyone can do, and it is not easy. This is why goodness is both rare and praiseworthy and noble.
D: Elegant: Moral virtue is a mean between two vices -- one of excess, one of deficiency -- aiming at the intermediate in passions and actions. This is why goodness is difficult. Anyone can get angry or spend money. But doing so to the right person, to the right extent, at the right time, for the right reason, and in the right way -- that is rare, difficult, and noble, like finding the center of a circle.
D: Summary: Aristotle summarizes the doctrine of the mean and draws a practical conclusion: being good is genuinely difficult. While anyone can feel emotions or perform actions, doing so in the right way -- toward the right people, at the right time, for the right reasons -- is rare, which is exactly what makes virtue praiseworthy.
D: Distilled: Goodness is rare and noble because while anyone can act or feel, doing so in exactly the right way, at the right time, for the right reason, and to the right degree is extraordinarily difficult.
Notes: The famous passage about it being easy to get angry but hard to get angry rightly has become one of the most quoted lines from the Ethics. -- The circle analogy connects mathematical precision to ethical precision -- finding the exact center requires expertise, just as finding the moral mean requires practical wisdom (phronesis). -- The claim that virtue is 'rare and laudable and noble' connects to Aristotle's view that moral achievement deserves genuine admiration, not just approval. -- This passage transitions from the theoretical account of virtue to practical advice about how to pursue it.

D: Original:
Hence he who aims at the intermediate must first depart from what is the more contrary to it, as Calypso advises-

Hold the ship out beyond that surf and spray.
D: Refashioned: Therefore, anyone aiming at the intermediate must first move away from the extreme that is more contrary to it, following the advice that Calypso gives: 'Hold the ship out beyond that surf and spray.'
D: Elegant: To aim at the mean, first steer away from the more dangerous extreme -- as Calypso warned: 'Hold the ship out beyond that surf and spray.'
D: Summary: Aristotle's first practical rule for hitting the mean: steer away from whichever extreme is more opposed to the virtue you seek, just as a sailor keeps the ship clear of the most dangerous rocks.
D: Distilled: To find the mean, first distance yourself from the more dangerous extreme.
Notes: The quotation is actually from Homer's Odyssey (XII.108-110), spoken by Circe (not Calypso) to Odysseus, warning him to steer his ship away from Scylla and Charybdis. The misattribution to Calypso appears in most manuscripts and may reflect a scribal error or a lapse in Aristotle's memory. Some scholars suggest this was a common confusion in antiquity. -- The navigation metaphor fits naturally with Aristotle's recurring comparison of ethics to navigation (see 2.2, thought 4), both being practical arts requiring situational judgment.

D: Original: For of the extremes one is more erroneous, one less so; therefore, since to hit the mean is hard in the extreme, we must as a second best, as people say, take the least of the evils; and this will be done best in the way we describe. But we must consider the things towards which we ourselves also are easily carried away; for some of us tend to one thing, some to another; and this will be recognizable from the pleasure and the pain we feel. We must drag ourselves away to the contrary extreme; for we shall get into the intermediate state by drawing well away from error, as people do in straightening sticks that are bent.
D: Refashioned: Since one extreme is more erroneous than the other, and since hitting the mean is extremely difficult, we should adopt the second-best strategy, as people say, and choose the lesser of the evils. This is best accomplished in the way we have described. But we must also consider which errors we ourselves are most prone to -- for different people tend in different directions -- and we can recognize our tendencies from the pleasure and pain we feel. We must then drag ourselves toward the opposite extreme. By pulling well away from our natural error, we will arrive at the intermediate state, just as people do when straightening bent sticks.
D: Elegant: Since hitting the mean is extremely hard, take the second-best course: choose the lesser evil by steering from the worse extreme. But also examine your own tendencies -- recognizable by what gives you pleasure and pain -- and drag yourself toward the opposite direction. Like straightening a bent stick, pulling toward the contrary brings you to the middle.
D: Summary: Aristotle offers practical advice for achieving virtue: since perfection is difficult, choose the lesser evil by avoiding the worse extreme. Know your own tendencies (revealed by your pleasures and pains) and deliberately overcorrect in the opposite direction, like straightening a bent stick.
D: Distilled: Know your natural biases through your pleasures and pains, and deliberately overcorrect toward the opposite extreme to arrive at the balanced mean.
Notes: The metaphor of straightening bent sticks was proverbial in Greek culture and appears in Plato's Laws (VII.789e). -- The practical strategy of deliberate overcorrection has influenced therapeutic and behavioral approaches: to correct a deeply ingrained habit, one must initially overshoot in the opposite direction. -- The emphasis on self-knowledge ('know your own tendencies') connects to the Delphic injunction 'Know thyself' (gnothi seauton), which Aristotle takes in a practical rather than metaphysical direction. -- The 'second-best' (deuteros plous) strategy is a nautical metaphor meaning to row when you cannot sail -- making the best of imperfect conditions.

D: Original: Now in everything the pleasant or pleasure is most to be guarded against; for we do not judge it impartially. We ought, then, to feel towards pleasure as the elders of the people felt towards Helen, and in all circumstances repeat their saying; for if we dismiss pleasure thus we are less likely to go astray. It is by doing this, then, (to sum the matter up) that we shall best be able to hit the mean.
D: Refashioned: Above all, we must guard against the pleasant -- against pleasure itself -- because we do not judge it impartially. We ought to feel toward pleasure the way the elders of Troy felt toward Helen, and repeat their words in every situation. For if we dismiss pleasure in this way, we are less likely to go astray. By doing this, in summary, we will be best able to hit the mean.
D: Elegant: Above all, guard against pleasure, for we judge it with bias. We should adopt the attitude of the Trojan elders toward Helen and repeat their words of dismissal. By sending pleasure away, we are less likely to err and more likely to hit the mean.
D: Summary: Aristotle warns that pleasure is the greatest threat to hitting the mean because we cannot judge it impartially. He advises adopting the attitude of the Trojan elders who, despite Helen's beauty, urged her dismissal -- we should similarly dismiss pleasure before it leads us astray.
D: Distilled: Pleasure is the greatest obstacle to virtue because we cannot judge it impartially, so we must be especially vigilant against its pull.
Notes: The reference is to Homer's Iliad (III.156-160), where the elders of Troy, upon seeing Helen on the city walls, acknowledge her extraordinary beauty but declare that she must be sent away because she will bring ruin. -- This is a striking use of Homer for ethical instruction, typical of how Greek philosophers treated the epic tradition as a source of practical wisdom. -- The warning against pleasure here should be read alongside Aristotle's more nuanced treatment in Books VII and X, where he distinguishes kinds of pleasure and argues that the pleasure accompanying virtuous activity is actually good. The warning here is specifically about the danger of being biased by sensory pleasure in moral judgment.

D: Original: But this is no doubt difficult, and especially in individual cases; for or is not easy to determine both how and with whom and on what provocation and how long one should be angry; for we too sometimes praise those who fall short and call them good-tempered, but sometimes we praise those who get angry and call them manly. The man, however, who deviates little from goodness is not blamed, whether he do so in the direction of the more or of the less, but only the man who deviates more widely; for he does not fail to be noticed. But up to what point and to what extent a man must deviate before he becomes blameworthy it is not easy to determine by reasoning, any more than anything else that is perceived by the senses; such things depend on particular facts, and the decision rests with perception. So much, then, is plain, that the intermediate state is in all things to be praised, but that we must incline sometimes towards the excess, sometimes towards the deficiency; for so shall we most easily hit the mean and what is right.
D: Refashioned: But this is undoubtedly difficult, especially in particular cases. It is not easy to determine precisely how, with whom, on what grounds, and for how long one should be angry. We ourselves sometimes praise people who fall short in anger, calling them even-tempered, and at other times praise those who get angry, calling them spirited. The person who deviates only slightly from what is good is not blamed, whether the deviation is toward excess or deficiency -- only the person who deviates significantly, since such a deviation is hard to miss. But exactly how far and in what way a person must deviate before becoming blameworthy is not easy to determine by reasoning, any more than any other matter of perception. Such judgments depend on particular circumstances, and the final decision rests with perception. This much, then, is clear: the intermediate state deserves praise in all things, but we must sometimes lean toward excess and sometimes toward deficiency. In this way, we will most easily hit the mean and what is right.
D: Elegant: Hitting the mean in particular cases is genuinely difficult. We cannot precisely determine how, with whom, or how long to be angry -- we even praise opposite reactions depending on context. Small deviations from goodness go unblamed; only large ones are noticed. But the exact boundary of blame cannot be fixed by reasoning -- it depends on particular circumstances and rests ultimately on perception. The intermediate state is always praiseworthy, yet we must sometimes lean toward excess, sometimes toward deficiency, to best hit the mean and what is right.
D: Summary: Aristotle concludes by acknowledging that applying the doctrine of the mean in specific situations is genuinely difficult. Precise rules cannot be formulated -- the judgment depends on perception of particular circumstances. While the mean is always praiseworthy, practical wisdom sometimes requires leaning toward one extreme or the other to find the right balance.
D: Distilled: The right action in particular circumstances cannot be determined by fixed rules but depends on perception and practical judgment, making virtue a matter of wisdom, not formula.
Notes: The appeal to perception (aisthesis) as the final arbiter of particular moral judgments is a signature Aristotelian position. It reflects his view that practical wisdom (phronesis) is a perceptual capacity for discerning the morally relevant features of particular situations -- not reducible to rules or algorithms. -- This contrasts sharply with later rule-based ethical systems (Kantian deontology, utilitarian calculus). -- The closing remark that 'we must incline sometimes towards the excess, sometimes towards the deficiency' is not a counsel of inconsistency but recognizes that the mean shifts with circumstances and that self-aware adjustment is essential. -- This passage concludes Book II and sets up the detailed treatment of particular virtues in Books III-V and the theory of practical wisdom in Book VI.

D: Original: Since virtue is concerned with passions and actions, and on voluntary passions and actions praise and blame are bestowed, on those that are involuntary pardon, and sometimes also pity, to distinguish the voluntary and the involuntary is presumably necessary for those who are studying the nature of virtue, and useful also for legislators with a view to the assigning both of honours and of punishments. Those things, then, are thought-involuntary, which take place under compulsion or owing to ignorance; and that is compulsory of which the moving principle is outside, being a principle in which nothing is contributed by the person who is acting or is feeling the passion, e.g. if he were to be carried somewhere by a wind, or by men who had him in their power.
D: Refashioned: Since virtue deals with passions and actions, and since we praise and blame voluntary passions and actions while pardoning (and sometimes pitying) involuntary ones, distinguishing the voluntary from the involuntary is presumably necessary for students of virtue. It is also useful for legislators when assigning honors and punishments. Actions are considered involuntary when they occur under compulsion or through ignorance. An act is compulsory when its moving principle is external -- when the person acting or experiencing the passion contributes nothing to it. For example, if someone were carried somewhere by a wind, or by captors who had him in their power.
D: Elegant: Virtue concerns passions and actions; the voluntary earns praise or blame, the involuntary earns pardon or pity. Distinguishing these is essential for understanding virtue and for legislation. An act is involuntary when done under compulsion or through ignorance. Compulsion means the moving principle is entirely external -- the agent contributes nothing, as when carried off by wind or captors.
D: Summary: Aristotle begins his analysis of voluntary and involuntary action, arguing that this distinction is essential for understanding virtue and for assigning praise, blame, and legal responsibility. An act is involuntary when its cause is entirely external to the agent or when it arises from ignorance.
D: Distilled: The distinction between voluntary and involuntary action is foundational to virtue, since moral responsibility requires that the agent -- not external forces or ignorance -- is the source of action.
Notes: This opens Book III's treatment of moral responsibility, building on the account of virtue as a mean in Book II. -- The distinction between voluntary (hekousion) and involuntary (akousion) action is central to Aristotle's moral psychology and has profoundly influenced Western legal and ethical theory. -- The criterion of the 'moving principle' (arche) being internal or external to the agent becomes Aristotle's key test for voluntariness. -- This framework influenced later discussions of free will, though Aristotle himself does not use that concept.

D: Original: But with regard to the things that are done from fear of greater evils or for some noble object (e.g. if a tyrant were to order one to do something base, having one's parents and children in his power, and if one did the action they were to be saved, but otherwise would be put to death), it may be debated whether such actions are involuntary or voluntary. Something of the sort happens also with regard to the throwing of goods overboard in a storm; for in the abstract no one throws goods away voluntarily, but on condition of its securing the safety of himself and his crew any sensible man does so. Such actions, then, are mixed, but are more like voluntary actions; for they are worthy of choice at the time when they are done, and the end of an action is relative to the occasion. Both the terms, then, 'voluntary' and 'involuntary', must be used with reference to the moment of action. Now the man acts voluntarily; for the principle that moves the instrumental parts of the body in such actions is in him, and the things of which the moving principle is in a man himself are in his power to do or not to do. Such actions, therefore, are voluntary, but in the abstract perhaps involuntary; for no one would choose any such act in itself.
D: Refashioned: But regarding actions done from fear of greater evils or for some noble purpose -- for example, if a tyrant ordered someone to do something shameful while holding his parents and children hostage, where compliance would save them but refusal would mean their death -- it is debatable whether such actions are involuntary or voluntary. Something similar occurs when cargo is thrown overboard in a storm: considered in the abstract, no one voluntarily throws goods away, but to secure the safety of himself and his crew, any sensible person does so. Such actions are therefore mixed, but more closely resemble voluntary actions, since they are chosen at the time they are performed, and the purpose of an action is relative to the occasion. Both terms -- 'voluntary' and 'involuntary' -- must be applied with reference to the moment of action. In these cases, the person acts voluntarily, because the principle that moves the body's instrumental parts is within him, and whatever has its moving principle within the agent is within his power to do or not do. Such actions are therefore voluntary, though in the abstract they might be called involuntary -- no one would choose any such act for its own sake.
D: Elegant: Actions done from fear of greater evils or for a noble purpose -- such as obeying a tyrant to save one's family, or throwing cargo overboard in a storm -- are mixed but more voluntary than not. No one would choose them in the abstract, yet at the moment of action they are chosen, and the moving principle is within the agent. The terms 'voluntary' and 'involuntary' must be applied relative to the occasion of action, not in the abstract.
D: Summary: Actions performed under duress or to avoid greater evils are 'mixed' but lean toward being voluntary, since the agent still initiates the action in the moment. The key test is whether the moving principle is in the agent at the time of acting, not whether one would choose such actions in ideal circumstances.
D: Distilled: Actions done under duress are mixed but ultimately voluntary, because at the moment of choice the moving principle remains within the agent, even though no one would choose such acts in the abstract.
Notes: The cargo-overboard example became a classic case in discussions of duress and moral responsibility. -- The concept of 'mixed actions' (miktai praxeis) is Aristotle's own innovation. -- This analysis later influenced legal doctrines of necessity and duress. -- The principle that voluntariness must be judged relative to particular circumstances, not abstractly, is central to Aristotle's practical philosophy and connects to his emphasis on particulars in phronesis (practical wisdom) discussed in Book VI.

D: Original: For such actions men are sometimes even praised, when they endure something base or painful in return for great and noble objects gained; in the opposite case they are blamed, since to endure the greatest indignities for no noble end or for a trifling end is the mark of an inferior person. On some actions praise indeed is not bestowed, but pardon is, when one does what he ought not under pressure which overstrains human nature and which no one could withstand. But some acts, perhaps, we cannot be forced to do, but ought rather to face death after the most fearful sufferings; for the things that 'forced' Euripides Alcmaeon to slay his mother seem absurd. It is difficult sometimes to determine what should be chosen at what cost, and what should be endured in return for what gain, and yet more difficult to abide by our decisions; for as a rule what is expected is painful, and what we are forced to do is base, whence praise and blame are bestowed on those who have been compelled or have not.
D: Refashioned: For such actions people are sometimes even praised, when they endure something shameful or painful in return for great and noble results. In the opposite case they are blamed, since enduring the greatest indignities for no noble purpose, or for a trivial one, is the mark of an inferior person. For some actions, praise is not given, but pardon is -- when someone does what he ought not under pressure that exceeds human endurance and that no one could withstand. Yet some acts perhaps cannot be forced upon us; rather, we should face death after the most terrible sufferings. The things that supposedly 'forced' Euripides' Alcmaeon to kill his mother seem absurd. It is difficult sometimes to determine what should be chosen at what cost, and what should be endured in return for what gain -- and harder still to stand by our decisions. For as a rule, what is anticipated is painful, and what we are forced to do is shameful; hence praise and blame are assigned to those who have or have not yielded to compulsion.
D: Elegant: People are praised for enduring shame or pain for noble ends, and blamed for enduring indignities for trivial ones. Pardon is granted when pressure exceeds human endurance -- yet some acts cannot truly be forced upon us, and we should sooner die. The claim that Alcmaeon was 'forced' to kill his mother is absurd. It is hard to determine what to choose at what cost, and harder still to abide by such decisions.
D: Summary: The moral evaluation of actions under duress depends on what is at stake: enduring hardship for noble ends earns praise, while yielding to pressure for trivial reasons earns blame. Some acts are so terrible that no compulsion can excuse them -- one should choose death instead.
D: Distilled: Not all compulsion excuses wrongdoing: some acts are so base that one ought to face death rather than perform them, and enduring hardship is praiseworthy only when the end is noble.
Notes: Alcmaeon is a figure from Greek tragedy; in Euripides' lost play, Alcmaeon killed his mother Eriphyle because she had been bribed to send his father Amphiaraus to his death at Thebes. Aristotle finds the claim of compulsion unconvincing. -- The reference to Euripides (c. 480-406 BC) is to a now-lost play. -- This passage establishes important limits on the duress defense: there are moral absolutes that no pressure can override. -- The difficulty of standing by hard decisions anticipates the discussion of akrasia (weakness of will) in Book VII.

D: Original: What sort of acts, then, should be called compulsory? We answer that without qualification actions are so when the cause is in the external circumstances and the agent contributes nothing. But the things that in themselves are involuntary, but now and in return for these gains are worthy of choice, and whose moving principle is in the agent, are in themselves involuntary, but now and in return for these gains voluntary. They are more like voluntary acts; for actions are in the class of particulars, and the particular acts here are voluntary. What sort of things are to be chosen, and in return for what, it is not easy to state; for there are many differences in the particular cases.
D: Refashioned: What sort of acts, then, should be called compulsory? Without qualification, actions are compulsory when the cause lies in external circumstances and the agent contributes nothing. But actions that are involuntary in themselves yet are chosen now and in return for particular gains -- and whose moving principle is in the agent -- are involuntary in themselves but voluntary here and now, given these circumstances. They more closely resemble voluntary acts, since actions belong to the realm of particulars, and these particular acts are voluntary. What sorts of things should be chosen, and at what cost, is not easy to state, for there are many differences among particular cases.
D: Elegant: Actions are compulsory without qualification only when the cause is entirely external. Acts that are involuntary in the abstract but chosen in specific circumstances, with the moving principle in the agent, are more like voluntary actions. Since actions belong to the realm of particulars, these particular acts are voluntary -- though what to choose at what cost admits no easy general answer.
D: Summary: True compulsion requires a wholly external cause with no contribution from the agent. Actions involuntary in the abstract but chosen under specific circumstances count as voluntary in practice, because all action is particular, and in the particular moment the agent is the moving principle.
D: Distilled: Since all action occurs in particulars, acts chosen under difficult circumstances are ultimately voluntary when the agent remains the source of movement, even if no one would choose them abstractly.
Notes: This reinforces Aristotle's methodological commitment to the priority of particular judgments over universal rules in ethics. -- The distinction between what is voluntary 'without qualification' (haplos) and 'in a particular case' (tis) is characteristic of Aristotle's philosophical method. -- Connects to the discussion of practical wisdom (phronesis) in Book VI, which deals precisely with judging particulars.

D: Original: But if some one were to say that pleasant and noble objects have a compelling power, forcing us from without, all acts would be for him compulsory; for it is for these objects that all men do everything they do. And those who act under compulsion and unwillingly act with pain, but those who do acts for their pleasantness and nobility do them with pleasure; it is absurd to make external circumstances responsible, and not oneself, as being easily caught by such attractions, and to make oneself responsible for noble acts but the pleasant objects responsible for base acts. The compulsory, then, seems to be that whose moving principle is outside, the person compelled contributing nothing.
D: Refashioned: But if someone were to claim that pleasant and noble things have a compelling power that forces us from without, then all actions would be compulsory for him -- since it is for the sake of pleasure and nobility that everyone does everything they do. Furthermore, those who act under genuine compulsion and unwillingly act with pain, while those who act for pleasure or nobility act with pleasure. It is absurd to blame external circumstances rather than oneself for being easily captured by such attractions, and to take credit for noble acts while blaming pleasant objects for base ones. The compulsory, then, is that whose moving principle is outside the agent, with the person compelled contributing nothing.
D: Elegant: If pleasant and noble things were truly compulsory forces, then all action would be compulsory -- since everyone acts for pleasure or nobility. But those genuinely compelled act with pain, while those drawn by pleasure act willingly. It is absurd to blame external attractions for base acts while taking credit for noble ones. True compulsion requires an external moving principle to which the agent contributes nothing.
D: Summary: Aristotle rejects the claim that pleasure or nobility can constitute compulsion. If they did, all actions would be compulsory, which is absurd. The test remains whether the moving principle is external and the agent contributes nothing.
D: Distilled: Pleasant and noble things do not compel us from without; claiming they do would make all action compulsory and would absurdly excuse us for base acts while crediting us for noble ones.
Notes: This argument targets a potential loophole in the compulsion defense -- the claim that overwhelming desire constitutes external force. -- Aristotle's refusal to treat desire as compulsion has important implications for his rejection of Socratic intellectualism (the view that no one does wrong voluntarily) discussed further in Book VII. -- The argument anticipates modern debates about whether addiction or overwhelming desire can serve as an excuse.

D: Original: Everything that is done by reason of ignorance is not voluntary; it is only what produces pain and repentance that is involuntary. For the man who has done something owing to ignorance, and feels not the least vexation at his action, has not acted voluntarily, since he did not know what he was doing, nor yet involuntarily, since he is not pained. Of people, then, who act by reason of ignorance he who repents is thought an involuntary agent, and the man who does not repent may, since he is different, be called a not voluntary agent; for, since he differs from the other, it is better that he should have a name of his own.
D: Refashioned: Not everything done through ignorance is involuntary; only what produces pain and regret qualifies as involuntary. A person who has done something through ignorance but feels no distress about his action has not acted voluntarily -- since he did not know what he was doing -- but neither has he acted involuntarily, since he feels no pain about it. Among those who act through ignorance, then, the one who feels regret is considered an involuntary agent, while the one who does not feel regret may be called a 'not-voluntary' agent, since he differs from the other. Because the cases are distinct, it is better to give each its own name.
D: Elegant: Not all action from ignorance is involuntary -- only that which produces pain and regret. Someone who acts in ignorance but feels no distress afterward acted neither voluntarily nor involuntarily; he is a 'not-voluntary' agent. Only the one who regrets qualifies as having acted involuntarily. The distinction warrants separate terminology.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes between 'involuntary' and 'not-voluntary' actions done through ignorance. An act done in ignorance is truly involuntary only if the agent feels regret afterward. If there is no regret, the action is 'not-voluntary' -- a distinct category requiring its own name.
D: Distilled: An action done through ignorance is involuntary only when accompanied by regret; without regret, it is merely 'not-voluntary' -- a distinct moral category.
Notes: The distinction between 'involuntary' (akousion) and 'not-voluntary' (ouch hekousion) is a characteristically Aristotelian fine-grained conceptual distinction. -- The role of regret as a criterion for involuntariness connects to the importance of emotional response in moral evaluation throughout the Ethics. -- This distinction has been influential in philosophy of action and criminal law theory.

D: Original:
Acting by reason of ignorance seems also to be different from acting in ignorance; for the man who is drunk or in a rage is thought to act as a result not of ignorance but of one of the causes mentioned, yet not knowingly but in ignorance.

Now every wicked man is ignorant of what he ought to do and what he ought to abstain from, and it is by reason of error of this kind that men become unjust and in general bad; but the term 'involuntary' tends to be used not if a man is ignorant of what is to his advantage- for it is not mistaken purpose that causes involuntary action (it leads rather to wickedness), nor ignorance of the universal (for that men are blamed), but ignorance of particulars, i.e. of the circumstances of the action and the objects with which it is concerned. For it is on these that both pity and pardon depend, since the person who is ignorant of any of these acts involuntarily.
D: Refashioned:
Acting 'through ignorance' also seems different from acting 'in ignorance.' A person who is drunk or in a rage is thought to act not through ignorance but through one of those conditions -- yet not knowingly, but in ignorance.

Now every wicked person is ignorant of what he ought to do and what he ought to avoid, and it is precisely this kind of error that makes people unjust and generally bad. But the term 'involuntary' is not meant to apply when someone is ignorant of what is to his advantage -- for mistaken judgment about one's interest does not make action involuntary (it leads rather to wickedness), nor does ignorance of universals (for that we blame people). Rather, it is ignorance of the particular circumstances -- the details of the action and its objects -- that makes an action involuntary. It is on these particulars that both pity and pardon depend, since a person ignorant of any of these acts involuntarily.
D: Elegant: Acting 'through ignorance' differs from acting 'in ignorance': the drunk or enraged person acts in ignorance but not through it. Every wicked person is ignorant of what he ought to do, but this moral ignorance does not make actions involuntary -- it makes them blameworthy. Only ignorance of particular circumstances -- who, what, how, and to whom -- renders action involuntary and grounds pity and pardon.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes ignorance as the cause of action from ignorance as a mere accompanying condition. Moral ignorance (not knowing what one ought to do) does not excuse -- it is the basis of blame. Only ignorance of particular circumstances (who is involved, what is being done, what instrument is used) makes an action genuinely involuntary.
D: Distilled: Only ignorance of particular circumstances makes an action involuntary; ignorance of moral principles -- not knowing what one ought to do -- is itself blameworthy and does not excuse.
Notes: The distinction between acting 'through ignorance' (di' agnoian) and 'in ignorance' (agnoōn) is philosophically significant: the drunk acts in ignorance but drunkenness, not ignorance, is the cause. -- The claim that ignorance of universals (moral principles) does not excuse while ignorance of particulars does has been highly influential in legal theory, distinguishing mistakes of law from mistakes of fact. -- This connects to the Socratic paradox that no one does wrong willingly -- Aristotle is refining and partly rejecting this position.

D: Original: Perhaps it is just as well, therefore, to determine their nature and number. A man may be ignorant, then, of who he is, what he is doing, what or whom he is acting on, and sometimes also what (e.g. what instrument) he is doing it with, and to what end (e.g. he may think his act will conduce to some one's safety), and how he is doing it (e.g. whether gently or violently). Now of all of these no one could be ignorant unless he were mad, and evidently also he could not be ignorant of the agent; for how could he not know himself? But of what he is doing a man might be ignorant, as for instance people say 'it slipped out of their mouths as they were speaking', or 'they did not know it was a secret', as Aeschylus said of the mysteries, or a man might say he 'let it go off when he merely wanted to show its working', as the man did with the catapult. Again, one might think one's son was an enemy, as Merope did, or that a pointed spear had a button on it, or that a stone was pumicestone; or one might give a man a draught to save him, and really kill him; or one might want to touch a man, as people do in sparring, and really wound him. The ignorance may relate, then, to any of these things, i.e. of the circumstances of the action, and the man who was ignorant of any of these is thought to have acted involuntarily, and especially if he was ignorant on the most important points; and these are thought to be the circumstances of the action and its end. Further, the doing of an act that is called involuntary in virtue of ignorance of this sort must be painful and involve repentance.
D: Refashioned: It is worth determining the nature and number of these particular circumstances. A person may be ignorant of who he is, what he is doing, what or whom he is acting upon, sometimes what instrument he is using, what end he aims at (perhaps thinking the act will save someone), and how he is performing the action (gently or violently). No one could be ignorant of all these unless he were insane, and obviously he could not be ignorant of the agent -- how could someone not know himself? But a person might be ignorant of what he is doing: people say things 'slipped out' as they were speaking, or they did not know something was secret, as Aeschylus said of the Mysteries. Someone might say the weapon 'went off when he merely wanted to demonstrate how it worked,' like the man with the catapult. One might mistake one's son for an enemy, as Merope did; or think a pointed spear had a protective tip; or think a stone was pumice-stone. One might administer a drink to save someone and actually kill him, or intend merely to touch someone, as in sparring, and actually wound him. Ignorance can relate to any of these circumstances, and someone ignorant of any of them is considered to have acted involuntarily -- especially if ignorant of the most important ones, which are thought to be the circumstances of the action and its end. Furthermore, an act called involuntary on account of this kind of ignorance must be painful to the agent and involve regret.
D: Elegant: The particular circumstances one may be ignorant of are: who one is, what one is doing, upon what or whom, with what instrument, to what end, and in what manner. No sane person is ignorant of all, and no one is ignorant of who he himself is. But one might not know what one is doing, or mistake a son for an enemy, or think a real weapon was harmless. Ignorance of any particular makes an action involuntary, especially ignorance of what one is doing and why -- and genuine involuntariness requires that the agent feels pain and regret.
D: Summary: Aristotle enumerates the particular circumstances about which one can be ignorant: the agent, the act, the object, the instrument, the end, and the manner. Ignorance of any of these -- especially the nature of the act and its purpose -- renders an action involuntary, provided the agent feels regret upon discovering the truth.
D: Distilled: Involuntary action through ignorance concerns specific particulars -- who, what, to whom, with what, why, and how -- and requires that the agent, upon learning the truth, feels genuine regret.
Notes: Aeschylus (c. 525-456 BC), the great tragedian, was reportedly charged with revealing secrets of the Eleusinian Mysteries in his plays and defended himself by claiming ignorance. -- Merope is a figure from Greek tragedy (in Euripides' lost Cresphontes) who nearly killed her own son, mistaking him for an enemy. -- The catapult example illustrates accidental discharge of a weapon. -- These examples constitute an early systematic analysis of what modern law calls mens rea (guilty mind) and actus reus (guilty act). -- The enumeration of particular circumstances anticipates the medieval scholastic analysis of the 'circumstances of action' (quis, quid, ubi, quibus auxiliis, cur, quomodo, quando).

D: Original: Since that which is done under compulsion or by reason of ignorance is involuntary, the voluntary would seem to be that of which the moving principle is in the agent himself, he being aware of the particular circumstances of the action. Presumably acts done by reason of anger or appetite are not rightly called involuntary. For in the first place, on that showing none of the other animals will act voluntarily, nor will children; and secondly, is it meant that we do not do voluntarily any of the acts that are due to appetite or anger, or that we do the noble acts voluntarily and the base acts involuntarily? Is not this absurd, when one and the same thing is the cause? But it would surely be odd to describe as involuntary the things one ought to desire; and we ought both to be angry at certain things and to have an appetite for certain things, e.g. for health and for learning. Also what is involuntary is thought to be painful, but what is in accordance with appetite is thought to be pleasant. Again, what is the difference in respect of involuntariness between errors committed upon calculation and those committed in anger? Both are to be avoided, but the irrational passions are thought not less human than reason is, and therefore also the actions which proceed from anger or appetite are the man's actions. It would be odd, then, to treat them as involuntary.
D: Refashioned: Since what is done under compulsion or through ignorance is involuntary, the voluntary would seem to be that whose moving principle is in the agent himself, when he is aware of the particular circumstances of the action. For it is presumably wrong to call acts done from anger or appetite involuntary. First, on that account none of the other animals or children would act voluntarily. Second, do we mean that none of our acts from appetite or anger are voluntary? Or that we do noble acts voluntarily and base acts involuntarily? This would be absurd, since one and the same cause -- appetite or anger -- drives both. And it would surely be strange to call involuntary the things one ought to desire: we ought to feel anger at certain things and to have appetites for certain things, such as health and learning. Moreover, what is involuntary is thought to be painful, while what accords with appetite is pleasant. Again, what difference in involuntariness is there between errors of calculation and those of anger? Both are to be avoided, yet irrational passions are thought to be no less human than reason, and therefore actions proceeding from anger or appetite are the person's own actions. It would be odd, then, to classify them as involuntary.
D: Elegant: The voluntary is that whose moving principle is in the agent, who is aware of the particular circumstances. Acts from anger or appetite should not be called involuntary: otherwise neither animals nor children would act voluntarily. It is absurd to call noble acts voluntary but base acts involuntary when the same cause produces both. We ought to desire certain things; the involuntary is painful while appetite brings pleasure; and irrational passions are no less human than reason. Actions from anger and appetite are the agent's own.
D: Summary: Aristotle defines voluntary action as that which originates in the agent who knows the particular circumstances, and argues against classifying actions from anger or appetite as involuntary. Since these passions are natural, shared with animals and children, and no less human than reason, the actions they produce are genuinely the agent's own.
D: Distilled: Voluntary action originates in an agent aware of the circumstances, and actions from anger or appetite are voluntary because irrational passions are no less a part of human nature than reason itself.
Notes: This passage concludes the analysis of voluntariness in Chapter 1 and provides Aristotle's positive definition. -- The argument against Plato's tendency to externalize passion (treating it as something alien that overwhelms the rational self) is implicit here. -- The claim that irrational passions are 'no less human than reason' connects to the account of the soul's parts in Book I (1102a-b) and the definition of moral virtue as concerned with passions in Book II. -- This analysis anticipates and partly answers the discussion of akrasia (weakness of will) in Book VII.

D: Original:
Both the voluntary and the involuntary having been delimited, we must next discuss choice; for it is thought to be most closely bound up with virtue and to discriminate characters better than actions do.

Choice, then, seems to be voluntary, but not the same thing as the voluntary; the latter extends more widely. For both children and the lower animals share in voluntary action, but not in choice, and acts done on the spur of the moment we describe as voluntary, but not as chosen.
D: Refashioned:
Having distinguished the voluntary from the involuntary, we must next discuss deliberate choice, since it is thought to be most closely connected with virtue and to reveal character better than actions do.

Deliberate choice seems to be voluntary, but it is not the same thing as the voluntary -- the voluntary is a broader category. Both children and animals share in voluntary action, but not in deliberate choice. And we call actions done on the spur of the moment voluntary, but not deliberately chosen.
D: Elegant: With the voluntary and involuntary distinguished, we turn to deliberate choice, which is most closely bound to virtue and reveals character better than actions alone. Choice is voluntary but not identical with the voluntary: the voluntary is broader. Children and animals act voluntarily but do not make deliberate choices, and spontaneous acts are voluntary but not chosen.
D: Summary: Aristotle introduces deliberate choice (prohairesis) as a narrower concept than voluntary action and argues it is the best indicator of moral character. Children and animals can act voluntarily but cannot make deliberate choices, and spontaneous actions are voluntary but not chosen.
D: Distilled: Deliberate choice is the truest measure of character -- narrower than voluntary action, excluding both the spontaneous acts of the moment and the behavior of children and animals.
Notes: This begins Chapter 2's treatment of prohairesis (deliberate choice), one of Aristotle's most important contributions to moral psychology. -- Prohairesis is a technical term Aristotle effectively coined for philosophical purposes; it literally means 'choosing before' or 'preferring.' -- The claim that choice reveals character better than actions connects to the role of intention in moral evaluation. -- This concept became central in Stoic ethics and in Epictetus especially.

D: Original: Those who say it is appetite or anger or wish or a kind of opinion do not seem to be right. For choice is not common to irrational creatures as well, but appetite and anger are. Again, the incontinent man acts with appetite, but not with choice; while the continent man on the contrary acts with choice, but not with appetite. Again, appetite is contrary to choice, but not appetite to appetite. Again, appetite relates to the pleasant and the painful, choice neither to the painful nor to the pleasant.
D: Refashioned: Those who identify deliberate choice with appetite, anger, wish, or a kind of opinion seem to be wrong. For choice is not shared with irrational creatures, but appetite and anger are. Again, the person lacking self-control acts from appetite but not from choice, while the self-controlled person acts from choice but not from appetite. Again, appetite can conflict with choice, but appetite does not conflict with appetite. Again, appetite concerns the pleasant and the painful, while choice concerns neither the pleasant nor the painful as such.
D: Elegant: Deliberate choice is not appetite, anger, wish, or opinion. Unlike appetite and anger, choice belongs only to rational beings. The person lacking self-control acts from appetite without choice; the self-controlled person chooses against appetite. Appetite can oppose choice, yet appetite does not oppose itself. And appetite concerns pleasure and pain, while choice does not.
D: Summary: Aristotle systematically eliminates candidates for what deliberate choice might be. It is not appetite (since the self-controlled choose against appetite), not anger, not wish, and not opinion. Choice is distinctive to rational beings and can stand in opposition to desire.
D: Distilled: Deliberate choice is distinct from appetite, anger, wish, and opinion -- uniquely rational, it can oppose desire and belongs only to beings capable of reasoned decision.
Notes: The contrast between the akratic (lacking self-control) and enkratic (self-controlled) person is central to Aristotle's moral psychology and receives extended treatment in Book VII. -- The argument that appetite can conflict with choice but not with itself establishes choice as belonging to a different psychological faculty than appetite. -- This analysis targets various predecessors: appetite-based accounts may reflect some Sophistic views; the opinion account may target Plato's Protagoras.

D: Original:
Still less is it anger; for acts due to anger are thought to be less than any others objects of choice.

But neither is it wish, though it seems near to it; for choice cannot relate to impossibles, and if any one said he chose them he would be thought silly; but there may be a wish even for impossibles, e.g. for immortality. And wish may relate to things that could in no way be brought about by one's own efforts, e.g. that a particular actor or athlete should win in a competition; but no one chooses such things, but only the things that he thinks could be brought about by his own efforts. Again, wish relates rather to the end, choice to the means; for instance, we wish to be healthy, but we choose the acts which will make us healthy, and we wish to be happy and say we do, but we cannot well say we choose to be so; for, in general, choice seems to relate to the things that are in our own power.
D: Refashioned:
Still less can choice be anger, since acts of anger are thought to be the least deliberately chosen of all.

But neither is choice the same as wish, though it seems close.
