For choice cannot relate to impossibilities -- anyone who said he 'chose' immortality would be thought foolish -- but there can be wishes for impossibilities, such as immortality. Wish can also relate to things that could never be brought about by one's own efforts -- for example, that a particular actor or athlete should win a competition. But no one chooses such things; we choose only what we think can be brought about by our own efforts. Again, wish relates more to the end, while choice relates to the means: for instance, we wish to be healthy, but we choose the actions that will make us healthy; we wish to be happy and say so, but we cannot properly say we 'choose' to be happy. In general, choice seems to concern things that are within our own power.
D: Elegant: Choice is not anger -- angry acts are the least chosen. Nor is it wish, despite their resemblance. We can wish for impossibilities like immortality, but cannot choose them. Wish extends to outcomes beyond our control; choice is limited to what our own efforts can achieve. Wish relates to the end -- we wish for health and happiness -- while choice relates to the means. Choice concerns what is in our power.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes deliberate choice from both anger and wish. Unlike wish, choice cannot target impossibilities or things outside our control. The crucial difference is that wish relates to ends while choice relates to means: we wish to be healthy but choose the actions that produce health.
D: Distilled: Choice differs from wish in being limited to achievable means within our power, while wish can extend to ends, impossibilities, and outcomes beyond our control.
Notes: The distinction between wish (boulesis) for ends and choice (prohairesis) of means is fundamental to Aristotle's practical philosophy. -- This framework was adopted and modified by Thomas Aquinas in his moral theory (Summa Theologiae I-II, qq. 8-16). -- The claim that we choose means but wish for ends will be qualified slightly: we choose 'things toward the end' (ta pros to telos), which is broader than strictly instrumental means. -- The examples of wishing for a particular competitor to win anticipate modern discussions of the distinction between intention and desire.

D: Original:
For this reason, too, it cannot be opinion; for opinion is thought to relate to all kinds of things, no less to eternal things and impossible things than to things in our own power; and it is distinguished by its falsity or truth, not by its badness or goodness, while choice is distinguished rather by these.

Now with opinion in general perhaps no one even says it is identical. But it is not identical even with any kind of opinion; for by choosing what is good or bad we are men of a certain character, which we are not by holding certain opinions. And we choose to get or avoid something good or bad, but we have opinions about what a thing is or whom it is good for or how it is good for him; we can hardly be said to opine to get or avoid anything. And choice is praised for being related to the right object rather than for being rightly related to it, opinion for being truly related to its object. And we choose what we best know to be good, but we opine what we do not quite know; and it is not the same people that are thought to make the best choices and to have the best opinions, but some are thought to have fairly good opinions, but by reason of vice to choose what they should not. If opinion precedes choice or accompanies it, that makes no difference; for it is not this that we are considering, but whether it is identical with some kind of opinion.
D: Refashioned:
For this reason, too, choice cannot be opinion. Opinion is thought to extend to all kinds of things -- eternal, impossible, and within our power alike -- and is distinguished by its truth or falsity, not by its goodness or badness, while choice is distinguished more by the latter.

With opinion in general, perhaps no one even claims it is identical to choice. But it is not identical with any particular kind of opinion either. For by choosing what is good or bad we become people of a certain character, which we do not become by holding certain opinions. And we choose to obtain or avoid something good or bad, but we form opinions about what a thing is, whom it benefits, or how -- we can hardly be said to 'opine' to obtain or avoid something. Choice is praised for being directed at the right object rather than for being correctly related to its object; opinion is praised for being true. We choose what we know best to be good, but we form opinions about what we do not fully know. And it is not the same people who are thought to make the best choices and to hold the best opinions: some people have fairly good opinions but, because of vice, choose what they should not. Whether opinion precedes or accompanies choice is irrelevant; what matters is that choice is not identical with any kind of opinion.
D: Elegant: Choice is not opinion. Opinion ranges over all things and is judged by truth or falsity; choice is judged by goodness or badness. Choosing well or badly shapes character; holding opinions does not. We choose to obtain or avoid goods; we opine about facts. Choice is praised for targeting the right object; opinion for being true. Good judgment and good choice often belong to different people -- some with sound opinions choose badly through vice. Whether opinion precedes or accompanies choice, they are not identical.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that deliberate choice is not a species of opinion. Opinion is evaluated by truth and falsity, choice by goodness and badness. Choice shapes character while opinion does not. People with excellent opinions may make poor choices due to vice, proving the two are distinct capacities.
D: Distilled: Choice differs from opinion because it is evaluated by goodness rather than truth, shapes character rather than merely reflecting beliefs, and can diverge from sound judgment when vice intervenes.
Notes: The separation of choice from opinion is partly directed against Plato's intellectualism, which closely linked right action to right belief. -- The observation that people with good opinions may choose badly through vice anticipates the extended discussion of akrasia (weakness of will) in Book VII. -- This passage establishes that moral character is constituted by what one chooses, not merely what one believes -- a foundational insight for virtue ethics.

D: Original: What, then, or what kind of thing is it, since it is none of the things we have mentioned? It seems to be voluntary, but not all that is voluntary to be an object of choice. Is it, then, what has been decided on by previous deliberation? At any rate choice involves a rational principle and thought. Even the name seems to suggest that it is what is chosen before other things.
D: Refashioned: What, then, is deliberate choice, or what kind of thing is it, since it is none of the things we have mentioned? It plainly belongs to the voluntary, but not everything voluntary is an object of choice. Is it, then, what has been decided upon through prior deliberation? At any rate, choice involves a rational principle and thought. Even the name seems to suggest that it is what is 'chosen before' other things.
D: Elegant: Since choice is none of the things examined -- not appetite, anger, wish, or opinion -- what is it? It is voluntary but narrower than the voluntary. It is what has been decided through prior deliberation, involving rational principle and thought. The very name suggests 'choosing before' other things.
D: Summary: Having eliminated all other candidates, Aristotle concludes that deliberate choice is what has been decided through prior deliberation. It involves rational thought and is narrower than the voluntary. The Greek word itself (prohairesis) suggests selecting one option before or in preference to others.
D: Distilled: Deliberate choice is the product of prior deliberation -- a rational, considered selection that goes beyond mere voluntary action to embody thought and purposive decision.
Notes: The etymology of prohairesis (pro- 'before' + hairesis 'taking/choosing') is used here as philosophical evidence, a common Aristotelian method. -- This transitional passage sets up the detailed analysis of deliberation (bouleusis) in Chapter 3. -- The definition of choice as 'deliberated desire' (orexis bouleutike) or 'desiderative deliberation' emerges fully at 3.18.

D: Original: Do we deliberate about everything, and is everything a possible subject of deliberation, or is deliberation impossible about some things? We ought presumably to call not what a fool or a madman would deliberate about, but what a sensible man would deliberate about, a subject of deliberation. Now about eternal things no one deliberates, e.g. about the material universe or the incommensurability of the diagonal and the side of a square. But no more do we deliberate about the things that involve movement but always happen in the same way, whether of necessity or by nature or from any other cause, e.g. the solstices and the risings of the stars; nor about things that happen now in one way, now in another, e.g. droughts and rains; nor about chance events, like the finding of treasure. But we do not deliberate even about all human affairs; for instance, no Spartan deliberates about the best constitution for the Scythians. For none of these things can be brought about by our own efforts.
D: Refashioned: Do we deliberate about everything? Is everything a possible subject of deliberation, or are some things beyond deliberation? Presumably we should call a 'subject of deliberation' not what a fool or a madman would deliberate about, but what a sensible person would. Now about eternal things no one deliberates -- for example, about the structure of the universe or the incommensurability of the diagonal and the side of a square. Nor do we deliberate about things that involve change but always occur the same way, whether by necessity or by nature or from any other cause -- for example, the solstices and the rising of the stars. Nor about things that happen sometimes one way and sometimes another, such as droughts and rains. Nor about chance events, like finding treasure. And we do not deliberate about all human affairs either: no Spartan deliberates about the best constitution for the Scythians. None of these things can be brought about by our own efforts.
D: Elegant: We deliberate not about everything but about what a sensible person would consider. No one deliberates about eternal truths, natural regularities, chance events, or affairs beyond our influence. A Spartan does not deliberate about the Scythian constitution. The common thread: we do not deliberate about what cannot be brought about by our own efforts.
D: Summary: Aristotle limits the scope of deliberation by excluding eternal truths, natural regularities, chance events, and matters beyond our influence. We deliberate only about things that can be affected by our own efforts.
D: Distilled: Deliberation is restricted to matters that are within our power to affect -- we do not deliberate about eternal truths, natural necessities, chance, or affairs beyond our reach.
Notes: The incommensurability of the diagonal refers to the famous discovery (attributed to the Pythagoreans) that the diagonal of a square is irrational relative to its side -- a fixed mathematical truth beyond deliberation. -- The Spartan/Scythian example illustrates that deliberation is limited not just by metaphysical possibility but by practical relevance -- what we can actually affect. -- This account of deliberation parallels the treatment of practical wisdom (phronesis) in Book VI, which also concerns the variable and actionable.

D: Original: We deliberate about things that are in our power and can be done; and these are in fact what is left. For nature, necessity, and chance are thought to be causes, and also reason and everything that depends on man. Now every class of men deliberates about the things that can be done by their own efforts. And in the case of exact and self-contained sciences there is no deliberation, e.g. about the letters of the alphabet (for we have no doubt how they should be written); but the things that are brought about by our own efforts, but not always in the same way, are the things about which we deliberate, e.g. questions of medical treatment or of money-making. And we do so more in the case of the art of navigation than in that of gymnastics, inasmuch as it has been less exactly worked out, and again about other things in the same ratio, and more also in the case of the arts than in that of the sciences; for we have more doubt about the former. Deliberation is concerned with things that happen in a certain way for the most part, but in which the event is obscure, and with things in which it is indeterminate. We call in others to aid us in deliberation on important questions, distrusting ourselves as not being equal to deciding.
D: Refashioned: We deliberate about things that are within our power and can be done -- and these are what remain after the exclusions. For nature, necessity, and chance are thought to be causes, as are reason and everything that depends on human agency. Each class of people deliberates about what can be accomplished by their own efforts. In the case of exact and self-contained bodies of knowledge, there is no deliberation -- for example, about the letters of the alphabet, since we have no doubt how they should be written. But the things brought about by our own efforts, which do not always turn out the same way, are what we deliberate about -- for example, questions of medical treatment or money-making. We deliberate more about navigation than gymnastics, inasmuch as navigation has been less precisely worked out, and similarly for other fields in proportion to their inexactness. We deliberate more in the arts than in the sciences, since we have more uncertainty in the arts. Deliberation concerns things that happen in a certain way for the most part, but where the outcome is unclear, and things whose resolution is indeterminate. We call in others to help us deliberate on important questions, lacking confidence in our own ability to decide.
D: Elegant: We deliberate about what is in our power and admits of variation. Exact sciences like spelling require no deliberation. We deliberate about medicine, money-making, and navigation -- fields where results are not fixed and outcomes are uncertain. The less exact the domain, the more room for deliberation. On important matters, we seek others' counsel, doubting our own sufficiency.
D: Summary: Deliberation concerns domains where human effort can make a difference but outcomes are uncertain. The less exact a field of knowledge, the more deliberation it requires. We deliberate most where results vary and we seek advisors when the stakes are high and our own judgment feels insufficient.
D: Distilled: We deliberate about matters where our efforts can make a difference but outcomes remain uncertain -- the less exact the domain, the greater the need for deliberation.
Notes: The contrast between exact sciences (episteme) and arts/crafts (techne) that require deliberation is central to Aristotle's epistemology. -- The examples of medicine and navigation as fields requiring deliberation recur throughout Aristotle's works and connect to his account of practical wisdom in Book VI. -- The observation that we seek counsel on important matters introduces a social dimension to deliberation, reflecting Greek political practice. -- This passage influenced later accounts of practical reasoning in medieval and modern philosophy.

D: Original: We deliberate not about ends but about means. For a doctor does not deliberate whether he shall heal, nor an orator whether he shall persuade, nor a statesman whether he shall produce law and order, nor does any one else deliberate about his end. They assume the end and consider how and by what means it is to be attained; and if it seems to be produced by several means they consider by which it is most easily and best produced, while if it is achieved by one only they consider how it will be achieved by this and by what means this will be achieved, till they come to the first cause, which in the order of discovery is last. For the person who deliberates seems to investigate and analyse in the way described as though he were analysing a geometrical construction (not all investigation appears to be deliberation- for instance mathematical investigations- but all deliberation is investigation), and what is last in the order of analysis seems to be first in the order of becoming. And if we come on an impossibility, we give up the search, e.g. if we need money and this cannot be got; but if a thing appears possible we try to do it. By 'possible' things I mean things that might be brought about by our own efforts; and these in a sense include things that can be brought about by the efforts of our friends, since the moving principle is in ourselves. The subject of investigation is sometimes the instruments, sometimes the use of them; and similarly in the other cases- sometimes the means, sometimes the mode of using it or the means of bringing it about. It seems, then, as has been said, that man is a moving principle of actions; now deliberation is about the things to be done by the agent himself, and actions are for the sake of things other than themselves. For the end cannot be a subject of deliberation, but only the means; nor indeed can the particular facts be a subject of it, as whether this is bread or has been baked as it should; for these are matters of perception. If we are to be always deliberating, we shall have to go on to infinity.
D: Refashioned: We deliberate not about ends but about means. A doctor does not deliberate about whether to heal, nor an orator about whether to persuade, nor a statesman about whether to produce good order. Rather, they assume the end and consider how and by what means to achieve it. If it seems achievable by several means, they consider which produces it most easily and best. If it is achievable by only one means, they consider how that means will be achieved, and by what further means, until they arrive at the first cause, which in the order of discovery is last. For the deliberator seems to investigate and analyze as if working backward through a geometrical construction. (Not all investigation is deliberation -- mathematical investigations, for instance, are not -- but all deliberation is investigation.) What is last in the order of analysis is first in the order of execution. If we encounter an impossibility, we abandon the inquiry -- for example, if we need money and it cannot be obtained. But if something appears possible, we attempt it. By 'possible' I mean things achievable through our own efforts, which in a sense includes things achievable through our friends, since the moving principle is in ourselves. The subject of investigation is sometimes the instruments and sometimes their use, and similarly in other cases -- sometimes the means and sometimes the mode of using them or the way of bringing them about. It seems, then, as stated, that a person is a moving principle of actions. Deliberation concerns actions to be performed by the agent himself, and actions exist for the sake of things beyond themselves. The end cannot be a subject of deliberation, only the means. Nor can particular perceptual facts be deliberated about -- such as whether this is bread or whether it has been properly baked -- for these are matters of perception. And if we were always to deliberate further, we would go on to infinity.
D: Elegant: We deliberate about means, not ends. The doctor assumes healing as the goal and considers how to achieve it. If several means exist, we select the easiest and best; if only one, we analyze how to bring it about, working backward to the first actionable step. This resembles reverse-engineering a geometric proof. If we hit an impossibility, we stop; if the path seems feasible, we act. 'Possible' means achievable by our own efforts, including through friends. The end is not deliberated about, nor are immediate perceptual facts. Infinite regress in deliberation must be avoided.
D: Summary: Deliberation works backward from an assumed end to discover the means of achieving it, like reverse-engineering a geometric proof. We select the best or most feasible means, and if we encounter an impossibility, we abandon the search. Ends themselves and immediate perceptual facts fall outside deliberation.
D: Distilled: Deliberation is a backward-working analysis from assumed ends to discoverable means, selecting the most feasible path and stopping either at an actionable first step or at an impossibility.
Notes: The geometrical analysis analogy is one of Aristotle's most influential models for practical reasoning. -- The claim that we deliberate about means (ta pros to telos) but not about ends has been extensively debated; 'means' here may include constitutive parts of the end, not just instrumental causes. -- The inclusion of friends' efforts as 'possible' reflects the Greek understanding that political friendship extends agency. -- The infinite regress problem shows Aristotle's awareness of foundational issues in practical reasoning. -- This account of deliberation deeply influenced medieval practical philosophy, especially Thomas Aquinas's analysis of human action.

D: Original:
The same thing is deliberated upon and is chosen, except that the object of choice is already determinate, since it is that which has been decided upon as a result of deliberation that is the object of choice. For every one ceases to inquire how he is to act when he has brought the moving principle back to himself and to the ruling part of himself; for this is what chooses. This is plain also from the ancient constitutions, which Homer represented; for the kings announced their choices to the people. The object of choice being one of the things in our own power which is desired after deliberation, choice will be deliberate desire of things in our own power; for when we have decided as a result of deliberation, we desire in accordance with our deliberation.

We may take it, then, that we have described choice in outline, and stated the nature of its objects and the fact that it is concerned with means.
D: Refashioned:
The object of deliberation and the object of choice are the same, except that the object of choice is already determined -- since what has been decided upon through deliberation is what is chosen. Everyone stops inquiring how to act when he has traced the moving principle back to himself and to the governing part of himself, for this is what chooses. This is also clear from the ancient constitutions that Homer depicted, where kings announced their choices to the people. Since the object of choice is something within our power that is desired after deliberation, choice will be the deliberate desire for things within our power. For when we have reached a decision through deliberation, we desire in accordance with our deliberation.

We may take it, then, that we have described choice in outline and stated the nature of its objects and the fact that it concerns means.
D: Elegant: What is deliberated about and what is chosen are the same thing, except that the object of choice is already determined by deliberation. Inquiry ceases when the moving principle is traced back to the agent's governing rational faculty -- the part that chooses. Choice is therefore deliberate desire for things within our power: we deliberate, decide, and then desire in accordance with our decision. Choice concerns means, not ends.
D: Summary: Choice and deliberation share the same object, but choice is the settled outcome of deliberation. Once the agent traces the chain of means back to something he can do, inquiry stops and choice occurs. Aristotle defines choice as 'deliberate desire for things in our power.'
D: Distilled: Choice is deliberate desire for things within our power -- the point where deliberation terminates in a settled decision to act.
Notes: The definition of choice as 'deliberate desire' (bouleutike orexis) or 'desiderative intellect' (orektikos nous) combines rational and appetitive elements, reflecting Aristotle's view that practical reason is inherently motivational. -- The reference to Homer's depiction of kingship (e.g., in the Iliad) illustrates deliberation as a political as well as individual process. -- This definition of prohairesis became foundational for subsequent ethical theory, especially in Stoicism (where Epictetus made prohairesis the center of his ethics) and in Aquinas's moral theology. -- The phrase 'governing part' (to hegemonikon) anticipates Stoic psychological terminology.

D: Original: That wish is for the end has already been stated; some think it is for the good, others for the apparent good. Now those who say that the good is the object of wish must admit in consequence that that which the man who does not choose aright wishes for is not an object of wish (for if it is to be so, it must also be good; but it was, if it so happened, bad); while those who say the apparent good is the object of wish must admit that there is no natural object of wish, but only what seems good to each man. Now different things appear good to different people, and, if it so happens, even contrary things.
D: Refashioned: It has already been stated that wish is directed at the end. Some thinkers hold that the object of wish is the good; others, the apparent good. Those who say the object of wish is the good must accept the consequence that what someone who chooses incorrectly wishes for is not truly an object of wish -- for if it were an object of wish, it would have to be good, but it may in fact be bad. On the other hand, those who say the object of wish is the apparent good must accept that there is no natural object of wish, only what seems good to each individual. But different things appear good to different people, and sometimes even contrary things do.
D: Elegant: Wish aims at the end. If its object is the good, then what the person who chooses wrongly wishes for is not truly wished for -- since it would have to be good, yet may be bad. If its object is the apparent good, then there is no natural object of wish, only what seems good to each person -- and contrary things may appear good to different people.
D: Summary: Aristotle presents a dilemma about the object of wish: if wish aims at the genuinely good, then mistaken wishes are not really wishes at all; if wish aims at the apparently good, then there is no objective standard and people may wish for contradictory things.
D: Distilled: Whether wish aims at the real good or the apparent good, each position faces difficulties: the first makes mistaken wishes impossible, the second eliminates any natural standard of what is wished for.
Notes: This chapter (3.4) addresses the relationship between wish (boulesis) and the good, a problem inherited from Plato's Meno and Gorgias. -- The distinction between real and apparent good recurs throughout the Ethics and connects to the discussion of pleasure in Books VII and X. -- Aristotle's solution in the next passage introduces the virtuous person as the standard of correct perception, a move characteristic of his ethical method.

D: Original: If these consequences are unpleasing, are we to say that absolutely and in truth the good is the object of wish, but for each person the apparent good; that that which is in truth an object of wish is an object of wish to the good man, while any chance thing may be so the bad man, as in the case of bodies also the things that are in truth wholesome are wholesome for bodies which are in good condition, while for those that are diseased other things are wholesome- or bitter or sweet or hot or heavy, and so on; since the good man judges each class of things rightly, and in each the truth appears to him? For each state of character has its own ideas of the noble and the pleasant, and perhaps the good man differs from others most by seeing the truth in each class of things, being as it were the norm and measure of them. In most things the error seems to be due to pleasure; for it appears a good when it is not. We therefore choose the pleasant as a good, and avoid pain as an evil.
D: Refashioned: If these consequences are unacceptable, should we say that in an absolute sense the good is the true object of wish, but that for each individual the apparent good serves as the object of wish? For the good person, what is truly an object of wish is what is genuinely wished for, while for the bad person, it may be anything at all -- just as with bodies, things truly wholesome are wholesome for bodies in good condition, while for diseased bodies other things are wholesome (or bitter, or sweet, or hot, or heavy, and so on). The good person judges each class of things correctly, and in each case the truth appears to him. For each state of character has its own conception of what is noble and pleasant, and perhaps what most distinguishes the good person from others is his ability to see the truth in each domain, being as it were the standard and measure of such things. In most cases, the error seems to arise from pleasure: it appears to be a good when it is not. We therefore choose the pleasant as good and avoid pain as evil.
D: Elegant: The solution: absolutely speaking, the good is the object of wish, but for each person it is the apparent good. The good person, like a healthy body, perceives correctly -- he judges each situation rightly and sees the truth, serving as the standard and measure. Each character state has its own sense of the noble and pleasant. Most error comes from pleasure, which appears good when it is not.
D: Summary: Aristotle resolves the dilemma by making the virtuous person the standard: objectively, the good is the true object of wish, but each person wishes for what appears good to them. The good person perceives correctly in each domain, serving as the norm and measure, while most people are deceived by pleasure into mistaking apparent goods for real ones.
D: Distilled: The virtuous person is the standard and measure of what is truly good, perceiving correctly where others are deceived by pleasure into mistaking appearance for reality.
Notes: The 'standard and measure' (kanon kai metron) language echoes Protagoras's 'man is the measure' doctrine but reverses it: not every person but the good person is the measure. -- The analogy between moral and physical health is characteristic of Aristotle and connects to Greek medical thinking (Hippocratic tradition). -- The identification of pleasure as the primary source of moral error anticipates the detailed discussions of pleasure in Books VII.11-14 and X.1-5. -- This passage is foundational for virtue epistemology -- the idea that good character is required for accurate moral perception.

D: Original: The end, then, being what we wish for, the means what we deliberate about and choose, actions concerning means must be according to choice and voluntary. Now the exercise of the virtues is concerned with means. Therefore virtue also is in our own power, and so too vice. For where it is in our power to act it is also in our power not to act, and vice versa; so that, if to act, where this is noble, is in our power, not to act, which will be base, will also be in our power, and if not to act, where this is noble, is in our power, to act, which will be base, will also be in our power. Now if it is in our power to do noble or base acts, and likewise in our power not to do them, and this was what being good or bad meant, then it is in our power to be virtuous or vicious.
D: Refashioned: Since the end is what we wish for, and the means are what we deliberate about and choose, actions concerning means must accord with choice and be voluntary. Now the exercise of the virtues is concerned with means. Therefore virtue too is within our power, and so is vice. For wherever it is in our power to act, it is also in our power not to act, and vice versa. If acting nobly is in our power, then not acting -- which would be base -- is also in our power. And if not acting, when that is noble, is in our power, then acting basely is also in our power. If doing noble and base acts is in our power, and likewise refraining from them, and if this is what it means to be good or bad, then it is in our power to be virtuous or vicious.
D: Elegant: Since we wish for ends and choose means, action concerning means is voluntary. Virtue deals with means, so virtue is in our power -- and so is vice. Wherever we can act, we can also refrain, and vice versa. If noble action is in our power, so is base action. Since being good or bad consists in doing noble or base acts that are in our power, virtue and vice are both voluntary.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that since virtue concerns the means we choose, and choosing is voluntary, both virtue and vice are in our power. Wherever we can act nobly, we can also refrain; wherever we can refrain from baseness, we can also act basely. Therefore moral character itself is voluntary.
D: Distilled: Virtue and vice are both within our power, because wherever we can choose to act nobly, we can equally choose not to -- and moral character consists in precisely these voluntary choices.
Notes: This opens Chapter 5's crucial argument for moral responsibility. -- The argument has the structure: (1) virtue concerns means; (2) means are objects of choice; (3) choice is voluntary; (4) therefore virtue is voluntary; (5) by parallel reasoning, vice is too. -- This is Aristotle's most explicit argument against moral determinism and for the voluntariness of character. -- The passage has been central to debates about free will, moral luck, and responsibility in Western philosophy.

D: Original: The saying that 'no one is voluntarily wicked nor involuntarily happy' seems to be partly false and partly true; for no one is involuntarily happy, but wickedness is voluntary. Or else we shall have to dispute what has just been said, at any rate, and deny that man is a moving principle or begetter of his actions as of children. But if these facts are evident and we cannot refer actions to moving principles other than those in ourselves, the acts whose moving principles are in us must themselves also be in our power and voluntary.
D: Refashioned: The saying that 'no one is voluntarily wicked nor involuntarily happy' seems partly false and partly true. No one is involuntarily happy, but wickedness is voluntary. Otherwise, we would have to dispute what has just been established and deny that a person is a moving principle or originator of his actions, as a parent is of children. But if these facts are evident, and we cannot trace actions back to moving principles other than those within ourselves, then actions whose moving principles are within us must themselves be in our power and voluntary.
D: Elegant: The proverb that 'no one is voluntarily wicked nor involuntarily happy' is half right: happiness is indeed not involuntary, but wickedness is voluntary. To deny this, we would have to deny that we are the originators of our own actions. Since we cannot trace our actions to any external moving principle, they are in our power and voluntary.
D: Summary: Aristotle challenges the old saying that no one is willingly wicked. Happiness is not involuntary, but wickedness is voluntary. Denying this would require denying that we originate our own actions, which contradicts the established analysis of voluntariness.
D: Distilled: Wickedness is voluntary because we are the moving principles of our own actions, and denying this would undermine the entire framework of moral agency.
Notes: The saying 'no one is voluntarily wicked' is attributed to Socrates (see Plato's Protagoras 345d-e and Laws 860d). -- Aristotle's partial rejection of this Socratic thesis is a major departure: he agrees no one is involuntarily happy but insists wickedness is voluntary. -- The metaphor of being an originator 'as a parent is of children' emphasizes that we produce our actions and bear responsibility for them. -- This argument is crucial for the coherence of Aristotle's ethical system, which requires that praise and blame be deserved.

D: Original: Witness seems to be borne to this both by individuals in their private capacity and by legislators themselves; for these punish and take vengeance on those who do wicked acts (unless they have acted under compulsion or as a result of ignorance for which they are not themselves responsible), while they honour those who do noble acts, as though they meant to encourage the latter and deter the former. But no one is encouraged to do the things that are neither in our power nor voluntary; it is assumed that there is no gain in being persuaded not to be hot or in pain or hungry or the like, since we shall experience these feelings none the less. Indeed, we punish a man for his very ignorance, if he is thought responsible for the ignorance, as when penalties are doubled in the case of drunkenness; for the moving principle is in the man himself, since he had the power of not getting drunk and his getting drunk was the cause of his ignorance. And we punish those who are ignorant of anything in the laws that they ought to know and that is not difficult, and so too in the case of anything else that they are thought to be ignorant of through carelessness; we assume that it is in their power not to be ignorant, since they have the power of taking care.
D: Refashioned: This view is confirmed both by individuals in their private conduct and by legislators themselves. For legislators punish and exact retribution from those who do wicked acts -- unless the wrongdoers acted under compulsion or through ignorance for which they were not themselves responsible -- while they honor those who do noble acts, as if to encourage the latter and deter the former. But no one is encouraged to do things that are neither in our power nor voluntary. It is assumed there is no point in being persuaded not to feel hot, or hungry, or in pain, since we will experience these regardless. Indeed, we punish people even for their very ignorance, if they are thought responsible for it -- as when penalties are doubled for offenses committed while drunk, since the moving principle is in the person himself: he had the power not to get drunk, and his drunkenness caused his ignorance. We also punish those who are ignorant of anything in the laws that they ought to know and that is not difficult to know, and similarly in other cases where ignorance is thought to result from negligence -- on the assumption that it was in their power not to be ignorant, since they had the power to take due care.
D: Elegant: Both private practice and legislation confirm that virtue and vice are voluntary. Legislators punish wrongdoers (unless compelled or blamelessly ignorant) and honor the virtuous -- to encourage and deter. No one is urged to control what lies beyond their power. We even punish culpable ignorance: penalties are doubled for crimes committed drunk, since the agent chose to drink. And we punish ignorance of the law when it results from negligence, assuming people could have learned what they ought to know.
D: Summary: Aristotle cites legal practice as evidence that virtue and vice are voluntary. Legislators punish wrongdoing and reward nobility, presupposing that both are in our power. Even culpable ignorance is punished -- penalties are doubled for drunken offenses, and ignorance of the law is no excuse when due to negligence.
D: Distilled: Legal practice confirms that virtue and vice are voluntary: legislators punish wrongdoing, reward nobility, and hold people accountable even for negligent ignorance, presupposing that conduct is within our control.
Notes: The doubled penalties for drunken offenses was an actual provision of Athenian law, attributed by some sources to Pittacus of Mytilene (one of the Seven Sages). -- The argument from legislative practice to moral theory is characteristic of Aristotle's method of beginning from endoxa (reputable opinions) and established practices. -- The concept of culpable ignorance (ignorance for which one is responsible) has been enormously influential in criminal law theory. -- The principle 'ignorance of the law is no excuse' reflects actual Greek legal practice and remains a legal maxim today.

D: Original: But perhaps a man is the kind of man not to take care. Still they are themselves by their slack lives responsible for becoming men of that kind, and men make themselves responsible for being unjust or self-indulgent, in the one case by cheating and in the other by spending their time in drinking bouts and the like; for it is activities exercised on particular objects that make the corresponding character. This is plain from the case of people training for any contest or action; they practise the activity the whole time. Now not to know that it is from the exercise of activities on particular objects that states of character are produced is the mark of a thoroughly senseless person. Again, it is irrational to suppose that a man who acts unjustly does not wish to be unjust or a man who acts self-indulgently to be self-indulgent. But if without being ignorant a man does the things which will make him unjust, he will be unjust voluntarily. Yet it does not follow that if he wishes he will cease to be unjust and will be just. For neither does the man who is ill become well on those terms. We may suppose a case in which he is ill voluntarily, through living incontinently and disobeying his doctors. In that case it was then open to him not to be ill, but not now, when he has thrown away his chance, just as when you have let a stone go it is too late to recover it; but yet it was in your power to throw it, since the moving principle was in you. So, too, to the unjust and to the self-indulgent man it was open at the beginning not to become men of this kind, and so they are unjust and selfindulgent voluntarily; but now that they have become so it is not possible for them not to be so.
D: Refashioned: But perhaps a person is the kind of person who does not take care. Still, people are responsible, through their slack way of living, for becoming that kind of person. And people make themselves unjust by cheating, or self-indulgent by spending their time in drinking bouts and the like. For it is the activities exercised on particular objects that produce the corresponding character. This is clear from the case of people training for any contest or performance: they practice the relevant activity continuously. Not to know that exercising activities on particular objects produces the corresponding character is the mark of a thoroughly senseless person. Furthermore, it is irrational to suppose that someone who acts unjustly does not wish to be unjust, or that someone who acts self-indulgently does not wish to be self-indulgent. But if, without being ignorant, a person does the things that will make him unjust, he will be unjust voluntarily. Yet it does not follow that he can simply choose to stop being unjust and become just. The sick person cannot simply choose to become well either. Suppose someone is sick voluntarily, through living without self-control and disobeying his doctors. It was once open to him not to be sick, but now that he has thrown away his chance, it is too late -- just as once you have let go of a stone it is too late to take it back, even though it was in your power to throw it, since the moving principle was in you. So too, it was open to the unjust and self-indulgent person at the beginning not to become such; therefore they are unjust and self-indulgent voluntarily. But now that they have become so, it is no longer possible for them not to be so.
D: Elegant: People are responsible for becoming careless through slack living. Habitual activities produce character: cheating makes one unjust, carousing makes one self-indulgent. It is irrational to deny that those who consistently act badly wish to be that way. Yet once character is formed, it cannot be reversed at will -- like a stone once thrown or illness caused by negligence. The unjust person chose freely at the beginning; now that he has become such, he cannot simply will himself just again.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that people are responsible for their character because habitual actions produce stable dispositions. Just as practice makes an athlete, repeated unjust acts make a person unjust. While character formation was once within one's control, once a disposition is established it cannot be instantly reversed -- like a thrown stone that cannot be recalled. The voluntariness lies in the initial choices that shaped the character.
D: Distilled: We are responsible for our characters because we formed them through repeated voluntary actions, even though once established, character -- like a thrown stone -- can no longer be simply reversed at will.
Notes: The stone analogy is one of Aristotle's most famous images for moral responsibility and the irreversibility of character formation. -- The medical analogy (voluntary illness through negligent living) connects to the Greek practice of holding patients responsible for health outcomes when they ignored medical advice. -- This argument addresses a key objection to moral responsibility: if character determines choice, how can we be responsible? Aristotle's answer is that we are responsible for forming the character. -- The passage has generated extensive debate about whether Aristotle adequately addresses the problem of moral luck and the conditions under which character formation begins (in childhood, before rational agency is fully developed). -- Connects to Book II's account of habituation (ethismos) as the source of moral virtue.

D: Original: But not only are the vices of the soul voluntary, but those of the body also for some men, whom we accordingly blame; while no one blames those who are ugly by nature, we blame those who are so owing to want of exercise and care. So it is, too, with respect to weakness and infirmity; no one would reproach a man blind from birth or by disease or from a blow, but rather pity him, while every one would blame a man who was blind from drunkenness or some other form of self-indulgence. Of vices of the body, then, those in our own power are blamed, those not in our power are not. And if this be so, in the other cases also the vices that are blamed must be in our own power.
D: Refashioned: Not only are vices of the soul voluntary, but in some cases vices of the body are too, and we blame people accordingly. No one blames those who are ugly by nature, but we do blame those who are ugly through lack of exercise and care. The same applies to weakness and physical disability: no one would reproach a person blind from birth, or from disease, or from a blow -- we would rather pity him. But everyone would blame someone who went blind from drunkenness or some other form of self-indulgence. Among bodily defects, then, those in our power are blamed, and those not in our power are not. And if this is so, then in other areas too, the vices that are blamed must be ones within our power.
D: Elegant: Even bodily vices can be voluntary and blameworthy. We do not blame natural ugliness but blame ugliness from neglect. We pity congenital blindness but blame blindness from drunkenness. Bodily defects within our power are blamed; those beyond it are not. By the same principle, the moral vices we blame must also be within our power.
D: Summary: Aristotle extends his argument to bodily conditions: we blame people for physical defects caused by negligence or self-indulgence but not for those that are natural or accidental. This parallel confirms that the moral vices we blame must similarly be within the agent's power.
D: Distilled: We blame bodily defects caused by negligence but not those caused by nature, confirming that blameworthy vices -- whether of body or soul -- must be within our power to prevent.
Notes: The argument from bodily to psychological responsibility uses analogy, a typical Aristotelian dialectical strategy. -- The distinction between natural and acquired defects maps onto the voluntary/involuntary distinction established earlier. -- This passage has implications for debates about responsibility for health outcomes, disability ethics, and the boundaries of moral blame. -- The underlying principle -- that blame requires control -- remains central to modern moral philosophy and law.

D: Original: Now some one may say that all men desire the apparent good, but have no control over the appearance, but the end appears to each man in a form answering to his character. We reply that if each man is somehow responsible for his state of mind, he will also be himself somehow responsible for the appearance; but if not, no one is responsible for his own evildoing, but every one does evil acts through ignorance of the end, thinking that by these he will get what is best, and the aiming at the end is not self-chosen but one must be born with an eye, as it were, by which to judge rightly and choose what is truly good, and he is well endowed by nature who is well endowed with this. For it is what is greatest and most noble, and what we cannot get or learn from another, but must have just such as it was when given us at birth, and to be well and nobly endowed with this will be perfect and true excellence of natural endowment. If this is true, then, how will virtue be more voluntary than vice? To both men alike, the good and the bad, the end appears and is fixed by nature or however it may be, and it is by referring everything else to this that men do whatever they do.
D: Refashioned: Now someone might object that all people desire the apparent good but have no control over how things appear to them -- that the end appears to each person in a form answering to his character. We reply: if each person is somehow responsible for his own state of character, he will also be responsible for how things appear to him. If not, then no one is responsible for his own wrongdoing; everyone does evil through ignorance of the true end, thinking that by these acts he will achieve what is best. The pursuit of the end would not be self-chosen but rather something one must be born with -- as if one needed an inborn eye to judge rightly and choose what is truly good, and the person well endowed by nature is the one who has this eye. This would be the greatest and noblest gift, something that cannot be acquired or learned from another but must be possessed exactly as one received it at birth. To be well and nobly endowed with this capacity would constitute the perfect and true excellence of natural endowment. But if this were true, how would virtue be any more voluntary than vice? For both alike -- the good person and the bad -- the end appears and is fixed by nature (or however it may be), and it is by referring everything else to this end that people do whatever they do.
D: Elegant: An objector might claim we cannot control how the good appears to us, since perception is shaped by character. But if we are responsible for our character, we are responsible for our perceptions. If not, then no one is responsible for wrongdoing -- all evil is mere ignorance, and moral discernment becomes an inborn gift like eyesight, the greatest natural endowment, which cannot be learned. But then virtue would be no more voluntary than vice, since both the good and bad person would have their ends fixed by nature.
D: Summary: Aristotle addresses the objection that moral perception is determined by character, which is determined by nature. If this were true, moral discernment would be an inborn gift like eyesight, and no one would be responsible for wrongdoing. But this would make virtue no more voluntary than vice, undermining the entire framework of moral responsibility.
D: Distilled: If moral perception were entirely determined by inborn nature, virtue would be no more voluntary than vice, and no one could be held responsible for wrongdoing -- a conclusion Aristotle finds unacceptable.
Notes: This passage engages with a form of moral determinism or naturalism that would undermine responsibility. -- The 'inborn eye' metaphor for moral discernment has parallels in Plato (Republic 518b-d, the 'eye of the soul'). -- The dilemma structure (either we're responsible for character or no one is responsible for anything) is a reductio ad absurdum of the determinist position. -- This argument has been central to debates about moral luck, natural endowment, and the voluntariness of virtue in the Aristotelian tradition. -- The objection Aristotle considers here anticipates modern concerns about the role of genetic and environmental factors in character formation.

D: Original: Whether, then, it is not by nature that the end appears to each man such as it does appear, but something also depends on him, or the end is natural but because the good man adopts the means voluntarily virtue is voluntary, vice also will be none the less voluntary; for in the case of the bad man there is equally present that which depends on himself in his actions even if not in his end. If, then, as is asserted, the virtues are voluntary (for we are ourselves somehow partly responsible for our states of character, and it is by being persons of a certain kind that we assume the end to be so and so), the vices also will be voluntary; for the same is true of them.
D: Refashioned: Whether the end appears to each person by nature or whether something also depends on the person himself -- or whether the end is natural but virtue is voluntary because the good person adopts the means voluntarily -- in either case, vice will be no less voluntary than virtue. For in the case of the bad person, what depends on himself is equally present in his actions, even if not in his perception of the end. If, then, as is claimed, the virtues are voluntary -- since we are ourselves somehow partly responsible for our states of character, and it is by being people of a certain kind that we take the end to be what we take it to be -- then the vices will also be voluntary, for the same reasoning applies to them.
D: Elegant: Whether moral perception is natural or partly self-determined, vice remains as voluntary as virtue. If virtue is voluntary because we shape our character and thereby our perception of the end, then by the same logic vice is voluntary too -- for the bad person's actions equally depend on himself, even if his perception of the end does not.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that regardless of whether our perception of the end is natural or self-determined, the symmetry between virtue and vice holds: if virtue is voluntary because we shape our character, then vice must be equally voluntary by the same reasoning.
D: Distilled: Whatever makes virtue voluntary -- our partial responsibility for character and thus for how we perceive the good -- makes vice equally voluntary by the same logic.
Notes: This completes the argument of Chapter 5 by establishing the parallel voluntariness of virtue and vice. -- The 'symmetry argument' is important: any account that makes virtue voluntary must, by parity of reasoning, make vice voluntary too. -- Aristotle's conclusion that 'we are ourselves somehow partly responsible for our states of character' (pos synaitioi) uses carefully hedged language ('somehow,' 'partly'), acknowledging complexity without abandoning responsibility. -- This passage has been influential in debates about moral responsibility and the voluntariness of character in virtue ethics.

D: Original:
With regard to the virtues in general we have stated their genus in outline, viz. that they are means and that they are states of character, and that they tend, and by their own nature, to the doing of the acts by which they are produced, and that they are in our power and voluntary, and act as the right rule prescribes. But actions and states of character are not voluntary in the same way; for we are masters of our actions from the beginning right to the end, if we know the particular facts, but though we control the beginning of our states of character the gradual progress is not obvious any more than it is in illnesses; because it was in our power, however, to act in this way or not in this way, therefore the states are voluntary.

Let us take up the several virtues, however, and say which they are and what sort of things they are concerned with and how they are concerned with them; at the same time it will become plain how many they are. And first let us speak of courage.
D: Refashioned:
Regarding the virtues in general, we have stated their genus in outline: they are means, they are stable dispositions, and by their own nature they tend toward performing the acts by which they are produced. They are in our power and voluntary, and they function as right reason prescribes. But actions and states of character are not voluntary in the same way. We are masters of our actions from beginning to end, provided we know the particular facts. But although we control the beginning of our states of character, their gradual development is not perceptible step by step -- just as with illnesses. Nevertheless, because it was in our power to act one way or another, the resulting states are voluntary.

Let us now take up the individual virtues and say what they are, what they are concerned with, and how they are concerned with them. At the same time, it will become clear how many there are. And first, let us speak of courage.
D: Elegant: The virtues are means, stable dispositions, and tendencies toward the acts that produce them. They are voluntary and follow right reason. But actions and character states differ in voluntariness: we control our actions from start to finish, but character develops gradually and imperceptibly, like illness. Still, because the initial acts were in our power, the resulting states are voluntary. We now turn to the individual virtues, beginning with courage.
D: Summary: Aristotle summarizes his general account of virtue: virtues are means, stable dispositions, voluntary, and guided by right reason. While we control individual actions from start to finish, character develops gradually and imperceptibly. Yet because the initial actions that shaped character were in our power, the resulting dispositions are voluntary. He transitions to examining individual virtues, beginning with courage.
D: Distilled: Virtues are voluntary stable dispositions that develop gradually through actions we control, and though their growth is imperceptible like the onset of illness, their origin in our choices makes them our responsibility.
Notes: This transitional passage summarizes Book II's account of virtue and Book III's account of voluntariness before turning to the individual virtues. -- The illness analogy for imperceptible character development is powerful: just as we don't notice each step toward illness, we don't notice each step toward vice, yet both originate in our choices. -- The mention of 'right reason' (orthos logos) points forward to the detailed account of practical wisdom (phronesis) in Book VI. -- The transition to courage begins the survey of individual virtues that occupies the rest of Book III and all of Books IV-V. -- 'Stable disposition' translates hexis, a key technical term meaning a settled state of character as opposed to a fleeting emotion or capacity.

D: Original: That it is a mean with regard to feelings of fear and confidence has already been made evident; and plainly the things we fear are terrible things, and these are, to speak without qualification, evils; for which reason people even define fear as expectation of evil. Now we fear all evils, e.g. disgrace, poverty, disease, friendlessness, death, but the brave man is not thought to be concerned with all; for to fear some things is even right and noble, and it is base not to fear them- e.g. disgrace; he who fears this is good and modest, and he who does not is shameless. He is, however, by some people called brave, by a transference of the word to a new meaning; for he has in him something which is like the brave man, since the brave man also is a fearless person. Poverty and disease we perhaps ought not to fear, nor in general the things that do not proceed from vice and are not due to a man himself.
D: Refashioned: It has already been established that courage is a mean regarding feelings of fear and confidence. The things we fear are clearly terrible things -- which are, broadly speaking, evils. This is why people define fear as the anticipation of evil. Now we fear all sorts of evils -- disgrace, poverty, disease, friendlessness, death -- but the courageous person is not thought to be concerned with all of them. To fear some things is actually right and noble, and not to fear them is base -- for example, disgrace. The person who fears disgrace is virtuous and modest; the person who does not is shameless. This shameless person is sometimes called 'brave' by an extension of the term, since he shares something with the truly courageous person: both are fearless in certain respects. But poverty and disease, we probably ought not to fear, nor in general things that do not arise from vice and are not due to the person himself.
D: Elegant: Courage is a mean regarding fear and confidence. We fear terrible things -- evils generally. But the courageous person is not concerned with all fears. Some fears are right and noble: fearing disgrace is virtuous, not fearing it is shameless. The shameless person is sometimes called 'brave' loosely, since both he and the truly courageous share fearlessness. But we need not fear poverty or disease -- things not arising from vice or our own doing.
D: Summary: Aristotle begins analyzing courage by noting it concerns fear and confidence but not all fears equally. Some fears are appropriate -- such as fearing disgrace -- and the absence of such fear indicates shamelessness, not courage. True courage is not concerned with poverty or disease, which are not moral evils arising from one's own vice.
D: Distilled: Courage concerns fear and confidence but not all fears: some fears like that of disgrace are morally appropriate, and true courage is specifically about facing terrors that test moral character, not all misfortunes.
Notes: This begins Chapter 6's detailed analysis of courage (andreia), the first individual virtue examined. -- The distinction between appropriate and inappropriate fears connects to the general account of virtue as hitting the mean in emotions. -- The observation that fearing disgrace is virtuous connects to the Greek concept of aidos (shame/modesty), discussed in Book IV.9. -- The point about poverty and disease not being proper objects of courage distinguishes moral courage from mere toughness or stoic endurance.

D: Original: But not even the man who is fearless of these is brave. Yet we apply the word to him also in virtue of a similarity; for some who in the dangers of war are cowards are liberal and are confident in face of the loss of money. Nor is a man a coward if he fears insult to his wife and children or envy or anything of the kind; nor brave if he is confident when he is about to be flogged. With what sort of terrible things, then, is the brave man concerned? Surely with the greatest; for no one is more likely than he to stand his ground against what is awe-inspiring.
D: Refashioned: But not even the person who is fearless regarding poverty and disease is properly called courageous. Yet we do apply the word to such people by way of analogy, since some who are cowardly in the dangers of war are generous and confident when facing financial loss. Nor is a person a coward if he fears insult to his wife and children, or envy, or anything of that kind; nor is he courageous if he is confident when about to be flogged. With what sort of terrible things, then, is the courageous person concerned? Surely with the greatest, for no one is more likely than the courageous person to stand firm against what is truly awe-inspiring.
D: Elegant: Fearlessness about poverty or disease does not constitute courage proper, though the word is applied by analogy. Some war-cowards face financial ruin with confidence. Fearing insult to one's family is not cowardice; confidence before a flogging is not courage. The courageous person is concerned with the greatest terrors -- those before which he stands firm when others cannot.
D: Summary: Aristotle narrows the scope of true courage: it is not about fearlessness regarding money, disease, or family concerns. The truly courageous person faces the greatest and most awe-inspiring dangers, particularly those involving the ultimate stakes.
D: Distilled: True courage is specifically concerned with the greatest and most awe-inspiring dangers, not with fearlessness regarding money, health, or other non-moral threats.
Notes: The distinction between courage proper and metaphorical uses of the term reflects Aristotle's method of focusing on the primary or focal meaning (pros hen) of a concept. -- The examples illustrate that courage and cowardice must be assessed relative to the appropriate domain of danger. -- The observation that some war-cowards are financially generous shows that virtues are domain-specific and do not automatically transfer.

D: Original: Now death is the most terrible of all things; for it is the end, and nothing is thought to be any longer either good or bad for the dead. But the brave man would not seem to be concerned even with death in all circumstances, e.g. at sea or in disease. In what circumstances, then? Surely in the noblest. Now such deaths are those in battle; for these take place in the greatest and noblest danger. And these are correspondingly honoured in city-states and at the courts of monarchs. Properly, then, he will be called brave who is fearless in face of a noble death, and of all emergencies that involve death; and the emergencies of war are in the highest degree of this kind.
D: Refashioned: Now death is the most terrible of all things, for it is the end, and nothing is thought to be either good or bad for the dead any longer. But the courageous person would not seem to be concerned with death in every circumstance -- for instance, death at sea or from disease. In what circumstances, then? Surely in the noblest. Deaths in battle are of this kind, for they occur in the greatest and most noble danger. And these are correspondingly honored in city-states and by monarchs. Properly speaking, then, the person called courageous is the one who is fearless in the face of a noble death, and of all emergencies that involve death -- and the emergencies of war are of this kind in the highest degree.
D: Elegant: Death is the most terrible thing -- it is the absolute end, beyond which nothing is good or bad. Yet courage does not concern death in every form, only in the noblest circumstances. Death in battle is the paradigm case: it occurs in the greatest danger and receives the highest public honors. The truly courageous person is fearless before a noble death, and war presents the supreme occasion for this.
D: Summary: Death is the ultimate terror, but courage specifically concerns death faced nobly -- paradigmatically, death in battle. This is the most honored form of facing mortality, recognized by states and rulers alike, because it involves the greatest danger undertaken for the noblest purpose.
D: Distilled: True courage faces death in its noblest form -- supremely in battle -- where the greatest danger is met for the sake of honor and the common good.
Notes: The claim that 'nothing is thought to be either good or bad for the dead' reflects a common Greek view but stands in tension with Aristotle's own discussion of posthumous reputation in Book I.10-11. -- The emphasis on death in battle as the paradigm of courage reflects the values of the Greek polis, where military service was a primary civic duty. -- This martial focus has drawn criticism from later commentators who note it excludes moral courage, civil courage, and other forms of bravery. -- The linkage of courage to nobility (to kalon) is characteristic: Aristotle consistently holds that virtuous action is motivated by the noble.

D: Original: Yet at sea also, and in disease, the brave man is fearless, but not in the same way as the seaman; for he has given up hope of safety, and is disliking the thought of death in this shape, while they are hopeful because of their experience. At the same time, we show courage in situations where there is the opportunity of showing prowess or where death is noble; but in these forms of death neither of these conditions is fulfilled.
D: Refashioned: At sea and in disease, too, the courageous person is fearless -- but not in the same way as the sailor. For the courageous person has given up hope of being saved and is distressed at the thought of dying in such a manner, while experienced sailors remain hopeful because of their expertise. Moreover, we display courage in situations where there is an opportunity to show prowess or where death is noble. But in deaths at sea or from disease, neither of these conditions is met.
D: Elegant: The courageous person faces death at sea or in illness without fear, but differently from the experienced sailor. The brave person has abandoned hope of survival and is pained at dying ingloriously, while the sailor is hopeful through experience. Courage proper requires the opportunity for prowess and a noble death -- conditions absent in storms and sickness.
D: Summary: The courageous person and the experienced sailor both face death at sea without fear, but for different reasons: the sailor from professional confidence, the courageous person from strength of character despite having no hope. True courage requires an occasion for noble action, which death at sea or from disease does not provide.
D: Distilled: True courage is distinguished from mere professional fearlessness by its orientation toward noble action -- the courageous person faces death resolutely even without hope, while the expert merely draws on experience.
Notes: The distinction between courage and professional confidence (empeiria) is developed further in the discussion of 'types of courage' in Chapter 8. -- The requirement that courage involve an opportunity for prowess (aristeia) reflects the Greek heroic tradition. -- This passage raises important questions about whether Aristotle's account is too narrow: modern virtue ethicists debate whether facing terminal illness with dignity counts as courage on Aristotle's terms.

D: Original: What is terrible is not the same for all men; but we say there are things terrible even beyond human strength. These, then, are terrible to every one- at least to every sensible man; but the terrible things that are not beyond human strength differ in magnitude and degree, and so too do the things that inspire confidence. Now the brave man is as dauntless as man may be. Therefore, while he will fear even the things that are not beyond human strength, he will face them as he ought and as the rule directs, for honour's sake; for this is the end of virtue. But it is possible to fear these more, or less, and again to fear things that are not terrible as if they were. Of the faults that are committed one consists in fearing what one should not, another in fearing as we should not, another in fearing when we should not, and so on; and so too with respect to the things that inspire confidence. The man, then, who faces and who fears the right things and from the right motive, in the right way and from the right time, and who feels confidence under the corresponding conditions, is brave; for the brave man feels and acts according to the merits of the case and in whatever way the rule directs. Now the end of every activity is conformity to the corresponding state of character. This is true, therefore, of the brave man as well as of others. But courage is noble. Therefore the end also is noble; for each thing is defined by its end. Therefore it is for a noble end that the brave man endures and acts as courage directs.
D: Refashioned: What is terrible is not the same for all people. But we recognize that some things are terrible beyond human capacity to endure. These are terrible to everyone -- at least to every person of sound mind. The terrible things within human capacity, however, differ in magnitude and degree, as do the things that inspire confidence. The courageous person is as fearless as a human being can be. He will therefore fear even things within human capacity, but he will face them as he ought and as reason directs, for the sake of what is noble -- for this is the goal of virtue. One can fear these things more or less than one should, and one can also fear things that are not truly terrible as if they were. The errors that occur include fearing what one should not, fearing in the wrong way, fearing at the wrong time, and so on -- and the same applies to the things that inspire confidence. The person who faces and fears the right things, from the right motive, in the right way, and at the right time, and who feels confidence under corresponding conditions, is courageous. For the courageous person feels and acts according to the merits of the case and as reason directs. The goal of every activity is conformity with the corresponding state of character. This holds for the courageous person as well. Courage is noble, and therefore its goal is noble too, since each thing is defined by its goal. It is for a noble end, then, that the courageous person endures and acts as courage directs.
D: Elegant: Some terrors exceed human endurance and frighten every sensible person; those within human capacity vary in degree. The courageous person is as fearless as humanly possible -- he fears what deserves fear but faces it rightly, as reason directs, for the sake of the noble. Errors include fearing the wrong things, in the wrong way, at the wrong time. True courage means feeling and acting rightly toward fear and confidence, according to circumstance and reason. Since courage is noble, its end is noble: the courageous person endures for nobility's sake.
D: Summary: Aristotle defines the courageous person as one who fears the right things, from the right motive, in the right way, and at the right time -- all guided by reason and aimed at what is noble. Courage is the mean between excess and deficiency in both fear and confidence, and its defining purpose is the noble.
D: Distilled: The courageous person fears and faces the right things, in the right way, at the right time, and for the right reason -- always for the sake of what is noble, which is the defining end of courage.
Notes: The 'right things, right way, right time' formula is the standard Aristotelian specification of the mean, applied here specifically to courage (cf. Book II.6, 1106b21-23). -- The identification of nobility (to kalon) as the end of courage -- and of all virtue -- is one of Aristotle's most important claims. -- 'As reason directs' points to the role of practical wisdom (phronesis) in determining the mean, discussed fully in Book VI. -- This passage establishes the paradigm that each virtue aims at the noble, which Aristotle applies throughout his treatment of the individual virtues.

D: Original: Of those who go to excess he who exceeds in fearlessness has no name (we have said previously that many states of character have no names), but he would be a sort of madman or insensible person if he feared nothing, neither earthquakes nor the waves, as they say the Celts do not; while the man who exceeds in confidence about what really is terrible is rash. The rash man, however, is also thought to be boastful and only a pretender to courage; at all events, as the brave man is with regard to what is terrible, so the rash man wishes to appear; and so he imitates him in situations where he can. Hence also most of them are a mixture of rashness and cowardice; for, while in these situations they display confidence, they do not hold their ground against what is really terrible. The man who exceeds in fear is a coward; for he fears both what he ought not and as he ought not, and all the similar characterizations attach to him. He is lacking also in confidence; but he is more conspicuous for his excess of fear in painful situations. The coward, then, is a despairing sort of person; for he fears everything. The brave man, on the other hand, has the opposite disposition; for confidence is the mark of a hopeful disposition. The coward, the rash man, and the brave man, then, are concerned with the same objects but are differently disposed towards them; for the first two exceed and fall short, while the third holds the middle, which is the right, position; and rash men are precipitate, and wish for dangers beforehand but draw back when they are in them, while brave men are keen in the moment of action, but quiet beforehand.
D: Refashioned: Among those who go to excess, the person who exceeds in fearlessness has no name (as we noted earlier, many states of character lack names), but he would be a sort of madman or insensible person if he feared nothing -- neither earthquakes nor waves, as the Celts are reportedly like. The person who exceeds in confidence regarding genuinely terrible things is rash. The rash person is also thought to be boastful and merely a pretender to courage. Just as the courageous person truly is regarding what is terrible, so the rash person wishes to appear -- and so he imitates courage in situations where he can get away with it. Most rash people are therefore a mixture of rashness and cowardice: they display confidence in easy situations but do not hold firm against what is truly terrible. The person who exceeds in fear is a coward: he fears what he should not, in ways he should not, and all similar failings attach to him. He is also deficient in confidence, though his excessive fear in painful situations is what makes him most conspicuous. The coward is a despairing sort of person, for he fears everything. The courageous person, by contrast, has the opposite disposition, for confidence is the mark of a hopeful character. The coward, the rash person, and the courageous person are all concerned with the same objects but are disposed toward them differently: the first two exhibit excess and deficiency, while the third holds the middle and correct position. Rash people are impetuous -- eager for dangers beforehand but shrinking from them in the moment -- while courageous people are calm beforehand but keen in the moment of action.
D: Elegant: The person fearless of everything is a nameless madman -- like the Celts who reportedly fear neither earthquake nor flood. The rash person is overconfident about real dangers, boastful, and a mere pretender to courage who imitates it only when stakes are low. Most rash people are actually rash-coward hybrids: bold in easy situations, fleeing real danger. The coward fears excessively and lacks confidence -- a despairing type who fears everything. The courageous person is hopeful. All three face the same objects differently: excess and deficiency versus the correct mean. The rash are eager before danger but shrink in the moment; the courageous are calm before but resolute during.
