Notes: The distinction between justified and random disdain is crucial: magnanimity is not simple arrogance but an accurate response to one's genuine standing. -- The claim that fortune's goods without virtue make people insolent (hubristēs) connects to the Greek concept of hubris, the arrogance that invites divine punishment in tragedy. -- This analysis resonates with Plato's portrayal of the tyrannical soul in Republic VIII-IX, where the person with power but without virtue becomes the worst of all. -- The observation that the many 'imitate' the magnanimous person prefigures later discussions of inauthenticity in moral character.

D: Original: He does not run into trifling dangers, nor is he fond of danger, because he honours few things; but he will face great dangers, and when he is in danger he is unsparing of his life, knowing that there are conditions on which life is not worth having. And he is the sort of man to confer benefits, but he is ashamed of receiving them; for the one is the mark of a superior, the other of an inferior. And he is apt to confer greater benefits in return; for thus the original benefactor besides being paid will incur a debt to him, and will be the gainer by the transaction. They seem also to remember any service they have done, but not those they have received (for he who receives a service is inferior to him who has done it, but the proud man wishes to be superior), and to hear of the former with pleasure, of the latter with displeasure; this, it seems, is why Thetis did not mention to Zeus the services she had done him, and why the Spartans did not recount their services to the Athenians, but those they had received. It is a mark of the proud man also to ask for nothing or scarcely anything, but to give help readily, and to be dignified towards people who enjoy high position and good fortune, but unassuming towards those of the middle class; for it is a difficult and lofty thing to be superior to the former, but easy to be so to the latter, and a lofty bearing over the former is no mark of ill-breeding, but among humble people it is as vulgar as a display of strength against the weak. Again, it is characteristic of the proud man not to aim at the things commonly held in honour, or the things in which others excel; to be sluggish and to hold back except where great honour or a great work is at stake, and to be a man of few deeds, but of great and notable ones. He must also be open in his hate and in his love (for to conceal one's feelings, i.e. to care less for truth than for what people will think, is a coward's part), and must speak and act openly; for he is free of speech because he is contemptuous, and he is given to telling the truth, except when he speaks in irony to the vulgar. He must be unable to make his life revolve round another, unless it be a friend; for this is slavish, and for this reason all flatterers are servile and people lacking in self-respect are flatterers.
D: Refashioned:
The magnanimous person does not rush into minor dangers or seek danger for its own sake, because they value few things. But they will face great dangers, and in danger they are unsparing of their life, knowing that there are conditions under which life is not worth living.

They are the sort of person who confers benefits but is ashamed to receive them, for conferring benefits is the mark of a superior, while receiving them marks an inferior. They tend to return benefits in greater measure, so that the original benefactor ends up indebted to them and becomes the one who has gained from the exchange.

Magnanimous people seem to remember the services they have performed but not those they have received -- for the recipient of a service is inferior to the one who performed it, and the magnanimous person wishes to be the superior party. They hear of their own services with pleasure and those done for them with displeasure. This, it seems, is why Thetis did not mention to Zeus the services she had done him, and why the Spartans recounted to the Athenians not the services they had rendered but only those they had received.

It is also characteristic of the magnanimous person to ask for nothing or hardly anything, but to give help readily. They are dignified toward people of high position and good fortune, but unassuming toward those of the middle class. For it is difficult and therefore impressive to be superior to the former, but easy to be superior to the latter -- and bearing oneself loftily over the great is no sign of bad breeding, while doing so among humble people is as vulgar as making a show of strength against the weak.

The magnanimous person does not pursue the things commonly valued or the areas where others excel. They are unhurried and hold back except when great honour or a great achievement is at stake, undertaking few actions but those of great significance and note.

They must also be open in their hatreds and their loves, for concealing one's feelings -- caring more for what others think than for truth -- is a coward's part. They speak and act openly, being free in speech because they are contemptuous of the opinion of others, and they tell the truth, except when they speak with irony to the common crowd.

They cannot arrange their life around another person, unless that person is a friend, for doing so is slavish -- and this is why all flatterers are servile, and all people lacking in self-respect are flatterers.
D: Elegant: The magnanimous person faces great dangers willingly -- knowing that some conditions make life not worth living -- but ignores trivial ones. They confer benefits readily but are ashamed to receive them, always repaying more than they received so as to remain the superior party. They remember services given, not received -- which is why Thetis did not remind Zeus of her aid, and the Spartans cited favours received, not given. They ask for nothing, help readily, and are dignified toward the great but relaxed with ordinary people. They pursue only great deeds, are open in love and hatred, speak freely and truthfully (using irony only with the common crowd), and refuse to organize their life around anyone but a friend -- since dependence is slavish.
D: Summary: The magnanimous person faces great dangers freely, confers benefits generously while avoiding receiving them, pursues only significant achievements, and lives with radical openness and independence. They are dignified toward equals but gentle with inferiors, and they refuse all flattery and dependence.
D: Distilled: The magnanimous person faces great dangers willingly, gives more than they receive, speaks with radical honesty, and refuses to subordinate their life to anyone.
Notes: Thetis, the sea-goddess and mother of Achilles, helped Zeus in the Iliad (I.396-406) by summoning the hundred-handed giant Briareus to protect him, yet at Iliad I.503ff she asks for a favour without mentioning past services. -- The Spartan reference is to diplomatic exchanges between Sparta and Athens; Spartan rhetorical modesty was proverbial. -- Aristotle's endorsement of irony (eirōneia) toward the vulgar connects to his discussion of truthfulness in NE IV.7 and to the Socratic tradition: Socrates was famous for irony. -- The claim that life is not worth living under certain conditions connects to NE III.1's discussion of what is voluntary under duress. -- The condemnation of flattery anticipates the fuller treatment in NE IV.6. -- This is the longest and most detailed portrait of the magnanimous person, amounting to an ideal of aristocratic virtue that profoundly influenced Western ethical thought.

D: Original: Nor is he given to admiration; for nothing to him is great. Nor is he mindful of wrongs; for it is not the part of a proud man to have a long memory, especially for wrongs, but rather to overlook them. Nor is he a gossip; for he will speak neither about himself nor about another, since he cares not to be praised nor for others to be blamed; nor again is he given to praise; and for the same reason he is not an evil-speaker, even about his enemies, except from haughtiness. With regard to necessary or small matters he is least of all me given to lamentation or the asking of favours; for it is the part of one who takes such matters seriously to behave so with respect to them. He is one who will possess beautiful and profitless things rather than profitable and useful ones; for this is more proper to a character that suffices to itself.
D: Refashioned:
The magnanimous person is not easily impressed, for nothing seems great to them. They do not dwell on past wrongs, for it is beneath a magnanimous person to carry grudges -- they would rather overlook them. They are not a gossip, for they will speak neither about themselves nor about others, caring neither for their own praise nor for the blame of others. For the same reason, they are not given to praising others. And they do not speak ill of people, not even their enemies -- unless from a position of deliberate superiority.

Regarding matters of necessity or triviality, they are the last person to complain or to ask for favours, for it is the mark of someone who takes such things seriously to behave that way about them.

They are the type of person who will possess beautiful and unprofitable things rather than profitable and useful ones, for this is more fitting to a character that is self-sufficient.
D: Elegant: Nothing seems great to the magnanimous person, so they are not easily impressed. They do not hold grudges but overlook wrongs; they neither gossip, nor praise, nor blame -- not even their enemies, except from deliberate superiority. They never complain about necessities or ask favours over trifles. They prefer beautiful but unprofitable possessions over useful ones, befitting a self-sufficient character.
D: Summary: The magnanimous person is difficult to impress, quick to forgive, and indifferent to gossip and petty complaints. They prefer what is beautiful to what is merely useful, reflecting their fundamental self-sufficiency.
D: Distilled: The magnanimous person overlooks wrongs, avoids gossip and complaint, and values beauty over utility, reflecting a character that is fundamentally self-sufficient.
Notes: The preference for 'beautiful and unprofitable things' over 'profitable and useful ones' encapsulates a core aristocratic Greek value: the truly free person transcends mere utility. -- The magnanimous person's reluctance to hold grudges contrasts with the common Greek endorsement of revenge and with the 'help friends, harm enemies' ethic visible in Homer and the tragedians. -- The self-sufficiency (autarkeia) mentioned here connects to Aristotle's account of happiness as self-sufficient in NE I.7, and to his claim that the contemplative life is the most self-sufficient in NE X.7. -- The portrait of someone who does not gossip, praise, or blame reflects an ideal of emotional and social independence rarely achieved in practice.

D: Original:
Further, a slow step is thought proper to the proud man, a deep voice, and a level utterance; for the man who takes few things seriously is not likely to be hurried, nor the man who thinks nothing great to be excited, while a shrill voice and a rapid gait are the results of hurry and excitement.

Such, then, is the proud man; the man who falls short of him is unduly humble, and the man who goes beyond him is vain. Now even these are not thought to be bad (for they are not malicious), but only mistaken. For the unduly humble man, being worthy of good things, robs himself of what he deserves, and to have something bad about him from the fact that he does not think himself worthy of good things, and seems also not to know himself; else he would have desired the things he was worthy of, since these were good. Yet such people are not thought to be fools, but rather unduly retiring. Such a reputation, however, seems actually to make them worse; for each class of people aims at what corresponds to its worth, and these people stand back even from noble actions and undertakings, deeming themselves unworthy, and from external goods no less. Vain people, on the other hand, are fools and ignorant of themselves, and that manifestly; for, not being worthy of them, they attempt honourable undertakings, and then are found out; and tetadorn themselves with clothing and outward show and such things, and wish their strokes of good fortune to be made public, and speak about them as if they would be honoured for them. But undue humility is more opposed to pride than vanity is; for it is both commoner and worse.

Pride, then, is concerned with honour on the grand scale, as has been said.
D: Refashioned:
Further, a slow step is thought fitting for the magnanimous person, along with a deep voice and a steady manner of speaking. For someone who takes few things seriously is not likely to be hurried, and someone who considers nothing truly great is not likely to become agitated. A shrill voice and a hurried walk are products of urgency and excitement.

Such, then, is the magnanimous person. The one who falls short is unduly humble, and the one who goes beyond is vain. Now even these are not considered bad people -- for they are not malicious -- but merely mistaken.

The unduly humble person, being worthy of good things, deprives themselves of what they deserve. They seem to have something defective about them, stemming from their failure to think themselves worthy of what is good. They appear not to know themselves, for otherwise they would have desired the things they deserved, since these were genuine goods. Yet such people are not considered foolish, but rather excessively retiring. However, this very reputation tends to make them worse, since each kind of person aims at what corresponds to their sense of their own worth, and these people hold back even from noble actions and undertakings, considering themselves unworthy, and from external goods no less.

Vain people, on the other hand, are foolish and ignorant of themselves -- and obviously so. Not being worthy of them, they attempt honourable undertakings and are then exposed. They adorn themselves with fine clothing and outward display, and wish their good fortune to be publicly known, boasting about it as though it would win them honour.

But undue humility is more opposed to magnanimity than vanity is, for it is both more common and worse.

Magnanimity, then, is concerned with honour on the grand scale, as we have said.
D: Elegant: A slow step, deep voice, and steady speech befit the magnanimous person -- one who is neither hurried nor agitated because few things seem important. The unduly humble fall short: being worthy of good things, they deprive themselves through self-underestimation and hold back from noble actions, growing worse as their retiring nature becomes habitual. The vain go to excess: foolish and self-ignorant, they attempt honours beyond them and are exposed, adorning themselves with outward show. Undue humility is more opposed to magnanimity than vanity, being both commoner and worse.
D: Summary: The magnanimous person's composure -- slow step, deep voice, steady speech -- reflects their inner disposition. Undue humility is worse than vanity because it causes worthy people to deprive themselves of deserved goods and withdraw from noble action, creating a self-reinforcing cycle of diminishment.
D: Distilled: Undue humility is worse than vanity because it causes the worthy to deprive themselves of what they deserve, creating a self-reinforcing withdrawal from noble action.
Notes: The physical description of the magnanimous person (slow gait, deep voice, level speech) shows Aristotle's characteristic attention to the bodily expression of character states; compare his Physiognomics and his remarks on emotional expression in the Rhetoric. -- The claim that undue humility is self-reinforcing anticipates modern psychological concepts of self-fulfilling prophecy and imposter syndrome. -- The judgment that undue humility is 'more opposed' to magnanimity than vanity follows the pattern identified in NE II.8: the deficiency is often more contrary to the virtue than the excess, though here (unusually) the deficiency of self-assessment is considered worse. -- The vain person's love of outward show connects to broader Greek critiques of appearance versus reality, a theme central to Plato's Republic.

D: Original: There seems to be in the sphere of honour also, as was said in our first remarks on the subject, a virtue which would appear to be related to pride as liberality is to magnificence. For neither of these has anything to do with the grand scale, but both dispose us as is right with regard to middling and unimportant objects; as in getting and giving of wealth there is a mean and an excess and defect, so too honour may be desired more than is right, or less, or from the right sources and in the right way. We blame both the ambitious man as am at honour more than is right and from wrong sources, and the unambitious man as not willing to be honoured even for noble reasons. But sometimes we praise the ambitious man as being manly and a lover of what is noble, and the unambitious man as being moderate and self-controlled, as we said in our first treatment of the subject. Evidently, since 'fond of such and such an object' has more than one meaning, we do not assign the term 'ambition' or 'love of honour' always to the same thing, but when we praise the quality we think of the man who loves honour more than most people, and when we blame it we think of him who loves it more than is right. The mean being without a name, the extremes seem to dispute for its place as though that were vacant by default. But where there is excess and defect, there is also an intermediate; now men desire honour both more than they should and less; therefore it is possible also to do so as one should; at all events this is the state of character that is praised, being an unnamed mean in respect of honour. Relatively to ambition it seems to be unambitiousness, and relatively to unambitiousness it seems to be ambition, while relatively to both severally it seems in a sense to be both together. This appears to be true of the other virtues also. But in this case the extremes seem to be contradictories because the mean has not received a name.
D: Refashioned:
There seems to be a virtue in the sphere of honour that relates to magnanimity as generosity relates to magnificence. As we noted in our earlier remarks on the subject, neither this virtue nor generosity has anything to do with the grand scale, but both dispose us rightly with regard to moderate and everyday matters. Just as in the getting and giving of wealth there is a mean, an excess, and a deficiency, so too honour may be desired more than is right, or less, or from the right sources and in the right way.

We blame both the ambitious person -- as someone who seeks honour more than is right and from wrong sources -- and the unambitious person, who is unwilling to be honoured even for noble reasons. But sometimes we praise the ambitious person as spirited and a lover of what is noble, and the unambitious person as moderate and self-controlled, as we said in our earlier treatment.

Clearly, since 'fond of honour' has more than one meaning, we do not always apply the term 'ambitious' to the same thing. When we praise it, we are thinking of someone who loves honour more than most people do. When we blame it, we are thinking of someone who loves it more than is right.

Since this mean has no name of its own, the extremes seem to compete for its place, as if the territory were vacant. But where there is excess and deficiency, there is also an intermediate. People desire honour both more and less than they should, so it must be possible to desire it as one should. This nameless mean state is what is praised -- appearing as unambitiousness relative to ambition, and as ambition relative to unambitiousness, and in a sense as both together relative to each extreme separately.

This appears to be true of the other virtues as well, but in this case the extremes seem like direct contradictories only because the mean has not received a name.
D: Elegant: As generosity relates to magnificence, so this unnamed virtue relates to magnanimity: both govern moderate matters rather than grand ones. In seeking honour, there is a mean between excessive ambition and insufficient ambition. We praise ambition when it means spirited pursuit of noble recognition, and blame it when honour is sought excessively or from wrong sources. Because the mean has no name, the extremes appear to be direct opposites competing for the vacant middle ground. But wherever excess and deficiency exist, an intermediate must exist too.
D: Summary: Just as generosity is the everyday counterpart of magnificence, there is an unnamed virtue that is the everyday counterpart of magnanimity. It governs the moderate pursuit of honour. Because this mean has no name, people oscillate between praising and blaming both ambition and unambitiousness depending on context.
D: Distilled: The unnamed virtue of properly measured ambition governs the everyday pursuit of honour, just as generosity governs everyday matters of wealth.
Notes: The parallel between unnamed honour-virtue/magnanimity and generosity/magnificence creates a systematic structure: each domain (wealth, honour) has both an everyday and a grand-scale virtue. -- The problem of the unnamed mean illustrates a genuine difficulty in Aristotle's ethical theory: not every virtue corresponds to a recognized term in ordinary Greek. He notes this problem at NE II.7 as well. -- The observation that the same quality can be praised or blamed depending on context prefigures later discussions of the context-dependence of moral evaluation. -- 'Our first remarks' refers back to NE II.7 (1107b30-1108a1), where Aristotle briefly catalogues virtues including this unnamed one.

D: Original: Good temper is a mean with respect to anger; the middle state being unnamed, and the extremes almost without a name as well, we place good temper in the middle position, though it inclines towards the deficiency, which is without a name. The excess might called a sort of 'irascibility'. For the passion is anger, while its causes are many and diverse.
D: Refashioned: Patience is a mean with respect to anger. The middle state has no established name, and the extremes are almost nameless as well. We place patience in the middle position, though it inclines toward the deficiency, which also lacks a name. The excess might be called a kind of 'irascibility.' For the underlying feeling is anger, while the things that provoke it are many and varied.
D: Elegant: Patience is the mean regarding anger, though neither the mean nor the extremes have established names. The mean inclines toward deficiency. The excess could be called irascibility. The feeling involved is anger, and its provocations are many.
D: Summary: Patience is the virtue governing anger, positioned as a mean that leans toward under-reaction rather than over-reaction. The excess is irascibility, and anger has many diverse causes.
D: Distilled: Patience is the mean regarding anger, leaning toward restraint rather than excess, in a domain where provocations are many and varied.
Notes: The Greek term praotēs is variously translated as 'patience,' 'gentleness,' 'mildness,' or 'good temper.' It connotes a composed disposition rather than mere passivity. -- Aristotle's acknowledgment that the terminology is inadequate (the mean and both extremes nearly lack names) is methodologically important: it shows that his ethical framework sometimes outstrips ordinary language. -- The observation that the mean 'inclines towards the deficiency' illustrates the principle from NE II.8 that the mean is not always equidistant from the extremes. -- The claim that anger has 'many and diverse' causes sets up the detailed analysis of varieties of anger that follows.

D: Original: The man who is angry at the right things and with the right people, and, further, as he ought, when he ought, and as long as he ought, is praised. This will be the good-tempered man, then, since good temper is praised. For the good-tempered man tends to be unperturbed and not to be led by passion, but to be angry in the manner, at the things, and for the length of time, that the rule dictates; but he is thought to err rather in the direction of deficiency; for the good-tempered man is not revengeful, but rather tends to make allowances.
D: Refashioned:
The person who is angry at the right things and with the right people, and further in the right way, at the right time, and for the right duration, is praised. This, then, will be the patient person, since patience is what we praise.

The patient person tends to be unperturbed, not led by passion, but angry in the manner, at the things, and for the length of time that right reason prescribes. However, they are thought to err rather on the side of deficiency, for the patient person is not vengeful but tends instead toward making allowances.
D: Elegant: The patient person is angry at the right things, with the right people, in the right way, at the right time, and for the right duration. They are unperturbed and governed by reason rather than passion, though they err on the side of deficiency -- being inclined to make allowances rather than seek revenge.
D: Summary: Patience means being angry only when appropriate -- at the right things, people, time, manner, and duration. The patient person is guided by reason rather than passion and tends to be forgiving rather than vengeful.
D: Distilled: The patient person gets angry only when, at whom, and for as long as right reason demands, erring if anything on the side of forgiveness.
Notes: The specification of anger as 'right things, right people, right way, right time, right duration' is the most detailed application of the mean formula in the Ethics, showing the multiple dimensions along which anger can go wrong. -- 'Right reason' (orthos logos) is the standard of practical wisdom (phronesis) discussed in NE VI; the practically wise person determines the mean in each case. -- The tendency to err toward deficiency parallels the claim in NE II.9 that we should aim toward the extreme that is less opposed to the mean. -- The opposition between vengefulness and making allowances connects to broader Greek debates about justice: the tragic tradition often explores the destructive consequences of vengeful anger.

D: Original: The deficiency, whether it is a sort of 'inirascibility' or whatever it is, is blamed. For those who are not angry at the things they should be angry at are thought to be fools, and so are those who are not angry in the right way, at the right time, or with the right persons; for such a man is thought not to feel things nor to be pained by them, and, since he does not get angry, he is thought unlikely to defend himself; and to endure being insulted and put up with insult to one's friends is slavish.
D: Refashioned:
The deficiency -- whether we call it a kind of 'unirascibility' or something else -- is blamed. For people who fail to get angry at the things they should be angry at are considered foolish. So are those who fail to get angry in the right way, at the right time, or with the right people. Such a person is thought not to feel things or to be pained by them. Since they never get angry, they seem unlikely to defend themselves.

To endure being insulted, and to tolerate insults directed at one's friends, is slavish.
D: Elegant: The deficiency -- a failure to feel anger when appropriate -- is blamed. Those who are not angry at what they should be, or not in the right way or at the right time, are considered foolish and insensible. They seem unlikely to defend themselves. Enduring insults to oneself or one's friends without anger is slavish.
D: Summary: Failing to get angry when anger is warranted is a vice, not a virtue. People who never get angry appear insensible and incapable of self-defense. Tolerating insults to oneself or one's friends is a form of servility.
D: Distilled: The inability to feel anger when it is warranted is not virtue but a slavish insensibility that leaves oneself and one's friends undefended.
Notes: The condemnation of excessive passivity is notable in a chapter often read as endorsing patience: Aristotle insists that the capacity for anger is morally necessary. -- The characterization of excessive tolerance as 'slavish' (andrapodōdes) reflects the Greek association of servility with the condition of slavery, a deeply stigmatized state. -- This passage is often cited in discussions of whether pacifism is morally admirable or morally deficient by Aristotelian standards. -- The duty to defend one's friends anticipates the treatment of friendship obligations in NE VIII-IX.

D: Original: The excess can be manifested in all the points that have been named (for one can be angry with the wrong persons, at the wrong things, more than is right, too quickly, or too long); yet all are not found in the same person. Indeed they could not; for evil destroys even itself, and if it is complete becomes unbearable. Now hot-tempered people get angry quickly and with the wrong persons and at the wrong things and more than is right, but their anger ceases quickly-which is the best point about them. This happens to them because they do not restrain their anger but retaliate openly owing to their quickness of temper, and then their anger ceases. By reason of excess choleric people are quick-tempered and ready to be angry with everything and on every occasion; whence their name. Sulky people are hard to appease, and retain their anger long; for they repress their passion. But it ceases when they retaliate; for revenge relieves them of their anger, producing in them pleasure instead of pain. If this does not happen they retain their burden; for owing to its not being obvious no one even reasons with them, and to digest one's anger in oneself takes time. Such people are most troublesome to themselves and to their dearest friends. We call had-tempered those who are angry at the wrong things, more than is right, and longer, and cannot be appeased until they inflict vengeance or punishment.
D: Refashioned:
The excess can manifest in every dimension mentioned: one can be angry with the wrong people, at the wrong things, more than is appropriate, too quickly, or for too long. Yet all these errors are not found in the same person -- indeed they could not be, for complete evil destroys even itself and becomes unbearable.

Hot-tempered people get angry quickly and with the wrong people and at the wrong things and more than is appropriate, but their anger subsides quickly -- which is the best thing about them. This happens because they do not restrain their anger but retaliate openly, thanks to their quickness of temper, and then the anger passes.

Choleric people, by contrast, are excessively quick-tempered and fly into a rage at everything and on every occasion -- hence their name.

Sulky people are hard to appease and hold onto their anger for a long time, because they repress it. The anger subsides only when they retaliate, for revenge relieves them by producing pleasure in place of pain. Without retaliation, they carry the burden indefinitely. Because their anger is not visible, no one thinks to reason with them, and working through anger internally takes a long time. Such people are the most troublesome -- to themselves and to those closest to them.

We call 'bad-tempered' those who are angry at the wrong things, more than is right, for longer than is right, and who cannot be appeased until they have inflicted retribution or punishment.
D: Elegant: Excessive anger manifests in multiple ways: wrong targets, wrong causes, disproportionate intensity, excessive speed, and excessive duration -- though all cannot coexist, since complete vice destroys itself. Hot-tempered people anger quickly but recover fast, since they retaliate openly and the anger passes. Choleric people rage at everything on every occasion. Sulky people suppress anger, holding it until revenge produces pleasure in place of pain; without retaliation, they carry the burden silently and endlessly, tormenting themselves and those closest to them. Bad-tempered people are angry at wrong things, too intensely, too long, and cannot rest until they have punished.
D: Summary: Excessive anger takes many forms: the hot-tempered flare up quickly but recover; the choleric rage indiscriminately; the sulky suppress their anger until revenge, tormenting themselves and others in the interim; the bad-tempered cannot be appeased without retribution.
D: Distilled: Excessive anger takes multiple forms -- quick but passing, indiscriminate, sulkily repressed, or vindictively sustained -- each destructive in its own way.
Notes: The taxonomy of anger types shows Aristotle's empirical, observational approach to ethics, resembling psychological classification. -- The claim that 'complete evil destroys itself' is a significant metaphysical principle connecting to Aristotle's view that evil is parasitic on good (compare Metaphysics IX.9). -- The distinction between those who express anger openly and those who repress it anticipates modern psychological distinctions between externalized and internalized anger. -- 'Choleric' (akrocholoi) literally means 'sharp-biled,' reflecting the Greek humoral theory that connected bile (cholē) to anger -- a connection developed further in Aristotle's biological works and later by Galen. -- The analysis of sulky people's self-torment through repressed anger has been noted as a precursor to psychoanalytic concepts.

D: Original:
To good temper we oppose the excess rather than the defect; for not only is it commoner since revenge is the more human), but bad-tempered people are worse to live with.

What we have said in our earlier treatment of the subject is plain also from what we are now saying; viz. that it is not easy to define how, with whom, at what, and how long one should be angry, and at what point right action ceases and wrong begins. For the man who strays a little from the path, either towards the more or towards the less, is not blamed; since sometimes we praise those who exhibit the deficiency, and call them good-tempered, and sometimes we call angry people manly, as being capable of ruling. How far, therefore, and how a man must stray before he becomes blameworthy, it is not easy to state in words; for the decision depends on the particular facts and on perception. But so much at least is plain, that the middle state is praiseworthy- that in virtue of which we are angry with the right people, at the right things, in the right way, and so on, while the excesses and defects are blameworthy- slightly so if they are present in a low degree, more if in a higher degree, and very much if in a high degree. Evidently, then, we must cling to the middle state.- Enough of the states relative to anger.
D: Refashioned:
We treat the excess as more opposed to patience than the deficiency, for several reasons: not only is excessive anger more common -- since the desire for revenge is more natural to humans -- but bad-tempered people are also harder to live with.

What we said in our earlier treatment of the subject is confirmed by our present analysis: it is not easy to define precisely how, with whom, at what, and for how long one should be angry, nor to determine the exact point where right action ends and wrong begins. Someone who strays a little from the ideal -- whether toward excess or deficiency -- is not blamed. Sometimes we even praise those who show the deficiency, calling them patient, and sometimes we call angry people spirited, as being capable of leadership.

How far and in what way a person must deviate before becoming blameworthy is not easy to state in a general rule, for the judgment depends on particular circumstances and on perception. But this much at least is clear: the middle state is praiseworthy -- the state in which we are angry with the right people, at the right things, in the right way, and so on -- while excesses and deficiencies are blameworthy. They are slightly blameworthy when small, more so when greater, and very much when extreme.

Evidently, then, we must hold to the middle state. Enough of the dispositions relating to anger.
D: Elegant: Excessive anger is more opposed to patience than deficiency, being both more common (revenge is more human) and harder to live with. Precisely defining the right measure of anger -- how, with whom, at what, for how long -- is impossible in general terms; the judgment depends on particular facts and perception. Sometimes we praise those who show deficiency as patient, and sometimes those who show excess as spirited. But the middle state is clearly praiseworthy: being angry at the right things, with the right people, in the right way. Deviations are blameworthy in proportion to their degree.
D: Summary: Excessive anger is more opposed to patience than insufficient anger, being both more natural and more socially destructive. The exact boundaries of appropriate anger cannot be stated as a universal rule but depend on perception of particular circumstances. The mean remains clearly praiseworthy.
D: Distilled: The right measure of anger cannot be captured in a general rule but depends on the perception of particular circumstances; the mean is always praiseworthy.
Notes: The claim that the decision 'depends on the particular facts and on perception' is one of the most important methodological statements in the Ethics, affirming the role of practical wisdom (phronesis) and moral perception (aisthēsis) over universal rules -- see NE II.2, II.9, and VI.8. -- 'Our earlier treatment' refers to NE II.7 (1108a4-9). -- The claim that revenge is 'more human' (anthrōpikōteron) acknowledges a natural tendency without endorsing it. -- The recognition that we sometimes praise both extremes depending on context shows the flexibility and context-sensitivity of Aristotle's ethical framework. -- This concluding section mirrors the structure of NE II.9, where Aristotle offers practical advice about aiming toward the less opposed extreme.

D: Original: In gatherings of men, in social life and the interchange of words and deeds, some men are thought to be obsequious, viz. those who to give pleasure praise everything and never oppose, but think it their duty 'to give no pain to the people they meet'; while those who, on the contrary, oppose everything and care not a whit about giving pain are called churlish and contentious. That the states we have named are culpable is plain enough, and that the middle state is laudable- that in virtue of which a man will put up with, and will resent, the right things and in the right way; but no name has been assigned to it, though it most resembles friendship.
D: Refashioned:
In social gatherings, in daily life, and in the exchange of words and actions, some people are thought to be obsequious. These are the ones who praise everything to give pleasure and never raise objections, thinking it their duty to cause no pain to anyone they meet. Others, by contrast, oppose everything and care nothing about causing pain -- these are called churlish and contentious.

It is plain enough that both these dispositions are blameworthy, and that the middle state is praiseworthy -- the one in which a person accepts and objects to the right things in the right way. But this middle state has no name of its own, though it most closely resembles friendliness.
D: Elegant: In social life, the obsequious agree with everything to avoid giving pain, while the churlish oppose everything with no concern for others' feelings. Both are blameworthy. The praiseworthy mean -- accepting and objecting to the right things in the right way -- has no name, though it most resembles friendliness.
D: Summary: Social virtue lies between obsequiousness (agreeing with everything to please) and churlishness (opposing everything without concern for others). This unnamed mean most resembles friendliness.
D: Distilled: The unnamed social virtue between obsequious agreement and churlish contentiousness most closely resembles friendliness.
Notes: This unnamed virtue is sometimes called 'friendliness' by translators, though Aristotle explicitly distinguishes it from actual friendship (philia), which he treats extensively in NE VIII-IX. -- The Greek term for 'obsequious' (areskos) connotes someone who tries to be pleasing in a morally servile way. -- 'Churlish' (dyskolos/dyseris) suggests someone who is quarrelsome and difficult to be around. -- The social virtues treated in NE IV.6-8 (friendliness, truthfulness, wit) form a group dealing with interpersonal pleasantness, distinct from the virtues concerning wealth and honour treated earlier.

D: Original: For the man who corresponds to this middle state is very much what, with affection added, we call a good friend. But the state in question differs from friendship in that it implies no passion or affection for one's associates; since it is not by reason of loving or hating that such a man takes everything in the right way, but by being a man of a certain kind. For he will behave so alike towards those he knows and those he does not know, towards intimates and those who are not so, except that in each of these cases he will behave as is befitting; for it is not proper to have the same care for intimates and for strangers, nor again is it the same conditions that make it right to give pain to them.
D: Refashioned:
For the person who embodies this middle state is very much like what we would call a good friend, if we added affection to the equation. But this disposition differs from friendship in that it involves no emotional attachment to one's associates. Such a person responds rightly to situations not because of love or dislike, but because of the kind of person they are.

They will behave the same way toward those they know and those they do not, toward intimates and strangers alike -- except that in each case they adjust their behavior as befits the relationship. For it is not appropriate to show the same degree of care for intimates and for strangers, nor are the conditions that make it right to cause pain the same in each case.
D: Elegant: This middle state resembles good friendship minus the affection. The person who has it responds rightly not from love or dislike but from their own character. They treat acquaintances and strangers similarly, but adjust to each relationship, since the same degree of care and the same threshold for causing pain are not appropriate for all.
D: Summary: This unnamed social virtue resembles friendship without the emotional bond. The person who possesses it treats people appropriately based on their relationship, responding rightly from character rather than personal attachment.
D: Distilled: Social virtue resembles friendship without affection: it is right conduct toward others based on character, adjusted to the nature of each relationship.
Notes: The distinction between this virtue and friendship proper is important: friendship (philia) requires mutual affection and goodwill (NE VIII.2), while this social virtue operates from character alone. -- The principle of adjusting behavior to the relationship anticipates the detailed discussion of different types of friendship in NE VIII.3-4. -- This passage illustrates Aristotle's view that virtue is a stable character disposition (hexis) that produces appropriate responses across varying circumstances, rather than a rule applied mechanically.

D: Original: Now we have said generally that he will associate with people in the right way; but it is by reference to what is honourable and expedient that he will aim at not giving pain or at contributing pleasure. For he seems to be concerned with the pleasures and pains of social life; and wherever it is not honourable, or is harmful, for him to contribute pleasure, he will refuse, and will choose rather to give pain; also if his acquiescence in another's action would bring disgrace, and that in a high degree, or injury, on that other, while his opposition brings a little pain, he will not acquiesce but will decline. He will associate differently with people in high station and with ordinary people, with closer and more distant acquaintances, and so too with regard to all other differences, rendering to each class what is befitting, and while for its own sake he chooses to contribute pleasure, and avoids the giving of pain, he will be guided by the consequences, if these are greater, i.e. honour and expediency.
D: Refashioned:
We have said in general terms that this person will associate with others in the right way. But it is by reference to what is honourable and expedient that they will aim at either not causing pain or contributing pleasure.

For this virtue seems to be concerned with the pleasures and pains of social life. Wherever contributing pleasure would be dishonourable or harmful, they will refuse and will choose instead to cause pain. If acquiescing in another's action would bring that person significant disgrace or injury, while opposing it would cause only a small amount of pain, they will not acquiesce but will decline.

They will associate differently with people of high standing and with ordinary people, with closer and more distant acquaintances, and similarly with respect to every other kind of social difference, giving to each group what is appropriate. While they naturally prefer to contribute pleasure and avoid causing pain, they will be guided by the consequences when these are significant -- that is, by what honour and expediency require.
D: Elegant: This person aims at contributing pleasure or avoiding pain in accordance with what is honourable and expedient. Where contributing pleasure would be dishonourable or harmful, they refuse. Where another's action would bring significant disgrace but opposing it causes only minor pain, they oppose. They adjust their treatment to each person's standing and relationship, preferring to give pleasure but guided by the consequences when honour and expediency demand it.
D: Summary: The socially virtuous person aims to give pleasure and avoid pain in social interactions, but they are guided by honour and expediency. They will cause minor pain to prevent serious harm, and they adjust their conduct to suit different relationships and social standings.
D: Distilled: Social virtue means giving pleasure when possible but choosing to cause minor pain when honour or the other person's genuine good requires it.
Notes: The balance between pleasantness and honesty in this account anticipates the discussion of truthfulness in NE IV.7. -- The principle that small present pains can be justified by preventing greater future harms reflects Aristotle's teleological thinking about action. -- The sensitivity to social standing and relational distance shows Aristotle's awareness of the complexity of social life -- a feature sometimes criticized as elitist but which reflects real social dynamics. -- The reference to 'honour and expediency' (to kalon kai to sympheron) pairs two of Aristotle's three fundamental categories of practical motivation (the third being pleasure).

D: Original:
For the sake of a great future pleasure, too, he will inflict small pains.

The man who attains the mean, then, is such as we have described, but has not received a name; of those who contribute pleasure, the man who aims at being pleasant with no ulterior object is obsequious, but the man who does so in order that he may get some advantage in the direction of money or the things that money buys is a flatterer; while the man who quarrels with everything is, as has been said, churlish and contentious. And the extremes seem to be contradictory to each other because the mean is without a name.
D: Refashioned:
For the sake of a great future pleasure, too, they will inflict small present pains.

The person who attains the mean, then, is as we have described, though they have not received a name. Among those who contribute pleasure, the person who aims at being pleasant with no ulterior motive is obsequious, while the person who does so to gain some advantage -- whether money or what money can buy -- is a flatterer. The person who objects to everything is, as we said, churlish and contentious.

The extremes seem to be contradictories of each other because the mean has received no name.
D: Elegant: The person who attains the mean will even inflict small present pains for the sake of great future pleasures. Though unnamed, this mean is distinct from both the obsequious (pleasant without ulterior motive) and the flatterer (pleasant for profit). The extremes appear contradictory only because the mean lacks a name.
D: Summary: The socially virtuous person will accept small present costs for greater future benefits. The obsequious person is pleasant without ulterior motive, the flatterer is pleasant for gain, and the churlish person objects to everything. The extremes appear contradictory only because the mean is unnamed.
D: Distilled: The socially virtuous person navigates between obsequious pleasantness and churlish contentiousness, unnamed but distinct from both flattery and quarrelsomeness.
Notes: The distinction between the obsequious person (pleasant without motive) and the flatterer (pleasant for profit) adds a motivational dimension to the analysis. -- The flatterer (kolax) is treated more extensively in NE IV.6 and is one of Aristotle's paradigm cases of a vice that corrupts social relationships. -- The repeated observation that unnamed means cause their extremes to appear as direct contradictories is a methodological insight: linguistic gaps can create the illusion that only two options exist where three are present. -- This pattern recurs in the virtues of ambition (4.30) and patience (4.31).

D: Original: The mean opposed to boastfulness is found in almost the same sphere; and this also is without a name. It will be no bad plan to describe these states as well; for we shall both know the facts about character better if we go through them in detail, and we shall be convinced that the virtues are means if we see this to be so in all cases. In the field of social life those who make the giving of pleasure or pain their object in associating with others have been described; let us now describe those who pursue truth or falsehood alike in words and deeds and in the claims they put forward. The boastful man, then, is thought to be apt to claim the things that bring glory, when he has not got them, or to claim more of them than he has, and the mock-modest man on the other hand to disclaim what he has or belittle it, while the man who observes the mean is one who calls a thing by its own name, being truthful both in life and in word, owning to what he has, and neither more nor less. Now each of these courses may be adopted either with or without an object. But each man speaks and acts and lives in accordance with his character, if he is not acting for some ulterior object. And falsehood is in itself mean and culpable, and truth noble and worthy of praise. Thus the truthful man is another case of a man who, being in the mean, is worthy of praise, and both forms of untruthful man are culpable, and particularly the boastful man.
D: Refashioned:
The mean opposed to boastfulness occupies nearly the same sphere, and it too is unnamed. It will be worthwhile to describe these states as well, for we will better understand character by examining them in detail, and we will be more convinced that the virtues are means if we see this confirmed in every case.

We have described those in social life who make the giving of pleasure or pain their object. Let us now describe those who pursue truth or falsehood -- in their words, their deeds, and the claims they make about themselves.

The boastful person is inclined to claim qualities that bring them glory, either when they do not possess them at all or when they possess them to a lesser degree than claimed. The self-deprecating person, by contrast, disclaims or belittles what they actually have. The person who observes the mean calls things by their true names, being truthful in both life and word, owning to what they have -- neither more nor less.

Each of these approaches may be adopted with or without a further purpose. But as a matter of character, each person speaks, acts, and lives in accordance with their disposition -- unless they are acting with some ulterior motive.

Falsehood is in itself base and blameworthy, while truth is noble and praiseworthy. Thus the truthful person, being in the mean, is worthy of praise, while both kinds of untruthful person are blameworthy -- the boastful person especially so.
D: Elegant: Truthfulness, though unnamed as a formal virtue, is the mean between boastfulness and self-deprecation. The boastful claim qualities they lack or exaggerate those they have; the self-deprecating disclaim or belittle what they possess; the truthful person owns to what they are -- neither more nor less. Each person's speech and conduct reflect their character unless driven by ulterior motives. Falsehood is inherently base; truth is inherently noble. The boastful person is the more blameworthy of the two extremes.
D: Summary: Truthfulness about oneself is a virtue -- the mean between boasting and self-deprecation. The truthful person accurately represents their qualities, while the boaster overclaims and the self-deprecator underclaims. Falsehood is inherently base, making boasting the worse vice.
D: Distilled: Truthfulness is the virtue of representing oneself accurately -- neither overclaiming like the boaster nor underclaiming like the self-deprecator.
Notes: The virtue of truthfulness (alētheia) here concerns self-presentation in everyday life, not truth in a philosophical or scientific sense. -- The Greek term for self-deprecation is eirōneia (irony), from which our word 'irony' derives. Aristotle treats it more favorably than boasting, and its association with Socrates gives it philosophical prestige. -- The claim that 'falsehood is in itself base' states a strong ethical position on lying, though Aristotle's view is more nuanced than Kant's absolute prohibition. -- This section covers the second of three social virtues (the others being friendliness and wit), all concerned with how we interact pleasantly and honestly with others.

D: Original: Let us discuss them both, but first of all the truthful man. We are not speaking of the man who keeps faith in his agreements, i.e. in the things that pertain to justice or injustice (for this would belong to another virtue), but the man who in the matters in which nothing of this sort is at stake is true both in word and in life because his character is such. But such a man would seem to be as a matter of fact equitable. For the man who loves truth, and is truthful where nothing is at stake, will still more be truthful where something is at stake; he will avoid falsehood as something base, seeing that he avoided it even for its own sake; and such a man is worthy of praise. He inclines rather to understate the truth; for this seems in better taste because exaggerations are wearisome.
D: Refashioned:
Let us discuss both types, beginning with the truthful person. We are not speaking of the person who keeps their agreements -- that concerns justice and would belong to a different virtue -- but of the person who is truthful in word and life in matters where nothing of that sort is at stake, simply because such is their character.

Such a person would seem to be equitable as a matter of fact. For someone who loves truth and is honest when nothing is at stake will be even more honest when something is. They will avoid falsehood as something base, having trained themselves to avoid it even when it costs them nothing. Such a person is worthy of praise.

The truthful person inclines rather toward understatement, for this seems to be in better taste, since exaggerations are tiresome.
D: Elegant: The truthfulness in question concerns self-presentation, not contractual fidelity (which belongs to justice). The person who is honest when nothing is at stake will be even more so when something is, avoiding falsehood as inherently base. Such a person tends toward understatement, which is in better taste than exaggeration.
D: Summary: The truthful person is honest about themselves as a matter of character, not obligation. Since they avoid falsehood even when nothing is at stake, they are even more reliable when something is. They prefer understatement to exaggeration.
D: Distilled: The person who is honest when nothing is at stake will be more honest when something is, and will prefer understatement to exaggeration.
Notes: The distinction between truthfulness as a character trait and truth-keeping as a matter of justice is important: the former concerns self-presentation, the latter contractual fidelity (treated in Book V). -- The preference for understatement connects to Aristotle's favorable treatment of eirōneia (irony/understatement) in the magnanimity chapter (4.27) and to the Greek ideal of sophrosyne (moderation/self-restraint). -- The claim that exaggerations are 'tiresome' reflects an aesthetic as well as moral judgment -- a recurring feature of Aristotle's ethical evaluations. -- 'Equitable' (epieikēs) carries a specific technical meaning in NE V.10, where it refers to a correction of strict legal justice; here it is used more loosely to mean 'fair-minded.'

D: Original: He who claims more than he has with no ulterior object is a contemptible sort of fellow (otherwise he would not have delighted in falsehood), but seems futile rather than bad; but if he does it for an object, he who does it for the sake of reputation or honour is (for a boaster) not very much to be blamed, but he who does it for money, or the things that lead to money, is an uglier character (it is not the capacity that makes the boaster, but the purpose; for it is in virtue of his state of character and by being a man of a certain kind that he is boaster); as one man is a liar because he enjoys the lie itself, and another because he desires reputation or gain. Now those who boast for the sake of reputation claim such qualities as will praise or congratulation, but those whose object is gain claim qualities which are of value to one's neighbours and one's lack of which is not easily detected, e.g. the powers of a seer, a sage, or a physician. For this reason it is such things as these that most people claim and boast about; for in them the above-mentioned qualities are found.
D: Refashioned:
The person who overclaims without any ulterior motive is a contemptible sort -- for otherwise they would not have taken delight in falsehood itself -- but they seem futile rather than truly bad. When someone overclaims for a specific purpose, the one who does so for reputation or honour is not greatly blameworthy as boasters go. But the one who does it for money or material gain is a more objectionable character.

What makes someone a boaster is not their ability to deceive but their deliberate purpose. It is their settled character and their choice to be that kind of person that makes them a boaster -- just as one person is a liar because they enjoy lying itself, while another lies because they want reputation or profit.

Those who boast for the sake of reputation claim qualities that attract praise or admiration. But those whose aim is gain claim qualities that are valuable to others and whose absence is hard to detect -- for instance, the powers of a prophet, a sage, or a physician. This is why most people who boast claim expertise in such areas, for these are the domains where the qualities just mentioned are found.
D: Elegant: The purposeless boaster is contemptible but merely futile. Among purposeful boasters, those seeking honour are less blameworthy than those seeking profit. What makes someone a boaster is not their capacity for deceit but their deliberate choice to live that way -- as some lie from enjoyment, others from desire for reputation or gain. Those who boast for reputation claim admirable qualities; those who boast for gain claim expertise that is hard to verify -- prophecy, wisdom, medicine -- which is why most boasting clusters in such domains.
D: Summary: Boasting varies in seriousness depending on its motive. Purposeless boasting is contemptible but trivial. Boasting for honour is relatively mild, but boasting for material gain is the ugliest form. Boasters for profit tend to claim hard-to-verify expertise like prophecy or medicine.
D: Distilled: What makes someone a boaster is not the capacity to deceive but the choice to live by exaggeration, with boasting for profit being worse than boasting for reputation.
Notes: The distinction between lying for its own sake and lying for an end parallels the distinction in NE VII between incontinence of desire and incontinence of spirited emotion. -- The list of hard-to-verify expertise (seer, sage, physician) reflects real social concerns in ancient Greece, where itinerant practitioners of these arts were common and their competence difficult to assess. -- Plato's dialogues (especially the Sophist and Hippias Minor) extensively explore the relationship between expertise, pretension, and deception. -- The emphasis on purpose (prohairesis) as the defining element of character echoes NE III.2 and VI.2, where deliberate choice is identified as the most revealing indicator of moral character.

D: Original: Mock-modest people, who understate things, seem more attractive in character; for they are thought to speak not for gain but to avoid parade; and here too it is qualities which bring reputation that they disclaim, as Socrates used to do. Those who disclaim trifling and obvious qualities are called humbugs and are more contemptible; and sometimes this seems to be boastfulness, like the Spartan dress; for both excess and great deficiency are boastful. But those who use understatement with moderation and understate about matters that do not very much force themselves on our notice seem attractive. And it is the boaster that seems to be opposed to the truthful man; for he is the worse character.
D: Refashioned:
Self-deprecating people, who understate their qualities, seem more attractive in character, for they appear to speak not for gain but to avoid ostentation. Here too, it is especially qualities that bring reputation that they disclaim -- as Socrates used to do.

Those who disclaim trivial and obvious qualities are called humbugs and are more contemptible. Sometimes this kind of false modesty actually looks like boasting -- like the Spartan style of dress; for both extreme excess and conspicuous deficiency can serve as forms of display.

But those who use understatement with moderation, understating about matters that are not too glaringly obvious, seem attractive.

It is the boaster who seems to be the true opposite of the truthful person, for they are the worse character.
D: Elegant: Self-deprecating people seem more attractive, since they appear to speak from modesty rather than for gain -- especially when they disclaim qualities that bring prestige, as Socrates did. But disclaiming trivial or obvious qualities is contemptible humbug, sometimes amounting to a form of boasting -- like the ostentatiously austere Spartan dress, where conspicuous deficiency becomes its own display. Moderate understatement about non-obvious matters is genuinely attractive. The boaster is the true opposite of the truthful person, being the worse character.
D: Summary: Self-deprecation is generally more attractive than boasting, especially when it involves genuine modesty about significant qualities. But disclaiming trivial things is a form of humbug, and conspicuous self-denial can become its own kind of display. The boaster is the true opposite of truthfulness.
D: Distilled: Moderate self-deprecation about significant qualities is attractive, but conspicuous self-denial about trivial things is itself a form of boasting.
Notes: The reference to Socrates' habitual self-deprecation connects to the tradition of 'Socratic irony' (eirōneia), where Socrates would profess ignorance while exposing others' pretensions. Aristotle discusses Socrates' irony more critically in the Rhetoric and elsewhere. -- The Spartan reference concerns the famous Spartan simplicity of dress (the rough tribōn or cloak), which other Greeks sometimes viewed as an inverted form of vanity -- ostentatious in its very plainness. Plutarch and Thucydides both note this phenomenon. -- The insight that conspicuous deficiency can be a form of display anticipates modern analyses of 'competitive asceticism' and the paradoxes of anti-consumerism. -- The judgment that the boaster is 'the worse character' and thus the true opposite follows the principle from NE II.8 that the more opposed vice is typically the more harmful one.

D: Original: Since life includes rest as well as activity, and in this is included leisure and amusement, there seems here also to be a kind of intercourse which is tasteful; there is such a thing as saying- and again listening to- what one should and as one should. The kind of people one is speaking or listening to will also make a difference. Evidently here also there is both an excess and a deficiency as compared with the mean. Those who carry humour to excess are thought to be vulgar buffoons, striving after humour at all costs, and aiming rather at raising a laugh than at saying what is becoming and at avoiding pain to the object of their fun; while those who can neither make a joke themselves nor put up with those who do are thought to be boorish and unpolished. But those who joke in a tasteful way are called ready-witted, which implies a sort of readiness to turn this way and that; for such sallies are thought to be movements of the character, and as bodies are discriminated by their movements, so too are characters. The ridiculous side of things is not far to seek, however, and most people delight more than they should in amusement and in jestinly. and so even buffoons are called ready-witted because they are found attractive; but that they differ from the ready-witted man, and to no small extent, is clear from what has been said.
D: Refashioned:
Since life includes rest as well as activity, and rest includes leisure and amusement, there seems to be a kind of social exchange here too that has its own standard of taste. There is such a thing as saying -- and listening to -- the right things in the right way. The kind of people one is speaking or listening to also makes a difference.

Here too there is clearly an excess and a deficiency compared with the mean. Those who carry humour to excess are considered vulgar buffoons. They strain after laughs at all costs, aiming to get a reaction rather than to say what is becoming or to avoid hurting the target of their humour. Those who can neither make a joke themselves nor tolerate those who do are considered boorish and unpolished.

But those who joke tastefully are called witty -- a term that suggests a kind of nimbleness, a readiness to turn. For such remarks are thought to be movements of character, and just as bodies are judged by their movements, so too are characters.

However, the ridiculous is never far to seek, and most people delight in humour and jest more than they should. This is why even buffoons are sometimes called witty, since they are found entertaining. But that they differ from the truly witty person -- and substantially so -- is clear from what we have said.
D: Elegant: Life requires rest and amusement as well as activity, and social amusement has its own standard of taste. Those who push humour to excess are vulgar buffoons, straining for laughs without regard for decorum or the feelings of their targets. Those who cannot joke or tolerate jokes are boorish. The witty person jokes tastefully, with a nimbleness that reveals character -- for just as bodies are known by their movements, characters are known by their wit. Most people enjoy humour too much, which is why buffoons are sometimes mistaken for the truly witty, but the difference is substantial.
D: Summary: Wit is the virtue of tasteful humour in social life, positioned between buffoonery (excessive, indiscriminate humour) and boorishness (inability to joke or enjoy jokes). Most people overindulge in humour, which blurs the line between buffoons and the genuinely witty.
D: Distilled: Wit is the virtue of tasteful humour -- a nimbleness of character distinct from the buffoon's indiscriminate jokes and the boor's inability to enjoy them.
Notes: The Greek term eutrapelos ('well-turning,' translated as 'witty' or 'ready-witted') literally connotes flexibility and agility of mind. -- The analogy between bodily movement and character is characteristically Aristotelian; he develops similar connections in the Physiognomics and the Rhetoric. -- The distinction between buffoonery and genuine wit is not merely aesthetic but moral: the buffoon causes pain and violates decorum, while the witty person respects social boundaries. -- The observation that most people enjoy humour too much shows Aristotle's awareness that the mean is not where most people naturally fall. -- This is the third of the social virtues (after friendliness and truthfulness), completing the treatment of how we interact pleasantly with others.

D: Original: To the middle state belongs also tact; it is the mark of a tactful man to say and listen to such things as befit a good and well-bred man; for there are some things that it befits such a man to say and to hear by way of jest, and the well-bred man's jesting differs from that of a vulgar man, and the joking of an educated man from that of an uneducated. One may see this even from the old and the new comedies; to the authors of the former indecency of language was amusing, to those of the latter innuendo is more so; and these differ in no small degree in respect of propriety. Now should we define the man who jokes well by his saying what is not unbecoming to a well-bred man, or by his not giving pain, or even giving delight, to the hearer? Or is the latter definition, at any rate, itself indefinite, since different things are hateful or pleasant to different people? The kind of jokes he will listen to will be the same; for the kind he can put up with are also the kind he seems to make. There are, then, jokes he will not make; for the jest is a sort of abuse, and there are things that lawgivers forbid us to abuse; and they should, perhaps, have forbidden us even to make a jest of such. The refined and well-bred man, therefore, will be as we have described, being as it were a law to himself.
D: Refashioned:
Tact also belongs to the middle state. It is the mark of a tactful person to say and listen to such things as befit someone of good character and breeding. For there are some things that are fitting for such a person to say and hear in jest, and the jesting of a well-bred person differs from that of a vulgar one, just as the humour of an educated person differs from that of an uneducated one. One can see this even from the difference between the old and the new comedies: for the authors of the former found indecent language amusing, while those of the latter prefer innuendo -- and these differ considerably in terms of propriety.

Should we define the person who jokes well as one who says nothing unbecoming to a person of good character? Or as one who causes no pain, or even gives delight, to the listener? But surely this latter definition is too vague, since different things are hateful or pleasant to different people.

The kind of jokes such a person will listen to will match the kind they make, for the jokes one can put up with are also the kind one seems to endorse. There are, then, jokes they will not make, for a jest is a kind of mockery, and there are things that lawmakers forbid us to mock. Perhaps they should have forbidden jesting about such things as well.

The refined and well-bred person, therefore, will be as we have described -- being, as it were, a law to themselves.
D: Elegant: Tact means saying and hearing in jest what befits a person of good character. The well-bred person's humour differs from the vulgar person's, as the old comedies (which found indecency amusing) differ from the new (which prefer innuendo). The well-judged joke neither disgraces the speaker nor pains the listener -- though what pleases or pains varies between individuals. Since jests are a form of mockery, and some targets are legally protected from abuse, the refined person serves as their own law regarding what may be jested about.
D: Summary: Tact in humour means joking in a way befitting good character. The refined person's humour resembles the shift from old comedy's crude jokes to new comedy's subtle innuendo. Since jests are a form of mockery, the person of good character is their own law about what may be mocked.
D: Distilled: The person of refined wit serves as their own law regarding humour, jesting in ways that befit good character and cause no undeserved pain.
Notes: The contrast between Old and New Comedy is historically significant. Old Comedy (exemplified by Aristophanes, c. 446-386 BCE) was characterized by obscenity, personal attacks, and political satire. New Comedy (exemplified by Menander, c. 342-290 BCE) favored character-based humour and innuendo. -- The idea that the well-bred person is 'a law to themselves' (nomos heautō) is a striking formulation suggesting that the truly virtuous person has internalized the standards of right conduct. This concept influenced later natural law theory. -- The mention of lawmakers forbidding certain kinds of mockery reflects actual Athenian legislation: laws against defamation (kakēgoria) regulated public speech. -- Aristotle discusses comedy's relationship to character more extensively in the Poetics (Ch. 5).

D: Original: Such, then, is the man who observes the mean, whether he be called tactful or ready-witted. The buffoon, on the other hand, is the slave of his sense of humour, and spares neither himself nor others if he can raise a laugh, and says things none of which a man of refinement would say, and to some of which he would not even listen. The boor, again, is useless for such social intercourse; for he contributes nothing and finds fault with everything. But relaxation and amusement are thought to be a necessary element in life.
D: Refashioned:
Such, then, is the person who observes the mean, whether we call them tactful or witty. The buffoon, on the other hand, is a slave to their own sense of humour. They spare neither themselves nor others if they can raise a laugh, saying things that no person of refinement would say, and some that such a person would not even listen to.

The boor, in contrast, is useless for social interaction: they contribute nothing and find fault with everything.

But relaxation and amusement are thought to be a necessary element in life.
D: Elegant: The witty person observes the mean. The buffoon is enslaved to humour, sparing no one -- not even themselves -- and saying things the refined would neither say nor hear. The boor contributes nothing and objects to everything. Yet relaxation and amusement are necessary parts of life.
D: Summary: The witty person holds the mean between the buffoon, who is enslaved to humour and will say anything for a laugh, and the boor, who contributes nothing to social enjoyment. Both extremes are defective, since amusement is a necessary part of life.
D: Distilled: The buffoon is enslaved to humour and the boor is hostile to it, but amusement is a necessary part of human life that requires its own virtue.
Notes: The characterization of the buffoon as a 'slave' (doulos) to humour uses the strongest possible language of unfreedom, connecting comic excess to a loss of autonomy. -- The claim that amusement is 'necessary' echoes NE X.6, where Aristotle discusses the role of relaxation and pleasure in the good life. -- The boor (agroikos, literally 'rustic') connotes someone lacking the social polish of urban life -- an important cultural distinction in ancient Athens. -- The brief treatment of the boor suggests that excessive seriousness or social withdrawal is as much a vice as excessive joking, reflecting Aristotle's balanced view of human sociality.

D: Original: The means in life that have been described, then, are three in number, and are all concerned with an interchange of words and deeds of some kind. They differ, however, in that one is concerned with truth; and the other two with pleasantness. Of those concerned with pleasure, one is displayed in jests, the other in the general social intercourse of life.
D: Refashioned: The means in social life that we have described are three in number, and all concern some kind of exchange of words and actions. They differ, however, in that one is concerned with truth, while the other two are concerned with pleasure. Of those concerned with pleasure, one is displayed in jesting, the other in the general social interactions of everyday life.
D: Elegant: Three social virtues have been described, all concerning the exchange of words and actions. One governs truth; two govern pleasure -- one in jesting, the other in everyday social interaction.
D: Summary: The three social virtues are truthfulness (concerned with honest self-presentation), wit (concerned with pleasure in jesting), and friendliness (concerned with pleasure in everyday social interaction).
D: Distilled: The three social virtues govern truth in self-presentation, pleasure in jest, and pleasure in everyday interaction.
Notes: This summary passage organizes the three social virtues treated in NE IV.6-8 into a systematic schema: friendliness (IV.6), truthfulness (IV.7), and wit (IV.8). -- The organizing principle -- one concerned with truth, two with pleasure -- provides the rational structure behind what might otherwise appear to be an arbitrary list. -- All three virtues are unnamed (or nearly so), which Aristotle notes as a recurring difficulty. -- The treatment of social virtues as a group is distinctive to Aristotle among ancient ethicists and reflects his keen awareness that human flourishing is essentially social (the 'political animal' of Politics I.2).

D: Original: Shame should not be described as a virtue; for it is more like a feeling than a state of character. It is defined, at any rate, as a kind of fear of dishonour, and produces an effect similar to that produced by fear of danger; for people who feel disgraced blush, and those who fear death turn pale. Both, therefore, seem to be in a sense bodily conditions, which is thought to be characteristic of feeling rather than of a state of character.
D: Refashioned: Shame should not be classified as a virtue, for it is more like a feeling than a state of character. It is defined as a kind of fear of dishonour, and it produces effects similar to those produced by the fear of danger. People who feel disgraced blush, and people who fear death turn pale. Both reactions seem to be bodily conditions, which is considered characteristic of feelings rather than of settled states of character.
D: Elegant: Shame is not a virtue but a feeling -- a fear of dishonour. Like the fear of death, it produces bodily responses: the disgraced blush as the frightened turn pale. Such bodily reactions are characteristic of feelings, not of settled character states.
