D: Summary: Shame is a feeling rather than a virtue. It is a form of fear -- specifically fear of dishonour -- and like other fears, it manifests in bodily reactions such as blushing, which marks it as an emotion rather than a stable character trait.
D: Distilled: Shame is not a virtue but an emotion -- a fear of dishonour that manifests in bodily reactions like blushing.
Notes: The distinction between feelings (pathē) and states of character (hexeis) is foundational to Aristotle's ethics; see NE II.5, where he argues that virtues are not feelings but dispositions to feel and act in the right way. -- The observation that shame produces blushing reflects Aristotle's interest in the bodily manifestations of emotion, developed more fully in De Anima and the Rhetoric (II.6). -- The placement of shame at the end of Book IV, after the virtues proper, signals its anomalous status in Aristotle's scheme. -- Aristotle's treatment of shame influenced the medieval distinction between verecundia (healthy shame) and pudor (unhealthy shame).

D: Original: The feeling is not becoming to every age, but only to youth. For we think young people should be prone to the feeling of shame because they live by feeling and therefore commit many errors, but are restrained by shame; and we praise young people who are prone to this feeling, but an older person no one would praise for being prone to the sense of disgrace, since we think he should not do anything that need cause this sense. For the sense of disgrace is not even characteristic of a good man, since it is consequent on bad actions (for such actions should not be done; and if some actions are disgraceful in very truth and others only according to common opinion, this makes no difference; for neither class of actions should be done, so that no disgrace should be felt); and it is a mark of a bad man even to be such as to do any disgraceful action. To be so constituted as to feel disgraced if one does such an action, and for this reason to think oneself good, is absurd; for it is for voluntary actions that shame is felt, and the good man will never voluntarily do bad actions. But shame may be said to be conditionally a good thing; if a good man does such actions, he will feel disgraced; but the virtues are not subject to such a qualification. And if shamelessness-not to be ashamed of doing base actions-is bad, that does not make it good to be ashamed of doing such actions. Continence too is not virtue, but a mixed sort of state; this will be shown later. Now, however, let us discuss justice.
D: Refashioned:
The feeling of shame is not appropriate to every age, but only to youth. We think young people should be prone to shame because they live by feeling and therefore commit many errors, which shame helps to restrain. We praise young people who are prone to shame, but no one would praise an older person for being easily embarrassed, since we believe they should not be doing anything that warrants embarrassment.

For shame is not even characteristic of a good person, since it arises from bad actions -- and such actions should not be done in the first place. Whether the actions are genuinely disgraceful or merely considered so by common opinion makes no difference: neither kind should be done, and so no occasion for shame should arise. It is the mark of a bad person even to be the kind of person who would do anything disgraceful.

To be so constituted as to feel disgraced if one does such things, and to think oneself good on that account, is absurd. For shame is felt for voluntary actions, and the good person will never voluntarily do what is bad.

Shame might be called conditionally good: if a good person were to do something disgraceful, they would feel shame. But the virtues are not subject to such conditional qualifications. And if shamelessness -- not being ashamed of doing base things -- is bad, it does not follow that being ashamed of doing them is good.

Similarly, continence is not a virtue but a mixed sort of state. This will be discussed later.

Now, however, let us turn to justice.
D: Elegant: Shame befits youth, not maturity. The young live by feeling, commit many errors, and are rightly restrained by shame. But an older person should not be doing things that cause shame. Shame is not characteristic of the good person, who should never do anything disgraceful -- whether truly or merely by reputation. Finding it absurd to think oneself good because one would feel shame after wrongdoing: the good person simply does not voluntarily do wrong. Shame is conditionally good -- useful if one does err -- but virtue admits no such conditions. That shamelessness is bad does not make shame good. Similarly, continence is not virtue but a mixed state, to be discussed later.
D: Summary: Shame is appropriate for the young, who err through inexperience, but not for mature adults, who should not be doing shameful things in the first place. Being disposed to feel shame after wrongdoing does not make one good -- it merely indicates one would recognize the wrongness of actions one should not be performing at all.
D: Distilled: Shame is fitting for the young but not the mature: the truly good person has no occasion for shame because they do not voluntarily do what is disgraceful.
Notes: The claim that shame is appropriate to youth but not maturity reflects a developmental view of moral psychology: the young need external and emotional checks on behaviour that the mature should have internalized as stable virtues. -- The argument that 'if shamelessness is bad, shame is not therefore good' is a logical point: two contraries can both be bad if the virtuous state transcends the opposition entirely. -- The brief mention of continence (enkrateia) as 'not virtue but a mixed state' points forward to the extended treatment in NE VII.1-10, where Aristotle distinguishes the continent person (who has bad desires but resists them) from the truly virtuous person (whose desires are already rightly ordered). -- The closing transition to justice sets up Book V, the longest book of the Ethics. -- Aristotle's treatment of shame influenced the medieval virtue of verecundia and modern discussions of the role of moral emotions in ethical life.

D: Original:
With regards to justice and injustice we must (1) consider what kind of actions they are concerned with, (2) what sort of mean justice is, and (3) between what extremes the just act is intermediate. Our investigation shall follow the same course as the preceding discussions.

We see that all men mean by justice that kind of state of character which makes people disposed to do what is just and makes them act justly and wish for what is just; and similarly by injustice that state which makes them act unjustly and wish for what is unjust. Let us too, then, lay this down as a general basis. For the same is not true of the sciences and the faculties as of states of character. A faculty or a science which is one and the same is held to relate to contrary objects, but a state of character which is one of two contraries does not produce the contrary results; e.g. as a result of health we do not do what is the opposite of healthy, but only what is healthy; for we say a man walks healthily, when he walks as a healthy man would.
D: Refashioned:
Regarding justice and injustice, we must consider: (1) what kinds of actions they concern, (2) what sort of mean justice is, and (3) between what extremes the just act lies. Our investigation will follow the same method as our earlier discussions.

We observe that everyone understands justice as the state of character that disposes people to do what is just, to act justly, and to wish for what is just. Similarly, injustice is understood as the state that makes people act unjustly and wish for what is unjust. Let us adopt this as our starting point. For states of character do not work the same way as sciences and faculties. A single science or faculty can deal with opposite objects, but a state of character aligned with one of two contraries does not produce the contrary results. For example, health does not lead us to do unhealthy things, but only healthy ones; we say a person walks healthily when they walk as a healthy person would.
D: Elegant: Justice is the settled state of character that disposes a person to do, choose, and desire what is just; injustice is its contrary. Unlike sciences and faculties, which can engage with opposites, a state of character produces only results consistent with itself -- just as health produces only healthy actions, never unhealthy ones.
D: Summary: Aristotle introduces the study of justice by defining it as a state of character -- a stable disposition that makes people act justly and desire just outcomes. He distinguishes states of character from sciences and faculties: while a science can address opposite subjects, a character state like health only produces effects aligned with itself.
D: Distilled: Justice is a stable state of character that consistently produces just actions, unlike sciences that can engage with opposites.
Notes: This passage opens Book V and establishes Aristotle's characteristic method of beginning with common opinions (endoxa) before philosophical analysis. -- The distinction between states of character (hexeis) and faculties/sciences recalls the discussion of virtue as hexis in Book II.5-6. -- The health analogy is one of Aristotle's favorites, recurring throughout the Ethics. -- This methodological opening ("our investigation shall follow the same course") refers back to the approach established in Books II-IV for the other virtues.

D: Original: Now often one contrary state is recognized from its contrary, and often states are recognized from the subjects that exhibit them; for (A) if good condition is known, bad condition also becomes known, and (B) good condition is known from the things that are in good condition, and they from it. If good condition is firmness of flesh, it is necessary both that bad condition should be flabbiness of flesh and that the wholesome should be that which causes firmness in flesh. And it follows for the most part that if one contrary is ambiguous the other also will be ambiguous; e.g. if 'just' is so, that 'unjust' will be so too.
D: Refashioned: Often one contrary state is recognized through its opposite, and often states are recognized from the things that exhibit them. (A) If good condition is known, bad condition becomes known as well, and (B) good condition is known from the things in good condition, and those things in turn from it. If good condition is firmness of flesh, then bad condition must be flabbiness of flesh, and what is wholesome must be what causes firmness in flesh. And it generally follows that if one of a pair of contraries is ambiguous, the other will be too -- for example, if 'just' has multiple meanings, so will 'unjust.'
D: Elegant: Contraries illuminate each other: knowing good condition reveals bad condition, and knowing either reveals what produces it. If one term in a pair of contraries is ambiguous, its opposite will be equally ambiguous -- so if 'just' has multiple senses, 'unjust' must as well.
D: Summary: Aristotle establishes that we can understand a concept by examining its opposite and by looking at the things that exemplify it. He notes that ambiguity in one term of a pair of contraries implies ambiguity in the other, setting up his analysis of the multiple senses of justice.
D: Distilled: Contraries illuminate each other, so the multiple senses of 'just' entail correspondingly multiple senses of 'unjust.'
Notes: The method of studying contraries is fundamental to Aristotle's dialectical approach, discussed in Topics I.15 and used extensively in the Physics. -- This principle of corresponding ambiguity becomes crucial for the coming distinction between universal justice and particular justice. -- The firmness-of-flesh example is drawn from ancient Greek medical theory, likely Hippocratic.

D: Original: Now 'justice' and 'injustice' seem to be ambiguous, but because their different meanings approach near to one another the ambiguity escapes notice and is not obvious as it is, comparatively, when the meanings are far apart, e.g. (for here the difference in outward form is great) as the ambiguity in the use of kleis for the collar-bone of an animal and for that with which we lock a door. Let us take as a starting-point, then, the various meanings of 'an unjust man'. Both the lawless man and the grasping and unfair man are thought to be unjust, so that evidently both the law-abiding and the fair man will be just. The just, then, is the lawful and the fair, the unjust the unlawful and the unfair.
D: Refashioned: Now 'justice' and 'injustice' seem to have multiple meanings, but because these meanings are close to one another, the ambiguity goes unnoticed. This is unlike cases where meanings are far apart -- as with the Greek word kleis, which means both 'collar-bone' and 'door-key,' where the difference is obvious. Let us begin, then, with the various meanings of 'an unjust person.' Both the lawless person and the grasping, unfair person are considered unjust. It follows that both the law-abiding and the fair person will be just. The just, then, is the lawful and the fair; the unjust is the unlawful and the unfair.
D: Elegant: The ambiguity in 'justice' and 'injustice' escapes notice because the different meanings lie close together. Starting from common usage: the unjust person is either lawless or grasping and unfair. Correspondingly, the just is both the lawful and the fair, the unjust both the unlawful and the unfair.
D: Summary: Aristotle observes that 'justice' is ambiguous but the ambiguity is hard to detect because the different meanings are closely related. By examining what people mean by 'unjust person,' he identifies two senses: the lawless and the unfairly grasping. Justice therefore means both law-abidingness and fairness.
D: Distilled: Justice has two closely related but distinct senses: obedience to law and fairness in one's dealings with others.
Notes: The kleis (collar-bone/key) example illustrates homonymy, a concept Aristotle treats systematically in Categories Ch.1. -- The distinction between the lawful and the fair maps onto the later distinction between universal (or general) justice and particular justice, which is the central framework of Book V. -- This method of beginning from how ordinary people use words (endoxa) is characteristic of Aristotle's ethical method, defended in NE VII.1.

D: Original: Since the unjust man is grasping, he must be concerned with goods-not all goods, but those with which prosperity and adversity have to do, which taken absolutely are always good, but for a particular person are not always good. Now men pray for and pursue these things; but they should not, but should pray that the things that are good absolutely may also be good for them, and should choose the things that are good for them. The unjust man does not always choose the greater, but also the less-in the case of things bad absolutely; but because the lesser evil is itself thought to be in a sense good, and graspingness is directed at the good, therefore he is thought to be grasping. And he is unfair; for this contains and is common to both.
D: Refashioned: Since the unjust person is grasping, they must be concerned with goods -- not all goods, but those bound up with prosperity and adversity, which are always good in an absolute sense but not always good for a particular person. People pray for and pursue these goods, but they should not; instead, they should pray that what is absolutely good may also be good for them, and choose what is actually good for them. The unjust person does not always choose the greater share; in the case of things that are absolutely bad, they also choose the lesser portion. But because the lesser evil is itself considered a kind of good, and graspingness is directed at the good, the person is still thought to be grasping. And they are unfair -- for unfairness encompasses and is common to both forms of injustice.
D: Elegant: The grasping person is concerned with goods tied to prosperity and adversity -- things good in themselves but not always good for a particular individual. Such a person grabs more of what is good and less of what is bad, but since lesser evils count as relative goods, even choosing less of the bad counts as graspingness. Unfairness encompasses both forms.
D: Summary: Aristotle clarifies what the unjust person grasps at: goods connected to prosperity and adversity. These are good absolutely but not always good for a given individual. The unjust person takes more of the good and less of the bad -- and since lesser evils are relative goods, this too counts as graspingness.
D: Distilled: The unjust person grasps at more than their share of goods and less than their share of evils, making unfairness the common element in both forms of injustice.
Notes: The distinction between what is good absolutely (haplos) and good for a particular person (tini) is central to Aristotle's ethics; see NE I.4 and VII.12. -- The insight that people should pray not for absolute goods but for those goods to be good for them echoes Socratic/Platonic themes in the Euthydemus 278e-282d. -- The concept of pleonexia (graspingness) becomes a key term in Book V, referring specifically to wanting more than one's fair share.

D: Original: Since the lawless man was seen to be unjust and the law-abiding man just, evidently all lawful acts are in a sense just acts; for the acts laid down by the legislative art are lawful, and each of these, we say, is just. Now the laws in their enactments on all subjects aim at the common advantage either of all or of the best or of those who hold power, or something of the sort; so that in one sense we call those acts just that tend to produce and preserve happiness and its components for the political society. And the law bids us do both the acts of a brave man (e.g. not to desert our post nor take to flight nor throw away our arms), and those of a temperate man (e.g. not to commit adultery nor to gratify one's lust), and those of a good-tempered man (e.g. not to strike another nor to speak evil), and similarly with regard to the other virtues and forms of wickedness, commanding some acts and forbidding others; and the rightly-framed law does this rightly, and the hastily conceived one less well. This form of justice, then, is complete virtue, but not absolutely, but in relation to our neighbour. And therefore justice is often thought to be the greatest of virtues, and 'neither evening nor morning star' is so wonderful; and proverbially 'in justice is every virtue comprehended'. And it is complete virtue in its fullest sense, because it is the actual exercise of complete virtue. It is complete because he who possesses it can exercise his virtue not only in himself but towards his neighbour also; for many men can exercise virtue in their own affairs, but not in their relations to their neighbour.
D: Refashioned:
Since the lawless person was seen to be unjust and the law-abiding person just, evidently all lawful acts are in a sense just acts; for the acts prescribed by legislation are lawful, and each of these, we say, is just. Now the laws in their various enactments aim at the common advantage -- whether of all citizens, of the best, of those who hold power, or something along these lines. So in one sense we call those acts just that tend to produce and preserve happiness and its components for the political community. And the law commands us to perform the acts of a courageous person (for example, not to desert our post, flee, or throw away our weapons), of a temperate person (not to commit adultery or indulge our appetites), of a good-tempered person (not to strike another or speak abusively), and similarly regarding the other virtues and vices, commanding some acts and forbidding others. A well-framed law does this correctly; a hastily conceived one, less well.

This form of justice, then, is complete virtue -- though not absolutely, but in relation to others. And for this reason justice is often considered the greatest of virtues, and 'neither evening star nor morning star' is so wonderful; and proverbially, 'in justice every virtue is comprehended.' It is complete virtue in the fullest sense because the one who possesses it can exercise virtue not only in their own affairs but toward others as well. For many people can exercise virtue in their private affairs but not in their relations with others.
D: Elegant: All lawful acts are in a sense just, because laws aim at the common good and command acts of every virtue -- courage, temperance, good temper, and the rest. This universal justice is therefore complete virtue, not in the abstract, but as exercised toward others. It is the greatest of virtues because many can be virtuous privately but few extend that virtue to their neighbors.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that since law-abidingness is a form of justice, and laws prescribe virtuous conduct across the board, justice in this broad sense is equivalent to complete virtue exercised in relation to other people. It is considered the greatest virtue because it requires practicing all virtues not just for oneself but toward others.
D: Distilled: Universal justice is complete virtue exercised toward others, and is therefore considered the greatest virtue because it encompasses all the rest.
Notes: The quotation 'neither evening nor morning star is so wonderful' is from Theognis (fragment 255) or possibly Euripides. -- 'In justice every virtue is comprehended' is a proverb Aristotle also attributes to Theognis. -- The distinction between justice as complete virtue (toward others) and particular justice becomes the structural backbone of Book V. -- This concept of justice as the social dimension of all virtue profoundly influenced Aquinas (Summa Theologiae II-II, Q.58) and the natural law tradition. -- The observation that laws aim at the common advantage connects to Politics III.6-7, where Aristotle distinguishes correct constitutions (aiming at common good) from deviant ones.

D: Original: This is why the saying of Bias is thought to be true, that 'rule will show the man'; for a ruler is necessarily in relation to other men and a member of a society. For this same reason justice, alone of the virtues, is thought to be 'another's good', because it is related to our neighbour; for it does what is advantageous to another, either a ruler or a copartner. Now the worst man is he who exercises his wickedness both towards himself and towards his friends, and the best man is not he who exercises his virtue towards himself but he who exercises it towards another; for this is a difficult task. Justice in this sense, then, is not part of virtue but virtue entire, nor is the contrary injustice a part of vice but vice entire. What the difference is between virtue and justice in this sense is plain from what we have said; they are the same but their essence is not the same; what, as a relation to one's neighbour, is justice is, as a certain kind of state without qualification, virtue.
D: Refashioned:
This is why the saying of Bias is thought to be true, that 'rule will show the man' -- for a ruler is necessarily in relation to other people and a member of a community. For this same reason, justice alone among the virtues is thought to be 'another's good,' because it is directed toward our neighbor; it does what is advantageous to another, whether a ruler or a fellow citizen. The worst person is one who exercises wickedness both toward themselves and toward their friends; the best is not one who exercises virtue toward themselves, but one who exercises it toward others -- for this is a difficult task.

Justice in this sense, then, is not a part of virtue but virtue entire, nor is the corresponding injustice a part of vice but vice entire. The difference between virtue and justice in this sense is clear from what we have said: they are the same disposition, but their essence differs. What, as a relation to one's neighbor, is justice is, considered as a certain kind of state without qualification, virtue.
D: Elegant: As Bias said, 'rule will show the man' -- because ruling requires relating to others. Justice alone is called 'another's good' because it benefits our neighbor. The best person exercises virtue toward others, which is harder than exercising it toward oneself. Justice in this universal sense is not a part of virtue but virtue entire; it is the same disposition as virtue, but its essence is relational -- what is virtue as a state of character is justice when directed toward others.
D: Summary: Aristotle explains that justice is uniquely 'another's good' -- the one virtue that by definition benefits other people. Justice as complete virtue and virtue itself are the same disposition, but they differ in essence: considered as a character state, it is virtue; considered in relation to others, it is justice.
D: Distilled: Justice and virtue are the same disposition viewed differently: virtue as a state of character, justice as that same virtue directed toward others.
Notes: Bias of Priene was one of the Seven Sages of ancient Greece (6th century BCE); his saying 'rule will show the man' (arche andra deixei) was proverbial. -- 'Another's good' (allotrian agathon) echoes Thrasymachus' definition in Plato's Republic I (343c), but Aristotle repurposes it positively. -- The distinction between virtue and justice as the same hexis (disposition) but different in definition (to einai) uses Aristotle's technical metaphysical vocabulary from Metaphysics VII. -- This passage directly engages the Republic's central question about whether justice benefits the just person.

D: Original:
But at all events what we are investigating is the justice which is a part of virtue; for there is a justice of this kind, as we maintain. Similarly it is with injustice in the particular sense that we are concerned.

That there is such a thing is indicated by the fact that while the man who exhibits in action the other forms of wickedness acts wrongly indeed, but not graspingly (e.g. the man who throws away his shield through cowardice or speaks harshly through bad temper or fails to help a friend with money through meanness), when a man acts graspingly he often exhibits none of these vices,-no, nor all together, but certainly wickedness of some kind (for we blame him) and injustice. There is, then, another kind of injustice which is a part of injustice in the wide sense, and a use of the word 'unjust' which answers to a part of what is unjust in the wide sense of 'contrary to the law'. Again if one man commits adultery for the sake of gain and makes money by it, while another does so at the bidding of appetite though he loses money and is penalized for it, the latter would be held to be self-indulgent rather than grasping, but the former is unjust, but not self-indulgent; evidently, therefore, he is unjust by reason of his making gain by his act. Again, all other unjust acts are ascribed invariably to some particular kind of wickedness, e.g. adultery to self-indulgence, the desertion of a comrade in battle to cowardice, physical violence to anger; but if a man makes gain, his action is ascribed to no form of wickedness but injustice. Evidently, therefore, there is apart from injustice in the wide sense another, 'particular', injustice which shares the name and nature of the first, because its definition falls within the same genus; for the significance of both consists in a relation to one's neighbour, but the one is concerned with honour or money or safety-or that which includes all these, if we had a single name for it-and its motive is the pleasure that arises from gain; while the other is concerned with all the objects with which the good man is concerned.
D: Refashioned:
But what we are investigating is the justice that is a part of virtue; for there is such a kind, as we maintain. Similarly, it is particular injustice that concerns us.

That such a thing exists is shown by the following: when someone exhibits the other forms of wickedness in action, they act wrongly but not graspingly -- for example, the person who throws away their shield through cowardice, speaks harshly through bad temper, or fails to help a friend financially through meanness. But when someone acts graspingly, they often exhibit none of these particular vices -- nor all of them combined -- yet they certainly display some kind of wickedness (for we blame them) and injustice. There is, then, another kind of injustice that is a part of injustice in the broad sense, and a use of the word 'unjust' that corresponds to only a part of what is unjust in the broad sense of 'contrary to the law.'

Consider further: if one person commits adultery for the sake of profit and makes money from it, while another does so driven by appetite even though they lose money and are penalized, the latter would be called self-indulgent rather than grasping, while the former is unjust but not self-indulgent. Evidently, then, the former is unjust because of their profit-seeking. Again, all other unjust acts are attributed to some particular vice -- adultery to self-indulgence, deserting a comrade in battle to cowardice, physical violence to anger -- but if someone acts for gain, the action is attributed to no vice other than injustice.

Evidently, therefore, apart from injustice in the broad sense, there is another, particular injustice that shares the name and nature of the first, because both fall within the same genus. The significance of both consists in a relation to one's neighbor, but particular injustice is concerned with honor, money, or safety -- or whatever single term might encompass all these -- and its motive is the pleasure that comes from gain. The other, universal injustice, is concerned with all the objects that concern the good person.
D: Elegant: Besides justice as complete virtue, there exists particular justice -- a distinct part of virtue. This is proven by the fact that grasping behavior is a recognizable vice separate from cowardice, bad temper, or meanness. When someone commits adultery for profit, they are unjust; when driven by appetite, they are self-indulgent. Other unjust acts are traced to specific vices, but acts done for gain point to injustice alone. Particular injustice shares the name and genus of universal injustice but is specifically concerned with honor, money, or safety, motivated by the pleasure of gain.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that particular justice exists as a distinct part of virtue, separate from justice as complete virtue. The proof is that grasping behavior is a recognizable vice distinct from cowardice, meanness, or self-indulgence. Particular injustice is specifically motivated by gain and concerns goods like honor, money, and safety.
D: Distilled: Particular injustice -- graspingness motivated by gain -- is a specific vice distinct from universal injustice and from all other vices like cowardice or self-indulgence.
Notes: This passage makes the pivotal move of Book V: distinguishing particular justice (a specific virtue) from universal/general justice (complete virtue toward others). -- The examples of adultery-for-profit vs. adultery-from-appetite illustrate Aristotle's method of individuating virtues and vices by their specific motivations. -- The concept of pleonexia (graspingness/gain-seeking) as the core of particular injustice becomes foundational for later theories of justice, especially in Aquinas (ST II-II, Q.59). -- The phrase 'the pleasure that arises from gain' (kerdos) connects particular injustice to the discussion of pleasures in Books VII and X.

D: Original:
It is clear, then, that there is more than one kind of justice, and that there is one which is distinct from virtue entire; we must try to grasp its genus and differentia.

The unjust has been divided into the unlawful and the unfair, and the just into the lawful and the fair. To the unlawful answers the afore-mentioned sense of injustice. But since unfair and the unlawful are not the same, but are different as a part is from its whole (for all that is unfair is unlawful, but not all that is unlawful is unfair), the unjust and injustice in the sense of the unfair are not the same as but different from the former kind, as part from whole; for injustice in this sense is a part of injustice in the wide sense, and similarly justice in the one sense of justice in the other. Therefore we must speak also about particular justice and particular and similarly about the just and the unjust. The justice, then, which answers to the whole of virtue, and the corresponding injustice, one being the exercise of virtue as a whole, and the other that of vice as a whole, towards one's neighbour, we may leave on one side. And how the meanings of 'just' and 'unjust' which answer to these are to be distinguished is evident; for practically the majority of the acts commanded by the law are those which are prescribed from the point of view of virtue taken as a whole; for the law bids us practise every virtue and forbids us to practise any vice. And the things that tend to produce virtue taken as a whole are those of the acts prescribed by the law which have been prescribed with a view to education for the common good. But with regard to the education of the individual as such, which makes him without qualification a good man, we must determine later whether this is the function of the political art or of another; for perhaps it is not the same to be a good man and a good citizen of any state taken at random.
D: Refashioned:
It is clear, then, that there is more than one kind of justice, and that one kind is distinct from virtue as a whole. We must try to grasp its genus and distinguishing features.

The unjust has been divided into the unlawful and the unfair, and the just into the lawful and the fair. To the unlawful corresponds the previously discussed broad sense of injustice. But since the unfair and the unlawful are not the same -- they differ as part from whole (for everything unfair is unlawful, but not everything unlawful is unfair) -- the unjust and injustice in the sense of unfairness are different from the broader kind, as part from whole. Particular injustice is a part of injustice in the broad sense, and similarly with particular justice. Therefore we must discuss particular justice and particular injustice, and likewise the particular senses of just and unjust.

The justice that corresponds to virtue as a whole -- the exercise of complete virtue toward one's neighbor -- and its corresponding injustice we may set aside. How the meanings of 'just' and 'unjust' that answer to these broader senses should be distinguished is evident: practically the majority of acts commanded by law are those prescribed from the standpoint of virtue as a whole, since the law bids us practice every virtue and forbids every vice. The acts that tend to produce virtue as a whole are those legal prescriptions aimed at education for the common good. But regarding the education of the individual as such -- what makes someone an unconditionally good person -- we must determine later whether this falls under political science or some other discipline. For perhaps being a good person and being a good citizen of any given state are not the same thing.
D: Elegant: There is more than one kind of justice. The unlawful corresponds to injustice in the broad sense; the unfair to particular injustice. These relate as whole to part: everything unfair is unlawful, but not vice versa. Setting aside universal justice (complete virtue toward others), we focus on particular justice. Most legal prescriptions correspond to universal justice, promoting virtue as a whole for the common good. Whether being a good person and a good citizen are identical remains to be determined.
D: Summary: Aristotle formally distinguishes universal justice (complete virtue toward others, corresponding to lawfulness) from particular justice (corresponding to fairness). They relate as whole to part. He sets aside universal justice and signals that the relation between individual virtue and civic virtue -- being a good person versus a good citizen -- requires further investigation.
D: Distilled: Universal justice (lawfulness) and particular justice (fairness) relate as whole to part, and being a good person may not be identical to being a good citizen.
Notes: The part-whole relationship between particular and universal justice is crucial to the structure of Book V and draws on Aristotle's logical framework in the Prior Analytics. -- The question whether being a good person and a good citizen are the same is taken up at length in Politics III.4. -- 'Education for the common good' refers to the Greek paideia and connects to the discussion in Politics VII-VIII about the state's role in education. -- The phrase 'acts prescribed from the standpoint of virtue taken as a whole' connects law to the broader ethical framework, foreshadowing the closing of the Ethics (X.9) where Aristotle transitions to the Politics.

D: Original: Of particular justice and that which is just in the corresponding sense, (A) one kind is that which is manifested in distributions of honour or money or the other things that fall to be divided among those who have a share in the constitution (for in these it is possible for one man to have a share either unequal or equal to that of another), and (B) one is that which plays a rectifying part in transactions between man and man. Of this there are two divisions; of transactions (1) some are voluntary and (2) others involuntary- voluntary such transactions as sale, purchase, loan for consumption, pledging, loan for use, depositing, letting (they are called voluntary because the origin of these transactions is voluntary), while of the involuntary (a) some are clandestine, such as theft, adultery, poisoning, procuring, enticement of slaves, assassination, false witness, and (b) others are violent, such as assault, imprisonment, murder, robbery with violence, mutilation, abuse, insult.
D: Refashioned:
Of particular justice and its corresponding sense of the just, (A) one kind is manifested in distributions of honor, money, or other goods that are to be divided among those who share in the constitution (for in these cases one person may receive a share either unequal or equal to another's), and (B) the other plays a rectifying role in transactions between individuals.

This rectificatory kind has two divisions: some transactions are (1) voluntary and others (2) involuntary. Voluntary transactions include sale, purchase, loan for consumption, pledging, loan for use, depositing, and letting -- they are called voluntary because their origin is voluntary. Involuntary transactions are of two kinds: (a) some are clandestine, such as theft, adultery, poisoning, procuring, enticement of slaves, assassination, and false witness; (b) others are violent, such as assault, imprisonment, murder, robbery with violence, mutilation, abuse, and insult.
D: Elegant: Particular justice has two forms: (A) distributive justice, which governs how honor, money, and other shared goods are divided among citizens, and (B) rectificatory justice, which corrects wrongs in transactions. Transactions are either voluntary (sale, purchase, loan, deposit, letting) or involuntary -- the latter being either clandestine (theft, adultery, poisoning, assassination, false witness) or violent (assault, imprisonment, murder, robbery, mutilation).
D: Summary: Aristotle divides particular justice into distributive justice (governing the allocation of shared goods like honor and money) and rectificatory justice (correcting wrongs in transactions). Transactions are either voluntary (commercial dealings) or involuntary (crimes, both covert and violent).
D: Distilled: Particular justice divides into distributive justice (fair allocation of shared goods) and rectificatory justice (correcting wrongs in voluntary and involuntary transactions).
Notes: The distinction between distributive (dianemetikon dikaion) and rectificatory/corrective (diorthotikon dikaion) justice is one of Aristotle's most enduring contributions to political philosophy. -- This classification profoundly influenced Aquinas (ST II-II, Q.61), who added commutative justice, and modern theorists including Rawls, whose 'justice as fairness' engages primarily with the distributive dimension. -- The list of involuntary transactions effectively catalogs the crimes recognized in Athenian law. -- 'Those who share in the constitution' (politeia) restricts distributive justice to citizens, reflecting the exclusionary nature of Greek political membership.

D: Original: (A) We have shown that both the unjust man and the unjust act are unfair or unequal; now it is clear that there is also an intermediate between the two unequals involved in either case. And this is the equal; for in any kind of action in which there's a more and a less there is also what is equal. If, then, the unjust is unequal, just is equal, as all men suppose it to be, even apart from argument. And since the equal is intermediate, the just will be an intermediate. Now equality implies at least two things. The just, then, must be both intermediate and equal and relative (i.e. for certain persons). And since the equall intermediate it must be between certain things (which are respectively greater and less); equal, it involves two things; qua just, it is for certain people. The just, therefore, involves at least four terms; for the persons for whom it is in fact just are two, and the things in which it is manifested, the objects distributed, are two. And the same equality will exist between the persons and between the things concerned; for as the latter the things concerned-are related, so are the former; if they are not equal, they will not have what is equal, but this is the origin of quarrels and complaints-when either equals have and are awarded unequal shares, or unequals equal shares. Further, this is plain from the fact that awards should be 'according to merit'; for all men agree that what is just in distribution must be according to merit in some sense, though they do not all specify the same sort of merit, but democrats identify it with the status of freeman, supporters of oligarchy with wealth (or with noble birth), and supporters of aristocracy with excellence.
D: Refashioned:
(A) We have shown that both the unjust person and the unjust act involve unfairness or inequality. Now it is clear that there is also an intermediate between the two unequal extremes in either case -- and this is the equal. In any kind of action where there is a more and a less, there is also an equal. If, then, the unjust is the unequal, the just is the equal, as everyone supposes even without argument. And since the equal is an intermediate, the just will be an intermediate.

Now equality implies at least two things. The just, then, must be both intermediate and equal, and relative to certain persons. As intermediate, it must be between certain things (respectively greater and less); as equal, it involves two things; as just, it is for certain people. The just therefore involves at least four terms: the persons for whom it is just are two, and the objects distributed are two. And the same equality will hold between the persons as between the things: as the things are related, so are the persons. If they are not equal, they will not receive equal shares -- and this is the origin of quarrels and complaints, when either equals receive unequal shares or unequals receive equal shares.

Further, this is clear from the principle that distributions should be 'according to merit.' Everyone agrees that what is just in distribution must accord with merit of some kind, though not everyone specifies the same kind: democrats identify merit with the status of being a free citizen, supporters of oligarchy with wealth or noble birth, and supporters of aristocracy with excellence.
D: Elegant: The unjust is the unequal, so the just is the equal -- an intermediate between extremes. Distributive justice involves at least four terms: two persons and two shares. The ratio between the persons must match the ratio between their shares. Unequal persons should not receive equal shares, nor equal persons unequal ones -- this is the source of disputes. All agree distribution should follow merit, but they disagree on what merit is: democrats say free status, oligarchs say wealth, aristocrats say excellence.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that distributive justice is a kind of equality that involves proportional distribution among at least two persons and two shares. The key principle is that the ratio between persons (based on their merit) must match the ratio between their shares. Everyone agrees distribution should follow merit, but democrats, oligarchs, and aristocrats define merit differently.
D: Distilled: Distributive justice requires proportional equality: shares must be distributed according to merit, though what counts as merit is politically contested.
Notes: This is Aristotle's foundational analysis of distributive justice, which has shaped virtually all subsequent discussions. -- The claim that disputes arise from disagreements about merit anticipates Rawls' observation that justice is the first virtue of social institutions (A Theory of Justice, 1971). -- The three conceptions of merit (freedom, wealth, excellence) map onto the three main constitutional types discussed in Politics III.7-8. -- The mathematical framing of justice as proportion draws on Pythagorean and Platonic traditions; Plato discusses proportional equality in Laws VI (757b-e). -- Aristotle's point that everyone agrees on formal justice (according to merit) but disagrees on substantive merit is sometimes called the 'formal principle of justice,' later articulated by Perelman.

D: Original: The just, then, is a species of the proportionate (proportion being not a property only of the kind of number which consists of abstract units, but of number in general). For proportion is equality of ratios, and involves four terms at least (that discrete proportion involves four terms is plain, but so does continuous proportion, for it uses one term as two and mentions it twice; e.g. 'as the line A is to the line B, so is the line B to the line C'; the line B, then, has been mentioned twice, so that if the line B be assumed twice, the proportional terms will be four); and the just, too, involves at least four terms, and the ratio between one pair is the same as that between the other pair; for there is a similar distinction between the persons and between the things. As the term A, then, is to B, so will C be to D, and therefore, alternando, as A is to C, B will be to D. Therefore also the whole is in the same ratio to the whole; and this coupling the distribution effects, and, if the terms are so combined, effects justly. The conjunction, then, of the term A with C and of B with D is what is just in distribution, and this species of the just is intermediate, and the unjust is what violates the proportion; for the proportional is intermediate, and the just is proportional. (Mathematicians call this kind of proportion geometrical; for it is in geometrical proportion that it follows that the whole is to the whole as either part is to the corresponding part.) This proportion is not continuous; for we cannot get a single term standing for a person and a thing.
D: Refashioned:
The just, then, is a species of the proportionate. Proportion is not a property only of abstract numbers but of number in general. It is an equality of ratios and involves at least four terms. (That discrete proportion involves four terms is obvious; but continuous proportion also does, since it uses one term as two, mentioning it twice. For example: 'as line A is to line B, so line B is to line C.' Line B has been mentioned twice, so if counted twice, there are four proportional terms.)

The just also involves at least four terms, and the ratio between one pair is the same as that between the other. As term A is to B, so C will be to D, and therefore, by alternation, as A is to C, so B is to D. Therefore the whole is in the same ratio to the other whole, and this is the pairing that distribution produces -- and if the terms are combined this way, the distribution is just.

The conjunction of term A with C and of B with D is what is just in distribution. This species of justice is intermediate, and the unjust violates the proportion; for the proportional is intermediate, and the just is proportional. Mathematicians call this kind of proportion geometrical, for in geometrical proportion the whole stands to the whole as either part stands to the corresponding part. This proportion is not continuous, because we cannot get a single term representing both a person and a thing.
D: Elegant: Distributive justice is a species of proportion -- an equality of ratios involving four terms: two persons and two shares. As person A is to person B, so share C must be to share D. Pairing A with C and B with D produces a just distribution. The just is the proportional, and the unjust is what violates this proportion. This is geometrical proportion, where the whole stands to the whole as each part to its corresponding part.
D: Summary: Aristotle explains the mathematics of distributive justice using geometrical proportion. Justice requires four terms -- two persons and two shares -- where the ratio between the persons equals the ratio between the shares. This proportion is geometrical (not arithmetic), meaning the relationship is multiplicative rather than additive.
D: Distilled: Distributive justice follows geometrical proportion: the ratio between persons' merits must equal the ratio between their shares.
Notes: Aristotle's use of geometrical proportion for distributive justice draws on Greek mathematical theory, particularly as developed by Eudoxus of Cnidus (a member of Plato's Academy), whose theory of proportion is preserved in Euclid's Elements V. -- The distinction between discrete and continuous proportion comes from Pythagorean mathematics. -- 'Alternando' (alternation) is a technical term from proportion theory (Euclid V, Def. 12). -- This mathematical formalization of justice influenced medieval Islamic philosophy (Al-Farabi, Averroes) and scholastic thought (Aquinas, ST II-II, Q.61, a.2).

D: Original:
This, then, is what the just is-the proportional; the unjust is what violates the proportion. Hence one term becomes too great, the other too small, as indeed happens in practice; for the man who acts unjustly has too much, and the man who is unjustly treated too little, of what is good. In the case of evil the reverse is true; for the lesser evil is reckoned a good in comparison with the greater evil, since the lesser evil is rather to be chosen than the greater, and what is worthy of choice is good, and what is worthier of choice a greater good.

This, then, is one species of the just.
D: Refashioned:
This, then, is what the just is -- the proportional. The unjust is what violates the proportion. One share becomes too great, the other too small, as indeed happens in practice: the person who acts unjustly gets too much of what is good, and the person who is unjustly treated gets too little. In the case of what is bad, the reverse is true: the lesser evil is reckoned a good in comparison with the greater evil, since the lesser evil is more worthy of choice than the greater, what is worthy of choice is good, and what is more worthy of choice is a greater good.

This, then, is one species of the just.
D: Elegant: The just is the proportional; the unjust violates proportion. The unjust person takes too much good and too little bad; the victim receives too little good and too much bad. The lesser evil counts as a relative good since it is more worthy of choice. This completes the account of distributive justice.
D: Summary: Aristotle concludes his account of distributive justice: the just is proportional distribution, and injustice is its violation. The person who acts unjustly gets an excess of goods and a deficit of evils; the victim gets the reverse. Even lesser evils count as relative goods because they are preferable to greater evils.
D: Distilled: Distributive justice is proportional allocation, and its violation means one party gets too much good and another too little.
Notes: The principle that lesser evils are relative goods connects to the discussion of choice in NE III.1 and the analysis of mixed actions. -- This completes Aristotle's treatment of distributive (dianemetikon) justice and transitions to rectificatory (diorthotikon) justice. -- The observation about excess and deficiency ties distributive justice to the general doctrine of the mean from Book II, though justice relates to a mean differently from other virtues.

D: Original: (B) The remaining one is the rectificatory, which arises in connexion with transactions both voluntary and involuntary. This form of the just has a different specific character from the former. For the justice which distributes common possessions is always in accordance with the kind of proportion mentioned above (for in the case also in which the distribution is made from the common funds of a partnership it will be according to the same ratio which the funds put into the business by the partners bear to one another); and the injustice opposed to this kind of justice is that which violates the proportion.
D: Refashioned: (B) The remaining species is rectificatory justice, which arises in connection with transactions both voluntary and involuntary. This form of justice has a different character from the previous one. For distributive justice, which allocates common possessions, always follows geometrical proportion as described above. (In the case of distribution from a partnership's common funds, it will follow the same ratio as the contributions the partners put into the business bear to one another.) And the injustice opposed to this kind of justice is what violates the proportion.
D: Elegant: The second species is rectificatory justice, which governs both voluntary and involuntary transactions. Unlike distributive justice, which follows geometrical proportion based on the ratio of contributions, rectificatory justice has a distinct character.
D: Summary: Aristotle introduces rectificatory justice as the second species of particular justice, noting that it differs from distributive justice. While distributive justice follows geometrical proportion (matching shares to contributions), rectificatory justice operates on different principles.
D: Distilled: Rectificatory justice, governing transactions between individuals, operates differently from distributive justice's geometrical proportion.
Notes: The transition from distributive to rectificatory (diorthotikon) justice is a major structural pivot in Book V. -- The partnership example shows how distributive justice applies to private as well as public allocations. -- The term 'rectificatory' (diorthotikon) literally means 'making straight again,' capturing the corrective function of this type of justice. -- Some translators use 'corrective justice' instead of 'rectificatory justice' -- both capture the Greek.

D: Original: But the justice in transactions between man and man is a sort of equality indeed, and the injustice a sort of inequality; not according to that kind of proportion, however, but according to arithmetical proportion. For it makes no difference whether a good man has defrauded a bad man or a bad man a good one, nor whether it is a good or a bad man that has committed adultery; the law looks only to the distinctive character of the injury, and treats the parties as equal, if one is in the wrong and the other is being wronged, and if one inflicted injury and the other has received it. Therefore, this kind of injustice being an inequality, the judge tries to equalize it; for in the case also in which one has received and the other has inflicted a wound, or one has slain and the other been slain, the suffering and the action have been unequally distributed; but the judge tries to equalize by means of the penalty, taking away from the gain of the assailant.
D: Refashioned:
But justice in transactions between individuals is a sort of equality, and injustice a sort of inequality -- not according to geometrical proportion, however, but according to arithmetical proportion. For it makes no difference whether a good person has defrauded a bad person or a bad person a good one, nor whether it is a good or bad person who has committed adultery. The law looks only to the distinctive character of the injury and treats the parties as equal, considering only whether one person has committed a wrong and the other has suffered it, whether one has inflicted harm and the other has received it.

Therefore, since this kind of injustice is an inequality, the judge tries to equalize it. When one person has received a wound and the other has inflicted it, or one has killed and the other been killed, the suffering and the action are unequally distributed. The judge tries to equalize things by means of a penalty, taking away from the gain of the offender.
D: Elegant: Rectificatory justice follows arithmetical, not geometrical, proportion. The law disregards the moral character of the parties and looks only to the nature of the injury: who wronged whom, who harmed whom. Since injustice creates inequality between parties the law treats as equal, the judge restores equality by imposing a penalty that takes away from the offender's gain.
D: Summary: Aristotle explains that rectificatory justice uses arithmetical rather than geometrical proportion. Unlike distributive justice, it treats all parties as equal regardless of moral character, looking only at the nature of the injury. The judge's role is to restore equality by penalizing the offender's gain.
D: Distilled: Rectificatory justice treats all parties as equal before the law and restores the balance disrupted by wrongdoing through arithmetical equalization.
Notes: The principle that rectificatory justice ignores personal merit and treats parties as equal is one of Aristotle's most influential ideas, anticipating the modern legal principle of equality before the law. -- Arithmetical proportion (adding and subtracting equal amounts) contrasts with the geometrical proportion (ratios) of distributive justice. -- This passage has been foundational for corrective justice theory, notably in Ernest Weinrib's The Idea of Private Law (1995). -- The judge's role as equalizer connects to the discussion at 5.15 of the judge as 'animate justice.'

D: Original: For the term 'gain' is applied generally to such cases, even if it be not a term appropriate to certain cases, e.g. to the person who inflicts a woundand 'loss' to the sufferer; at all events when the suffering has been estimated, the one is called loss and the other gain. Therefore the equal is intermediate between the greater and the less, but the gain and the loss are respectively greater and less in contrary ways; more of the good and less of the evil are gain, and the contrary is loss; intermediate between them is, as we saw, equal, which we say is just; therefore corrective justice will be the intermediate between loss and gain. This is why, when people dispute, they take refuge in the judge; and to go to the judge is to go to justice; for the nature of the judge is to be a sort of animate justice; and they seek the judge as an intermediate, and in some states they call judges mediators, on the assumption that if they get what is intermediate they will get what is just. The just, then, is an intermediate, since the judge is so.
D: Refashioned:
The term 'gain' is applied generally to such cases, even when it is not strictly appropriate -- for example, to the person who inflicts a wound -- and 'loss' to the sufferer. At any rate, once the suffering has been assessed, one side is called loss and the other gain. Therefore the equal is intermediate between the greater and the less, but gain and loss are respectively greater and less in contrary ways: more of the good and less of the evil is gain, and the contrary is loss. Intermediate between them is the equal, which we say is just. Therefore rectificatory justice will be the intermediate between loss and gain.

This is why, when people have a dispute, they take refuge in the judge. To go to the judge is to go to justice, for the very nature of the judge is to be a sort of living embodiment of justice. People seek the judge as an intermediate, and in some states they call judges 'mediators,' on the assumption that if they get what is intermediate they will get what is just. The just, then, is an intermediate, since the judge is so.
D: Elegant: In rectificatory justice, 'gain' and 'loss' are generalized terms for the unequal advantage created by wrongdoing. The equal lies between gain and loss, and this equal is the just. To go to a judge is to go to justice itself -- the judge is a living embodiment of justice, a mediator who finds the intermediate between the parties' competing claims.
D: Summary: Aristotle explains that 'gain' and 'loss' are generalized terms in rectificatory justice, applicable even to injuries where profit is not literally involved. The just is the intermediate between gain and loss. Judges are living embodiments of justice, which is why disputants seek them out as mediators who can find the fair middle ground.
D: Distilled: The judge is living justice -- a mediator who finds the equal point between one party's gain and another's loss.
Notes: The description of the judge as 'animate justice' (dikaion empsychon) became one of Aristotle's most quoted phrases, cited by Aquinas (ST II-II, Q.58, a.1) and throughout the Western legal tradition. -- The etymology connecting 'mediator' (mesidios) to 'middle' (meson) reflects Aristotle's characteristic interest in language as revealing conceptual truths. -- The idea that seeking a judge is seeking justice connects to the discussion of political justice in 5.6 (5.27) and the role of law in political life.

D: Original: Now the judge restores equality; it is as though there were a line divided into unequal parts, and he took away that by which the greater segment exceeds the half, and added it to the smaller segment. And when the whole has been equally divided, then they say they have 'their own'-i.e. when they have got what is equal. The equal is intermediate between the greater and the lesser line according to arithmetical proportion. It is for this reason also that it is called just (sikaion), because it is a division into two equal parts (sicha), just as if one were to call it sichaion; and the judge (sikastes) is one who bisects (sichastes).
D: Refashioned:
The judge restores equality as follows: it is as though there were a line divided into unequal parts, and the judge took away the amount by which the greater segment exceeds the half and added it to the smaller segment. When the whole has been equally divided, people say they have 'their own' -- that is, when they have received what is equal. The equal is intermediate between the greater and the lesser line according to arithmetical proportion.

This is also why it is called 'just' (dikaion) -- because it is a division into two equal parts (dicha), as if one were to call it dichaion. And the judge (dikastes) is one who bisects (dichastes).
D: Elegant: The judge restores equality like someone dividing an unequal line: removing the excess from the longer segment and adding it to the shorter until both are equal to the half. When each party has their equal share, they have what is 'their own.' The very word 'just' (dikaion) suggests division in two (dicha) -- and the judge (dikastes) is one who bisects (dichastes).
D: Summary: Aristotle uses a geometric analogy to explain rectificatory justice: the judge acts like someone equalizing an unequally divided line by transferring the excess to the deficit. He also offers a folk etymology connecting the Greek word for 'just' (dikaion) to the act of bisecting into equal halves.
D: Distilled: The judge restores justice by transferring excess from one party to the other until both have their equal share.
Notes: The folk etymology connecting dikaion (just) to dicha (in two) is not linguistically accurate but was taken seriously in antiquity. -- The line-division analogy makes rectificatory justice vivid and concrete, anticipating modern economic models of zero-sum redistribution. -- This passage illustrates Aristotle's fondness for mathematical and geometric analogies in ethics, a tendency he inherited partly from Plato's Academy. -- The phrase 'having their own' (to hauton echein) connects to the concept of to ison (the equal) as the foundation of justice.

D: Original: For when something is subtracted from one of two equals and added to the other, the other is in excess by these two; since if what was taken from the one had not been added to the other, the latter would have been in excess by one only. It therefore exceeds the intermediate by one, and the intermediate exceeds by one that from which something was taken. By this, then, we shall recognize both what we must subtract from that which has more, and what we must add to that which has less; we must add to the latter that by which the intermediate exceeds it, and subtract from the greatest that by which it exceeds the intermediate. Let the lines AA', BB', CC' be equal to one another; from the line AA' let the segment AE have been subtracted, and to the line CC' let the segment Cd have been added, so that the whole line DCC' exceeds the line EA' by the segment CD and the segment CF; therefore it exceeds the line Bb' by the segment CD. (See diagram.)
D: Refashioned:
When something is subtracted from one of two equal quantities and added to the other, the latter exceeds by twice the amount taken -- since if what was taken from the one had not been added to the other, the other would exceed by only that amount. Therefore the quantity with the addition exceeds the intermediate by one unit, and the intermediate exceeds the quantity from which something was taken by one unit. By this we can recognize both what must be subtracted from the party that has more and what must be added to the party that has less: we must add to the latter the amount by which the intermediate exceeds it, and subtract from the former the amount by which it exceeds the intermediate.

Let lines AA', BB', and CC' be equal to one another. From line AA', let segment AE be subtracted, and to line CC', let segment CD be added, so that the whole line DCC' exceeds line EA' by segment CD and segment CF; therefore it exceeds line BB' by segment CD.
D: Elegant: When an amount is taken from one equal share and added to another, the recipient exceeds the middle by the transferred amount, and the middle exceeds the diminished party by the same. To restore equality: add to the lesser what the middle exceeds it by, and subtract from the greater what it exceeds the middle by.
D: Summary: Aristotle provides a mathematical demonstration of how rectificatory justice works using equal line segments. When something is taken from one and given to another, the excess is double the amount transferred. The judge restores equality by identifying the intermediate point and redistributing accordingly.
D: Distilled: Rectificatory justice works by identifying the arithmetical mean between excess and deficit, then redistributing to restore that mean.
Notes: This is the most technical and mathematical passage in Book V, demonstrating Aristotle's training in Plato's Academy where mathematical reasoning was highly valued (cf. 'Let no one ignorant of geometry enter here'). -- The line diagram (referred to but not preserved in manuscripts) would have clarified the arithmetic. -- Modern commentators (e.g., W.D. Ross, H.H. Joachim) have reconstructed the diagram. -- This passage has been important for economic theories of restitution and tort law.

D: Original:
These names, both loss and gain, have come from voluntary exchange; for to have more than one's own is called gaining, and to have less than one's original share is called losing, e.g. in buying and selling and in all other matters in which the law has left people free to make their own terms; but when they get neither more nor less but just what belongs to themselves, they say that they have their own and that they neither lose nor gain.

Therefore the just is intermediate between a sort of gain and a sort of loss, viz. those which are involuntary; it consists in having an equal amount before and after the transaction.
D: Refashioned:
The terms 'loss' and 'gain' originated in voluntary exchange: to have more than one's own is called gaining, and to have less than one's original share is called losing -- as in buying and selling and all other transactions where the law leaves people free to make their own terms. But when people get neither more nor less than what belongs to them, they say they have their own and neither lose nor gain.

Therefore the just is intermediate between a kind of gain and a kind of loss -- those which are involuntary. It consists in having an equal amount before and after the transaction.
D: Elegant: The terms 'gain' and 'loss' come from voluntary exchange: gaining is having more than one started with, losing is having less. When neither occurs, each has their own. The just in rectificatory matters is the intermediate between involuntary gain and loss -- having an equal amount before and after the transaction.
D: Summary: Aristotle traces the language of 'gain' and 'loss' to voluntary commerce and then applies it to rectificatory justice more broadly. Justice in this context means that after a transaction or its correction, each party ends up with what they had before -- neither gaining nor losing.
D: Distilled: Rectificatory justice restores the original equality between parties, ensuring neither involuntary gain nor involuntary loss.
Notes: The distinction between voluntary and involuntary gain/loss is crucial: voluntary exchanges are by definition agreed upon, while rectificatory justice addresses involuntary disruptions to the status quo ante. -- This idea of restoring the pre-transaction baseline influenced the concept of restitutio in integrum in Roman law and modern tort law's aim of making the plaintiff whole. -- The passage bridges the economic and juridical dimensions of justice in Book V.

D: Original:
Some think that reciprocity is without qualification just, as the Pythagoreans said; for they defined justice without qualification as reciprocity. Now 'reciprocity' fits neither distributive nor rectificatory justice-yet people want even the justice of Rhadamanthus to mean this:

Should a man suffer what he did, right justice would be done -for in many cases reciprocity and rectificatory justice are not in accord; e.g. (1) if an official has inflicted a wound, he should not be wounded in return, and if some one has wounded an official, he ought not to be wounded only but punished in addition. Further (2) there is a great difference between a voluntary and an involuntary act. But in associations for exchange this sort of justice does hold men together-reciprocity in accordance with a proportion and not on the basis of precisely equal return. For it is by proportionate requital that the city holds together. Men seek to return either evil for evil-and if they cana not do so, think their position mere slavery-or good for good-and if they cannot do so there is no exchange, but it is by exchange that they hold together. This is why they give a prominent place to the temple of the Graces-to promote the requital of services; for this is characteristic of grace-we should serve in return one who has shown grace to us, and should another time take the initiative in showing it.
D: Refashioned:
Some think that reciprocity is justice without qualification -- as the Pythagoreans held, for they defined justice simply as reciprocity. But reciprocity fits neither distributive nor rectificatory justice, even though people want it to -- they even attribute this principle to the justice of Rhadamanthus: 'Should a man suffer what he did, right justice would be done.'

In many cases, reciprocity and rectificatory justice are not in accord. For example: (1) if an official has inflicted a wound, they should not simply be wounded in return; and if someone has wounded an official, they ought not merely to be wounded but to receive additional punishment. Further, (2) there is a great difference between a voluntary and an involuntary act.

But in associations for exchange, this sort of justice -- reciprocity in accordance with a proportion, not on the basis of precisely equal return -- does hold people together. For it is by proportionate reciprocity that the city is held together. People seek to return either evil for evil (and if they cannot, they consider their condition mere slavery) or good for good (and if they cannot, there is no exchange -- and it is by exchange that they hold together). This is why people give a prominent place to the temple of the Graces: to promote the repayment of services. For this is characteristic of grace -- we should serve in return one who has shown grace to us, and should on another occasion take the initiative in showing it.
D: Elegant: The Pythagoreans defined justice as simple reciprocity, but this fits neither distributive nor rectificatory justice. An official who wounds should not merely be wounded back; an attacker of an official deserves additional punishment. Voluntary and involuntary acts also demand different responses. Yet in exchange, proportionate reciprocity -- not strict tit-for-tat -- holds the city together. People must return both good for good and evil for evil. The temple of the Graces stands as a reminder to repay kindness and initiate it.
D: Summary: Aristotle rejects the Pythagorean view that justice is simple reciprocity (an eye for an eye), showing it fails in cases involving officials or the voluntary/involuntary distinction. However, proportionate reciprocity -- not strict equal return -- is essential to economic exchange and social cohesion. The temple of the Graces symbolizes the duty to repay and initiate acts of goodwill.
D: Distilled: Simple reciprocity is not justice, but proportionate reciprocity in exchange is what holds communities together.
Notes: The Pythagoreans were a philosophical-religious community founded by Pythagoras of Samos (6th century BCE), known for finding mathematical structure in all things. Their definition of justice as reciprocity (to antipeponthos) reflects this mathematical orientation. -- Rhadamanthus was a mythological son of Zeus and Europa, legendary judge of the dead in Hades, known for stern justice. The verse quoted is of uncertain origin. -- The temple of the Graces (Charites) was a real civic institution in Greek cities, typically located in the agora. -- The concept of proportionate reciprocity (antipeponthos kat' analogian) becomes Aristotle's bridge to his theory of economic exchange. -- This section profoundly influenced both Aquinas on commutative justice and modern theories of reciprocity in game theory and social contract theory.

D: Original: Now proportionate return is secured by cross-conjunction. Let A be a builder, B a shoemaker, C a house, D a shoe. The builder, then, must get from the shoemaker the latter's work, and must himself give him in return his own. If, then, first there is proportionate equality of goods, and then reciprocal action takes place, the result we mention will be effected. If not, the bargain is not equal, and does not hold; for there is nothing to prevent the work of the one being better than that of the other; they must therefore be equated. (And this is true of the other arts also; for they would have been destroyed if what the patient suffered had not been just what the agent did, and of the same amount and kind.) For it is not two doctors that associate for exchange, but a doctor and a farmer, or in general people who are different and unequal; but these must be equated.
D: Refashioned:
Proportionate return is achieved through cross-conjunction. Let A be a builder, B a shoemaker, C a house, D a shoe. The builder must receive the shoemaker's product and must in return give their own. If proportionate equality of goods is first established, and then reciprocal exchange takes place, the result we described will be achieved. If not, the exchange is not equal and does not hold, for there is nothing to prevent one person's work from being better than the other's -- so they must be equated.

This applies to the other crafts as well: they would have been destroyed if what the recipient received had not been equal in amount and kind to what the producer made. For it is not two doctors who associate for exchange, but a doctor and a farmer, or in general people who are different and unequal. But these must be made equal.
D: Elegant: Proportionate exchange works through cross-conjunction: as the builder is to the shoemaker, so the shoe must be to the house. Without proportionate equality of goods, exchange fails because one person's work may be superior to another's. Exchange occurs not between equals in craft but between people of different occupations -- doctor and farmer, builder and shoemaker -- who must first be equated.
D: Summary: Aristotle explains that fair exchange requires cross-conjunction: matching the ratio between producers to the ratio between their products. Since exchange occurs between people in different crafts whose products differ in value, these products must be equated before fair exchange can occur.
D: Distilled: Fair exchange requires that the ratio between different producers match the ratio between their products, achieved through proportionate equalization.
Notes: The cross-conjunction (kata diametron suzeusis, literally 'coupling along the diagonal') uses the mathematical image of a rectangle with producers and products at the four corners. -- This analysis of exchange value is one of Aristotle's most influential contributions to economics, discussed extensively by Marx in Capital Vol. I, Ch. 1. -- The insight that exchange occurs between people of different occupations connects to the theory of social differentiation in Politics II.2 and the division of labor. -- Karl Polanyi drew heavily on this passage in his economic anthropology.

D: Original: This is why all things that are exchanged must be somehow comparable. It is for this end that money has been introduced, and it becomes in a sense an intermediate; for it measures all things, and therefore the excess and the defect-how many shoes are equal to a house or to a given amount of food. The number of shoes exchanged for a house (or for a given amount of food) must therefore correspond to the ratio of builder to shoemaker. For if this be not so, there will be no exchange and no intercourse. And this proportion will not be effected unless the goods are somehow equal. All goods must therefore be measured by some one thing, as we said before.
D: Refashioned:
This is why all things that are exchanged must be somehow comparable.
