It is for this purpose that money has been introduced: it serves as a kind of intermediate, for it measures all things -- including excess and deficiency -- determining how many shoes are equal to a house or a given amount of food. The number of shoes exchanged for a house or food must correspond to the ratio of builder to shoemaker. For without this correspondence, there will be no exchange and no social interaction.

And this proportion will not be achieved unless the goods are somehow made equal. All goods must therefore be measured by some single standard, as we said before.
D: Elegant: All exchanged goods must be comparable, and money was introduced to serve as this common measure. It determines how many shoes equal a house by measuring the ratio between different producers' work. Without such commensuration, exchange and social interaction would be impossible.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that exchange requires all goods to be comparable through a common measure, which is the role money serves. Money makes different goods commensurable by quantifying the ratios between them, enabling the proportionate exchanges that sustain social interaction.
D: Distilled: Money exists to make different goods commensurable, enabling the proportionate exchange that sustains social life.
Notes: This passage presents one of the earliest systematic analyses of money's function as a measure of value, influencing economic thought from Aquinas through Adam Smith to Marx. -- Marx specifically engaged with Aristotle's analysis in Capital, arguing that Aristotle could not fully solve the commensurability problem because he lacked the concept of abstract labor. -- The idea that money is an 'intermediate' (meson) connects it to the broader theme of justice as a mean. -- The claim that exchange requires commensurability raises deep philosophical questions about value theory that remain debated.

D: Original: Now this unit is in truth demand, which holds all things together (for if men did not need one another's goods at all, or did not need them equally, there would be either no exchange or not the same exchange); but money has become by convention a sort of representative of demand; and this is why it has the name 'money' (nomisma)-because it exists not by nature but by law (nomos) and it is in our power to change it and make it useless. There will, then, be reciprocity when the terms have been equated so that as farmer is to shoemaker, the amount of the shoemaker's work is to that of the farmer's work for which it exchanges.
D: Refashioned:
In truth, this common unit is demand, which holds all things together. For if people did not need one another's goods at all, or did not need them equally, there would be either no exchange or not the same kind of exchange. But money has become, by convention, a sort of representative of demand. This is why it has the name 'money' (nomisma) -- because it exists not by nature but by law (nomos), and it is in our power to change it and make it worthless.

There will be reciprocity when the terms have been equated, so that as the farmer is to the shoemaker, the amount of the shoemaker's work stands to the amount of the farmer's work for which it is exchanged.
D: Elegant: The true measure underlying exchange is demand -- need for one another's goods. Money is merely the conventional representative of this demand, existing by law (nomos), not nature, which is why it is called nomisma. Reciprocity is achieved when goods are equated so that the ratio of producers matches the ratio of their products.
D: Summary: Aristotle identifies demand as the real basis of exchange value, with money serving as its conventional representative. He offers an etymology connecting 'money' (nomisma) to 'law' (nomos), emphasizing that money is a social convention, not a natural fact. True reciprocity in exchange requires matching the ratio between producers to their products.
D: Distilled: Demand is the true measure of exchange value; money is merely its conventional stand-in, existing by social agreement rather than nature.
Notes: The etymology connecting nomisma (money/coin) to nomos (law/convention) is linguistically sound and reflects the Greek understanding that coinage was a political institution. -- Aristotle's insight that money is conventional, not natural, was revolutionary and influenced the nominalist tradition in monetary theory. -- The identification of demand (chreia) as the real basis of value anticipates subjective value theory, though Aristotle's concept is closer to social need than individual preference. -- This passage is central to debates between 'substantivist' and 'formalist' interpretations of ancient economies (Polanyi vs. Finley).

D: Original: But we must not bring them into a figure of proportion when they have already exchanged (otherwise one extreme will have both excesses), but when they still have their own goods. Thus they are equals and associates just because this equality can be effected in their case. Let A be a farmer, C food, B a shoemaker, D his product equated to C. If it had not been possible for reciprocity to be thus effected, there would have been no association of the parties. That demand holds things together as a single unit is shown by the fact that when men do not need one another, i.e. when neither needs the other or one does not need the other, they do not exchange, as we do when some one wants what one has oneself, e.g. when people permit the exportation of corn in exchange for wine. This equation therefore must be established. And for the future exchange-that if we do not need a thing now we shall have it if ever we do need it-money is as it were our surety; for it must be possible for us to get what we want by bringing the money.
D: Refashioned:
But we must not set up the proportion after the parties have already exchanged -- otherwise one party will end up with both excesses -- but while they still have their own goods. In this way they are equals and associates, because this kind of equality can be established between them. Let A be a farmer, C food, B a shoemaker, D his product equated to C. If reciprocity could not be achieved in this way, there would be no association between the parties.

That demand holds things together as a single unit is shown by the fact that when people do not need one another -- when neither needs the other, or one does not need the other -- they do not exchange. We do exchange, however, when someone wants what we have, for example when people permit the export of grain in exchange for wine. This equation must therefore be established.

For future exchange -- so that if we do not need something now, we shall have it when we do -- money serves as our guarantee. It must be possible to get what we need by bringing money.
D: Elegant: The proportion must be established before exchange, not after -- otherwise one party gets both excesses. People are associates because such equality can be established between them. That demand is the unifying principle is clear: where there is no mutual need, there is no exchange. Money serves as a guarantee for future exchange -- storing present value for future need.
D: Summary: Aristotle insists that the proportionate equation must be set before exchange occurs, not retroactively. He confirms that demand is what binds people in exchange relationships, as shown by the fact that exchange ceases where mutual need ceases. Money functions as a guarantee for future transactions, storing present value for later use.
D: Distilled: Money serves as a guarantee of future exchange, storing the value of present demand for when it is needed later.
Notes: The observation that money serves as a 'surety' (engye) for future exchange anticipates the modern economic concept of money as a store of value, one of money's three classical functions (medium of exchange, unit of account, store of value). -- The grain-for-wine example reflects common Mediterranean trade patterns. -- The point about establishing proportion before exchange addresses a logical problem in barter economics. -- This passage influenced scholastic economic theory, particularly discussions of just price in Aquinas and the late scholastics of Salamanca.

D: Original: Now the same thing happens to money itself as to goods-it is not always worth the same; yet it tends to be steadier. This is why all goods must have a price set on them; for then there will always be exchange, and if so, association of man with man. Money, then, acting as a measure, makes goods commensurate and equates them; for neither would there have been association if there were not exchange, nor exchange if there were not equality, nor equality if there were not commensurability. Now in truth it is impossible that things differing so much should become commensurate, but with reference to demand they may become so sufficiently. There must, then, be a unit, and that fixed by agreement (for which reason it is called money); for it is this that makes all things commensurate, since all things are measured by money. Let A be a house, B ten minae, C a bed. A is half of B, if the house is worth five minae or equal to them; the bed, C, is a tenth of B; it is plain, then, how many beds are equal to a house, viz. five. That exchange took place thus before there was money is plain; for it makes no difference whether it is five beds that exchange for a house, or the money value of five beds.
D: Refashioned:
Now the same thing happens to money as to goods -- it is not always worth the same -- yet it tends to be more stable. This is why all goods must have a price set on them, for then there will always be exchange, and if there is exchange, there will be human association. Money, then, acting as a measure, makes goods commensurable and equates them. Without exchange there would be no association, without equality no exchange, and without commensurability no equality.

In truth, it is impossible for things that differ so greatly to become perfectly commensurable, but with reference to demand they can become sufficiently so. There must, then, be a unit, fixed by agreement -- which is why it is called money. Money makes all things commensurable, since all things are measured by money. Let A be a house, B ten minae, C a bed. A is half of B if the house is worth five minae or equal to them; the bed, C, is a tenth of B. It is plain, then, how many beds equal a house -- namely, five. That exchange took place in this manner before money existed is clear, for it makes no difference whether it is five beds that are exchanged for a house, or the money value of five beds.
D: Elegant: Money fluctuates in value but is more stable than goods, which is why everything must be priced -- enabling exchange and thus human association. Goods vastly different in nature cannot be perfectly commensurable, but demand makes them sufficiently so. Money, fixed by agreement, provides the common measure. A house worth five minae equals five beds worth one mina each -- the same ratio held in barter before money existed.
D: Summary: Aristotle acknowledges that money's value fluctuates but is more stable than goods, making it useful as a universal measure. While perfectly different things cannot be truly commensurable, demand makes them comparable enough for practical purposes. Money, established by convention, enables this commensuration and thus makes exchange and human association possible.
D: Distilled: Money provides the stable conventional measure that makes vastly different goods sufficiently commensurable for exchange and social cooperation.
Notes: The mina was a unit of currency in ancient Greece (1 mina = 100 drachmas; a skilled laborer earned about 1 drachma per day). -- Aristotle's acknowledgment that perfect commensurability is impossible but practical commensurability suffices is philosophically sophisticated and anticipates modern debates about incommensurability of values. -- The observation that barter preceded money reflects a historical theory found also in Politics I.9. -- Marx devoted extensive analysis to this passage in Capital, arguing that Aristotle identified the problem of commensurability but could not solve it without the concept of abstract human labor as the substance of value.

D: Original:
We have now defined the unjust and the just. These having been marked off from each other, it is plain that just action is intermediate between acting unjustly and being unjustly treated; for the one is to have too much and the other to have too little. Justice is a kind of mean, but not in the same way as the other virtues, but because it relates to an intermediate amount, while injustice relates to the extremes. And justice is that in virtue of which the just man is said to be a doer, by choice, of that which is just, and one who will distribute either between himself and another or between two others not so as to give more of what is desirable to himself and less to his neighbour (and conversely with what is harmful), but so as to give what is equal in accordance with proportion; and similarly in distributing between two other persons. Injustice on the other hand is similarly related to the unjust, which is excess and defect, contrary to proportion, of the useful or hurtful. For which reason injustice is excess and defect, viz. because it is productive of excess and defect-in one's own case excess of what is in its own nature useful and defect of what is hurtful, while in the case of others it is as a whole like what it is in one's own case, but proportion may be violated in either direction. In the unjust act to have too little is to be unjustly treated; to have too much is to act unjustly.

Let this be taken as our account of the nature of justice and injustice, and similarly of the just and the unjust in general.
D: Refashioned:
We have now defined the unjust and the just. With these distinguished from each other, it is clear that just action is intermediate between acting unjustly and being unjustly treated -- for the former is to have too much and the latter to have too little. Justice is a kind of mean, but not in the same way as the other virtues: it is a mean because it relates to an intermediate amount, while injustice relates to the extremes.

Justice is the virtue by which the just person is said to be a doer, by choice, of what is just -- one who will distribute between themselves and another, or between two others, not so as to give more of what is desirable to themselves and less to their neighbor (and conversely with what is harmful), but so as to give what is equal in accordance with proportion. Similarly when distributing between two other persons.

Injustice, conversely, is related to the unjust, which is excess and deficiency contrary to proportion in what is useful or harmful. For this reason, injustice is excess and deficiency: it produces excess and deficiency -- in one's own case, excess of what is naturally useful and deficiency of what is harmful; in the case of others, the overall result is similar, but the proportion may be violated in either direction. In the unjust act, to have too little is to be unjustly treated; to have too much is to act unjustly.

Let this stand as our account of the nature of justice and injustice, and similarly of the just and the unjust in general.
D: Elegant: Just action lies between acting unjustly (having too much) and being unjustly treated (having too little). Justice is a mean, but uniquely so -- it relates to an intermediate amount rather than an intermediate feeling or action. The just person distributes by choice according to proportion, giving neither excessive advantage to themselves nor disadvantage to others. Injustice is the contrary: excess and deficiency in violation of proportion.
D: Summary: Aristotle summarizes his account of justice and injustice. Justice is a mean between doing and suffering injustice, but it is a mean of amount (proportional distribution) rather than of feeling. The just person deliberately distributes according to proportion, while injustice produces excess for the wrongdoer and deficiency for the wronged.
D: Distilled: Justice is a unique kind of mean -- not of feeling but of proportional distribution -- where the just person deliberately gives each party their fair share.
Notes: This summary passage ties the discussion of particular justice back to the doctrine of the mean from Book II. -- Aristotle's qualification that justice is a mean 'not in the same way as the other virtues' is important: justice is a mean of result (proportional outcome), not a mean between two emotional extremes (as courage is between fear and rashness). -- The emphasis on 'by choice' (prohairesis) connects to the account of voluntary action and choice in Book III. -- This closes the first major section of Book V (chapters 1-5 in modern numbering), covering distributive, rectificatory, and reciprocal justice.

D: Original: Since acting unjustly does not necessarily imply being unjust, we must ask what sort of unjust acts imply that the doer is unjust with respect to each type of injustice, e.g. a thief, an adulterer, or a brigand. Surely the answer does not turn on the difference between these types. For a man might even lie with a woman knowing who she was, but the origin of his might be not deliberate choice but passion. He acts unjustly, then, but is not unjust; e.g. a man is not a thief, yet he stole, nor an adulterer, yet he committed adultery; and similarly in all other cases.
D: Refashioned: Since acting unjustly does not necessarily mean being an unjust person, we must ask what sorts of unjust acts imply that the doer is truly unjust with respect to each type of injustice -- as a thief, an adulterer, or a brigand. Surely the answer does not turn on the difference between the types of injustice. A person might sleep with another's spouse knowing exactly who they are, but the origin of the act might be passion rather than deliberate choice. Such a person acts unjustly but is not an unjust person -- just as someone is not a thief even though they stole, nor an adulterer even though they committed adultery. The same applies in all other cases.
D: Elegant: Acting unjustly does not make one an unjust person. Someone who commits adultery from passion rather than deliberate choice acts unjustly but is not an adulterer in character. The distinction lies not in the type of act but in whether it originates from deliberate choice or from passion.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes between committing an unjust act and being an unjust person. The difference lies in the origin of the act: actions from passion are unjust but do not make the agent unjust in character. Only acts originating from deliberate choice reveal an unjust character.
D: Distilled: Committing an unjust act from passion makes one a wrongdoer, but only choosing injustice deliberately makes one an unjust person.
Notes: This distinction between unjust acts and unjust character is crucial to Aristotle's moral psychology and connects to the analysis of voluntary action and choice (prohairesis) in Book III.1-5. -- The emphasis on choice (prohairesis) as the marker of settled character rather than individual acts is distinctively Aristotelian and influenced virtue ethics broadly. -- This passage anticipates the fuller taxonomy of injuries in 5.34-36 (misadventure, mistake, act of injustice, unjust character). -- The distinction parallels modern legal theory's mens rea requirements.

D: Original: Now we have previously stated how the reciprocal is related to the just; but we must not forget that what we are looking for is not only what is just without qualification but also political justice. This is found among men who share their life with a view to selfsufficiency, men who are free and either proportionately or arithmetically equal, so that between those who do not fulfil this condition there is no political justice but justice in a special sense and by analogy. For justice exists only between men whose mutual relations are governed by law; and law exists for men between whom there is injustice; for legal justice is the discrimination of the just and the unjust. And between men between whom there is injustice there is also unjust action (though there is not injustice between all between whom there is unjust action), and this is assigning too much to oneself of things good in themselves and too little of things evil in themselves. This is why we do not allow a man to rule, but rational principle, because a man behaves thus in his own interests and becomes a tyrant. The magistrate on the other hand is the guardian of justice, and, if of justice, then of equality also. And since he is assumed to have no more than his share, if he is just (for he does not assign to himself more of what is good in itself, unless such a share is proportional to his merits-so that it is for others that he labours, and it is for this reason that men, as we stated previously, say that justice is 'another's good'), therefore a reward must be given him, and this is honour and privilege; but those for whom such things are not enough become tyrants.
D: Refashioned:
We have previously stated how reciprocity relates to the just, but we must not forget that what we are seeking is not only what is just without qualification but also political justice. Political justice is found among people who share their life with a view to self-sufficiency -- people who are free and either proportionately or arithmetically equal. Between those who do not meet this condition, there is no political justice, but only justice in a special sense and by analogy.

For justice exists only between those whose mutual relations are governed by law, and law exists where there is injustice -- for legal justice is the adjudication of the just and the unjust. Where there is injustice between people, there is also unjust action (though unjust action does not always imply injustice as a character state), and this consists in assigning too much to oneself of things good in themselves and too little of things evil in themselves.

This is why we do not allow a person to rule, but rational principle, because a person acting in their own interests becomes a tyrant. The magistrate, on the other hand, is the guardian of justice, and if of justice, then of equality too. Since the just magistrate is assumed to have no more than their fair share (for they do not assign to themselves more of what is good in itself, unless such a share is proportional to their merits -- so that they labor for others, which is why people say justice is 'another's good'), a reward must be given them, and this reward is honor and privilege. Those for whom such rewards are not enough become tyrants.
D: Elegant: Political justice exists only among free people who share a life aimed at self-sufficiency and who are governed by law. Law exists because injustice exists, requiring adjudication of just and unjust. We entrust rule to rational principle rather than individuals because self-interested rulers become tyrants. The just magistrate is a guardian of equality who labors for others' good and is compensated with honor and privilege. Those unsatisfied with such rewards become tyrants.
D: Summary: Aristotle defines political justice as existing only among free, equal people living together under law. Law presupposes the possibility of injustice. Rule must be by rational principle, not personal will, because self-interested rulers become tyrants. The just magistrate guards equality, works for others' benefit, and is compensated with honor -- not material excess.
D: Distilled: Political justice requires free and equal persons under law, with rule by rational principle rather than personal will to prevent tyranny.
Notes: The requirement that political justice exists only among the free and equal reflects the conditions of the Greek polis and connects directly to Politics III.6-13. -- 'We do not allow a person to rule, but rational principle' is one of Aristotle's most famous political maxims, foundational for the rule of law tradition. -- The connection between justice and self-sufficiency (autarkeia) ties to the definition of the polis in Politics I.2. -- 'Justice is another's good' again echoes Thrasymachus in Republic I, but here validated rather than criticized. -- The discussion of how insufficient honor leads to tyranny connects to Politics V on revolutions.

D: Original: The justice of a master and that of a father are not the same as the justice of citizens, though they are like it; for there can be no injustice in the unqualified sense towards thing that are one's own, but a man's chattel, and his child until it reaches a certain age and sets up for itself, are as it were part of himself, and no one chooses to hurt himself (for which reason there can be no injustice towards oneself). Therefore the justice or injustice of citizens is not manifested in these relations; for it was as we saw according to law, and between people naturally subject to law, and these as we saw' are people who have an equal share in ruling and being ruled. Hence justice can more truly be manifested towards a wife than towards children and chattels, for the former is household justice; but even this is different from political justice.
D: Refashioned:
The justice of a master toward slaves and that of a father toward children are not the same as the justice of citizens, though they resemble it. For there can be no injustice in the full sense toward things that are one's own: a person's slave, and their child until it reaches a certain age and becomes independent, are in a sense parts of themselves, and no one chooses to harm themselves -- which is why there can be no injustice toward oneself.

Therefore the justice of citizens is not manifested in these relations, for as we saw, it operates according to law and among people naturally subject to law -- that is, people who have an equal share in ruling and being ruled. Hence justice can more truly be manifested toward a wife than toward children and slaves, for this is household justice. But even household justice is different from political justice.
D: Elegant: Justice toward slaves and children differs from civic justice because they are, in a sense, extensions of oneself, and one cannot commit injustice against oneself. Political justice requires people who share equally in ruling and being ruled under law. Justice is more fully realized toward a wife than toward children or slaves -- this is household justice, but it remains distinct from political justice.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that full political justice applies only between citizens who share equally in ruling and being ruled. The master-slave and father-child relationships involve a different, lesser form of justice because dependents are considered extensions of the master or father. Justice toward a wife is closer to full justice but still constitutes a separate category of household justice.
D: Distilled: Full political justice exists only between equals who share in ruling and being ruled; household relations involve analogous but lesser forms of justice.
Notes: This passage reflects Aristotle's views on household governance (oikonomia) elaborated in Politics I.12-13. -- The claim that slaves and children are 'parts' of the master/father is deeply problematic by modern standards; Aristotle's defense of natural slavery in Politics I.4-7 is one of the most criticized aspects of his philosophy. -- The distinction between household and political justice influenced Roman law's distinction between ius gentium and ius civile, and medieval discussions of different spheres of authority. -- The claim that justice toward wives is more complete than toward children or slaves represents a limited recognition of wives' higher status, though Aristotle still considers the husband-wife relationship asymmetrical (Politics I.12).

D: Original: Of political justice part is natural, part legal, natural, that which everywhere has the same force and does not exist by people's thinking this or that; legal, that which is originally indifferent, but when it has been laid down is not indifferent, e.g. that a prisoner's ransom shall be a mina, or that a goat and not two sheep shall be sacrificed, and again all the laws that are passed for particular cases, e.g. that sacrifice shall be made in honour of Brasidas, and the provisions of decrees. Now some think that all justice is of this sort, because that which is by nature is unchangeable and has everywhere the same force (as fire burns both here and in Persia), while they see change in the things recognized as just. This, however, is not true in this unqualified way, but is true in a sense; or rather, with the gods it is perhaps not true at all, while with us there is something that is just even by nature, yet all of it is changeable; but still some is by nature, some not by nature. It is evident which sort of thing, among things capable of being otherwise, is by nature, and which is not but is legal and conventional, assuming that both are equally changeable. And in all other things the same distinction will apply; by nature the right hand is stronger, yet it is possible that all men should come to be ambidextrous. The things which are just by virtue of convention and expediency are like measures; for wine and corn measures are not everywhere equal, but larger in wholesale and smaller in retail markets. Similarly, the things which are just not by nature but by human enactment are not everywhere the same, since constitutions also are not the same, though there is but one which is everywhere by nature the best. Of things just and lawful each is related as the universal to its particulars; for the things that are done are many, but of them each is one, since it is universal.
D: Refashioned:
Of political justice, part is natural and part is legal. Natural justice has the same force everywhere and does not depend on what people happen to think; legal justice concerns matters originally indifferent that become fixed once established -- for example, that a prisoner's ransom shall be one mina, or that a goat rather than two sheep shall be sacrificed, as well as all laws passed for particular cases (such as that sacrifice shall be made in honor of Brasidas) and the provisions of specific decrees.

Now some people think all justice is merely legal, because what is natural is unchangeable and has the same force everywhere (as fire burns both here and in Persia), while they observe change in what is recognized as just. This, however, is not true without qualification -- though it is true in a sense. Among the gods, perhaps nothing changes at all; but among us, while there is something that is just by nature, all of it is nonetheless changeable. Still, some things are just by nature and some not by nature but by convention.

It is evident which sort of thing is by nature and which is legal and conventional, assuming both are equally changeable. The same distinction applies in other domains: by nature the right hand is stronger, yet it is possible for all people to become ambidextrous. Things that are just by convention and expediency are like standard measures: wine and grain measures are not the same everywhere, but larger in wholesale and smaller in retail markets. Similarly, what is just by human enactment rather than nature is not the same everywhere, since constitutions also vary -- though there is but one constitution that is everywhere by nature the best.

Each thing just and lawful relates to particular actions as the universal relates to its particulars: the actions performed are many, but each rule is one, since it is universal.
D: Elegant: Political justice is partly natural (having the same force everywhere, independent of human opinion) and partly legal (originally indifferent, binding once established). Some deny natural justice exists because human conceptions of justice change -- but fire burns the same everywhere, and similarly there is natural justice, even though among humans all things admit of change. The distinction is like that between natural right-handedness and acquired ambidexterity, or between varying local measures and the naturally best constitution. Legal rules relate to specific acts as universals to particulars.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes natural justice (which has the same force everywhere regardless of opinion) from legal justice (conventions that become binding once established). Against those who claim all justice is conventional because it varies, he argues that natural justice exists even though it admits of change among humans. Just as right-handedness is natural though ambidexterity is possible, natural justice is real though not absolutely fixed.
D: Distilled: Natural justice has universal force independent of convention, but unlike divine things, even natural justice among humans admits of variation and change.
Notes: This is one of the most important passages in the history of natural law theory, foundational for the entire tradition from the Stoics through Aquinas (ST I-II, Q.94) to modern natural law theorists like John Finnis. -- Brasidas was a Spartan general killed at Amphipolis in 422 BCE, afterward honored with sacrifices as a hero-founder. -- The fire analogy (burning the same in Greece and Persia) recalls Heraclitus and presocratic natural philosophy. -- The claim that natural justice is changeable among humans while perhaps unchangeable among gods is philosophically nuanced and distinguishes Aristotle's position from both rigid natural law (Stoics) and pure conventionalism (Sophists like Thrasymachus and Callicles). -- The 'one constitution everywhere by nature the best' is discussed in Politics III.7 and VII.1-3. -- This passage is central to the debate between Leo Strauss (Natural Right and History, 1953) and historicist interpretations of Aristotle.

D: Original:
There is a difference between the act of injustice and what is unjust, and between the act of justice and what is just; for a thing is unjust by nature or by enactment; and this very thing, when it has been done, is an act of injustice, but before it is done is not yet that but is unjust. So, too, with an act of justice (though the general term is rather 'just action', and 'act of justice' is applied to the correction of the act of injustice).

Each of these must later be examined separately with regard to the nature and number of its species and the nature of the things with which it is concerned.
D: Refashioned:
There is a difference between an act of injustice and what is unjust, and between an act of justice and what is just. A thing may be unjust either by nature or by enactment; and this very thing, when it has been done, is an act of injustice -- but before it is done, it is not yet that; it is merely unjust. So too with an act of justice, though the general term is better rendered as 'just action,' since 'act of justice' is more properly applied to the correction of an act of injustice.

Each of these must later be examined separately regarding the nature and number of its species and the kinds of things with which it is concerned.
D: Elegant: What is unjust (by nature or convention) becomes an act of injustice only when performed; before performance, it is unjust but not yet an act of injustice. Similarly, 'just action' refers to doing what is just, while 'act of justice' more precisely denotes correcting injustice. Each requires further analysis of its species and subject matter.
D: Summary: Aristotle draws a subtle distinction between what is unjust (a state of affairs defined by nature or law) and an act of injustice (the actual performance of it). Similarly, 'just action' and 'act of justice' differ: the latter specifically refers to correcting injustice. He signals that further detailed analysis will follow.
D: Distilled: What is unjust becomes an act of injustice only when actually performed, just as 'act of justice' properly refers to the correction of a wrong already done.
Notes: This distinction between the unjust thing (to adikon) and the act of injustice (adikema) is a careful piece of philosophical terminology that has parallels in modern philosophy of action. -- The preference for 'just action' (dikaipraxia) over 'act of justice' (dikaioma) as the general term reflects Aristotle's attention to linguistic precision. -- The reference to future examination signals the transition to the discussion of voluntariness and responsibility in chapters 8-9.

D: Original: Acts just and unjust being as we have described them, a man acts unjustly or justly whenever he does such acts voluntarily; when involuntarily, he acts neither unjustly nor justly except in an incidental way; for he does things which happen to be just or unjust. Whether an act is or is not one of injustice (or of justice) is determined by its voluntariness or involuntariness; for when it is voluntary it is blamed, and at the same time is then an act of injustice; so that there will be things that are unjust but not yet acts of injustice, if voluntariness be not present as well. By the voluntary I mean, as has been said before, any of the things in a man's own power which he does with knowledge, i.e. not in ignorance either of the person acted on or of the instrument used or of the end that will be attained (e.g. whom he is striking, with what, and to what end), each such act being done not incidentally nor under compulsion (e.g. if A takes B's hand and therewith strikes C, B does not act voluntarily; for the act was not in his own power). The person struck may be the striker's father, and the striker may know that it is a man or one of the persons present, but not know that it is his father; a similar distinction may be made in the case of the end, and with regard to the whole action.
D: Refashioned:
With just and unjust acts described as above, a person acts unjustly or justly whenever they perform such acts voluntarily. When they act involuntarily, they act neither unjustly nor justly, except in an incidental way -- for they do things that happen to be just or unjust without intending them as such.

Whether an act constitutes an injustice or a just deed is determined by its voluntariness or involuntariness. When an act is voluntary, it is blamed, and at the same time it becomes an act of injustice. So there will be things that are unjust but not yet acts of injustice, if voluntariness is absent as well.

By 'voluntary' I mean, as stated before, anything within a person's power that they do with knowledge -- not in ignorance of the person acted upon, the instrument used, or the end to be attained (for example, whom they are striking, with what, and to what end). Each such element must be neither incidental nor performed under compulsion. (For instance, if A takes B's hand and uses it to strike C, B does not act voluntarily, since the act was not within B's power.) The person struck might be the striker's father, and the striker might know it is a person or someone present, but not know it is their father. A similar distinction applies to the end and to the action as a whole.
D: Elegant: Voluntariness determines whether an act counts as an injustice or a just deed. Involuntary acts that happen to be just or unjust are so only incidentally. The voluntary requires acting within one's power with knowledge of the person affected, the instrument, and the intended result -- not incidentally or under compulsion. One may know one is striking a person without knowing it is one's father.
D: Summary: Aristotle establishes that the moral and legal character of an act depends on its voluntariness. Only voluntary acts -- performed knowingly and within one's power, without compulsion -- count as genuine acts of injustice or justice. Involuntary acts may coincidentally be just or unjust but are so only incidentally. Knowledge must extend to the person, instrument, and purpose of the act.
D: Distilled: An act becomes a genuine injustice or just deed only when performed voluntarily -- with knowledge of the person, instrument, and purpose, and without compulsion.
Notes: This passage applies the general theory of voluntariness developed in Book III.1-5 to the specific context of justice. -- The example of A using B's hand to strike C illustrates the requirement that the action be 'in one's power' (eph' hauto). -- The father-striking example recurs in Aristotle and connects to tragic scenarios in Greek drama (e.g., Oedipus). -- The distinction between 'in ignorance' and incidental ignorance is crucial and anticipates modern discussions of mens rea in criminal law. -- This analysis influenced the entire Western legal tradition's treatment of intent, knowledge, and culpability.

D: Original: Therefore that which is done in ignorance, or though not done in ignorance is not in the agent's power, or is done under compulsion, is involuntary (for many natural processes, even, we knowingly both perform and experience, none of which is either voluntary or involuntary; e.g. growing old or dying). But in the case of unjust and just acts alike the injustice or justice may be only incidental; for a man might return a deposit unwillingly and from fear, and then he must not be said either to do what is just or to act justly, except in an incidental way.
D: Refashioned:
What is done in ignorance, or what is not in the agent's power though not done in ignorance, or what is done under compulsion, is involuntary. For we knowingly both perform and undergo many natural processes, none of which is either voluntary or involuntary -- growing old or dying, for example.

But in the case of unjust and just acts alike, the injustice or justice may be only incidental. A person might return a deposit unwillingly and out of fear, and we should not say they are doing what is just or acting justly, except in an incidental way.
D: Elegant: Actions done in ignorance, beyond the agent's power, or under compulsion are involuntary. Natural processes like aging and dying, though knowingly undergone, are neither voluntary nor involuntary. Justice and injustice may be incidental: returning a deposit from fear rather than choice is just only incidentally, not genuinely.
D: Summary: Aristotle clarifies that involuntary acts include those done in ignorance, beyond one's power, or under compulsion. Natural processes are in a separate category entirely. Even apparently just acts -- like returning a deposit out of fear rather than choice -- are just only incidentally, not in the full moral sense.
D: Distilled: True justice requires voluntary choice, not mere compliance; returning a deposit out of fear is just only incidentally.
Notes: The deposit example was a standard test case in Greek ethical discussion, also used by Plato in Republic I (331c) where Cephalus defines justice as returning what is owed. -- The distinction between incidental and genuine justice reinforces the importance of prohairesis (deliberate choice) established in Book III. -- The category of natural processes (neither voluntary nor involuntary) shows the limits of the voluntary/involuntary framework. -- This connects to the Stoic distinction between what is 'up to us' (eph' hemin) and what is not, which Epictetus later developed extensively.

D: Original: Similarly the man who under compulsion and unwillingly fails to return the deposit must be said to act unjustly, and to do what is unjust, only incidentally. Of voluntary acts we do some by choice, others not by choice; by choice those which we do after deliberation, not by choice those which we do without previous deliberation. Thus there are three kinds of injury in transactions between man and man; those done in ignorance are mistakes when the person acted on, the act, the instrument, or the end that will be attained is other than the agent supposed; the agent thought either that he was not hiting any one or that he was not hitting with this missile or not hitting this person or to this end, but a result followed other than that which he thought likely (e.g. he threw not with intent to wound but only to prick), or the person hit or the missile was other than he supposed.
D: Refashioned:
Similarly, the person who under compulsion and unwillingly fails to return a deposit must be said to act unjustly, and to do what is unjust, only incidentally.

Of voluntary acts, we do some by deliberate choice and others not. Those done after deliberation are by choice; those done without prior deliberation are not.

Thus there are three kinds of injury in dealings between people. Those done in ignorance are mistakes -- when the person acted upon, the act itself, the instrument, or the expected result is other than the agent supposed. The agent thought either that they were not hitting anyone, or not with this weapon, or not this particular person, or not to this end; but a result followed other than what they thought likely. For example, they threw something not intending to wound but only to prick, or the person hit or the weapon was different from what they supposed.
D: Elegant: Compelled failure to return a deposit is unjust only incidentally. Voluntary acts divide into those done by deliberate choice and those without prior deliberation. Three kinds of injury exist: mistakes (done in ignorance, where the person, act, instrument, or result differs from what the agent supposed), misadventures, and deliberate wrongs.
D: Summary: Aristotle continues classifying injuries. Acts under compulsion are unjust only incidentally. He distinguishes voluntary acts done by deliberate choice from those done without deliberation, then identifies mistakes as one of three kinds of injury -- cases where ignorance about the person, instrument, or result leads to an unintended outcome.
D: Distilled: Mistakes are injuries arising from ignorance about the person, instrument, or result, where the outcome differs from the agent's reasonable expectation.
Notes: The threefold classification of injuries (mistakes, misadventures, and deliberate wrongs) in 5.33-5.35 is one of Aristotle's most important contributions to legal theory. -- The distinction between acts of choice (ek proaireseōs) and voluntary acts without choice is subtle but important: all chosen acts are voluntary, but not all voluntary acts are chosen (cf. NE III.2). -- This taxonomy directly influenced Roman law's categories of culpability and, through them, modern criminal law's distinctions between negligence, recklessness, and intent.

D: Original: Now when (1) the injury takes place contrary to reasonable expectation, it is a misadventure. When (2) it is not contrary to reasonable expectation, but does not imply vice, it is a mistake (for a man makes a mistake when the fault originates in him, but is the victim of accident when the origin lies outside him). When (3) he acts with knowledge but not after deliberation, it is an act of injustice-e.g. the acts due to anger or to other passions necessary or natural to man; for when men do such harmful and mistaken acts they act unjustly, and the acts are acts of injustice, but this does not imply that the doers are unjust or wicked; for the injury is not due to vice.
D: Refashioned: Now (1) when the injury occurs contrary to reasonable expectation, it is a misadventure. (2) When it is not contrary to reasonable expectation but does not involve vice, it is a mistake -- for a person makes a mistake when the fault originates in them, but is a victim of accident when the origin lies outside them. (3) When a person acts with knowledge but without prior deliberation, it is an act of injustice -- for example, acts due to anger or other passions that are natural and necessary to human beings. When people commit such harmful and misguided acts, they act unjustly and the acts are acts of injustice, but this does not imply that the doers are unjust or wicked, since the injury is not due to vice.
D: Elegant: A misadventure is an injury contrary to reasonable expectation. A mistake occurs when the fault originates in the agent but without vice. When someone acts knowingly but without deliberation -- from anger or natural passion -- the act is unjust, but the agent is not thereby an unjust or wicked person, since the injury stems from passion rather than vice.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes three categories of injury: misadventures (results no one could foresee), mistakes (foreseeable errors without vicious intent), and acts of injustice from passion (done knowingly but without deliberation, such as in anger). The third category produces genuine acts of injustice but does not make the agent an unjust person, since the cause is passion rather than settled vice.
D: Distilled: Injuries from unforeseeable accidents are misadventures; foreseeable errors without vice are mistakes; knowing acts from passion are unjust but do not reveal unjust character.
Notes: This classification of misadventure (atuchēma), mistake (hamartēma), and act of injustice (adikēma) is foundational for Western legal thought. -- The category of hamartēma (mistake/error) connects to the concept of hamartia in Aristotle's Poetics, the tragic hero's error. -- The claim that acts from anger are unjust but not wicked reflects the analysis of anger (thumos) in Book IV and Rhetoric II. -- Modern criminal law's distinction between strict liability, negligence, recklessness, and intent broadly corresponds to Aristotle's framework. -- This passage directly influenced Aquinas' treatment of circumstances of action (ST I-II, Q.7).

D: Original:
But when (4) a man acts from choice, he is an unjust man and a vicious man.

Hence acts proceeding from anger are rightly judged not to be done of malice aforethought; for it is not the man who acts in anger but he who enraged him that starts the mischief. Again, the matter in dispute is not whether the thing happened or not, but its justice; for it is apparent injustice that occasions rage. For they do not dispute about the occurrence of the act-as in commercial transactions where one of the two parties must be vicious-unless they do so owing to forgetfulness; but, agreeing about the fact, they dispute on which side justice lies (whereas a man who has deliberately injured another cannot help knowing that he has done so), so that the one thinks he is being treated unjustly and the other disagrees.
D: Refashioned:
(4) But when a person acts from deliberate choice, they are an unjust person and a vicious person.

Hence acts proceeding from anger are rightly judged not to be done with malice aforethought -- for it is not the person who acts in anger but the one who provoked the anger that starts the trouble. Furthermore, the matter in dispute is not whether the act occurred, but whether it was just. It is apparent injustice that provokes anger. The parties do not dispute about whether the act happened -- as happens in commercial transactions where one party must be acting in bad faith -- unless forgetfulness is the cause. Rather, agreeing on the facts, they dispute which side justice favors. (A person who has deliberately injured another cannot be unaware of having done so.) Thus one party thinks they are being treated unjustly, while the other disagrees.
D: Elegant: When someone acts from deliberate choice, they are truly unjust and vicious. Acts from anger are rightly judged not premeditated -- the provoker, not the angry person, initiates the conflict. In anger-driven disputes, the facts are not contested; what is contested is whether justice was done. By contrast, the deliberate wrongdoer knows full well what they have done.
D: Summary: Aristotle completes his taxonomy: only those who act from deliberate choice are truly unjust and vicious. Acts from anger are not premeditated because the provoker bears initial responsibility. In disputes arising from anger, both sides agree on what happened but disagree about justice -- unlike commercial disputes where the facts themselves may be contested.
D: Distilled: Only deliberate choice to do wrong makes a person truly unjust; anger-driven wrongs lack premeditation because the provocation, not the agent's character, initiates the conflict.
Notes: The distinction between premeditated injustice (ek proaireseōs) and impulsive injustice (from anger) was important in Athenian law, which distinguished between phonos ek pronoias (premeditated homicide, tried at the Areopagus) and phonos akousios (unintentional homicide, tried at the Palladion). -- The observation that anger-disputes concern justice rather than facts is psychologically astute. -- 'Malice aforethought' (pronoia) is a legal term in Athenian law. -- This passage influenced the common law distinction between murder (premeditated) and manslaughter (provoked/impulsive).

D: Original:
But if a man harms another by choice, he acts unjustly; and these are the acts of injustice which imply that the doer is an unjust man, provided that the act violates proportion or equality. Similarly, a man is just when he acts justly by choice; but he acts justly if he merely acts voluntarily.

Of involuntary acts some are excusable, others not. For the mistakes which men make not only in ignorance but also from ignorance are excusable, while those which men do not from ignorance but (though they do them in ignorance) owing to a passion which is neither natural nor such as man is liable to, are not excusable.
D: Refashioned:
If a person harms another by deliberate choice, they act unjustly; and these are the acts of injustice that imply the doer is an unjust person, provided that the act violates proportion or equality. Similarly, a person is just when they act justly by choice; but a person acts justly if they merely act voluntarily.

Of involuntary acts, some are excusable and others not. Mistakes that people make not only in ignorance but from ignorance are excusable, while those done not from ignorance but in ignorance, owing to a passion that is neither natural nor characteristic of human beings, are not excusable.
D: Elegant: Deliberate harm violating proportion makes one unjust in character. One is just by choosing to act justly; merely acting voluntarily suffices for a just action. Among involuntary acts, those done from ignorance are excusable, but those done in ignorance due to unnatural passion are not.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes between being just (requiring deliberate choice) and acting justly (requiring only voluntariness). He also draws a line between excusable and inexcusable involuntary acts: mistakes arising from genuine ignorance are excusable, but those arising from unnatural passion -- where ignorance is self-caused rather than the source of the act -- are not.
D: Distilled: Deliberate choice to violate fairness makes one unjust; and while genuine ignorance excuses, ignorance caused by unnatural passion does not.
Notes: The distinction between acting 'in ignorance' (en agnoiai) and 'from ignorance' (di' agnoian) is crucial and comes from NE III.1 (1110b24-27). Acting from ignorance means the ignorance causes the act; acting in ignorance means one is ignorant but the ignorance is not the true cause (e.g., the real cause is drunkenness). -- The category of 'passions neither natural nor human' may refer to extreme states like homicidal rage or bestial behavior discussed in NE VII.5. -- This passage's distinction between just character and just action anticipates modern virtue ethics' emphasis on the agent's dispositions versus act-evaluation.

D: Original:
Assuming that we have sufficiently defined the suffering and doing of injustice, it may be asked (1) whether the truth in expressed in Euripides' paradoxical words:

I slew my mother, that's my tale in brief. Were you both willing, or unwilling both?
D: Refashioned:
Assuming we have sufficiently defined the suffering and doing of injustice, one might ask whether there is truth in Euripides' paradoxical words:

'I slew my mother, that's my tale in brief.'
'Were you both willing, or unwilling both?'
D: Elegant: Euripides poses the question sharply: when Alcmaeon killed his mother, were both parties willing -- or both unwilling?
D: Summary: Aristotle introduces a new puzzle about justice by quoting Euripides: can someone be willingly killed, and can the killer be simultaneously unwilling? This dramatic question opens the inquiry into whether being unjustly treated can ever be voluntary.
D: Distilled: Euripides' paradox of mutual willingness in a killing opens the question of whether one can voluntarily suffer injustice.
Notes: The quotation is from Euripides' lost play Alcmaeon in Corinth (or possibly Alcmaeon in Psophis). Alcmaeon killed his mother Eriphyle to avenge his father Amphiaraus, whom she had betrayed. -- Euripides (c. 480-406 BCE) was the youngest of the three great Athenian tragedians. -- The paradox of willing victim and unwilling killer was a favorite philosophical puzzle in the Academy. -- This passage marks the beginning of chapter 9 (in modern numbering), which explores several puzzles about justice.

D: Original: Is it truly possible to be willingly treated unjustly, or is all suffering of injustice the contrary involuntary, as all unjust action is voluntary? And is all suffering of injustice of the latter kind or else all of the former, or is it sometimes voluntary, sometimes involuntary? So, too, with the case of being justly treated; all just action is voluntary, so that it is reasonable that there should be a similar opposition in either case-that both being unjustly and being justly treated should be either alike voluntary or alike involuntary. But it would be thought paradoxical even in the case of being justly treated, if it were always voluntary; for some are unwillingly treated justly. (2) One might raise this question also, whether every one who has suffered what is unjust is being unjustly treated, or on the other hand it is with suffering as with acting. In action and in passivity alike it is possible to partake of justice incidentally, and similarly (it is plain) of injustice; for to do what is unjust is not the same as to act unjustly, nor to suffer what is unjust as to be treated unjustly, and similarly in the case of acting justly and being justly treated; for it is impossible to be unjustly treated if the other does not act unjustly, or justly treated unless he acts justly.
D: Refashioned:
Is it truly possible to be willingly treated unjustly, or is all suffering of injustice involuntary -- just as all unjust action is voluntary? Is all suffering of injustice of one kind, or the other, or sometimes voluntary and sometimes involuntary?

The same question arises for being justly treated: all just action is voluntary, so it seems reasonable that there should be a parallel opposition in each case -- that both being unjustly and being justly treated should be either alike voluntary or alike involuntary. But it would be paradoxical even in the case of being justly treated to say it is always voluntary, for some people are unwillingly treated justly.

(2) One might also raise this question: is everyone who has suffered something unjust thereby being unjustly treated? Or is it with suffering as with acting -- that one can partake of justice incidentally in both action and passion? So too with injustice, clearly. For to do what is unjust is not the same as to act unjustly, nor to suffer what is unjust the same as to be unjustly treated. The same applies to acting justly and being justly treated. For it is impossible to be unjustly treated unless someone acts unjustly, or to be justly treated unless someone acts justly.
D: Elegant: Is suffering injustice always involuntary, as committing it is always voluntary? Both being unjustly and justly treated seem they should match the voluntary or involuntary nature of the corresponding actions -- yet some people are unwillingly treated justly. Moreover, suffering something unjust is not the same as being unjustly treated, just as doing something unjust is not the same as acting unjustly. Being unjustly treated requires someone who acts unjustly; being justly treated requires someone who acts justly.
D: Summary: Aristotle explores whether suffering injustice is always involuntary, noting a parallel with just treatment -- some are unwillingly treated justly. He distinguishes between suffering something unjust (which can be incidental) and genuinely being unjustly treated (which requires an agent who acts unjustly). The same distinction applies to just treatment.
D: Distilled: Suffering something unjust is not the same as being unjustly treated; the latter requires an agent who voluntarily acts unjustly.
Notes: The distinction between suffering what is unjust (paschein ta adika) and being unjustly treated (adikeisthai) is philosophically important and parallels the earlier distinction between doing what is unjust and acting unjustly. -- The observation that some are unwillingly treated justly (e.g., criminals punished against their will) complicates simple parallels between action and passion in justice. -- This analysis anticipates modern discussions of consent in political philosophy, particularly regarding justice imposed without consent.

D: Original: Now if to act unjustly is simply to harm some one voluntarily, and 'voluntarily' means 'knowing the person acted on, the instrument, and the manner of one's acting', and the incontinent man voluntarily harms himself, not only will he voluntarily be unjustly treated but it will be possible to treat oneself unjustly. (This also is one of the questions in doubt, whether a man can treat himself unjustly.) Again, a man may voluntarily, owing to incontinence, be harmed by another who acts voluntarily, so that it would be possible to be voluntarily treated unjustly. Or is our definition incorrect; must we to 'harming another, with knowledge both of the person acted on, of the instrument, and of the manner' add 'contrary to the wish of the person acted on'? Then a man may be voluntarily harmed and voluntarily suffer what is unjust, but no one is voluntarily treated unjustly; for no one wishes to be unjustly treated, not even the incontinent man. He acts contrary to his wish; for no one wishes for what he does not think to be good, but the incontinent man does do things that he does not think he ought to do. Again, one who gives what is his own, as Homer says Glaucus gave Diomede
D: Refashioned:
Now if to act unjustly is simply to harm someone voluntarily, and 'voluntarily' means 'knowing the person acted upon, the instrument, and the manner of acting,' then the person who lacks self-control and voluntarily harms themselves would be voluntarily treated unjustly -- and it would be possible to treat oneself unjustly. (This is in fact one of the questions under debate: whether a person can treat themselves unjustly.)

Again, a person might voluntarily -- owing to lack of self-control -- be harmed by another who acts voluntarily, making it seemingly possible to be voluntarily treated unjustly.

Or is our definition incorrect? Must we add to 'harming another, with knowledge of the person, instrument, and manner' the further condition 'contrary to the wish of the person acted upon'? Then a person may be voluntarily harmed and voluntarily suffer what is unjust, but no one is voluntarily treated unjustly -- for no one wishes to be unjustly treated, not even the person who lacks self-control. Such a person acts contrary to their wish, since no one wishes for what they do not think is good, and the person lacking self-control does things they know they ought not to do.

Again, one who gives away what is their own -- as Homer says Glaucus gave Diomedes --
D: Elegant: If acting unjustly means voluntarily harming someone with knowledge, then self-harming through lack of self-control would constitute self-injustice. But the definition needs refinement: 'contrary to the wish of the person acted upon' must be added. One can be voluntarily harmed, but no one is voluntarily treated unjustly -- no one wishes for injustice, not even the weak-willed, who act against their own better judgment.
D: Summary: Aristotle tests whether someone can treat themselves unjustly or voluntarily suffer injustice. If acting unjustly simply means voluntary harm, then self-harm through weakness of will would be self-injustice. But Aristotle refines the definition: genuine unjust treatment requires acting against the victim's wishes. Since no one -- not even the weak-willed person -- wishes to be treated unjustly, voluntary unjust treatment of oneself is impossible.
D: Distilled: No one is voluntarily treated unjustly, because injustice requires acting against the victim's wish, and no one wishes to suffer injustice -- not even those who lack self-control.
Notes: The question of whether one can treat oneself unjustly was a recognized philosophical puzzle, discussed also in Plato's Laws IX (860d-861d). -- The analysis of akrasia (lack of self-control/incontinence) connects to the extended treatment in NE VII.1-10, one of Aristotle's most famous discussions. -- The key move -- adding 'contrary to the wish of the person acted upon' to the definition -- is a significant refinement that shows Aristotle's method of testing definitions against counterexamples. -- The Glaucus-Diomedes reference anticipates the next passage; this is from Homer's Iliad VI.234-236.

D: Original:
Armour of gold for brazen, the price of a hundred beeves for nine, is not unjustly treated; for though to give is in his power, to be unjustly treated is not, but there must be some one to treat him unjustly. It is plain, then, that being unjustly treated is not voluntary.

Of the questions we intended to discuss two still remain for discussion; (3) whether it is the man who has assigned to another more than his share that acts unjustly, or he who has the excessive share, and (4) whether it is possible to treat oneself unjustly. The questions are connected; for if the former alternative is possible and the distributor acts unjustly and not the man who has the excessive share, then if a man assigns more to another than to himself, knowingly and voluntarily, he treats himself unjustly; which is what modest people seem to do, since the virtuous man tends to take less than his share. Or does this statement too need qualification? For (a) he perhaps gets more than his share of some other good, e.g. of honour or of intrinsic nobility. (b) The question is solved by applying the distinction we applied to unjust action; for he suffers nothing contrary to his own wish, so that he is not unjustly treated as far as this goes, but at most only suffers harm.
D: Refashioned:
'Armor of gold for bronze, the price of a hundred oxen for nine' -- yet Glaucus is not unjustly treated, for though giving is in his power, being unjustly treated is not; there must be someone to treat him unjustly. It is plain, then, that being unjustly treated is not voluntary.

Two of the questions we intended to discuss still remain: (3) whether it is the person who has assigned to another more than their share who acts unjustly, or the one who has the excessive share, and (4) whether it is possible to treat oneself unjustly.

The questions are connected, for if the first alternative is possible -- the distributor acts unjustly and not the person who has the excessive share -- then if someone knowingly and voluntarily assigns more to another than to themselves, they treat themselves unjustly. This is what modest people seem to do, since the virtuous person tends to take less than their share.

Or does this claim need qualification? For (a) perhaps such a person gets more of some other good in return -- honor or intrinsic nobility, for example. (b) The question is also resolved by applying the distinction we drew regarding unjust action: the person suffers nothing contrary to their own wish, so they are not unjustly treated -- at most, they merely suffer harm.
D: Elegant: Glaucus, who gave gold armor for bronze, was not unjustly treated -- giving was in his power, but being treated unjustly was not, since that requires an unjust agent. As for whether someone who voluntarily takes less than their share treats themselves unjustly: the modest person who does so gains honor or nobility in return, and since nothing happens contrary to their wish, they are not treated unjustly but merely suffer material harm.
D: Summary: Aristotle uses the Homeric example of Glaucus exchanging gold armor for bronze to show that voluntary giving, even at a loss, is not being unjustly treated -- injustice requires an agent who acts unjustly against the victim's will. Similarly, the virtuous person who voluntarily takes less than their share is not self-unjust: they gain honor or nobility, and since nothing happens against their wish, they at most suffer harm, not injustice.
D: Distilled: Voluntarily taking less than one's share is not self-injustice because nothing occurs against one's wish, and the sacrifice may be compensated by honor or nobility.
Notes: The Glaucus-Diomedes exchange (Iliad VI.232-236) was proverbial in antiquity for a foolishly generous trade -- Zeus stole Glaucus' wits, making him trade gold for bronze. -- The observation that modest people take less connects to the virtue of megalopsuchia (greatness of soul) in NE IV.3, where the magnanimous person is concerned with honor. -- The distinction between suffering harm and being treated unjustly is crucial: material disadvantage without violation of one's wishes is harm, not injustice. -- This analysis has implications for consent theory in modern political philosophy, particularly regarding voluntary acceptance of disadvantage.

D: Original: It is plain too that the distributor acts unjustly, but not always the man who has the excessive share; for it is not he to whom what is unjust appertains that acts unjustly, but he to whom it appertains to do the unjust act voluntarily, i.e. the person in whom lies the origin of the action, and this lies in the distributor, not in the receiver. Again, since the word 'do' is ambiguous, and there is a sense in which lifeless things, or a hand, or a servant who obeys an order, may be said to slay, he who gets an excessive share does not act unjustly, though he 'does' what is unjust.
D: Refashioned:
It is also clear that the distributor acts unjustly, not always the person who receives the excessive share. For it is not the person who ends up with what is unjust who acts unjustly, but the person who voluntarily does the unjust act -- that is, the person in whom the origin of the action lies. And this lies in the distributor, not in the receiver.

Furthermore, since the word 'do' is ambiguous -- lifeless things, or a hand, or a servant obeying an order may be said to 'do' the killing -- the person who receives an excessive share does not act unjustly, though they 'do' what is unjust.
D: Elegant: The distributor, not the recipient of excess, acts unjustly -- because injustice lies with the person in whom the action originates. Just as a sword or a servant obeying orders may 'do' a killing without acting unjustly, the recipient 'does' what is unjust without committing injustice.
D: Summary: Aristotle clarifies that responsibility for unjust distribution lies with the distributor, not the recipient. The origin of the action determines moral responsibility. Just as a weapon or an obedient servant may be involved in killing without being the agent of injustice, the recipient of an unfair share receives what is unjust without acting unjustly.
D: Distilled: Injustice in distribution lies with the distributor who originates the act, not with the recipient who merely receives the result.
Notes: The principle that moral responsibility attaches to the agent who originates the action (arche tes praxeōs) is fundamental to Aristotle's action theory (NE III.1-3). -- The ambiguity of 'doing' (poiein) -- covering both agents and instruments -- anticipates modern discussions of moral agency, complicity, and the distinction between principals and accessories. -- The servant example raises questions about moral responsibility under orders, prefiguring debates from Aquinas through the Nuremberg trials. -- This passage has been important for legal theories of vicarious liability and respondeat superior.

D: Original: Again, if the distributor gave his judgement in ignorance, he does not act unjustly in respect of legal justice, and his judgement is not unjust in this sense, but in a sense it is unjust (for legal justice and primordial justice are different); but if with knowledge he judged unjustly, he is himself aiming at an excessive share either of gratitude or of revenge. As much, then, as if he were to share in the plunder, the man who has judged unjustly for these reasons has got too much; the fact that what he gets is different from what he distributes makes no difference, for even if he awards land with a view to sharing in the plunder he gets not land but money.
D: Refashioned: Again, if the distributor rendered judgment in ignorance, they do not act unjustly with respect to legal justice, and their judgment is not unjust in that sense -- though in another sense it is unjust (for legal justice and primordial justice are different). But if the distributor knowingly judged unjustly, they are seeking an excessive share for themselves -- either of gratitude or of revenge. Just as surely as if they shared in the plunder, the person who judged unjustly for such reasons has gotten too much. The fact that what they receive differs from what they distribute makes no difference -- for even if they award land with a view to sharing in the plunder, they get not land but money.
D: Elegant: A distributor who judges wrongly from ignorance does not commit legal injustice, though the judgment may violate primordial justice. But one who knowingly judges unjustly -- seeking gratitude or revenge -- is as guilty as if they shared in the plunder directly. That the judge receives a different currency (money or favors rather than the distributed goods) is irrelevant.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes between a distributor who errs from ignorance (not legally unjust though perhaps unjust in a deeper sense) and one who knowingly renders an unjust judgment seeking personal gain through gratitude or revenge. The corrupt judge is effectively taking an excessive share, regardless of whether the benefit received differs in kind from what was distributed.
D: Distilled: A judge who knowingly decides unjustly for personal benefit -- whether gratitude, revenge, or indirect gain -- is as guilty as if they directly took more than their share.
Notes: The distinction between legal justice (to nomimon dikaion) and primordial or natural justice (to proton dikaion) recapitulates the natural/legal justice distinction from 5.29 in a practical judicial context. -- Corruption of judges was a serious concern in Athenian democracy, where large citizen juries decided cases. -- The insight that corrupt judges 'share in the plunder' regardless of the form their benefit takes is remarkably modern in its analysis of corruption. -- This passage has been cited in discussions of judicial ethics from Aquinas to modern jurisprudence.

D: Original: Men think that acting unjustly is in their power, and therefore that being just is easy. But it is not; to lie with one's neighbour's wife, to wound another, to deliver a bribe, is easy and in our power, but to do these things as a result of a certain state of character is neither easy nor in our power. Similarly to know what is just and what is unjust requires, men think, no great wisdom, because it is not hard to understand the matters dealt with by the laws (though these are not the things that are just, except incidentally); but how actions must be done and distributions effected in order to be just, to know this is a greater achievement than knowing what is good for the health; though even there, while it is easy to know that honey, wine, hellebore, cautery, and the use of the knife are so, to know how, to whom, and when these should be applied with a view to producing health, is no less an achievement than that of being a physician. Again, for this very reason men think that acting unjustly is characteristic of the just man no less than of the unjust, because he would be not less but even more capable of doing each of these unjust acts; for he could lie with a woman or wound a neighbour; and the brave man could throw away his shield and turn to flight in this direction or in that. But to play the coward or to act unjustly consists not in doing these things, except incidentally, but in doing them as the result of a certain state of character, just as to practise medicine and healing consists not in applying or not applying the knife, in using or not using medicines, but in doing so in a certain way.
D: Refashioned:
People think that acting unjustly is within their power, and therefore that being just is easy. But it is not. To sleep with a neighbor's spouse, to wound another, to deliver a bribe -- these are easy and within our power. But to do these things as the result of a certain state of character is neither easy nor within our power.

Similarly, people think that knowing what is just and unjust requires no great wisdom, because it is not hard to understand the matters dealt with by the laws. But these are not what is truly just, except incidentally. How actions must be performed and distributions made in order to be just -- knowing this is a greater achievement than knowing what promotes health. Even in medicine, while it is easy to know that honey, wine, hellebore, cautery, and the knife are remedies, knowing how, to whom, and when to apply them in order to produce health is no less an achievement than being a physician.

Again, for this very reason people think the just person is no less capable of acting unjustly than the unjust person -- indeed more so, since they could lie with a woman or wound someone, just as the courageous person could throw away their shield and flee. But being a coward or acting unjustly consists not in doing these things (except incidentally) but in doing them as the result of a certain state of character -- just as practicing medicine consists not in applying or withholding the knife, or using or not using drugs, but in doing so in a certain way.
D: Elegant: People wrongly think acting unjustly is easy and therefore being just is easy. Performing unjust acts is easy; performing them from an unjust character state is not. Similarly, knowing the laws is easy, but knowing how to distribute and act justly in specific cases is as difficult as practicing medicine -- where knowing remedies is easy but knowing when and how to apply them is the physician's art. The just person could perform unjust acts, but doing them would not make them unjust, just as a physician using or withholding the knife is defined not by the act but by doing so from the right disposition.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues against the common belief that justice is easy. While performing unjust acts is simple, doing them from a settled unjust character is not -- and this is what true injustice consists in. Similarly, knowing laws is easy, but knowing how to apply justice in particular cases is as difficult as medical practice. The just person has the capacity for unjust acts but would never perform them from the relevant character state.
