D: Distilled: Justice is not easy because it consists not in performing certain actions but in acting from the right character state -- just as medicine consists not in knowing remedies but in knowing how to apply them.
Notes: The medical analogy, comparing justice to the physician's art, is one of Aristotle's most powerful. It illustrates his general point that virtue is a practical disposition (hexis praktikē), not mere knowledge. -- The comparison with the courageous person throwing away their shield echoes the shield-discarding example in 5.7 and connects to the discussion of courage in NE III.6-9. -- Hellebore was widely used as a purgative in ancient medicine. -- This passage is a direct response to the Socratic/Platonic view that virtue is knowledge (epistēmē): Aristotle insists on the difference between knowing what is just and having the character to act justly. -- The passage also challenges the view in Republic I that the just person, like a skilled craftsman, would be equally adept at injustice.

D: Original: Just acts occur between people who participate in things good in themselves and can have too much or too little of them; for some beings (e.g. presumably the gods) cannot have too much of them, and to others, those who are incurably bad, not even the smallest share in them is beneficial but all such goods are harmful, while to others they are beneficial up to a point; therefore justice is essentially something human.
D: Refashioned: Just acts occur between people who participate in things good in themselves and can have too much or too little of them. For some beings -- presumably the gods -- cannot have too much of them, and for others -- those who are incurably bad -- not even the smallest share is beneficial, since all such goods are harmful to them. For yet others, these goods are beneficial up to a point. Therefore justice is essentially something human.
D: Elegant: Justice exists between beings who can have too much or too little of intrinsic goods. Gods cannot have excess; the incurably vicious are harmed by any share of goods; for the rest of us, goods are beneficial within limits. Justice is therefore essentially a human affair.
D: Summary: Aristotle limits the scope of justice to human beings: gods cannot have too much of what is good, the incurably wicked are harmed by any goods, and only humans occupy the middle ground where having the right amount matters. Justice is therefore an essentially human concern.
D: Distilled: Justice is essentially human because only humans occupy the middle ground where goods can be had in excess, deficiency, or proper measure.
Notes: The claim that gods cannot have too much good recalls the theological discussions in NE X.8, where Aristotle argues that the gods' activity is purely contemplative. -- The 'incurably bad' (aniatos) recalls the discussion of bestial vice in NE VII.5. -- The idea that external goods are beneficial only up to a point connects to the analysis of eudaimonia in NE I.8-10 and the discussion of external goods in Politics VII.1. -- This passage sets a boundary on the domain of justice: it is not cosmic or metaphysical but specifically tied to the human condition of living with others in a community.

D: Original: Our next subject is equity and the equitable (to epiekes), and their respective relations to justice and the just. For on examination they appear to be neither absolutely the same nor generically different; and while we sometime praise what is equitable and the equitable man (so that we apply the name by way of praise even to instances of the other virtues, instead of 'good' meaning by epieikestebon that a thing is better), at other times, when we reason it out, it seems strange if the equitable, being something different from the just, is yet praiseworthy; for either the just or the equitable is not good, if they are different; or, if both are good, they are the same.
D: Refashioned: Our next subject is equity and the equitable, and their respective relations to justice and the just. On examination, they appear to be neither absolutely the same nor generically different. Sometimes we praise what is equitable and the equitable person, even applying the term as praise to instances of other virtues -- using 'more equitable' to mean 'better.' At other times, when we reason it out, it seems strange that the equitable, being something different from the just, should be praiseworthy. For either the just or the equitable is not good, if they are different; or, if both are good, they are the same.
D: Elegant: Equity and justice appear neither identical nor fundamentally different. We praise equity -- even using 'equitable' as a general term for what is better -- yet if equity differs from justice, it seems either one must not be good, or if both are good, they must be the same. This is the puzzle.
D: Summary: Aristotle introduces the concept of equity (epieikeia) and poses a puzzle: equity and justice seem neither identical nor wholly different. We praise equity, even using it as a synonym for what is better, yet logically, if equity differs from justice, either one cannot be good -- or they must be the same thing.
D: Distilled: Equity appears to be both praiseworthy and different from justice, which creates the puzzle of how two different things in the same domain can both be good.
Notes: Epieikeia (equity) is one of Aristotle's most influential concepts, central to legal philosophy from Roman jurisprudence (aequitas) through Aquinas to modern equity law. -- The word epieikeia in ordinary Greek means 'reasonableness' or 'decency' and was used broadly as praise. -- This passage opens chapter 10, Aristotle's discussion of equity as a correction of legal justice -- one of the most cited passages in the history of jurisprudence. -- The puzzle format (aporia) is characteristic of Aristotle's dialectical method.

D: Original: These, then, are pretty much the considerations that give rise to the problem about the equitable; they are all in a sense correct and not opposed to one another; for the equitable, though it is better than one kind of justice, yet is just, and it is not as being a different class of thing that it is better than the just. The same thing, then, is just and equitable, and while both are good the equitable is superior. What creates the problem is that the equitable is just, but not the legally just but a correction of legal justice. The reason is that all law is universal but about some things it is not possible to make a universal statement which shall be correct. In those cases, then, in which it is necessary to speak universally, but not possible to do so correctly, the law takes the usual case, though it is not ignorant of the possibility of error. And it is none the less correct; for the error is in the law nor in the legislator but in the nature of the thing, since the matter of practical affairs is of this kind from the start. When the law speaks universally, then, and a case arises on it which is not covered by the universal statement, then it is right, where the legislator fails us and has erred by oversimplicity, to correct the omission-to say what the legislator himself would have said had he been present, and would have put into his law if he had known. Hence the equitable is just, and better than one kind of justice-not better than absolute justice but better than the error that arises from the absoluteness of the statement. And this is the nature of the equitable, a correction of law where it is defective owing to its universality. In fact this is the reason why all things are not determined by law, that about some things it is impossible to lay down a law, so that a decree is needed. For when the thing is indefinite the rule also is indefinite, like the leaden rule used in making the Lesbian moulding; the rule adapts itself to the shape of the stone and is not rigid, and so too the decree is adapted to the facts.
D: Refashioned:
These, then, are roughly the considerations that give rise to the problem about the equitable. They are all in a sense correct and not opposed to one another. For the equitable, though better than one kind of justice, is itself just -- it is not as though it belongs to a different category that makes it better than the just. The same thing, then, is just and equitable, and while both are good, the equitable is superior.

What creates the puzzle is that the equitable is just, but not the legally just -- rather, it is a correction of legal justice. The reason is that all law is universal, but about some things it is not possible to make a correct universal statement. In cases where it is necessary to speak universally but not possible to do so correctly, the law takes the usual case, though it is not unaware of the possibility of error. The law is nonetheless correct, for the error lies not in the law or the legislator but in the nature of the subject matter, since the material of practical affairs is inherently variable.

When the law speaks universally, then, and a case arises that is not covered by the universal statement, it is right -- where the legislator falls short by oversimplification -- to correct the omission, saying what the legislator themselves would have said had they been present, and would have enacted had they known. Hence the equitable is just, and better than one kind of justice -- not better than absolute justice, but better than the error that arises from the absoluteness of the law's statement.

This is the nature of the equitable: a correction of law where it is defective owing to its universality. This is also why not everything can be determined by law: about some things it is impossible to legislate, so a decree is needed. For when the subject matter is indefinite, the rule must also be indefinite -- like the leaden rule used in making the Lesbian style of molding, which adapts itself to the shape of the stone and is not rigid. Just so, the decree adapts itself to the facts.
D: Elegant: The equitable is just and better than one kind of justice -- not better than justice absolutely, but better than the error arising from law's necessary universality. All law is universal, but practical matters are inherently variable; the law takes the usual case, knowing it cannot cover everything. Equity corrects law where universality fails, saying what the legislator would have said if they had foreseen the case. Not everything can be legislated; like the flexible leaden rule used in Lesbian masonry that adapts to the stone's shape, decrees must adapt to irregular facts.
D: Summary: Aristotle resolves the puzzle of equity: it is just, but corrects legal justice where law's universality fails to cover particular cases. Law must speak universally, yet practical affairs are inherently variable. Equity fills this gap by determining what the legislator would have intended. Like the flexible leaden rule used in Lesbian architecture, equity adapts to the contours of specific situations where rigid rules cannot.
D: Distilled: Equity is a correction of law where its necessary universality fails to cover particular cases, determining what the legislator would have enacted had they foreseen the situation.
Notes: The Lesbian rule (kanon Lesbios) was a strip of lead used by masons on Lesbos to measure curved moldings, bending to fit the stone's contour. It became proverbial for flexible standards. -- This passage is perhaps the single most influential text in the history of equity jurisprudence, shaping Roman aequitas, English Chancery equity, and modern equitable doctrines. -- Aquinas adopted Aristotle's account directly in ST II-II, Q.120. -- The insight that law must be universal but practical reality is particular anticipates H.L.A. Hart's discussion of the 'open texture' of law in The Concept of Law (1961). -- The claim that equity says 'what the legislator himself would have said' is the ancestor of modern purposive interpretation in statutory construction.

D: Original: It is plain, then, what the equitable is, and that it is just and is better than one kind of justice. It is evident also from this who the equitable man is; the man who chooses and does such acts, and is no stickler for his rights in a bad sense but tends to take less than his share though he has the law oft his side, is equitable, and this state of character is equity, which is a sort of justice and not a different state of character.
D: Refashioned: It is plain, then, what the equitable is: it is just, and better than one kind of justice. It is also evident who the equitable person is: one who chooses and does such acts, who is not a stickler for their rights in a negative sense but tends to take less than their share even though the law is on their side. This state of character is equity, which is a kind of justice and not a different state of character.
D: Elegant: The equitable person chooses to take less than their legal entitlement, not insisting on rights in a rigid way. Equity is a kind of justice -- a character state, not a separate virtue -- that inclines toward generosity in the application of one's own claims.
D: Summary: Aristotle characterizes the equitable person as someone who voluntarily takes less than what the law entitles them to, rather than rigidly insisting on their legal rights. Equity is not a separate virtue but a special expression of justice as a character trait.
D: Distilled: The equitable person takes less than their legal due, treating equity not as a separate virtue but as justice's highest expression.
Notes: The portrait of the equitable person as one who 'takes less than their share' connects to the description of the liberal person (NE IV.1) and the magnanimous person (NE IV.3). -- The insistence that equity is not a separate character state (hexis) but a form of justice is methodologically important: it maintains the unity of the virtue of justice. -- This characterization profoundly influenced the ideal of the 'reasonable person' in common law and the equitable discretion exercised by judges. -- The passage closes Aristotle's treatment of equity, one of the most compact but influential sections of the Ethics.

D: Original: Whether a man can treat himself unjustly or not, is evident from what has been said. For (a) one class of just acts are those acts in accordance with any virtue which are prescribed by the law; e.g. the law does not expressly permit suicide, and what it does not expressly permit it forbids. Again, when a man in violation of the law harms another (otherwise than in retaliation) voluntarily, he acts unjustly, and a voluntary agent is one who knows both the person he is affecting by his action and the instrument he is using; and he who through anger voluntarily stabs himself does this contrary to the right rule of life, and this the law does not allow; therefore he is acting unjustly. But towards whom? Surely towards the state, not towards himself. For he suffers voluntarily, but no one is voluntarily treated unjustly. This is also the reason why the state punishes; a certain loss of civil rights attaches to the man who destroys himself, on the ground that he is treating the state unjustly.
D: Refashioned:
Whether a person can treat themselves unjustly or not is evident from what has been said. (a) One class of just acts consists of those in accordance with any virtue that are prescribed by law. For example, the law does not expressly permit suicide, and what it does not expressly permit, it forbids. Furthermore, when a person voluntarily harms another in violation of the law -- otherwise than in retaliation -- they act unjustly; and a voluntary agent is one who knows both the person affected by the action and the instrument being used.

Now the person who through anger voluntarily stabs themselves does this contrary to right reason, which the law does not allow. Therefore they act unjustly. But toward whom? Surely toward the state, not toward themselves. For they suffer voluntarily, but no one is voluntarily treated unjustly. This is also why the state imposes punishment: a certain loss of civil rights attaches to the person who destroys themselves, on the ground that they are treating the state unjustly.
D: Elegant: One cannot treat oneself unjustly. The law does not permit suicide -- what it does not permit, it forbids. The person who stabs themselves in anger acts against right reason and thus acts unjustly, but toward the state, not toward themselves. They suffer voluntarily, and no one is voluntarily treated unjustly. Hence the state punishes self-destruction with loss of civil rights: the wrong is against the political community.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that self-harm, including suicide, is not injustice toward oneself but toward the state. Since no one voluntarily suffers injustice, and the self-harmer acts voluntarily, the wrong must be directed at the community. This is why the state penalizes suicide with loss of civil rights -- the person wrongs the polis, not themselves.
D: Distilled: Suicide is unjust not toward oneself but toward the state, because no one voluntarily suffers injustice and the community is deprived by the loss.
Notes: Aristotle's treatment of suicide was enormously influential in Western thought. Aquinas adopted and expanded it (ST II-II, Q.64, a.5), adding theological arguments, and it became the basis for centuries of legal prohibition. -- The principle that 'what the law does not expressly permit, it forbids' reflects one view of Athenian legal interpretation, though it is disputed. -- The loss of civil rights (atimia) for suicide was a real Athenian practice: the hand that struck the blow could be buried separately from the body. -- The argument that suicide wrongs the community draws on the view that citizens belong partly to the polis (cf. Politics I.2: the individual is not self-sufficient apart from the city). -- This passage engages with the broader Greek debate about suicide, in which Plato also participated (Phaedo 61c-62c).

D: Original: Further (b) in that sense of 'acting unjustly' in which the man who 'acts unjustly' is unjust only and not bad all round, it is not possible to treat oneself unjustly (this is different from the former sense; the unjust man in one sense of the term is wicked in a particularized way just as the coward is, not in the sense of being wicked all round, so that his 'unjust act' does not manifest wickedness in general). For (i) that would imply the possibility of the same thing's having been subtracted from and added to the same thing at the same time; but this is impossible-the just and the unjust always involve more than one person. Further, (ii) unjust action is voluntary and done by choice, and takes the initiative (for the man who because he has suffered does the same in return is not thought to act unjustly); but if a man harms himself he suffers and does the same things at the same time. Further, (iii) if a man could treat himself unjustly, he could be voluntarily treated unjustly. Besides, (iv) no one acts unjustly without committing particular acts of injustice; but no one can commit adultery with his own wife or housebreaking on his own house or theft on his own property,
D: Refashioned:
Further, (b) in the sense of 'acting unjustly' where the person who acts unjustly is unjust in a particular way rather than bad in general, it is not possible to treat oneself unjustly. (This is different from the previous case: the unjust person in this particular sense is wicked in a specific way -- just as the coward is -- not in the sense of being wicked all around, so their unjust act does not manifest wickedness in general.)

For (i) self-injustice would imply that the same thing has been simultaneously subtracted from and added to the same person -- but this is impossible, since the just and the unjust always involve more than one person. Further, (ii) unjust action is voluntary, done by choice, and takes the initiative -- for the person who retaliates after being harmed is not thought to act unjustly. But if someone harms themselves, they suffer and do the same things at the same time. Further, (iii) if a person could treat themselves unjustly, they could be voluntarily treated unjustly -- which we have shown is impossible. Besides, (iv) no one acts unjustly without committing specific acts of injustice, but no one can commit adultery with their own spouse, housebreaking on their own house, or theft of their own property.
D: Elegant: In the particular sense of injustice, one cannot treat oneself unjustly, for four reasons: (i) injustice requires that what is subtracted from one be added to another, and the same person cannot play both roles simultaneously; (ii) injustice must be initiative, not reactive, but self-harm is suffered and inflicted at once; (iii) self-injustice would entail voluntary unjust treatment, which is impossible; (iv) specific acts of injustice like adultery, housebreaking, and theft logically require a distinct victim.
D: Summary: Aristotle offers four arguments against the possibility of self-injustice in the particular sense: justice always involves at least two parties; the perpetrator must initiate rather than retaliate; self-injustice would entail the impossibility of voluntary unjust treatment; and specific injustices like theft or adultery logically require a separate victim.
D: Distilled: One cannot treat oneself unjustly because injustice inherently involves two distinct parties -- a perpetrator and a separate victim.
Notes: The four arguments form a tight logical sequence, each approaching the impossibility from a different angle. -- Argument (i) uses the mathematical framework of rectificatory justice (5.14-17): justice involves transfer between distinct parties. -- Argument (ii) draws on the distinction between initiative and retaliatory action from 5.35. -- Argument (iii) invokes the earlier conclusion (5.39) that no one is voluntarily treated unjustly. -- Argument (iv) uses reductio ad absurdum with concrete examples. -- This analysis influenced debates about self-regarding vs. other-regarding duties, later central in John Stuart Mill's On Liberty.

D: Original:
In general, the question 'can a man treat himself unjustly?' is solved also by the distinction we applied to the question 'can a man be voluntarily treated unjustly?'

(It is evident too that both are bad, being unjustly treated and acting unjustly; for the one means having less and the other having more than the intermediate amount, which plays the part here that the healthy does in the medical art, and that good condition does in the art of bodily training. But still acting unjustly is the worse, for it involves vice and is blameworthy-involves vice which is either of the complete and unqualified kind or almost so (we must admit the latter alternative, because not all voluntary unjust action implies injustice as a state of character), while being unjustly treated does not involve vice and injustice in oneself. In itself, then, being unjustly treated is less bad, but there is nothing to prevent its being incidentally a greater evil. But theory cares nothing for this; it calls pleurisy a more serious mischief than a stumble; yet the latter may become incidentally the more serious, if the fall due to it leads to your being taken prisoner or put to death the enemy.)
D: Refashioned:
In general, the question 'can a person treat themselves unjustly?' is also resolved by the distinction we applied to the question 'can a person be voluntarily treated unjustly?'

It is evident, too, that both are bad -- being unjustly treated and acting unjustly. For the one means having less and the other having more than the intermediate amount, which plays the same role here that health plays in medicine and good condition in physical training. But still, acting unjustly is the worse of the two, for it involves vice and is blameworthy -- involving vice that is either complete and unqualified or nearly so (we must allow this qualification, since not all voluntary unjust action implies injustice as a settled character state). Being unjustly treated, by contrast, does not involve vice or injustice in the victim.

In itself, then, being unjustly treated is less bad. But there is nothing to prevent it from being incidentally a greater evil. Theory, however, does not concern itself with this. It calls pleurisy a more serious ailment than a stumble, yet a stumble may become incidentally more serious -- if the fall leads to one's being captured or killed by the enemy.
D: Elegant: Both acting unjustly and being unjustly treated are bad -- one involves excess, the other deficiency relative to the just mean. But acting unjustly is worse in itself, since it involves vice and blame, while suffering injustice involves no vice in the victim. Yet incidentally, being unjustly treated may cause greater harm. Theory, however, classifies by intrinsic severity: pleurisy is objectively worse than a stumble, though a stumble may incidentally prove fatal.
D: Summary: Aristotle concludes that while both doing and suffering injustice are bad, doing injustice is intrinsically worse because it involves vice, whereas being a victim does not. However, the consequences of suffering injustice can incidentally be worse. Philosophical theory, like medicine, classifies by intrinsic severity rather than accidental outcomes.
D: Distilled: Acting unjustly is intrinsically worse than suffering it because it involves vice, though suffering injustice may incidentally produce greater harm.
Notes: The claim that doing injustice is worse than suffering it echoes Socrates' famous thesis in Plato's Gorgias (469b-c, 474b-475e), though Aristotle's reasoning differs -- he grounds it in virtue theory rather than the health of the soul. -- The distinction between intrinsic (kath' hauto) and incidental (kata sumbebēkos) severity reflects Aristotle's metaphysical categories. -- The medical analogy (pleurisy vs. stumble) shows Aristotle's characteristic method of appealing to other arts to illuminate ethics. -- This passage is important for the relationship between virtue ethics (character evaluation) and consequentialism (outcome evaluation): Aristotle prioritizes intrinsic character assessment while acknowledging that consequences matter.

D: Original:
Metaphorically and in virtue of a certain resemblance there is a justice, not indeed between a man and himself, but between certain parts of him; yet not every kind of justice but that of master and servant or that of husband and wife. For these are the ratios in which the part of the soul that has a rational principle stands to the irrational part; and it is with a view to these parts that people also think a man can be unjust to himself, viz. because these parts are liable to suffer something contrary to their respective desires; there is therefore thought to be a mutual justice between them as between ruler and ruled.

Let this be taken as our account of justice and the other, i.e. the other moral, virtues.
D: Refashioned:
Metaphorically and by a certain resemblance, there is a kind of justice not between a person and themselves, but between certain parts of the soul. Yet it is not every kind of justice, but that which exists between ruler and ruled -- the kind found in master-servant or husband-wife relationships. For these are the ratios in which the rational part of the soul stands to the irrational part. It is with a view to these parts that people think a person can be unjust to themselves: because these parts are capable of experiencing something contrary to their respective desires, there appears to be a kind of mutual justice between them, as between ruler and ruled.

Let this stand as our account of justice and the other moral virtues.
D: Elegant: Metaphorically, there is justice within the soul -- between its rational and irrational parts, as between ruler and ruled. Because these parts can have conflicting desires, there appears to be a kind of internal justice or injustice between them. But this is justice by analogy, not in the full political sense. This concludes the account of justice and the moral virtues.
D: Summary: Aristotle closes Book V by allowing that justice exists metaphorically within the soul, between its rational and irrational parts, analogous to the justice between ruler and ruled. This internal 'justice' arises because the soul's parts can have conflicting desires. However, this is justice by analogy only, not the full political justice discussed throughout the book.
D: Distilled: Justice exists metaphorically within the soul as the proper governance of the rational part over the irrational, analogous to just rule in a political community.
Notes: The idea of justice within the soul recalls Plato's tripartite psychology and the 'allegory of the soul' in Republic IV (441d-442d), where justice is the harmony of the soul's parts. Aristotle's version uses his own bipartite psychology (rational/irrational) rather than Plato's tripartite model. -- The ruler-ruled analogy connects to Politics I.5 and I.13, where Aristotle draws parallels between household governance and the soul's internal structure. -- This closing passage links Book V back to the larger project of the Ethics and transitions toward Book VI's treatment of intellectual virtues. -- The statement 'let this stand as our account of justice and the other moral virtues' effectively closes the treatment of moral virtue that began in Book II, making Book V the culmination of that extended discussion before Aristotle turns to intellectual virtues in Book VI.

D: Original: Since we have previously said that one ought to choose that which is intermediate, not the excess nor the defect, and that the intermediate is determined by the dictates of the right rule, let us discuss the nature of these dictates. In all the states of character we have mentioned, as in all other matters, there is a mark to which the man who has the rule looks, and heightens or relaxes his activity accordingly, and there is a standard which determines the mean states which we say are intermediate between excess and defect, being in accordance with the right rule. But such a statement, though true, is by no means clear; for not only here but in all other pursuits which are objects of knowledge it is indeed true to say that we must not exert ourselves nor relax our efforts too much nor too little, but to an intermediate extent and as the right rule dictates; but if a man had only this knowledge he would be none the wiser e.g. we should not know what sort of medicines to apply to our body if some one were to say 'all those which the medical art prescribes, and which agree with the practice of one who possesses the art'. Hence it is necessary with regard to the states of the soul also not only that this true statement should be made, but also that it should be determined what is the right rule and what is the standard that fixes it.
D: Refashioned: We have previously said that one ought to choose the intermediate, not the excess or the deficiency, and that the intermediate is determined by what the right rule prescribes. Let us now examine the nature of this right rule. In all the states of character we have discussed, as in every other discipline, there is a target at which the person who possesses the rule aims, adjusting effort accordingly; and there is a standard that determines the mean states we call intermediate between excess and deficiency, those that accord with the right rule. But this statement, though true, is far from clear. For not only here, but in every pursuit that admits of systematic knowledge, it is true to say that we must exert ourselves neither too much nor too little but to an intermediate degree, as the right rule dictates. Yet if someone possessed only this knowledge, they would be no wiser for it. We would not know what medicines to apply to our bodies if someone merely said 'all those that the medical art prescribes, and that accord with the practice of a skilled physician.' Hence, regarding the states of the soul as well, it is necessary not only to make this true but general statement, but also to determine what the right rule is and what standard fixes it.
D: Elegant: We have established that virtue lies in the mean as determined by the right rule, but saying 'follow the right rule' is no more helpful than telling a sick person to 'do whatever medicine prescribes.' We must specify what the right rule actually is and what standard determines it.
D: Summary: Aristotle opens Book VI by noting that the doctrine of the mean requires a standard -- the 'right rule' -- to be practically useful. Simply telling someone to follow the right rule without defining it is as unhelpful as telling a patient to take whatever medicine prescribes. The task now is to determine what this right rule is.
D: Distilled: The doctrine of the mean is incomplete without an account of what the 'right rule' actually is and how it is determined.
Notes: This passage picks up directly from Book II's discussion of virtue as a mean (II.6, 1106b36-1107a2), where the mean is said to be determined by the 'right rule' (orthos logos). The medical analogy is characteristic of Aristotle's method. -- The question 'what is the right rule?' effectively motivates the entire investigation of Book VI, which will culminate in identifying practical wisdom (phronesis) as the faculty that determines it.

D: Original: We divided the virtues of the soul and a said that some are virtues of character and others of intellect. Now we have discussed in detail the moral virtues; with regard to the others let us express our view as follows, beginning with some remarks about the soul. We said before that there are two parts of the soul-that which grasps a rule or rational principle, and the irrational; let us now draw a similar distinction within the part which grasps a rational principle. And let it be assumed that there are two parts which grasp a rational principle-one by which we contemplate the kind of things whose originative causes are invariable, and one by which we contemplate variable things; for where objects differ in kind the part of the soul answering to each of the two is different in kind, since it is in virtue of a certain likeness and kinship with their objects that they have the knowledge they have. Let one of these parts be called the scientific and the other the calculative; for to deliberate and to calculate are the same thing, but no one deliberates about the invariable. Therefore the calculative is one part of the faculty which grasps a rational principle. We must, then, learn what is the best state of each of these two parts; for this is the virtue of each.
D: Refashioned: We divided the virtues of the soul into virtues of character and virtues of intellect. Having discussed the moral virtues in detail, let us now address the intellectual virtues, beginning with some observations about the soul. We said before that the soul has two parts: one that grasps a rational principle and one that is irrational. Let us now make a similar division within the rational part itself. Assume there are two rational faculties: one by which we contemplate things whose fundamental causes are invariable, and one by which we contemplate things that are variable. For where objects differ in kind, the corresponding part of the soul differs in kind as well, since these faculties possess their knowledge by virtue of a certain likeness and kinship with their objects. Let the first of these be called the scientific faculty and the second the calculative faculty, since to deliberate and to calculate are the same thing, and no one deliberates about what cannot be otherwise. The calculative faculty, then, is one division of the rational part of the soul. We must therefore determine what the best state of each of these two faculties is, for that best state is the virtue of each.
D: Elegant: The rational soul divides into two faculties: the scientific, which contemplates what is necessary and invariable, and the calculative, which contemplates what is variable and subject to deliberation. Each faculty has its own virtue -- its own optimal state -- and we must determine what these are.
D: Summary: Aristotle divides the rational part of the soul into two faculties: one for necessary, invariable truths (the scientific faculty) and one for variable, contingent matters (the calculative faculty). The virtue of each faculty is its best possible state, which he sets out to identify.
D: Distilled: The rational soul has two parts -- one for necessary truths, one for contingent matters -- and each has its own distinctive excellence.
Notes: The twofold division of the rational soul corresponds to the division between theoretical and practical knowledge that runs throughout Aristotle's philosophy. -- The principle that the soul's faculties are differentiated by their objects (like is known by like) appears also in De Anima III.4-5. -- The scientific (epistemonikon) and calculative (logistikon) distinction grounds the later taxonomy of the five intellectual virtues (6.3ff). -- Books II-V covered the moral virtues; Book VI now turns to the intellectual virtues promised at I.13, 1103a3-10.

D: Original:
The virtue of a thing is relative to its proper work. Now there are three things in the soul which control action and truth-sensation, reason, desire.

Of these sensation originates no action; this is plain from the fact that the lower animals have sensation but no share in action.
D: Refashioned: The virtue of anything is relative to its proper function. Now there are three things in the soul that govern action and the attainment of truth: sensation, reason, and desire. Of these, sensation initiates no action; this is evident from the fact that animals possess sensation but have no share in deliberate action.
D: Elegant: Three things in the soul govern action and truth: sensation, reason, and desire. Sensation alone produces no action, as shown by the fact that animals have sensation but do not act deliberately.
D: Summary: Aristotle identifies sensation, reason, and desire as the three elements in the soul that control action and truth. He eliminates sensation as a source of action, since animals have it but do not perform deliberate actions.
D: Distilled: Sensation, reason, and desire govern action and truth, but sensation alone never initiates action.
Notes: The claim that animals lack deliberate action (praxis) while possessing sensation connects to Aristotle's account in De Anima III.9-11, where desire and cognition together produce movement. -- 'Action' (praxis) here means specifically rational, deliberate conduct, not mere animal movement, which Aristotle attributes to appetite and phantasia (imagination) in De Anima III.10.

D: Original: What affirmation and negation are in thinking, pursuit and avoidance are in desire; so that since moral virtue is a state of character concerned with choice, and choice is deliberate desire, therefore both the reasoning must be true and the desire right, if the choice is to be good, and the latter must pursue just what the former asserts. Now this kind of intellect and of truth is practical; of the intellect which is contemplative, not practical nor productive, the good and the bad state are truth and falsity respectively (for this is the work of everything intellectual); while of the part which is practical and intellectual the good state is truth in agreement with right desire.
D: Refashioned: What affirmation and negation are in thought, pursuit and avoidance are in desire. Since moral virtue is a state of character concerned with choice, and choice is deliberate desire, it follows that both the reasoning must be true and the desire right if the choice is to be good; and what desire pursues must be exactly what reasoning affirms. This kind of intellect and truth is practical. For the contemplative intellect, which is neither practical nor productive, the good and bad states are simply truth and falsity, since arriving at truth is the function of every intellectual faculty. But for the part of the intellect that is both practical and intellectual, the good state is truth in agreement with right desire.
D: Elegant: In thought, affirmation and negation correspond to pursuit and avoidance in desire. Good choice requires true reasoning aligned with right desire. The contemplative intellect aims simply at truth; the practical intellect aims at truth that agrees with correct desire.
D: Summary: Aristotle draws a parallel between thought and desire: just as thought affirms or denies, desire pursues or avoids. Good choice requires that reasoning be true and desire be rightly directed, with both in alignment. While the contemplative intellect seeks truth for its own sake, practical intellect succeeds when its truth accords with right desire.
D: Distilled: Good choice requires the alignment of true reasoning with right desire -- practical truth is truth that corresponds to what one rightly wants.
Notes: The concept of 'practical truth' (aletheia praktike) is distinctive to this passage and has been extensively debated. Some commentators (e.g., Aquinas, Broadie) read it as truth about what to do; others emphasize the agreement between intellect and desire. -- The parallel between affirmation/negation and pursuit/avoidance echoes De Anima III.7, 431a8-14. -- The distinction between contemplative, practical, and productive intellect structures Book VI's entire classification.

D: Original: The origin of action-its efficient, not its final cause-is choice, and that of choice is desire and reasoning with a view to an end. This is why choice cannot exist either without reason and intellect or without a moral state; for good action and its opposite cannot exist without a combination of intellect and character. Intellect itself, however, moves nothing, but only the intellect which aims at an end and is practical; for this rules the productive intellect, as well, since every one who makes makes for an end, and that which is made is not an end in the unqualified sense (but only an end in a particular relation, and the end of a particular operation)-only that which is done is that; for good action is an end, and desire aims at this. Hence choice is either desiderative reason or ratiocinative desire, and such an origin of action is a man. (It is to be noted that nothing that is past is an object of choice, e.g. no one chooses to have sacked Troy; for no one deliberates about the past, but about what is future and capable of being otherwise, while what is past is not capable of not having taken place; hence Agathon is right in saying
D: Refashioned: The origin of action -- its efficient cause, not its final cause -- is choice, and the origin of choice is desire combined with reasoning directed toward an end. This is why choice cannot exist without both reason and intellect and a moral state of character, for good action and its opposite require a combination of intellect and character. Intellect by itself moves nothing; only intellect that aims at a goal and is practical has motivating power. Practical intellect also governs the productive intellect, since everyone who makes something makes it for an end. But what is made is not an end in the unqualified sense -- it is only an end in a particular relation, the end of a particular productive process. Only what is done through action is an end without qualification, for good action is itself an end, and desire aims at this. Hence choice is either desire informed by reasoning or reasoning informed by desire, and such a composite origin of action is what a human being essentially is. It should be noted that nothing past is an object of choice: no one chooses to have sacked Troy, for no one deliberates about the past but only about what is future and capable of being otherwise. What is past cannot not have occurred; hence Agathon is right in saying --
D: Elegant: Choice -- the efficient cause of action -- is desire combined with means-end reasoning, and requires both intellect and moral character. Intellect alone moves nothing; only practical intellect directed at a goal motivates action. Good action, unlike production, is its own end. Choice is thus reasoning desire or desiring reason, and this composite is the essence of human agency. The past is beyond choice, since deliberation concerns only what can still be otherwise.
D: Summary: Choice, the source of action, is a fusion of desire and reasoning toward an end. It requires both intellect and moral character. Unlike making things, where the product is the goal, good action is an end in itself. Aristotle defines choice as either 'reasoning desire' or 'desiring reason,' marking it as distinctively human.
D: Distilled: Choice is the union of desire and practical reasoning, and this fusion of thought and appetite is the distinctive origin of human action.
Notes: Agathon was a 5th-century BCE Athenian tragic poet, host of the banquet in Plato's Symposium. The quotation concludes in 6.6. -- The claim that 'intellect by itself moves nothing' (nous autos ouden kinei) is a key thesis, echoed in De Anima III.9, 432b26-433a1. -- 'Choice is desiderative reason or ratiocinative desire' (orexis dianoetike or orektikos nous) has been central to debates about Aristotle's action theory. -- The distinction between making (poiesis) and doing (praxis) is fundamental to Book VI and recurs in the account of art (6.9) and practical wisdom (6.10-14).

D: Original:
For this alone is lacking even to God, To make undone things thathave once been done.)

The work of both the intellectual parts, then, is truth. Therefore the states that are most strictly those in respect of which each of these parts will reach truth are the virtues of the two parts.
D: Refashioned:
-- 'For this alone is lacking even to God: to make undone things that have once been done.'

The function of both intellectual parts of the soul, then, is truth. Therefore the states that most fully enable each part to attain truth are the virtues of those two parts.
D: Elegant: Even God cannot undo what has been done. Since truth is the function of both rational faculties, their virtues are the states that best attain truth.
D: Summary: Concluding the quotation from Agathon -- that not even God can change the past -- Aristotle states that truth is the proper function of both intellectual faculties, and their virtues are the states that most reliably produce truth.
D: Distilled: The virtue of each intellectual faculty is whatever state best enables it to grasp truth.
Notes: The Agathon quotation (fragment 5, Nauck) was well known in antiquity and is also cited in Plato's Symposium 197e in a different context. -- The identification of truth as the 'work' (ergon) of both rational parts sets up the classification of the five truth-attaining states in 6.7. -- The ergon (function) argument here parallels the function argument for human happiness in Book I.7, 1097b24-1098a20.

D: Original: Let us begin, then, from the beginning, and discuss these states once more. Let it be assumed that the states by virtue of which the soul possesses truth by way of affirmation or denial are five in number, i.e. art, scientific knowledge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, intuitive reason; we do not include judgement and opinion because in these we may be mistaken.
D: Refashioned: Let us begin afresh and discuss these states once more. Assume that the states by virtue of which the soul grasps truth through affirmation or denial are five in number: art, scientific knowledge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, and intuitive reason. We exclude judgment and opinion from this list because in these we can be mistaken.
D: Elegant: There are five states through which the soul reliably grasps truth: art, scientific knowledge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, and intuitive reason. Judgment and opinion are excluded because they admit of error.
D: Summary: Aristotle enumerates five intellectual states that reliably yield truth: art (techne), scientific knowledge (episteme), practical wisdom (phronesis), philosophic wisdom (sophia), and intuitive reason (nous). Opinion and judgment are excluded because they can be false.
D: Distilled: The soul possesses truth through exactly five reliable states: art, scientific knowledge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, and intuitive reason.
Notes: This is the foundational taxonomy of Book VI. The five states (hexeis) are: techne (art/craft), episteme (scientific/demonstrative knowledge), phronesis (practical wisdom), sophia (philosophic/theoretical wisdom), and nous (intuitive reason/intellect). -- The exclusion of doxa (opinion) and hypolepsis (judgment/supposition) because they admit of falsehood distinguishes these five as truth-guaranteeing states. -- This classification influenced the medieval and scholastic tradition extensively, especially Aquinas's commentary on the Ethics.

D: Original:
Now what scientific knowledge is, if we are to speak exactly and not follow mere similarities, is plain from what follows. We all suppose that what we know is not even capable of being otherwise; of things capable of being otherwise we do not know, when they have passed outside our observation, whether they exist or not. Therefore the object of scientific knowledge is of necessity. Therefore it is eternal; for things that are of necessity in the unqualified sense are all eternal; and things that are eternal are ungenerated and imperishable. Again, every science is thought to be capable of being taught, and its object of being learned. And all teaching starts from what is already known, as we maintain in the Analytics also; for it proceeds sometimes through induction and sometimes by syllogism. Now induction is the starting-point which knowledge even of the universal presupposes, while syllogism proceeds from universals. There are therefore starting-points from which syllogism proceeds, which are not reached by syllogism; it is therefore by induction that they are acquired. Scientific knowledge is, then, a state of capacity to demonstrate, and has the other limiting characteristics which we specify in the Analytics, for it is when a man believes in a certain way and the starting-points are known to him that he has scientific knowledge, since if they are not better known to him than the conclusion, he will have his knowledge only incidentally.

Let this, then, be taken as our account of scientific knowledge.
D: Refashioned:
What scientific knowledge is, if we are to speak precisely and not rely on loose similarities, is clear from the following. We all assume that what we know scientifically cannot be otherwise; things that can be otherwise pass beyond our knowledge once they leave our observation, and we cannot tell whether they still exist. Therefore the object of scientific knowledge exists of necessity, and what exists of necessity is eternal -- ungenerated and imperishable.

Furthermore, every science is thought to be teachable, and its object learnable. All teaching proceeds from what is already known, as we maintain in the Analytics: sometimes through induction and sometimes through deductive reasoning. Induction provides the starting point that grounds knowledge of universals, while deductive reasoning proceeds from universals. There are therefore starting points from which deduction proceeds that are not themselves reached by deduction; they are acquired through induction.

Scientific knowledge, then, is a state enabling demonstration, with the additional defining characteristics we specify in the Analytics. A person has scientific knowledge when they believe in a certain way and the starting points are known to them, since if the starting points are not better known to them than the conclusion, they will possess knowledge only incidentally.

Let this serve as our account of scientific knowledge.
D: Elegant: Scientific knowledge concerns what is necessary, eternal, and cannot be otherwise. It is a demonstrative capacity proceeding from first principles -- principles that are themselves grasped not through demonstration but through induction. One truly knows something scientifically only when the first principles are better known than the conclusion.
D: Summary: Scientific knowledge deals exclusively with what is necessary and eternal. It is the capacity for demonstration from first principles, which must be grasped through induction rather than deduction. Genuine scientific knowledge requires that the knower understand the first principles more securely than the conclusions drawn from them.
D: Distilled: Scientific knowledge is the capacity for demonstrative reasoning from necessary first principles that are themselves more certain than anything derived from them.
Notes: The references to 'the Analytics' are to Aristotle's Posterior Analytics, especially I.1-3 on the nature of demonstration and the requirement that premises be 'better known' (gnorimotera) than the conclusion. -- The claim that the objects of episteme are necessary and eternal reflects the strict sense of scientific knowledge in Aristotle, contrasting with modern usage. -- Posterior Analytics I.2, 71b9-72a5 gives the fuller account of what demonstration requires. -- The distinction between induction (epagoge) and syllogism (syllogismos) as the two paths to knowledge is also discussed in Topics I.12.

D: Original: In the variable are included both things made and things done; making and acting are different (for their nature we treat even the discussions outside our school as reliable); so that the reasoned state of capacity to act is different from the reasoned state of capacity to make. Hence too they are not included one in the other; for neither is acting making nor is making acting. Now since architecture is an art and is essentially a reasoned state of capacity to make, and there is neither any art that is not such a state nor any such state that is not an art, art is identical with a state of capacity to make, involving a true course of reasoning. All art is concerned with coming into being, i.e. with contriving and considering how something may come into being which is capable of either being or not being, and whose origin is in the maker and not in the thing made; for art is concerned neither with things that are, or come into being, by necessity, nor with things that do so in accordance with nature (since these have their origin in themselves). Making and acting being different, art must be a matter of making, not of acting. And in a sense chance and art are concerned with the same objects; as Agathon says, 'art loves chance and chance loves art'. Art, then, as has been is a state concerned with making, involving a true course of reasoning, and lack of art on the contrary is a state concerned with making, involving a false course of reasoning; both are concerned with the variable.
D: Refashioned:
Among variable things, we must distinguish between things made and things done. Making and acting are different (for the nature of this distinction we may rely even on discussions outside our school). Therefore, the reasoned state of capacity to act is different from the reasoned state of capacity to make; neither includes the other, for acting is not making, nor is making acting.

Now since architecture is an art, and is essentially a reasoned state of capacity to make, and since there is no art that is not such a state, nor any such state that is not an art, art is identical with a state of capacity to make involving a true course of reasoning. All art is concerned with bringing something into being -- that is, with contriving and figuring out how something may come into existence that is capable of either existing or not existing, and whose origin lies in the maker rather than in the thing made. For art deals neither with things that exist or come into being by necessity, nor with things that do so by nature, since these have their origin in themselves.

Since making and acting are different, art must belong to the domain of making, not acting. In a sense, chance and art deal with the same objects, as Agathon says: 'Art loves chance and chance loves art.'

Art, then, as stated, is a state concerned with making, involving a true course of reasoning; and lack of art, conversely, is a state concerned with making involving a false course of reasoning. Both deal with what is variable.
D: Elegant: Making and acting are fundamentally different. Art is a reasoned capacity to make -- to bring into being things whose origin lies in the maker, not in nature or necessity. It involves true reasoning about how to produce something that could either exist or not exist. As Agathon noted, art and chance operate in the same domain of contingency.
D: Summary: Art (techne) is defined as a reasoned state of capacity to make things, involving true reasoning about production. It deals with what is contingent -- things that could be otherwise -- and whose cause lies in the maker rather than in nature or necessity. Aristotle sharply distinguishes making from acting, placing art squarely in the domain of making.
D: Distilled: Art is the capacity for making guided by true reasoning, operating in the domain of what is contingent and whose origin lies in the maker.
Notes: The distinction between making (poiesis) and acting (praxis) is central to Book VI and ultimately to Aristotle's classification of the sciences into theoretical, practical, and productive (Metaphysics VI.1, 1025b25). -- 'Discussions outside our school' likely refers to works in general circulation (exoterikoi logoi), possibly including published Platonic dialogues. -- Agathon (c. 448-400 BCE), the tragic poet, is quoted again (see 6.5-6.6). The saying about art and chance captures how both operate in the domain of contingency. -- The claim that art's origin is in the maker, not the thing made, contrasts with nature (physis), where the origin of change is in the thing itself (Physics II.1, 192b13-23).

D: Original: Regarding practical wisdom we shall get at the truth by considering who are the persons we credit with it. Now it is thought to be the mark of a man of practical wisdom to be able to deliberate well about what is good and expedient for himself, not in some particular respect, e.g. about what sorts of thing conduce to health or to strength, but about what sorts of thing conduce to the good life in general. This is shown by the fact that we credit men with practical wisdom in some particular respect when they have calculated well with a view to some good end which is one of those that are not the object of any art.
D: Refashioned: To understand practical wisdom, let us consider who the people are that we credit with possessing it. It is thought to be the mark of the practically wise person to be able to deliberate well about what is good and beneficial for themselves -- not in some particular respect, such as what conduces to health or to strength, but about what conduces to the good life in general. This is shown by the fact that we credit people with practical wisdom in a particular respect when they have calculated well toward some good end that falls outside the scope of any art.
D: Elegant: The practically wise person deliberates well not about some particular good like health or strength, but about what promotes the good life as a whole. We attribute practical wisdom when someone calculates well toward good ends that no specific art covers.
D: Summary: Practical wisdom is identified by looking at whom we consider practically wise: those who deliberate well about what is good for life as a whole, not merely about particular goods like health or strength. It applies where no specialized art provides the answer.
D: Distilled: Practical wisdom is the ability to deliberate well about what conduces to the good life as a whole, not just to any particular good.
Notes: Aristotle's method of beginning with endoxa (reputable opinions) and phainomena (what appears to be the case) is characteristic of his ethical methodology, described in VII.1, 1145b2-7. -- The distinction between practical wisdom and particular arts connects to Book I's discussion of the 'architectonic' good that encompasses all particular goods (I.2, 1094a26-b7). -- The phrase 'good life in general' (eu zen holos) links practical wisdom back to eudaimonia (happiness/flourishing) as defined in Book I.

D: Original: It follows that in the general sense also the man who is capable of deliberating has practical wisdom. Now no one deliberates about things that are invariable, nor about things that it is impossible for him to do. Therefore, since scientific knowledge involves demonstration, but there is no demonstration of things whose first principles are variable (for all such things might actually be otherwise), and since it is impossible to deliberate about things that are of necessity, practical wisdom cannot be scientific knowledge nor art; not science because that which can be done is capable of being otherwise, not art because action and making are different kinds of thing. The remaining alternative, then, is that it is a true and reasoned state of capacity to act with regard to the things that are good or bad for man.
D: Refashioned: It follows that in the general sense, the person who is capable of deliberating well possesses practical wisdom. Now, no one deliberates about what is invariable or about what is impossible for them to do. Therefore, since scientific knowledge involves demonstration and there can be no demonstration of things whose first principles are variable (since all such things could actually be otherwise), and since it is impossible to deliberate about what is necessary, practical wisdom cannot be either scientific knowledge or art. It is not scientific knowledge because what can be done is capable of being otherwise; it is not art because action and making are different kinds of thing. The remaining alternative, then, is that practical wisdom is a true and reasoned state of capacity to act with regard to things that are good or bad for human beings.
D: Elegant: Practical wisdom cannot be scientific knowledge, since what can be done is always capable of being otherwise; nor can it be art, since action and making are fundamentally different. It must therefore be a true, reasoned capacity to act concerning what is good or bad for human beings.
D: Summary: Aristotle eliminates scientific knowledge and art as candidates for practical wisdom through a process of exclusion. Scientific knowledge deals with the necessary, but action concerns the variable. Art deals with making, but practical wisdom deals with acting. Practical wisdom is therefore defined as a true and reasoned capacity to act regarding human goods.
D: Distilled: Practical wisdom is a true, reasoned capacity to act well regarding what is good and bad for human beings -- distinct from both science and art.
Notes: The definition reached here (hexis alethes meta logou praktike peri ta anthropo agatha kai kaka) is one of the most important in the Ethics and has generated extensive commentary. -- The method of elimination (it is not X, not Y, so it must be Z) mirrors the procedure used in Posterior Analytics II for arriving at definitions. -- The distinction between practical wisdom (concerned with praxis) and art (concerned with poiesis) was introduced in 6.9 and is here applied to complete the definition.

D: Original: For while making has an end other than itself, action cannot; for good action itself is its end. It is for this reason that we think Pericles and men like him have practical wisdom, viz. because they can see what is good for themselves and what is good for men in general; we consider that those can do this who are good at managing households or states. (This is why we call temperance (sophrosune) by this name; we imply that it preserves one's practical wisdom (sozousa tan phronsin). Now what it preserves is a judgement of the kind we have described.
D: Refashioned:
For while making always has an end beyond itself, action cannot, since good action is itself its end. It is for this reason that we think Pericles and people like him possess practical wisdom: because they can see what is good for themselves and what is good for people in general. We consider that those who can manage households or states well have this capacity.

This is also why we call temperance 'sophrosune' -- the name implies that it preserves one's practical wisdom ('sozousa ten phronesin'). And what it preserves is a judgment of the kind we have described.
D: Elegant: Good action, unlike production, is its own end. This is why we attribute practical wisdom to statesmen like Pericles who can discern what is good both for themselves and for others. Temperance preserves practical wisdom -- hence the Greek name sophrosune, suggesting 'that which saves phronesis.'
D: Summary: Good action is an end in itself, unlike making. Pericles exemplifies practical wisdom because he could discern what is good for individuals and for the community. Aristotle offers an etymology of temperance (sophrosune) as 'that which preserves practical wisdom,' suggesting the two virtues are deeply connected.
D: Distilled: Good action is its own end, and practical wisdom -- the ability to see what is truly good for oneself and others -- is preserved by temperance.
Notes: Pericles (c. 495-429 BCE) was the leading Athenian statesman during the golden age of Athens, celebrated for his political judgment and rhetorical skill. Thucydides portrays him as a model of deliberative excellence. -- The etymology of sophrosune as 'sozousa ten phronesin' (saving/preserving practical wisdom) is a Platonic-Aristotelian wordplay; Plato gives a similar etymology in the Cratylus 411e. -- The claim that praxis is its own end while poiesis has an external end is fundamental to Aristotle's practical philosophy and distinguishes his ethics from purely consequentialist frameworks.

D: Original: For it is not any and every judgement that pleasant and painful objects destroy and pervert, e.g. the judgement that the triangle has or has not its angles equal to two right angles, but only judgements about what is to be done. For the originating causes of the things that are done consist in the end at which they are aimed; but the man who has been ruined by pleasure or pain forthwith fails to see any such originating cause-to see that for the sake of this or because of this he ought to choose and do whatever he chooses and does; for vice is destructive of the originating cause of action.) Practical wisdom, then, must be a reasoned and true state of capacity to act with regard to human goods.
D: Refashioned:
For it is not just any judgment that pleasant and painful experiences destroy and distort -- not, for example, the judgment that a triangle does or does not have angles equal to two right angles -- but only judgments about what is to be done. For the originating causes of action consist in the end at which actions aim; but the person who has been corrupted by pleasure or pain immediately fails to see any such originating cause -- fails to see that for the sake of this end, or because of this end, they ought to choose and do whatever they choose and do. For vice destroys the originating cause of action.

Practical wisdom, then, must be a reasoned and true state of capacity to act with regard to human goods.
D: Elegant: Pleasure and pain do not corrupt mathematical judgments but do corrupt practical ones -- they blind us to the ends for which we should act. Vice destroys our grasp of action's originating causes. Thus practical wisdom must be a reasoned, true capacity to act regarding human goods.
D: Summary: Pleasure and pain selectively corrupt practical judgment, not theoretical knowledge like geometry. When someone is ruined by pleasure or pain, they can no longer perceive the correct ends of action. Since vice destroys our grasp of these ends, practical wisdom requires both true reasoning and right orientation toward human goods.
D: Distilled: Vice corrupts not our grasp of abstract truths but our perception of the ends worth pursuing, which is why practical wisdom requires moral soundness.
Notes: The claim that pleasure and pain distort practical but not theoretical judgment is crucial to Aristotle's view that moral virtue and practical wisdom are interdependent. -- This anticipates the stronger claim in 6.35 that one cannot be practically wise without being good. -- The idea that vice 'destroys the originating cause' (arche) of action connects to Book VII's discussion of incontinence (akrasia), where the incontinent person's judgment is temporarily overridden by desire. -- The triangle example illustrates the immunity of mathematical truths to motivational corruption.

D: Original: But further, while there is such a thing as excellence in art, there is no such thing as excellence in practical wisdom; and in art he who errs willingly is preferable, but in practical wisdom, as in the virtues, he is the reverse. Plainly, then, practical wisdom is a virtue and not an art. There being two parts of the soul that can follow a course of reasoning, it must be the virtue of one of the two, i.e. of that part which forms opinions; for opinion is about the variable and so is practical wisdom. But yet it is not only a reasoned state; this is shown by the fact that a state of that sort may forgotten but practical wisdom cannot.
D: Refashioned:
Furthermore, while there is such a thing as excellence in art, there is no such thing as excellence in practical wisdom. In art, the person who errs willingly is preferable to one who errs unwillingly, but in practical wisdom -- as in the moral virtues -- it is the reverse. Plainly, then, practical wisdom is a virtue and not an art.

Since there are two parts of the soul that can follow a course of reasoning, practical wisdom must be the virtue of one of them -- namely, the part that forms opinions. For opinion deals with what is variable, and so does practical wisdom. Yet practical wisdom is not merely a reasoned state, as shown by the fact that such a state can be forgotten, but practical wisdom cannot.
D: Elegant: Unlike art, where deliberate error shows greater skill, in practical wisdom deliberate error is worse -- making practical wisdom a genuine virtue, not merely a skill. It belongs to the soul's opinion-forming faculty, which deals with variable things. But it exceeds a mere reasoned state, since it cannot be forgotten as ordinary knowledge can.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes practical wisdom from art by noting that in art, someone who errs on purpose shows greater mastery, while in practical wisdom the reverse holds -- deliberate moral error is worse. This proves practical wisdom is a virtue. It belongs to the calculative faculty that deals with variable things, but goes beyond mere rational capacity because, unlike ordinary knowledge, it cannot be forgotten.
D: Distilled: Practical wisdom is a virtue rather than an art, because deliberate error in it is a deficiency, not a sign of mastery, and unlike mere knowledge it cannot be forgotten.
Notes: The contrast between voluntary error in art versus virtue comes from Plato's Hippias Minor (375d-376c), where Socrates provocatively argues that the person who errs willingly is better. Aristotle here tacitly corrects Plato by restricting this to techne. -- The claim that practical wisdom 'cannot be forgotten' suggests it is more like a character trait than a body of information -- it is a settled disposition (hexis) that integrates with one's entire way of being. -- Practical wisdom is assigned to the calculative (doxastikon) part of the rational soul, as established in 6.2.

D: Original: Scientific knowledge is judgement about things that are universal and necessary, and the conclusions of demonstration, and all scientific knowledge, follow from first principles (for scientific knowledge involves apprehension of a rational ground). This being so, the first principle from which what is scientifically known follows cannot be an object of scientific knowledge, of art, or of practical wisdom; for that which can be scientifically known can be demonstrated, and art and practical wisdom deal with things that are variable. Nor are these first principles the objects of philosophic wisdom, for it is a mark of the philosopher to have demonstration about some things. If, then, the states of mind by which we have truth and are never deceived about things invariable or even variable are scientific knowlededge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, and intuitive reason, and it cannot be any of the three (i.e. practical wisdom, scientific knowledge, or philosophic wisdom), the remaining alternative is that it is intuitive reason that grasps the first principles.
D: Refashioned:
Scientific knowledge is judgment about things that are universal and necessary, and the conclusions of demonstration -- all scientific knowledge -- follow from first principles, since scientific knowledge involves apprehension of a rational ground. This being so, the first principle from which what is scientifically known follows cannot itself be an object of scientific knowledge, art, or practical wisdom. It cannot be scientifically known because what is scientifically known can be demonstrated, and first principles are not reached by demonstration. It cannot be grasped by art or practical wisdom because these deal with things that are variable.

Nor are first principles the objects of philosophic wisdom, for it is characteristic of the philosopher to possess demonstrations about certain things. If, then, the states of mind by which we grasp truth and are never deceived about things invariable or variable are scientific knowledge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, and intuitive reason -- and if it cannot be any of the first three -- then the remaining alternative is that it is intuitive reason that grasps the first principles.
D: Elegant: First principles cannot be grasped through demonstration (since they ground demonstration), nor through art or practical wisdom (which concern variable things), nor through philosophic wisdom (which includes demonstration). By elimination, first principles must be grasped by intuitive reason.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues by elimination that first principles -- the foundations of all demonstration -- must be grasped by intuitive reason (nous). They cannot be objects of scientific knowledge (which requires demonstration from them), art or practical wisdom (which deal with variable things), or philosophic wisdom (which itself involves demonstration).
D: Distilled: First principles, which ground all demonstration but cannot themselves be demonstrated, are grasped by intuitive reason alone.
Notes: This argument parallels Posterior Analytics II.19, where Aristotle explains how nous grasps the indemonstrable starting points of science through a process beginning with perception. -- The problem of first principles (how we know them if not by demonstration) was recognized by Plato as leading to a 'regress' problem (Republic 510b-511d). Aristotle's solution through nous avoids infinite regress. -- The elimination argument (it must be one of the five; it cannot be these four; therefore it is the fifth) is a characteristic Aristotelian dialectical procedure.

D: Original: Wisdom (1) in the arts we ascribe to their most finished exponents, e.g. to Phidias as a sculptor and to Polyclitus as a maker of portrait-statues, and here we mean nothing by wisdom except excellence in art; but (2) we think that some people are wise in general, not in some particular field or in any other limited respect, as Homer says in the Margites,
D: Refashioned: In the arts, we ascribe wisdom to the most accomplished practitioners -- to Phidias as a sculptor and to Polyclitus as a maker of statues -- and by 'wisdom' here we mean nothing more than excellence in art. But we also think some people are wise in general, not merely in some particular field or other limited respect, as Homer says in the Margites:
D: Elegant: In the arts, 'wisdom' simply means supreme skill -- as we attribute it to Phidias in sculpture or Polyclitus in statue-making. But we also speak of wisdom in a general, unrestricted sense, as Homer notes in the Margites.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes two senses of wisdom: in the narrow sense, it means supreme excellence within a particular art (as with Phidias in sculpture); in the broader sense, it means a general wisdom not limited to any particular domain.
D: Distilled: Wisdom in its full sense is not mastery of any particular art but a comprehensive excellence of understanding.
Notes: Phidias (c. 480-430 BCE) was the most celebrated sculptor of ancient Greece, responsible for the Parthenon sculptures and the great statue of Zeus at Olympia. Polyclitus (5th century BCE) was famous for his bronze statues and his Canon, a treatise on ideal proportions. -- The Margites was a comic epic attributed to Homer in antiquity, now largely lost. Aristotle cites it also in Poetics 4, 1448b30. -- The two senses of sophia (craft-wisdom vs. general wisdom) set up Aristotle's formal definition of philosophic wisdom in 6.17.

D: Original:
Him did the gods make neither a digger nor yet a ploughman

Nor wise in anything else. Therefore wisdom must plainly be the most finished of the forms of knowledge. It follows that the wise man must not only know what follows from the first principles, but must also possess truth about the first principles. Therefore wisdom must be intuitive reason combined with scientific knowledge-scientific knowledge of the highest objects which has received as it were its proper completion.
D: Refashioned:
'Him did the gods make neither a digger nor yet a ploughman, nor wise in anything else.'

Therefore wisdom must plainly be the most complete and finished form of knowledge. It follows that the wise person must not only know what follows from first principles, but must also possess truth about the first principles themselves. Therefore philosophic wisdom must be intuitive reason combined with scientific knowledge -- scientific knowledge of the highest objects, which has received, as it were, its proper completion.
D: Elegant: Wisdom is the most complete form of knowledge. The wise person must both know what follows from first principles and possess truth about those principles themselves. Philosophic wisdom is therefore intuitive reason combined with scientific knowledge of the highest things.
D: Summary: Completing the Homer quotation, Aristotle defines philosophic wisdom (sophia) as the most finished form of knowledge. It combines intuitive reason, which grasps first principles, with scientific knowledge, which derives conclusions from them -- and it is directed at the highest objects of understanding.
D: Distilled: Philosophic wisdom is intuitive reason united with scientific knowledge, directed at the highest objects of understanding.
Notes: The definition of sophia as nous + episteme directed at the highest objects is Aristotle's formal account. 'Highest objects' (ta timiotata) will be specified in 6.18-19 as the most divine things in the cosmos. -- This definition makes sophia a compound virtue, incorporating both the capacity to grasp first principles (nous) and the capacity to demonstrate from them (episteme). -- The formulation 'scientific knowledge that has received its proper completion' (hoion kephalen echousa) literally means 'having received its head/capstone,' a vivid architectural metaphor.
