D: Original: Of the highest objects, we say; for it would be strange to think that the art of politics, or practical wisdom, is the best knowledge, since man is not the best thing in the world. Now if what is healthy or good is different for men and for fishes, but what is white or straight is always the same, any one would say that what is wise is the same but what is practically wise is different; for it is to that which observes well the various matters concerning itself that one ascribes practical wisdom, and it is to this that one will entrust such matters. This is why we say that some even of the lower animals have practical wisdom, viz. those which are found to have a power of foresight with regard to their own life. It is evident also that philosophic wisdom and the art of politics cannot be the same; for if the state of mind concerned with a man's own interests is to be called philosophic wisdom, there will be many philosophic wisdoms; there will not be one concerned with the good of all animals (any more than there is one art of medicine for all existing things), but a different philosophic wisdom about the good of each species.
D: Refashioned:
We say 'of the highest objects' because it would be strange to think that political science or practical wisdom is the highest form of knowledge, since human beings are not the highest thing in the universe.

Now if what is healthy or good differs for humans and for fishes, but what is white or straight is always the same, everyone would say that what is wise is always the same but what is practically wise differs. For it is to whatever creature observes well the various matters concerning its own life that one ascribes practical wisdom, and it is to this creature that one would entrust such matters. This is why we say that even some of the lower animals possess practical wisdom -- those that are found to have a capacity for foresight regarding their own lives.

It is also evident that philosophic wisdom and political science cannot be the same. For if the state of mind concerned with one's own interests were to be called philosophic wisdom, there would be many philosophic wisdoms -- not one concerned with the good of all animals, any more than there is one art of medicine for all existing things, but a different philosophic wisdom for each species.
D: Elegant: It would be absurd to call practical wisdom the highest knowledge, since humans are not the highest beings. What counts as wise is universal, but what counts as practically wise varies by species -- even some animals display practical foresight regarding their own lives. Philosophic wisdom therefore cannot be the same as political science; otherwise there would be as many 'wisdoms' as there are species.
D: Summary: Philosophic wisdom cannot be identified with practical wisdom or political science, because humans are not the most important beings in the universe. While practical wisdom varies by species -- even some animals display it as foresight about their own survival -- philosophic wisdom concerns universal, unchanging truths that transcend any species' particular interests.
D: Distilled: Philosophic wisdom transcends species-relative concerns, since what is wise is universal, while what is practically wise varies for each kind of being.
Notes: The claim that 'man is not the best thing in the world' reflects Aristotle's cosmological hierarchy, in which the celestial bodies and unmoved mover surpass human beings in dignity. -- Attributing practical wisdom to animals (e.g., cranes, bees) appears to be a deliberate departure from the strictest definition, using 'practical wisdom' in an extended sense for prudential foresight. -- The argument against identifying sophia with political wisdom anticipates 6.40's claim that practical wisdom serves philosophic wisdom rather than ruling over it.

D: Original: But if the argument be that man is the best of the animals, this makes no difference; for there are other things much more divine in their nature even than man, e.g., most conspicuously, the bodies of which the heavens are framed. From what has been said it is plain, then, that philosophic wisdom is scientific knowledge, combined with intuitive reason, of the things that are highest by nature. This is why we say Anaxagoras, Thales, and men like them have philosophic but not practical wisdom, when we see them ignorant of what is to their own advantage, and why we say that they know things that are remarkable, admirable, difficult, and divine, but useless; viz. because it is not human goods that they seek.
D: Refashioned:
And if one argues that human beings are the highest of the animals, that makes no difference, for there are other things far more divine in their nature than humans -- most conspicuously, the celestial bodies of which the heavens are composed.

From what has been said, it is plain that philosophic wisdom is scientific knowledge combined with intuitive reason, directed at the things that are highest by nature. This is why we say that Anaxagoras, Thales, and people like them possess philosophic but not practical wisdom, when we see them ignorant of what is to their own advantage. We say that they know things that are remarkable, admirable, difficult, and divine, but useless -- because it is not human goods that they seek.
D: Elegant: Even if humans are the highest animals, there exist far more divine things -- above all, the celestial bodies. Philosophic wisdom is therefore scientific knowledge combined with intuitive reason, concerning what is highest by nature. Thinkers like Anaxagoras and Thales possess this wisdom while lacking practical wisdom, knowing things remarkable and divine but useless for everyday life.
D: Summary: Aristotle completes his argument that philosophic wisdom concerns things higher than human affairs, pointing to the celestial bodies. He illustrates the distinction by noting that Anaxagoras and Thales possessed philosophic wisdom -- understanding remarkable and divine matters -- while being famously impractical about their own worldly interests.
D: Distilled: Philosophic wisdom grasps what is highest in nature -- remarkable, divine, but indifferent to human advantage -- as exemplified by Anaxagoras and Thales.
Notes: Anaxagoras (c. 500-428 BCE) was a pre-Socratic philosopher who proposed nous (mind) as the organizing principle of the cosmos; he was prosecuted in Athens for impiety. Thales (c. 624-546 BCE) was traditionally the first Greek philosopher, known for predicting a solar eclipse and for the thesis that everything is water. Both were proverbially impractical. -- The story of Thales falling into a well while stargazing (Plato, Theaetetus 174a) illustrates this contrast. Aristotle tells a counter-story of Thales' olive-press speculation in Politics I.11, 1259a5-18. -- The 'bodies of which the heavens are framed' refers to the celestial spheres, composed of aether (the fifth element) in Aristotle's cosmology (De Caelo I.2-3).

D: Original:
Practical wisdom on the other hand is concerned with things human and things about which it is possible to deliberate; for we say this is above all the work of the man of practical wisdom, to deliberate well, but no one deliberates about things invariable, nor about things which have not an end, and that a good that can be brought about by action. The man who is without qualification good at deliberating is the man who is capable of aiming in accordance with calculation at the best for man of things attainable by action. Nor is practical wisdom concerned with universals only-it must also recognize the particulars; for it is practical, and practice is concerned with particulars. This is why some who do not know, and especially those who have experience, are more practical than others who know; for if a man knew that light meats are digestible and wholesome, but did not know which sorts of meat are light, he would not produce health, but the man who knows that chicken is wholesome is more likely to produce health.

Now practical wisdom is concerned with action; therefore one should have both forms of it, or the latter in preference to the former. But of practical as of philosophic wisdom there must be a controlling kind.
D: Refashioned:
Practical wisdom, on the other hand, is concerned with things human and things about which it is possible to deliberate. For we say this is above all the work of the practically wise person: to deliberate well. But no one deliberates about things that are invariable, nor about things that lack an achievable good end that can be brought about by action. The person who is unconditionally good at deliberating is the one capable of aiming, through calculation, at the best for human beings among things attainable by action.

Nor is practical wisdom concerned with universals only -- it must also recognize particulars, for it is practical, and practice concerns particulars. This is why some who lack theoretical knowledge, especially those with experience, are more effective in practice than others who have such knowledge. For if someone knew that light meats are digestible and wholesome but did not know which sorts of meat are light, they would not produce health; but the person who knows that chicken is wholesome is more likely to produce health.

Since practical wisdom is concerned with action, one should possess both universal and particular knowledge, or, if only one, the particular in preference to the universal. But as with philosophic wisdom, so with practical wisdom there must be a controlling, architectonic form.
D: Elegant: Practical wisdom concerns human affairs and what can be achieved through deliberation and action. It requires not just universal principles but knowledge of particulars -- someone who knows 'light meats are healthy' but not that chicken is light will fail in practice. Experience with particulars often outweighs abstract knowledge. One needs both forms of knowledge, but if forced to choose, particular knowledge is more useful for action.
D: Summary: Practical wisdom deals with human affairs and requires both universal principles and particular knowledge. Someone who knows general rules but lacks particular knowledge will fail in practice, which is why experienced people often outperform those with only theoretical understanding. If one must choose, particular knowledge is more practically useful, though ideally one should have both.
D: Distilled: Practical wisdom requires knowledge of both universals and particulars, but knowledge of the particular is more important for action than knowledge of the universal alone.
Notes: The chicken example is characteristically Aristotelian -- concrete, mundane, and effective. It illustrates the primacy of particular knowledge for practical purposes. -- The claim that experience (empeiria) sometimes surpasses theoretical knowledge echoes Metaphysics I.1, 981a12-24, where Aristotle says experienced people are often more successful in action than those who know the universal but lack experience. -- The mention of an 'architectonic' form of practical wisdom sets up the discussion in 6.21 of political wisdom as the master form of phronesis. -- The emphasis on particulars anticipates 6.25's comparison of practical wisdom to perception.

D: Original: Political wisdom and practical wisdom are the same state of mind, but their essence is not the same. Of the wisdom concerned with the city, the practical wisdom which plays a controlling part is legislative wisdom, while that which is related to this as particulars to their universal is known by the general name 'political wisdom'; this has to do with action and deliberation, for a decree is a thing to be carried out in the form of an individual act. This is why the exponents of this art are alone said to 'take part in politics'; for these alone 'do things' as manual labourers 'do things'.
D: Refashioned: Political wisdom and practical wisdom are the same state of mind, though their essential nature differs. Among the forms of wisdom concerned with the city, the controlling, architectonic form is legislative wisdom, while the form related to legislation as particulars are related to universals is known by the general name 'political wisdom.' This latter form has to do with action and deliberation, for a decree is something to be carried out as an individual act. This is why those who engage in this work are alone said to 'take part in politics,' for they alone 'do things' in the way that manual laborers 'do things.'
D: Elegant: Political wisdom and practical wisdom are the same capacity applied differently. Legislative wisdom is its architectonic form, setting universal frameworks; political wisdom in the narrower sense applies these to particular decisions and decrees. Only those who deal in such particulars are truly said to 'do politics.'
D: Summary: Political wisdom and practical wisdom are the same mental capacity, differing only in application. Legislative wisdom is the universal, architectonic form that establishes broad frameworks, while political wisdom in the everyday sense deals with particular decisions and decrees -- the actual 'doing' of politics.
D: Distilled: Political and practical wisdom are the same capacity; legislation provides the universal framework, while day-to-day politics applies it to particular actions.
Notes: The identification of phronesis with politike in some sense connects to Book I.2, where politics is called the 'architectonic' science that orders all others. -- The observation that only those who deal in particular decrees are said to 'do politics' reflects Aristotle's awareness that 'politics' in common usage meant the active, particular work of governing, not abstract theorizing about constitutions. -- The analogy with manual laborers (cheirotechnai) suggests that both politicians and craftsmen are valued for their practical engagement with particulars.

D: Original: Practical wisdom also is identified especially with that form of it which is concerned with a man himself-with the individual; and this is known by the general name 'practical wisdom'; of the other kinds one is called household management, another legislation, the third politics, and of the latter one part is called deliberative and the other judicial. Now knowing what is good for oneself will be one kind of knowledge, but it is very different from the other kinds; and the man who knows and concerns himself with his own interests is thought to have practical wisdom, while politicians are thought to be busybodies; hence the word of Euripides,
D: Refashioned:
Practical wisdom is also identified especially with the form of it that concerns oneself as an individual, and this is what commonly bears the general name 'practical wisdom.' The other forms are called household management, legislation, and politics -- the last being divided into deliberative and judicial branches.

Now knowing what is good for oneself is one kind of knowledge, but it differs greatly from the other kinds. The person who knows and attends to their own interests is thought to have practical wisdom, while politicians are considered busybodies. Hence the words of Euripides:
D: Elegant: Practical wisdom in its most personal form -- knowing what is good for oneself -- is what people typically mean by the term. Other branches include household management, legislation, and politics (deliberative and judicial). People who attend to their own interests are called practically wise, while politicians are dismissed as busybodies, as Euripides suggests.
D: Summary: In common usage, practical wisdom primarily refers to the individual capacity to know what is good for oneself. Its broader forms include household management, legislation, and politics. There is a popular tendency to view self-interested prudence as true wisdom and political engagement as meddlesome busywork.
D: Distilled: People most readily call 'practically wise' those who manage their own affairs well, often dismissing political engagement as mere busybodiness.
Notes: Euripides (c. 480-406 BCE) was one of the three great Athenian tragedians. The quotation continues in 6.23. -- The division into household management (oikonomia), legislation (nomothetike), and politics (politike) maps onto the three practical sciences Aristotle recognizes. -- The popular identification of practical wisdom with self-interested prudence is a view Aristotle will partially correct: genuine practical wisdom, he argues, necessarily involves concern for others, since the individual good cannot be separated from the common good.

D: Original: But how could I be wise, who might at ease, Numbered among the army's multitude, Have had an equal share? For those who aim too high and do too much. Those who think thus seek their own good, and consider that one ought to do so. From this opinion, then, has come the view that such men have practical wisdom; yet perhaps one's own good cannot exist without household management, nor without a form of government. Further, how one should order one's own affairs is not clear and needs inquiry.
D: Refashioned:
'But how could I be wise, who might at ease, numbered among the army's multitude, have had an equal share? ... For those who aim too high and do too much ...'

Those who think this way seek their own good and consider that one ought to do so. From this opinion, then, has come the view that such people have practical wisdom. Yet perhaps one's own good cannot exist without household management or a form of government. Furthermore, how one should order one's own affairs is not at all obvious and requires inquiry.
D: Elegant: The Euripides quotation voices the view that wisdom means quietly looking after oneself rather than overreaching in public life. But this view is questionable: one's own good may be inseparable from household management and political order. How to manage one's own affairs is itself far from obvious.
D: Summary: Aristotle cites Euripides to illustrate the popular view that wisdom consists in quietly minding one's own business. He challenges this, noting that personal good may require sound household management and political order, and that managing one's own life is itself a difficult problem requiring inquiry.
D: Distilled: One's own good cannot be fully separated from the good management of households and cities, and even self-governance requires serious deliberation.
Notes: The Euripides quotation is from the Philoctetes (fragment 787 Nauck), a lost play. The sentiment reflects a quietist stance common in some Greek thought, contrasting with the Periclean ideal of active citizenship. -- Aristotle's response -- that individual good requires political order -- is consistent with his claim in Politics I.2 that humans are by nature political animals (zoon politikon, 1253a2-3). -- The observation that ordering one's own affairs 'is not clear and needs inquiry' is a characteristically modest Aristotelian disclaimer that motivates further investigation.

D: Original: What has been said is confirmed by the fact that while young men become geometricians and mathematicians and wise in matters like these, it is thought that a young man of practical wisdom cannot be found. The cause is that such wisdom is concerned not only with universals but with particulars, which become familiar from experience, but a young man has no experience, for it is length of time that gives experience; indeed one might ask this question too, why a boy may become a mathematician, but not a philosopher or a physicist. It is because the objects of mathematics exist by abstraction, while the first principles of these other subjects come from experience, and because young men have no conviction about the latter but merely use the proper language, while the essence of mathematical objects is plain enough to them?
D: Refashioned:
What has been said is confirmed by the fact that while young people can become geometricians and mathematicians and wise in such subjects, a young person of practical wisdom is not thought to exist. The reason is that practical wisdom is concerned not only with universals but with particulars, which become familiar only through experience, and a young person lacks experience -- for it is length of time that gives experience.

Indeed, one might also ask why a young person can become a mathematician but not a philosopher or a natural scientist. Is it not because the objects of mathematics exist by abstraction, while the first principles of philosophy and natural science come from experience? Young people lack genuine conviction about the latter, merely using the proper language, whereas the essence of mathematical objects is plain enough to them.
D: Elegant: Young people can master mathematics but not practical wisdom, because practical wisdom requires experience with particulars, which only time provides. Mathematics deals in abstractions accessible to any capable mind, but philosophy and natural science rest on first principles derived from experience, about which the young have no real conviction despite using the right vocabulary.
D: Summary: Aristotle explains why young people can excel at mathematics but lack practical wisdom: mathematics deals with abstractions, while practical wisdom requires experience with particular situations that only accumulates over time. Similarly, philosophy and natural science rest on experiential first principles that the young merely parrot without genuine understanding.
D: Distilled: Practical wisdom, unlike mathematics, requires lived experience with particulars that only time can provide, which is why the young cannot possess it.
Notes: This is one of Aristotle's most frequently cited passages on the relationship between age, experience, and wisdom. -- The distinction between mathematical objects (existing 'by abstraction,' ex aphareseos) and physical/ethical first principles (from experience) reflects Aristotle's philosophy of mathematics in Metaphysics XIII-XIV. -- The observation that young people 'merely use the proper language' without conviction anticipates modern discussions of surface-level versus deep understanding. -- 'Philosopher' here likely means 'natural philosopher' (physikos), not philosopher in the modern sense.

D: Original:
Further, error in deliberation may be either about the universal or about the particular; we may fall to know either that all water that weighs heavy is bad, or that this particular water weighs heavy.

That practical wisdom is not scientific knowledge is evident; for it is, as has been said, concerned with the ultimate particular fact, since the thing to be done is of this nature. It is opposed, then, to intuitive reason; for intuitive reason is of the limiting premisses, for which no reason can be given, while practical wisdom is concerned with the ultimate particular, which is the object not of scientific knowledge but of perception-not the perception of qualities peculiar to one sense but a perception akin to that by which we perceive that the particular figure before us is a triangle; for in that direction as well as in that of the major premiss there will be a limit. But this is rather perception than practical wisdom, though it is another kind of perception than that of the qualities peculiar to each sense.
D: Refashioned:
Furthermore, error in deliberation may concern either the universal or the particular: one may fail to know that all heavy water is unhealthy, or that this particular water is heavy.

That practical wisdom is not scientific knowledge is evident, for as has been said, it is concerned with the ultimate particular fact, since the thing to be done is of this nature. It is thus opposed to intuitive reason in a certain way: intuitive reason grasps the limiting first principles for which no further reason can be given, while practical wisdom grasps the ultimate particular, which is the object not of scientific knowledge but of perception -- not the perception of qualities belonging to a single sense, but a perception akin to that by which we perceive that the particular figure before us is a triangle. For in that direction too -- the direction of the ultimate particular, just as in the direction of the first principles -- there is a limit beyond which reasoning cannot go. But this is perception rather than practical wisdom, though it is a different kind of perception from that of the individual senses.
D: Elegant: Practical wisdom grasps the ultimate particular -- the concrete situation demanding action -- just as intuitive reason grasps first principles at the other extreme. This grasp of particulars resembles perception more than scientific knowledge, though it is not ordinary sense-perception but something like the immediate recognition that a figure is a triangle.
D: Summary: Practical wisdom deals with ultimate particulars -- the concrete facts of the situation at hand -- just as intuitive reason deals with ultimate first principles. This grasp of particulars is more like perception than scientific knowledge, though it is a refined, intellectual form of perception rather than ordinary sense experience.
D: Distilled: Practical wisdom grasps the concrete particular situation through a kind of intellectual perception, just as intuitive reason grasps the indemonstrable first principles.
Notes: The comparison of practical wisdom's grasp of particulars to perception (aisthesis) is significant: it suggests that knowing what to do in a particular situation is not a matter of applying rules but of seeing what the situation requires. -- The triangle example illustrates non-sensory 'perception' -- recognizing that a shape is a triangle is an intellectual act, not a raw sensation. Cf. De Anima III.1, 425a14-b4 on the 'common sense.' -- The structural parallel between nous at the top (grasping first principles) and phronesis at the bottom (grasping particulars) creates a symmetry that Aristotle develops further in 6.30.

D: Original: There is a difference between inquiry and deliberation; for deliberation is inquiry into a particular kind of thing. We must grasp the nature of excellence in deliberation as well whether it is a form of scientific knowledge, or opinion, or skill in conjecture, or some other kind of thing. Scientific knowledge it is not; for men do not inquire about the things they know about, but good deliberation is a kind of deliberation, and he who deliberates inquires and calculates. Nor is it skill in conjecture; for this both involves no reasoning and is something that is quick in its operation, while men deliberate a long time, and they say that one should carry out quickly the conclusions of one's deliberation, but should deliberate slowly. Again, readiness of mind is different from excellence in deliberation; it is a sort of skill in conjecture. Nor again is excellence in deliberation opinion of any sort. But since the man who deliberates badly makes a mistake, while he who deliberates well does so correctly, excellence in deliberation is clearly a kind of correctness, but neither of knowledge nor of opinion; for there is no such thing as correctness of knowledge (since there is no such thing as error of knowledge), and correctness of opinion is truth; and at the same time everything that is an object of opinion is already determined. But again excellence in deliberation involves reasoning. The remaining alternative, then, is that it is correctness of thinking; for this is not yet assertion, since, while even opinion is not inquiry but has reached the stage of assertion, the man who is deliberating, whether he does so well or ill, is searching for something and calculating.
D: Refashioned:
There is a difference between inquiry and deliberation, for deliberation is inquiry into a particular kind of thing. We must determine the nature of excellence in deliberation -- whether it is a form of scientific knowledge, opinion, skill in conjecture, or something else entirely.

It is not scientific knowledge, since people do not inquire about what they already know, but good deliberation is a kind of deliberation, and the person who deliberates inquires and calculates. Nor is it skill in conjecture, since conjecture involves no reasoning and is quick in operation, while deliberation takes time -- as the saying goes, one should carry out the conclusions of deliberation quickly, but deliberate slowly. Again, readiness of mind differs from excellence in deliberation; readiness of mind is a kind of skill in conjecture.

Nor is excellence in deliberation any kind of opinion. But since the person who deliberates badly makes a mistake while the person who deliberates well does so correctly, excellence in deliberation is clearly a kind of correctness -- though neither correctness of knowledge (since there is no error in knowledge) nor correctness of opinion (since correctness of opinion is simply truth, and everything that is an object of opinion is already determined).

Yet excellence in deliberation does involve reasoning. The remaining alternative, then, is that it is correctness of thinking. For thinking is not yet assertion -- while opinion has passed the inquiry stage and reached assertion, the person who deliberates, well or badly, is still searching and calculating.
D: Elegant: Excellence in deliberation (euboulia) is not scientific knowledge, conjecture, or opinion. It is a form of correct thinking -- the reasoning process of inquiry and calculation that has not yet reached the stage of assertion. One should deliberate slowly but execute quickly.
D: Summary: Aristotle defines excellence in deliberation (euboulia) by eliminating alternatives. It is not scientific knowledge (which precludes inquiry), not skill in conjecture (which is quick and unreasoned), and not opinion (which is already settled). It is correctness of thinking -- the accuracy of the reasoning process itself while one is still searching and calculating.
D: Distilled: Excellence in deliberation is correctness in the process of thinking itself -- the accuracy of inquiry and calculation before one reaches a settled conclusion.
Notes: Euboulia (excellence in deliberation, or 'good counsel') is introduced here as a subordinate capacity related to phronesis. -- The distinction between deliberating slowly and executing quickly became a common maxim in ancient practical wisdom literature. -- The careful elimination of alternatives (not episteme, not eustochia, not doxa) follows the same dialectical method used to define other intellectual virtues. -- The key insight is that euboulia concerns the process of reasoning, not the final product -- it is a procedural rather than a propositional excellence.

D: Original: But excellence in deliberation is a certain correctness of deliberation; hence we must first inquire what deliberation is and what it is about. And, there being more than one kind of correctness, plainly excellence in deliberation is not any and every kind; for (1) the incontinent man and the bad man, if he is clever, will reach as a result of his calculation what he sets before himself, so that he will have deliberated correctly, but he will have got for himself a great evil. Now to have deliberated well is thought to be a good thing; for it is this kind of correctness of deliberation that is excellence in deliberation, viz. that which tends to attain what is good. But (2) it is possible to attain even good by a false syllogism, and to attain what one ought to do but not by the right means, the middle term being false; so that this too is not yet excellence in deliberation this state in virtue of which one attains what one ought but not by the right means. Again (3) it is possible to attain it by long deliberation while another man attains it quickly. Therefore in the former case we have not yet got excellence in deliberation, which is rightness with regard to the expedient-rightness in respect both of the end, the manner, and the time. (4) Further it is possible to have deliberated well either in the unqualified sense or with reference to a particular end. Excellence in deliberation in the unqualified sense, then, is that which succeeds with reference to what is the end in the unqualified sense, and excellence in deliberation in a particular sense is that which succeeds relatively to a particular end. If, then, it is characteristic of men of practical wisdom to have deliberated well, excellence in deliberation will be correctness with regard to what conduces to the end of which practical wisdom is the true apprehension.
D: Refashioned:
But excellence in deliberation is a certain correctness of deliberation, so we must first determine what deliberation is and what it is about. Since there is more than one kind of correctness, excellence in deliberation is clearly not just any kind. Consider: (1) the person lacking self-control and the bad person, if clever, will reach through calculation whatever goal they set for themselves, and so will have 'deliberated correctly' in a sense -- yet will have obtained a great evil. But having deliberated well is thought to be something good. Therefore, it is this kind of correctness in deliberation -- the kind that tends to attain what is genuinely good -- that constitutes excellence in deliberation.

(2) It is also possible to arrive at something good through a false chain of reasoning -- reaching the right conclusion but not by the right means, when the middle term is false. So this, too, is not yet excellence in deliberation: reaching the right goal but not by correct reasoning.

(3) Further, it is possible for one person to reach a good result through long deliberation while another reaches it quickly. Therefore, the slow result is not yet full excellence in deliberation either; rather, excellence in deliberation is correctness with respect to what is beneficial -- correctness in the end, the manner, and the time.

(4) Finally, it is possible to have deliberated well either in the unqualified sense or with reference to a particular end. Excellence in deliberation in the unqualified sense succeeds with reference to what is the end in the unqualified sense, while excellence in deliberation in a particular sense succeeds relative to a particular end.

If, then, it is characteristic of practically wise people to have deliberated well, excellence in deliberation will be correctness with regard to what conduces to the end of which practical wisdom is the true apprehension.
D: Elegant: Excellence in deliberation requires four conditions: (1) it must aim at genuine goods, not merely achieve whatever one happens to want; (2) it must reach the right conclusion through correct reasoning, not by lucky accident; (3) it must arrive at the right result in the right manner and in the right time; and (4) in its fullest sense, it must aim at what is good without qualification. It is the correctness of reasoning that leads to the end that practical wisdom truly apprehends.
D: Summary: Aristotle specifies four conditions for genuine excellence in deliberation: it must aim at true goods (not merely achieve bad goals cleverly); it must use correct reasoning (not reach good conclusions through faulty logic); it must operate in the right timeframe; and in the fullest sense, it must aim at the unconditionally good. Excellence in deliberation is the correct reasoning toward the end that practical wisdom accurately grasps.
D: Distilled: Genuine excellence in deliberation requires reaching the right end, by the right reasoning, in the right manner and time, aimed at what is unconditionally good.
Notes: The four conditions progressively narrow the definition: not just any success, not accidental success, not slow success, and ultimately success directed at the unqualified good (the end of Book I: eudaimonia). -- The case of the clever but vicious person (1) is important: cleverness (deinotes) achieves whatever one aims at, but euboulia requires that the aim itself be good. This is developed further in 6.35. -- The mention of a 'false middle term' (2) uses the language of syllogistic logic from the Prior Analytics to describe a case where one reasons fallaciously but luckily reaches a true conclusion.

D: Original: Understanding, also, and goodness of understanding, in virtue of which men are said to be men of understanding or of good understanding, are neither entirely the same as opinion or scientific knowledge (for at that rate all men would have been men of understanding), nor are they one of the particular sciences, such as medicine, the science of things connected with health, or geometry, the science of spatial magnitudes. For understanding is neither about things that are always and are unchangeable, nor about any and every one of the things that come into being, but about things which may become subjects of questioning and deliberation. Hence it is about the same objects as practical wisdom; but understanding and practical wisdom are not the same. For practical wisdom issues commands, since its end is what ought to be done or not to be done; but understanding only judges. (Understanding is identical with goodness of understanding, men of understanding with men of good understanding.) Now understanding is neither the having nor the acquiring of practical wisdom; but as learning is called understanding when it means the exercise of the faculty of knowledge, so 'understanding' is applicable to the exercise of the faculty of opinion for the purpose of judging of what some one else says about matters with which practical wisdom is concerned-and of judging soundly; for 'well' and 'soundly' are the same thing. And from this has come the use of the name 'understanding' in virtue of which men are said to be 'of good understanding', viz. from the application of the word to the grasping of scientific truth; for we often call such grasping understanding.
D: Refashioned:
Comprehension, and goodness of comprehension, in virtue of which people are said to be 'understanding' or 'of good understanding,' are neither entirely the same as opinion or scientific knowledge (for then everyone would possess comprehension), nor are they any one of the particular sciences such as medicine or geometry. For comprehension is not about things that are eternal and unchangeable, nor about just any subject that comes into being, but about things that may become subjects of questioning and deliberation. Hence it concerns the same objects as practical wisdom.

But comprehension and practical wisdom are not the same thing. For practical wisdom issues commands -- its end is what ought or ought not to be done -- while comprehension only judges. (Comprehension is the same as goodness of comprehension, and people of understanding are the same as people of good understanding.)

Now comprehension is neither the possession nor the acquisition of practical wisdom. Rather, just as 'learning' can mean the exercise of knowledge one already has, 'understanding' applies to the exercise of the faculty of opinion for the purpose of judging what someone else says about matters with which practical wisdom is concerned -- and judging soundly, since 'well' and 'soundly' are the same thing. This is the origin of the term 'understanding' as applied to people of 'good understanding': it comes from the application of the word to the grasping of truth, for we often call such grasping 'understanding.'
D: Elegant: Comprehension (synesis) concerns the same domain as practical wisdom -- matters subject to deliberation -- but differs in function: practical wisdom commands action, while comprehension judges. It is the capacity to evaluate soundly what another person says about practical matters, exercising opinion for the purpose of accurate judgment rather than directing action oneself.
D: Summary: Comprehension (synesis) shares its subject matter with practical wisdom -- both concern matters open to deliberation -- but serves a different function. While practical wisdom commands action, comprehension evaluates and judges. It is the ability to assess soundly what others say about practical matters, a capacity for accurate judgment rather than for directing one's own conduct.
D: Distilled: Comprehension is the capacity to judge soundly about matters of deliberation -- sharing practical wisdom's domain but evaluating rather than commanding.
Notes: Synesis is often translated as 'understanding' or 'comprehension.' Aristotle carefully distinguishes it from phronesis: synesis is a critical, evaluative capacity (it judges), while phronesis is a directive one (it commands). -- The distinction between commanding and judging maps roughly onto a distinction between active decision-making and the evaluative capacity of an adviser or critic. -- The etymological observation connecting synesis with the act of grasping (synienai) truth illustrates Aristotle's typical practice of drawing philosophical distinctions from ordinary language usage.

D: Original: What is called judgement, in virtue of which men are said to 'be sympathetic judges' and to 'have judgement', is the right discrimination of the equitable. This is shown by the fact that we say the equitable man is above all others a man of sympathetic judgement, and identify equity with sympathetic judgement about certain facts. And sympathetic judgement is judgement which discriminates what is equitable and does so correctly; and correct judgement is that which judges what is true.
D: Refashioned: What is called judgment -- in virtue of which people are said to 'have sympathetic judgment' and to 'be sympathetic judges' -- is the correct discernment of what is equitable. This is shown by the fact that we say the equitable person is above all others a person of sympathetic judgment, and we identify equity with sympathetic judgment about certain matters. And sympathetic judgment is judgment that discerns what is equitable and does so correctly; and correct judgment is that which judges what is true.
D: Elegant: Judgment (gnome) is the correct discernment of what is equitable. The person of sympathetic judgment is the equitable person, who accurately perceives when strict rules should be tempered by fairness in particular circumstances.
D: Summary: Judgment (gnome) is defined as the correct discernment of equity -- the ability to perceive when strict rules should be adjusted to fit particular circumstances fairly. The person of good judgment and the equitable person are essentially the same.
D: Distilled: Judgment is the correct perception of what equity requires in particular cases where strict rules fall short.
Notes: Gnome (judgment, fellow-feeling, sympathetic judgment) is connected to epieikeia (equity), which Aristotle discusses more fully in Book V.10, 1137a31-1138a3. Equity is a correction of law where law falls short due to its universality. -- The connection between gnome and equity makes judgment a quasi-perceptual capacity for fairness in particular cases -- closely related to phronesis but focused specifically on equitable treatment. -- The Greek gnome also means 'maxim' or 'considered opinion,' and the double sense is relevant: good judgment both grasps and can articulate what fairness requires.

D: Original: Now all the states we have considered converge, as might be expected, to the same point; for when we speak of judgement and understanding and practical wisdom and intuitive reason we credit the same people with possessing judgement and having reached years of reason and with having practical wisdom and understanding. For all these faculties deal with ultimates, i.e. with particulars; and being a man of understanding and of good or sympathetic judgement consists in being able judge about the things with which practical wisdom is concerned; for the equities are common to all good men in relation to other men. Now all things which have to be done are included among particulars or ultimates; for not only must the man of practical wisdom know particular facts, but understanding and judgement are also concerned with things to be done, and these are ultimates. And intuitive reason is concerned with the ultimates in both directions; for both the first terms and the last are objects of intuitive reason and not of argument, and the intuitive reason which is presupposed by demonstrations grasps the unchangeable and first terms, while the intuitive reason involved in practical reasonings grasps the last and variable fact, i.e. the minor premiss. For these variable facts are the starting-points for the apprehension of the end, since the universals are reached from the particulars; of these therefore we must have perception, and this perception is intuitive reason.
D: Refashioned:
Now all the states we have examined converge, as one would expect, on the same point. When we speak of judgment, comprehension, practical wisdom, and intuitive reason, we credit the same people with possessing judgment, having reached years of reason, and having practical wisdom and comprehension. For all these faculties deal with ultimates -- that is, with particulars. Being a person of comprehension and of good or sympathetic judgment consists in being able to judge about the matters with which practical wisdom is concerned, since equitable treatment is common to all good people in their relations with others.

Now all things that must be done fall among particulars or ultimates, for the practically wise person must not only know particular facts, but comprehension and judgment are also concerned with things to be done, and these are ultimates. And intuitive reason is concerned with ultimates in both directions: both first terms and last terms are objects of intuitive reason, not of argument. The intuitive reason presupposed by demonstrations grasps the unchangeable first principles, while the intuitive reason involved in practical reasoning grasps the last and variable fact -- the minor premise. For these variable particular facts are the starting points for apprehending the end, since universals are reached from particulars. Of these particulars, therefore, we must have perception, and this perception is intuitive reason.
D: Elegant: Judgment, comprehension, practical wisdom, and intuitive reason all converge on the same point: the grasp of particular, concrete facts. Intuitive reason operates at both extremes -- grasping indemonstrable first principles in theory and grasping the ultimate particular facts in practice. In practical reasoning, it is through perception of particulars that we arrive at universal ends, for universals are built from particulars.
D: Summary: All the practical intellectual faculties -- judgment, comprehension, practical wisdom, and intuitive reason -- converge on the ability to grasp particulars. Intuitive reason works at both extremes: grasping first principles in theoretical reasoning and grasping concrete particular facts in practical reasoning. Since universals are reached from particulars, this perceptual grasp of the particular situation is the foundation of all practical knowledge.
D: Distilled: All practical intellectual virtues converge on the grasp of particulars, and intuitive reason operates at both extremes -- first principles in theory and concrete facts in practice.
Notes: This passage reveals a deep structural symmetry in Aristotle's epistemology: nous (intuitive reason) is needed both at the 'top' (first principles) and the 'bottom' (particular facts), while the middle is handled by demonstrative reasoning. -- The claim that 'universals are reached from particulars' echoes the doctrine of Posterior Analytics II.19 on how nous arises from experience. -- The convergence of all practical intellectual virtues on particulars underscores Aristotle's emphasis that practical knowledge is irreducibly concrete and situation-specific, not merely a matter of applying general rules.

D: Original:
This is why these states are thought to be natural endowments-why, while no one is thought to be a philosopher by nature, people are thought to have by nature judgement, understanding, and intuitive reason. This is shown by the fact that we think our powers correspond to our time of life, and that a particular age brings with it intuitive reason and judgement; this implies that nature is the cause. (Hence intuitive reason is both beginning and end; for demonstrations are from these and about these.) Therefore we ought to attend to the undemonstrated sayings and opinions of experienced and older people or of people of practical wisdom not less than to demonstrations; for because experience has given them an eye they see aright.

We have stated, then, what practical and philosophic wisdom are, and with what each of them is concerned, and we have said that each is the virtue of a different part of the soul.
D: Refashioned:
This is why these states are thought to be natural endowments. While no one is thought to be a philosopher by nature, people are thought to possess judgment, comprehension, and intuitive reason by nature. This is shown by the fact that we think these capacities correspond to our time of life, and that a particular age brings with it intuitive reason and judgment -- implying that nature is the cause.

Hence intuitive reason is both beginning and end, for demonstrations proceed from first principles and are about these same principles. Therefore we ought to attend to the undemonstrated sayings and opinions of experienced and older people -- or of people of practical wisdom -- no less than to formal demonstrations. For because experience has given them an eye, they see correctly.

We have stated, then, what practical and philosophic wisdom are, what each is concerned with, and that each is the virtue of a different part of the soul.
D: Elegant: Judgment, comprehension, and intuitive reason are thought to be natural endowments that develop with age, unlike philosophic wisdom which must be learned. The experienced eye of older, practically wise people perceives truths that cannot be formally demonstrated -- their unproven sayings and opinions deserve no less respect than rigorous proofs.
D: Summary: People naturally develop judgment, comprehension, and intuitive reason with age and experience, unlike philosophic wisdom which must be deliberately acquired. Because experienced people have developed a perceptual 'eye' for truth through long practice, their undemonstrated opinions deserve as much respect as formal proofs. Aristotle concludes the survey of practical and philosophic wisdom as virtues of different parts of the soul.
D: Distilled: Experience gives older and practically wise people a perceptive eye for truth, making their undemonstrated insights as worthy of attention as formal proofs.
Notes: The claim that we should attend to the 'undemonstrated sayings' (aphantos lexeis) of the experienced is a remarkable concession from the author of the Posterior Analytics, where demonstration is the gold standard of knowledge. It shows Aristotle recognizes the limits of formal reasoning in practical domains. -- The metaphor of experience giving an 'eye' (omma) echoes the broader Greek tradition connecting wisdom with vision (cf. Plato, Republic VII, the allegory of the cave). -- This passage summarizes the results of Book VI's first eleven chapters before turning to the utility objection in 6.32.

D: Original: Difficulties might be raised as to the utility of these qualities of mind. For (1) philosophic wisdom will contemplate none of the things that will make a man happy (for it is not concerned with any coming into being), and though practical wisdom has this merit, for what purpose do we need it? Practical wisdom is the quality of mind concerned with things just and noble and good for man, but these are the things which it is the mark of a good man to do, and we are none the more able to act for knowing them if the virtues are states of character, just as we are none the better able to act for knowing the things that are healthy and sound, in the sense not of producing but of issuing from the state of health; for we are none the more able to act for having the art of medicine or of gymnastics. But (2) if we are to say that a man should have practical wisdom not for the sake of knowing moral truths but for the sake of becoming good, practical wisdom will be of no use to those who are good; again it is of no use to those who have not virtue; for it will make no difference whether they have practical wisdom themselves or obey others who have it, and it would be enough for us to do what we do in the case of health; though we wish to become healthy, yet we do not learn the art of medicine. (3) Besides this, it would be thought strange if practical wisdom, being inferior to philosophic wisdom, is to be put in authority over it, as seems to be implied by the fact that the art which produces anything rules and issues commands about that thing.
D: Refashioned:
Difficulties might be raised about the utility of these intellectual qualities. (1) Philosophic wisdom contemplates none of the things that will make a person happy, for it is not concerned with anything coming into being. And though practical wisdom has this concern, for what purpose do we actually need it? Practical wisdom is the quality of mind concerned with things just, noble, and good for human beings, but these are precisely the things a good person does. We are no better able to act for knowing them, if the virtues are states of character -- just as we are no better able to act for knowing what is healthy and sound, if 'healthy' refers not to what produces health but to what issues from a state of health. We are no better at acting healthily just because we possess the art of medicine or gymnastics.

(2) If the point is that we should have practical wisdom not for knowing moral truths but for becoming good, then practical wisdom will be useless to those who are already good. Again, it is useless to those who lack virtue, for it makes no difference whether they possess practical wisdom themselves or follow others who have it -- and it would be enough for us to do what we do with health: we wish to be healthy but do not learn the art of medicine.

(3) Besides, it would seem strange if practical wisdom, being inferior to philosophic wisdom, is to have authority over it, as seems implied by the fact that the art which produces something rules and commands regarding that thing.
D: Elegant: Three objections arise: (1) Philosophic wisdom produces nothing useful, and practical wisdom tells us what the good person already does -- knowing virtue does not make us more virtuous, just as knowing medicine does not make us healthier. (2) Practical wisdom is useless both to the already-good and to the bad, who could simply follow others' advice. (3) It seems absurd that practical wisdom, which is inferior, should have authority over philosophic wisdom.
D: Summary: Aristotle raises three objections to the utility of intellectual virtue. First, philosophic wisdom produces nothing practical, and practical wisdom merely identifies what good people already do. Second, practical wisdom seems unnecessary for both the virtuous (who already act well) and the vicious (who could just follow advice). Third, it seems paradoxical that the lesser virtue (practical wisdom) should govern the greater (philosophic wisdom).
D: Distilled: The utility of intellectual virtue faces serious objections: philosophic wisdom produces nothing practical, practical wisdom seems redundant for both good and bad people, and the hierarchy between the two appears paradoxical.
Notes: These three aporia (difficulties) structure the remainder of Book VI. Aristotle's method of raising objections before resolving them is characteristic of his dialectical approach (cf. Metaphysics III for a systematic deployment of aporiai). -- Objection (1) parallels the debate about whether knowledge of the good is sufficient for being good, which Aristotle attributes to Socrates (cf. VII.2, 1145b25-27). -- Objection (3) about the hierarchy of practical and philosophic wisdom connects to the broader question of whether the contemplative or practical life is superior (Book X.7-8).

D: Original:
These, then, are the questions we must discuss; so far we have only stated the difficulties.

(1) Now first let us say that in themselves these states must be worthy of choice because they are the virtues of the two parts of the soul respectively, even if neither of them produce anything.
D: Refashioned:
These, then, are the questions we must discuss. So far we have only stated the difficulties.

(1) First, let us say that these states must be worthy of choice in themselves, because they are the virtues of the two parts of the soul respectively, even if neither of them produces anything further.
D: Elegant: Even if philosophic and practical wisdom produced nothing, they would still be worth choosing as the virtues -- the excellences -- of their respective parts of the soul.
D: Summary: Before resolving the detailed objections, Aristotle makes a foundational point: philosophic and practical wisdom are intrinsically worth having because they are the excellences of the two rational parts of the soul, regardless of any practical output they might produce.
D: Distilled: Intellectual virtues are worth possessing for their own sake as the excellences of the rational soul, independent of any practical results.
Notes: This response draws on the intrinsic value of virtue established in Book I, where happiness is defined as activity of the soul in accordance with virtue (I.7, 1098a16-18). -- The argument that virtues are choiceworthy in themselves, independent of consequences, represents an important strand in Aristotle's ethical thinking that resists purely instrumentalist accounts of virtue. -- This is the first of three responses to the objections raised in 6.32.

D: Original:
(2) Secondly, they do produce something, not as the art of medicine produces health, however, but as health produces health; so does philosophic wisdom produce happiness; for, being a part of virtue entire, by being possessed and by actualizing itself it makes a man happy.

(3) Again, the work of man is achieved only in accordance with practical wisdom as well as with moral virtue; for virtue makes us aim at the right mark, and practical wisdom makes us take the right means. (Of the fourth part of the soul-the nutritive-there is no such virtue; for there is nothing which it is in its power to do or not to do., 4) With regard to our being none the more able to do because of our practical wisdom what is noble and just, let us begin a little further back, starting with the following principle. As we say that some people who do just acts are not necessarily just, i.e. those who do the acts ordained by the laws either unwillingly or owing to ignorance or for some other reason and not for the sake of the acts themselves (though, to be sure, they do what they should and all the things that the good man ought), so is it, it seems, that in order to be good one must be in a certain state when one does the several acts, i.e. one must do them as a result of choice and for the sake of the acts themselves.
D: Refashioned:
(2) Secondly, they do in fact produce something -- not, however, as medicine produces health, but as health produces health. In this way, philosophic wisdom produces happiness: being a part of complete virtue, by being possessed and exercised it makes a person happy.

(3) Again, a person's proper function is achieved only through the combination of practical wisdom and moral virtue. Virtue makes us aim at the right end, and practical wisdom makes us take the right means. (Of the fourth part of the soul -- the nutritive -- there is no such virtue, for there is nothing it is in its power to do or not to do.)

(4) As for the objection that practical wisdom makes us no better able to do what is noble and just, let us begin a little further back. We say that some people who perform just acts are not necessarily just -- for instance, those who do what the laws require unwillingly, or through ignorance, or for some extraneous reason, and not for the sake of the acts themselves (though they do what they should and everything the good person ought to do). Similarly, it seems, to be truly good one must be in a certain state when performing these acts -- one must do them as a result of choice and for the sake of the acts themselves.
D: Elegant: Philosophic wisdom produces happiness not as a cause produces an effect but as health produces healthy activity -- by being a part of complete virtue. Practical wisdom and moral virtue work together: virtue sets the right end, practical wisdom finds the right means. Moreover, true goodness requires not just doing the right things but doing them from the right state of character -- from choice and for their own sake.
D: Summary: Aristotle replies that philosophic wisdom does produce happiness, but as an intrinsic constituent rather than an external cause. Practical wisdom and moral virtue cooperate: virtue determines the right end, practical wisdom determines the right means. True goodness requires acting from the right inner state -- performing good acts from choice and for their own sake, not merely doing what happens to be right.
D: Distilled: Virtue sets the right end and practical wisdom finds the right means; together they produce genuine goodness, which requires acting from choice and for the sake of what is noble.
Notes: The distinction between acting rightly and acting from the right state of character is fundamental to Aristotle's ethics. Merely doing the right thing is not sufficient for virtue; one must do it knowingly, by choice, and from a firm character (cf. II.4, 1105a28-33). -- The formula 'virtue makes the aim right, practical wisdom makes the means right' is one of the most cited in Aristotelian ethics and has generated debate about whether Aristotle's practical wisdom is merely instrumental. -- The claim that sophia produces eudaimonia 'as health produces health' uses the formal-cause model rather than the efficient-cause model, making sophia a constitutive part of flourishing.

D: Original:
Now virtue makes the choice right, but the question of the things which should naturally be done to carry out our choice belongs not to virtue but to another faculty. We must devote our attention to these matters and give a clearer statement about them. There is a faculty which is called cleverness; and this is such as to be able to do the things that tend towards the mark we have set before ourselves, and to hit it. Now if the mark be noble, the cleverness is laudable, but if the mark be bad, the cleverness is mere smartness; hence we call even men of practical wisdom clever or smart. Practical wisdom is not the faculty, but it does not exist without this faculty. And this eye of the soul acquires its formed state not without the aid of virtue, as has been said and is plain; for the syllogisms which deal with acts to be done are things which involve a starting-point, viz. 'since the end, i.e. what is best, is of such and such a nature', whatever it may be (let it for the sake of argument be what we please); and this is not evident except to the good man; for wickedness perverts us and causes us to be deceived about the starting-points of action.

Therefore it is evident that it is impossible to be practically wise without being good.
D: Refashioned:
Now virtue makes the choice right, but the question of what things should naturally be done to carry out our choice belongs not to virtue but to another faculty. We must give this matter closer attention and explain it more clearly.

There is a faculty called cleverness, which is the ability to perform the actions that tend toward whatever goal we have set and to hit that goal. If the goal is noble, this cleverness is praiseworthy; if the goal is base, it is mere cunning. Hence we call even practically wise people clever or shrewd. Practical wisdom is not this faculty of cleverness, but it does not exist without it.

This eye of the soul acquires its developed state not without the aid of virtue, as has been said and as is clear. For the practical syllogisms that deal with action involve a starting point: 'Since the end -- that is, what is best -- is of such and such a nature' (whatever it may be; let it be whatever we please for the sake of argument). This starting point is not evident except to the good person, for wickedness distorts us and causes us to be deceived about the starting points of action.

Therefore it is evident that it is impossible to be practically wise without being good.
D: Elegant: Cleverness is the ability to achieve whatever goal one sets; it is praiseworthy when directed at noble ends, mere cunning when directed at base ones. Practical wisdom requires cleverness but is not reducible to it, because it also requires virtue: only the good person can perceive the correct starting points of action. Wickedness distorts our perception of ends. Therefore, one cannot be practically wise without being morally good.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes cleverness (the ability to achieve any goal effectively) from practical wisdom (which requires that the goal be genuinely good). Practical wisdom depends on cleverness but goes beyond it by requiring moral virtue, since only the good person correctly perceives the true ends of action. Wickedness corrupts our grasp of what is worth pursuing. Therefore, practical wisdom and moral goodness are inseparable.
D: Distilled: Practical wisdom requires moral goodness because only the good person correctly perceives the true ends of action, which wickedness distorts.
Notes: Deinotes (cleverness) is morally neutral -- it becomes practical wisdom only when combined with correct ends, i.e., with moral virtue. This distinction is Aristotle's answer to the Socratic identification of virtue with knowledge: knowledge of means (cleverness) is not virtue unless the ends are right. -- The 'eye of the soul' (omma tes psyches) echoes Plato's Republic 533d, where the dialectician's 'eye of the soul' is drawn upward. Aristotle repurposes the metaphor for practical perception. -- The conclusion -- 'it is impossible to be practically wise without being good' -- is the capstone of Book VI's argument and establishes the mutual dependence of intellectual and moral virtue.

D: Original: We must therefore consider virtue also once more; for virtue too is similarly related; as practical wisdom is to cleverness-not the same, but like it-so is natural virtue to virtue in the strict sense. For all men think that each type of character belongs to its possessors in some sense by nature; for from the very moment of birth we are just or fitted for selfcontrol or brave or have the other moral qualities; but yet we seek something else as that which is good in the strict sense-we seek for the presence of such qualities in another way. For both children and brutes have the natural dispositions to these qualities, but without reason these are evidently hurtful. Only we seem to see this much, that, while one may be led astray by them, as a strong body which moves without sight may stumble badly because of its lack of sight, still, if a man once acquires reason, that makes a difference in action; and his state, while still like what it was, will then be virtue in the strict sense. Therefore, as in the part of us which forms opinions there are two types, cleverness and practical wisdom, so too in the moral part there are two types, natural virtue and virtue in the strict sense, and of these the latter involves practical wisdom.
D: Refashioned:
We must therefore consider virtue once more, for virtue is related to practical wisdom in a way that parallels the relationship between practical wisdom and cleverness. As practical wisdom stands to cleverness -- not the same, but similar -- so natural virtue stands to virtue in the strict sense.

Everyone thinks that each type of character belongs to its possessor in some sense by nature: from birth we are disposed toward justice, self-control, courage, and the other moral qualities. But we seek something more as genuine goodness in the strict sense -- we seek for these qualities to be present in a different way. For both children and animals have natural dispositions toward these qualities, but without reason these dispositions are evidently harmful. One can see at least this much: just as a powerful body moving without sight may stumble badly because it lacks sight, so too with natural virtue without reason.

But if a person once acquires reason, that makes all the difference in action; and their state, while still resembling what it was, will then be virtue in the strict sense. Therefore, just as in the part of us that forms opinions there are two types -- cleverness and practical wisdom -- so in the moral part there are two types: natural virtue and virtue in the strict sense. And of these, virtue in the strict sense involves practical wisdom.
D: Elegant: As practical wisdom is to cleverness, so true virtue is to natural virtue. We are born with natural tendencies toward courage, justice, and other qualities, but without reason these dispositions can be harmful -- like a powerful body moving without sight. When reason is added through practical wisdom, natural virtue becomes true virtue. There are thus two moral types: raw natural virtue and genuine virtue informed by practical wisdom.
D: Summary: Aristotle draws a parallel: as practical wisdom relates to cleverness, so true virtue relates to natural virtue. We are born with tendencies toward various moral qualities, but without rational guidance these can be harmful. When practical wisdom informs natural dispositions, they become genuine virtues. True virtue therefore requires practical wisdom, just as practical wisdom requires moral goodness.
D: Distilled: Natural moral dispositions become genuine virtues only when guided by practical wisdom, just as cleverness becomes practical wisdom only when directed at genuinely good ends.
Notes: The distinction between natural virtue (physike arete) and virtue in the strict sense (kyria arete) is crucial to Aristotle's moral psychology. Natural virtues are inborn dispositions; full virtues require the addition of practical wisdom. -- The metaphor of a strong body without sight powerfully conveys how natural virtues without reason can lead to harmful action. -- This passage completes the circle of mutual dependence: moral virtue needs practical wisdom to become genuine, and practical wisdom needs moral virtue to perceive correct ends (6.35). -- The connection between natural virtue and habituation (ethismos, Book II) remains debated: natural virtue provides the raw material that moral education shapes.

D: Original: This is why some say that all the virtues are forms of practical wisdom, and why Socrates in one respect was on the right track while in another he went astray; in thinking that all the virtues were forms of practical wisdom he was wrong, but in saying they implied practical wisdom he was right. This is confirmed by the fact that even now all men, when they define virtue, after naming the state of character and its objects add 'that (state) which is in accordance with the right rule'; now the right rule is that which is in accordance with practical wisdom. All men, then, seem somehow to divine that this kind of state is virtue, viz. that which is in accordance with practical wisdom.
D: Refashioned:
This is why some say that all the virtues are forms of practical wisdom, and why Socrates was in one respect on the right track while in another he went astray. In thinking that all the virtues were forms of practical wisdom, he was wrong; but in saying they implied practical wisdom, he was right.

This is confirmed by the fact that even now, when people define virtue, they name the state of character and its objects and then add 'that state which accords with the right rule.' And the right rule is that which accords with practical wisdom. All people, then, seem somehow to intuit that this kind of state -- the kind that accords with practical wisdom -- is virtue.
D: Elegant: Socrates was partly right: all virtues imply practical wisdom, though they are not identical with it. Even common definitions of virtue confirm this, since people always add 'in accordance with the right rule' -- and the right rule is practical wisdom.
D: Summary: Aristotle assesses the Socratic thesis that virtue is knowledge. Socrates was wrong to identify virtues with practical wisdom, but right to say they require it. This is confirmed by how people naturally define virtue: they always include the phrase 'in accordance with the right rule,' and the right rule just is practical wisdom.
D: Distilled: Socrates was right that virtue requires practical wisdom but wrong to reduce all virtue to it -- virtue accords with the right rule, and practical wisdom is that right rule.
Notes: The Socratic doctrine that virtue is knowledge (episteme) is discussed in several Platonic dialogues, notably Protagoras 361b and Meno 87c-89a. Aristotle's correction -- virtues involve (meta) a rational principle but are not (identical with) rational principles -- is a characteristically nuanced revision. -- The 'right rule' (orthos logos) here receives its definitive identification with phronesis, completing the promise made in 6.1. -- This passage is important for understanding Aristotle's relationship to his teacher Plato and to the Socratic tradition more broadly.

D: Original: But we must go a little further. For it is not merely the state in accordance with the right rule, but the state that implies the presence of the right rule, that is virtue; and practical wisdom is a right rule about such matters. Socrates, then, thought the virtues were rules or rational principles (for he thought they were, all of them, forms of scientific knowledge), while we think they involve a rational principle.
D: Refashioned:
But we must go a little further. For it is not merely the state that accords with the right rule that is virtue, but the state that implies the presence of the right rule. And practical wisdom is the right rule about such matters.

Socrates thought the virtues were rules or rational principles -- since he thought they were all forms of scientific knowledge -- while we hold that they involve a rational principle.
D: Elegant: True virtue is not merely in accordance with the right rule but embodies it -- the right rule must be present within the virtuous state itself. Practical wisdom is that right rule. Socrates identified virtues with rational principles; Aristotle holds that they involve rational principles.
D: Summary: Aristotle sharpens the distinction: virtue is not just acting in line with the right rule externally, but having the right rule -- practical wisdom -- as an internal component. Socrates treated virtues as identical to rational principles (forms of knowledge); Aristotle insists they involve rational principles without being reducible to them.
D: Distilled: Virtue does not merely follow the right rule from outside but contains practical wisdom as an internal, constitutive element.
Notes: The shift from 'in accordance with' (kata) to 'involving the presence of' (meta) the right rule is a subtle but important philosophical distinction. Merely acting in accordance with a rule could be accidental or externally motivated; having the rule present within oneself implies understanding and endorsement. -- This refines the definition of virtue from Book II.6, 1106b36-1107a2. -- The correction of Socrates is diplomatically expressed but philosophically significant: Aristotle preserves the intellectual element in virtue while insisting on its integration with character and desire.

D: Original: It is clear, then, from what has been said, that it is not possible to be good in the strict sense without practical wisdom, nor practically wise without moral virtue. But in this way we may also refute the dialectical argument whereby it might be contended that the virtues exist in separation from each other; the same man, it might be said, is not best equipped by nature for all the virtues, so that he will have already acquired one when he has not yet acquired another. This is possible in respect of the natural virtues, but not in respect of those in respect of which a man is called without qualification good; for with the presence of the one quality, practical wisdom, will be given all the virtues. And it is plain that, even if it were of no practical value, we should have needed it because it is the virtue of the part of us in question; plain too that the choice will not be right without practical wisdom any more than without virtue; for the one deter, mines the end and the other makes us do the things that lead to the end.
D: Refashioned:
It is clear, then, from what has been said, that it is not possible to be good in the strict sense without practical wisdom, nor practically wise without moral virtue.

In this way, we can also refute the dialectical argument that the virtues exist in separation from each other. The objection would be: the same person is not best equipped by nature for all the virtues, so they might have acquired one virtue without yet having acquired another. This is possible with respect to natural virtues, but not with respect to the virtues that qualify someone as unconditionally good. For with the presence of the one quality, practical wisdom, all the virtues will be present.

It is also plain that even if practical wisdom had no practical value, we would still need it because it is the virtue of its part of the soul. And it is plain too that choice will not be right without practical wisdom any more than without virtue, for the one determines the end while the other makes us do the things that lead to the end.
D: Elegant: One cannot be truly good without practical wisdom, nor practically wise without moral virtue. This unity thesis also refutes the claim that virtues can exist independently: natural virtues may be separate, but genuine virtues come as a package with practical wisdom. Virtue determines the right end; practical wisdom determines the right means. Neither suffices without the other.
D: Summary: Aristotle establishes the reciprocal dependence of practical wisdom and moral virtue, and uses this to argue for the unity of the virtues. While natural virtues can exist independently, genuine virtues cannot -- possessing practical wisdom entails possessing all the virtues. Virtue sets the correct end and practical wisdom identifies the correct means; right choice requires both.
D: Distilled: Genuine virtue and practical wisdom are mutually dependent, and possessing practical wisdom guarantees the unity of all the virtues.
Notes: The unity of the virtues (hen echonti panti) is one of the most debated theses in Aristotelian ethics. Strong unity holds that you cannot have any one virtue without having them all; weak unity holds merely that the virtues are deeply interconnected. This passage supports the strong version for kyria arete (virtue in the strict sense). -- The argument structure is: practical wisdom requires moral virtue (6.35); moral virtue requires practical wisdom (6.36); therefore they come together; and since practical wisdom is one, it unifies all the virtues. -- The final formula -- 'virtue determines the end, practical wisdom the means' -- has become perhaps the most influential summary of Aristotle's practical philosophy.
