Among foolish people, those who are naturally thoughtless and live entirely by their senses are brutish (like some distant peoples), while those who are so because of disease such as epilepsy or madness are morbid.

It is possible to have some of these traits only at times without being mastered by them. Phalaris, for instance, may have restrained a desire to eat a child's flesh or an appetite for unnatural sexual pleasure. But it is also possible to be mastered by them, not merely to experience the urges.

Thus, just as wickedness on the human level is called wickedness simply, while that which falls outside the human level is called wickedness with the qualifier 'brutish' or 'morbid,' so too it is plain that some incontinence is brutish and some morbid, while only that which corresponds to ordinary human self-indulgence is incontinence in the proper sense.

It is clear, then, that incontinence and continence properly concern only the same objects as self-indulgence and temperance, and that what concerns other objects is a distinct type, called incontinence metaphorically rather than in the primary sense.
D: Elegant:
Those driven by nature or disease to deviant pleasures are not incontinent in the proper sense -- their condition lies beyond vice, as brutishness does. Every extreme disposition is either brutish (from nature) or morbid (from disease). One may have such urges without being mastered by them, or one may be mastered.

Just as wickedness at the human level is wickedness simply, while brutish or morbid wickedness receives a qualifier, so incontinence properly concerns only the same objects as temperance and self-indulgence. All other applications of the term are metaphorical.
D: Summary: Brutish and morbid conditions fall outside the normal moral categories. Incontinence and continence in the proper sense concern only the bodily pleasures that temperance and self-indulgence also address. Everything else called 'incontinence' is metaphorical -- whether it involves anger, honor, or pathological conditions.
D: Distilled: Incontinence proper concerns only the bodily pleasures of temperance and self-indulgence; brutish and morbid conditions, like incontinence regarding anger or honor, receive the label only by metaphor.
Notes: Phalaris appears again (see 7.20) -- here Aristotle makes the subtle point that even someone with brutish desires might resist them, showing that the capacity for self-control exists even outside normal moral categories. -- The distinction between brutish and morbid wickedness anticipates modern distinctions between antisocial personality traits and mental illness. -- This passage concludes the discussion of what objects incontinence properly concerns, resolving puzzle (6) from chapter 2. -- The claim that animals and distant peoples are 'brutish' reflects Greek cultural assumptions but the underlying philosophical distinction between natural disposition and moral character remains important.

D: Original: That incontinence in respect of anger is less disgraceful than that in respect of the appetites is what we will now proceed to see. (1) Anger seems to listen to argument to some extent, but to mishear it, as do hasty servants who run out before they have heard the whole of what one says, and then muddle the order, or as dogs bark if there is but a knock at the door, before looking to see if it is a friend; so anger by reason of the warmth and hastiness of its nature, though it hears, does not hear an order, and springs to take revenge. For argument or imagination informs us that we have been insulted or slighted, and anger, reasoning as it were that anything like this must be fought against, boils up straightway; while appetite, if argument or perception merely says that an object is pleasant, springs to the enjoyment of it. Therefore anger obeys the argument in a sense, but appetite does not. It is therefore more disgraceful; for the man who is incontinent in respect of anger is in a sense conquered by argument, while the other is conquered by appetite and not by argument.
D: Refashioned:
We will now show that incontinence regarding anger is less disgraceful than incontinence regarding the appetites.

(1) Anger seems to listen to reason to some extent, but to mishear it -- like hasty servants who rush out before hearing the full message and then muddle the order, or like dogs that bark at a knock on the door without checking whether it is a friend. Similarly, anger, because of its hot and hasty nature, hears but does not properly hear the command, and springs to take revenge. For when reasoning or imagination informs us that we have been insulted or slighted, anger -- reasoning, as it were, that such treatment must be fought against -- boils up immediately. Appetite, by contrast, needs only for reasoning or perception to declare that something is pleasant, and it springs straight to enjoyment.

Therefore anger obeys reason in a sense, while appetite does not. Incontinence regarding appetite is thus more disgraceful: the person who is incontinent in anger is in a sense overcome by a (mis)application of reason, while the one who succumbs to appetite is conquered by appetite alone, not by reason at all.
D: Elegant: Anger is less disgraceful than appetite because anger at least responds to reason, even if it mishears -- like hasty servants who garble orders, or dogs that bark before checking who knocked. Anger receives the message of insult and rashly infers 'fight back,' while appetite simply perceives 'pleasant' and springs to enjoyment. The angry person is overcome by a crude form of reasoning; the appetitive person is conquered by desire with no reasoning at all.
D: Summary: Incontinence regarding anger is less blameworthy than incontinence regarding bodily appetite because anger at least partially responds to reason -- it hears the judgment of insult but reacts too hastily. Appetite bypasses reason entirely, springing directly to enjoyment the moment something is perceived as pleasant.
D: Distilled: Anger is less disgraceful than appetite because anger at least engages with reason, however imperfectly, while appetite bypasses reason entirely.
Notes: The vivid analogies of hasty servants and barking dogs illustrate Aristotle's view that anger has a quasi-rational structure -- it responds to perceived insults (a cognitive appraisal) but acts before fully processing the information. -- This account of anger as involving a kind of practical reasoning connects to Aristotle's Rhetoric II.2, where anger is defined as a desire for apparent revenge accompanied by pain at apparent slight. -- The chapter begins Aristotle's comparative evaluation of different forms of incontinence, arguing for a hierarchy of blameworthiness.

D: Original: (2) Further, we pardon people more easily for following natural desires, since we pardon them more easily for following such appetites as are common to all men, and in so far as they are common; now anger and bad temper are more natural than the appetites for excess, i.e. for unnecessary objects. Take for instance the man who defended himself on the charge of striking his father by saying 'yes, but he struck his father, and he struck his, and' (pointing to his child) 'this boy will strike me when he is a man; it runs in the family'; or the man who when he was being dragged along by his son bade him stop at the doorway, since he himself had dragged his father only as far as that.
D: Refashioned:
(2) Furthermore, we pardon people more readily for following natural desires, since we are more forgiving of appetites that are common to all people, and to the extent that they are universal. Now anger and bad temper are more natural than appetites for excess -- that is, for unnecessary pleasures.

Consider the man who defended himself against the charge of striking his father by saying: 'Yes, but he struck his father, and his father struck his father before him,' and then, pointing to his child, 'This boy will strike me when he is a man -- it runs in the family.' Or consider the man who, while being dragged along by his son, told him to stop at the doorway, since he himself had dragged his own father only that far.
D: Elegant: We pardon natural desires more readily, and anger is more natural than appetite for unnecessary excess. As the anecdotes illustrate -- a man defending himself for striking his father by claiming it runs in the family, or a father being dragged by his son asking him to stop at the doorway because he had dragged his own father only that far -- anger has a natural, almost hereditary quality that elicits more sympathy than indulgence in unnecessary pleasure.
D: Summary: Anger deserves more pardon than appetitive excess because anger is more natural and universal. Aristotle illustrates with darkly humorous anecdotes about intergenerational violence: a man who defends striking his father by calling it a family tradition, and a father who, while being dragged by his son, asks him to stop where he stopped with his own father.
D: Distilled: Anger deserves more pardon than appetite for excess because it is more natural and universal, as illustrated by the near-instinctive, hereditary quality of aggressive impulses.
Notes: These anecdotes appear to be well-known Greek jokes or legal stories. The dark humor about intergenerational violence illustrates how deeply ingrained anger is in human nature. -- The distinction between natural/necessary and unnatural/unnecessary appetites was established in Book III.11, where Aristotle distinguishes universal human desires (hunger, thirst) from individual excessive desires. -- The argument that more natural impulses are more pardonable reflects a consistent Aristotelian principle: we are less responsible for what is deeply rooted in our nature.

D: Original:
(2) Further, those who are more given to plotting against others are more criminal. Now a passionate man is not given to plotting, nor is anger itself-it is open; but the nature of appetite is illustrated by what the poets call Aphrodite, 'guile-weaving daughter of Cyprus', and by Homer's words about her 'embroidered girdle':

And the whisper of wooing is there, Whose subtlety stealeth the wits of the wise, how prudent soe'er. Therefore if this form of incontinence is more criminal and disgraceful than that in respect of anger, it is both incontinence without qualification and in a sense vice.
D: Refashioned:
(3) Furthermore, those who are more given to plotting are more criminal. A passionate person does not scheme, and anger itself is open and unconcealed. But appetite is crafty by nature, as illustrated by what the poets call Aphrodite: 'guile-weaving daughter of Cyprus,' and by Homer's description of her embroidered girdle:

'And the whisper of wooing is there, whose subtlety steals the wits of the wise, however prudent they be.'

Therefore, if incontinence regarding appetite is more criminal and disgraceful than incontinence regarding anger, it is both incontinence in the unqualified sense and, in a way, a kind of vice.
D: Elegant: Those who plot are more criminal, and appetite is inherently more cunning than anger. Anger is open; appetite is deceptive -- as the poets say of Aphrodite, 'guile-weaving daughter of Cyprus,' whose 'subtlety steals the wits of the wise.' Therefore incontinence regarding appetite is more criminal and more truly deserves the name incontinence.
D: Summary: A third reason appetite-based incontinence is worse: appetite involves cunning and deception, while anger is open and straightforward. The poets describe Aphrodite (desire) as 'guile-weaving,' whose seductions steal the wits even of the wise. Plotting makes a wrongdoer more criminal.
D: Distilled: Appetite is more culpable than anger because it operates through cunning and deception, while anger acts openly and without scheming.
Notes: Aphrodite is called 'guile-weaving daughter of Cyprus' (doloplokos Kuprogenes) in a tradition going back to Sappho (fragment 1). Cyprus was one of her chief cult centers. -- The Homeric quotation is from Iliad 14.214-217, describing the magic girdle (kestos himas) that Aphrodite lends to Hera to seduce Zeus. -- The contrast between anger's openness and appetite's cunning reflects a broader Greek cultural value: frank speech (parrhesia) was admired, while deception was despised. -- This argument adds a moral dimension beyond the psychological one in 7.23.

D: Original: (4) Further, no one commits wanton outrage with a feeling of pain, but every one who acts in anger acts with pain, while the man who commits outrage acts with pleasure. If, then, those acts at which it is most just to be angry are more criminal than others, the incontinence which is due to appetite is the more criminal; for there is no wanton outrage involved in anger.
D: Refashioned: (4) Furthermore, no one commits wanton outrage with a feeling of pain, but everyone who acts in anger acts with pain, while the person who commits outrage acts with pleasure. If, then, the acts that most justly provoke anger are the more criminal ones, the incontinence that stems from appetite is more criminal still -- for there is no wanton outrage involved in anger.
D: Elegant: Angry action involves pain; wanton outrage involves pleasure. Since outrageous acts done with pleasure are more criminal than those done in pain, incontinence from appetite (which involves pleasure) is worse than incontinence from anger (which involves pain).
D: Summary: A fourth argument: anger involves pain (at perceived injustice), while appetitive indulgence involves pleasure. Since acting with pleasure in wrongdoing (wanton outrage) is more criminal than acting from pain, appetite-driven incontinence is worse than anger-driven incontinence.
D: Distilled: Wrongdoing accompanied by pleasure is more criminal than wrongdoing accompanied by pain, making appetitive incontinence worse than angry incontinence.
Notes: The term 'wanton outrage' (hubris) is a key concept in Greek ethics and law. Hubris involves deliberately humiliating someone for one's own pleasure and was a serious legal offense in Athens. -- The argument that pleasure in wrongdoing increases its gravity connects to Aristotle's discussion of voluntary action in Book III and anticipates modern retributive theories that consider the perpetrator's mental state. -- This is the fourth and final argument in Aristotle's case that incontinence regarding appetite is worse than incontinence regarding anger.

D: Original: Plainly, then, the incontinence concerned with appetite is more disgraceful than that concerned with anger, and continence and incontinence are concerned with bodily appetites and pleasures; but we must grasp the differences among the latter themselves. For, as has been said at the beginning, some are human and natural both in kind and in magnitude, others are brutish, and others are due to organic injuries and diseases. Only with the first of these are temperance and self-indulgence concerned; this is why we call the lower animals neither temperate nor self-indulgent except by a metaphor, and only if some one race of animals exceeds another as a whole in wantonness, destructiveness, and omnivorous greed; these have no power of choice or calculation, but they are departures from the natural norm, as, among men, madmen are. Now brutishness is a less evil than vice, though more alarming; for it is not that the better part has been perverted, as in man,-they have no better part. Thus it is like comparing a lifeless thing with a living in respect of badness; for the badness of that which has no originative source of movement is always less hurtful, and reason is an originative source. Thus it is like comparing injustice in the abstract with an unjust man. Each is in some sense worse; for a bad man will do ten thousand times as much evil as a brute.
D: Refashioned:
It is plain, then, that incontinence regarding appetite is more disgraceful than that regarding anger, and that continence and incontinence in the proper sense concern bodily appetites and pleasures.

But we must also grasp the differences among bodily pleasures themselves. As stated at the beginning, some are human and natural in both kind and degree, others are brutish, and still others result from organic injuries and diseases. Only the first kind concerns temperance and self-indulgence. This is why we do not call the lower animals temperate or self-indulgent except metaphorically -- and only when one entire species exceeds another in wantonness, destructiveness, or indiscriminate greed. Animals lack the power of choice and rational calculation; they are departures from the natural norm, as madmen are among humans.

Brutishness is a lesser evil than vice, though more alarming. It is not that the higher part of the soul has been corrupted, as happens in human vice -- brutes have no higher part to corrupt. It is like comparing a lifeless thing with a living one in respect of harmfulness: the destructiveness of that which lacks an internal source of purposive movement is always less damaging, and reason is precisely such a source. It is like comparing injustice as an abstract concept with an actual unjust person. Each is worse in some respect, but a bad human being will do ten thousand times more evil than a brute.
D: Elegant:
Appetite-based incontinence is more disgraceful than anger-based. Among bodily pleasures themselves, only the naturally human kind concerns temperance proper -- animals lack choice and calculation, so they are called temperate or self-indulgent only metaphorically.

Brutishness, though more alarming than vice, is a lesser evil: brutes lack the higher rational faculty that vice corrupts. A lifeless thing's destructiveness is less harmful than a living thing's, because reason -- the source of purposive action -- can multiply evil enormously. An unjust person does ten thousand times more harm than a brute.
D: Summary: Appetite-based incontinence is conclusively worse than anger-based. Among bodily pleasures, only naturally human ones concern temperance. Brutishness, though frightening, is a lesser evil than human vice because brutes lack the rational faculty whose corruption makes vice so destructive. A wicked human causes vastly more harm than any beast.
D: Distilled: Brutishness is more alarming but less evil than human vice, because reason -- the very faculty that vice corrupts -- is what gives human wickedness its extraordinary destructive power.
Notes: The claim that animals lack choice (prohairesis) and calculation (logismos) is central to Aristotle's moral psychology and his broader philosophical anthropology in De Anima and Politics I. -- The comparison of injustice-in-the-abstract with an unjust person echoes Plato's Republic (Book I) and Aristotle's own discussion in the Categories and Metaphysics of whether abstractions or their instances are more real. -- The 'ten thousand times' figure is rhetorical but makes a genuine philosophical point: rational agency amplifies both good and evil. -- This passage completes chapters 4-6's discussion and transitions to the detailed analysis of continence and incontinence in chapter 7.

D: Original: With regard to the pleasures and pains and appetites and aversions arising through touch and taste, to which both self-indulgence and temperance were formerly narrowed down, it possible to be in such a state as to be defeated even by those of them which most people master, or to master even those by which most people are defeated; among these possibilities, those relating to pleasures are incontinence and continence, those relating to pains softness and endurance. The state of most people is intermediate, even if they lean more towards the worse states.
D: Refashioned:
Regarding the pleasures, pains, appetites, and aversions arising through touch and taste -- to which both self-indulgence and temperance were earlier narrowed down -- it is possible to be in a state where one is defeated even by those that most people master, or where one masters even those by which most people are defeated. Among these possibilities, those relating to pleasures are incontinence and continence; those relating to pains are softness and endurance.

The condition of most people falls in between, even if they tend to lean toward the worse states.
D: Elegant: Within the pleasures and pains of touch and taste, one can either resist what most people resist, or be defeated by what most resist, or resist what defeats most. Regarding pleasures, these states are continence and incontinence; regarding pains, endurance and softness. Most people fall in between, leaning toward the worse side.
D: Summary: Aristotle maps out the dispositions regarding bodily pleasure and pain. Continence and incontinence concern whether one masters or is defeated by pleasures; endurance and softness concern the same for pains. Most people occupy the middle ground, tilting slightly toward the worse states.
D: Distilled: Continence and incontinence measure one's resistance to pleasure, while endurance and softness measure one's resistance to pain, with most people falling in the intermediate range.
Notes: The restriction to touch and taste goes back to Book III.10, where Aristotle argues that temperance concerns only these two senses, not sight, hearing, or smell. -- The four-fold schema (continence/incontinence for pleasure, endurance/softness for pain) provides a systematic framework that parallels the temperance/self-indulgence pair. -- The observation that most people lean toward worse states reflects Aristotle's realism about human nature, echoing his general view that virtue is difficult and vice is easy (Book II.9).

D: Original: Now, since some pleasures are necessary while others are not, and are necessary up to a point while the excesses of them are not, nor the deficiencies, and this is equally true of appetites and pains, the man who pursues the excesses of things pleasant, or pursues to excess necessary objects, and does so by choice, for their own sake and not at all for the sake of any result distinct from them, is self-indulgent; for such a man is of necessity unlikely to repent, and therefore incurable, since a man who cannot repent cannot be cured. The man who is deficient in his pursuit of them is the opposite of self-indulgent; the man who is intermediate is temperate. Similarly, there is the man who avoids bodily pains not because he is defeated by them but by choice. (Of those who do not choose such acts, one kind of man is led to them as a result of the pleasure involved, another because he avoids the pain arising from the appetite, so that these types differ from one another. Now any one would think worse of a man with no appetite or with weak appetite were he to do something disgraceful, than if he did it under the influence of powerful appetite, and worse of him if he struck a blow not in anger than if he did it in anger; for what would he have done if he had been strongly affected? This is why the self-indulgent man is worse than the incontinent.) of the states named, then, the latter is rather a kind of softness; the former is self-indulgence. While to the incontinent man is opposed the continent, to the soft is opposed the man of endurance; for endurance consists in resisting, while continence consists in conquering, and resisting and conquering are different, as not being beaten is different from winning; this is why continence is also more worthy of choice than endurance. Now the man who is defective in respect of resistance to the things which most men both resist and resist successfully is soft and effeminate; for effeminacy too is a kind of softness; such a man trails his cloak to avoid the pain of lifting it, and plays the invalid without thinking himself wretched, though the man he imitates is a wretched man.
D: Refashioned:
Since some pleasures are necessary (up to a point, though their excesses and deficiencies are not), while others are not necessary, and the same holds for appetites and pains: the person who pursues excesses of pleasant things, or pursues necessary objects to excess, doing so by deliberate choice, for their own sake and not for the sake of any further result, is self-indulgent. Such a person is necessarily unlikely to feel regret, and is therefore incurable, since someone who cannot regret cannot be cured. The person deficient in pursuit of pleasure is the opposite of the self-indulgent; the intermediate person is temperate.

Similarly, there is the person who avoids bodily pains not because he is overpowered by them but by choice. Among those who do not choose their actions, one type is led to act by the pleasure involved, another because he is avoiding the pain of unfulfilled appetite -- so these types differ from one another. Everyone would judge more harshly a person who does something disgraceful with little or no appetite urging him on than one who acts under the influence of powerful appetite, and more harshly one who strikes without anger than one who does so in anger. For what would such a person do if he were strongly affected? This is why the self-indulgent person is worse than the incontinent.

Of the states described, then, the latter -- avoiding pain by choice -- is rather a kind of softness, while the former is self-indulgence proper. The incontinent person is opposed to the continent, and the soft to the person of endurance. Endurance consists in resisting, while continence consists in conquering, and resisting and conquering are different, just as not being beaten is different from winning. This is why continence is more worthy of choice than endurance.

The person who is defective in resisting things that most people both resist and resist successfully is soft and effeminate -- for effeminacy is a kind of softness. Such a person trails his cloak to avoid the pain of lifting it and plays the invalid without considering himself wretched, even though the person he imitates truly is wretched.
D: Elegant:
The self-indulgent person pursues excessive pleasure by choice and without regret -- making him incurable. The temperate person is the mean; the person deficient in pleasure-pursuit is the opposite extreme.

Self-indulgence is worse than incontinence because acting disgracefully without strong appetite reveals deeper corruption. Softness is the pain-side counterpart: choosing to avoid any bodily discomfort.

Endurance means resisting pain; continence means conquering pleasure. Resisting and conquering differ as 'not being beaten' differs from 'winning' -- so continence is more choiceworthy than endurance. The soft person fails to resist what most people handle easily, trailing his cloak to avoid the effort of lifting it.
D: Summary: Aristotle distinguishes self-indulgence (choosing excess pleasure without regret, therefore incurable) from incontinence (pursuing excess against one's judgment, therefore curable). He pairs softness (yielding to pain by choice) with self-indulgence, and endurance with continence. Continence is superior to endurance because conquering is greater than merely resisting.
D: Distilled: Self-indulgence is incurable because it involves deliberate, unregretted choice of excess; continence surpasses mere endurance as winning surpasses not losing.
Notes: The claim that the self-indulgent person is incurable because he cannot repent is one of Aristotle's strongest moral psychological claims, connecting moral improvement to the capacity for regret. -- The distinction between 'resisting' (anterchesthai) and 'conquering' (nikān) -- endurance versus continence -- is characteristically Aristotelian in its precision. -- The image of trailing one's cloak to avoid the effort of lifting it is a vivid picture of effeminacy (malakia) in the ancient Greek cultural context, where physical toughness was highly valued. -- This passage resolves parts of puzzles (2) and (3) from chapter 2 about the relationships among temperance, continence, self-indulgence, and incontinence.

D: Original: The case is similar with regard to continence and incontinence. For if a man is defeated by violent and excessive pleasures or pains, there is nothing wonderful in that; indeed we are ready to pardon him if he has resisted, as Theodectes' Philoctetes does when bitten by the snake, or Carcinus' Cercyon in the Alope, and as people who try to restrain their laughter burst out into a guffaw, as happened to Xenophantus. But it is surprising if a man is defeated by and cannot resist pleasures or pains which most men can hold out against, when this is not due to heredity or disease, like the softness that is hereditary with the kings of the Scythians, or that which distinguishes the female sex from the male.
D: Refashioned:
The situation is similar regarding continence and incontinence. If someone is defeated by violent and excessive pleasures or pains, this is not surprising. Indeed, we are ready to pardon such a person if he has put up a fight -- as Philoctetes does when bitten by the serpent in Theodectes' play, or Cercyon in Carcinus' Alope, and as people who try to suppress their laughter burst out in a guffaw, as happened to Xenophantus.

But it is surprising and blameworthy if someone is defeated by and cannot resist pleasures or pains that most people can withstand -- unless the cause is heredity or disease, like the hereditary softness of the Scythian kings, or the natural difference in resilience between women and men.
D: Elegant: Being overcome by overwhelming pleasure or pain is pardonable -- we excuse those who resist but are overpowered, as Philoctetes was by the serpent's bite, or as people who burst out laughing despite trying not to. But being defeated by what most people easily withstand is blameworthy, unless heredity or disease explains it.
D: Summary: We pardon those overwhelmed by extreme pleasures or pains, especially when they have resisted. But someone who cannot withstand ordinary pleasures or pains that most people manage is rightly blamed -- unless the cause is hereditary or pathological, like the reported softness of the Scythian kings.
D: Distilled: We pardon those overcome by extraordinary pressures but blame those who succumb to what most people easily resist, unless heredity or disease explains their weakness.
Notes: Theodectes of Phaselis (c. 375-334 BCE) was a rhetorician and tragedian, a student of both Isocrates and Aristotle. His Philoctetes depicted the hero's agony from the wound inflicted by a sacred serpent on the island of Lemnos. -- Carcinus was a 4th-century BCE Athenian tragedian; his Alope dramatized the myth of Cercyon (king of Eleusis) and his daughter Alope. -- Xenophantus is otherwise unknown; the anecdote about suppressed laughter serves as a homely illustration of being overcome by irresistible impulse. -- The Scythians were nomadic peoples of the Eurasian steppe; Greek sources often commented on their customs. Herodotus (Histories 1.105, 4.67) mentions the 'female disease' among Scythian kings. -- The remark about female softness reflects conventional Greek gender assumptions.

D: Original:
The lover of amusement, too, is thought to be self-indulgent, but is really soft. For amusement is a relaxation, since it is a rest from work; and the lover of amusement is one of the people who go to excess in this.

Of incontinence one kind is impetuosity, another weakness. For some men after deliberating fail, owing to their emotion, to stand by the conclusions of their deliberation, others because they have not deliberated are led by their emotion; since some men (just as people who first tickle others are not tickled themselves), if they have first perceived and seen what is coming and have first roused themselves and their calculative faculty, are not defeated by their emotion, whether it be pleasant or painful. It is keen and excitable people that suffer especially from the impetuous form of incontinence; for the former by reason of their quickness and the latter by reason of the violence of their passions do not await the argument, because they are apt to follow their imagination.
D: Refashioned:
The lover of amusement is also thought to be self-indulgent, but is really soft. For amusement is a form of relaxation -- a rest from work -- and the lover of amusement is simply someone who goes to excess in this.

Incontinence takes two forms: impetuosity and weakness. Some people deliberate but then fail, because of emotion, to stand by the conclusions of their deliberation. Others are led by emotion precisely because they have not deliberated at all. Some people -- just as those who tickle others first are not themselves tickled -- if they have first perceived and anticipated what is coming and have first roused themselves and their capacity for reasoning, are not defeated by their emotion, whether pleasant or painful. It is keen and excitable people who suffer especially from the impetuous form of incontinence. The keen act too quickly and the excitable are too passionate to wait for the argument; both are apt to follow their imagination instead.
D: Elegant:
The amusement-lover is soft, not self-indulgent -- amusement is rest taken to excess.

Incontinence has two forms: impetuosity (acting before deliberating) and weakness (deliberating but failing to follow through). Those who anticipate temptation and prepare their reasoning in advance can resist emotion. Keen and excitable people are most prone to impetuous incontinence -- the keen because of their quickness, the excitable because of the violence of their passions.
D: Summary: The lover of amusement is soft rather than self-indulgent. Incontinence takes two forms: impetuosity (acting on emotion before deliberating) and weakness (deliberating correctly but failing to follow through). Keen and excitable people are most prone to the impetuous variety because they act on imagination before reason can intervene.
D: Distilled: Incontinence divides into impetuosity, where emotion acts before reason engages, and weakness, where emotion overrides reason's conclusions after deliberation.
Notes: The distinction between impetuous (propeteia) and weak (astheneia) incontinence is one of Aristotle's most important contributions to the taxonomy of moral failure. -- The tickling analogy illustrates the advantage of anticipation: just as one cannot tickle oneself because the sensation is anticipated, anticipated temptation is easier to resist. -- This connects to Aristotle's general emphasis on habituation and preparation in moral development (Book II). -- The observation that amusement-lovers are soft rather than self-indulgent refines the categories established in 7.28-7.29.

D: Original: The self-indulgent man, as was said, is not apt to repent; for he stands by his choice; but incontinent man is likely to repent. This is why the position is not as it was expressed in the formulation of the problem, but the selfindulgent man is incurable and the incontinent man curable; for wickedness is like a disease such as dropsy or consumption, while incontinence is like epilepsy; the former is a permanent, the latter an intermittent badness. And generally incontinence and vice are different in kind; vice is unconscious of itself, incontinence is not (of incontinent men themselves, those who become temporarily beside themselves are better than those who have the rational principle but do not abide by it, since the latter are defeated by a weaker passion, and do not act without previous deliberation like the others); for the incontinent man is like the people who get drunk quickly and on little wine, i.e. on less than most people.
D: Refashioned:
The self-indulgent person, as noted, is not inclined to feel regret, since he stands by his deliberate choice. But the incontinent person is likely to feel regret. This is why the situation is not as the puzzle suggested: in fact, the self-indulgent person is incurable while the incontinent person is curable. For vice is like a chronic disease such as dropsy or tuberculosis, while incontinence is like epilepsy -- the former is a permanent badness, the latter an intermittent one.

More broadly, incontinence and vice differ in kind. Vice is unconscious of itself; incontinence is not. Among incontinent people themselves, those who become temporarily beside themselves are better than those who possess the rational principle but fail to abide by it, since the latter are defeated by a weaker passion and do not act without prior deliberation as the impetuous do. The incontinent person of the weak type is like people who get drunk quickly and on little wine -- that is, on less than it takes for most people.
D: Elegant:
The self-indulgent person feels no regret and so is incurable; the incontinent person feels regret and so can be cured. Vice is like chronic disease (permanent); incontinence is like epilepsy (intermittent). Vice does not recognize itself; incontinence does.

Among the incontinent, the impetuous are better than the weak: the impetuous act without deliberation, while the weak deliberate correctly but are defeated by even mild passion -- like people who get drunk on little wine.
D: Summary: Self-indulgence is incurable because it involves unregretted choice, while incontinence is curable because the incontinent person retains awareness that his actions are wrong. Vice and incontinence differ in kind: vice is permanent and self-unaware, incontinence is intermittent and self-conscious. Among the incontinent, the impetuous are better than the weak.
D: Distilled: Vice is incurable because it is unaware of itself; incontinence is curable because the incontinent person knows he is acting wrongly and can feel regret.
Notes: This passage resolves puzzle (5) from 7.9, which seemed to suggest the self-indulgent person was more curable. Aristotle reverses this: the incontinent person is more curable precisely because his principles remain intact. -- The disease metaphors (dropsy/consumption vs. epilepsy) were drawn from Hippocratic medicine: dropsy (edema) and consumption (phthisis/tuberculosis) were considered chronic and progressive, while epilepsy was episodic. -- The comparison of weak incontinence to getting drunk on little wine is a characteristically vivid Aristotelian analogy. -- The claim that vice is 'unconscious of itself' anticipates modern discussions of self-deception in moral philosophy.

D: Original: Evidently, then, incontinence is not vice (though perhaps it is so in a qualified sense); for incontinence is contrary to choice while vice is in accordance with choice; not but what they are similar in respect of the actions they lead to; as in the saying of Demodocus about the Milesians, 'the Milesians are not without sense, but they do the things that senseless people do', so too incontinent people are not criminal, but they will do criminal acts.
D: Refashioned: Evidently, then, incontinence is not vice -- though perhaps it is so in a qualified sense. For incontinence is contrary to one's deliberate choice, while vice is in accordance with it. Nevertheless, they are similar in the actions they produce. As Demodocus said about the Milesians: 'The Milesians are not without sense, but they do the things that senseless people do.' So too, incontinent people are not criminal, but they will do criminal acts.
D: Elegant: Incontinence is not vice, since incontinence opposes one's own choice while vice accords with it. Yet they produce similar actions. As Demodocus said of the Milesians: 'They are not without sense, but they do what senseless people do.' Incontinent people are not criminals, but they commit criminal acts.
D: Summary: Incontinence differs from vice in kind: vice acts in accordance with one's corrupted choice, while incontinence acts against one's correct choice. However, both produce the same bad actions, just as the Milesians were not senseless but did senseless things.
D: Distilled: Incontinence is not vice because it opposes one's own choice rather than expressing it, even though the resulting actions may look identical.
Notes: Demodocus was a poet (possibly 6th or 5th century BCE) known for satirical epigrams about various Greek cities. Miletus was a prosperous Ionian city on the coast of Asia Minor, and the butt of this particular joke. -- The distinction between incontinence and vice based on the role of choice (prohairesis) is fundamental to Aristotle's moral psychology and resolves a core puzzle of Book VII. -- This passage has been influential in philosophy of action: it shows that the moral character of an act depends not just on what is done but on the agent's relationship to the act.

D: Original: Now, since the incontinent man is apt to pursue, not on conviction, bodily pleasures that are excessive and contrary to the right rule, while the self-indulgent man is convinced because he is the sort of man to pursue them, it is on the contrary the former that is easily persuaded to change his mind, while the latter is not. For virtue and vice respectively preserve and destroy the first principle, and in actions the final cause is the first principle, as the hypotheses are in mathematics; neither in that case is it argument that teaches the first principles, nor is it so here-virtue either natural or produced by habituation is what teaches right opinion about the first principle. Such a man as this, then, is temperate; his contrary is the self-indulgent.
D: Refashioned:
Since the incontinent person is inclined to pursue bodily pleasures that are excessive and contrary to right reason -- but not from settled conviction -- while the self-indulgent person is convinced he should pursue them because that is the kind of person he is, it is actually the former who is more easily persuaded to change his mind, not the latter.

For virtue and vice respectively preserve and destroy the first principle of action. In action, the final cause serves as the first principle, just as hypotheses serve as first principles in mathematics. Neither in mathematics nor in ethics is it argument that teaches the first principles; rather, it is virtue -- whether natural or developed through habituation -- that teaches right opinion about the first principle. The person who has this right opinion about the end is temperate; the person whose first principles are corrupted is self-indulgent.
D: Elegant: The incontinent person pursues excess without conviction and so can be persuaded to change; the self-indulgent person acts from conviction and cannot. Virtue preserves the first principle of action (the correct end to pursue), while vice destroys it. Just as mathematical reasoning cannot establish its own axioms, ethical reasoning cannot establish its own starting points -- virtue, natural or habitual, is what teaches correct judgment about the ultimate end.
D: Summary: The incontinent person can be reformed because his fundamental moral principles are intact; the self-indulgent person cannot because vice has corrupted his first principles about what is good. Virtue teaches correct judgment about the ultimate end of action, just as mathematical axioms must be accepted before reasoning can proceed.
D: Distilled: The first principles of moral action -- our fundamental sense of what is good -- are preserved by virtue and destroyed by vice, and no argument alone can restore what vice has corrupted.
Notes: The analogy between ethical first principles and mathematical hypotheses (axioms) is one of Aristotle's most important methodological claims, connecting his Ethics to his logical works (Posterior Analytics I.2-3). -- The idea that virtue 'teaches' right opinion about the first principle means that moral perception -- seeing what is truly good -- depends on having a well-formed character, not on theoretical reasoning alone. -- This connects to Book VI.12-13's argument that practical wisdom requires moral virtue. -- The passage has been influential in virtue epistemology, which holds that intellectual virtues are necessary for proper knowledge.

D: Original: But there is a sort of man who is carried away as a result of passion and contrary to the right rule-a man whom passion masters so that he does not act according to the right rule, but does not master to the extent of making him ready to believe that he ought to pursue such pleasures without reserve; this is the incontinent man, who is better than the self-indulgent man, and not bad without qualification; for the best thing in him, the first principle, is preserved. And contrary to him is another kind of man, he who abides by his convictions and is not carried away, at least as a result of passion. It is evident from these considerations that the latter is a good state and the former a bad one.
D: Refashioned:
But there is a kind of person who is carried away by passion contrary to right reason -- someone whom passion masters so that he does not act according to right reason, yet does not master to the extent of making him believe that he ought to pursue such pleasures without restraint. This is the incontinent person, who is better than the self-indulgent and not bad without qualification, because the best thing in him -- his first principle, his sense of what is truly good -- is preserved.

Opposite to him stands another kind of person: one who abides by his convictions and is not swept away by passion. It is clear from these considerations that the latter state is good and the former bad.
D: Elegant: The incontinent person is mastered by passion in action but not in conviction -- he never comes to believe that unbounded pleasure-seeking is right. His first principle remains intact, making him better than the self-indulgent and not wholly bad. His opposite, the continent person who holds firm against passion, is in a genuinely good state.
D: Summary: The incontinent person is not wholly bad because passion overrides his actions but not his fundamental beliefs about what is good. The continent person, who resists passion and holds to his convictions, is in a genuinely good state. What distinguishes them is whether the first principle of moral judgment survives intact.
D: Distilled: The incontinent person retains correct moral principles even while acting against them, making him better than the self-indulgent person whose principles themselves are corrupted.
Notes: The preservation of the 'first principle' (archē) in the incontinent person is the key to Aristotle's claim that incontinence is better than vice. -- This resolves puzzle (5) from 7.9 definitively: despite appearances, the incontinent person is more curable and less bad than the self-indulgent person. -- The passage establishes a clear moral hierarchy: temperance (best) > continence > incontinence > self-indulgence (worst). -- This analysis influenced Thomas Aquinas's treatment of weakness of will in the Summa Theologiae (I-II, q.77).

D: Original: Is the man continent who abides by any and every rule and any and every choice, or the man who abides by the right choice, and is he incontinent who abandons any and every choice and any and every rule, or he who abandons the rule that is not false and the choice that is right; this is how we put it before in our statement of the problem. Or is it incidentally any and every choice but per se the true rule and the right choice by which the one abides and the other does not? If any one chooses or pursues this for the sake of that, per se he pursues and chooses the latter, but incidentally the former. But when we speak without qualification we mean what is per se. Therefore in a sense the one abides by, and the other abandons, any and every opinion; but without qualification, the true opinion.
D: Refashioned:
Is the continent person one who abides by any and every principle and choice, or specifically one who abides by the right choice? And is the incontinent person one who abandons any and every choice and principle, or specifically one who abandons the principle that is correct and the choice that is right? This was how we framed the puzzle earlier.

Or is it that incidentally the continent person abides by any and every opinion, but essentially he abides by the true principle and the right choice? When someone chooses or pursues one thing for the sake of another, what he essentially pursues and chooses is the latter, while the former is only incidental. And when we speak without qualification, we mean what is essential.

Therefore, in one sense the continent person abides by, and the incontinent person abandons, any and every opinion. But speaking without qualification, it is the true opinion that matters.
D: Elegant: The continent person essentially abides by the right choice and true principle; the incontinent person essentially abandons them. When we call someone continent or incontinent without qualification, we mean their relationship to the correct rule, not to just any opinion they happen to hold.
D: Summary: Aristotle resolves the puzzle about whether continence means standing by any belief or specifically the right one. Properly speaking, continence is about adhering to the true and right principle; incontinence is about abandoning it. Any other application is incidental.
D: Distilled: Continence in the proper sense means abiding by the correct principle and right choice, not stubbornly clinging to any opinion whatsoever.
Notes: This resolves puzzle (3) from 7.7 about the possibility of good incontinence and bad continence. -- The distinction between 'per se' (kath' hauto) and 'incidentally' (kata symbebēkos) is one of Aristotle's fundamental logical and metaphysical distinctions, appearing throughout the Organon, Physics, and Metaphysics. -- The resolution shows that while incidentally a continent person might cling to any belief, essentially (and in the unqualified sense) continence pertains only to right beliefs. -- This also resolves the Neoptolemus puzzle from 7.7: his abandonment of a false belief was not true incontinence.

D: Original: There are some who are apt to abide by their opinion, who are called strong-headed, viz. those who are hard to persuade in the first instance and are not easily persuaded to change; these have in them something like the continent man, as the prodigal is in a way like the liberal man and the rash man like the confident man; but they are different in many respects. For it is to passion and appetite that the one will not yield, since on occasion the continent man will be easy to persuade; but it is to argument that the others refuse to yield, for they do form appetites and many of them are led by their pleasures. Now the people who are strong-headed are the opinionated, the ignorant, and the boorish-the opinionated being influenced by pleasure and pain; for they delight in the victory they gain if they are not persuaded to change, and are pained if their decisions become null and void as decrees sometimes do; so that they are liker the incontinent than the continent man.
D: Refashioned:
There are people who tend to stick by their opinions and are called strong-headed or obstinate -- those who are hard to persuade in the first place and not easily moved to change. These people resemble the continent person in some ways, just as the prodigal resembles the generous person and the reckless person resembles the courageous one. But they differ in many respects.

The continent person refuses to yield to passion and appetite, and on other occasions will be easy enough to persuade by argument. The obstinate person, by contrast, refuses to yield to argument, while readily forming appetites and often being led by pleasures. The types of strong-headed people are the opinionated, the ignorant, and the boorish. The opinionated are motivated by pleasure and pain: they delight in the sense of victory when they are not persuaded to change, and feel pain when their positions are overturned -- like decrees that are nullified. In this way, they are actually more like the incontinent than the continent person.
D: Elegant: The obstinate resemble the continent in clinging to their positions, but the resemblance is superficial. The continent person resists passion and appetite, remaining open to argument. The obstinate resist argument itself, driven by pleasure in being right and pain at being overruled. The opinionated, the ignorant, and the boorish are the main types of obstinate people -- and they are closer to the incontinent than the continent, since they are ultimately governed by pleasure and pain.
D: Summary: Strong-headed or obstinate people resemble the continent in standing firm, but differ fundamentally: the continent person resists passion while remaining open to argument, whereas the obstinate resist argument itself, driven by the pleasure of seeming right and the pain of being refuted. This makes them closer to the incontinent than to the continent.
D: Distilled: Obstinacy resembles continence superficially, but the obstinate person clings to opinions from pleasure and pain rather than reason, making him more like the incontinent person than the continent one.
Notes: The comparison of the obstinate (ischyrognomos) to the continent parallels Aristotle's broader method of distinguishing genuine virtues from their look-alikes (e.g., true courage vs. recklessness in Book III). -- The analogy to decrees being overturned may refer to the Athenian practice of annulling laws (graphē paranomōn) -- a real source of political frustration. -- This analysis enriches Aristotle's account by showing that the mere behavior of standing firm does not constitute continence; the motivation matters.

D: Original: But there are some who fail to abide by their resolutions, not as a result of incontinence, e.g. Neoptolemus in Sophocles' Philoctetes; yet it was for the sake of pleasure that he did not stand fast-but a noble pleasure; for telling the truth was noble to him, but he had been persuaded by Odysseus to tell the lie. For not every one who does anything for the sake of pleasure is either self-indulgent or bad or incontinent, but he who does it for a disgraceful pleasure.
D: Refashioned:
There are some who fail to abide by their resolutions but not as a result of incontinence -- for example, Neoptolemus in Sophocles' Philoctetes. He did not stand fast, and his abandonment was indeed for the sake of pleasure -- but a noble pleasure, for telling the truth was noble to him, and he had been persuaded by Odysseus to tell a lie.

Not everyone who does something for the sake of pleasure is self-indulgent or bad or incontinent. Only the person who does it for a disgraceful pleasure deserves those labels.
D: Elegant: Neoptolemus in Sophocles' Philoctetes abandoned his resolution not from incontinence but for a noble pleasure -- the pleasure of truthfulness over the lie Odysseus had urged. Not all action for pleasure's sake is blameworthy; only action for disgraceful pleasure.
D: Summary: Abandoning a resolution is not always incontinence. Neoptolemus abandoned Odysseus' plan of deception because truth-telling was noble and pleasant to him. Acting for the sake of pleasure is only blameworthy when the pleasure is disgraceful.
D: Distilled: Abandoning a conviction for the sake of a noble pleasure, as Neoptolemus abandoned a lie for the pleasure of truthfulness, is not incontinence but moral integrity.
Notes: This resolves puzzle (3) from 7.7, which used Neoptolemus as an apparent case of praiseworthy incontinence. Aristotle's solution: it was not incontinence at all, because the pleasure motivating his change was noble (truth) rather than base. -- Neoptolemus, son of Achilles, Odysseus, and Philoctetes are characters in Sophocles' Philoctetes (409 BCE). Odysseus engineers a scheme to steal Heracles' bow from the marooned Philoctetes, but Neoptolemus's natural honesty prevails. -- The distinction between noble and disgraceful pleasure is important for Aristotle's broader account of pleasure in chapters 11-14.

D: Original: Since there is also a sort of man who takes less delight than he should in bodily things, and does not abide by the rule, he who is intermediate between him and the incontinent man is the continent man; for the incontinent man fails to abide by the rule because he delights too much in them, and this man because he delights in them too little; while the continent man abides by the rule and does not change on either account. Now if continence is good, both the contrary states must be bad, as they actually appear to be; but because the other extreme is seen in few people and seldom, as temperance is thought to be contrary only to self-indulgence, so is continence to incontinence.
D: Refashioned:
Since there is also a type of person who takes less delight than he should in bodily things and does not abide by the right principle for that reason, the continent person stands as the intermediate between this person and the incontinent. The incontinent person fails to abide by the principle because he delights too much in bodily pleasures; this other person fails because he delights in them too little. The continent person abides by the principle and does not deviate on either account.

Now if continence is good, both contrary states must be bad, as indeed they appear to be. But because the opposite extreme -- deficiency in bodily pleasure -- is seen in very few people and rarely, continence seems to be opposed only to incontinence, just as temperance seems opposed only to self-indulgence.
D: Elegant: The continent person is the mean between two failures: the incontinent who delights too much in bodily pleasure, and the person who delights too little. Both contraries are bad, but because the deficient extreme is so rare, continence appears to have only one opposite -- incontinence -- just as temperance seems opposed only to self-indulgence.
D: Summary: Continence is the intermediate state between incontinence (too much delight in bodily pleasure) and an unnamed deficiency (too little delight). Both extremes are bad, but the deficient extreme is so rare that continence appears to have only one opposite.
D: Distilled: Continence is a mean between excessive and deficient delight in bodily pleasure, though the deficient extreme is so rare that it is usually overlooked.
Notes: This applies the doctrine of the mean (Book II.6-8) to continence, though continence is itself not a full virtue but an intermediate condition between virtue and vice. -- The 'nameless' deficiency in bodily pleasure parallels similar unnamed extremes in Aristotle's virtue theory (e.g., the person deficient in anger, discussed in Book IV.5). -- The observation that one extreme is rare while the other is common reflects Aristotle's empirical approach to ethics and his awareness that the practical emphasis should be on the more common failing.

D: Original: Since many names are applied analogically, it is by analogy that we have come to speak of the 'continence' the temperate man; for both the continent man and the temperate man are such as to do nothing contrary to the rule for the sake of the bodily pleasures, but the former has and the latter has not bad appetites, and the latter is such as not to feel pleasure contrary to the rule, while the former is such as to feel pleasure but not to be led by it. And the incontinent and the self-indulgent man are also like another; they are different, but both pursue bodily pleasures- the latter, however, also thinking that he ought to do so, while the former does not think this.
D: Refashioned:
Since many terms are applied by analogy, we have come to speak of the 'continence' of the temperate person by analogy as well. For both the continent and the temperate person are such as to do nothing contrary to the right principle for the sake of bodily pleasures, but there is a crucial difference: the continent person has bad appetites while the temperate person does not. The temperate person is such as not to feel pleasure contrary to the right principle; the continent person feels such pleasure but is not led by it.

Similarly, the incontinent and the self-indulgent resemble each other -- they are different, but both pursue bodily pleasures. The difference is that the self-indulgent person also thinks he ought to pursue them, while the incontinent person does not think this.
D: Elegant: We call the temperate person 'continent' by analogy, since both resist bodily pleasure -- but the temperate person has no bad appetites to resist, while the continent person has them and overcomes them. Similarly, both the incontinent and self-indulgent pursue bodily pleasure, but the self-indulgent person believes he should, while the incontinent person knows he should not.
D: Summary: The temperate and the continent both avoid excess bodily pleasure, but the temperate person does so without internal conflict (having no bad desires), while the continent person succeeds despite having them. The self-indulgent and the incontinent both pursue excess, but the self-indulgent does so from conviction while the incontinent does so against his conviction.
D: Distilled: Temperance and continence differ as effortless goodness differs from hard-won resistance; self-indulgence and incontinence differ as principled pursuit of pleasure differs from conflicted pursuit.
Notes: This passage provides the clearest summary of the four-way distinction among temperance, continence, incontinence, and self-indulgence -- one of the most important classifications in Aristotle's moral psychology. -- The idea that temperance involves the absence of bad desires while continence involves overcoming them has generated extensive debate about whether Aristotle's ideal is closer to Kantian 'acting from duty against inclination' or to the effortless virtue emphasized by virtue ethicists. -- The analogical use of terms reflects Aristotle's broader theory of homonymy (Categories 1) and focal meaning (Metaphysics IV.2).

D: Original: Nor can the same man have practical wisdom and be incontinent; for it has been shown' that a man is at the same time practically wise, and good in respect of character. Further, a man has practical wisdom not by knowing only but by being able to act; but the incontinent man is unable to act-there is, however, nothing to prevent a clever man from being incontinent; this is why it is sometimes actually thought that some people have practical wisdom but are incontinent, viz. because cleverness and practical wisdom differ in the way we have described in our first discussions, and are near together in respect of their reasoning, but differ in respect of their purpose-nor yet is the incontinent man like the man who knows and is contemplating a truth, but like the man who is asleep or drunk. And he acts willingly (for he acts in a sense with knowledge both of what he does and of the end to which he does it), but is not wicked, since his purpose is good; so that he is half-wicked. And he is not a criminal; for he does not act of malice aforethought; of the two types of incontinent man the one does not abide by the conclusions of his deliberation, while the excitable man does not deliberate at all. And thus the incontinent man like a city which passes all the right decrees and has good laws, but makes no use of them, as in Anaxandrides' jesting remark,
D: Refashioned:
The same person cannot have practical wisdom and be incontinent. For it has been demonstrated that a person is practically wise only if he is also good in character. Furthermore, a person has practical wisdom not merely by knowing what is right but by being able to act on it -- and the incontinent person is precisely someone unable to act on what he knows.

However, nothing prevents a clever person from being incontinent. This is why people sometimes think that practically wise individuals are incontinent: because cleverness and practical wisdom differ in the way we described in our earlier discussions -- they are close in their reasoning but differ in their purpose.

Nor is the incontinent person like someone who knows and is actively contemplating a truth. Rather, he is like someone who is asleep or drunk. He acts willingly -- for he acts in a sense with knowledge of what he is doing and what end he is pursuing -- but he is not wicked, since his fundamental purpose is good. So he is, as it were, half-wicked. And he is not a criminal, for he does not act from malice aforethought. Of the two types of incontinent person, the weak type fails to abide by the conclusions of his deliberation, while the impetuous type does not deliberate at all.

The incontinent person is thus like a city that passes all the right decrees and has good laws but makes no use of them, as Anaxandrides jested:
D: Elegant:
Practical wisdom and incontinence are incompatible: practical wisdom requires both knowing the good and acting on it, while the incontinent person cannot act on what he knows. Cleverness, however, is compatible with incontinence -- which explains why people confuse the two.

The incontinent person acts willingly and with a kind of knowledge, but his purpose remains good, making him 'half-wicked' -- not criminal, since he acts without malice. He resembles a city with excellent laws that are never enforced.
D: Summary: Practical wisdom excludes incontinence because it requires not just knowing but acting rightly. Cleverness, which shares reasoning ability but not moral purpose, can coexist with incontinence. The incontinent person is 'half-wicked' -- his purpose is good but his actions are bad, like a city with good laws it never enforces.
D: Distilled: The incontinent person is like a city with good laws but no enforcement -- his principles are sound but his will to act on them fails.
Notes: The distinction between cleverness (deinotes) and practical wisdom (phronesis) was established in Book VI.12: cleverness is the ability to find means to any end, while practical wisdom directs this ability toward good ends. -- Anaxandrides was a 4th-century BCE Athenian comic playwright. The quotation continues in the next entry (7.42). -- The image of the city with unenforced laws is one of Aristotle's most memorable political analogies and reflects his constant interplay between ethical and political analysis. -- The 'half-wicked' characterization is uniquely Aristotelian and captures the paradox of a person whose character is divided against itself. -- This resolves puzzle (4) from 7.4 about whether the practically wise person can be incontinent.

D: Original:
The city willed it, that cares nought for laws; but the wicked man is like a city that uses its laws, but has wicked laws to use.

Now incontinence and continence are concerned with that which is in excess of the state characteristic of most men; for the continent man abides by his resolutions more and the incontinent man less than most men can.
D: Refashioned:
'The city willed it -- that city which cares nothing for its laws.'

But the wicked person is like a city that does enforce its laws, except that the laws it enforces are wicked ones.

Now incontinence and continence concern what exceeds the condition characteristic of most people. The continent person abides by his resolutions more firmly than most people can manage, while the incontinent person does so less firmly than most.
D: Elegant: The wicked person is like a city that enforces wicked laws -- the system works, but toward bad ends. Continence and incontinence mark the extremes beyond ordinary human capacity: the continent person holds firm more than most can, the incontinent person less.
D: Summary: Completing the city analogy: the incontinent person has good laws (principles) but does not enforce them, while the wicked person enforces bad laws (corrupted principles). Continence and incontinence are defined by exceeding the normal range of human self-control in either direction.
D: Distilled: The incontinent person fails to enforce good principles, while the wicked person efficiently enforces bad ones -- and both continence and incontinence exceed the normal range of human resolve.
Notes: The quotation is from a lost play by Anaxandrides, an Athenian comic poet of the 4th century BCE. -- The city-laws analogy neatly captures the structural difference between incontinence (good principles, poor execution) and vice (corrupted principles, consistent execution). -- The claim that continence and incontinence exceed the normal human range places most people in an intermediate state -- neither fully continent nor fully incontinent -- consistent with Aristotle's general view that most people are morally mediocre (Book VII.7/7.28).

D: Original: Of the forms of incontinence, that of excitable people is more curable than that of those who deliberate but do not abide by their decisions, and those who are incontinent through habituation are more curable than those in whom incontinence is innate; for it is easier to change a habit than to change one's nature; even habit is hard to change just because it is like nature, as Evenus says:
D: Refashioned: Among the forms of incontinence, that of excitable or impetuous people is more curable than that of those who deliberate but fail to abide by their decisions. And those who are incontinent through acquired habit are more curable than those in whom incontinence is innate, since it is easier to change a habit than to change one's nature. Even habit is difficult to change precisely because it is like nature, as Evenus says:
D: Elegant: Impetuous incontinence is more curable than weak incontinence, and habitual incontinence is more curable than innate. Habit is hard to change because it resembles nature -- but nature is harder still.
D: Summary: Impetuous incontinence (acting before deliberation) is more curable than weak incontinence (deliberating but failing to follow through). Similarly, incontinence from habit is more curable than innate incontinence, though habit too is difficult to alter because it approximates a second nature.
D: Distilled: The more deeply rooted the source of incontinence -- from momentary impulse to ingrained habit to inborn nature -- the harder it is to cure.
Notes: Evenus of Paros (5th century BCE) was a poet and sophist mentioned several times in Plato's dialogues (Apology 20b, Phaedo 60d-61c). -- The idea that habit is a 'second nature' became proverbial in Western philosophy and was later developed extensively by Thomas Aquinas and the Scholastics. -- The ranking of curability (impetuous > habitual > innate) reflects Aristotle's practical orientation: ethics should help people improve, so understanding what is more or less changeable matters greatly. -- This also connects to the nature vs. nurture discussion in Book II about how virtues are acquired through habituation.

D: Original:
I say that habit's but a long practice, friend, And this becomes men's nature in the end.

We have now stated what continence, incontinence, endurance, and softness are, and how these states are related to each other.
D: Refashioned:
'I say that habit is nothing but long practice, friend, and this in the end becomes men's nature.'

We have now stated what continence, incontinence, endurance, and softness are, and how these states are related to each other.
D: Elegant: As Evenus says: habit is long practice that becomes a second nature. This concludes the account of continence, incontinence, endurance, and softness, and their interrelations.
D: Summary: Aristotle concludes the discussion of continence, incontinence, endurance, and softness with Evenus' verse that habit, through long practice, becomes second nature.
D: Distilled: Habit, through long practice, becomes indistinguishable from nature -- completing the account of how self-control and its failures are structured.
Notes: This verse from Evenus of Paros became one of the most quoted lines in ancient philosophy. -- The conclusion marks a major structural division in Book VII: chapters 1-10 (7.1-7.44) treat incontinence and continence; chapters 11-14 (7.45-7.60) turn to pleasure. -- Aristotle's emphasis on habit as quasi-nature connects to his foundational claim in Book II.1 that virtues are acquired through habituation, not by nature and not by teaching alone.

D: Original: The study of pleasure and pain belongs to the province of the political philosopher; for he is the architect of the end, with a view to which we call one thing bad and another good without qualification. Further, it is one of our necessary tasks to consider them; for not only did we lay it down that moral virtue and vice are concerned with pains and pleasures, but most people say that happiness involves pleasure; this is why the blessed man is called by a name derived from a word meaning enjoyment.
D: Refashioned: The study of pleasure and pain falls within the province of the political philosopher, since he is the architect of the ultimate end with reference to which we call things good or bad without qualification. Moreover, this study is one of our necessary tasks: not only did we establish earlier that moral virtue and vice are concerned with pleasures and pains, but most people maintain that happiness involves pleasure. Indeed, the Greek word for the 'blessed' or supremely happy person is derived from a word meaning 'enjoyment.'
D: Elegant: Pleasure and pain must be studied by the political philosopher, who determines the ultimate human good. Moral virtue and vice concern pleasures and pains, and most people identify happiness with pleasure -- the very word for 'blessed' derives from 'enjoyment.'
D: Summary: Aristotle justifies turning to the topic of pleasure. The political philosopher must understand it because he determines the human good, moral virtue and vice are defined in terms of pleasure and pain, and common opinion closely links happiness with pleasure.
D: Distilled: The study of pleasure is essential to ethics because moral virtue concerns pleasure and pain, and common opinion identifies happiness itself with enjoyment.
Notes: The 'political philosopher' (politikos) is the ethicist in Aristotle's scheme, since ethics is a branch of political science (Book I.2). -- The etymological point connects makarios ('blessed') to chairein ('to enjoy/rejoice'), though modern linguists consider this etymology folk etymology. -- This begins the first of Aristotle's two treatments of pleasure (the second is in Book X.1-5). Scholars debate whether these represent different periods of his thought or complementary analyses. -- The connection between virtue and pleasure/pain was established in Book II.3.

D: Original: Now (1) some people think that no pleasure is a good, either in itself or incidentally, since the good and pleasure are not the same; (2) others think that some pleasures are good but that most are bad. (3) Again there is a third view, that even if all pleasures are good, yet the best thing in the world cannot be pleasure. (1) The reasons given for the view that pleasure is not a good at all are (a) that every pleasure is a perceptible process to a natural state, and that no process is of the same kind as its end, e.g. no process of building of the same kind as a house. (b) A temperate man avoids pleasures. (c) A man of practical wisdom pursues what is free from pain, not what is pleasant. (d) The pleasures are a hindrance to thought, and the more so the more one delights in them, e.g. in sexual pleasure; for no one could think of anything while absorbed in this. (e) There is no art of pleasure; but every good is the product of some art. (f) Children and the brutes pursue pleasures. (2) The reasons for the view that not all pleasures are good are that (a) there are pleasures that are actually base and objects of reproach, and (b) there are harmful pleasures; for some pleasant things are unhealthy. (3) The reason for the view that the best thing in the world is not pleasure is that pleasure is not an end but a process.
D: Refashioned:
Now (1) some people think that no pleasure is a good, either in itself or incidentally, since the good and pleasure are not the same thing. (2) Others think that some pleasures are good but most are bad. (3) A third view holds that even if all pleasures are good, the best thing in the world still cannot be pleasure.

The reasons given for view (1) -- that pleasure is not a good at all -- are these: (a) Every pleasure is a perceptible process toward a natural state, and no process is the same in kind as its end -- for instance, the process of building is not the same kind of thing as a house. (b) The temperate person avoids pleasures. (c) The person of practical wisdom pursues what is free from pain, not what is pleasant. (d) Pleasures are a hindrance to thought, and the more one delights in them the greater the hindrance -- in sexual pleasure, for example, no one can think about anything else while absorbed in it. (e) There is no art of pleasure, yet every good is the product of some art. (f) Children and animals pursue pleasures.

The reasons for view (2) -- that not all pleasures are good -- are: (a) Some pleasures are actually base and worthy of reproach, and (b) some are harmful, since certain pleasant things are unhealthy.

The reason for view (3) -- that the best thing cannot be pleasure -- is that pleasure is not an end but a process.
D: Elegant: Three anti-pleasure positions: (1) no pleasure is good, because pleasure is mere process, the temperate avoid it, it hinders thought, has no art, and is pursued by children and beasts; (2) not all pleasures are good, since some are base or harmful; (3) even if all pleasures are good, the supreme good cannot be pleasure, since pleasure is process, not end.
D: Summary: Aristotle catalogues three views hostile to pleasure: that no pleasure is good at all (supported by six arguments), that most pleasures are bad (because some are base or harmful), and that the supreme good cannot be pleasure (because pleasure is a process rather than an end). These represent the positions he will systematically refute.
D: Distilled: The main arguments against pleasure are that it is mere process rather than an end, that it hinders thought, that the wise avoid it, and that children and animals pursue it.
Notes: View (1) is associated with Speusippus (Plato's nephew and successor as head of the Academy), who held that both pleasure and pain are evils, and the good is a neutral state. -- View (3) is broadly Platonic, drawing on arguments in the Philebus where Plato argues pleasure is an unlimited 'becoming' (genesis) rather than a 'being' (ousia). -- The argument that pleasure hinders thought (d) echoes a Platonic theme but Aristotle will argue this applies only to foreign (alien) pleasures, not to pleasures proper to an activity. -- The observation about sexual pleasure and thought anticipates Aristotle's more nuanced treatment in Book X.

D: Original: These are pretty much the things that are said. That it does not follow from these grounds that pleasure is not a good, or even the chief good, is plain from the following considerations. (A, a) First, since that which is good may be so in either of two senses (one thing good simply and another good for a particular person), natural constitutions and states of being, and therefore also the corresponding movements and processes, will be correspondingly divisible.
