Of those which are thought to be bad some will be bad if taken without qualification but not bad for a particular person, but worthy of his choice, and some will not be worthy of choice even for a particular person, but only at a particular time and for a short period, though not without qualification; while others are not even pleasures, but seem to be so, viz. all those which involve pain and whose end is curative, e.g. the processes that go on in sick persons.
D: Refashioned:
These are more or less the arguments that have been offered. That it does not follow from them that pleasure is not a good, or even the chief good, is plain from the following considerations.

(A, a) First, since what is good can be so in two senses -- good without qualification and good for a particular person -- natural constitutions and states, and therefore the corresponding movements and processes, will admit of the same distinction. Among those pleasures thought to be bad, some are bad without qualification but not bad for a particular person (indeed they may be worthy of his choice), while others are not worthy of choice even for a particular person except at a particular time and for a brief period, though they are not bad without qualification. And still others are not really pleasures at all, but only appear to be -- namely, all those that involve pain and serve a curative purpose, such as the processes experienced by sick people.
D: Elegant: The anti-pleasure arguments fail. 'Good' has two senses -- good simply and good for a particular person -- and pleasures divide accordingly. Some pleasures thought bad are not bad for a particular person; others are acceptable only temporarily; and some are not truly pleasures at all but curative processes involving pain that merely seem pleasant.
D: Summary: Aristotle begins refuting the anti-pleasure arguments. Since 'good' means both 'good without qualification' and 'good for a particular person,' pleasures must be similarly divided. Some apparently bad pleasures are merely bad in general but appropriate for specific individuals or situations. Others are not truly pleasures but curative processes that only seem pleasant.
D: Distilled: The case against pleasure collapses once we distinguish what is good simply from what is good for a particular person, and genuine pleasures from curative processes that merely resemble them.
Notes: The distinction between 'good simply' (haplos agathon) and 'good for a particular person' (tini agathon) is fundamental to Aristotle's ethics and appears throughout the Ethics, especially in the discussion of the good in Book I. -- The example of curative pleasures (sick persons enjoying remedies) draws on Hippocratic medical theory. -- This begins a systematic point-by-point refutation of the arguments catalogued in 7.46, following the order (A) through (G).

D: Original: (b) Further, one kind of good being activity and another being state, the processes that restore us to our natural state are only incidentally pleasant; for that matter the activity at work in the appetites for them is the activity of so much of our state and nature as has remained unimpaired; for there are actually pleasures that involve no pain or appetite (e.g. those of contemplation), the nature in such a case not being defective at all. That the others are incidental is indicated by the fact that men do not enjoy the same pleasant objects when their nature is in its settled state as they do when it is being replenished, but in the former case they enjoy the things that are pleasant without qualification, in the latter the contraries of these as well; for then they enjoy even sharp and bitter things, none of which is pleasant either by nature or without qualification. The states they produce, therefore, are not pleasures naturally or without qualification; for as pleasant things differ, so do the pleasures arising from them.
D: Refashioned:
(b) Furthermore, since one kind of good is an activity and another is a state, the processes that restore us to our natural state are only incidentally pleasant. The activity at work in our appetites for these restorative things is actually the activity of whatever remains unimpaired in our state and nature. For there are pleasures that involve no pain or appetite at all -- the pleasures of contemplation, for instance -- where our nature is not deficient in any way.

That the restorative pleasures are only incidental is shown by this: people do not enjoy the same things when their nature is in its settled, healthy state as they do when it is being replenished. In the settled state they enjoy things that are pleasant without qualification; during replenishment they enjoy even their contraries -- sharp and bitter things that are not naturally pleasant or pleasant without qualification. The states these restorative pleasures produce are therefore not genuine pleasures in the natural or unqualified sense, for just as pleasant things differ, so do the pleasures that arise from them.
D: Elegant: Restorative processes are only incidentally pleasant; the real pleasure comes from the activity of our unimpaired nature. Proof: the pleasures of contemplation involve no pain, appetite, or deficiency at all. And people in a healthy state enjoy different things than those being restored -- in recovery, even bitter things seem pleasant, though they are not pleasant by nature. True pleasure corresponds to the activity of a healthy, complete nature.
D: Summary: Pleasures of restoration (eating when hungry, drinking when thirsty) are only incidentally pleasant -- the genuine pleasure comes from the activity of whatever remains healthy in our nature. The pleasures of contemplation, which involve no deficiency or appetite, prove that pleasure can exist without a restorative process. What we enjoy in health differs from what we enjoy during recovery.
D: Distilled: Restorative pleasures are incidental; genuine pleasure is the activity of an unimpaired nature, as the pleasures of contemplation -- requiring no deficiency to exist -- demonstrate.
Notes: The pleasures of contemplation (theōria) being cited as the purest example of non-restorative pleasure anticipates the argument of Book X.7-8 that contemplative activity is the highest happiness. -- The distinction between incidental and natural pleasure is crucial to Aristotle's defense of pleasure as a good: it allows him to acknowledge that some pleasures arise from deficiency while maintaining that the best pleasures do not. -- The observation that sick people enjoy bitter remedies comes from Greek medical practice, where sharp and bitter substances were used therapeutically.

D: Original: (c) Again, it is not necessary that there should be something else better than pleasure, as some say the end is better than the process; for leasures are not processes nor do they all involve process-they are activities and ends; nor do they arise when we are becoming something, but when we are exercising some faculty; and not all pleasures have an end different from themselves, but only the pleasures of persons who are being led to the perfecting of their nature. This is why it is not right to say that pleasure is perceptible process, but it should rather be called activity of the natural state, and instead of 'perceptible' 'unimpeded'. It is thought by some people to be process just because they think it is in the strict sense good; for they think that activity is process, which it is not.
D: Refashioned:
(c) Again, it is not necessary that there be something better than pleasure, as some claim by arguing that the end is better than the process. For pleasures are not processes, nor do they all involve process -- they are activities and ends in themselves. They arise not when we are becoming something but when we are exercising some faculty. And not all pleasures have an end distinct from themselves; only the pleasures of those who are being led toward the completion of their nature have a separate end.

This is why it is wrong to call pleasure a 'perceptible process.' It should rather be called an activity of one's natural state, and instead of 'perceptible,' we should say 'unimpeded.' Some people think pleasure is a process only because they consider it to be good in the fullest sense, and they assume that activity is a kind of process -- but it is not.
D: Elegant: Pleasures are not processes but activities and ends in themselves. They arise when we exercise a faculty, not when we are becoming something. Only restorative pleasures have an end beyond themselves. Pleasure should be defined not as 'perceptible process' but as 'unimpeded activity of the natural state.' The confusion arises from wrongly equating activity with process.
D: Summary: Aristotle rejects the claim that pleasure is a mere process toward an end (and therefore inferior to that end). Pleasures are activities and ends in themselves, arising from the exercise of faculties in their natural state. The definition of pleasure should be 'unimpeded activity of the natural state,' not 'perceptible process.'
D: Distilled: Pleasure is not a process toward something better but an unimpeded activity of our natural state -- an end in itself, not a means.
Notes: This is one of Aristotle's most important philosophical claims about pleasure, directly contradicting Plato's characterization of pleasure as genesis (becoming/process) in the Philebus 53c-55a. -- The distinction between kinēsis (process/movement) and energeia (activity/actuality) is fundamental to Aristotle's metaphysics (Metaphysics IX.6) and his philosophy of action. An activity is complete at every moment, while a process is incomplete until it reaches its end. -- The definition of pleasure as 'unimpeded activity' anticipates the more refined account in Book X.4, where pleasure is said to 'complete' (teleioun) activity. -- This passage has been central to debates about whether Aristotle's two treatments of pleasure (Books VII and X) are consistent.

D: Original:
(B) The view that pleasures are bad because some pleasant things are unhealthy is like saying that healthy things are bad because some healthy things are bad for money-making; both are bad in the respect mentioned, but they are not bad for that reason-indeed, thinking itself is sometimes injurious to health.

Neither practical wisdom nor any state of being is impeded by the pleasure arising from it; it is foreign pleasures that impede, for the pleasures arising from thinking and learning will make us think and learn all the more.
D: Refashioned:
(B) The view that pleasures are bad because some pleasant things are unhealthy is like saying that healthy things are bad because some healthy things are bad for making money. Both are bad in the respect mentioned, but they are not bad on that account -- indeed, thinking itself is sometimes injurious to health.

Neither practical wisdom nor any other state of being is impeded by the pleasure that arises from it. It is only foreign pleasures that interfere. The pleasures that arise from thinking and learning will actually make us think and learn all the more.
D: Elegant: That some pleasant things are unhealthy no more proves pleasure bad than the fact that some healthy things hurt one's finances proves health bad. Thinking itself can harm health. The key distinction: pleasures proper to an activity enhance it, while foreign pleasures impede it. The pleasure of learning makes us learn more, not less.
D: Summary: The argument that pleasure is bad because some pleasures are unhealthy commits a logical error -- it confuses incidental harm with essential badness. More importantly, pleasure proper to an activity enhances rather than hinders it: the pleasure of thinking promotes thinking. Only foreign pleasures (pleasures from unrelated activities) cause interference.
D: Distilled: Pleasure proper to an activity enhances it; only foreign pleasures impede -- the joy of learning makes us learn more, not less.
Notes: The distinction between proper (oikeia) and foreign (allotria) pleasures is one of Aristotle's most enduring contributions to the philosophy of pleasure and motivation. It reappears in Book X.5. -- This refutes argument (1d) from 7.46 about pleasure hindering thought: it is only sexual pleasure (a foreign pleasure relative to intellectual activity) that hinders thinking, not the pleasure of thinking itself. -- The analogy with health and money-making illustrates Aristotle's frequent use of the fallacy of accident (treating an incidental property as essential). -- The claim that the pleasure of learning enhances learning has modern empirical support in educational psychology and flow theory.

D: Original:
(C) The fact that no pleasure is the product of any art arises naturally enough; there is no art of any other activity either, but only of the corresponding faculty; though for that matter the arts of the perfumer and the cook are thought to be arts of pleasure.

(D) The arguments based on the grounds that the temperate man avoids pleasure and that the man of practical wisdom pursues the painless life, and that children and the brutes pursue pleasure, are all refuted by the same consideration. We have pointed out in what sense pleasures are good without qualification and in what sense some are not good; now both the brutes and children pursue pleasures of the latter kind (and the man of practical wisdom pursues tranquil freedom from that kind), viz. those which imply appetite and pain, i.e. the bodily pleasures (for it is these that are of this nature) and the excesses of them, in respect of which the self-indulgent man is self-indulent. This is why the temperate man avoids these pleasures; for even he has pleasures of his own.
D: Refashioned:
(C) That no pleasure is the product of any art arises naturally enough: there is no art that produces any other activity either, only arts that produce the corresponding capacity or faculty. That said, the arts of the perfumer and the cook are commonly considered arts of pleasure.

(D) The arguments that the temperate person avoids pleasure, that the practically wise person pursues a painless life, and that children and brutes pursue pleasure -- all of these are refuted by a single consideration. We have already pointed out in what sense pleasures are good without qualification and in what sense some are not. Now both animals and children pursue the latter kind -- and the practically wise person seeks tranquil freedom from precisely that kind -- namely, those pleasures that involve appetite and pain, that is, the bodily pleasures and their excesses, which define the self-indulgent person. This is why the temperate person avoids these particular pleasures. But even the temperate person has pleasures of his own.
D: Elegant:
No art produces pleasure directly, but no art produces any activity directly -- only the capacity for it. The perfumer and cook are considered artists of pleasure nonetheless.

The temperate person avoids bodily pleasures involving appetite and pain, not all pleasure. Children and animals pursue exactly these lower pleasures. The practically wise person seeks freedom from these, not from pleasure as such. Even the temperate person has pleasures of his own.
D: Summary: Aristotle dispatches two more objections. That no art produces pleasure is unsurprising, since no art produces any activity directly -- only the capacity for it. And the claim that the wise avoid pleasure while children and animals pursue it is refuted by distinguishing bodily pleasures (which involve appetite and pain) from higher pleasures. The temperate person avoids the former but enjoys the latter.
D: Distilled: The temperate person avoids bodily pleasures that involve appetite and pain, not pleasure as such -- even he has pleasures proper to his virtuous activity.
Notes: The mention of perfumers and cooks as 'artists of pleasure' reflects the Greek cultural context where these crafts were associated with luxury and sensuality. -- This refutes arguments (1b), (1c), (1e), and (1f) from 7.46 in a single stroke by distinguishing types of pleasure. -- The claim that the temperate person 'has pleasures of his own' is important: Aristotelian virtue is not asceticism but a proper ordering of pleasure. This connects to Book II.3 and Book X.1-5. -- The practically wise person's pursuit of freedom from bodily disturbance (aochlēsia) anticipates Epicurean ataraxia, though Aristotle's framework is quite different.

D: Original: But further (E) it is agreed that pain is bad and to be avoided; for some pain is without qualification bad, and other pain is bad because it is in some respect an impediment to us. Now the contrary of that which is to be avoided, qua something to be avoided and bad, is good. Pleasure, then, is necessarily a good. For the answer of Speusippus, that pleasure is contrary both to pain and to good, as the greater is contrary both to the less and to the equal, is not successful; since he would not say that pleasure is essentially just a species of evil.
D: Refashioned:
(E) Furthermore, it is agreed that pain is bad and to be avoided: some pain is bad without qualification, while other pain is bad because it impedes us in some way. Now the contrary of what is to be avoided, insofar as it is to be avoided and bad, is good. Pleasure, then, is necessarily a good.

Speusippus' response -- that pleasure is contrary both to pain and to the good, just as the greater is contrary both to the less and to the equal -- does not succeed. He would not want to say that pleasure is essentially just a species of evil.
D: Elegant: Pain is agreed to be bad and to be avoided. The contrary of what is bad and to be avoided must be good. Therefore pleasure is necessarily a good. Speusippus' attempt to make pleasure contrary to both pain and the good -- as 'greater' is contrary to both 'less' and 'equal' -- fails, since he would not accept that pleasure is essentially a form of evil.
D: Summary: Since pain is universally acknowledged to be bad, and pleasure is the contrary of pain, pleasure must be a good. Speusippus tried to avoid this conclusion by arguing that pleasure could be contrary to both pain and the good simultaneously, but this would make pleasure essentially evil -- which even he would not accept.
D: Distilled: Since pain is acknowledged as bad and pleasure is its contrary, pleasure must be a good -- a conclusion that Speusippus' attempt to make pleasure neutral rather than good fails to block.
Notes: Speusippus (c. 407-339 BCE) was Plato's nephew and his immediate successor as head of the Academy (347-339 BCE). He held that both pleasure and pain were evils, and the good was a neutral state -- a position Aristotle frequently criticizes. -- The analogy of 'greater' being contrary to both 'less' and 'equal' is a sophisticated logical point: Speusippus argued that just as a quantity can be opposed to two different things, pleasure can be opposed to both pain and the good. -- Aristotle's refutation turns on the principle that being contrary to something bad makes a thing good, not neutral or evil. -- This argument from contrariety was influential in later Stoic-Epicurean debates about the value of pleasure.

D: Original: And (F) if certain pleasures are bad, that does not prevent the chief good from being some pleasure, just as the chief good may be some form of knowledge though certain kinds of knowledge are bad. Perhaps it is even necessary, if each disposition has unimpeded activities, that, whether the activity (if unimpeded) of all our dispositions or that of some one of them is happiness, this should be the thing most worthy of our choice; and this activity is pleasure. Thus the chief good would be some pleasure, though most pleasures might perhaps be bad without qualification. And for this reason all men think that the happy life is pleasant and weave pleasure into their ideal of happiness-and reasonably too; for no activity is perfect when it is impeded, and happiness is a perfect thing; this is why the happy man needs the goods of the body and external goods, i.e. those of fortune, viz. in order that he may not be impeded in these ways. Those who say that the victim on the rack or the man who falls into great misfortunes is happy if he is good, are, whether they mean to or not, talking nonsense. Now because we need fortune as well as other things, some people think good fortune the same thing as happiness; but it is not that, for even good fortune itself when in excess is an impediment, and perhaps should then be no longer called good fortune; for its limit is fixed by reference to happiness.
D: Refashioned:
(F) And if certain pleasures are bad, this does not prevent the chief good from being some pleasure, just as the chief good may be some form of knowledge even though certain kinds of knowledge are bad.

Perhaps it is even necessary that if each disposition has unimpeded activities, and if happiness is the unimpeded activity of all our dispositions (or of some one of them), then this -- being the thing most worthy of choice -- is happiness. And this activity is pleasure. Thus the chief good would be some pleasure, even though most pleasures might be bad without qualification.

This is why all people think the happy life is pleasant and weave pleasure into their ideal of happiness -- and reasonably so, since no activity is complete when it is impeded, and happiness is something complete. This is why the happy person needs bodily goods and external goods (the goods of fortune): so that he may not be impeded in these ways.

Those who say that a good person is happy even on the rack or when falling into great misfortunes are talking nonsense, whether they mean to or not. And because we need fortune as well as other things, some people mistake good fortune for happiness itself. But it is not the same thing, since even good fortune when excessive becomes an impediment -- and perhaps should then no longer be called good fortune, since its proper limit is set by reference to happiness.
D: Elegant:
That some pleasures are bad does not prevent the chief good from being a pleasure, any more than bad forms of knowledge prevent the chief good from being a form of knowledge. If happiness is the unimpeded activity of our best disposition, and this activity is pleasant, then the chief good is a kind of pleasure.

All people rightly weave pleasure into happiness, since no impeded activity is complete, and happiness must be complete. The happy person needs bodily and external goods to avoid impediment. Those who call a person happy on the rack are talking nonsense. Good fortune serves happiness but is not identical to it -- and in excess, even fortune becomes an impediment.
D: Summary: Some pleasures being bad does not prevent the supreme good from being a type of pleasure. If happiness is the unimpeded activity of our highest capacity, and such activity is inherently pleasant, then the chief good is a kind of pleasure. Happiness requires bodily and external goods to remain unimpeded. Neither torture nor excessive fortune is compatible with happiness.
D: Distilled: The chief good may well be a kind of pleasure, since happiness is unimpeded excellent activity, and such activity is inherently and necessarily pleasant.
Notes: The argument that happiness requires external goods (bodily health, wealth, friends) is characteristic of Aristotle's rejection of the Stoic-like view that virtue alone suffices for happiness. This connects to Book I.8-10. -- The dismissal of the 'happy on the rack' position targets views associated with the Cynics and later the Stoics, who held that virtue alone guaranteed happiness regardless of external circumstances. -- The idea that excessive fortune is an impediment is a distinctively Aristotelian insight: too much wealth or beauty can distract from virtuous activity. -- The identification of pleasure with unimpeded activity here anticipates Book X.4-5's more refined account. -- This is arguably the strongest passage in Book VII for the view that Aristotle identifies the chief good with pleasure in some sense.

D: Original:
And indeed the fact that all things, both brutes and men, pursue pleasure is an indication of its being somehow the chief good:

No voice is wholly lost that many peoples... But since no one nature or state either is or is thought the best for all, neither do all pursue the same pleasure; yet all pursue pleasure. And perhaps they actually pursue not the pleasure they think they pursue nor that which they would say they pursue, but the same pleasure; for all things have by nature something divine in them. But the bodily pleasures have appropriated the name both because we oftenest steer our course for them and because all men share in them; thus because they alone are familiar, men think there are no others.
D: Refashioned:
Indeed, the fact that all creatures -- both animals and humans -- pursue pleasure is an indication that pleasure is somehow the chief good:

'No voice is wholly lost that many peoples speak...'

But since no single nature or condition is or is thought to be best for all, not all creatures pursue the same pleasure -- yet all pursue pleasure. And perhaps they are not actually pursuing the pleasure they think they pursue or would say they pursue, but the same underlying pleasure. For all things have by nature something divine in them.

Bodily pleasures, however, have appropriated the name 'pleasure' as their own, both because we most often steer our course toward them and because everyone shares in them. Since they alone are familiar, people assume there are no other kinds.
D: Elegant:
That all creatures pursue pleasure suggests it is somehow the chief good -- 'No voice is wholly lost that many peoples speak.' Not all pursue the same pleasure, since natures differ, yet all pursue pleasure. Perhaps all things, having something divine in them, unknowingly pursue the same deep pleasure.

Bodily pleasures have monopolized the name 'pleasure' because they are the most common and familiar, leading people to suppose no other pleasures exist.
D: Summary: The universal pursuit of pleasure by all creatures suggests it is connected to the chief good. Though different beings pursue different pleasures, there may be a single underlying pleasure all are drawn toward, reflecting something divine in nature. Bodily pleasures have monopolized the concept of 'pleasure' because of their universality and familiarity, obscuring the existence of higher pleasures.
D: Distilled: The universal pursuit of pleasure across all creatures points to something divine in nature, though bodily pleasures have monopolized the name and obscured the existence of higher forms.
Notes: The quoted verse ('No voice is wholly lost that many peoples speak...') is from Hesiod's Works and Days (763-764), used to support the principle that widespread opinion contains some truth -- a principle central to Aristotle's dialectical method. -- The claim that 'all things have by nature something divine in them' echoes Aristotle's teleological metaphysics and connects to his argument in Metaphysics XII that all things are oriented toward the divine unmoved mover. -- The observation that bodily pleasures have 'appropriated the name' anticipates the modern philosophical distinction between lower and higher pleasures, later developed by John Stuart Mill in Utilitarianism (1863). -- This passage contains one of Aristotle's most expansive and almost mystical claims about the unity of pleasure across all living things.

D: Original: It is evident also that if pleasure, i.e. the activity of our faculties, is not a good, it will not be the case that the happy man lives a pleasant life; for to what end should he need pleasure, if it is not a good but the happy man may even live a painful life? For pain is neither an evil nor a good, if pleasure is not; why then should he avoid it? Therefore, too, the life of the good man will not be pleasanter than that of any one else, if his activities are not more pleasant.
D: Refashioned:
It is also evident that if pleasure -- that is, the activity of our faculties -- is not a good, then the happy person will not live a pleasant life. For why would he need pleasure if it is not a good? The happy person might even live a painful life -- for pain would be neither evil nor good if pleasure is neither. So why should he avoid pain?

Furthermore, the life of the good person will not be pleasanter than anyone else's, if his activities are not more pleasant.
D: Elegant: If pleasure is not a good, the happy person need not live pleasantly -- and might even live painfully, since pain would be no more an evil than pleasure is a good. But this is absurd. Moreover, the good person's life would be no pleasanter than anyone else's, which contradicts our intuitions about virtue and happiness.
D: Summary: If pleasure were not a good, the happy person would have no reason to live pleasantly and might even live in pain without it being a problem. This absurd consequence, along with the implication that the virtuous life would be no more pleasant than any other, shows that pleasure must be a genuine good.
D: Distilled: Denying that pleasure is a good leads to the absurdity that the happy person's life need not be pleasant and the virtuous life would be no better than any other.
Notes: This is a reductio ad absurdum argument: if pleasure is not a good, several plainly false consequences follow. -- The claim that the good person's activities should be more pleasant connects to Book I.8, where Aristotle argues that virtuous activities are inherently pleasant to the person of good character. -- The argument also connects to Book X.7-8, where Aristotle argues that the contemplative life is the most pleasant as well as the most excellent. -- The passage implicitly targets Speusippus and others who tried to separate pleasure entirely from the good.

D: Original: (G) With regard to the bodily pleasures, those who say that some pleasures are very much to be chosen, viz. the noble pleasures, but not the bodily pleasures, i.e. those with which the self-indulgent man is concerned, must consider why, then, the contrary pains are bad. For the contrary of bad is good. Are the necessary pleasures good in the sense in which even that which is not bad is good? Or are they good up to a point? Is it that where you have states and processes of which there cannot be too much, there cannot be too much of the corresponding pleasure, and that where there can be too much of the one there can be too much of the other also? Now there can be too much of bodily goods, and the bad man is bad by virtue of pursuing the excess, not by virtue of pursuing the necessary pleasures (for all men enjoy in some way or other both dainty foods and wines and sexual intercourse, but not all men do so as they ought). The contrary is the case with pain; for he does not avoid the excess of it, he avoids it altogether; and this is peculiar to him, for the alternative to excess of pleasure is not pain, except to the man who pursues this excess.
D: Refashioned:
(G) Regarding bodily pleasures: those who maintain that some pleasures are very much worth choosing -- namely the noble ones -- but not the bodily pleasures that concern the self-indulgent person, must consider why the corresponding bodily pains are bad. For the contrary of something bad is good.

Are the necessary pleasures good merely in the sense that even what is not bad qualifies as good? Or are they good up to a certain point? Where you have states and processes that do not admit of excess, the corresponding pleasure cannot be excessive either. But where there can be too much of the state, there can be too much of the corresponding pleasure as well.

Now there can be too much of bodily goods. The bad person is bad by virtue of pursuing the excess, not by pursuing the necessary pleasures themselves -- for everyone enjoys good food, wine, and sexual intercourse in some fashion, though not everyone does so as they ought. The reverse holds for pain: the bad person does not merely avoid excessive pain; he avoids pain altogether. And this is peculiar to him, since the alternative to excessive pleasure is not pain -- except for the person who specifically pursues that excess.
D: Elegant: If bodily pleasures are not good, why are the corresponding bodily pains bad? Necessary pleasures are good up to a point -- where excess in the underlying state is possible, excess in pleasure is possible too. Everyone enjoys food, wine, and sex to some degree; the bad person errs by pursuing the excess, not the necessity. Conversely, the bad person avoids all pain, not just excessive pain.
D: Summary: Those who reject bodily pleasures as goods must explain why bodily pains are evils. Necessary pleasures are genuinely good up to a point. The fault of the self-indulgent lies not in enjoying food, wine, and sex (which everyone does) but in pursuing their excess. The bad person's distinctive error with pain is avoiding it altogether rather than just avoiding its excess.
D: Distilled: Necessary bodily pleasures are good up to a point; the fault lies in pursuing their excess, not in enjoying them, and the corresponding pains confirm their goodness by being genuinely bad.
Notes: This addresses the position (G) that noble pleasures are good but bodily ones are not. -- The argument from contraries (if the pain is bad, the corresponding pleasure must be good) extends the logic used in 7.52. -- The distinction between pursuing necessary pleasures (acceptable) and their excess (vicious) connects to Book III.11's account of temperance. -- The observation that everyone enjoys 'dainty foods, wines, and sexual intercourse' reflects Aristotle's realism about human nature and his refusal to endorse asceticism.

D: Original: Since we should state not only the truth, but also the cause of error-for this contributes towards producing conviction, since when a reasonable explanation is given of why the false view appears true, this tends to produce belief in the true view-therefore we must state why the bodily pleasures appear the more worthy of choice. (a) Firstly, then, it is because they expel pain; owing to the excesses of pain that men experience, they pursue excessive and in general bodily pleasure as being a cure for the pain. Now curative agencies produce intense feeling-which is the reason why they are pursued-because they show up against the contrary pain. (Indeed pleasure is thought not to be good for these two reasons, as has been said, viz. that (a) some of them are activities belonging to a bad nature-either congenital, as in the case of a brute, or due to habit, i.e. those of bad men; while (b) others are meant to cure a defective nature, and it is better to be in a healthy state than to be getting into it, but these arise during the process of being made perfect and are therefore only incidentally good., b) Further, they are pursued because of their violence by those who cannot enjoy other pleasures. (At all events they go out of their way to manufacture thirsts somehow for themselves. When these are harmless, the practice is irreproachable; when they are hurtful, it is bad.) For they have nothing else to enjoy, and, besides, a neutral state is painful to many people because of their nature.
D: Refashioned:
Since we should state not only the truth but also the cause of error -- for explaining why the false view appears true helps produce conviction in the true view -- we must explain why bodily pleasures appear to be the most choiceworthy.

(a) First, it is because they expel pain. When people experience great pain, they pursue excessive bodily pleasure as a remedy. These curative pleasures produce intense feeling -- which is precisely why they are sought -- because they stand out against the background of pain. (Indeed, pleasure is thought not to be good for two reasons, as already mentioned: first, some pleasures are activities belonging to a bad nature, whether congenital, as in brutes, or acquired through habit, as in bad people; second, other pleasures are meant to cure a defective nature, and it is better to be in a healthy state than to be getting into one -- but these curative pleasures arise during the process of being restored and are therefore only incidentally good.)

(b) Second, bodily pleasures are pursued for their intensity by people who cannot enjoy other pleasures. They even go out of their way to manufacture artificial thirsts for themselves. When these artificial desires are harmless, the practice is unobjectionable; when they are harmful, it is bad. For such people have nothing else to enjoy, and besides, a neutral state -- neither pleasure nor pain -- is actually painful to many people because of their nature.
D: Elegant: We must explain why bodily pleasures seem most desirable. First, they expel pain: intense curative pleasures stand out against a background of suffering, making them seem supremely good -- though they are only incidentally pleasant. Second, people who cannot enjoy higher pleasures pursue bodily ones for their sheer intensity, even manufacturing artificial appetites. For some, a neutral state is itself painful, driving them toward any strong sensation.
D: Summary: Aristotle explains why people mistakenly prize bodily pleasure above all else. First, bodily pleasures are curative -- they relieve pain intensely, which makes them seem supremely valuable though they are only incidentally good. Second, people who lack access to higher pleasures pursue bodily intensity as the only available option, sometimes even creating artificial appetites. For some natures, even a neutral state is painful.
D: Distilled: Bodily pleasures seem supreme because they relieve pain with intense feeling and are the only pleasure available to those who cannot access higher forms of enjoyment.
Notes: The principle that explaining the cause of error strengthens belief in the truth is an important methodological claim, connecting to Aristotle's dialectical practice in the Topics and his discussion of rhetorical persuasion in the Rhetoric. -- The 'manufactured thirsts' may refer to practices like eating salty or spicy foods to increase the pleasure of drinking -- a phenomenon recognized in Greek symposium culture. -- The observation that a neutral state is painful for some people anticipates modern psychological research on sensation-seeking personality types and boredom intolerance. -- The two reasons pleasure seems bad (bad nature, curative process) summarize Aristotle's analysis from 7.47-7.48.

D: Original: For the animal nature is always in travail, as the students of natural science also testify, saying that sight and hearing are painful; but we have become used to this, as they maintain. Similarly, while, in youth, people are, owing to the growth that is going on, in a situation like that of drunken men, and youth is pleasant, on the other hand people of excitable nature always need relief; for even their body is ever in torment owing to its special composition, and they are always under the influence of violent desire; but pain is driven out both by the contrary pleasure, and by any chance pleasure if it be strong; and for these reasons they become self-indulgent and bad.
D: Refashioned:
For animal nature is always in a state of strain, as the students of natural science also attest, saying that even sight and hearing are painful -- we have simply become habituated to them, they maintain.

Similarly, young people, because of the growth occurring in them, are in a state resembling intoxication, and youth is pleasant precisely for this reason. On the other hand, people of excitable temperament always need relief: even their body is constantly in a state of agitation due to their natural constitution, and they are perpetually under the influence of intense desire. Pain is driven out both by the contrary pleasure and by any chance pleasure, so long as it is strong enough. And it is for these reasons that such people become self-indulgent and bad.
D: Elegant: Animal nature is always under strain -- even basic sensation involves a kind of pain we have grown accustomed to. Youth resembles intoxication because of the body's constant growth. Excitable temperaments are in perpetual agitation, always craving relief through strong sensation. Pain is driven out by any sufficiently intense contrary pleasure -- which is why such people become self-indulgent.
D: Summary: According to natural science, all animal sensation involves a degree of strain. Youth resembles intoxication because of bodily growth. People with excitable temperaments are in constant physical agitation and always crave intense relief through pleasure. This perpetual need for strong sensation explains how they become self-indulgent.
D: Distilled: Excitable natures are in constant physical agitation and therefore perpetually crave intense pleasure as relief, which is how they develop into self-indulgent people.
Notes: The claim that sight and hearing are painful comes from natural philosophers, possibly Anaxagoras or the Hippocratic tradition, who held that all sensation involves a degree of alteration (alloiōsis) that is inherently painful. -- The comparison of youth to intoxication reflects the Greek medical view that the growing body is in constant flux. -- The 'excitable nature' (melancholikos in some readings) may refer to the melancholic temperament in Hippocratic medicine, associated with black bile and restlessness. -- This passage provides a naturalistic, almost physiological explanation for self-indulgence, complementing the moral-psychological account of earlier chapters.

D: Original: But the pleasures that do not involve pains do not admit of excess; and these are among the things pleasant by nature and not incidentally. By things pleasant incidentally I mean those that act as cures (for because as a result people are cured, through some action of the part that remains healthy, for this reason the process is thought pleasant); by things naturally pleasant I mean those that stimulate the action of the healthy nature.
D: Refashioned:
But the pleasures that do not involve pain admit of no excess. These belong to things that are naturally pleasant, not incidentally so.

By things 'incidentally pleasant' I mean those that act as cures -- the reason the process seems pleasant is that people are restored to health through the action of whatever part of them remains healthy. By things 'naturally pleasant' I mean those that stimulate the activity of a healthy nature as such.
D: Elegant: Pleasures not involving pain cannot be excessive. These are naturally pleasant -- they stimulate the activity of a healthy nature. Incidentally pleasant things are merely curative: they seem pleasant because recovery occurs through the activity of what remains healthy.
D: Summary: Pleasures that involve no pain or deficiency cannot be excessive and are naturally pleasant, stimulating the activity of a healthy nature. Curative pleasures are only incidentally pleasant -- they seem pleasant because the healthy part of one's nature is active during the healing process.
D: Distilled: Pleasures arising from the activity of a healthy nature cannot be excessive; only curative pleasures -- which are incidentally pleasant -- admit of excess.
Notes: This distinction between naturally pleasant (kata phusin) and incidentally pleasant (kata symbebēkos) is crucial to Aristotle's defense of pleasure. The pleasures of contemplation and virtuous activity are naturally pleasant and cannot be excessive, while bodily pleasures of restoration can be. -- The claim that natural pleasures admit no excess provides the philosophical basis for the view that the highest forms of pleasure (intellectual, contemplative) are immune to the problems associated with bodily pleasure. -- This anticipates Book X.4-5's refined account of pleasure as the completion of activity.

D: Original:
There is no one thing that is always pleasant, because our nature is not simple but there is another element in us as well, inasmuch as we are perishable creatures, so that if the one element does something, this is unnatural to the other nature, and when the two elements are evenly balanced, what is done seems neither painful nor pleasant; for if the nature of anything were simple, the same action would always be most pleasant to it. This is why God always enjoys a single and simple pleasure; for there is not only an activity of movement but an activity of immobility, and pleasure is found more in rest than in movement. But 'change in all things is sweet', as the poet says, because of some vice; for as it is the vicious man that is changeable, so the nature that needs change is vicious; for it is not simple nor good.

We have now discussed continence and incontinence, and pleasure and pain, both what each is and in what sense some of them are good and others bad; it remains to speak of friendship.
D: Refashioned:
There is no single thing that is always pleasant, because our nature is not simple. There is another element within us as well, since we are perishable creatures. When one element in us does something, this is unnatural for the other element. When the two are evenly balanced, what is done seems neither painful nor pleasant. If any being's nature were truly simple, the same activity would always be most pleasant to it.

This is why God always enjoys a single, simple pleasure. For there is not only an activity of movement but also an activity of rest, and pleasure is found more in rest than in movement. But 'change in all things is sweet,' as the poet says, because of a kind of deficiency. For just as a changeable person is deficient in character, so a nature that needs change is deficient -- it is neither simple nor wholly good.

We have now discussed continence and incontinence, and pleasure and pain -- both what each is and in what sense some of them are good and others bad. It remains to speak of friendship.
D: Elegant:
No single thing is always pleasant because human nature is composite: what satisfies one element in us conflicts with another, and only perfect balance produces neutrality. A simple nature would find the same activity always most pleasant. This is why God enjoys a single, unchanging pleasure -- for pleasure resides more in rest than in movement.

'Change in all things is sweet' only because of a deficiency: the nature that craves change, like the changeable person, is not simple or wholly good.

This completes the discussion of continence, incontinence, pleasure, and pain. Friendship comes next.
D: Summary: No single pleasure is permanent for humans because our composite nature means that satisfying one element conflicts with another. God, having a simple nature, enjoys a single unchanging pleasure. The proverb that 'change is sweet' reflects a deficiency in our nature, not a truth about pleasure itself. Aristotle concludes the discussion of pleasure and transitions to friendship.
D: Distilled: Our love of variety in pleasure reflects the imperfection of our composite nature; a perfectly simple being like God would enjoy a single, unchanging pleasure forever.
Notes: The quotation 'change in all things is sweet' is from Euripides (Orestes 234). -- The theological point that God enjoys a single, simple pleasure connects to Metaphysics XII.7-9, where the divine thinking-of-thinking (noēsis noēseōs) is described as the highest actuality. -- The contrast between divine simplicity and human complexity is fundamental to Aristotle's cosmology and theology. -- The idea that pleasure in rest exceeds pleasure in motion echoes the priority of actuality (energeia) over potentiality (dynamis) in Aristotle's metaphysics. -- This passage concludes Book VII. The transition to friendship (Books VIII-IX) follows, comprising the longest single treatment of any topic in the Ethics. -- Aristotle's two treatments of pleasure (Book VII.11-14 and Book X.1-5) have generated extensive scholarly debate about their consistency. Book VII emphasizes pleasure as unimpeded activity; Book X describes pleasure as something that 'completes' activity like the bloom on youth.

D: Original: After what we have said, a discussion of friendship would naturally follow, since it is a virtue or implies virtue, and is besides most necessary with a view to living. For without friends no one would choose to live, though he had all other goods; even rich men and those in possession of office and of dominating power are thought to need friends most of all; for what is the use of such prosperity without the opportunity of beneficence, which is exercised chiefly and in its most laudable form towards friends? Or how can prosperity be guarded and preserved without friends? The greater it is, the more exposed is it to risk. And in poverty and in other misfortunes men think friends are the only refuge. It helps the young, too, to keep from error; it aids older people by ministering to their needs and supplementing the activities that are failing from weakness; those in the prime of life it stimulates to noble actions-'two going together'-for with friends men are more able both to think and to act. Again, parent seems by nature to feel it for offspring and offspring for parent, not only among men but among birds and among most animals; it is felt mutually by members of the same race, and especially by men, whence we praise lovers of their fellowmen. We may even in our travels how near and dear every man is to every other. Friendship seems too to hold states together, and lawgivers to care more for it than for justice; for unanimity seems to be something like friendship, and this they aim at most of all, and expel faction as their worst enemy; and when men are friends they have no need of justice, while when they are just they need friendship as well, and the truest form of justice is thought to be a friendly quality.
D: Refashioned:
Having discussed the virtues, it is natural to turn next to friendship, since friendship is itself a virtue or at least involves virtue, and is moreover one of the most essential things for human life. No one would choose to live without friends, even if they possessed every other good. The wealthy, the powerful, and those who hold office are thought to need friends most of all -- for what good is prosperity without the opportunity to do good for others, which is exercised above all toward friends? And how can prosperity be safeguarded without friends? The greater one's fortune, the more vulnerable it is. In poverty and misfortune, people regard friends as their only refuge. Friendship helps the young avoid mistakes; it supports older people by attending to their needs and compensating for their declining abilities; and it spurs those in their prime to noble action -- as Homer says, 'two going together' -- for with friends, people are better able both to think and to act.

Furthermore, the bond between parent and offspring seems to be planted by nature -- not only among humans but among birds and most animals. Members of the same species feel a natural affinity for one another, and this is especially strong among human beings, which is why we praise those who love their fellow humans. We can see even in our travels how every person feels a kinship with every other.

Friendship also seems to hold political communities together, and lawmakers care about it even more than about justice. For concord -- which is something like friendship -- is what they most strive to achieve, while they seek above all to drive out faction, which is friendship's greatest enemy. When people are friends, they have no need of justice; but when they are merely just, they still need friendship as well. Indeed, the highest form of justice is thought to have a friendly quality to it.
D: Elegant:
Friendship is a virtue, or involves virtue, and is indispensable for a good life. No one would choose to live without friends, even possessing all other goods. The wealthy need friends to exercise generosity; the unfortunate need friends as refuge. Friendship helps the young avoid error, supports the old in weakness, and spurs the mature to noble action.

Nature implants this bond -- between parent and child, between members of the same kind, and especially among humans. Friendship holds states together; lawmakers prize it above justice itself. Concord resembles friendship, and faction is its enemy. When people are friends they need no justice, but when merely just they still need friendship. The truest justice has a friendly quality.
D: Summary: Friendship is essential to human life at every stage and social position. It is natural, found across species, and politically vital -- lawmakers value it even above justice, since friends have no need of justice while just people still need friendship.
D: Distilled: Friendship is indispensable to human flourishing, holding together both individual lives and political communities, and surpassing even justice in its binding power.
Notes: The Homer quotation 'two going together' is from Iliad X.224, where Diomedes asks for a companion on a night raid. -- The claim that friendship is more important than justice to lawmakers anticipates Aristotle's extensive parallel treatment of friendship and justice throughout Book VIII, especially VIII.9 (8.22-8.23). -- 'Concord' translates homonoia, a key political concept Aristotle develops further in IX.6. -- The discussion of friendship holding states together connects directly to Politics III, where Aristotle treats civic friendship (politike philia) as essential to the polis.

D: Original:
But it is not only necessary but also noble; for we praise those who love their friends, and it is thought to be a fine thing to have many friends; and again we think it is the same people that are good men and are friends.

Not a few things about friendship are matters of debate. Some define it as a kind of likeness and say like people are friends, whence come the sayings 'like to like', 'birds of a feather flock together', and so on; others on the contrary say 'two of a trade never agree'. On this very question they inquire for deeper and more physical causes, Euripides saying that 'parched earth loves the rain, and stately heaven when filled with rain loves to fall to earth', and Heraclitus that 'it is what opposes that helps' and 'from different tones comes the fairest tune' and 'all things are produced through strife'; while Empedocles, as well as others, expresses the opposite view that like aims at like. The physical problems we may leave alone (for they do not belong to the present inquiry); let us examine those which are human and involve character and feeling, e.g. whether friendship can arise between any two people or people cannot be friends if they are wicked, and whether there is one species of friendship or more than one. Those who think there is only one because it admits of degrees have relied on an inadequate indication; for even things different in species admit of degree. We have discussed this matter previously.
D: Refashioned:
Friendship is not only necessary but also noble, for we praise those who love their friends, and having many friends is considered admirable. Moreover, we tend to think that being a good person and being a good friend go together.

There is no shortage of debate about friendship. Some define it as a kind of likeness, claiming that like is drawn to like -- hence the proverbs 'like to like' and 'birds of a feather flock together.' Others take the opposite view, saying 'two of a trade never agree.' Some thinkers pursue deeper, more physical explanations: Euripides says that 'parched earth loves the rain, and stately heaven when filled with rain loves to fall to earth'; Heraclitus holds that 'it is what opposes that helps' and 'from different tones comes the fairest tune' and 'all things are produced through strife'; while Empedocles, along with others, argues the reverse -- that like aims at like.

The physical questions we may set aside, since they do not belong to the present inquiry. Let us instead examine those that concern human character and feeling: whether friendship can arise between any two people, or whether the wicked cannot be friends at all; and whether there is one kind of friendship or more than one. Those who maintain there is only one kind -- on the grounds that friendship admits of degrees -- rely on an insufficient indicator, for even things that differ in kind can admit of degree. This point we have addressed previously.
D: Elegant: Friendship is not only necessary but noble; we praise those who love their friends and assume good people make good friends. The nature of friendship is debated: some say like attracts like, others that opposites attract. Euripides, Heraclitus, and Empedocles each offer physical explanations. But setting aside natural philosophy, the human questions are: can any two people be friends, or only the good? And is there one species of friendship or several? Those who claim only one kind, because friendship admits of degrees, reason poorly -- even things different in kind admit of degree.
D: Summary: Friendship is both necessary and noble. Ancient thinkers debated whether like attracts like or opposites attract, but Aristotle sets aside these physical theories to focus on ethical questions: who can be friends, and how many kinds of friendship exist.
D: Distilled: Beyond being necessary, friendship is noble -- and the key ethical questions are who can truly be friends and whether friendship comes in distinct kinds.
Notes: Euripides (c. 480-406 BC): Athenian tragedian; the quotation is from a lost play, possibly the Chrysippus. -- Heraclitus (c. 535-475 BC): pre-Socratic philosopher of Ephesus, famous for the doctrine that conflict and opposition underlie all reality. -- Empedocles (c. 494-434 BC): pre-Socratic philosopher who taught that Love (philia) and Strife are the two cosmic forces; like elements are drawn together by Love. -- 'We have discussed this matter previously' likely refers to the discussion of degrees within genera in the Categories or to ethical discussions earlier in the Ethics. -- The debate between like-attracts-like and opposites-attract was a major theme in pre-Socratic natural philosophy, which Aristotle here deliberately brackets to keep the inquiry ethical.

D: Original: The kinds of friendship may perhaps be cleared up if we first come to know the object of love. For not everything seems to be loved but only the lovable, and this is good, pleasant, or useful; but it would seem to be that by which some good or pleasure is produced that is useful, so that it is the good and the useful that are lovable as ends. Do men love, then, the good, or what is good for them? These sometimes clash. So too with regard to the pleasant. Now it is thought that each loves what is good for himself, and that the good is without qualification lovable, and what is good for each man is lovable for him; but each man loves not what is good for him but what seems good. This however will make no difference; we shall just have to say that this is 'that which seems lovable'. Now there are three grounds on which people love; of the love of lifeless objects we do not use the word 'friendship'; for it is not mutual love, nor is there a wishing of good to the other (for it would surely be ridiculous to wish wine well; if one wishes anything for it, it is that it may keep, so that one may have it oneself); but to a friend we say we ought to wish what is good for his sake. But to those who thus wish good we ascribe only goodwill, if the wish is not reciprocated; goodwill when it is reciprocal being friendship. Or must we add 'when it is recognized'? For many people have goodwill to those whom they have not seen but judge to be good or useful; and one of these might return this feeling. These people seem to bear goodwill to each other; but how could one call them friends when they do not know their mutual feelings? To be friends, then, the must be mutually recognized as bearing goodwill and wishing well to each other for one of the aforesaid reasons.
D: Refashioned:
The kinds of friendship may become clearer if we first identify what is lovable. Not everything is loved -- only what is lovable, and the lovable is what is good, pleasant, or useful. But the useful appears to be that which produces some good or pleasure, so that strictly speaking only the good and the pleasant are lovable as ends.

Do people love what is truly good, or what is good for them? These sometimes conflict -- and so too with the pleasant. Each person is thought to love what is good for himself, and while the good is lovable without qualification, what is lovable for each person is what is good for him specifically. In practice, each person loves not what is actually good for him but what appears good. This need not trouble us; we simply note that 'the lovable' means 'what seems lovable.'

There are, then, three grounds for loving. We do not speak of 'friendship' toward lifeless objects, since there is no mutual affection involved, nor any wishing of good for the object's own sake. It would be absurd to wish wine well -- if we wish anything for it, it is that it keep so we can enjoy it. But we say we should wish good to a friend for his own sake. Those who wish good to another without reciprocation we credit only with goodwill; goodwill that is mutual constitutes friendship.

Or must we add a further condition -- that the goodwill be recognized? Many people bear goodwill toward those they have never met but judge to be good or useful, and the feeling may well be returned. Such people seem to have goodwill toward each other, yet how can we call them friends when neither knows of the other's feelings? To be friends, then, people must have mutually recognized goodwill and wish each other well for one of the reasons mentioned above.
D: Elegant:
To understand friendship, we must first identify what is lovable. Only the good, the pleasant, and the useful are lovable -- though the useful is really just what produces good or pleasure. Each person loves what seems good to him, giving us three grounds for love.

We do not speak of friendship toward lifeless things, since there is no mutual affection and no wishing good for the object's sake. Wishing good to someone without return is mere goodwill. When goodwill is mutual, it begins to resemble friendship -- but true friendship requires that each party recognize the other's goodwill. People who bear goodwill to strangers they have never met cannot be called friends. Friendship, then, requires mutually recognized goodwill grounded in goodness, pleasure, or utility.
D: Summary: There are three reasons people love: goodness, pleasure, and utility. Friendship differs from mere goodwill in requiring mutual and recognized well-wishing between the parties, not just one-sided feeling toward someone or fondness for things.
D: Distilled: Friendship requires mutually recognized goodwill between people who wish each other well for the sake of goodness, pleasure, or utility.
Notes: This passage establishes the tripartite division of the lovable (good, pleasant, useful) that structures the entire friendship discussion through Books VIII-IX. -- The distinction between what is 'good without qualification' and 'good for a particular person' echoes discussions in Book I and Book VII. -- The wine example (wishing wine well) is one of Aristotle's characteristic reductio illustrations. -- The requirement that goodwill be 'recognized' (gnorizomenon) adds an epistemic condition beyond mere feeling, distinguishing Aristotle's account from purely affective theories of friendship.

D: Original: Now these reasons differ from each other in kind; so, therefore, do the corresponding forms of love and friendship. There are therefore three kinds of friendship, equal in number to the things that are lovable; for with respect to each there is a mutual and recognized love, and those who love each other wish well to each other in that respect in which they love one another. Now those who love each other for their utility do not love each other for themselves but in virtue of some good which they get from each other. So too with those who love for the sake of pleasure; it is not for their character that men love ready-witted people, but because they find them pleasant. Therefore those who love for the sake of utility love for the sake of what is good for themselves, and those who love for the sake of pleasure do so for the sake of what is pleasant to themselves, and not in so far as the other is the person loved but in so far as he is useful or pleasant. And thus these friendships are only incidental; for it is not as being the man he is that the loved person is loved, but as providing some good or pleasure. Such friendships, then, are easily dissolved, if the parties do not remain like themselves; for if the one party is no longer pleasant or useful the other ceases to love him.
D: Refashioned:
Since these three reasons for loving differ in kind, so do the corresponding forms of love and friendship. There are therefore three kinds of friendship, matching the three things that are lovable. In each case, the love is mutual and recognized, and those who love each other wish each other well in the respect in which they love.

Those who love each other for utility do not love each other for who they are, but for the benefit each derives from the other. The same goes for those who love for the sake of pleasure: people enjoy the company of the witty not because of their character, but because they find them amusing. Thus those who love for utility love for the sake of what is good for themselves, and those who love for pleasure love for the sake of what is pleasant to themselves -- not insofar as the loved person is who he is, but insofar as he is useful or pleasant.

These friendships are therefore incidental: the person is loved not as the individual he is, but as someone who provides a certain good or pleasure. Such friendships dissolve easily, since if one party ceases to be pleasant or useful, the other ceases to love him.
D: Elegant: The three grounds for loving produce three kinds of friendship. Those who love for utility love not the person but the benefit; those who love for pleasure love not the character but the enjoyment. In both cases the person is loved incidentally -- not for who he is, but for what he provides. These friendships dissolve easily when one party ceases to be useful or pleasant.
D: Summary: The three reasons for loving -- goodness, pleasure, and utility -- produce three corresponding kinds of friendship. Friendships based on utility or pleasure are incidental, since the person is valued not for who they are but for what they provide, and they dissolve when the benefit ends.
D: Distilled: Friendships based on utility or pleasure are incidental and fragile, since the person is loved not for themselves but for the benefit they provide.
Notes: The term 'incidental' (kata sumbebekos) is a technical Aristotelian term meaning the friendship attaches to an accidental rather than essential quality of the person. -- This tripartite classification of friendship becomes one of the most influential frameworks in the history of ethics, widely discussed by Thomas Aquinas, Montaigne, and modern virtue ethicists. -- The argument that utility and pleasure friendships are 'not for the person himself' prefigures the Kantian distinction between treating persons as ends versus means, though Aristotle's framework differs significantly.

D: Original: Now the useful is not permanent but is always changing. Thus when the motive of the friendship is done away, the friendship is dissolved, inasmuch as it existed only for the ends in question. This kind of friendship seems to exist chiefly between old people (for at that age people pursue not the pleasant but the useful) and, of those who are in their prime or young, between those who pursue utility. And such people do not live much with each other either; for sometimes they do not even find each other pleasant; therefore they do not need such companionship unless they are useful to each other; for they are pleasant to each other only in so far as they rouse in each other hopes of something good to come. Among such friendships people also class the friendship of a host and guest. On the other hand the friendship of young people seems to aim at pleasure; for they live under the guidance of emotion, and pursue above all what is pleasant to themselves and what is immediately before them; but with increasing age their pleasures become different. This is why they quickly become friends and quickly cease to be so; their friendship changes with the object that is found pleasant, and such pleasure alters quickly. Young people are amorous too; for the greater part of the friendship of love depends on emotion and aims at pleasure; this is why they fall in love and quickly fall out of love, changing often within a single day. But these people do wish to spend their days and lives together; for it is thus that they attain the purpose of their friendship.
D: Refashioned:
The useful is not permanent but constantly changing. So when the reason for the friendship disappears, the friendship dissolves with it, since it existed only for those ends. This kind of friendship seems to arise chiefly among the elderly, who at that age pursue the useful rather than the pleasant, and among those in their prime or youth who are focused on practical advantage. Such people do not even spend much time together -- sometimes they do not find each other pleasant -- and so they have no need for regular companionship unless they are useful to each other. They find each other agreeable only insofar as they kindle hopes of some future benefit. The friendship between host and guest is also placed in this category.

The friendship of the young, by contrast, seems to aim at pleasure. Young people live under the sway of emotion and pursue above all what is immediately pleasant. But as they grow older their pleasures change, so they become friends quickly and fall out of friendship just as quickly -- their friendship shifts along with whatever they find pleasant, and such pleasures change rapidly. Young people are also prone to romantic love, since much of erotic friendship depends on emotion and aims at pleasure. This is why they fall in love and out of love so easily, often changing within a single day. Yet they do wish to spend their days and lives together, for that is how they fulfill the purpose of their friendship.
D: Elegant:
The useful is ever-changing, so utility friendships dissolve when the benefit ends. These arise chiefly among the old, who seek advantage over pleasure, and among the practically minded at any age. Such friends need not even enjoy each other's company.

Young people's friendships aim at pleasure. Living by emotion, they pursue what is immediately delightful -- but since their pleasures shift rapidly, they become friends quickly and part just as quickly. Romantic love belongs here too: driven by feeling and aimed at pleasure, it flares up and dies down within a day. Yet these friends desire to spend their lives together, which is the very purpose of their friendship.
D: Summary: Utility friendships are common among the elderly and dissolve when the advantage ends. Pleasure friendships are common among the young, who form and break bonds rapidly as their emotions shift. Romantic love, driven by feeling and aimed at pleasure, falls into this volatile category.
D: Distilled: Utility friendships belong to the old and dissolve with changing circumstances; pleasure friendships belong to the young and shift with changing emotions.
Notes: The friendship of host and guest (xenia) was a sacred institution in ancient Greece, governed by Zeus Xenios, involving reciprocal obligations of hospitality. -- Aristotle's association of youth with emotion-driven friendship reflects his broader developmental psychology, in which reason increasingly governs as one matures (see also Rhetoric II.12-14 on the characters of different ages). -- The observation that lovers 'change often within a single day' reflects Greek literary treatments of eros, especially in comedy and lyric poetry.

D: Original: Perfect friendship is the friendship of men who are good, and alike in virtue; for these wish well alike to each other qua good, and they are good themselves. Now those who wish well to their friends for their sake are most truly friends; for they do this by reason of own nature and not incidentally; therefore their friendship lasts as long as they are good-and goodness is an enduring thing. And each is good without qualification and to his friend, for the good are both good without qualification and useful to each other. So too they are pleasant; for the good are pleasant both without qualification and to each other, since to each his own activities and others like them are pleasurable, and the actions of the good are the same or like. And such a friendship is as might be expected permanent, since there meet in it all the qualities that friends should have. For all friendship is for the sake of good or of pleasure-good or pleasure either in the abstract or such as will be enjoyed by him who has the friendly feeling-and is based on a certain resemblance; and to a friendship of good men all the qualities we have named belong in virtue of the nature of the friends themselves; for in the case of this kind of friendship the other qualities also are alike in both friends, and that which is good without qualification is also without qualification pleasant, and these are the most lovable qualities. Love and friendship therefore are found most and in their best form between such men.
D: Refashioned:
Complete friendship is the friendship of people who are good and alike in virtue. Such friends wish each other well as good people, and they are good in themselves. Those who wish well to their friends for the friends' own sake are friends in the truest sense, for they feel this way because of their own character, not for any incidental reason. Their friendship therefore lasts as long as they remain good -- and goodness is an enduring quality.

Moreover, each is good both without qualification and to his friend, since the good are both genuinely good and mutually useful. They are also pleasant to each other, since good people take pleasure both in their own activities and in similar activities in others, and the actions of the good are the same or alike.

Such friendship is, as one would expect, permanent, because it combines all the qualities friends should have. Every friendship is for the sake of the good or the pleasant -- whether in the abstract or as experienced by the one who feels the affection -- and is grounded in a certain likeness. In the friendship of good people, all these qualities belong to the friends by virtue of their own nature: goodness, usefulness, pleasantness, and likeness are all present. What is good without qualification is also pleasant without qualification, and these are the most lovable qualities of all. Love and friendship are therefore found at their fullest and best between such people.
D: Elegant: Complete friendship exists between people who are good and alike in virtue. They wish each other well for the other's own sake, motivated by their own character rather than any incidental benefit. Since goodness endures, so does their friendship. Each is genuinely good, useful to the other, and pleasant -- since the good take pleasure in good activity, whether their own or their friend's. This friendship unites all the qualities that friendship requires: goodness, utility, pleasure, and likeness. What is unconditionally good is also unconditionally pleasant, making such friends the most lovable. Love and friendship reach their highest form between such people.
D: Summary: The highest friendship exists between virtuous people who love each other for their own sake. This friendship is permanent because goodness endures, and it naturally combines all three lovable qualities -- goodness, utility, and pleasure -- making it the most complete form of human bond.
D: Distilled: Perfect friendship exists between good people who love each other for their character itself, uniting goodness, pleasure, and utility in an enduring bond.
Notes: This is the central passage defining Aristotle's concept of teleia philia (complete/perfect friendship), one of the most discussed concepts in the history of ethics. -- The argument that goodness is 'enduring' (monimon) contrasts with the instability of pleasure and utility discussed in the previous sections. -- The claim that the good are 'pleasant to each other' connects to Book X's discussion of pleasure as completing virtuous activity. -- The friendship of virtue has influenced Christian theological discussions of caritas, Aquinas's treatment of friendship with God, and modern virtue ethics (e.g., Alasdair MacIntyre, Martha Nussbaum).

D: Original: But it is natural that such friendships should be infrequent; for such men are rare. Further, such friendship requires time and familiarity; as the proverb says, men cannot know each other till they have 'eaten salt together'; nor can they admit each other to friendship or be friends till each has been found lovable and been trusted by each. Those who quickly show the marks of friendship to each other wish to be friends, but are not friends unless they both are lovable and know the fact; for a wish for friendship may arise quickly, but friendship does not.
D: Refashioned:
But it is natural that such friendships should be rare, because such people are rare. Furthermore, this kind of friendship requires time and familiarity. As the proverb says, people cannot know each other until they have 'eaten salt together.' They cannot accept each other into friendship or be friends until each has shown himself to be lovable and has earned the other's trust.

Those who quickly display the signs of friendship to each other wish to be friends, but they are not yet friends unless both are truly lovable and each recognizes this in the other. A wish for friendship can arise quickly, but friendship itself cannot.
D: Elegant: Such friendships are naturally rare because virtuous people are rare. They also require time and familiarity -- as the proverb says, you must 'eat salt together' before you can know someone. Those who quickly show signs of friendship wish to be friends but are not yet friends until each has proven lovable and trustworthy. The desire for friendship arises quickly; friendship itself does not.
D: Summary: Perfect friendships are rare because virtuous people are rare, and even between good people friendship requires extended time and familiarity to develop. Quick affinity signals a wish for friendship, not friendship itself.
D: Distilled: True friendship is rare and slow to form, requiring time, familiarity, and mutual proof of character.
Notes: The proverb about 'eating salt together' (hals sunanalōsai) was widespread in the ancient Mediterranean world; salt was a symbol of shared meals and lasting bonds. -- The distinction between wishing for friendship (boulēsis philias) and actual friendship reinforces Aristotle's insistence on the conditions of mutuality and recognition established in 8.3. -- This passage is often cited in discussions of whether Aristotelian friendship is compatible with modern social conditions and the pace of contemporary life.
