These friendships involve the superiority of one party over the other, which is why ancestors are honored. The justice that exists between such persons is therefore not the same on both sides but is in every case proportioned to merit -- and the same is true of the friendship.

The friendship of husband and wife is the same as that found in an aristocracy: the better partner receives more of what is good, and each receives what befits them. The justice in these relations follows the same pattern.

The friendship of brothers is like that of comrades: they are equal and of similar age, and such people tend to be alike in their feelings and character. This also resembles the friendship appropriate to timocratic government, where the ideal is for citizens to be equal and fair, and therefore they rule in turn and on equal terms. The friendship appropriate to such a constitution corresponds accordingly.
D: Elegant: Ancestors too are honored for their superiority, as fathers naturally rule sons and kings rule subjects. Justice and friendship in these unequal relationships are proportioned to merit. Husband-wife friendship mirrors aristocracy: the worthier partner receives more, and each receives what befits them. Brotherhood resembles comradeship and timocracy: equals of similar age and character who rule in turn on equal terms.
D: Summary: In unequal relationships like parent-child or ruler-subject, both friendship and justice are proportioned to each party's merit. Husband-wife friendship mirrors aristocracy, while brotherhood mirrors the equal, turn-taking friendship of timocratic citizenship.
D: Distilled: Each form of friendship mirrors its political counterpart: unequal bonds demand proportional justice, while brotherly friendship mirrors the equal, reciprocal relations of timocratic citizens.
Notes: The principle that justice is 'not the same on both sides' in unequal relationships connects directly to the discussion of proportional justice in Book V.3-4. -- The analogy between comradeship (hetairia) and brotherhood reflects the importance of age-group associations in Greek social life. -- The timocratic ideal of ruling 'in turn' (en merei) is central to Aristotle's concept of political citizenship in Politics III.1, where he defines the citizen as one who shares in ruling and being ruled.

D: Original: But in the deviation-forms, as justice hardly exists, so too does friendship. It exists least in the worst form; in tyranny there is little or no friendship. For where there is nothing common to ruler and ruled, there is not friendship either, since there is not justice; e.g. between craftsman and tool, soul and body, master and slave; the latter in each case is benefited by that which uses it, but there is no friendship nor justice towards lifeless things. But neither is there friendship towards a horse or an ox, nor to a slave qua slave. For there is nothing common to the two parties; the slave is a living tool and the tool a lifeless slave. Qua slave then, one cannot be friends with him. But qua man one can; for there seems to be some justice between any man and any other who can share in a system of law or be a party to an agreement; therefore there can also be friendship with him in so far as he is a man. Therefore while in tyrannies friendship and justice hardly exist, in democracies they exist more fully; for where the citizens are equal they have much in common.
D: Refashioned:
In the deviant constitutions, friendship barely exists, just as justice barely exists. It is least present in the worst form. In tyranny there is little or no friendship, for where ruler and ruled have nothing in common, there can be no friendship -- just as there can be no justice. The relationship is like that between a craftsman and his tool, or between soul and body, or between master and slave: in each case the latter is benefited by the one who uses it, but there is no friendship or justice toward lifeless things.

There is no friendship toward a horse or an ox either, nor toward a slave insofar as he is a slave. For master and slave have nothing in common: the slave is a living tool and the tool a lifeless slave. Insofar as someone is a slave, then, one cannot be friends with him. But insofar as he is a human being, one can. For there seems to be some form of justice between any person and any other who can participate in a system of law or enter into agreements. Therefore there can also be friendship with a slave insofar as he is a human being.

Thus, while friendship and justice barely exist in tyrannies, they exist more fully in democracies, where citizens are equal and have much in common.
D: Elegant: In deviant constitutions, friendship exists barely, mirroring the scarcity of justice. Tyranny has the least friendship: where ruler and ruled share nothing, neither friendship nor justice is possible -- as between craftsman and tool, soul and body, master and slave. There is no friendship with lifeless things, animals, or slaves qua slaves, since there is nothing in common. But with a slave qua human being, friendship is possible, since justice exists between any persons who can share in law and agreements. Friendship and justice exist most fully in democracies, where citizens are equal and share much in common.
D: Summary: Friendship requires something in common between the parties. In tyranny, where ruler and ruled share nothing, friendship barely exists. A slave cannot be befriended as a slave but can as a human being. Democracy, where citizens are equal, produces the most friendship and justice.
D: Distilled: Friendship requires common ground between the parties -- tyranny destroys it by eliminating what ruler and ruled share, while democracy fosters it through civic equality.
Notes: The description of the slave as a 'living tool' (empsuchon organon) echoes Politics I.4 (1253b33). Aristotle's view of slavery is one of the most criticized aspects of his philosophy. -- The concession that a slave can be befriended 'qua human being' creates a tension with Aristotle's defense of natural slavery in Politics I.4-7. -- The soul-body analogy reflects Aristotle's hylomorphic metaphysics (De Anima II.1). -- The claim that democracy has the most friendship because of equality among citizens contrasts interestingly with Aristotle's generally more favorable view of aristocracy and mixed constitutions in the Politics. -- This passage has been important in debates about Aristotle's views on human dignity and universal moral status.

D: Original: Every form of friendship, then, involves association, as has been said. One might, however, mark off from the rest both the friendship of kindred and that of comrades. Those of fellow-citizens, fellow-tribesmen, fellow-voyagers, and the like are more like mere friendships of association; for they seem to rest on a sort of compact. With them we might class the friendship of host and guest. The friendship of kinsmen itself, while it seems to be of many kinds, appears to depend in every case on parental friendship; for parents love their children as being a part of themselves, and children their parents as being something originating from them.
D: Refashioned:
Every form of friendship, then, involves association, as we have said. One might, however, distinguish from the rest both the friendship of kindred and that of comrades. The friendships of fellow-citizens, fellow-tribesmen, and fellow-voyagers are more like friendships of association -- they seem to rest on a kind of agreement. The friendship between host and guest may be placed in this group.

The friendship of kinsmen itself, while it appears to take many forms, seems in every case to be rooted in parental friendship. Parents love their children as a part of themselves, and children love their parents as the source from which they originated.
D: Elegant: All friendship involves association, but kinship and comradeship stand apart from the rest. Civic, tribal, and travel friendships are mere friendships of association, resting on agreement -- as does the bond between host and guest. The many forms of kinship friendship all trace back to the parental bond: parents love children as parts of themselves, and children love parents as their origin.
D: Summary: All friendships involve some form of association, but kinship and comradeship are distinctive. Civic and professional friendships rest on agreements. All forms of kinship friendship ultimately derive from the foundational bond between parent and child.
D: Distilled: All kinship friendships trace back to the parental bond, in which parents love children as extensions of themselves and children love parents as their origin.
Notes: The distinction between friendship based on kinship (suggeneia) and friendship based on compact or agreement (sunthēkē) maps onto the distinction between natural and conventional justice in Book V.7. -- The friendship of host and guest (xenia) was one of the most sacred institutions in Greek life, protected by Zeus Xenios. -- The idea that parents love children 'as a part of themselves' is developed more fully in the next section and anticipates IX.7, where Aristotle argues that the producer's love for the product exceeds the reverse.

D: Original: Now (1) arents know their offspring better than there children know that they are their children, and (2) the originator feels his offspring to be his own more than the offspring do their begetter; for the product belongs to the producer (e.g. a tooth or hair or anything else to him whose it is), but the producer does not belong to the product, or belongs in a less degree. And (3) the length of time produces the same result; parents love their children as soon as these are born, but children love their parents only after time has elapsed and they have acquired understanding or the power of discrimination by the senses. From these considerations it is also plain why mothers love more than fathers do. Parents, then, love their children as themselves (for their issue are by virtue of their separate existence a sort of other selves), while children love their parents as being born of them, and brothers love each other as being born of the same parents; for their identity with them makes them identical with each other (which is the reason why people talk of 'the same blood', 'the same stock', and so on). They are, therefore, in a sense the same thing, though in separate individuals. Two things that contribute greatly to friendship are a common upbringing and similarity of age; for 'two of an age take to each other', and people brought up together tend to be comrades; whence the friendship of brothers is akin to that of comrades. And cousins and other kinsmen are bound up together by derivation from brothers, viz. by being derived from the same parents. They come to be closer together or farther apart by virtue of the nearness or distance of the original ancestor.
D: Refashioned:
There are three reasons why parents' love exceeds that of children. First, parents know their offspring better than children know that these adults are their parents. Second, the originator feels his offspring to be his own more than the offspring feel their begetter to be theirs -- the product belongs to the producer (as a tooth or a strand of hair belongs to the person whose it is), but the producer does not belong to the product, or belongs in a lesser degree. Third, time produces the same effect: parents love their children from the moment of birth, while children come to love their parents only after time has elapsed and they have developed understanding or at least the capacity of sensory recognition.

From these considerations it is also clear why mothers love more than fathers. Parents love their children as other selves, since their offspring are, by virtue of their separate existence, a kind of extension of themselves. Children love their parents as the beings from whom they were born. Brothers love each other as born from the same parents, for their shared identity with those parents makes them identical with each other -- hence people speak of 'the same blood,' 'the same stock,' and similar expressions. Brothers are, in a sense, the same thing existing in separate individuals.

Two things contribute greatly to friendship: a common upbringing and similarity of age. As the saying goes, 'two of an age take to each other,' and people raised together tend to become comrades. This is why the friendship of brothers resembles that of comrades. Cousins and other kinsmen are connected through their derivation from common ancestors -- from brothers and therefore from the same parents. They become closer or more distant according to the nearness or distance of the original ancestor.
D: Elegant:
Parents love children more than children love parents for three reasons: parents know their children better; the originator feels the product is his own more than the reverse; and time gives parents a head start in love. Mothers love most because of these factors. Parents love children as other selves -- extensions of their own being in separate existence. Children love parents as their source. Brothers share identity through common parents, hence 'same blood, same stock' -- they are the same thing in separate persons.

Common upbringing and similar age greatly foster friendship: 'two of an age take to each other.' Brotherhood thus resembles comradeship. Cousins and wider kin connect through shared ancestors, growing closer or more distant according to genealogical proximity.
D: Summary: Parents love children more than children love parents because they know them better, feel stronger ownership, and begin loving earlier. Parents see children as extensions of themselves, while brothers share identity through common origin. Common upbringing and similar age are powerful bonds, making brotherhood akin to comradeship.
D: Distilled: Parents love children more intensely because the originator's bond to the product is stronger than the reverse -- parents see children as other selves existing separately.
Notes: The argument that the producer loves the product more than vice versa is developed further in IX.7, where Aristotle compares it to the poet's love for his poems. -- The claim that mothers love more than fathers has been much discussed; Aristotle seems to ground it partly in greater certainty of parentage and partly in the mother's more direct role in bringing the child into being. -- 'Two of an age take to each other' is proverbial in Greek; cf. Homer, Odyssey XVII.218: 'god always brings like to like.' -- The analysis of kinship as degrees of shared identity through common ancestors anticipates modern discussions of kin selection in evolutionary biology, though Aristotle's framework is philosophical rather than biological.

D: Original: The friendship of children to parents, and of men to gods, is a relation to them as to something good and superior; for they have conferred the greatest benefits, since they are the causes of their being and of their nourishment, and of their education from their birth; and this kind of friendship possesses pleasantness and utility also, more than that of strangers, inasmuch as their life is lived more in common. The friendship of brothers has the characteristics found in that of comrades (and especially when these are good), and in general between people who are like each other, inasmuch as they belong more to each other and start with a love for each other from their very birth, and inasmuch as those born of the same parents and brought up together and similarly educated are more akin in character; and the test of time has been applied most fully and convincingly in their case.
D: Refashioned:
The friendship of children toward parents, and of human beings toward gods, is a relationship with something good and superior. Parents and gods have conferred the greatest benefits: they are the causes of our existence, our nourishment, and our education from birth. This kind of friendship also possesses both pleasantness and utility to a greater degree than friendship with strangers, since the lives of parents and children are more deeply intertwined.

The friendship of brothers has the same characteristics found in that of comrades -- and especially when the comrades are good -- and in general between people who are alike. Brothers belong to each other more closely and begin with a love for each other from their very birth. Those born of the same parents, raised together, and similarly educated are more alike in character. And the test of time has been applied most fully and convincingly in their case.
D: Elegant:
Children's friendship toward parents, like humans' toward gods, is directed at something good and superior. Parents and gods give the greatest benefits: existence, nourishment, and education. This bond surpasses stranger-friendship in both pleasure and utility because of the deeper intertwining of their lives.

Brothers share the virtues of comradeship -- especially when both are good -- with the added advantages of belonging to each other from birth, being alike in upbringing and character, and having their bond tested by the longest familiarity.
D: Summary: The bond between children and parents, like that between humans and gods, rests on gratitude for the greatest benefits -- existence, nourishment, and education. Brotherhood combines the qualities of comradeship with deeper natural bonds and longer-tested familiarity.
D: Distilled: The love of children for parents and of humans for gods is directed at benefactors of the highest order -- those responsible for our very existence and formation.
Notes: The parallel between parental and divine friendship is significant for Aristotle's theology; in Metaphysics XII, God is the unmoved mover who inspires love but may not reciprocate it, creating a philosophical puzzle about whether friendship with God is possible (see also 8.19). -- The emphasis on common upbringing (suntrophia) as a source of friendship reflects the importance Aristotle places on habituation in moral development (Book II). -- The 'test of time' criterion connects to the proverb about 'eating salt together' in 8.7.

D: Original:
Between other kinsmen friendly relations are found in due proportion. Between man and wife friendship seems to exist by nature; for man is naturally inclined to form couples-even more than to form cities, inasmuch as the household is earlier and more necessary than the city, and reproduction is more common to man with the animals. With the other animals the union extends only to this point, but human beings live together not only for the sake of reproduction but also for the various purposes of life; for from the start the functions are divided, and those of man and woman are different; so they help each other by throwing their peculiar gifts into the common stock. It is for these reasons that both utility and pleasure seem to be found in this kind of friendship. But this friendship may be based also on virtue, if the parties are good; for each has its own virtue and they will delight in the fact. And children seem to be a bond of union (which is the reason why childless people part more easily); for children are a good common to both and what is common holds them together.

How man and wife and in general friend and friend ought mutually to behave seems to be the same question as how it is just for them to behave; for a man does not seem to have the same duties to a friend, a stranger, a comrade, and a schoolfellow.
D: Refashioned:
Among other kinsmen, friendly relations are found in appropriate proportion. Between husband and wife, friendship seems to exist by nature. Human beings are naturally inclined to form couples -- even more than to form political communities, since the household is prior to and more necessary than the city, and reproduction is something humans share with all animals.

With other animals, the union extends only to reproduction. But human beings live together not only for the sake of having children but also for the various purposes of life. From the very start, their functions are divided: the tasks of husband and wife are different, and so they help each other by contributing their distinct abilities to the common stock. For these reasons, both utility and pleasure seem to be found in this kind of friendship. But this friendship may also be grounded in virtue, if both parties are good -- for each has a distinctive excellence, and they can take delight in that.

Children seem to serve as a bond of union, which is why childless couples part more easily. Children are a good shared by both, and what is held in common holds people together.

How husband and wife -- and friends in general -- ought to behave toward each other appears to be the same question as how it is just for them to behave. For a person clearly does not have the same duties toward a friend, a stranger, a comrade, and a schoolfellow.
D: Elegant: Kinsmen are friends in due proportion. Husband and wife are friends by nature: humans are inclined to pair even more than to form cities, since the household is prior to and more necessary than the state. Unlike other animals, humans live together not merely for reproduction but for all of life's purposes, dividing functions and pooling different abilities. This yields both utility and pleasure -- and virtue too, if both are good, since each has a distinctive excellence. Children bind couples together as a shared good; childless pairs separate more easily. How spouses and friends should treat each other is the same question as what justice requires between them, and these duties differ with each type of relationship.
D: Summary: Marriage is a natural friendship grounded in the complementary contributions of husband and wife, yielding utility, pleasure, and potentially virtue. Children strengthen the bond as a shared good. How friends should behave toward each other is fundamentally a question of justice, which varies by relationship.
D: Distilled: Marriage is the most natural human partnership, prior even to the city, yielding utility, pleasure, and virtue through the complementary contributions of husband and wife.
Notes: The claim that the household is 'earlier and more necessary' than the city echoes Politics I.2, though there Aristotle also argues the city is prior 'in nature' (by final cause). -- The idea that human coupling surpasses animal mating in its purposes reflects Aristotle's characteristic emphasis on humans' rational and social nature. -- The observation about childless couples parting more easily is an empirical claim about Greek social life, where marriage was closely tied to the production of legitimate heirs. -- The final remark about different duties to different relationships sets up the discussion of complaints and justice in friendship in VIII.13-14.

D: Original: There are three kinds of friendship, as we said at the outset of our inquiry, and in respect of each some are friends on an equality and others by virtue of a superiority (for not only can equally good men become friends but a better man can make friends with a worse, and similarly in friendships of pleasure or utility the friends may be equal or unequal in the benefits they confer). This being so, equals must effect the required equalization on a basis of equality in love and in all other respects, while unequals must render what is in proportion to their superiority or inferiority. Complaints and reproaches arise either only or chiefly in the friendship of utility, and this is only to be expected. For those who are friends on the ground of virtue are anxious to do well by each other (since that is a mark of virtue and of friendship), and between men who are emulating each other in this there cannot be complaints or quarrels; no one is offended by a man who loves him and does well by him-if he is a person of nice feeling he takes his revenge by doing well by the other. And the man who excels the other in the services he renders will not complain of his friend, since he gets what he aims at; for each man desires what is good. Nor do complaints arise much even in friendships of pleasure; for both get at the same time what they desire, if they enjoy spending their time together; and even a man who complained of another for not affording him pleasure would seem ridiculous, since it is in his power not to spend his days with him.
D: Refashioned:
There are three kinds of friendship, as we said at the beginning of our inquiry, and within each kind some friends are equals while others stand in a relationship of superiority. Not only can equally good people become friends, but a better person can also befriend a worse one. The same holds for friendships of pleasure or utility -- the friends may be equal or unequal in the benefits they provide.

This being so, equals must achieve the required balance on the basis of equality in love and in all other respects, while unequals must render what is proportional to their superiority or inferiority.

Complaints and reproaches arise either only or chiefly in friendships of utility, and this is to be expected. Those who are friends on the basis of virtue are eager to do well by each other, since this is a mark of both virtue and friendship. Between people who vie with each other in doing good, there can be no complaints or quarrels: no one is offended by someone who loves him and treats him well. If a person has any refinement of feeling, he takes his revenge by doing even more good in return. And the one who surpasses the other in the services he renders will not complain, since he gets what he aims at -- for each person desires what is good.

Nor do complaints arise much in friendships of pleasure. Both parties get what they want at the same time, if they enjoy spending time together. Anyone who complained that the other person was not giving him pleasure would seem ridiculous, since he is free to stop spending time with that person.
D: Elegant: Within each of the three kinds of friendship, friends may be equals or unequals. Equals must balance love equally; unequals must give proportionally to their standing. Complaints arise chiefly in utility friendships. Virtue friends eagerly do good for each other and cannot quarrel -- the refined person 'retaliates' by doing more good. The one who gives more is not aggrieved, since giving good is precisely what he wants. Pleasure friends rarely complain either, since both enjoy the shared time, and anyone dissatisfied is free to leave.
D: Summary: Within all three kinds of friendship, equals must balance love equally while unequals must give proportionally. Complaints arise mainly in utility friendships, since virtue friends compete in doing good and pleasure friends are satisfied by shared enjoyment.
D: Distilled: Complaints in friendship arise almost exclusively from utility, since virtue friends compete in generosity and pleasure friends are satisfied by shared enjoyment.
Notes: The idea that virtuous friends 'compete' in doing good (hamillōntai) presents an attractive picture of moral friendship as mutually elevating. -- The claim that the superior giver in a virtue friendship is not aggrieved because 'each person desires what is good' connects to the discussion of self-love in IX.8. -- This passage begins the treatment of complaints and dissolution in friendship, which continues through VIII.13-14 and is further developed in IX.1-3.

D: Original: But the friendship of utility is full of complaints; for as they use each other for their own interests they always want to get the better of the bargain, and think they have got less than they should, and blame their partners because they do not get all they 'want and deserve'; and those who do well by others cannot help them as much as those whom they benefit want.
D: Refashioned: The friendship of utility, however, is full of complaints. Since each party uses the other for his own benefit, each always wants to get the better of the bargain. Each thinks he is getting less than he deserves and blames his partner for not providing everything he wants and is owed. Meanwhile, those who do good for others cannot help their beneficiaries as much as those beneficiaries would like.
D: Elegant: Utility friendship breeds constant complaint. Each party seeks the better bargain, feels shortchanged, and blames the other for not providing enough. Benefactors can never fully satisfy those they help.
D: Summary: Utility friendships are plagued by complaints because each side wants to gain more than they give and feels they deserve more than they receive.
D: Distilled: Utility friendships breed constant grievance because each party measures the relationship by what they receive and inevitably feels shortchanged.
Notes: This brief but incisive observation captures the inherent instability of instrumental relationships. -- The dynamic described here connects to the discussion of exchange justice in Book V.5, where Aristotle analyzes how economic transactions require proportional reciprocity. -- The complaint dynamic anticipates modern economic and psychological analyses of transactional relationships.

D: Original: Now it seems that, as justice is of two kinds, one unwritten and the other legal, one kind of friendship of utility is moral and the other legal. And so complaints arise most of all when men do not dissolve the relation in the spirit of the same type of friendship in which they contracted it. The legal type is that which is on fixed terms; its purely commercial variety is on the basis of immediate payment, while the more liberal variety allows time but stipulates for a definite quid pro quo. In this variety the debt is clear and not ambiguous, but in the postponement it contains an element of friendliness; and so some states do not allow suits arising out of such agreements, but think men who have bargained on a basis of credit ought to accept the consequences. The moral type is not on fixed terms; it makes a gift, or does whatever it does, as to a friend; but one expects to receive as much or more, as having not given but lent; and if a man is worse off when the relation is dissolved than he was when it was contracted he will complain. This happens because all or most men, while they wish for what is noble, choose what is advantageous; now it is noble to do well by another without a view to repayment, but it is the receiving of benefits that is advantageous. Therefore if we can we should return the equivalent of what we have received (for we must not make a man our friend against his will; we must recognize that we were mistaken at the first and took a benefit from a person we should not have taken it from-since it was not from a friend, nor from one who did it just for the sake of acting so-and we must settle up just as if we had been benefited on fixed terms). Indeed, one would agree to repay if one could (if one could not, even the giver would not have expected one to do so); therefore if it is possible we must repay. But at the outset we must consider the man by whom we are being benefited and on what terms he is acting, in order that we may accept the benefit on these terms, or else decline it.
D: Refashioned:
It seems that, just as justice is of two kinds -- one unwritten and the other legal -- so too there are two kinds of utility friendship: one moral and the other legal. Complaints arise most of all when people do not dissolve the relationship in the same spirit as the one in which they formed it.

The legal type operates on fixed terms. Its purely commercial variety requires immediate payment, while the more generous variety allows time but stipulates a definite exchange. In the latter, the debt is clear and unambiguous, though the deferral contains an element of friendliness. This is why some states do not permit lawsuits arising from such agreements, holding that those who bargain on credit must accept the consequences.

The moral type operates without fixed terms. A person gives -- or does whatever he does -- as though giving to a friend, but he expects to receive as much or more in return, as though he had lent rather than given. If he finds himself worse off when the relationship ends than when it began, he will complain. This happens because everyone, while wishing for what is noble, chooses what is advantageous. It is noble to benefit another without thought of repayment, but it is advantageous to receive benefits.

Therefore, if we can, we should return the equivalent of what we have received. We must not make someone our friend against his will; rather, we should recognize that we were mistaken at the outset and accepted a benefit from someone we should not have -- since it was not from a true friend, nor from someone acting purely for the sake of giving -- and we must settle up as if we had agreed to fixed terms. Indeed, one would agree to repay if one could -- and if one could not, even the giver would not have expected repayment.

So if it is possible, we must repay. But at the outset we should consider who is benefiting us and on what terms, so that we may either accept the benefit on those terms or decline it.
D: Elegant:
Just as justice divides into written and unwritten, utility friendship divides into legal and moral. Complaints arise when parties dissolve relationships in a different spirit from how they formed them. Legal utility friendship has fixed terms -- either immediate exchange or deferred payment with a clear debt. Moral utility friendship has no fixed terms: one gives as to a friend but expects equal or greater return, treating gifts as loans. When the giver ends up worse off, he complains.

This happens because people wish for the noble but choose the advantageous. Giving without expecting return is noble; receiving benefits is advantageous. We should repay what we receive -- recognizing our error if we accepted from the wrong person -- and settle as if terms had been fixed. At the outset, we should carefully consider who benefits us and on what terms, so we can accept or decline accordingly.
D: Summary: Utility friendship has two forms: legal (with fixed terms of exchange) and moral (without fixed terms, where gifts are implicitly treated as loans). Complaints arise when parties disagree about which type they were in. We should repay benefits when possible, and should carefully consider the terms on which we accept them.
D: Distilled: Most disputes in friendship arise from confusion between gift and loan -- when one party gives as to a friend but expects repayment as from a debtor.
Notes: The distinction between written and unwritten justice echoes Book V.7's discussion of natural versus conventional justice. -- The analysis of moral versus legal utility friendship is one of the most practically oriented passages in the Ethics, dealing with the everyday problems of obligation and expectation. -- Some Greek states, including Athens, had variable policies about enforcing informal credit agreements, as Aristotle notes. -- The psychological observation that people 'wish for what is noble but choose what is advantageous' is a recurring theme in Aristotle's moral psychology, related to the discussion of weakness of will (akrasia) in Book VII. -- This analysis is continued and refined in IX.1-2.

D: Original: It is disputable whether we ought to measure a service by its utility to the receiver and make the return with a view to that, or by the benevolence of the giver. For those who have received say they have received from their benefactors what meant little to the latter and what they might have got from others-minimizing the service; while the givers, on the contrary, say it was the biggest thing they had, and what could not have been got from others, and that it was given in times of danger or similar need. Now if the friendship is one that aims at utility, surely the advantage to the receiver is the measure. For it is he that asks for the service, and the other man helps him on the assumption that he will receive the equivalent; so the assistance has been precisely as great as the advantage to the receiver, and therefore he must return as much as he has received, or even more (for that would be nobler). In friendships based on virtue on the other hand, complaints do not arise, but the purpose of the doer is a sort of measure; for in purpose lies the essential element of virtue and character.
D: Refashioned:
It is a matter of dispute whether we should measure a service by its value to the receiver and calculate repayment accordingly, or by the generosity of the giver. Those who have received benefits tend to say that what they received meant little to the giver and could have been obtained from others -- thus minimizing the service. The givers, on the contrary, claim it was the greatest thing they had, that it could not have been obtained from anyone else, and that it was given in times of danger or similar need.

If the friendship is one that aims at utility, then surely the benefit to the receiver is the proper measure. The receiver is the one who asked for the service, and the giver helps on the assumption that he will receive an equivalent return. The assistance has been exactly as great as the advantage to the receiver, and so he should return as much as he received -- or even more, since that would be nobler.

In friendships based on virtue, by contrast, complaints do not arise, and the purpose of the giver serves as a kind of measure. For it is in purpose that the essential element of virtue and character resides.
D: Elegant: Should a service be measured by its value to the receiver or the sacrifice of the giver? Receivers minimize: 'it cost you little and I could have gotten it elsewhere.' Givers maximize: 'it was my greatest possession, given in your hour of need.' In utility friendship, the receiver's benefit is the proper measure, since the giver helped expecting equivalent return -- so the receiver should repay accordingly, or more if he can. In virtue friendship, complaints do not arise: the giver's intention is the measure, since purpose is the essence of virtue and character.
D: Summary: In utility friendships, services should be measured by their value to the receiver, since the giver expects equivalent return. In virtue friendships, the giver's intention is what matters, since character is defined by purpose rather than outcomes.
D: Distilled: In utility friendships the measure of a service is its value to the receiver; in virtue friendships the measure is the intention of the giver.
Notes: The debate about measuring services from giver's versus receiver's perspective remains live in moral philosophy and in discussions of gift economies versus market economies. -- The claim that 'purpose' (prohairesis) is the essential element of virtue and character is one of Aristotle's most important ethical claims, emphasized in Book II.4 and III.2-3. -- This passage is closely connected to the fuller treatment of repayment obligations in IX.2, where Aristotle discusses cases like repaying a benefactor versus helping a friend. -- The rhetorical strategies of minimizers and maximizers that Aristotle describes reflect the Greek forensic tradition familiar from the law courts.

D: Original: Differences arise also in friendships based on superiority; for each expects to get more out of them, but when this happens the friendship is dissolved. Not only does the better man think he ought to get more, since more should be assigned to a good man, but the more useful similarly expects this; they say a useless man should not get as much as they should, since it becomes an act of public service and not a friendship if the proceeds of the friendship do not answer to the worth of the benefits conferred. For they think that, as in a commercial partnership those who put more in get more out, so it should be in friendship. But the man who is in a state of need and inferiority makes the opposite claim; they think it is the part of a good friend to help those who are in need; what, they say, is the use of being the friend of a good man or a powerful man, if one is to get nothing out of it?
D: Refashioned:
Disagreements also arise in friendships based on superiority. Each party expects to receive more from the friendship, and when this expectation is not met, the friendship dissolves. The superior in virtue thinks he should receive more, since more should be assigned to a good person. The more useful party similarly thinks he deserves more -- arguing that a useless person should not receive an equal share, since the relationship becomes a public service rather than a friendship if its returns do not match the worth of the contributions.

They reason that friendship should work like a business partnership: those who invest more should earn more. But the person in need or in an inferior position makes the opposite claim: it is the very purpose of friendship with a good or powerful person to provide help. What use is it, they ask, to be the friend of someone good or powerful if you get nothing from it?
D: Elegant: In friendships of superiority, each side claims more. The virtuous person argues he deserves more because goodness merits greater reward. The useful person argues the same -- otherwise the relationship is charity, not friendship. Both reason like business partners: greater contribution should yield greater return. But the needy and inferior counter: the whole point of befriending someone good or powerful is to receive help. What use is such a friend if you gain nothing?
D: Summary: Friendships between unequals breed disagreements because each side expects to receive more. The superior claims greater return for greater worth or contribution; the inferior claims that helping the needy is the very purpose of friendship with the powerful.
D: Distilled: Friendships between unequals breed conflict because the superior expects reward proportional to worth while the inferior expects help proportional to need.
Notes: The business partnership analogy (koinōnia) connects to the broader discussion of commercial justice in Book V.5. -- The tension between merit-based and need-based claims on friendship resources foreshadows modern debates in distributive justice between desert-based and needs-based principles. -- This passage sets up the resolution in VIII.14 (8.38), where Aristotle argues that each party should receive more of different things. -- The problem of unequal friendships was already raised in VIII.7 (8.18-8.19) and receives its fullest treatment here.

D: Original: At all events it seems that each party is justified in his claim, and that each should get more out of the friendship than the other-not more of the same thing, however, but the superior more honour and the inferior more gain; for honour is the prize of virtue and of beneficence, while gain is the assistance required by inferiority.
D: Refashioned: At any rate, it seems that each party is justified in his claim, and that each should indeed receive more from the friendship than the other -- but not more of the same thing. The superior party should receive more honor, while the inferior should receive more material gain. Honor is the fitting reward for virtue and beneficence, while gain is the assistance that inferiority requires.
D: Elegant: Both sides are justified: each should receive more -- but of different things. The superior should receive more honor, the fitting reward for virtue and beneficence. The inferior should receive more material gain, the assistance their position requires.
D: Summary: The solution to disputes in unequal friendships is that each party should receive more, but of different things: the superior receives more honor and the inferior receives more material benefit.
D: Distilled: In unequal friendships, the superior rightly receives more honor while the inferior rightly receives more material help -- each getting more of what they need.
Notes: This elegant resolution parallels the structure of distributive justice in Book V.3, where Aristotle argues that equality is achieved through proportion rather than identical shares. -- The distinction between honor (timē) and gain (kerdos) as different currencies of social exchange is fundamental to Greek ethical thinking and anticipates modern discussions of multiple forms of social capital. -- This solution applies to all the unequal relationships discussed in VIII.7 and VIII.10-11: parent-child, ruler-subject, husband-wife.

D: Original: It seems to be so in constitutional arrangements also; the man who contributes nothing good to the common stock is not honoured; for what belongs to the public is given to the man who benefits the public, and honour does belong to the public. It is not possible to get wealth from the common stock and at the same time honour. For no one puts up with the smaller share in all things; therefore to the man who loses in wealth they assign honour and to the man who is willing to be paid, wealth, since the proportion to merit equalizes the parties and preserves the friendship, as we have said. This then is also the way in which we should associate with unequals; the man who is benefited in respect of wealth or virtue must give honour in return, repaying what he can. For friendship asks a man to do what he can, not what is proportional to the merits of the case; since that cannot always be done, e.g. in honours paid to the gods or to parents; for no one could ever return to them the equivalent of what he gets, but the man who serves them to the utmost of his power is thought to be a good man. This is why it would not seem open to a man to disown his father (though a father may disown his son); being in debt, he should repay, but there is nothing by doing which a son will have done the equivalent of what he has received, so that he is always in debt. But creditors can remit a debt; and a father can therefore do so too. At the same time it is thought that presumably no one would repudiate a son who was not far gone in wickedness; for apart from the natural friendship of father and son it is human nature not to reject a son's assistance. But the son, if he is wicked, will naturally avoid aiding his father, or not be zealous about it; for most people wish to get benefits, but avoid doing them, as a thing unprofitable.-So much for these questions.
D: Refashioned:
The same principle seems to hold in constitutional arrangements. The person who contributes nothing good to the common stock receives no honor, for what belongs to the public is given to whoever benefits the public, and honor is a public good. One cannot simultaneously extract wealth from the common fund and also receive public honor. No one tolerates getting the lesser share in everything, so the one who loses in wealth is compensated with honor, and the one who is willing to accept payment receives wealth instead. Distribution according to merit equalizes the parties and preserves friendship, as we have said.

This is also how we should associate with unequals. The person who is benefited in terms of wealth or virtue must give honor in return, repaying what he can. Friendship asks a person to do what he can, not what is strictly proportional to what he has received -- since strict proportionality is not always possible. Consider the honors paid to the gods or to parents: no one could ever return to them the equivalent of what he has received, but the person who serves them to the utmost of his ability is considered a good person.

This is why it would seem wrong for a son to disown his father, though a father may disown his son. The son, being in debt, should repay. But nothing a son can do would be equivalent to what he has received, so he remains always in debt. Creditors, however, can forgive a debt, and a father can therefore do so as well.

At the same time, it is generally assumed that no one would disown a son unless that son had sunk deeply into wickedness. Apart from the natural friendship between father and son, it is simply human nature not to reject a son's help. But the son, if he is wicked, will naturally avoid helping his father, or will not do so eagerly -- for most people wish to receive benefits but avoid providing them, seeing generosity as unprofitable.

Let this suffice for our discussion of these questions.
D: Elegant:
The same principle governs constitutional arrangements. Public honor goes to those who benefit the community; no one gets both maximum wealth and maximum honor from the common stock. Distribution according to merit equalizes and preserves friendship.

With unequals, the beneficiary should return honor for what he can. Friendship demands what is possible, not strict equivalence -- for some debts can never be fully repaid. No one can return to gods or parents the equivalent of existence, nourishment, and education, but serving them to the utmost of one's ability suffices.

A son should not disown his father, being permanently in debt; but a father may disown a son, as creditors may forgive debts. Yet normally no one would abandon a son unless he were deeply wicked -- natural affection and self-interest both resist it. A wicked son, however, may avoid helping his father, since most people prefer receiving benefits to giving them.
D: Summary: In political life and personal relationships alike, honor compensates those who contribute, while material support goes to those in need. Some debts -- to gods and parents -- can never be fully repaid, so we owe them whatever we can give. A son may not disown his father, being always in debt, but a father may forgive that debt by disowning a wicked son.
D: Distilled: Some debts can never be fully repaid -- especially those to parents and gods -- so friendship requires not strict equivalence but the best one can give.
Notes: The claim that public honor should go to public benefactors connects to the discussion of megaloprepeia (magnificence) in Book IV.2 and to the general theory of distributive justice in Book V.3. -- The asymmetry between father's right to disown and son's duty not to reflects Athenian law, where fathers had considerable legal authority (patria potestas was even more extreme in Roman law). -- The comparison of parental-filial duty to debts owed the gods connects to the broader Greek concept of piety (eusebeia) and the earlier discussion in 8.31. -- The closing observation that 'most people wish to receive benefits but avoid providing them' is characteristically Aristotelian in its sober assessment of common human motivation. -- This passage concludes Book VIII; the discussion of friendship continues directly in Book IX, which treats the dissolution of friendships, duties to different friends, and the relationship between self-love and friendship.

D: Original: In all friendships between dissimilars it is, as we have said, proportion that equalizes the parties and preserves the friendship; e.g. in the political form of friendship the shoemaker gets a return for his shoes in proportion to his worth, and the weaver and all other craftsmen do the same. Now here a common measure has been provided in the form of money, and therefore everything is referred to this and measured by this; but in the friendship of lovers sometimes the lover complains that his excess of love is not met by love in return though perhaps there is nothing lovable about him), while often the beloved complains that the lover who formerly promised everything now performs nothing. Such incidents happen when the lover loves the beloved for the sake of pleasure while the beloved loves the lover for the sake of utility, and they do not both possess the qualities expected of them. If these be the objects of the friendship it is dissolved when they do not get the things that formed the motives of their love; for each did not love the other person himself but the qualities he had, and these were not enduring; that is why the friendships also are transient. But the love of characters, as has been said, endures because it is self-dependent. Differences arise when what they get is something different and not what they desire; for it is like getting nothing at all when we do not get what we aim at; compare the story of the person who made promises to a lyre-player, promising him the more, the better he sang, but in the morning, when the other demanded the fulfilment of his promises, said that he had given pleasure for pleasure. Now if this had been what each wanted, all would have been well; but if the one wanted enjoyment but the other gain, and the one has what he wants while the other has not, the terms of the association will not have been properly fulfilled; for what each in fact wants is what he attends to, and it is for the sake of that that that he will give what he has.
D: Refashioned:
In all friendships between unlike people, as we have said, it is proportional exchange that equalizes the parties and preserves the friendship. In political or transactional friendship, for instance, the shoemaker receives payment for his shoes in proportion to their worth, and the weaver and every other craftsman does the same. Here money has been established as a common measure, and so everything is referred to it and measured by it.

But in the friendship of lovers, sometimes the lover complains that his intense love is not returned in kind -- though perhaps there is nothing lovable about him -- while often the beloved complains that the lover, who once promised everything, now delivers nothing. Such things happen when the lover loves the beloved for the sake of pleasure, while the beloved loves the lover for the sake of utility, and neither actually possesses what the other expected. When these are the grounds of the friendship, it dissolves once those qualities disappear, since neither loved the other person as such, but only the qualities the other had -- and those were not enduring. That is why such friendships are also transient. But the love grounded in character, as we have said, endures because it depends on what each person truly is.

Differences arise when people receive something other than what they desired, for getting something other than what we aimed at is like getting nothing at all. Consider the story of the man who made promises to a lyre-player, pledging him greater rewards the better he sang; but in the morning, when the player demanded fulfillment of the promises, the man said he had already given pleasure in return for pleasure. If that had been what both wanted, all would have been well. But if one wanted enjoyment while the other wanted profit, and only one got what he wanted, the terms of the relationship have not been properly fulfilled. For what each person actually wants is what he attends to, and it is for the sake of that that he will give what he has.
D: Elegant: In friendships between unlike people, proportional exchange preserves the relationship. Money serves as a common measure in transactional friendships, but in romantic and personal relationships, conflicts arise when the parties seek different things -- one seeking pleasure, the other utility. When neither loves the other person as such but only certain qualities, the friendship dissolves once those qualities fade. Only friendship grounded in character endures, because it depends on who each person truly is. When people receive something other than what they desired, it is as if they received nothing at all, for each person gives only for the sake of what he himself wants in return.
D: Summary: Friendships between unlike people survive through proportional exchange. When lovers or friends seek different things from the relationship -- one wanting pleasure, the other utility -- conflict arises and the friendship dissolves once those qualities fade. Only character-based friendship endures because it is not dependent on transient qualities.
D: Distilled: Friendships between dissimilar people endure only when there is proportional exchange -- and only character-based friendship is truly stable, since it does not depend on fleeting qualities.
Notes: Continues the analysis of unequal friendships from Book VIII.8-14. -- The lyre-player anecdote is otherwise unattested and may be a common fable used in philosophical schools. -- The distinction between loving a person vs. loving their qualities is central to Aristotle's friendship typology (VIII.3-4). -- 'Political friendship' (politike philia) refers to civic/commercial relationships based on exchange, not modern political friendship. -- The principle that proportional equality preserves friendship draws on the mathematical proportion theory from Book V on justice.

D: Original: But who is to fix the worth of the service; he who makes the sacrifice or he who has got the advantage? At any rate the other seems to leave it to him. This is what they say Protagoras used to do; whenever he taught anything whatsoever, he bade the learner assess the value of the knowledge, and accepted the amount so fixed. But in such matters some men approve of the saying 'let a man have his fixed reward'. Those who get the money first and then do none of the things they said they would, owing to the extravagance of their promises, naturally find themselves the objects of complaint; for they do not fulfil what they agreed to. The sophists are perhaps compelled to do this because no one would give money for the things they do know. These people then, if they do not do what they have been paid for, are naturally made the objects of complaint.
D: Refashioned:
But who should determine the worth of the service -- the one who makes the sacrifice or the one who receives the benefit? At any rate, the other party seems to leave it to the receiver. This is what they say Protagoras used to do: whenever he taught any subject, he would tell the student to assess how much the knowledge was worth to him, and then accepted whatever amount the student named.

But in such matters, some people prefer the principle "let a man have his fixed reward agreed upon in advance." Those who take their payment first and then fail to deliver what they promised -- because their promises were extravagant -- naturally become objects of complaint, for they do not fulfill their agreement. The sophists are perhaps forced to operate this way, because no one would pay money for what they actually know. When people fail to deliver what they have been paid for, they naturally invite complaint.
D: Elegant: Who should determine the value of a service -- the giver or the receiver? Protagoras let his students set the price of his teaching. Some prefer fixed fees agreed in advance. Those who take payment first but fail to deliver what they promised rightly invite complaint. The sophists must rely on advance payment because no one would pay for their knowledge after receiving it.
D: Summary: The question of who should set the value of a service is difficult. Protagoras let students assess the worth of his teaching themselves. Those who demand payment upfront but fail to deliver -- as the sophists often must -- rightly face complaints.
D: Distilled: The value of a service should ideally be set by the receiver, but those who take payment before delivering must fulfill what they promised or face justified complaint.
Notes: Protagoras of Abdera (c. 490-420 BC): the most famous of the sophists, known for the doctrine 'man is the measure of all things'; Plato's dialogue Protagoras depicts his approach to teaching. -- Aristotle's critique of the sophists here is gentler than in Sophistical Refutations, where he attacks their methods more directly. -- The 'fixed reward' saying may echo Hesiod's Works and Days on fair dealing. -- The question of pricing knowledge foreshadows debates about commodifying education that persist to this day.

D: Original: But where there is no contract of service, those who give up something for the sake of the other party cannot (as we have said) be complained of (for that is the nature of the friendship of virtue), and the return to them must be made on the basis of their purpose (for it is purpose that is the characteristic thing in a friend and in virtue). And so too, it seems, should one make a return to those with whom one has studied philosophy; for their worth cannot be measured against money, and they can get no honour which will balance their services, but still it is perhaps enough, as it is with the gods and with one's parents, to give them what one can.
D: Refashioned:
But where there is no contract for services, those who give something for the sake of the other person cannot, as we have said, be justly complained of -- for that is the very nature of virtue-based friendship -- and the return to them must be made on the basis of their intention, for it is intention that is the defining feature of both a friend and a virtuous person.

This seems to be the right approach, too, toward those with whom one has studied philosophy: their worth cannot be measured in money, and no amount of honor could balance what they have given. Yet it is perhaps enough, as with the gods and one's parents, to give them what one can.
D: Elegant: In virtue-based friendships with no service contract, there can be no legitimate complaint, since the giver acts from good intention. Returns should match the giver's purpose, not a market price. Those who teach us philosophy, like gods and parents, deserve more than we can ever repay -- yet giving what we can is enough.
D: Summary: In friendships based on virtue with no explicit agreement, the giver cannot be rightfully complained about. The return should match the giver's intention. Teachers of philosophy, like gods and parents, deserve more than can ever be repaid, but giving what one can suffices.
D: Distilled: Virtue-based friendship requires no contract -- returns should match the giver's intention, and toward those who have given us wisdom, as toward gods and parents, we can only give what we are able.
Notes: The comparison of philosophy teachers with gods and parents reflects the deep reverence for the teacher-student relationship in Greek culture, also seen in Plato's Republic and Laws. -- 'Purpose' (prohairesis) is Aristotle's key ethical concept, central to his account of virtue in Books II-III; here it defines the essential feature of friendship. -- The idea that some debts can never be fully repaid connects to the discussion of gratitude and honor in Book IV.

D: Original: If the gift was not of this sort, but was made with a view to a return, it is no doubt preferable that the return made should be one that seems fair to both parties, but if this cannot be achieved, it would seem not only necessary that the person who gets the first service should fix the reward, but also just; for if the other gets in return the equivalent of the advantage the beneficiary has received, or the price lie would have paid for the pleasure, he will have got what is fair as from the other.
D: Refashioned: If the gift was not of this sort -- that is, if it was made with an expectation of return -- then ideally the return should be one that seems fair to both parties. But if that cannot be achieved, it would seem not only necessary but also just for the person who receives the first service to determine the repayment. For if the beneficiary returns the equivalent of the advantage he received, or the price he would have paid for that pleasure, he will have received what is fair from the other person.
D: Elegant: When a gift is given with expectation of return, the repayment should ideally satisfy both parties. If agreement cannot be reached, the recipient of the benefit should set the terms, returning the equivalent of what the service was worth to him.
D: Summary: When a gift is made with an expectation of return and the parties cannot agree on fair repayment, the person who received the benefit should determine the value, returning the equivalent of the advantage gained.
D: Distilled: When return is expected for a gift, the recipient should set the repayment at the value the benefit was actually worth to him.
Notes: This passage develops the principle of corrective justice from Book V.4, applying it to informal exchanges between friends. -- The idea that the beneficiary determines repayment follows from the asymmetry of valuation discussed in the next passage (9.5).

D: Original: We see this happening too with things put up for sale, and in some places there are laws providing that no actions shall arise out of voluntary contracts, on the assumption that one should settle with a person to whom one has given credit, in the spirit in which one bargained with him. The law holds that it is more just that the person to whom credit was given should fix the terms than that the person who gave credit should do so. For most things are not assessed at the same value by those who have them and those who want them; each class values highly what is its own and what it is offering; yet the return is made on the terms fixed by the receiver. But no doubt the receiver should assess a thing not at what it seems worth when he has it, but at what he assessed it at before he had it.
D: Refashioned:
We see this happening with goods put up for sale too. In some places, there are even laws providing that no legal actions can arise from voluntary contracts, on the assumption that one should settle with a person to whom one extended credit in the same spirit as when one originally made the bargain. The law holds that it is more just for the person who received credit to set the terms than for the person who extended it.

For most things are not valued the same way by those who already have them and those who want them: each party overvalues what is its own and what it is offering. Yet the return is made on the terms set by the receiver. But the receiver should assess the thing not at what it seems worth once he already has it, but at what he valued it before he had it.
D: Elegant: Some laws prohibit legal claims from voluntary contracts, holding that disputes should be settled in the spirit of the original agreement. The receiver rather than the giver should set the terms of repayment. People overvalue what they have and what they offer, so the receiver must assess the benefit not at its value after receipt, but at what he judged it worth beforehand.
D: Summary: In voluntary exchanges, the receiver should set the terms of repayment. Since people tend to overvalue what they already possess, the fair assessment is what the receiver judged the benefit to be worth before receiving it, not after.
D: Distilled: Fair repayment requires the receiver to assess a benefit at the value he placed on it before receiving it, not after -- since possession distorts judgment.
Notes: The principle that possession distorts valuation anticipates what behavioral economists now call the 'endowment effect.' -- The reference to laws governing voluntary contracts may reflect Athenian commercial law or practices in other Greek cities. -- This passage applies principles from Book V's discussion of justice in exchange (V.5) to the context of friendship.

D: Original: A further problem is set by such questions as, whether one should in all things give the preference to one's father and obey him, or whether when one is ill one should trust a doctor, and when one has to elect a general should elect a man of military skill; and similarly whether one should render a service by preference to a friend or to a good man, and should show gratitude to a benefactor or oblige a friend, if one cannot do both.
D: Refashioned: A further problem is raised by questions like these: Should one always defer to one's father and obey him in all things, or should one trust a doctor when ill, and elect a man of military skill when choosing a general? Similarly, should one do a favor for a friend in preference to a good man, and should one show gratitude to a benefactor rather than assist a friend, when one cannot do both?
D: Elegant: Difficult questions arise about competing obligations: Must one always obey one's father, or trust a doctor when ill and a military expert when choosing a general? Should one favor a friend over a good man, or repay a benefactor rather than help a friend, when both cannot be done?
D: Summary: Aristotle raises the problem of conflicting obligations: whether to always obey one's father, defer to expertise, or prioritize friends over benefactors when one cannot satisfy both duties simultaneously.
D: Distilled: When obligations to parents, friends, benefactors, and experts conflict, one faces a genuine ethical dilemma about whom to prioritize.
Notes: This aporetic passage sets up the discussion of competing claims that runs through IX.2. -- The tension between filial duty and other obligations is a recurring theme in Greek ethics; compare Socrates' argument in the Crito about obeying one's parents and the city. -- The examples (doctor, general) connect to Aristotle's frequent analogy between practical expertise and ethical judgment.

D: Original: All such questions are hard, are they not, to decide with precision? For they admit of many variations of all sorts in respect both of the magnitude of the service and of its nobility necessity. But that we should not give the preference in all things to the same person is plain enough; and we must for the most part return benefits rather than oblige friends, as we must pay back a loan to a creditor rather than make one to a friend. But perhaps even this is not always true; e.g. should a man who has been ransomed out of the hands of brigands ransom his ransomer in return, whoever he may be (or pay him if he has not been captured but demands payment) or should he ransom his father? It would seem that he should ransom his father in preference even to himself. As we have said, then, generally the debt should be paid, but if the gift is exceedingly noble or exceedingly necessary, one should defer to these considerations. For sometimes it is not even fair to return the equivalent of what one has received, when the one man has done a service to one whom he knows to be good, while the other makes a return to one whom he believes to be bad. For that matter, one should sometimes not lend in return to one who has lent to oneself; for the one person lent to a good man, expecting to recover his loan, while the other has no hope of recovering from one who is believed to be bad. Therefore if the facts really are so, the demand is not fair; and if they are not, but people think they are, they would be held to be doing nothing strange in refusing. As we have often pointed out, then, discussions about feelings and actions have just as much definiteness as their subject-matter.
D: Refashioned:
All such questions are difficult to decide with precision, are they not? For they admit of many variations in both the magnitude and the nobility or necessity of the service. But it is clear enough that we should not give the preference in all things to the same person, and that we must generally repay benefits received rather than do favors for friends -- just as we must repay a loan to a creditor before making a loan to a friend.

But perhaps even this is not always true. For example, should a man who has been ransomed from brigands ransom his rescuer in return, whoever that person may be -- or pay him back if the rescuer has not himself been captured but simply requests repayment -- or should he instead ransom his own father? It would seem that he should ransom his father in preference even to himself.

As we have said, then, generally the debt should be paid. But if the alternative gift is exceedingly noble or exceedingly necessary, one should defer to those considerations. For sometimes it is not even fair to return the equivalent of what one has received, as when one man has done a service to someone he knows to be virtuous, while the other must make a return to someone he believes to be wicked. Indeed, sometimes one should not even lend in return to someone who has lent to oneself, for the original lender lent to a good man expecting repayment, while the other has no hope of recovering from someone believed to be dishonest. If the facts truly are as described, the demand is not fair; and even if they are not, but people believe them to be so, those who refuse would not be thought to be acting strangely.

As we have often pointed out, then, discussions about feelings and actions can only be as precise as their subject matter allows.
D: Elegant: Questions of competing obligations resist precise answers due to variations in magnitude and necessity. Generally one should repay debts before doing new favors -- but not always. A man should ransom his father over his rescuer if he must choose. When the alternative is exceedingly noble or necessary, it takes priority over strict repayment. Context matters: returning an equivalent is not always fair, since the moral character of the parties affects the justice of the exchange. As always, discussions about feelings and actions can only be as precise as their subject matter permits.
D: Summary: While debts should generally be repaid before doing new favors, exceptions arise when an alternative action is exceedingly noble or necessary -- such as ransoming one's father over one's rescuer. The moral character of the parties involved also affects what counts as fair. Ethical discussions about such matters can only be as precise as the subject allows.
D: Distilled: Debts should generally be repaid first, but when an alternative action is far nobler or more necessary, it can rightly take priority -- for ethical judgment must be sensitive to context.
Notes: The ransom scenario was a real concern in the ancient Mediterranean, where piracy and brigandage were common. -- 'Discussions about feelings and actions have just as much definiteness as their subject-matter' restates the methodological principle from Book I.3 (1094b12-27) about the precision appropriate to ethics. -- The flexibility Aristotle demands here contrasts with more rigid deontological approaches that would emerge later.

D: Original: That we should not make the same return to every one, nor give a father the preference in everything, as one does not sacrifice everything to Zeus, is plain enough; but since we ought to render different things to parents, brothers, comrades, and benefactors, we ought to render to each class what is appropriate and becoming. And this is what people seem in fact to do; to marriages they invite their kinsfolk; for these have a part in the family and therefore in the doings that affect the family; and at funerals also they think that kinsfolk, before all others, should meet, for the same reason. And it would be thought that in the matter of food we should help our parents before all others, since we owe our own nourishment to them, and it is more honourable to help in this respect the authors of our being even before ourselves; and honour too one should give to one's parents as one does to the gods, but not any and every honour; for that matter one should not give the same honour to one's father and one's mother, nor again should one give them the honour due to a philosopher or to a general, but the honour due to a father, or again to a mother. To all older persons, too, one should give honour appropriate to their age, by rising to receive them and finding seats for them and so on; while to comrades and brothers one should allow freedom of speech and common use of all things. To kinsmen, too, and fellow-tribesmen and fellow-citizens and to every other class one should always try to assign what is appropriate, and to compare the claims of each class with respect to nearness of relation and to virtue or usefulness. The comparison is easier when the persons belong to the same class, and more laborious when they are different. Yet we must not on that account shrink from the task, but decide the question as best we can.
D: Refashioned:
That we should not give every person the same treatment, nor give our father the preference in everything -- just as we do not sacrifice everything to Zeus -- is clear enough. But since we owe different things to parents, brothers, companions, and benefactors, we should render to each class what is appropriate and fitting.

And this is what people actually seem to do. To weddings they invite their relatives, since relatives share in the family and therefore in the affairs that concern the family. At funerals too, people think that relatives above all should attend, for the same reason. In the matter of sustenance, it would seem we should help our parents before all others, since we owe our own nourishment to them, and it is more honorable to help the authors of our being in this way -- even before ourselves.

Honor too one should give to one's parents, as one does to the gods -- but not any and every honor. One should not give the same honor to one's father as to one's mother, nor should one give them the honor due to a philosopher or a general, but rather the honor appropriate to a father or a mother respectively.

To all older persons one should give the honor appropriate to their age -- rising to receive them, offering them seats, and so on. With companions and brothers, one should allow freedom of speech and share everything in common. To relatives, fellow tribesmen, fellow citizens, and every other group, one should always try to give what is appropriate, comparing the claims of each class with respect to closeness of relationship, virtue, and usefulness. The comparison is easier when the people belong to the same class, and more difficult when they are from different classes. Yet we must not on that account shrink from the task, but decide the question as best we can.
D: Elegant:
We do not give every person the same treatment, nor do we sacrifice everything to Zeus. Different relationships demand different things: relatives are invited to weddings and funerals, parents deserve our support in sustenance above all, and honor should be given as appropriate to each role -- not the same honor to a father as to a general or philosopher.

Older persons deserve respect suited to their age; companions deserve frank speech and shared resources. With all groups, we should assign what is appropriate by weighing closeness of relationship, virtue, and usefulness. Comparing claims across different classes of relationship is harder than within them, but we must not shrink from the task.
D: Summary: Different relationships require different forms of respect and support. Parents deserve priority in sustenance, each person deserves honor appropriate to their role, and claims should be weighed by closeness, virtue, and usefulness. Though comparing obligations across different types of relationships is difficult, we must make the best judgment we can.
D: Distilled: We owe different things to different people -- parents, friends, elders, fellow citizens -- and must render to each what is appropriate to their particular relationship and role.
Notes: The comparison with Zeus illustrates the Greek principle that even divine worship has proper limits and forms. -- The mention of inviting kinsfolk to marriages and funerals reflects Athenian social customs, where family participation in such events had legal and religious significance. -- The categories (parents, brothers, companions, fellow tribesmen, fellow citizens) map onto the concentric circles of Greek social organization. -- This passage anticipates the Stoic theory of oikeiosis (circles of concern), later developed by Hierocles.
