D: Original: Another question that arises is whether friendships should or should not be broken off when the other party does not remain the same. Perhaps we may say that there is nothing strange in breaking off a friendship based on utility or pleasure, when our friends no longer have these attributes. For it was of these attributes that we were the friends; and when these have failed it is reasonable to love no longer. But one might complain of another if, when he loved us for our usefulness or pleasantness, he pretended to love us for our character. For, as we said at the outset, most differences arise between friends when they are not friends in the spirit in which they think they are. So when a man has deceived himself and has thought he was being loved for his character, when the other person was doing nothing of the kind, he must blame himself; when he has been deceived by the pretences of the other person, it is just that he should complain against his deceiver; he will complain with more justice than one does against people who counterfeit the currency, inasmuch as the wrongdoing is concerned with something more valuable.
D: Refashioned:
Another question arises: Should friendships be broken off when the other party changes and is no longer the same person? Perhaps we can say that there is nothing strange about ending a friendship based on utility or pleasure when our friends no longer possess those qualities. For it was those qualities that made us friends, and when they disappear, it is reasonable to stop caring for the person.

But one might justly complain of someone who, while actually loving us for our usefulness or pleasantness, pretended to love us for our character. For, as we said at the outset, most conflicts between friends arise when the friendship is not of the kind they believe it to be. So when a person has deceived himself into thinking he was loved for his character, when the other was doing nothing of the sort, he must blame himself. But when he has been deceived by the other person's pretenses, it is right that he should complain against the deceiver -- and with more justice than one complains against counterfeiters of currency, since the wrongdoing involves something far more valuable.
D: Elegant: There is nothing wrong with ending a friendship based on utility or pleasure once those qualities disappear -- we were friends with those qualities, not the person as such. But one may justly complain against someone who pretended to love us for our character while really valuing only our usefulness or pleasantness. Most conflicts arise when friends misunderstand what kind of friendship they have. Deceiving someone about the basis of friendship is worse than counterfeiting currency, since what is at stake is more valuable.
D: Summary: Friendships based on utility or pleasure may reasonably end when those qualities fade. But pretending to love someone for their character while actually valuing their usefulness is a form of deception worse than counterfeiting, because what is at stake -- genuine friendship -- is more valuable than money.
D: Distilled: It is reasonable to end a friendship when its basis -- utility or pleasure -- disappears, but feigning character-based friendship while pursuing mere advantage is a deception worse than counterfeiting currency.
Notes: The counterfeiting analogy is striking: debasing coinage was a serious offense in Greek city-states, and Aristotle uses it to emphasize that friendship fraud is even worse. -- The passage recalls Aristotle's distinction among three types of friendship in VIII.3. -- 'Most differences arise between friends when they are not friends in the spirit in which they think they are' is one of Aristotle's most psychologically acute observations about relationships.

D: Original: But if one accepts another man as good, and he turns out badly and is seen to do so, must one still love him? Surely it is impossible, since not everything can be loved, but only what is good. What is evil neither can nor should be loved; for it is not one's duty to be a lover of evil, nor to become like what is bad; and we have said that like is dear like. Must the friendship, then, be forthwith broken off? Or is this not so in all cases, but only when one's friends are incurable in their wickedness? If they are capable of being reformed one should rather come to the assistance of their character or their property, inasmuch as this is better and more characteristic of friendship. But a man who breaks off such a friendship would seem to be doing nothing strange; for it was not to a man of this sort that he was a friend; when his friend has changed, therefore, and he is unable to save him, he gives him up.
D: Refashioned:
But what if one accepts another person as good, and that person turns out badly and is seen to be so -- must one still love him? Surely it is impossible, since not everything can be loved, but only what is good. What is evil neither can nor should be loved, for it is not one's duty to be a lover of evil, nor to make oneself like what is bad. And we have said that like is drawn to like.

Must the friendship, then, be immediately broken off? Or not in all cases, but only when one's friend is incurable in his wickedness? If a friend is capable of being reformed, one should rather come to the aid of his character or his material situation, since this is better and more characteristic of true friendship. But a person who ends such a friendship would not seem to be doing anything strange, for it was not a person of this sort that he befriended. When a friend has changed and one is unable to save him, one lets him go.
D: Elegant: If a friend turns out to be bad, must one still love him? No -- only what is good can be loved, and one should not become like what is bad. But the friendship need not be immediately dissolved: if the friend can be reformed, one should try to help his character, since that is what true friendship demands. Only when the friend is incurably wicked is it right to let him go, for the friendship was with a different person than who he has become.
D: Summary: When a friend turns bad, we are not obligated to continue loving him, since only what is good deserves love. However, if reform is possible, a true friend should try to help. Only when the friend is incurably wicked should the friendship be dissolved -- because the person one befriended no longer exists.
D: Distilled: A true friend tries to help a friend who has gone astray, but when someone becomes incurably wicked, the person one befriended has effectively ceased to exist, and letting go is justified.
Notes: The principle that 'like is drawn to like' (to homoion to homoio philon) echoes a traditional Greek maxim found in Plato's Lysis 214a and elsewhere. -- The distinction between curable and incurable vice connects to Aristotle's discussion of voluntary vs. involuntary action in Book III. -- This passage has been influential in Christian ethics regarding the obligation to 'hate the sin but love the sinner.'

D: Original: But if one friend remained the same while the other became better and far outstripped him in virtue, should the latter treat the former as a friend? Surely he cannot. When the interval is great this becomes most plain, e.g. in the case of childish friendships; if one friend remained a child in intellect while the other became a fully developed man, how could they be friends when they neither approved of the same things nor delighted in and were pained by the same things? For not even with regard to each other will their tastes agree, and without this (as we saw) they cannot be friends; for they cannot live together. But we have discussed these matters.
D: Refashioned: But what if one friend remained the same while the other became better and far surpassed him in virtue -- should the more virtuous one still treat the other as a friend? Surely not -- at least not in the fullest sense. When the gap becomes great, this is most obvious. Consider childhood friendships: if one friend remained a child in intellect while the other became a fully developed person, how could they be friends when they neither approve of the same things nor take delight in and are pained by the same things? They will not even agree in their feelings toward each other, and without that, as we saw, they cannot be friends -- for they cannot live together. But we have already discussed these matters.
D: Elegant: When one friend grows far beyond the other in virtue, the friendship cannot be maintained in its original form. This is clearest with childhood friendships: if one person matures while the other remains childish in understanding, they can no longer share the same values, pleasures, or pains -- and without these shared experiences, friendship and shared life become impossible.
D: Summary: When one friend significantly outgrows the other in virtue and understanding, the friendship cannot be sustained. Without shared values, pleasures, and the ability to live together, the basis for friendship disappears.
D: Distilled: Friendship requires rough equality in virtue -- when one person far outgrows the other, the shared values and pleasures that sustain friendship inevitably disappear.
Notes: Cross-reference to Book VIII.5 (1157a-b) on the requirement that friends share pleasures and activities. -- The childhood friendship example illustrates Aristotle's developmental view of human nature, central to his ethical theory. -- The claim that friends must be able to 'live together' (suzēn) is one of the key criteria of friendship repeatedly emphasized in Books VIII-IX.

D: Original: Should he, then, behave no otherwise towards him than he would if he had never been his friend? Surely he should keep a remembrance of their former intimacy, and as we think we ought to oblige friends rather than strangers, so to those who have been our friends we ought to make some allowance for our former friendship, when the breach has not been due to excess of wickedness.
D: Refashioned: Should one, then, treat a former friend no differently than someone who was never a friend at all? Surely not. One should preserve a remembrance of the former intimacy. And just as we think we ought to do more for friends than for strangers, so too we ought to make some allowance for those who were once our friends, on account of that former friendship -- provided the break was not caused by extreme wickedness.
D: Elegant: A former friend should not be treated as a mere stranger. The memory of past intimacy deserves respect, and we owe some special consideration to those who were once our friends -- unless the breach was caused by extreme wickedness.
D: Summary: Former friends deserve more consideration than strangers. Past intimacy creates a lasting obligation to show some goodwill, unless the friendship ended due to extreme moral failure.
D: Distilled: Past friendship creates an enduring claim to goodwill -- former friends deserve more than strangers, unless extreme wickedness caused the break.
Notes: This brief passage completes the discussion of dissolving friendships (IX.3) with a characteristically Aristotelian sense of moderation: even ended friendships leave moral residue. -- The qualification about 'excess of wickedness' connects to the curable/incurable distinction in 9.10.

D: Original: Friendly relations with one's neighbours, and the marks by which friendships are defined, seem to have proceeded from a man's relations to himself. For (1) we define a friend as one who wishes and does what is good, or seems so, for the sake of his friend, or (2) as one who wishes his friend to exist and live, for his sake; which mothers do to their children, and friends do who have come into conflict. And (3) others define him as one who lives with and (4) has the same tastes as another, or (5) one who grieves and rejoices with his friend; and this too is found in mothers most of all. It is by some one of these characterstics that friendship too is defined.
D: Refashioned: The friendly feelings we have toward our neighbors, and the marks by which friendships are defined, seem to have originated from a person's relationship to himself. For we define a friend as (1) one who wishes and does what is good, or what seems good, for the sake of his friend; or (2) one who wishes his friend to exist and live, for the friend's own sake -- which is what mothers feel toward their children, and what friends feel who have come into conflict with each other. Others define a friend as (3) one who spends time with another and (4) shares the same tastes, or (5) one who grieves and rejoices with his friend -- and this too is found most of all in mothers. It is by some one of these characteristics that friendship itself is defined.
D: Elegant: Friendly feelings toward others originate from one's relationship with oneself. A friend is defined as someone who: wishes and does good for the other's sake; wishes the other to exist and live; spends time together; shares the same tastes; or grieves and rejoices together. These qualities are found most purely in mothers toward their children, and each captures an essential aspect of friendship.
D: Summary: Aristotle identifies five defining marks of friendship -- wishing good for another, wanting them to exist, spending time together, sharing tastes, and sharing emotions -- and argues these all originate from one's relationship with oneself. Mothers exemplify these qualities most fully.
D: Distilled: The defining marks of friendship -- wishing good, wanting the other to exist, sharing time and tastes, and sharing emotions -- all derive from one's relationship with oneself.
Notes: This passage begins IX.4, one of the most important chapters in the Ethics, which grounds friendship in self-relation. -- The five criteria of friendship listed here become the framework for the subsequent argument. -- The emphasis on mothers as exemplars of friendship is notable in the context of ancient Greek philosophy, which often marginalizes women's experiences. -- Compare Plato's Lysis for a different approach to defining friendship.

D: Original: Now each of these is true of the good man's relation to himself (and of all other men in so far as they think themselves good; virtue and the good man seem, as has been said, to be the measure of every class of things). For his opinions are harmonious, and he desires the same things with all his soul; and therefore he wishes for himself what is good and what seems so, and does it (for it is characteristic of the good man to work out the good), and does so for his own sake (for he does it for the sake of the intellectual element in him, which is thought to be the man himself); and he wishes himself to live and be preserved, and especially the element by virtue of which he thinks. For existence is good to the virtuous man, and each man wishes himself what is good, while no one chooses to possess the whole world if he has first to become some one else (for that matter, even now God possesses the good); he wishes for this only on condition of being whatever he is; and the element that thinks would seem to be the individual man, or to be so more than any other element in him. And such a man wishes to live with himself; for he does so with pleasure, since the memories of his past acts are delightful and his hopes for the future are good, and therefore pleasant. His mind is well stored too with subjects of contemplation. And he grieves and rejoices, more than any other, with himself; for the same thing is always painful, and the same thing always pleasant, and not one thing at one time and another at another; he has, so to speak, nothing to repent of.
D: Refashioned:
Now each of these characteristics is true of the good person's relationship to himself -- and of other people insofar as they believe themselves to be good. Virtue and the good person seem, as we have said, to be the standard by which everything of this kind is measured.

For the good person's judgments are harmonious, and he desires the same things with his whole soul. Therefore he wishes for himself what is good and what appears good, and he does it -- for it is characteristic of the good person to work out the good -- and he does so for his own sake, that is, for the sake of the thinking part of himself, which is considered to be the person's true self. He wishes himself to live and to be preserved, and especially that element in him by which he thinks. For existence is good for the virtuous person, and everyone wishes for himself what is good -- though no one would choose to possess the whole world at the price of becoming someone else (for as things stand, God already possesses the good). Rather, each person wishes for good things on the condition of remaining whoever he is. And the element that thinks would seem to be the individual person, or to be so more than any other element in him.

Such a person also wishes to spend time with himself, and does so with pleasure, since the memories of his past actions are delightful and his hopes for the future are good -- and therefore pleasant. His mind is well stocked, too, with subjects for contemplation. And he grieves and rejoices with himself more than anyone else does, for the same thing is always painful to him and the same thing always pleasant -- not one thing at one time and another at another. He has, so to speak, nothing to regret.
D: Elegant:
Every mark of friendship applies to the good person's relation to himself. His judgments are harmonious and his desires unified; he wishes and pursues the good for the sake of his rational self -- which is his truest self. He wishes to live and be preserved, especially the thinking element in him, since existence is good for the virtuous. No one would choose to possess everything at the cost of becoming someone else.

The good person enjoys his own company because his memories are pleasant, his hopes are good, and his mind is rich in contemplation. He grieves and rejoices consistently, always responding the same way to the same things. He has nothing to regret.
D: Summary: All five marks of friendship apply to the good person's relationship with himself. His soul is unified, he pursues the good for the sake of his rational nature, and he enjoys his own company because his memories are pleasant, his hopes bright, and his responses consistent. The thinking element is the truest self, and no one would choose all the goods in the world at the price of becoming someone else.
D: Distilled: The good person is a friend to himself: his soul is unified, his rational nature is his true self, and he enjoys his own company because he has nothing to regret.
Notes: The identification of the 'thinking element' (to nooun) with the true self is one of Aristotle's most important claims, developed further in Book X.7-8 where contemplation is identified as the highest happiness. -- The argument that no one would choose to become someone else to gain all goods connects to personal identity -- a theme later central in Locke and Parfit. -- 'God already possesses the good' reflects Aristotle's theology of the unmoved mover (Metaphysics XII). -- The unity of the good person's soul contrasts with the divided soul of the akratic (incontinent) person discussed in Book VII.

D: Original: Therefore, since each of these characteristics belongs to the good man in relation to himself, and he is related to his friend as to himself (for his friend is another self), friendship too is thought to be one of these attributes, and those who have these attributes to be friends. Whether there is or is not friendship between a man and himself is a question we may dismiss for the present; there would seem to be friendship in so far as he is two or more, to judge from the afore-mentioned attributes of friendship, and from the fact that the extreme of friendship is likened to one's love for oneself.
D: Refashioned:
Therefore, since each of these characteristics of friendship belongs to the good person in relation to himself, and since he relates to his friend as to himself -- for a friend is another self -- friendship too is thought to involve one or more of these qualities, and those who possess them are thought to be friends.

Whether there can be friendship between a person and himself is a question we may set aside for now. There would seem to be such friendship insofar as a person is, in a sense, two or more parts -- to judge from the characteristics of friendship we have described, and from the fact that the highest degree of friendship is compared to one's love for oneself.
D: Elegant: Since the marks of friendship all belong to the good person's self-relation, and since a friend is another self, friendship is essentially an extension of self-love. Whether one can truly befriend oneself remains an open question, but insofar as a person contains multiple parts, something like self-friendship seems possible -- and the highest friendship is indeed compared to self-love.
D: Summary: Since all the defining characteristics of friendship describe the good person's relationship with himself, and a friend is 'another self,' friendship is an extension of self-relation. The deepest friendship mirrors self-love.
D: Distilled: A friend is another self, and friendship is ultimately an extension of the good person's unified, harmonious relationship with himself.
Notes: The phrase 'another self' (allos autos, or heteros autos) is one of the most famous formulations in the Ethics and has been enormously influential in Western thought. -- The puzzle about self-friendship arises from the soul's composite nature (rational and non-rational parts, as discussed in Book I.13). -- This concept anticipates Hegel's analysis of recognition and the self-other dialectic.

D: Original: But the attributes named seem to belong even to the majority of men, poor creatures though they may be. Are we to say then that in so far as they are satisfied with themselves and think they are good, they share in these attributes? Certainly no one who is thoroughly bad and impious has these attributes, or even seems to do so. They hardly belong even to inferior people; for they are at variance with themselves, and have appetites for some things and rational desires for others. This is true, for instance, of incontinent people; for they choose, instead of the things they themselves think good, things that are pleasant but hurtful; while others again, through cowardice and laziness, shrink from doing what they think best for themselves. And those who have done many terrible deeds and are hated for their wickedness even shrink from life and destroy themselves. And wicked men seek for people with whom to spend their days, and shun themselves; for they remember many a grevious deed, and anticipate others like them, when they are by themselves, but when they are with others they forget. And having nothing lovable in them they have no feeling of love to themselves. Therefore also such men do not rejoice or grieve with themselves; for their soul is rent by faction, and one element in it by reason of its wickedness grieves when it abstains from certain acts, while the other part is pleased, and one draws them this way and the other that, as if they were pulling them in pieces. If a man cannot at the same time be pained and pleased, at all events after a short time he is pained because he was pleased, and he could have wished that these things had not been pleasant to him; for bad men are laden with repentance.
D: Refashioned:
But the qualities we described seem to belong even to the majority of people, flawed though they may be. Do we say, then, that insofar as they are satisfied with themselves and believe themselves good, they share in these qualities? Certainly no one who is thoroughly bad and impious possesses them, or even appears to.

These qualities scarcely belong even to inferior people, for they are at war with themselves. They have appetites pulling them toward some things and rational desires toward others. This is the case, for instance, with people who lack self-control: they choose what is merely pleasant but harmful instead of what they themselves judge to be good. Others, through cowardice and laziness, shrink from doing what they believe is best for themselves.

Those who have committed many terrible deeds and are hated for their wickedness even shrink from life and destroy themselves. Wicked people seek out the company of others and avoid being alone, for when by themselves they remember many grievous deeds and anticipate more like them, but in company they can forget. Having nothing lovable in themselves, they feel no love for themselves.

Such people do not even share in their own joys and griefs, for their soul is torn by internal conflict: one part, by reason of its wickedness, grieves when it abstains from certain acts, while another part is pleased; one element pulls them this way and another pulls them that, as if tearing them apart. If a person cannot feel pain and pleasure at the same time, at the very least he soon feels pain because he felt pleasure, and wishes those things had never been pleasant to him. For bad people are weighed down with regret.
D: Elegant:
The marks of friendship with oneself barely belong to ordinary people and not at all to the thoroughly wicked. Inferior people are at war with themselves: their appetites pull one way, their reason another. The incontinent choose harmful pleasures over what they know to be good; the cowardly and lazy fail to do what they believe is best.

The worst become so self-alienated that they shrink from life itself. They avoid solitude because it forces them to face their own deeds, and seek company to forget. Having nothing lovable in themselves, they cannot love themselves. Their soul is torn by faction -- one part grieving while another rejoices -- and they are weighed down with regret.
D: Summary: The self-friendship that characterizes the good person is absent in the wicked. Bad people are internally divided: their appetites conflict with their reason, they avoid solitude because it confronts them with their own deeds, and they feel no genuine self-love. Their souls are torn by faction, and they are burdened with constant regret.
D: Distilled: The wicked person cannot be a friend to himself because his soul is at war -- divided between appetite and reason, haunted by past deeds, and burdened with regret.
Notes: The description of the wicked person's inner conflict is one of Aristotle's most powerful psychological portraits. -- The reference to incontinent (akratic) people connects to the extensive discussion in Book VII.1-10. -- 'Their soul is rent by faction' uses political language (stasis = civil strife), consistent with Aristotle's analogy between the soul and the city. -- The observation that the wicked 'shrink from life and destroy themselves' may reflect awareness of suicide in the Greek world, discussed also in Book V.11 (1138a). -- Compare Plato's Republic IV (435-441) on the tripartite soul and inner conflict.

D: Original: Therefore the bad man does not seem to be amicably disposed even to himself, because there is nothing in him to love; so that if to be thus is the height of wretchedness, we should strain every nerve to avoid wickedness and should endeavour to be good; for so and only so can one be either friendly to oneself or a friend to another.
D: Refashioned: Therefore the bad person does not seem to be on friendly terms even with himself, because there is nothing in him to love. If being in such a condition is the height of wretchedness, we should strain every nerve to avoid wickedness and endeavor to be good. For only in this way can one be either a friend to oneself or a friend to another.
D: Elegant: The bad person cannot befriend even himself, since there is nothing in him to love. If this self-alienation is the height of misery, we must strive to be good -- for only goodness makes possible friendship with oneself and with others.
D: Summary: The bad person cannot love himself because there is nothing lovable in him. Since this inner alienation is the worst form of wretchedness, becoming good is essential -- it is the only path to friendship with oneself and with others.
D: Distilled: Only the good person can be a friend to himself, and self-friendship is the necessary foundation for all other friendship.
Notes: This passage concludes IX.4 with a powerful exhortation that links self-love, virtue, and friendship into a single ethical imperative. -- The argument that wickedness is self-defeating (one cannot even be a friend to oneself) is distinctively Aristotelian and contrasts with purely external accounts of the harm of vice. -- Compare Plato's Gorgias (469-479) where Socrates argues that doing wrong harms the wrongdoer more than anyone.

D: Original: Goodwill is a friendly sort of relation, but is not identical with friendship; for one may have goodwill both towards people whom one does not know, and without their knowing it, but not friendship. This has indeed been said already.' But goodwill is not even friendly feeling. For it does not involve intensity or desire, whereas these accompany friendly feeling; and friendly feeling implies intimacy while goodwill may arise of a sudden, as it does towards competitors in a contest; we come to feel goodwill for them and to share in their wishes, but we would not do anything with them; for, as we said, we feel goodwill suddenly and love them only superficially.
D: Refashioned: Goodwill is a kind of friendly feeling, but it is not the same as friendship. For one can feel goodwill toward people one does not know, and without their knowing it -- but friendship requires mutual awareness. This has already been said. But goodwill is not even the same as affection. For it lacks the intensity and desire that accompany affection; affection implies intimacy, while goodwill can arise suddenly. This happens, for example, with competitors in a contest: we come to feel goodwill toward them and share in their wishes, but we would not actually do anything with them. As we said, we feel goodwill suddenly and care for them only superficially.
D: Elegant: Goodwill resembles friendship but is not the same thing. It can exist without mutual awareness, lacks the intensity and desire of genuine affection, and requires no intimacy. Goodwill can arise suddenly -- as when we root for competitors in a contest -- but remains superficial: we share their wishes without acting on their behalf.
D: Summary: Goodwill is a friendly feeling that falls short of friendship. Unlike friendship, it requires no mutual awareness, lacks intensity and desire, and can arise suddenly -- as when we feel sympathy for competitors. It involves wishing well without any commitment to act.
D: Distilled: Goodwill is a friendly wish for another's good that arises easily and requires neither intimacy nor action -- making it friendship's beginning but not friendship itself.
Notes: Eunoia (goodwill) is distinguished from philia (friendship) and philēsis (affection/friendly feeling). -- The contest example likely refers to athletic competitions at Greek festivals (Olympic, Isthmian, etc.), where spectators would develop sudden sympathies for unknown competitors. -- This section (IX.5) begins Aristotle's analysis of friendship-adjacent phenomena: goodwill, concord, and beneficence.

D: Original: Goodwill seems, then, to be a beginning of friendship, as the pleasure of the eye is the beginning of love. For no one loves if he has not first been delighted by the form of the beloved, but he who delights in the form of another does not, for all that, love him, but only does so when he also longs for him when absent and craves for his presence; so too it is not possible for people to be friends if they have not come to feel goodwill for each other, but those who feel goodwill are not for all that friends; for they only wish well to those for whom they feel goodwill, and would not do anything with them nor take trouble for them. And so one might by an extension of the term friendship say that goodwill is inactive friendship, though when it is prolonged and reaches the point of intimacy it becomes friendship-not the friendship based on utility nor that based on pleasure; for goodwill too does not arise on those terms. The man who has received a benefit bestows goodwill in return for what has been done to him, but in doing so is only doing what is just; while he who wishes some one to prosper because he hopes for enrichment through him seems to have goodwill not to him but rather to himself, just as a man is not a friend to another if he cherishes him for the sake of some use to be made of him. In general, goodwill arises on account of some excellence and worth, when one man seems to another beautiful or brave or something of the sort, as we pointed out in the case of competitors in a contest.
D: Refashioned:
Goodwill seems, then, to be the beginning of friendship, just as the pleasure of the eye is the beginning of romantic love. For no one falls in love without first being delighted by the beloved's appearance; but someone who delights in another's appearance does not thereby love that person -- only when he also longs for the absent beloved and craves the beloved's presence. In the same way, it is not possible for people to be friends without first feeling goodwill toward each other, but those who feel goodwill are not yet friends: they merely wish well to those for whom they feel it, and would not actually do anything with them or take trouble for them.

By extending the term, one might call goodwill an inactive friendship. But when goodwill is prolonged and reaches the point of intimacy, it becomes friendship -- not friendship based on utility or pleasure, since goodwill does not arise on those terms either. A person who has received a benefit returns goodwill for what has been done for him, which is simply doing what is just. But someone who wishes another well because he hopes to be enriched through that person has goodwill not toward the other but toward himself -- just as a person is not truly a friend to another if he values him only for the use he can get from him.

In general, goodwill arises on account of some excellence and worth -- when one person seems to another to be admirable, brave, or something of the sort, as we described in the case of competitors in a contest.
D: Elegant:
Goodwill is the beginning of friendship as visual delight is the beginning of love: necessary but not sufficient. Goodwill becomes friendship only when prolonged into intimacy -- and only character-based friendship, not that based on utility or pleasure. Goodwill that hopes for personal enrichment is really self-directed.

Genuine goodwill arises from recognizing excellence in another -- their virtue, courage, or admirable qualities.
D: Summary: Goodwill is to friendship what visual attraction is to love: a necessary beginning but not the thing itself. Goodwill becomes friendship only through prolonged intimacy, and only the character-based kind. Goodwill motivated by expected personal benefit is really self-interest in disguise. Genuine goodwill arises from recognizing excellence in another person.
D: Distilled: Goodwill is the seed of friendship -- arising from recognizing another's excellence -- but it becomes true friendship only through sustained intimacy and genuine concern for the other's good.
Notes: The analogy between goodwill/friendship and visual attraction/love is one of Aristotle's most elegant comparisons. -- The distinction between genuine goodwill and self-interested well-wishing anticipates Kant's distinction between acting from duty and acting from inclination. -- 'Inactive friendship' (argē philia) is a striking phrase that captures goodwill's potential quality. -- The emphasis on excellence (aretē) as the trigger for goodwill reinforces the primacy of virtue-based friendship.

D: Original: Unanimity also seems to be a friendly relation. For this reason it is not identity of opinion; for that might occur even with people who do not know each other; nor do we say that people who have the same views on any and every subject are unanimous, e.g. those who agree about the heavenly bodies (for unanimity about these is not a friendly relation), but we do say that a city is unanimous when men have the same opinion about what is to their interest, and choose the same actions, and do what they have resolved in common. It is about things to be done, therefore, that people are said to be unanimous, and, among these, about matters of consequence and in which it is possible for both or all parties to get what they want; e.g. a city is unanimous when all its citizens think that the offices in it should be elective, or that they should form an alliance with Sparta, or that Pittacus should be their ruler-at a time when he himself was also willing to rule. But when each of two people wishes himself to have the thing in question, like the captains in the Phoenissae, they are in a state of faction; for it is not unanimity when each of two parties thinks of the same thing, whatever that may be, but only when they think of the same thing in the same hands, e.g. when both the common people and those of the better class wish the best men to rule; for thus and thus alone do all get what they aim at. Unanimity seems, then, to be political friendship, as indeed it is commonly said to be; for it is concerned with things that are to our interest and have an influence on our life.
D: Refashioned:
Concord also seems to be a friendly relation. For this reason, it is not merely identity of opinion: people who do not know each other can hold the same views. Nor do we call people who agree on any and every subject concordant -- for instance, those who agree about astronomical matters, since agreement about such things is not a friendly relation.

Rather, we say a city is concordant when its citizens share the same opinion about what is in their interest, choose the same course of action, and carry out what they have resolved in common. Concord, therefore, concerns matters of action -- and specifically matters of consequence in which it is possible for all parties to get what they want.

For example, a city is concordant when all its citizens think that public offices should be elected, or that they should form an alliance with Sparta, or that Pittacus should be their ruler -- at a time when Pittacus himself was also willing to rule. But when each of two parties wants the position for himself -- like the rival brothers in the Phoenician Women -- they are in a state of faction. For concord does not mean merely thinking of the same thing, whatever it may be, but thinking of it in the same hands. For instance, when both the common people and the upper class wish the best men to rule, all get what they aim at.

Concord, then, seems to be political friendship, as it is commonly said to be, for it concerns things that are in our interest and have an influence on our lives.
D: Elegant: Concord is not mere agreement in opinion but shared commitment to action in matters of public interest. A city is concordant when citizens agree on what serves the common good, choose the same policies, and carry them out together. When each faction wants power for itself -- like the rival brothers in the Phoenician Women -- the result is strife, not concord. True concord requires wanting the same outcome in the same hands: when all agree that the best should rule, everyone gets what they want. Concord is thus political friendship -- agreement about how to live together.
D: Summary: Concord is not merely holding the same opinions but sharing practical commitments about matters of public interest. A city enjoys concord when citizens agree on policies and execute them together. When factions compete for power, concord breaks down. True concord -- political friendship -- exists when all agree that the best should govern.
D: Distilled: Concord is political friendship: not agreement in theory but shared commitment to common action and common good.
Notes: Homonoia (concord/unanimity) is analyzed as a political concept, connecting to Aristotle's Politics where civic friendship (politikē philia) is essential to the polis. -- Pittacus of Mytilene (c. 640-568 BC) was one of the Seven Sages of Greece and served as elected tyrant (aisymnētēs) of Mytilene for ten years before voluntarily stepping down. -- The Phoenician Women (Phoinissai) is a tragedy by Euripides depicting the conflict between Eteocles and Polynices, sons of Oedipus, over the kingship of Thebes. -- Sparta's alliance politics were a live issue throughout the 4th century BC. -- The distinction between wanting the same thing 'in the same hands' vs. 'for oneself' is a subtle and important political insight.

D: Original: Now such unanimity is found among good men; for they are unanimous both in themselves and with one another, being, so to say, of one mind (for the wishes of such men are constant and not at the mercy of opposing currents like a strait of the sea), and they wish for what is just and what is advantageous, and these are the objects of their common endeavour as well. But bad men cannot be unanimous except to a small extent, any more than they can be friends, since they aim at getting more than their share of advantages, while in labour and public service they fall short of their share; and each man wishing for advantage to himself criticizes his neighbour and stands in his way; for if people do not watch it carefully the common weal is soon destroyed. The result is that they are in a state of faction, putting compulsion on each other but unwilling themselves to do what is just.
D: Refashioned:
Such concord is found among good people, for they are concordant both within themselves and with one another. Being of one mind, so to speak -- since their wishes are stable and not subject to opposing currents like a strait of the sea -- they wish for what is just and what is advantageous, and these are the objects of their common endeavor as well.

But bad people cannot achieve concord except to a small extent, just as they can scarcely be friends. They aim at getting more than their share of advantages while doing less than their share of the labor and public service. And since each person pursues his own advantage, he scrutinizes and obstructs his neighbor. If people do not watch carefully over the common good, it is soon destroyed. The result is that they fall into faction, each trying to compel the others while being unwilling to do what is just themselves.
D: Elegant: Good people achieve concord because their wishes are stable and directed toward justice and the common advantage. Bad people cannot sustain concord: they grab more than their share of benefits while shirking labor and public service. Each pursues private advantage, obstructs others, and neglects the common good -- resulting in faction and mutual compulsion.
D: Summary: Good people achieve concord because their desires are stable and directed toward justice. Bad people cannot maintain concord because each seeks personal advantage while shirking shared responsibilities, leading to faction and the destruction of the common good.
D: Distilled: Concord flourishes among the good because they pursue justice together; among the bad, it collapses into faction as each grabs advantages while shirking duties.
Notes: The image of wishes 'at the mercy of opposing currents like a strait of the sea' likely refers to the Euripus, the narrow strait between Euboea and mainland Greece known for its unpredictable reversing currents. -- The analysis of faction (stasis) among the bad mirrors Aristotle's discussion of political instability in Politics V. -- The free-rider problem Aristotle identifies -- seeking more benefits while contributing less -- is a classic collective action problem analyzed in modern political science and game theory.

D: Original: Benefactors are thought to love those they have benefited, more than those who have been well treated love those that have treated them well, and this is discussed as though it were paradoxical. Most people think it is because the latter are in the position of debtors and the former of creditors; and therefore as, in the case of loans, debtors wish their creditors did not exist, while creditors actually take care of the safety of their debtors, so it is thought that benefactors wish the objects of their action to exist since they will then get their gratitude, while the beneficiaries take no interest in making this return. Epicharmus would perhaps declare that they say this because they 'look at things on their bad side', but it is quite like human nature; for most people are forgetful, and are more anxious to be well treated than to treat others well. But the cause would seem to be more deeply rooted in the nature of things; the case of those who have lent money is not even analogous. For they have no friendly feeling to their debtors, but only a wish that they may kept safe with a view to what is to be got from them; while those who have done a service to others feel friendship and love for those they have served even if these are not of any use to them and never will be. This is what happens with craftsmen too; every man loves his own handiwork better than he would be loved by it if it came alive; and this happens perhaps most of all with poets; for they have an excessive love for their own poems, doting on them as if they were their children. This is what the position of benefactors is like; for that which they have treated well is their handiwork, and therefore they love this more than the handiwork does its maker. The cause of this is that existence is to all men a thing to be chosen and loved, and that we exist by virtue of activity (i.e. by living and acting), and that the handiwork is in a sense, the producer in activity; he loves his handiwork, therefore, because he loves existence. And this is rooted in the nature of things; for what he is in potentiality, his handiwork manifests in activity.
D: Refashioned:
Benefactors are thought to love those they have benefited more than the recipients love those who treated them well, and this is discussed as though it were paradoxical. Most people explain it by analogy with debts: just as debtors wish their creditors did not exist while creditors take care to preserve their debtors, so benefactors want the objects of their generosity to survive in order to receive gratitude, while beneficiaries take no interest in making this return.

Epicharmus would perhaps say that people who think this way are simply "looking at things from their bad side." But it is quite true to human nature, for most people are forgetful and more eager to receive benefits than to give them.

However, the real cause seems to be more deeply rooted in the nature of things, and the analogy with moneylenders is not really apt. Creditors have no friendly feeling toward their debtors -- only a wish that they survive so the debt can be collected. But those who have benefited others feel genuine friendship and love for those they have helped, even if those people are of no use to them and never will be.

This is what happens with craftsmen too: every craftsman loves his own work more than that work would love him if it came alive. And this is perhaps truest of all with poets, who have an extravagant love for their own poems, doting on them as if they were their children.

This resembles the position of benefactors: the person they have helped is their handiwork, and so they love this handiwork more than the handiwork loves its maker. The reason is that existence is something all people value and love, and that we exist through activity -- through living and acting. The handiwork is, in a sense, the maker expressed in activity. The maker loves his handiwork, therefore, because he loves existence. And this is rooted in the nature of things: what he is in potential, his handiwork manifests in actuality.
D: Elegant:
Benefactors love those they benefit more than recipients love their benefactors -- and the reason goes deeper than the debtor-creditor analogy suggests. Creditors merely want debtors to survive to repay; benefactors feel genuine love because those they help are their handiwork.

Every craftsman loves his creation -- poets most of all, doting on their works like children. Similarly, benefactors love the recipients as expressions of themselves in action. We exist through activity, and what we are in potential, our works manifest in actuality. The benefactor loves his handiwork because he loves his own existence expressed through it.
D: Summary: Benefactors love those they help more than recipients love their benefactors -- not because of expected gratitude, but because every maker loves his handiwork. The person benefited is the benefactor's creation, manifesting in actuality what the benefactor is in potential. We love our works because through them we express and extend our own existence.
D: Distilled: Benefactors love those they help more than they are loved in return, because every maker loves his own handiwork -- it expresses in actuality what he is in potential.
Notes: Epicharmus of Kos (c. 540-450 BC): Sicilian comic playwright, often credited with founding Greek comedy; only fragments survive. His quip about 'looking at things on their bad side' reflects his reputation for witty moralizing. -- The craftsman analogy connects to Aristotle's theory of the four causes (Metaphysics I, Physics II): the efficient cause (maker) takes pleasure in seeing its product (formal and final cause actualized). -- The potentiality/actuality (dynamis/energeia) distinction is one of Aristotle's most fundamental metaphysical concepts, here applied to human psychology. -- The comparison of poets with parents who dote on children anticipates Romantic notions of artistic creation as self-expression.

D: Original: At the same time to the benefactor that is noble which depends on his action, so that he delights in the object of his action, whereas to the patient there is nothing noble in the agent, but at most something advantageous, and this is less pleasant and lovable. What is pleasant is the activity of the present, the hope of the future, the memory of the past; but most pleasant is that which depends on activity, and similarly this is most lovable. Now for a man who has made something his work remains (for the noble is lasting), but for the person acted on the utility passes away. And the memory of noble things is pleasant, but that of useful things is not likely to be pleasant, or is less so; though the reverse seems true of expectation.
D: Refashioned:
At the same time, for the benefactor there is something noble in what depends on his action, so that he takes delight in the person he has helped. But for the recipient, there is nothing noble in the benefactor himself -- at most something advantageous, and this is less pleasant and less worthy of love.

What is pleasant comes in three forms: the activity of the present, the hope of the future, and the memory of the past. But most pleasant of all is what depends on our own activity, and this is also most lovable. Now for the person who has created something, his work endures -- for what is noble is lasting -- but for the person who received the benefit, the usefulness passes away. And the memory of noble deeds is pleasant, while the memory of useful things received is unlikely to be pleasant, or less so -- though when it comes to expectation, the reverse seems to hold true.
D: Elegant: The benefactor finds nobility and lasting delight in his action, while the recipient gains only temporary advantage. Present activity, future hope, and past memory are all pleasant -- but our own activity is most pleasant of all. The benefactor's noble work endures in memory; the recipient's utility fades. Though the memory of noble deeds is sweet and that of received benefits less so, expectation works in reverse: we look forward more to receiving than to giving.
D: Summary: Benefactors find lasting pleasure in the nobility of their actions, while recipients gain only temporary advantage. Our own activity gives the deepest pleasure, and noble deeds endure in memory while received benefits fade. However, when it comes to anticipation, receiving benefits is more eagerly expected than giving them.
D: Distilled: The pleasure of giving endures because noble action lives on in memory, while the utility of receiving fades -- though we anticipate receiving more eagerly than giving.
Notes: The tripartite division of pleasure into present activity, future hope, and past memory is also found in Plato's Philebus (36a). -- The distinction between the lasting pleasure of noble action and the transient pleasure of utility reinforces Aristotle's hierarchy of goods (Book I). -- The observation that expectation reverses the pattern (we look forward to receiving more than giving) shows Aristotle's psychological realism.

D: Original:
Further, love is like activity, being loved like passivity; and loving and its concomitants are attributes of those who are the more active.

Again, all men love more what they have won by labour; e.g. those who have made their money love it more than those who have inherited it; and to be well treated seems to involve no labour, while to treat others well is a laborious task. These are the reasons, too, why mothers are fonder of their children than fathers; bringing them into the world costs them more pains, and they know better that the children are their own. This last point, too, would seem to apply to benefactors.
D: Refashioned:
Furthermore, loving is like activity, while being loved is like passivity; and the impulse to love, together with the feelings that accompany it, belongs to those who are more active.

Again, all people love more what they have won through effort. Those who have earned their money love it more than those who inherited it, and being helped seems to require no effort, while helping others is hard work.

These are also the reasons why mothers are fonder of their children than fathers are: bringing children into the world costs them more pain, and they know more certainly that the children are their own. This last point would seem to apply to benefactors as well.
D: Elegant: Loving is activity, being loved is passivity -- and we are naturally drawn to activity. We love more what we have won through effort: the self-made love their wealth more than heirs do. Mothers love their children more than fathers because childbirth costs them more pain and gives them greater certainty the children are theirs. The same principle applies to benefactors, who invest more effort than recipients.
D: Summary: Loving is active while being loved is passive, and we value more what we achieve through effort. This explains why mothers love their children more than fathers do -- childbirth requires greater labor and gives greater certainty of parentage. The same principle explains why benefactors love more than beneficiaries.
D: Distilled: We love most what costs us the most effort -- which is why mothers love their children more than fathers, and benefactors love more than those they benefit.
Notes: The active/passive (poiesis/pathesis) distinction reflects Aristotle's Categories and Physics. -- The observation about mothers' greater attachment due to the labor of childbirth is remarkably empirical for ancient philosophy. -- The claim about certainty of parentage ('they know better that the children are their own') reflects the ancient world's concern with paternity certainty, which had legal implications for inheritance and citizenship in Athens. -- The 'effort justification' principle Aristotle describes is well-documented in modern social psychology (the 'IKEA effect').

D: Original: The question is also debated, whether a man should love himself most, or some one else. People criticize those who love themselves most, and call them self-lovers, using this as an epithet of disgrace, and a bad man seems to do everything for his own sake, and the more so the more wicked he is-and so men reproach him, for instance, with doing nothing of his own accord-while the good man acts for honour's sake, and the more so the better he is, and acts for his friend's sake, and sacrifices his own interest.
D: Refashioned: The question is also debated whether a person should love himself most, or someone else. People criticize those who love themselves above all and call them "self-lovers," using this as a term of reproach. The bad person, it is thought, does everything for his own sake -- and the more wicked he is, the more he does so. People reproach him, for instance, for never doing anything unless it serves his own interest. The good person, by contrast, acts for the sake of what is noble, and the better he is, the more he does so. He acts for his friend's sake and sacrifices his own interest.
D: Elegant: Whether one should love oneself most is debated. 'Self-lover' is used as a term of reproach, since bad people seem to do everything for their own sake. The good person, by contrast, acts for what is noble and for his friend's sake, sacrificing personal interest.
D: Summary: Common opinion treats 'self-lover' as an insult, since bad people are thought to do everything for their own benefit. Good people, by contrast, act for what is noble and sacrifice their own interests for friends.
D: Distilled: Common opinion condemns self-love because bad people act selfishly, while good people sacrifice their own interests for what is noble.
Notes: This passage opens IX.8, one of the most philosophically rich chapters in the Ethics, which rehabilitates a proper form of self-love. -- The Greek term philautos (self-lover) was pejorative in common usage, as Aristotle notes. -- The tension between self-love and altruism that Aristotle sets up here remains central to ethical theory, from the Stoics through Christianity to modern debates between egoism and altruism.

D: Original: But the facts clash with these arguments, and this is not surprising. For men say that one ought to love best one's best friend, and man's best friend is one who wishes well to the object of his wish for his sake, even if no one is to know of it; and these attributes are found most of all in a man's attitude towards himself, and so are all the other attributes by which a friend is defined; for, as we have said, it is from this relation that all the characteristics of friendship have extended to our neighbours. All the proverbs, too, agree with this, e.g. 'a single soul', and 'what friends have is common property', and 'friendship is equality', and 'charity begins at home'; for all these marks will be found most in a man's relation to himself; he is his own best friend and therefore ought to love himself best. It is therefore a reasonable question, which of the two views we should follow; for both are plausible.
D: Refashioned:
But the facts clash with these arguments, and this is not surprising. For people say that one ought to love one's best friend most, and a person's best friend is the one who wishes well to the object of his care for that person's own sake, even if no one will ever know of it. These qualities are found most of all in a person's attitude toward himself -- as are all the other qualities by which a friend is defined. For, as we have said, it is from one's relationship with oneself that all the characteristics of friendship extend to others.

All the proverbs agree with this too: "a single soul," "what friends have is common property," "friendship is equality," and "charity begins at home." All these marks are found most fully in a person's relation to himself: he is his own best friend and therefore ought to love himself most.

It is, then, a reasonable question which of the two views we should follow, for both are plausible.
D: Elegant: Yet the facts contradict the common reproach of self-love. One's best friend is defined as whoever wishes one well for one's own sake, even anonymously -- and these qualities are found most fully in one's relation to oneself. All proverbs confirm this: 'a single soul,' 'friends share everything,' 'friendship is equality,' 'charity begins at home.' A person is his own best friend. Both views -- that self-love is shameful and that it is natural -- are plausible, creating a genuine puzzle.
D: Summary: Despite the common reproach of self-love, the defining qualities of friendship -- wishing someone well for their own sake, sharing everything, being of one soul -- are found most fully in one's relationship with oneself. Proverbs confirm that a person is his own best friend. Both the case for and against self-love are plausible.
D: Distilled: By every definition of friendship, a person is his own best friend -- yet self-love is commonly condemned, creating a paradox that demands resolution.
Notes: The proverbs cited are well-attested in Greek literature: 'a single soul' (mia psychē) appears in various forms; 'friends share everything' (koina ta philōn) is attributed to Pythagoras by Diogenes Laertius; 'charity begins at home' translates a Greek proverb about the knee being closer than the shin (Theocritus). -- The dialectical method here -- setting up opposing plausible views and then resolving them -- is characteristic of Aristotle's procedure throughout the Ethics (cf. Book VII.1 on akrasia). -- The argument that friendship's criteria point back to self-relation picks up from IX.4 (9.13-9.15).

D: Original: Perhaps we ought to mark off such arguments from each other and determine how far and in what respects each view is right. Now if we grasp the sense in which each school uses the phrase 'lover of self', the truth may become evident. Those who use the term as one of reproach ascribe self-love to people who assign to themselves the greater share of wealth, honours, and bodily pleasures; for these are what most people desire, and busy themselves about as though they were the best of all things, which is the reason, too, why they become objects of competition. So those who are grasping with regard to these things gratify their appetites and in general their feelings and the irrational element of the soul; and most men are of this nature (which is the reason why the epithet has come to be used as it is-it takes its meaning from the prevailing type of self-love, which is a bad one); it is just, therefore, that men who are lovers of self in this way are reproached for being so. That it is those who give themselves the preference in regard to objects of this sort that most people usually call lovers of self is plain; for if a man were always anxious that he himself, above all things, should act justly, temperately, or in accordance with any other of the virtues, and in general were always to try to secure for himself the honourable course, no one will call such a man a lover of self or blame him.
D: Refashioned:
Perhaps we should distinguish these arguments from each other and determine how far and in what respects each view is correct. If we grasp the sense in which each side uses the phrase "lover of self," the truth may become clear.

Those who use the term as a reproach apply it to people who assign to themselves the greater share of wealth, honors, and bodily pleasures. These are what most people desire and compete for, treating them as though they were the highest goods. So those who are grasping with regard to these things gratify their appetites and, in general, the irrational element of their soul. Since most people are of this nature, the term "self-lover" has acquired its negative meaning from this prevailing type of self-love, which is indeed a bad one. It is just, therefore, that people who love themselves in this way are reproached.

That most people ordinarily use "self-lover" for those who give themselves the advantage in such matters is clear enough. For if someone were always anxious above all to act justly, temperately, or in accordance with any other virtue -- if he were always striving to secure for himself the honorable course -- no one would call such a person a "self-lover" or blame him for it.
D: Elegant:
The resolution lies in distinguishing two senses of 'self-lover.' Those rightly reproached as self-lovers are people who grab the largest share of wealth, honors, and bodily pleasures -- gratifying appetite and the irrational soul. Most people are like this, which is why the term has become pejorative.

But if someone strove above all to act justly and virtuously -- always securing for himself the honorable course -- no one would call him a 'self-lover' or blame him. The term condemns one kind of self-love while another kind goes unnamed and unblameable.
D: Summary: The apparent contradiction about self-love dissolves once we distinguish two kinds. The commonly reproached 'self-lover' is someone who grabs the largest share of wealth, honor, and pleasure -- gratifying the irrational soul. But someone who strives above all to act virtuously and honorably would never be called a self-lover, even though he too assigns himself the best things.
D: Distilled: The reproach of self-love applies only to those who serve their appetites -- no one blames a person who 'selfishly' pursues virtue and nobility above all else.
Notes: This is the crucial argumentative turn of IX.8, where Aristotle resolves the paradox by distinguishing between two types of self-love. -- The distinction between rational and irrational elements of the soul draws on the soul's division established in Book I.13. -- The 'objects of competition' (wealth, honors, bodily pleasures) correspond to the goods pursued by the three inferior lives discussed in Book I.5. -- This argument has been deeply influential on subsequent ethical thought, informing debates about whether true self-interest and morality can coincide.

D: Original: But such a man would seem more than the other a lover of self; at all events he assigns to himself the things that are noblest and best, and gratifies the most authoritative element in and in all things obeys this; and just as a city or any other systematic whole is most properly identified with the most authoritative element in it, so is a man; and therefore the man who loves this and gratifies it is most of all a lover of self. Besides, a man is said to have or not to have self-control according as his reason has or has not the control, on the assumption that this is the man himself; and the things men have done on a rational principle are thought most properly their own acts and voluntary acts. That this is the man himself, then, or is so more than anything else, is plain, and also that the good man loves most this part of him. Whence it follows that he is most truly a lover of self, of another type than that which is a matter of reproach, and as different from that as living according to a rational principle is from living as passion dictates, and desiring what is noble from desiring what seems advantageous. Those, then, who busy themselves in an exceptional degree with noble actions all men approve and praise; and if all were to strive towards what is noble and strain every nerve to do the noblest deeds, everything would be as it should be for the common weal, and every one would secure for himself the goods that are greatest, since virtue is the greatest of goods.
D: Refashioned:
But such a person would actually seem to be a lover of self in a deeper sense. At any rate, he assigns to himself the things that are noblest and best, and he gratifies the most authoritative element in himself, obeying it in all things. Just as a city or any other organized whole is most properly identified with its most authoritative element, so too is a person. Therefore the person who loves this authoritative element and gratifies it is most of all a lover of self.

Moreover, a person is said to have or lack self-control depending on whether his reason is or is not in command -- on the assumption that reason is the person's true self. And the things people have done on a rational principle are considered most properly their own acts and voluntary acts. That this rational element is the person himself -- or is so more than anything else -- is clear. And it is clear too that the good person loves this element most.

Therefore the good person is most truly a lover of self -- but of a different type from the one that is a matter of reproach, as different as living according to reason is from living as passion dictates, and as different as desiring what is noble is from desiring what merely seems advantageous.

Those who devote themselves exceptionally to noble actions win universal approval and praise. And if everyone were to strive toward what is noble and strain every nerve to do the noblest deeds, everything would be as it should for the common good, and every individual would secure for himself the greatest of goods -- since virtue is the greatest of goods.
D: Elegant:
The person who pursues nobility and gratifies his reason -- the most authoritative element in him -- is the truest self-lover. Just as a city is identified with its governing element, a person is most properly identified with his rational nature. Self-control means reason is in command, and rational acts are most truly our own.

This noble self-love differs from the vulgar kind as reason differs from passion, and as desiring what is noble differs from chasing apparent advantage. If everyone competed in nobility rather than material goods, both the common good and individual good would be perfectly served -- since virtue is the greatest good.
D: Summary: The good person is the truest self-lover because he gratifies reason, his most authoritative and essential element. This noble self-love is as different from vulgar self-love as reason is from passion. If everyone competed in virtue rather than material goods, both individual and common good would flourish, since virtue is the greatest good.
D: Distilled: True self-love means gratifying reason -- your deepest self -- and if everyone competed to be most virtuous rather than most wealthy, both personal and common good would be perfectly served.
Notes: The identification of the self with reason (nous) is developed further in Book X.7 (1177b-1178a), where Aristotle argues that contemplative activity is the most complete happiness because it exercises the most divine element in us. -- The political analogy (the self is to reason as the city is to its governing element) connects to Politics III.6 on constitutions. -- The claim that universal pursuit of virtue would optimize the common good represents Aristotle's version of the harmony between individual and social good -- a question that dominates modern political philosophy from Hobbes to Rawls. -- The distinction between 'what is noble' (to kalon) and 'what seems advantageous' (to dokoun sympherein) is fundamental to Aristotle's ethical framework.

D: Original: Therefore the good man should be a lover of self (for he will both himself profit by doing noble acts, and will benefit his fellows), but the wicked man should not; for he will hurt both himself and his neighbours, following as he does evil passions. For the wicked man, what he does clashes with what he ought to do, but what the good man ought to do he does; for reason in each of its possessors chooses what is best for itself, and the good man obeys his reason. It is true of the good man too that he does many acts for the sake of his friends and his country, and if necessary dies for them; for he will throw away both wealth and honours and in general the goods that are objects of competition, gaining for himself nobility; since he would prefer a short period of intense pleasure to a long one of mild enjoyment, a twelvemonth of noble life to many years of humdrum existence, and one great and noble action to many trivial ones. Now those who die for others doubtless attain this result; it is therefore a great prize that they choose for themselves. They will throw away wealth too on condition that their friends will gain more; for while a man's friend gains wealth he himself achieves nobility; he is therefore assigning the greater good to himself. The same too is true of honour and office; all these things he will sacrifice to his friend; for this is noble and laudable for himself. Rightly then is he thought to be good, since he chooses nobility before all else. But he may even give up actions to his friend; it may be nobler to become the cause of his friend's acting than to act himself. In all the actions, therefore, that men are praised for, the good man is seen to assign to himself the greater share in what is noble. In this sense, then, as has been said, a man should be a lover of self; but in the sense in which most men are so, he ought not.
D: Refashioned:
Therefore the good person should be a lover of self -- for he will both profit himself by doing noble acts and benefit his fellows. But the wicked person should not be a lover of self, for he will harm both himself and his neighbors by following evil passions. For the wicked person, what he does clashes with what he ought to do; but what the good person ought to do, he does, since reason in each of its possessors chooses what is best for itself, and the good person obeys his reason.

It is also true that the good person does many things for the sake of his friends and his country, and if necessary dies for them. He will throw away wealth, honors, and all the goods that people compete for, gaining for himself nobility. For he would prefer a short period of intense pleasure to a long one of mild enjoyment, a year of noble life to many years of humdrum existence, and one great and noble action to many trivial ones.

This is perhaps what happens with those who die for others: they choose a great prize for themselves. They will give away wealth too, on the condition that their friends will gain more; for while the friend gains money, the good person gains nobility -- and so he assigns the greater good to himself.

The same is true of honors and public offices -- he will sacrifice all of these to his friend, for this is noble and praiseworthy for himself. Rightly, then, is he considered good, since he chooses nobility above all else. He may even yield opportunities for action to his friend, for it may be nobler to be the cause of his friend's acting than to act himself.

In all the actions for which people are praised, then, the good person is seen to assign to himself the greater share of what is noble. In this sense, as we have said, a person should be a lover of self; but in the sense in which most people are lovers of self, he should not be.
D: Elegant:
The good person should be a self-lover: he benefits himself through noble acts while also benefiting others. The wicked should not, since their passions harm themselves and others alike.

The good person will sacrifice wealth, honors, and even life for friends and country -- choosing a short, noble life over a long, mediocre one, and one great action over many trivial ones. Those who die for others choose the greater prize: nobility. By giving away wealth so friends gain materially while he gains nobility, he assigns himself the greater good.

He may even yield opportunities for action, since being the cause of a friend's noble deed can be nobler than acting oneself. In all praiseworthy actions, the good person claims the larger share of what is noble. One should be a self-lover in this sense -- but not in the vulgar sense.
D: Summary: The good person should love himself because through noble acts he benefits both himself and others. He willingly sacrifices wealth, honor, and even life because he prefers nobility to material goods -- choosing a short but glorious life over a long but mediocre one. By giving away material goods while claiming nobility, he actually assigns himself the greater good. True self-love means claiming the greater share of what is noble, not what is materially advantageous.
D: Distilled: True self-love means choosing nobility over comfort -- even sacrificing life itself -- because the person who claims the greater share of what is noble assigns himself the genuinely greater good.
Notes: This is the culmination of IX.8's argument, one of the most celebrated passages in the Ethics. -- The claim that dying for others is a form of self-love (choosing 'a great prize') is paradoxical and profound, reframing sacrifice as the ultimate expression of rational self-interest. -- The preference for 'a short period of intense pleasure to a long one of mild enjoyment' connects to debates about the temporal structure of happiness (cf. Book I.10 on Solon's 'count no man happy until dead'). -- The idea that being the cause of a friend's action can be nobler than acting oneself shows Aristotle's nuanced understanding of moral agency. -- This passage has influenced Christian ethics (kenosis, self-emptying love), Nietzsche's critique of altruism, and contemporary virtue ethics.

D: Original: It is also disputed whether the happy man will need friends or not. It is said that those who are supremely happy and self-sufficient have no need of friends; for they have the things that are good, and therefore being self-sufficient they need nothing further, while a friend, being another self, furnishes what a man cannot provide by his own effort; whence the saying 'when fortune is kind, what need of friends?' But it seems strange, when one assigns all good things to the happy man, not to assign friends, who are thought the greatest of external goods. And if it is more characteristic of a friend to do well by another than to be well done by, and to confer benefits is characteristic of the good man and of virtue, and it is nobler to do well by friends than by strangers, the good man will need people to do well by. This is why the question is asked whether we need friends more in prosperity or in adversity, on the assumption that not only does a man in adversity need people to confer benefits on him, but also those who are prospering need people to do well by. Surely it is strange, too, to make the supremely happy man a solitary; for no one would choose the whole world on condition of being alone, since man is a political creature and one whose nature is to live with others. Therefore even the happy man lives with others; for he has the things that are by nature good. And plainly it is better to spend his days with friends and good men than with strangers or any chance persons. Therefore the happy man needs friends.
D: Refashioned:
It is also debated whether the happy person will need friends or not. It is said that those who are supremely happy and self-sufficient have no need of friends, since they already possess what is good. Being self-sufficient, they need nothing further -- while a friend, being another self, furnishes what a person cannot provide through his own effort. Hence the saying: "When fortune is kind, what need of friends?"

But it seems strange, when we assign all good things to the happy person, not to assign him friends, who are thought to be the greatest of external goods. And if it is more characteristic of a friend to do well by others than to be well treated, and if conferring benefits is characteristic of the good person and of virtue, and if it is nobler to do well by friends than by strangers, then the good person will need people to do well by.
