This is why the question is raised whether we need friends more in prosperity or in adversity -- on the assumption that both the person in misfortune who needs help and the prosperous person who needs people to benefit require friends.

Surely it is strange, too, to make the supremely happy person a solitary. For no one would choose to possess the whole world on the condition of being alone, since a human being is a political creature whose nature is to live with others. Therefore even the happy person lives with others, for he possesses the things that are by nature good. And plainly it is better to spend one's days with friends and good people than with strangers or random persons. Therefore the happy person needs friends.
D: Elegant:
Some argue that the supremely happy and self-sufficient need no friends. But it is strange to assign someone all good things while withholding friends -- the greatest external good. If virtue means conferring benefits and it is nobler to benefit friends than strangers, the good person needs friends to benefit.

Making the happy person a solitary is absurd: no one would choose to possess everything in isolation, since humans are by nature political and social. The happy person needs good friends to live with, since it is better to spend one's days with friends than with strangers.
D: Summary: Against the view that the self-sufficient need no friends, Aristotle argues that friends are the greatest external good and essential to happiness. The good person needs friends to benefit, and since humans are inherently social, no one would choose total prosperity in isolation. The happy person needs virtuous friends.
D: Distilled: No one would choose to possess everything in isolation -- the happy person needs friends because humans are social by nature and because virtue itself requires others to benefit.
Notes: This passage opens IX.9, which provides Aristotle's most sustained argument for why the happy person needs friends. -- 'When fortune is kind, what need of friends?' is attributed to Euripides (Orestes, line 665 in some editions). -- 'A human being is a political creature' (politikon zōon) echoes the famous statement in Politics I.2 (1253a2-3). -- The tension between self-sufficiency (autarkeia) and the need for friends is one of the central puzzles of the Ethics; self-sufficiency was defined in Book I.7 as 'that which makes life desirable and lacking in nothing.' -- The Epicureans later argued that friendship is essential to happiness despite their emphasis on individual pleasure, partly drawing on this Aristotelian argument.

D: Original: What then is it that the first school means, and in what respect is it right? Is it that most identify friends with useful people? Of such friends indeed the supremely happy man will have no need, since he already has the things that are good; nor will he need those whom one makes one's friends because of their pleasantness, or he will need them only to a small extent (for his life, being pleasant, has no need of adventitious pleasure); and because he does not need such friends he is thought not to need friends.
D: Refashioned: What then does the first school mean, and in what respect is it right? Is it perhaps that most people identify friends with useful people? The supremely happy person will indeed have no need of such friends, since he already possesses what is good. Nor will he need friends sought for the sake of pleasure, or only to a very small extent -- for his life is already pleasant and has no need of externally supplied pleasure. And because he does not need friends of these kinds, he is thought not to need friends at all.
D: Elegant: The claim that the happy person needs no friends is only correct if 'friends' means friends of utility or pleasure. The supremely happy person already has good things and a pleasant life, so he needs neither useful nor amusing companions. But this limited conception of friendship does not exhaust what friendship truly is.
D: Summary: The argument that the happy person needs no friends is only valid if friendship means friends for utility or pleasure. The supremely happy person already has these things. But this overlooks character-based friendship, which serves a deeper need.
D: Distilled: The happy person needs no friends of utility or pleasure, but this does not mean he needs no friends at all -- it means the objection misunderstands what true friendship is.
Notes: This passage dissolves the apparent contradiction by applying the three-fold typology of friendship from Book VIII.3. -- The move is characteristic of Aristotle's dialectical method: showing that an apparently strong objection rests on an equivocation.

D: Original: But that is surely not true. For we have said at the outset that happiness is an activity; and activity plainly comes into being and is not present at the start like a piece of property. If (1) happiness lies in living and being active, and the good man's activity is virtuous and pleasant in itself, as we have said at the outset, and (2) a thing's being one's own is one of the attributes that make it pleasant, and (3) we can contemplate our neighbours better than ourselves and their actions better than our own, and if the actions of virtuous men who are their friends are pleasant to good men (since these have both the attributes that are naturally pleasant),-if this be so, the supremely happy man will need friends of this sort, since his purpose is to contemplate worthy actions and actions that are his own, and the actions of a good man who is his friend have both these qualities.
D: Refashioned:
But that conclusion is surely not true. For we said at the outset that happiness is an activity, and activity is something that comes into being and is not simply present from the start like a piece of property.

If happiness lies in living and being active, and the good person's activity is virtuous and pleasant in itself, as we said at the outset; and if something's being one's own is one of the things that makes it pleasant; and if we can observe our neighbors better than ourselves and their actions better than our own; and if the actions of virtuous people who are friends are pleasant to good people, since these actions have both naturally pleasant qualities -- being both virtuous and one's own, in a sense -- then the supremely happy person will need friends of this sort. For his purpose is to contemplate worthy actions and actions that are his own, and the actions of a good person who is his friend have both these qualities.
D: Elegant: Happiness is an activity, not a possession, so the happy person needs ongoing virtuous activity. Since we observe others better than ourselves, and the actions of virtuous friends are both inherently pleasant and in a sense our own, the happy person needs good friends. Through them he contemplates worthy actions that belong to him -- fulfilling happiness's requirement for sustained virtuous activity.
D: Summary: Since happiness is an ongoing activity rather than a static possession, the happy person needs virtuous friends. We observe others better than ourselves, and the actions of virtuous friends are both intrinsically pleasant and, in a sense, our own. Through such friends, we can contemplate worthy actions -- which is essential to happiness.
D: Distilled: Happiness is an activity, not a possession -- and since we see our friends' virtuous actions more clearly than our own, good friends are essential for contemplating the worthy actions that constitute a happy life.
Notes: The argument that happiness is an activity (energeia) rather than a state (hexis) or possession (ktēsis) is established in Book I.7-8 and is one of Aristotle's most important ethical claims. -- The observation that we can observe others better than ourselves has interesting connections to modern psychology on self-knowledge and bias. -- The idea that a friend's actions are 'one's own in a sense' follows from the 'another self' doctrine (IX.4). -- This passage combines metaphysics (actuality/potentiality), epistemology (self-knowledge), and ethics in a remarkably compressed argument.

D: Original: Further, men think that the happy man ought to live pleasantly. Now if he were a solitary, life would be hard for him; for by oneself it is not easy to be continuously active; but with others and towards others it is easier. With others therefore his activity will be more continuous, and it is in itself pleasant, as it ought to be for the man who is supremely happy; for a good man qua good delights in virtuous actions and is vexed at vicious ones, as a musical man enjoys beautiful tunes but is pained at bad ones. A certain training in virtue arises also from the company of the good, as Theognis has said before us.
D: Refashioned:
Furthermore, people think that the happy person ought to live pleasantly. Now if he were a solitary, life would be difficult for him, since it is not easy to be continuously active by oneself; but with others and in relation to others, it is easier. With others, therefore, his activity will be more continuous -- which is itself pleasant, as it ought to be for the supremely happy person.

For the good person, insofar as he is good, delights in virtuous actions and is troubled by vicious ones, just as a musical person enjoys beautiful melodies but is pained by bad ones. Moreover, a certain training in virtue comes from keeping the company of good people, as Theognis said before us.
D: Elegant: The happy person needs others for sustained activity, since continuous virtuous action is difficult in isolation but easier in company. A good person naturally delights in virtue and is pained by vice, like a musician who enjoys fine melodies and is distressed by poor ones. The company of good people also provides training in virtue, as Theognis observed.
D: Summary: Solitary life makes sustained virtuous activity difficult. With friends, the happy person can maintain continuous activity, which is itself pleasant. Good people naturally enjoy virtuous actions, and the company of other good people provides ongoing training in virtue.
D: Distilled: Sustained virtuous activity -- the core of happiness -- is difficult in isolation but natural in the company of good friends, who both delight and train us in virtue.
Notes: Theognis of Megara (6th century BC): elegiac poet whose verses on aristocratic virtue and the company one keeps were widely quoted in Greek education. The relevant passage is likely Theognis 35-36: 'From noble men you will learn noble things; if you mingle with the base, you will lose even the wits you have.' -- The musician analogy is typical of Aristotle's use of craft analogies throughout the Ethics. -- The argument that continuous activity requires company connects to the social nature of human beings (politikon zōon) stressed in the Politics.

D: Original: If we look deeper into the nature of things, a virtuous friend seems to be naturally desirable for a virtuous man. For that which is good by nature, we have said, is for the virtuous man good and pleasant in itself. Now life is defined in the case of animals by the power of perception in that of man by the power of perception or thought; and a power is defined by reference to the corresponding activity, which is the essential thing; therefore life seems to be essentially the act of perceiving or thinking. And life is among the things that are good and pleasant in themselves, since it is determinate and the determinate is of the nature of the good; and that which is good by nature is also good for the virtuous man (which is the reason why life seems pleasant to all men); but we must not apply this to a wicked and corrupt life nor to a life spent in pain; for such a life is indeterminate, as are its attributes. The nature of pain will become plainer in what follows.
D: Refashioned:
If we look more deeply into the nature of things, a virtuous friend seems to be naturally desirable for a virtuous person. For what is good by nature, we have said, is good and pleasant in itself for the virtuous person.

Now life is defined for animals by the power of perception, and for human beings by the power of perception or thought. A power is defined by reference to its corresponding activity, which is the essential thing. Therefore life seems to consist essentially in the act of perceiving or thinking.

Life is among the things that are good and pleasant in themselves, since it is determinate, and the determinate belongs to the nature of the good. What is good by nature is also good for the virtuous person, which is why life seems pleasant to everyone. But we must not apply this to a wicked or corrupt life, nor to a life spent in pain, for such a life is indeterminate, as are its qualities. The nature of pain will become clearer in what follows.
D: Elegant: A virtuous friend is naturally desirable for a virtuous person because what is good by nature is good and pleasant for the virtuous. Life itself -- defined essentially as the activity of perception or thought -- is among things good and pleasant in themselves, since it possesses determinacy, which belongs to the nature of the good. Life is good for the virtuous, though not a wicked or painful life, which lacks this determinacy.
D: Summary: Aristotle argues that a virtuous friend is naturally desirable by building from first principles: life consists essentially in perception and thought, and is inherently good and pleasant because it is determinate. Since what is naturally good is good for the virtuous person, life is pleasant to the virtuous -- though not a wicked or pain-filled life, which lacks determinacy.
D: Distilled: Life itself -- the activity of perceiving and thinking -- is inherently good and pleasant for the virtuous, because determinacy belongs to the nature of the good.
Notes: This passage begins the most metaphysically dense argument in IX.9, building toward the conclusion that friends are essential to happiness. -- The identification of life with the activity (energeia) of perception or thought connects to De Anima II.1-2, where Aristotle defines the soul as the first actuality of a natural body that has life potentially. -- The principle that the determinate (horismenon) is good while the indeterminate (aoriston) is bad echoes Pythagorean and Platonic metaphysics (cf. Plato's Philebus on limit and the unlimited). -- 'The nature of pain will become plainer in what follows' refers to the discussion of pleasure and pain in Book X.1-5.

D: Original: But if life itself is good and pleasant (which it seems to be, from the very fact that all men desire it, and particularly those who are good and supremely happy; for to such men life is most desirable, and their existence is the most supremely happy) and if he who sees perceives that he sees, and he who hears, that he hears, and he who walks, that he walks, and in the case of all other activities similarly there is something which perceives that we are active, so that if we perceive, we perceive that we perceive, and if we think, that we think; and if to perceive that we perceive or think is to perceive that we exist (for existence was defined as perceiving or thinking); and if perceiving that one lives is in itself one of the things that are pleasant (for life is by nature good, and to perceive what is good present in oneself is pleasant); and if life is desirable, and particularly so for good men, because to them existence is good and pleasant for they are pleased at the consciousness of the presence in them of what is in itself good); and if as the virtuous man is to himself, he is to his friend also (for his friend is another self):-if all this be true, as his own being is desirable for each man, so, or almost so, is that of his friend.
D: Refashioned:
But if life itself is good and pleasant -- which it seems to be, from the very fact that all people desire it, and especially those who are good and supremely happy, for whom life is most desirable and whose existence is the most supremely happy --

And if the person who sees perceives that he sees, and the person who hears perceives that he hears, and the person who walks perceives that he walks, and similarly for all other activities there is something that perceives that we are active, so that if we perceive, we perceive that we perceive, and if we think, we perceive that we think --

And if to perceive that we perceive or think is to perceive that we exist, since existence was defined as perceiving or thinking --

And if perceiving that one is alive is in itself one of the things that are pleasant, since life is by nature good and to perceive what is good present in oneself is pleasant --

And if life is desirable, and particularly so for good people, because to them existence is good and pleasant, since they are pleased by the consciousness of the presence in them of what is in itself good --

And if, as the virtuous person is to himself, so he is to his friend also, for his friend is another self --

Then, if all this is true, just as each person's own existence is desirable for him, so too, or nearly so, is the existence of his friend.
D: Elegant:
Life is good and pleasant, especially for the virtuous. We are conscious not only of our activities but of our consciousness itself: perceiving that we perceive, thinking that we think. This self-awareness is awareness of our own existence, since to exist is to perceive and think. Consciousness of one's own goodness is inherently pleasant.

Since the virtuous person relates to his friend as to himself -- the friend being another self -- the friend's existence is desirable in the same way as one's own.
D: Summary: Aristotle constructs a chain of reasoning: life is good and pleasant; we are aware of our own perceiving and thinking, which is awareness of our existence; this self-awareness is pleasant for the virtuous because they perceive the presence of genuine good in themselves. Since a friend is another self, the friend's existence is as desirable as one's own.
D: Distilled: Since we find our own conscious existence desirable -- because self-awareness reveals the good within us -- and a friend is another self, our friend's existence is as desirable as our own.
Notes: This passage contains one of the earliest philosophical analyses of self-consciousness (synaisthesis -- perceiving that one perceives). It anticipates Descartes' cogito by nearly two millennia, though the purpose is ethical rather than epistemological. -- The chain argument structure (protasis after protasis building to a single conclusion) is characteristic of Aristotle's formal demonstrations. -- The 'another self' (allos autos) doctrine reaches its fullest expression here, grounding the need for friends in the metaphysics of consciousness and existence. -- Modern phenomenologists (Husserl, Merleau-Ponty) have drawn on this passage in their analyses of intersubjectivity.

D: Original: Now his being was seen to be desirable because he perceived his own goodness, and such perception is pleasant in itself. He needs, therefore, to be conscious of the existence of his friend as well, and this will be realized in their living together and sharing in discussion and thought; for this is what living together would seem to mean in the case of man, and not, as in the case of cattle, feeding in the same place.
D: Refashioned: Now his own existence was seen to be desirable because he perceived his own goodness, and such perception is pleasant in itself. He needs, therefore, to be conscious of the existence of his friend as well. And this will be realized in their living together and sharing in discussion and thought, for this is what living together would seem to mean in the case of human beings -- not, as with cattle, merely feeding in the same place.
D: Elegant: Since one's own existence is desirable through perceiving one's own goodness, one needs to perceive the friend's existence too. This is achieved through shared life -- which for humans means sharing in discussion and thought, not merely occupying the same space like animals.
D: Summary: Since we desire our own existence because we perceive our own goodness, we also need to perceive our friend's existence -- and this requires truly living together, which for humans means sharing in conversation and thought, not merely being physically near each other.
D: Distilled: Human friendship is realized through shared thought and conversation -- for living together means sharing in rational activity, not merely occupying the same space.
Notes: The contrast between human community (sharing in logos -- reason/speech/discussion) and animal herding echoes Politics I.2, where Aristotle argues that humans alone possess logos and therefore form genuine political communities. -- 'Discussion and thought' (logos and dianoia) emphasizes that friendship's shared life is fundamentally intellectual and communicative. -- This passage grounds the need for friends in the distinctively human mode of existence.

D: Original: If, then, being is in itself desirable for the supremely happy man (since it is by its nature good and pleasant), and that of his friend is very much the same, a friend will be one of the things that are desirable. Now that which is desirable for him he must have, or he will be deficient in this respect. The man who is to be happy will therefore need virtuous friends.
D: Refashioned: If, then, existence is in itself desirable for the supremely happy person -- since it is by nature good and pleasant -- and if the existence of his friend is very much the same, then a friend will be one of the things that are desirable. Now whatever is desirable for the happy person he must have, or he will be deficient in that respect. The person who is to be happy will therefore need virtuous friends.
D: Elegant: Since existence is desirable for the happy person and his friend's existence is equally desirable, a friend is among the things necessary for happiness. Without virtuous friends, the happy person would be deficient -- so happiness requires them.
D: Summary: Since the happy person's own existence is desirable and his friend's existence is equally so, virtuous friends are among the things necessary for happiness. Without them, the happy person would lack something essential.
D: Distilled: Virtuous friends are necessary for happiness because the friend's existence is as desirable as one's own, and to lack what is desirable is to be deficient.
Notes: This passage completes the argument of IX.9 with a clean syllogistic conclusion. -- The claim that the happy person 'must have' what is desirable connects to the definition of happiness in Book I.7 as 'lacking in nothing' (mēdenos endees). -- This conclusion has been seen as one of Aristotle's most important departures from purely self-sufficient conceptions of the good life, such as those later developed by the Stoics and Epicureans.

D: Original:
Should we, then, make as many friends as possible, or-as in the case of hospitality it is thought to be suitable advice, that one should be 'neither a man of many guests nor a man with none'-will that apply to friendship as well; should a man neither be friendless nor have an excessive number of friends?

To friends made with a view to utility this saying would seem thoroughly applicable; for to do services to many people in return is a laborious task and life is not long enough for its performance. Therefore friends in excess of those who are sufficient for our own life are superfluous, and hindrances to the noble life; so that we have no need of them. Of friends made with a view to pleasure, also, few are enough, as a little seasoning in food is enough.
D: Refashioned:
Should we, then, make as many friends as possible? Or -- as with hospitality, where the advice "be neither a man of many guests nor one with none" is thought appropriate -- does the same apply to friendship? Should a person neither be friendless nor have an excessive number of friends?

To friends made with a view to utility, this saying seems thoroughly applicable, for doing services in return for many people is a laborious task and life is not long enough to accomplish it. Friends in excess of what is sufficient for our own life are superfluous and a hindrance to living nobly; we have no need of them.

Of friends made with a view to pleasure, a few are also enough -- like a little seasoning in food.
D: Elegant: Should one have as many friends as possible? For friends of utility, the answer is clearly no: returning services to many is exhausting and life is too short. Excess utility-friends hinder noble living. Friends of pleasure also need be few -- like seasoning, a little suffices.
D: Summary: One should not seek to maximize the number of friends. Friends of utility should be limited, since reciprocating services to many is burdensome and life is short. Friends of pleasure should also be few, like seasoning that requires only a small amount to be effective.
D: Distilled: Friends of utility and pleasure should be few -- too many become a burden that hinders noble living.
Notes: The hospitality maxim ('neither a man of many guests nor one with none') is from Hesiod, Works and Days 715. -- The seasoning metaphor is characteristically Aristotelian in its use of everyday analogies to illuminate ethical points. -- This passage opens IX.10 on the proper number of friends.

D: Original: But as regards good friends, should we have as many as possible, or is there a limit to the number of one's friends, as there is to the size of a city? You cannot make a city of ten men, and if there are a hundred thousand it is a city no longer. But the proper number is presumably not a single number, but anything that falls between certain fixed points. So for friends too there is a fixed number perhaps the largest number with whom one can live together (for that, we found, thought to be very characteristic of friendship); and that one cannot live with many people and divide oneself up among them is plain. Further, they too must be friends of one another, if they are all to spend their days together; and it is a hard business for this condition to be fulfilled with a large number. It is found difficult, too, to rejoice and to grieve in an intimate way with many people, for it may likely happen that one has at once to be happy with one friend and to mourn with another. Presumably, then, it is well not to seek to have as many friends as possible, but as many as are enough for the purpose of living together; for it would seem actually impossible to be a great friend to many people. This is why one cannot love several people; love is ideally a sort of excess of friendship, and that can only be felt towards one person; therefore great friendship too can only be felt towards a few people. This seems to be confirmed in practice; for we do not find many people who are friends in the comradely way of friendship, and the famous friendships of this sort are always between two people. Those who have many friends and mix intimately with them all are thought to be no one's friend, except in the way proper to fellow-citizens, and such people are also called obsequious. In the way proper to fellow-citizens, indeed, it is possible to be the friend of many and yet not be obsequious but a genuinely good man; but one cannot have with many people the friendship based on virtue and on the character of our friends themselves, and we must be content if we find even a few such.
D: Refashioned:
But as regards good friends -- friends of character -- should we have as many as possible? Or is there a limit to the number of one's friends, as there is to the size of a city? You cannot make a city of ten people, and if there are a hundred thousand, it is a city no longer. But the proper number is presumably not a single fixed number; rather, it is anything that falls between certain limits.

So too for friends: there is presumably a fixed range, perhaps the largest number with whom one can live together -- for that, we found, is thought to be the most essential feature of friendship. And one plainly cannot live with many people and divide oneself among them.

Furthermore, those friends must themselves be friends with one another if they are all to spend their days together, and it is difficult for this condition to be met with a large number. It is also hard to share intimately in the joys and griefs of many people, since it may well happen that one must rejoice with one friend and mourn with another at the same time.

Perhaps, then, it is best not to seek as many friends as possible, but as many as are enough for the purpose of living together. It would actually seem impossible to be a close friend to many people. This is why one cannot be in love with several people at once: love is ideally a sort of excess of friendship, and that can only be felt toward one person; therefore deep friendship too can only extend to a few.

This seems to be confirmed in practice, for we do not find many people who are friends in the deeply companionate way, and the famous friendships of this kind are always between two people. Those who have many friends and are intimate with all of them are thought to be genuine friends of no one -- except in the way appropriate to fellow citizens -- and such people are also called obsequious.

In the way appropriate to fellow citizens, it is indeed possible to be a friend of many and yet not be obsequious but genuinely good. But one cannot have with many people the friendship based on virtue and on who our friends truly are. We must be content if we find even a few such friends.
D: Elegant:
There is a natural limit to character-based friends, just as there is to the size of a city. The limit is roughly the number one can truly live with -- since one cannot divide oneself among many. All the friends must also befriend one another, and it is hard to share intimately in many people's joys and sorrows simultaneously.

Deep friendship, like love, is an excess that can only be directed toward few. Famous friendships are always between two. Those intimate with everyone are truly close to no one. While civic friendship can extend widely, virtue-based friendship is rare -- we should be grateful to find even a few such friends.
D: Summary: Character-based friendship has natural limits, like the size of a city. One can only truly live together with a few people, those friends must also befriend each other, and intimate sharing of joys and griefs cannot extend to many. Deep friendship, like love, can only be directed at a few. We should count ourselves fortunate to find even a small number of true friends.
D: Distilled: Deep friendship, like love, can only extend to a few -- those intimate with everyone are truly close to no one, and we should count ourselves fortunate to find even a small number of genuine friends.
Notes: The city-size analogy draws on Aristotle's discussion in Politics VII.4 (1326a-b), where he argues that a city must be large enough to be self-sufficient but small enough for citizens to know each other's characters. -- The claim that love (erōs) is 'an excess of friendship' interestingly subordinates romantic love to the broader category of friendship. -- The observation that famous friendships are between two people may allude to pairs like Achilles and Patroclus, Orestes and Pylades, or Damon and Pythias. -- The distinction between civic friendship (wide) and virtue friendship (narrow) maps onto the three types of friendship from Book VIII.3.

D: Original: Do we need friends more in good fortune or in bad? They are sought after in both; for while men in adversity need help, in prosperity they need people to live with and to make the objects of their beneficence; for they wish to do well by others. Friendship, then, is more necessary in bad fortune, and so it is useful friends that one wants in this case; but it is more noble in good fortune, and so we also seek for good men as our friends, since it is more desirable to confer benefits on these and to live with these. For the very presence of friends is pleasant both in good fortune and also in bad, since grief is lightened when friends sorrow with us. Hence one might ask whether they share as it were our burden, or-without that happening-their presence by its pleasantness, and the thought of their grieving with us, make our pain less. Whether it is for these reasons or for some other that our grief is lightened, is a question that may be dismissed; at all events what we have described appears to take place.
D: Refashioned:
Do we need friends more in good fortune or in bad? They are sought in both situations. People in adversity need help, while those in prosperity need people to live with and to be the recipients of their generosity, for they wish to do well by others.

Friendship, then, is more necessary in bad fortune -- which is why useful friends are what one wants in that case. But friendship is more noble in good fortune, which is why we also seek good people as friends in prosperity, since it is more desirable to confer benefits on such people and to spend time with them.

For the very presence of friends is pleasant both in good fortune and in bad, since grief is lightened when friends share in our sorrow. This is why one might ask whether they literally share our burden, or whether -- without that actually happening -- their presence, by its pleasantness and by the thought that they grieve with us, makes our pain less. Whether grief is lightened for these reasons or for some other may be left aside; at any rate, what we have described does appear to occur.
D: Elegant:
Friends are needed in both good and bad fortune, but differently. In adversity we need useful friends for help; in prosperity we need good friends to benefit and share life with. Friendship is more necessary in misfortune but more noble in good fortune.

Friends' presence is pleasant in both conditions. Grief is lightened when friends share our sorrow -- whether because they literally bear part of the burden or because their sympathetic presence makes pain more bearable.
D: Summary: We need friends in both prosperity and adversity: useful friends for help in hard times, good friends to benefit and live with in good times. Friendship is more necessary in adversity but more noble in prosperity. The presence of friends lightens grief, whether through shared burden or sympathetic companionship.
D: Distilled: Friendship is more necessary in misfortune but more noble in prosperity -- and in both conditions, friends' presence lightens our burdens and enriches our joys.
Notes: The distinction between friendship being 'more necessary' in adversity and 'more noble' in prosperity reflects Aristotle's hierarchy of goods, where nobility (to kalon) ranks above necessity. -- The question of whether friends literally share our emotional burden or merely reduce it through pleasant presence is psychologically sophisticated and anticipates modern debates about empathy and emotional contagion in psychology.

D: Original: But their presence seems to contain a mixture of various factors. The very seeing of one's friends is pleasant, especially if one is in adversity, and becomes a safeguard against grief (for a friend tends to comfort us both by the sight of him and by his words, if he is tactful, since he knows our character and the things that please or pain us); but to see him pained at our misfortunes is painful; for every one shuns being a cause of pain to his friends. For this reason people of a manly nature guard against making their friends grieve with them, and, unless he be exceptionally insensible to pain, such a man cannot stand the pain that ensues for his friends, and in general does not admit fellow-mourners because he is not himself given to mourning; but women and womanly men enjoy sympathisers in their grief, and love them as friends and companions in sorrow. But in all things one obviously ought to imitate the better type of person.
D: Refashioned:
But the presence of friends seems to involve a mixture of factors. The very sight of friends is pleasant, especially in adversity, and serves as a safeguard against grief -- for a friend tends to comfort us both by his presence and by his words, if he is tactful, since he knows our character and what pleases or pains us. But seeing a friend pained by our misfortunes is itself painful, for everyone avoids being a cause of pain to his friends.

For this reason, people of a manly nature guard against making their friends grieve with them. Unless a person is exceptionally insensible to pain, he cannot stand the distress that his own suffering causes his friends, and in general he does not welcome fellow-mourners because he is not himself given to mourning. But women and people of a womanly nature enjoy having sympathizers in their grief and love them as friends and companions in sorrow.

In all things, however, one should obviously take the better type of person as one's model.
D: Elegant:
Friends' presence in adversity is a complex good. Seeing friends is pleasant and their tactful words comfort us, since they know our character. But seeing them pained by our misfortunes is itself painful, since we dislike causing friends distress.

People of strong character avoid burdening friends with their grief and reject excessive commiseration, while those of weaker temperament welcome sympathizers. In all things, one should model the stronger character.
D: Summary: The comfort of friends in adversity is mixed with the pain of seeing them distressed on our behalf. People of strong character avoid burdening friends with their grief, while others welcome sympathizers. One should model the stronger approach, accepting comfort while avoiding being a cause of unnecessary pain to friends.
D: Distilled: Strong character means accepting comfort from friends while guarding against burdening them with excessive grief -- valuing their wellbeing even in one's own suffering.
Notes: The contrast between 'manly' and 'womanly' responses to grief reflects ancient Greek gender norms, where public emotional display was discouraged for men (cf. Pericles' Funeral Oration in Thucydides II.45). -- The psychological observation about the pain of causing friends to suffer is acute: it recognizes that empathy creates a feedback loop of distress. -- The emphasis on the 'tactful' (epidexios) friend who knows what to say connects to Aristotle's discussion of social virtues in Book IV.6-8.

D: Original: On the other hand, the presence of friends in our prosperity implies both a pleasant passing of our time and the pleasant thought of their pleasure at our own good fortune. For this cause it would seem that we ought to summon our friends readily to share our good fortunes (for the beneficent character is a noble one), but summon them to our bad fortunes with hesitation; for we ought to give them as little a share as possible in our evils whence the saying 'enough is my misfortune'. We should summon friends to us most of all when they are likely by suffering a few inconveniences to do us a great service.
D: Refashioned:
On the other hand, the presence of friends in prosperity carries both a pleasant way of passing time and the pleasant awareness that our friends take pleasure in our good fortune. For this reason, it would seem that we ought to invite friends readily to share in our good fortune -- since a generous character is a noble one -- but summon them to our misfortunes only with hesitation. We ought to give them as little a share as possible in our troubles, hence the saying: "My own misfortune is enough."

We should call on friends most of all when they are able to help us greatly at little cost to themselves.
D: Elegant: In prosperity, we should readily share our good fortune with friends, since generosity is noble. In adversity, we should hesitate to burden them, sharing as little of our troubles as possible. The best time to call on friends is when they can help us greatly at little cost to themselves.
D: Summary: We should eagerly share our prosperity with friends but reluctantly impose our misfortunes on them. The ideal request for help comes when friends can do us great good at little cost to themselves.
D: Distilled: Share your prosperity generously but your misfortune sparingly -- and call on friends most when they can help you greatly with little sacrifice.
Notes: The saying 'enough is my misfortune' (arkei gar hē emē tychē) appears in various forms in Greek literature. -- The principle of minimizing the cost imposed on friends while maximizing benefit anticipates modern economic reasoning about efficient allocation of social capital. -- The characterization of generosity (to euergetikon) as noble connects to the virtue of liberality discussed in Book IV.1.

D: Original:
Conversely, it is fitting to go unasked and readily to the aid of those in adversity (for it is characteristic of a friend to render services, and especially to those who are in need and have not demanded them; such action is nobler and pleasanter for both persons); but when our friends are prosperous we should join readily in their activities (for they need friends for these too), but be tardy in coming forward to be the objects of their kindness; for it is not noble to be keen to receive benefits. Still, we must no doubt avoid getting the reputation of kill-joys by repulsing them; for that sometimes happens.

The presence of friends, then, seems desirable in all circumstances.
D: Refashioned:
Conversely, it is fitting to go to the aid of those in adversity without being asked and without hesitation. For it is characteristic of a friend to render services, especially to those who are in need and have not demanded help; such action is nobler and more pleasant for both parties.

But when friends are prospering, we should join readily in their activities -- for they need friends for these purposes too -- while being slow to come forward as recipients of their generosity, for it is not noble to be eager to receive benefits. Still, we must be careful not to get the reputation of being joyless by pushing friends away; for that sometimes happens.

The presence of friends, then, seems to be desirable in all circumstances.
D: Elegant:
Go to friends in adversity without being asked -- unsolicited help for those in need is nobler and more pleasant for everyone. In friends' prosperity, join their activities readily but be slow to accept their generosity, since eagerness to receive is not noble. Yet avoid repelling friends so firmly that you seem joyless.

Friends' presence is desirable in all circumstances.
D: Summary: One should go to friends in adversity without waiting to be asked, since unsolicited help is nobler. In prosperity, join friends' activities but be reluctant to receive their generosity. Avoid both eagerness to receive and the coldness that repels others. Friends' presence is desirable in all circumstances.
D: Distilled: Go to friends in adversity without being asked, join in their prosperity without seeking their generosity, and never be so self-sufficient that you repel the friends who wish to be present.
Notes: The asymmetry -- rushing to help without being asked, but being slow to receive -- captures an important ethical insight about the relationship between giving and receiving. -- The warning against being a 'kill-joy' (aēdēs) shows Aristotle's awareness that excessive self-reliance can itself become a vice, connecting to his doctrine of the mean. -- This passage concludes the discussion of IX.11 on friendship in prosperity and adversity.

D: Original: Does it not follow, then, that, as for lovers the sight of the beloved is the thing they love most, and they prefer this sense to the others because on it love depends most for its being and for its origin, so for friends the most desirable thing is living together? For friendship is a partnership, and as a man is to himself, so is he to his friend; now in his own case the consciousness of his being is desirable, and so therefore is the consciousness of his friend's being, and the activity of this consciousness is produced when they live together, so that it is natural that they aim at this. And whatever existence means for each class of men, whatever it is for whose sake they value life, in that they wish to occupy themselves with their friends; and so some drink together, others dice together, others join in athletic exercises and hunting, or in the study of philosophy, each class spending their days together in whatever they love most in life; for since they wish to live with their friends, they do and share in those things which give them the sense of living together. Thus the friendship of bad men turns out an evil thing (for because of their instability they unite in bad pursuits, and besides they become evil by becoming like each other), while the friendship of good men is good, being augmented by their companionship; and they are thought to become better too by their activities and by improving each other; for from each other they take the mould of the characteristics they approve-whence the saying 'noble deeds from noble men'.-So much, then, for friendship; our next task must be to discuss pleasure.
D: Refashioned:
Does it not follow, then, that just as for lovers the sight of the beloved is what they love most -- and they prefer the sense of sight to the others because love depends on it most for its existence and its origin -- so for friends the most desirable thing is living together?

For friendship is a partnership, and as a person is to himself, so he is to his friend. Now in his own case, the consciousness of his own existence is desirable; therefore the consciousness of his friend's existence is desirable too. And the activity of this consciousness is produced when they live together; so it is natural that friends aim at this.

Whatever existence means for each kind of person -- whatever it is for the sake of which they value life -- that is what they wish to pursue together with their friends. And so some drink together, others play games together, others join in athletic exercises and hunting, and still others in the study of philosophy -- each group spending their days together in whatever they love most in life. For since they wish to live with their friends, they do and share in those things that give them the sense of living together.

Thus the friendship of bad people turns out to be an evil thing: because of their instability they unite in bad pursuits, and besides, they become evil by becoming like each other. But the friendship of good people is good, being strengthened by their companionship. And they are thought to become better too through their shared activities and by improving each other, for from each other they take the imprint of the qualities they admire -- hence the saying, "Noble deeds from noble men."

So much, then, for friendship. Our next task must be to discuss pleasure.
D: Elegant:
As lovers prize the sight of the beloved above all, friends prize living together above all -- for friendship is a partnership, and the consciousness of a friend's existence is as desirable as consciousness of one's own.

Whatever people value most in life, they wish to share with friends: some drink, some play, some exercise, some philosophize together. The friendship of bad people becomes an evil, since they unite in bad pursuits and make each other worse. But the friendship of good people is strengthened by companionship -- they improve each other by modeling the qualities they admire. 'Noble deeds from noble men.'
D: Summary: Friends, like lovers, prize togetherness above all -- since friendship is a partnership and consciousness of a friend's existence is as desirable as one's own. People share with friends whatever they value most in life. Bad people's friendships make them worse, but good people's friendships make them better, as they shape each other through the qualities they admire.
D: Distilled: The essence of friendship is shared life -- and while bad friends make each other worse, good friends become better by taking the imprint of each other's virtues.
Notes: This final passage of Book IX brings the discussion full circle, connecting the metaphysics of consciousness from IX.9 to the practical reality of shared daily life. -- 'Noble deeds from noble men' (esthlōn ... esthla) is attributed to Theognis (Elegies 35). -- The observation that friends shape each other's character -- for good or ill -- anticipates modern social psychology research on peer effects and behavioral contagion. -- The transition to pleasure (Book X) is significant: Books VIII-IX on friendship and Book X on pleasure together complete Aristotle's account of the components of the good life beyond virtue itself. -- The partnership (koinōnia) language echoes Politics I.1, where every community is established for some good.

D: Original: After these matters we ought perhaps next to discuss pleasure. For it is thought to be most intimately connected with our human nature, which is the reason why in educating the young we steer them by the rudders of pleasure and pain; it is thought, too, that to enjoy the things we ought and to hate the things we ought has the greatest bearing on virtue of character. For these things extend right through life, with a weight and power of their own in respect both to virtue and to the happy life, since men choose what is pleasant and avoid what is painful; and such things, it will be thought, we should least of all omit to discuss, especially since they admit of much dispute. For some say pleasure is the good, while others, on the contrary, say it is thoroughly bad-some no doubt being persuaded that the facts are so, and others thinking it has a better effect on our life to exhibit pleasure as a bad thing even if it is not; for most people (they think) incline towards it and are the slaves of their pleasures, for which reason they ought to lead them in the opposite direction, since thus they will reach the middle state. But surely this is not correct. For arguments about matters concerned with feelings and actions are less reliable than facts: and so when they clash with the facts of perception they are despised, and discredit the truth as well; if a man who runs down pleasure is once seen to be alming at it, his inclining towards it is thought to imply that it is all worthy of being aimed at; for most people are not good at drawing distinctions. True arguments seem, then, most useful, not only with a view to knowledge, but with a view to life also; for since they harmonize with the facts they are believed, and so they stimulate those who understand them to live according to them.-Enough of such questions; let us proceed to review the opinions that have been expressed about pleasure.
D: Refashioned:
After these topics, we should probably turn next to pleasure. Pleasure seems to be deeply bound up with human nature itself, which is precisely why we guide the young by means of pleasure and pain, as if by rudders. Moreover, taking pleasure in the right things and feeling aversion to the wrong ones is thought to matter enormously for developing virtue of character. These responses to pleasure and pain run through our entire lives, carrying great weight and influence over both virtue and the happy life, since people choose what is pleasant and avoid what is painful. It would seem, then, that we should hardly pass over such matters in silence, especially since they are subject to considerable disagreement.

Some thinkers claim that pleasure is the good, while others insist, on the contrary, that it is thoroughly bad. Some of the latter are probably genuinely convinced of this, while others merely think it better for how we live to portray pleasure as bad even if it is not, on the grounds that most people lean toward pleasure and are enslaved by their pleasures, and so ought to be pushed in the opposite direction to arrive at the mean. But surely this approach is mistaken. Arguments about matters involving feelings and actions are less convincing than the facts themselves. When such arguments conflict with what we actually perceive, they are dismissed and end up discrediting the truth along with themselves. If someone who denounces pleasure is then seen pursuing it, his inclination toward it is taken to mean that all pleasure is worth pursuing, since most people are poor at making fine distinctions.

True arguments, then, seem most useful not only for knowledge but also for life. Because they harmonize with the facts, they are believed, and thus they encourage those who understand them to live accordingly. Enough of these preliminary remarks; let us now review the opinions that have been put forward about pleasure.
D: Elegant:
Pleasure is deeply intertwined with human nature, which is why we educate the young through pleasure and pain, and why enjoying the right things and hating the wrong ones is crucial for virtue. Since people choose what is pleasant and avoid what is painful, these matters deserve serious examination despite the controversy surrounding them.

Some say pleasure is the good; others say it is thoroughly bad, whether from conviction or from a pedagogical strategy of pushing people away from their natural excess. But this corrective approach is mistaken, for arguments that clash with observable facts are dismissed and discredit the truth itself. If a critic of pleasure is caught pursuing it, people conclude all pleasure must be worthwhile. True arguments, those that harmonize with facts, are the most useful for both knowledge and life, since they earn trust and inspire people to live accordingly.
D: Summary: Pleasure deserves careful examination because it is central to human nature, moral education, and the pursuit of happiness. Both those who say pleasure is the supreme good and those who call it entirely bad are mistaken in different ways, and arguments about pleasure must be tested against observable facts to be credible and useful.
D: Distilled: Pleasure is so central to human life and moral development that it demands careful, honest analysis rather than either uncritical embrace or strategic denunciation.
Notes: This passage reopens the discussion of pleasure begun in Book VII (chapters 11-14), now with greater precision. Aristotle's methodological remark about arguments harmonizing with facts reflects his broader empirical commitments (cf. De Caelo I.3). -- The strategy of denouncing pleasure to correct popular excess is attributed to unnamed thinkers, possibly Speusippus or Cynics. -- The pedagogical use of pleasure and pain ("rudders") anticipates the discussion of moral habituation in X.9 and connects to II.3, where Aristotle first established pleasure and pain as central to moral training. -- The principle that theory must answer to observed phenomena (phainomena) is a hallmark of Aristotle's method, discussed in detail in VII.1.

D: Original: Eudoxus thought pleasure was the good because he saw all things, both rational and irrational, aiming at it, and because in all things that which is the object of choice is what is excellent, and that which is most the object of choice the greatest good; thus the fact that all things moved towards the same object indicated that this was for all things the chief good (for each thing, he argued, finds its own good, as it finds its own nourishment); and that which is good for all things and at which all aim was the good. His arguments were credited more because of the excellence of his character than for their own sake; he was thought to be remarkably self-controlled, and therefore it was thought that he was not saying what he did say as a friend of pleasure, but that the facts really were so. He believed that the same conclusion followed no less plainly from a study of the contrary of pleasure; pain was in itself an object of aversion to all things, and therefore its contrary must be similarly an object of choice. And again that is most an object of choice which we choose not because or for the sake of something else, and pleasure is admittedly of this nature; for no one asks to what end he is pleased, thus implying that pleasure is in itself an object of choice. Further, he argued that pleasure when added to any good, e.g. to just or temperate action, makes it more worthy of choice, and that it is only by itself that the good can be increased.
D: Refashioned:
Eudoxus held that pleasure was the good because he observed that all things, both rational and non-rational, aim at it, and because in every case the object of choice is what is excellent, and the most chosen thing is the greatest good. The fact that everything moves toward the same object, he argued, shows that this is the chief good for all things, since each thing finds its own good just as it finds its own nourishment. His arguments carried weight more because of the excellence of his character than because of their intrinsic force: he was reputed to be remarkably self-controlled, and so people believed he was not speaking as a partisan of pleasure, but that the facts truly were as he described.

He also believed the same conclusion followed from considering the contrary of pleasure. Pain is in itself something all things avoid, and therefore its contrary must be something all things choose. Furthermore, that is most choiceworthy which we choose not for the sake of anything else, and pleasure is admittedly of this nature, since no one asks to what end he is pleased, implying that pleasure is choiceworthy in itself. Finally, he argued that pleasure, when added to any good — for example, to just or temperate action — makes it more worthy of choice, and that the good can only be increased by itself.
D: Elegant: Eudoxus argued that pleasure is the good on three grounds: all beings, rational and non-rational alike, aim at it; it is chosen for its own sake, since no one asks why they take pleasure; and adding pleasure to any good makes that good more choiceworthy. His arguments gained credibility from his exceptional self-discipline — people trusted he spoke from conviction about the facts, not from personal indulgence.
D: Summary: Eudoxus argued that pleasure is the supreme good because all creatures pursue it, it is desired for its own sake rather than as a means to something else, and it enhances every good to which it is added. His well-known self-control lent his arguments special credibility.
D: Distilled: Eudoxus's case that pleasure is the good rests on its universality as an object of pursuit, its being chosen for its own sake, and its power to enhance every other good.
Notes: Eudoxus of Cnidus (c. 390-340 BCE) was a distinguished mathematician and astronomer who studied at Plato's Academy. He is credited with the theory of homocentric spheres in astronomy and the method of exhaustion in mathematics. -- His hedonistic ethics was unusual for an Academic philosopher, and Aristotle's note that his character lent his arguments credibility is significant: it suggests that in ethical argument, the ethos of the speaker matters. -- The argument that what all things aim at is the good echoes the opening of the Nicomachean Ethics (I.1). -- The final argument (that the good can be increased only by itself) is the one Plato deploys against Eudoxus in the Philebus (20c-22b), as Aristotle notes in the next passage.

D: Original: This argument seems to show it to be one of the goods, and no more a good than any other; for every good is more worthy of choice along with another good than taken alone. And so it is by an argument of this kind that Plato proves the good not to be pleasure; he argues that the pleasant life is more desirable with wisdom than without, and that if the mixture is better, pleasure is not the good; for the good cannot become more desirable by the addition of anything to it. Now it is clear that nothing else, any more than pleasure, can be the good if it is made more desirable by the addition of any of the things that are good in themselves. What, then, is there that satisfies this criterion, which at the same time we can participate in? It is something of this sort that we are looking for. Those who object that that at which all things aim is not necessarily good are, we may surmise, talking nonsense. For we say that that which every one thinks really is so; and the man who attacks this belief will hardly have anything more credible to maintain instead. If it is senseless creatures that desire the things in question, there might be something in what they say; but if intelligent creatures do so as well, what sense can there be in this view? But perhaps even in inferior creatures there is some natural good stronger than themselves which aims at their proper good.
D: Refashioned:
This last argument, however, seems only to show that pleasure is one among the goods, and no more a good than any other. For every good becomes more choiceworthy when combined with another good than when taken alone. In fact, it is by precisely this kind of reasoning that Plato argues that pleasure is not the good: he contends that the pleasant life is more desirable with wisdom than without, and that if the mixture is better than pleasure alone, then pleasure cannot be the good, since the good cannot become more desirable by the addition of anything to it.

Now it is clear that nothing else can be the good either, if it too becomes more desirable when something intrinsically good is added to it. What, then, satisfies this criterion while also being something in which we can share? That is what we are looking for.

As for those who object that what all things aim at is not necessarily good, they are, we may suppose, talking nonsense. What everyone believes really is so, and anyone who attacks this conviction will hardly have anything more credible to offer in its place. If only mindless creatures desired these things, there might be something to the objection. But when rational beings desire them too, what force can the objection have? Perhaps even in lower creatures there is some natural good, something stronger than the creatures themselves, that aims at their proper good.
D: Elegant:
Eudoxus's argument that pleasure enhances any good actually proves only that pleasure is one good among many, not the good. Plato uses the same reasoning: since a pleasant life combined with wisdom is better than pleasure alone, pleasure cannot be the supreme good — for the supreme good cannot be improved by additions. Yet nothing else passes this test either, so the question of what the good is remains open.

The objection that universal pursuit does not prove something good is weak: what everyone believes carries real weight, and the objector has nothing more credible to offer. Even non-rational creatures may possess a natural orientation toward their proper good.
D: Summary: Eudoxus's argument that pleasure enhances other goods actually proves it is merely one good among many, not the supreme good, since the supreme good cannot be improved by additions. Plato exploits this same logic. Meanwhile, the universal pursuit of pleasure by all creatures — rational and non-rational alike — is strong evidence that it is genuinely good, even if not the highest good.
D: Distilled: Pleasure is genuinely a good, since all creatures pursue it, but the fact that it can be enhanced by combining it with wisdom shows it cannot be the supreme good.
Notes: Plato's argument appears in the Philebus (20c-22b), where Socrates argues that neither pure pleasure nor pure intellect constitutes the good life, but rather a mixture of both. -- The principle that the good cannot be made more desirable by additions is a key criterion throughout ancient ethics. -- Aristotle's defense of common opinion (endoxa) — that what all believe is so — is methodologically significant and connects to his dialectical method in the Topics. -- Speusippus, Plato's nephew and successor as head of the Academy, held that pleasure was neither good nor bad, which Aristotle critiques in the next passage.

D: Original: Nor does the argument about the contrary of pleasure seem to be correct. They say that if pain is an evil it does not follow that pleasure is a good; for evil is opposed to evil and at the same time both are opposed to the neutral state-which is correct enough but does not apply to the things in question. For if both pleasure and pain belonged to the class of evils they ought both to be objects of aversion, while if they belonged to the class of neutrals neither should be an object of aversion or they should both be equally so; but in fact people evidently avoid the one as evil and choose the other as good; that then must be the nature of the opposition between them.
D: Refashioned: Nor does the argument about the contrary of pleasure seem correct. Some say that even if pain is an evil, it does not follow that pleasure is a good, since one evil can be opposed to another evil, and both can also be opposed to a neutral state. This is logically correct as a general point, but it does not apply to the case at hand. For if both pleasure and pain belonged to the class of evils, they would both be things to avoid. And if they both belonged to the class of neutrals, neither should be avoided, or both should be avoided equally. But in fact people evidently shun pain as an evil and choose pleasure as a good. That, then, must be the real nature of the opposition between them.
D: Elegant: The argument that pleasure need not be good even if pain is evil — because two evils can be opposed, and both can be opposed to a neutral — is logically valid in the abstract but fails when applied to pleasure and pain. If both were evils, both would be avoided. If both were neutrals, neither would be. In reality, people shun pain as evil and choose pleasure as good, revealing the true nature of their opposition.
D: Summary: The claim that pleasure is not necessarily good even though pain is evil rests on abstract logic about opposites but fails in practice: people consistently avoid pain as bad and choose pleasure as good, which reveals that they are genuinely opposed as evil to good.
D: Distilled: The real opposition between pleasure and pain is not evil-to-evil or neutral-to-neutral, but evil-to-good, as shown by how people actually experience and respond to them.
Notes: This argument is directed against Speusippus (c. 408-339 BCE), Plato's nephew and successor as head of the Academy, who held that both pleasure and pain were evils, with the good being a neutral state. -- Aristotle's response draws on his characteristic method of testing logical arguments against observed human behavior. -- The logical point about contraries and intermediates draws on the Categories (chs. 10-11) where Aristotle discusses types of opposition. -- This passage also revisits the treatment of pleasure's relationship to pain in VII.11-14.

D: Original: Nor again, if pleasure is not a quality, does it follow that it is not a good; for the activities of virtue are not qualities either, nor is happiness. They say, however, that the good is determinate, while pleasure is indeterminate, because it admits of degrees. Now if it is from the feeling of pleasure that they judge thus, the same will be true of justice and the other virtues, in respect of which we plainly say that people of a certain character are so more or less, and act more or less in accordance with these virtues; for people may be more just or brave, and it is possible also to act justly or temperately more or less. But if their judgement is based on the various pleasures, surely they are not stating the real cause, if in fact some pleasures are unmixed and others mixed. Again, just as health admits of degrees without being indeterminate, why should not pleasure? The same proportion is not found in all things, nor a single proportion always in the same thing, but it may be relaxed and yet persist up to a point, and it may differ in degree. The case of pleasure also may therefore be of this kind.
D: Refashioned:
Nor again does it follow that pleasure is not a good because it is not a quality. The activities of virtue are not qualities either, nor is happiness. Some say the good is determinate while pleasure is indeterminate because it admits of degrees. But if their judgment rests on the feeling of pleasure, the same will hold true of justice and the other virtues, in regard to which we readily say that people have them to a greater or lesser degree and act more or less in accordance with them. People can be more just or more brave, and it is possible to act justly or temperately more or less.

But if their judgment is based on the different kinds of pleasure, they are surely not identifying the real cause — not if some pleasures are unmixed and others are mixed. Moreover, just as health admits of degrees without being indeterminate, why should pleasure not be the same? The same proportion is not found in all things, nor does a single proportion always persist in the same thing, but it can relax and still continue up to a point, and it can differ in degree. The case of pleasure may therefore be of this kind as well.
D: Elegant: Pleasure is not disqualified as a good simply because it is not a quality — the activities of virtue and happiness are not qualities either. Nor is it disqualified for admitting of degrees, since justice, courage, and all the virtues also admit of degrees. Just as health varies in degree without being indeterminate, so too can pleasure vary while remaining a genuine good.
D: Summary: Two objections against pleasure being a good — that it is not a quality and that it is indeterminate because it admits of degrees — both fail. Virtue and happiness are not qualities either, and virtues like justice and courage also come in degrees. Health provides a clear analogy: it varies in degree without being indeterminate.
D: Distilled: Pleasure's admitting of degrees no more disqualifies it as a good than the fact that justice, courage, and health also come in degrees.
Notes: The objection that pleasure is not a quality likely comes from Platonic or Academic critics drawing on the Categories. -- The claim that the good must be determinate while pleasure is indeterminate reflects Plato's association of the good with limit (peras) and pleasure with the unlimited (apeiron) in the Philebus (23c-27c). -- The distinction between mixed and unmixed pleasures also echoes the Philebus (50e-53c). -- Aristotle's analogy with health anticipates his broader use of health as a model for understanding variable states throughout his ethical and biological works.

D: Original: Again, they assume that the good is perfect while movements and comings into being are imperfect, and try to exhibit pleasure as being a movement and a coming into being. But they do not seem to be right even in saying that it is a movement. For speed and slowness are thought to be proper to every movement, and if a movement, e.g. that of the heavens, has not speed or slowness in itself, it has it in relation to something else; but of pleasure neither of these things is true. For while we may become pleased quickly as we may become angry quickly, we cannot be pleased quickly, not even in relation to some one else, while we can walk, or grow, or the like, quickly. While, then, we can change quickly or slowly into a state of pleasure, we cannot quickly exhibit the activity of pleasure, i.e. be pleased. Again, how can it be a coming into being? It is not thought that any chance thing can come out of any chance thing, but that a thing is dissolved into that out of which it comes into being; and pain would be the destruction of that of which pleasure is the coming into being.
D: Refashioned:
Again, some assume that the good is perfect and complete while processes of motion and coming-into-being are imperfect, and they try to show that pleasure is a kind of motion or coming-into-being. But they do not seem to be right even in calling it a motion. Speed and slowness are thought to belong to every motion, and if a particular motion — such as the revolution of the heavens — does not have speed or slowness in itself, it has them in relation to something else. But neither of these applies to pleasure. We can become pleased quickly, just as we can become angry quickly, but we cannot be pleased quickly — not even relative to someone else — though we can walk, grow, or do similar things quickly. The transition into a state of pleasure can happen quickly or slowly, but the actual activity of being pleased cannot.

Moreover, how can pleasure be a coming-into-being? It is not thought that any random thing comes into being from any random thing; rather, a thing dissolves back into whatever it came into being from. If pleasure were a coming-into-being, then pain would have to be the destruction of whatever pleasure is the coming-into-being of.
D: Elegant: Those who classify pleasure as a motion or a coming-into-being, on the grounds that these are imperfect while the good must be perfect, are mistaken. Motion involves speed and slowness, but while we can become pleased quickly, we cannot be pleased quickly — the activity of pleasure itself does not admit of speed. Nor can pleasure be a coming-into-being, since that would require pain to be the destruction of whatever pleasure brings into existence.
D: Summary: Pleasure cannot be classified as a motion or a process of coming-into-being. Motions involve speed and slowness, but the activity of being pleased does not. And if pleasure were a process of becoming, pain would have to be the corresponding destruction, which does not fit the facts.
D: Distilled: Pleasure is neither a motion nor a process of becoming, since it lacks the defining features of both — speed and slowness in the one case, a correlative destruction in the other.
Notes: The view that pleasure is a motion (kinesis) or coming-into-being (genesis) derives from Plato's Philebus (53c-55a) and possibly from some Academy members. -- Aristotle's discussion of motion draws on his precise analysis in the Physics (Books III and V), where he distinguishes kinesis from energeia (activity). -- The distinction between becoming pleased (a transition) and being pleased (an activity) is crucial to Aristotle's positive account of pleasure in X.4. -- The example of celestial motion reflects Aristotle's cosmology in De Caelo.

D: Original: They say, too, that pain is the lack of that which is according to nature, and pleasure is replenishment. But these experiences are bodily. If then pleasure is replenishment with that which is according to nature, that which feels pleasure will be that in which the replenishment takes place, i.e. the body; but that is not thought to be the case; therefore the replenishment is not pleasure, though one would be pleased when replenishment was taking place, just as one would be pained if one was being operated on. This opinion seems to be based on the pains and pleasures connected with nutrition; on the fact that when people have been short of food and have felt pain beforehand they are pleased by the replenishment. But this does not happen with all pleasures; for the pleasures of learning and, among the sensuous pleasures, those of smell, and also many sounds and sights, and memories and hopes, do not presuppose pain. Of what then will these be the coming into being? There has not been lack of anything of which they could be the supplying anew.
D: Refashioned:
Some also say that pain is the lack of what accords with nature, and that pleasure is the replenishment of that lack. But these are bodily experiences. If pleasure is replenishment of what accords with nature, then whatever feels pleasure must be the thing being replenished — that is, the body. But this is not thought to be right. Therefore replenishment is not the same as pleasure, though someone might feel pleased while replenishment is occurring, just as someone might feel pain while being operated on.

This view seems to rest on the pains and pleasures connected with eating and drinking — the fact that when people have gone without food and suffered from the lack, they are pleased by being fed. But this does not happen with all pleasures. The pleasures of learning, for instance, involve no prior pain, and neither do many sensory pleasures — those of smell, many sounds and sights, and also memories and hopes. Of what, then, are these the coming-into-being? There was no prior lack of anything for them to be the replenishing of.
D: Elegant: The theory that pleasure is the replenishment of a natural lack works only for certain bodily pleasures like eating after hunger. It cannot account for the pleasures of learning, the enjoyment of fragrances, beautiful sounds and sights, or the delight of memory and hope — none of which presuppose any prior deficiency that needs filling.
D: Summary: The view that pleasure is the replenishment of a natural deficiency fails because many pleasures — such as learning, perceiving beautiful sights, sounds, and smells, and enjoying memories and hopes — do not involve any prior lack or pain that requires restoration.
D: Distilled: Pleasure cannot be mere replenishment of a lack, since the pleasures of learning, perception, memory, and hope arise without any prior deficiency.
Notes: The replenishment (anaplerosis) theory of pleasure derives from Plato's discussions in the Philebus (31d-32b) and Timaeus (64a-65b), where pleasure is restoration of the natural state after depletion. -- Aristotle's counterexamples — learning, smell, music, visual beauty, memory, hope — are chosen to show that the theory reduces all pleasure to the bodily and nutritive model. -- The pleasures of learning play a central role for Aristotle; cf. Metaphysics I.1 ("all men by nature desire to know") and Rhetoric I.11. -- This argument also connects to De Anima II-III on the different modalities of sense perception.

D: Original: In reply to those who bring forward the disgraceful pleasures one may say that these are not pleasant; if things are pleasant to people of vicious constitution, we must not suppose that they are also pleasant to others than these, just as we do not reason so about the things that are wholesome or sweet or bitter to sick people, or ascribe whiteness to the things that seem white to those suffering from a disease of the eye. Or one might answer thus-that the pleasures are desirable, but not from these sources, as wealth is desirable, but not as the reward of betrayal, and health, but not at the cost of eating anything and everything. Or perhaps pleasures differ in kind; for those derived from noble sources are different from those derived from base sources, and one cannot the pleasure of the just man without being just, nor that of the musical man without being musical, and so on.
D: Refashioned:
In reply to those who cite disgraceful pleasures as evidence against pleasure, one may respond that these are not truly pleasant. If things are pleasant to people of corrupt constitution, we need not assume they are pleasant to anyone else, just as we do not take what seems wholesome, sweet, or bitter to sick people as evidence of what is truly wholesome, sweet, or bitter, nor do we ascribe whiteness to things that appear white to those with diseased eyes.

Alternatively, one might respond that pleasures are indeed desirable, but not from every source — just as wealth is desirable but not as the reward for betrayal, and health is desirable but not at the cost of eating anything whatsoever.
