Or perhaps pleasures differ in kind: those derived from noble sources are genuinely different from those derived from base ones. One cannot experience the pleasure of the just person without being just, nor the pleasure of the musical person without being musical, and so on for the rest.
D: Elegant: Disgraceful pleasures do not discredit pleasure as such. What seems pleasant to a corrupt person need not be truly pleasant, just as what tastes sweet to a sick person need not be truly sweet. Alternatively, pleasure is desirable but not from every source — just as wealth is desirable but not from betrayal. Or perhaps pleasures simply differ in kind: the pleasure of the just person requires being just, and the pleasure of the musician requires being musical.
D: Summary: The existence of disgraceful pleasures does not prove that pleasure itself is bad. Either these are not truly pleasant but only seem so to corrupted people, or pleasures are desirable only from certain sources, or pleasures genuinely differ in kind — the pleasure of a just person can only be felt by someone who is just.
D: Distilled: Disgraceful pleasures do not indict pleasure itself, since they may not be truly pleasant, may come from improper sources, or may simply be different in kind from noble pleasures.
Notes: The argument from disgraceful pleasures was a standard objection in the Academy. -- Aristotle's response using the analogy of sick perception draws on his epistemology: the properly functioning perceiver is the standard of truth (cf. Metaphysics IV.5 on the truthfulness of normal perception). -- The idea that pleasures differ in kind is central to Aristotle's positive theory in X.5. -- The claim that one cannot feel the just person's pleasure without being just connects to II.3 on the role of pleasure in demonstrating genuine virtue: the truly virtuous person takes pleasure in virtuous action. -- This anticipates the "standard of the good person" argument in X.22.

D: Original: The fact, too, that a friend is different from a flatterer seems to make it plain that pleasure is not a good or that pleasures are different in kind; for the one is thought to consort with us with a view to the good, the other with a view to our pleasure, and the one is reproached for his conduct while the other is praised on the ground that he consorts with us for different ends. And no one would choose to live with the intellect of a child throughout his life, however much he were to be pleased at the things that children are pleased at, nor to get enjoyment by doing some most disgraceful deed, though he were never to feel any pain in consequence. And there are many things we should be keen about even if they brought no pleasure, e.g. seeing, remembering, knowing, possessing the virtues. If pleasures necessarily do accompany these, that makes no odds; we should choose these even if no pleasure resulted. It seems to be clear, then, that neither is pleasure the good nor is all pleasure desirable, and that some pleasures are desirable in themselves, differing in kind or in their sources from the others. So much for the things that are said about pleasure and pain.
D: Refashioned:
The difference between a friend and a flatterer also makes it clear that pleasure is not the good, or that pleasures differ in kind. The friend is thought to associate with us for the sake of our good, the flatterer for the sake of our pleasure, and the flatterer is reproached while the friend is praised, precisely because they associate with us for different purposes.

Moreover, no one would choose to live their entire life with the mind of a child, however much pleasure they might take in childish things; nor would anyone choose to enjoy some utterly disgraceful act, even if no pain ever followed from it. And there are many things we would eagerly pursue even if they brought no pleasure at all — seeing, remembering, knowing, possessing the virtues. If pleasures necessarily accompany these, that makes no difference; we would choose them even if no pleasure resulted.

It seems clear, then, that pleasure is neither the good nor that all pleasure is desirable, but that some pleasures are desirable in themselves, differing in kind or in their sources from others. Let this suffice for our review of what has been said about pleasure and pain.
D: Elegant:
The contrast between a friend and a flatterer shows either that pleasure is not the good, or that pleasures differ in kind: one associates with us for our good, the other for our pleasure. No one would choose a lifetime of childish pleasure or enjoy a disgraceful act without consequence. And we would pursue seeing, knowing, remembering, and possessing virtue even if no pleasure came with them.

Pleasure is therefore neither the good nor universally desirable; some pleasures are worthwhile in themselves, differing from others in kind or source.
D: Summary: The distinction between friends and flatterers, the absurdity of choosing a lifetime of childish pleasure, and our willingness to pursue knowledge and virtue even without pleasure all demonstrate that pleasure is not the supreme good. Still, some pleasures are genuinely desirable in themselves, differing from inferior pleasures in kind or source.
D: Distilled: We would choose seeing, knowing, and virtue even without pleasure, proving pleasure is not the supreme good, though some pleasures are intrinsically worthwhile.
Notes: The friend-flatterer distinction connects to the extensive treatment of friendship in Books VIII-IX, especially VIII.3-4 on the three types of friendship (utility, pleasure, virtue). -- The thought experiment about living with a child's mind parallels the famous "experience machine" argument later formulated by Robert Nozick (Anarchy, State, and Utopia, 1974), challenging hedonism by showing we value more than subjective experience. -- The claim that seeing, remembering, and knowing are intrinsically valuable connects to Metaphysics I.1 and to Aristotle's broader commitment to theoria as the highest human good, which he develops in X.7-8. -- The conclusion that pleasures differ in kind is the bridge to Aristotle's own positive theory of pleasure beginning in X.4.

D: Original: What pleasure is, or what kind of thing it is, will become plainer if we take up the question aga from the beginning. Seeing seems to be at any moment complete, for it does not lack anything which coming into being later will complete its form; and pleasure also seems to be of this nature. For it is a whole, and at no time can one find a pleasure whose form will be completed if the pleasure lasts longer. For this reason, too, it is not a movement. For every movement (e.g. that of building) takes time and is for the sake of an end, and is complete when it has made what it aims at. It is complete, therefore, only in the whole time or at that final moment. In their parts and during the time they occupy, all movements are incomplete, and are different in kind from the whole movement and from each other.
D: Refashioned: What pleasure is, or what kind of thing it is, will become clearer if we take up the question again from the beginning. Seeing seems to be complete at every moment: it does not lack anything that would come into being later to complete its form. Pleasure too seems to be of this nature. It is a whole, and at no point can one find a pleasure whose form would be completed by the pleasure lasting longer. For this reason, too, pleasure is not a motion. Every motion — building, for example — takes time and is directed toward an end, and is complete only when it has achieved what it aims at. A motion is complete only in the whole time it takes, or at the final moment. In their parts and during the time they occupy, all motions are incomplete and differ in kind both from the whole motion and from each other.
D: Elegant: Pleasure, like seeing, is complete at every moment — it does not become more complete by lasting longer. This distinguishes it fundamentally from motion. Every motion, such as building, takes time, aims at an end beyond itself, and is complete only in its entirety or at the final moment. Its parts are incomplete and differ in kind from the whole. Pleasure has none of these features.
D: Summary: Aristotle begins his positive account of pleasure by arguing that pleasure, like the act of seeing, is complete at every moment rather than building toward completion over time. This distinguishes pleasure fundamentally from motion, which is always incomplete until it reaches its end.
D: Distilled: Pleasure is complete at every instant, unlike motion, which is inherently incomplete until it achieves its end.
Notes: This passage marks the beginning of Aristotle's own positive theory of pleasure, after the critical review of others' views in X.1-5. -- The distinction between energeia (activity, complete at every moment) and kinesis (motion, incomplete until reaching its goal) is one of Aristotle's most important philosophical contributions, developed in Metaphysics IX.6 (1048b18-35). -- The example of seeing as an activity complete at every moment is Aristotle's paradigm case of energeia in the Metaphysics. -- This analysis supersedes and refines the earlier treatment of pleasure in Book VII.11-14, which was more provisional. -- The contrast between pleasure and motion/process has been influential in philosophy of action, particularly in distinguishing activities from performances (Gilbert Ryle, Anthony Kenny).

D: Original: For the fitting together of the stones is different from the fluting of the column, and these are both different from the making of the temple; and the making of the temple is complete (for it lacks nothing with a view to the end proposed), but the making of the base or of the triglyph is incomplete; for each is the making of only a part. They differ in kind, then, and it is not possible to find at any and every time a movement complete in form, but if at all, only in the whole time. So, too, in the case of walking and all other movements.
D: Refashioned: For the fitting together of stones is different from the fluting of a column, and both are different from the construction of the temple as a whole. The construction of the temple is complete — it lacks nothing relative to its intended end — but the making of the base or the triglyph is incomplete, since each is only the making of a part. These sub-processes differ in kind from one another, and it is impossible to find at any arbitrary moment a motion that is complete in form; if at all, only in the whole span of time. The same holds for walking and all other motions.
D: Elegant: Fitting stones, fluting columns, and building the whole temple are different in kind. The temple's construction is complete only when finished; building a base or triglyph is inherently incomplete, being only part of the whole. No motion can be found complete at an arbitrary moment — only in its full duration.
D: Summary: The sub-processes of building — fitting stones, carving details, constructing the whole — differ in kind from each other and are each incomplete until the whole project is finished, illustrating that motion is never complete at an arbitrary moment.
D: Distilled: The parts of a building process differ in kind from each other and from the whole, and none is complete until the entire project reaches its end.
Notes: The architectural examples (base, triglyph, column) are drawn from Greek temple construction, reflecting Aristotle's familiarity with the building projects of 4th-century Athens. -- A triglyph is a three-grooved block in a Doric frieze, a characteristic element of Greek temple architecture. -- This concrete illustration of kinesis being incomplete in its parts reinforces the abstract distinction between kinesis and energeia from Metaphysics IX.6. -- The point that parts of a motion differ in kind from the whole is crucial: a half-completed building is not a building in any complete sense.

D: Original: For if locomotion is a movement from to there, it, too, has differences in kind-flying, walking, leaping, and so on. And not only so, but in walking itself there are such differences; for the whence and whither are not the same in the whole racecourse and in a part of it, nor in one part and in another, nor is it the same thing to traverse this line and that; for one traverses not only a line but one which is in a place, and this one is in a different place from that. We have discussed movement with precision in another work, but it seems that it is not complete at any and every time, but that the many movements are incomplete and different in kind, since the whence and whither give them their form.
D: Refashioned:
If locomotion is a motion from one place to another, it too has differences in kind — flying, walking, leaping, and so on. And not only that: within walking itself there are such differences. The starting point and destination are not the same for the whole racecourse as for a part of it, nor the same in one part as in another. Nor is traversing one line the same as traversing another, since one traverses not merely a line in the abstract but a line in a particular place, and one stretch of ground is in a different place from another.

We have discussed motion with precision elsewhere, but it appears that motion is never complete at any arbitrary moment. Rather, the many constituent motions are incomplete and different in kind from one another, since their starting points and endpoints give them their specific character.
D: Elegant: Even walking, a single type of locomotion, contains internal differences: traversing the first part of a racecourse differs from traversing the last, since each segment has different starting and ending points that give it its specific character. Motion is never complete at an arbitrary moment; its many phases are incomplete and differ in kind from one another.
D: Summary: Even within a single type of motion like walking, each segment differs in kind because the starting and ending points differ. This confirms that motion is inherently incomplete at any given moment, with its parts being different in kind from each other and from the whole.
D: Distilled: Each segment of a motion differs in kind from every other, since the specific character of any motion is determined by its starting and ending points.
Notes: The reference to having "discussed motion with precision in another work" points to the Physics, particularly Books III, V, and VI, where Aristotle analyzes motion (kinesis), continuity, and the infinite divisibility of spatial magnitudes. -- The racecourse example is vivid: Greek athletic stadiums were common reference points. -- The point that the whence (pothen) and whither (poi) define the character of a motion connects to Physics V.1, where changes are classified by their termini. -- This analysis supports the broader metaphysical distinction between processes (kinesis) and activities (energeia) that is foundational to Aristotle's theory of pleasure.

D: Original: But of pleasure the form is complete at any and every time. Plainly, then, pleasure and movement must be different from each other, and pleasure must be one of the things that are whole and complete. This would seem to be the case, too, from the fact that it is not possible to move otherwise than in time, but it is possible to be pleased; for that which takes place in a moment is a whole.
D: Refashioned: But the form of pleasure is complete at any and every moment. Plainly, then, pleasure and motion must be different from each other, and pleasure must be one of the things that are whole and complete. This would also seem to follow from the fact that motion cannot occur except in time, whereas it is possible to be pleased in a moment — for what takes place in an instant is a whole.
D: Elegant: Pleasure's form is complete at every moment, making it fundamentally different from motion. Motion requires time and is always incomplete in its parts; pleasure can occur in an instant and is always a whole.
D: Summary: Since pleasure is complete at every moment while motion is always incomplete until finished, pleasure must be categorically different from motion and must belong to the class of things that are whole and complete.
D: Distilled: Pleasure is complete at every instant and therefore belongs to the category of wholes, not to the category of motions.
Notes: This is the culmination of Aristotle's argument distinguishing pleasure from kinesis. The claim that pleasure is complete at every moment (teleion en hōtōioun chronōi) identifies pleasure as an energeia in the strict sense of Metaphysics IX.6. -- The temporal criterion — motion requires extended time while pleasure can be instantaneous — is a key differentiator. -- This has important implications: if pleasure is an activity rather than a process, it cannot be identified with bodily replenishment or any kind of becoming, which are processes by nature. -- J.L. Ackrill and others have debated whether Aristotle's energeia/kinesis distinction is primarily temporal or teleological.

D: Original: From these considerations it is clear, too, that these thinkers are not right in saying there is a movement or a coming into being of pleasure. For these cannot be ascribed to all things, but only to those that are divisible and not wholes; there is no coming into being of seeing nor of a point nor of a unit, nor is any of these a movement or coming into being; therefore there is no movement or coming into being of pleasure either; for it is a whole.
D: Refashioned: From these considerations it is also clear that those thinkers are wrong to speak of a motion or a coming-into-being of pleasure. Motion and coming-into-being can only be attributed to things that are divisible and not wholes. There is no coming-into-being of seeing, nor of a point, nor of a unit, and none of these is a motion or coming-into-being. Therefore there is no motion or coming-into-being of pleasure either, since pleasure is a whole.
D: Elegant: Since motion and coming-into-being belong only to divisible things that are not wholes, and pleasure — like seeing, a point, or a unit — is a whole and indivisible, pleasure cannot be a motion or coming-into-being.
D: Summary: Motion and coming-into-being can only be ascribed to divisible things, not to wholes. Since pleasure, like seeing or a geometric point, is complete and indivisible at every moment, it cannot be classified as either a motion or a coming-into-being.
D: Distilled: Pleasure, being whole and indivisible like an act of seeing, is categorically excluded from the class of motions and processes of becoming.
Notes: This passage concludes the negative argument against pleasure-as-kinesis and pleasure-as-genesis. -- The analogy with points and units draws on Aristotle's philosophy of mathematics; a point has no parts and thus cannot undergo a process of becoming (cf. Metaphysics V.6, XIII.2). -- The comparison of pleasure with seeing reinforces Aristotle's paradigm: seeing is the canonical example of energeia in Metaphysics IX.6 ("one sees and at the same time has seen"). -- Having disposed of these rival theories, Aristotle now turns to his positive account of how pleasure relates to activity.

D: Original: Since every sense is active in relation to its object, and a sense which is in good condition acts perfectly in relation to the most beautiful of its objects (for perfect activity seems to be ideally of this nature; whether we say that it is active, or the organ in which it resides, may be assumed to be immaterial), it follows that in the case of each sense the best activity is that of the best-conditioned organ in relation to the finest of its objects. And this activity will be the most complete and pleasant. For, while there is pleasure in respect of any sense, and in respect of thought and contemplation no less, the most complete is pleasantest, and that of a well-conditioned organ in relation to the worthiest of its objects is the most complete; and the pleasure completes the activity. But the pleasure does not complete it in the same way as the combination of object and sense, both good, just as health and the doctor are not in the same way the cause of a man's being healthy. (That pleasure is produced in respect to each sense is plain; for we speak of sights and sounds as pleasant. It is also plain that it arises most of all when both the sense is at its best and it is active in reference to an object which corresponds; when both object and perceiver are of the best there will always be pleasure, since the requisite agent and patient are both present.) Pleasure completes the activity not as the corresponding permanent state does, by its immanence, but as an end which supervenes as the bloom of youth does on those in the flower of their age. So long, then, as both the intelligible or sensible object and the discriminating or contemplative faculty are as they should be, the pleasure will be involved in the activity; for when both the passive and the active factor are unchanged and are related to each other in the same way, the same result naturally follows.
D: Refashioned:
Since every sense is active in relation to its object, and a sense in good condition acts perfectly in relation to the finest of its objects (for perfect activity seems to be of this nature — whether we attribute it to the sense itself or to the organ in which it resides makes no difference for our purposes), it follows that in each case the best activity is that of the best-conditioned organ in relation to the most excellent of its objects. And this activity will be the most complete and pleasant.

For there is pleasure attending every sense, and no less attending thought and contemplation. The most complete activity is the most pleasant, and the most complete is that of a well-conditioned faculty in relation to the worthiest of its objects. Pleasure completes the activity — though not in the same way as the combination of a fine object and a sound sense does. Just as health and the doctor are not causes of a person's being healthy in the same way, so too pleasure and the object-sense combination do not complete the activity in the same way.

That pleasure is produced in connection with each sense is clear, since we speak of sights and sounds as pleasant. It is also clear that pleasure arises most of all when the sense is at its best and is directed at an object that matches it. When both object and perceiver are at their best, pleasure will always be present, since the active and passive factors are both there.

Pleasure completes the activity not as the inherent state or disposition does, by being internal to it, but as a supervening end — like the bloom of youth on those in their prime. So long as the object of intellect or perception and the faculty of judgment or contemplation are as they should be, pleasure will be involved in the activity. For when the active and passive factors are unchanged and in the right relation, the same result naturally follows.
D: Elegant:
Each sense achieves its best activity when in good condition and directed at its finest object, and this optimal activity is the most complete and pleasant. Pleasure accompanies every form of perception and thought, arising most fully when faculty and object are both at their best.

Pleasure completes the activity — not as an inherent state like a disposition, but as something that supervenes upon it, the way the bloom of youth crowns those in their prime. Whenever perceiver and perceived, or thinker and object of thought, are both in excellent condition and properly related, pleasure naturally follows.
D: Summary: Pleasure arises from and completes every well-functioning activity of sense or thought. The best pleasure accompanies the best activity — when a faculty in peak condition engages with the finest object. Pleasure does not complete activity as an internal disposition does, but supervenes upon it like the bloom that crowns youth.
D: Distilled: Pleasure is the natural bloom that supervenes on any activity when both the faculty and its object are functioning at their best.
Notes: The image of pleasure as a bloom (hoion tois akmaiois he hora) supervening on activity is one of the most famous in Aristotle's corpus. -- The distinction between pleasure completing activity and the way a disposition (hexis) completes it is subtle: a hexis is internal to the possessor, while pleasure comes as an additional perfection from outside, so to speak. -- This connects to De Anima II.5 on the relationship between sense faculties and their objects, and to III.4-5 on intellect and its objects. -- The agent-patient (active-passive) framework reflects Aristotle's general metaphysics of potentiality and actuality. -- Aquinas developed this account extensively in Summa Theologiae I-II, Q.31-34, using Aristotle's analysis as the basis for his own theory of pleasure (delectatio).

D: Original: How, then, is it that no one is continuously pleased? Is it that we grow weary? Certainly all human beings are incapable of continuous activity. Therefore pleasure also is not continuous; for it accompanies activity. Some things delight us when they are new, but later do so less, for the same reason; for at first the mind is in a state of stimulation and intensely active about them, as people are with respect to their vision when they look hard at a thing, but afterwards our activity is not of this kind, but has grown relaxed; for which reason the pleasure also is dulled.
D: Refashioned: How is it, then, that no one experiences pleasure continuously? Is it not because we grow weary? All human beings are incapable of continuous activity, and since pleasure accompanies activity, pleasure too cannot be continuous. Some things delight us when they are new but later do so less, for the same reason. At first the mind is stimulated and intensely active about them, as when people stare hard at something, but afterward the activity relaxes and becomes slack, and the pleasure is dulled along with it.
D: Elegant: No one can be pleased continuously because no one can be active continuously. When a thing is new, the mind is intensely engaged and the pleasure is vivid; as the activity relaxes through fatigue, the pleasure fades with it.
D: Summary: Pleasure cannot be continuous because human activity cannot be continuous. Novelty stimulates intense mental engagement and vivid pleasure, but as our activity slackens through fatigue, the accompanying pleasure fades proportionally.
D: Distilled: Pleasure fades not because it is inherently fleeting but because the activity it accompanies inevitably tires.
Notes: This passage explains the waning of pleasure through the waning of activity, consistent with Aristotle's view that pleasure supervenes on activity rather than existing independently. -- The observation about novelty connects to Rhetoric I.11, where Aristotle discusses why new things are pleasant. -- The analysis anticipates modern psychological research on hedonic adaptation (the "hedonic treadmill"), though Aristotle's explanation is specifically tied to cognitive and perceptual fatigue rather than a general adaptation mechanism. -- The inability to sustain continuous activity is important for the argument in X.7-8 about contemplation being the most continuous human activity.

D: Original: One might think that all men desire pleasure because they all aim at life; life is an activity, and each man is active about those things and with those faculties that he loves most; e.g. the musician is active with his hearing in reference to tunes, the student with his mind in reference to theoretical questions, and so on in each case; now pleasure completes the activities, and therefore life, which they desire. It is with good reason, then, that they aim at pleasure too, since for every one it completes life, which is desirable. But whether we choose life for the sake of pleasure or pleasure for the sake of life is a question we may dismiss for the present. For they seem to be bound up together and not to admit of separation, since without activity pleasure does not arise, and every activity is completed by the attendant pleasure.
D: Refashioned:
One might think that all people desire pleasure because they all aim at life. Life is an activity, and each person is active about those things and with those faculties that they love most — the musician with hearing in relation to melodies, the student with their mind in relation to theoretical questions, and so on in each case. Pleasure completes these activities, and therefore completes life, which is what people desire. It is with good reason, then, that they also aim at pleasure, since for each person it completes that life which they find desirable.

Whether we choose life for the sake of pleasure or pleasure for the sake of life is a question we may set aside for now. The two seem to be bound up together and not to admit of separation, since without activity pleasure does not arise, and every activity is completed by the pleasure that attends it.
D: Elegant: All people desire pleasure because they desire life, and life is activity. Each person is most active in what they love most, and pleasure completes that activity. Pleasure and life are so intertwined as to be inseparable: without activity there is no pleasure, and every activity is completed by its attendant pleasure.
D: Summary: People desire pleasure because they desire life, and life is activity. Pleasure completes whatever activities we love most — music, study, and so on — and thereby completes life itself. Pleasure and life are inseparable, since there is no pleasure without activity and no activity reaches its fullness without pleasure.
D: Distilled: Pleasure and life are inseparable because pleasure completes the activities that constitute life, and without activity there is no pleasure.
Notes: The identification of life with activity (energeia) is foundational for Aristotle; cf. De Anima II.1, where the soul is defined as the first actuality of a natural body with the potential for life. -- The examples of the musician and the student illustrate the diversity of human lives and the corresponding diversity of pleasures, leading into the argument of X.5. -- The question of whether we choose life for pleasure or pleasure for life and the conclusion that they are inseparable anticipates the close connection Aristotle draws between the best activity and the best pleasure in X.7-8. -- This passage is important for understanding why Aristotle, unlike the Stoics, gives pleasure a central and positive role in the good life rather than treating it as indifferent.

D: Original: For this reason pleasures seem, too, to differ in kind. For things different in kind are, we think, completed by different things (we see this to be true both of natural objects and of things produced by art, e.g. animals, trees, a painting, a sculpture, a house, an implement); and, similarly, we think that activities differing in kind are completed by things differing in kind. Now the activities of thought differ from those of the senses, and both differ among themselves, in kind; so, therefore, do the pleasures that complete them.
D: Refashioned: For this reason, pleasures also seem to differ in kind. Things that differ in kind are, we think, completed by different things — this is true of both natural objects and artifacts such as animals, trees, paintings, sculptures, houses, and tools. Similarly, activities that differ in kind are completed by things that differ in kind. Now the activities of thought differ from those of the senses, and both differ among themselves, in kind. Therefore the pleasures that complete them also differ in kind.
D: Elegant: Since things different in kind are completed by different things — as we see with animals, artworks, and tools — and since the activities of thought differ in kind from those of the senses, and both differ among themselves, the pleasures that complete these activities must also differ in kind.
D: Summary: Just as different kinds of things are completed by different kinds of perfections, so different kinds of activities — intellectual and sensory, and the varieties within each — are completed by different kinds of pleasure. Pleasures therefore differ in kind, not merely in degree.
D: Distilled: Pleasures differ in kind because the activities they complete differ in kind, just as different kinds of things require different kinds of perfection.
Notes: This is the key thesis of X.5: pleasures are not homogeneous but differ in kind (eidei) according to the activities they complete. This directly contradicts utilitarian and hedonistic assumptions that pleasures can be measured on a single scale. -- The argument from analogy (natural objects and artifacts require different completions) draws on the four-cause framework of Physics II and Metaphysics. -- This position has significant ethical implications: if pleasures differ in kind, then the pleasure of contemplation is not merely more pleasure than bodily enjoyment, but a fundamentally different kind of pleasure. -- J.S. Mill's distinction between "higher" and "lower" pleasures in Utilitarianism (1863) is sometimes seen as an attempt to incorporate an Aristotelian insight into a utilitarian framework.

D: Original: This may be seen, too, from the fact that each of the pleasures is bound up with the activity it completes. For an activity is intensified by its proper pleasure, since each class of things is better judged of and brought to precision by those who engage in the activity with pleasure; e.g. it is those who enjoy geometrical thinking that become geometers and grasp the various propositions better, and, similarly, those who are fond of music or of building, and so on, make progress in their proper function by enjoying it; so the pleasures intensify the activities, and what intensifies a thing is proper to it, but things different in kind have properties different in kind.
D: Refashioned: This can also be seen from the fact that each pleasure is bound up with the activity it completes. An activity is intensified by its proper pleasure, since people who engage in an activity with pleasure judge and grasp its details better. Those who enjoy geometry, for instance, become better geometers and understand the various propositions more deeply. Similarly, those who love music or architecture make greater progress in their respective pursuits by taking pleasure in them. The pleasures intensify the activities, and what intensifies a thing is proper to it. But things different in kind have properties different in kind.
D: Elegant: Each pleasure is bound to the activity it completes, and intensifies it: those who take pleasure in geometry become better geometers, and those who love music advance further in music. Since what intensifies a thing is proper to it, and things different in kind have different proper properties, pleasures that enhance different activities must themselves differ in kind.
D: Summary: Pleasure intensifies the activity it accompanies: people who enjoy geometry become better geometers, and musicians who love their art progress further. Since what intensifies an activity must be proper to it, and different activities have different proper properties, pleasures must differ in kind.
D: Distilled: The right pleasure does not merely accompany its activity but actively intensifies and perfects it, which is why geometers who enjoy geometry become better geometers.
Notes: The principle that pleasure intensifies its proper activity has significant pedagogical implications and connects to Aristotle's views on education in the Politics (VIII.3-7). -- The examples of geometry and music as pleasurable learning echo the Metaphysics I.1 claim that learning is naturally pleasant. -- This argument provides empirical support for the thesis that pleasures differ in kind: the pleasure of geometry specifically improves geometric ability, not musical ability. -- The concept of "proper pleasure" (oikeia hedone) is central: each activity has a pleasure native to it that enhances performance. -- This insight resonates with modern psychology's concept of "flow" (Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi), where intrinsic enjoyment of an activity correlates with optimal performance.

D: Original: This will be even more apparent from the fact that activities are hindered by pleasures arising from other sources. For people who are fond of playing the flute are incapable of attending to arguments if they overhear some one playing the flute, since they enjoy flute-playing more than the activity in hand; so the pleasure connected with fluteplaying destroys the activity concerned with argument. This happens, similarly, in all other cases, when one is active about two things at once; the more pleasant activity drives out the other, and if it is much more pleasant does so all the more, so that one even ceases from the other. This is why when we enjoy anything very much we do not throw ourselves into anything else, and do one thing only when we are not much pleased by another; e.g. in the theatre the people who eat sweets do so most when the actors are poor. Now since activities are made precise and more enduring and better by their proper pleasure, and injured by alien pleasures, evidently the two kinds of pleasure are far apart. For alien pleasures do pretty much what proper pains do, since activities are destroyed by their proper pains; e.g. if a man finds writing or doing sums unpleasant and painful, he does not write, or does not do sums, because the activity is painful. So an activity suffers contrary effects from its proper pleasures and pains, i.e. from those that supervene on it in virtue of its own nature. And alien pleasures have been stated to do much the same as pain; they destroy the activity, only not to the same degree.
D: Refashioned:
This becomes even more apparent from the fact that activities are hindered by pleasures arising from other sources. People who love flute-playing cannot pay attention to an argument if they overhear someone playing the flute, since they enjoy the flute-playing more than the activity at hand. The pleasure connected with flute-playing thus destroys the activity of reasoning. The same thing happens in all other cases when someone is engaged in two activities at once: the more pleasant activity drives out the other, and the more pleasant it is, the more completely it does so, until one ceases the other activity entirely. This is why when we enjoy something very much, we do not throw ourselves into anything else, and we engage in a second activity only when we are not much pleased by the first — as when people in the theater eat sweets most when the actors are poor.

Since activities are made more precise, more enduring, and better by their proper pleasure, but damaged by alien pleasures, it is clear that the two kinds of pleasure are far apart. Alien pleasures do essentially what proper pains do: activities are destroyed by the pains that belong to them. If someone finds writing or calculating unpleasant and painful, they do not write or calculate, because the activity is painful. An activity thus suffers contrary effects from its proper pleasures and its proper pains — that is, from those that supervene on it by virtue of its own nature. And alien pleasures have been shown to do much the same as pain: they destroy the activity, though not quite to the same degree.
D: Elegant:
Activities are hindered by pleasures from alien sources. Flute-lovers cannot concentrate on arguments when they hear flute music; theater-goers eat sweets when the acting is poor. The more pleasant the competing activity, the more completely it displaces the other.

Proper pleasure enhances an activity, making it more precise, enduring, and excellent. Alien pleasure undermines it, much as the activity's own proper pain would — though not quite as destructively. This stark contrast between proper and alien pleasures confirms that pleasures differ fundamentally in kind.
D: Summary: Pleasures from unrelated activities interfere with and can destroy the activity at hand, just as pain does. The pleasure of flute music disrupts argument; poor performances drive audiences to eat sweets. By contrast, an activity's proper pleasure enhances it. This opposition between proper and alien pleasures confirms they differ fundamentally in kind.
D: Distilled: An activity's own pleasure perfects it while alien pleasures destroy it, proving that pleasures differ not just in degree but in kind.
Notes: The flute-playing example is characteristically vivid and drawn from everyday Athenian life. The theater example — eating sweets during bad performances — is a wonderfully observed detail of audience behavior. -- The concept of alien (allotria) vs. proper (oikeia) pleasure is the practical corollary of the thesis that pleasures differ in kind. -- The parallel between alien pleasure and proper pain is striking: both undermine activity, though alien pleasure does so less severely. -- This analysis has implications for contemporary discussions of distraction, multitasking, and attention: Aristotle would view the inability to resist competing pleasures as a structural feature of how pleasure and attention interact, not merely a matter of willpower. -- The passage also connects to the discussion of akrasia (weakness of will) in Book VII.

D: Original: Now since activities differ in respect of goodness and badness, and some are worthy to be chosen, others to be avoided, and others neutral, so, too, are the pleasures; for to each activity there is a proper pleasure. The pleasure proper to a worthy activity is good and that proper to an unworthy activity bad; just as the appetites for noble objects are laudable, those for base objects culpable. But the pleasures involved in activities are more proper to them than the desires; for the latter are separated both in time and in nature, while the former are close to the activities, and so hard to distinguish from them that it admits of dispute whether the activity is not the same as the pleasure. (Still, pleasure does not seem to be thought or perception-that would be strange; but because they are not found apart they appear to some people the same.) As activities are different, then, so are the corresponding pleasures. Now sight is superior to touch in purity, and hearing and smell to taste; the pleasures, therefore, are similarly superior, and those of thought superior to these, and within each of the two kinds some are superior to others.
D: Refashioned:
Now since activities differ in goodness and badness — some being worthy of choice, others of avoidance, and others neutral — so too are their pleasures. The pleasure proper to a worthy activity is good, and the pleasure proper to an unworthy activity is bad, just as appetites for noble objects are praiseworthy while appetites for base objects are blameworthy.

The pleasures involved in activities are more closely connected to them than the desires that precede them. Desires are separated from their activities both in time and in nature, while pleasures are so close to the activities that it is difficult to distinguish them, leading some to wonder whether the activity might simply be the same thing as the pleasure. Still, pleasure does not seem to be identical with thinking or perceiving — that would be strange. But because they are never found apart, they appear to some people to be the same.

As activities differ, then, so do their corresponding pleasures. Sight is purer than touch, and hearing and smell are purer than taste. The pleasures are therefore similarly ranked, and the pleasures of thought are superior to all of these, with further hierarchies within each of the two groups — sensory and intellectual.
D: Elegant:
Since activities differ in worth, their pleasures do too: the pleasure of a worthy activity is good, that of an unworthy one bad. Pleasures are so intimately connected to their activities that some wonder whether they are identical — though identifying pleasure with thinking or perceiving would be strange.

A hierarchy of pleasures mirrors the hierarchy of activities: sight's pleasures are purer than touch's, hearing's and smell's purer than taste's, and the pleasures of thought surpass all sensory pleasures, with further gradations within each domain.
D: Summary: Pleasures inherit the moral quality of their activities: good activities produce good pleasures, bad activities bad ones. Because pleasure is so intimately tied to activity, a hierarchy of pleasures follows the hierarchy of senses and faculties — with intellectual pleasures ranking highest, followed by the purer senses like sight and hearing over touch and taste.
D: Distilled: Pleasures rank in the same hierarchy as the activities they complete, with intellectual pleasures highest and touch-pleasures lowest.
Notes: The ranking of senses — sight above hearing, hearing above taste, touch lowest — reflects Aristotle's analysis in De Anima II.7-11 and his conviction that the senses differ in precision and remove from bodily contact. -- The near-identification of pleasure with activity that Aristotle entertains and then rejects is philosophically significant: it shows how close his view comes to making pleasure constitutive of (rather than merely attendant upon) activity. -- The moral valuation of pleasures by the moral quality of their activities is central to Aristotle's ethical framework and distinguishes his position from both hedonism (which values all pleasure) and anti-hedonism (which suspects all pleasure). -- Aquinas adopted this hierarchy in his discussion of delectatio in the Summa Theologiae.

D: Original: Each animal is thought to have a proper pleasure, as it has a proper function; viz. that which corresponds to its activity. If we survey them species by species, too, this will be evident; horse, dog, and man have different pleasures, as Heraclitus says 'asses would prefer sweepings to gold'; for food is pleasanter than gold to asses. So the pleasures of creatures different in kind differ in kind, and it is plausible to suppose that those of a single species do not differ. But they vary to no small extent, in the case of men at least; the same things delight some people and pain others, and are painful and odious to some, and pleasant to and liked by others. This happens, too, in the case of sweet things; the same things do not seem sweet to a man in a fever and a healthy man-nor hot to a weak man and one in good condition. The same happens in other cases. But in all such matters that which appears to the good man is thought to be really so. If this is correct, as it seems to be, and virtue and the good man as such are the measure of each thing, those also will be pleasures which appear so to him, and those things pleasant which he enjoys. If the things he finds tiresome seem pleasant to some one, that is nothing surprising; for men may be ruined and spoilt in many ways; but the things are not pleasant, but only pleasant to these people and to people in this condition. Those which are admittedly disgraceful plainly should not be said to be pleasures, except to a perverted taste; but of those that are thought to be good what kind of pleasure or what pleasure should be said to be that proper to man? Is it not plain from the corresponding activities? The pleasures follow these. Whether, then, the perfect and supremely happy man has one or more activities, the pleasures that perfect these will be said in the strict sense to be pleasures proper to man, and the rest will be so in a secondary and fractional way, as are the activities.
D: Refashioned:
Each animal is thought to have its own proper pleasure, just as it has its own proper function — namely, the pleasure that corresponds to its characteristic activity. This becomes evident when we survey animals species by species: horse, dog, and human being have different pleasures. As Heraclitus says, "asses would prefer sweepings to gold," for food is more pleasant than gold to an ass.

So the pleasures of creatures different in kind differ in kind, and it would be reasonable to suppose that those within a single species do not differ. Yet they vary to no small extent, at least among human beings. The same things delight some people and cause pain to others; what is painful and repulsive to some is pleasant and attractive to others. This also happens with sweet things: the same foods do not taste sweet to someone with a fever and to a healthy person, nor does the same thing feel hot to a weak person and to one in good condition.

But in all such matters, what appears so to the good person is taken to be truly so. If this is correct, as it seems to be, and if virtue and the good person as such are the measure of each thing, then those will be genuine pleasures which appear as pleasures to the good person, and those things will be truly pleasant which they enjoy. If things the good person finds tiresome seem pleasant to someone else, that is not surprising, since human beings can be corrupted and spoiled in many ways. Such things are not truly pleasant — only pleasant to these particular people in their particular condition.

As for admittedly disgraceful pleasures, plainly they should not be called pleasures at all, except from the perspective of a corrupted sensibility. But among pleasures thought to be good, which kind, or which particular pleasure, should be called the pleasure proper to a human being? Is it not evident from the corresponding activities? The pleasures follow from these. Whether, then, the fully good and supremely happy person has one or more activities, the pleasures that complete these activities will be called pleasures proper to a human being in the truest sense, while the rest will be so only in a secondary and diminished way, just as their activities are.
D: Elegant:
Each animal has a proper pleasure corresponding to its proper function, and species differ: as Heraclitus said, asses prefer sweepings to gold. Even within the human species, pleasures vary enormously — what delights one person pains another.

The standard is the good person. What appears pleasant to the virtuous person is truly pleasant; if others disagree, it is because corruption distorts their perception. Disgraceful pleasures are not genuinely pleasures at all. The pleasures proper to human beings are those that complete the activities of the fully virtuous and happy person; all others are pleasures only in a secondary sense.
D: Summary: Different species have different proper pleasures corresponding to their different functions. Even among humans, tastes vary widely. The good person serves as the standard of what is truly pleasant: genuine human pleasures are those that complete the activities of the virtuous person, while all others are pleasures only in a degraded sense.
D: Distilled: The good person is the measure of true pleasure, and the pleasures proper to human beings are those that complete the activities of the virtuous.
Notes: Heraclitus of Ephesus (c. 535-475 BCE), the pre-Socratic philosopher known for doctrines of flux and the unity of opposites. The fragment about asses preferring sweepings to gold (DK B9) illustrates the relativity of value across different natures. -- The principle that the good person (spoudaios) is the measure (metron) of what is truly pleasant and truly good is one of Aristotle's most distinctive ethical claims, connecting to his treatment of the practically wise person (phronimos) in Book VI. -- This echoes Protagoras's "man is the measure" doctrine while transforming it: not any person, but the virtuous person, is the standard. -- The argument that corrupted people perceive falsely parallels Aristotle's epistemology of normal vs. diseased perception in Metaphysics IV.5. -- This passage brings the discussion of pleasure full circle back to the question of happiness, preparing for the famous argument of X.6-8.

D: Original: Now that we have spoken of the virtues, the forms of friendship, and the varieties of pleasure, what remains is to discuss in outline the nature of happiness, since this is what we state the end of human nature to be. Our discussion will be the more concise if we first sum up what we have said already. We said, then, that it is not a disposition; for if it were it might belong to some one who was asleep throughout his life, living the life of a plant, or, again, to some one who was suffering the greatest misfortunes. If these implications are unacceptable, and we must rather class happiness as an activity, as we have said before, and if some activities are necessary, and desirable for the sake of something else, while others are so in themselves, evidently happiness must be placed among those desirable in themselves, not among those desirable for the sake of something else; for happiness does not lack anything, but is self-sufficient. Now those activities are desirable in themselves from which nothing is sought beyond the activity. And of this nature virtuous actions are thought to be; for to do noble and good deeds is a thing desirable for its own sake.
D: Refashioned:
Now that we have discussed the virtues, the forms of friendship, and the varieties of pleasure, what remains is to give an outline account of happiness, since this is what we identified as the end of human nature. Our discussion will be more concise if we first summarize what we have already established.

We said that happiness is not a disposition. If it were, it could belong to someone asleep throughout their entire life, living the life of a plant, or to someone enduring the worst misfortunes. If these implications are unacceptable, and we must instead classify happiness as an activity — as we have said before — and if some activities are merely necessary or desirable as means to other things while others are desirable in themselves, then clearly happiness must be placed among things desirable in themselves, not among those desired for the sake of something else. Happiness lacks nothing and is self-sufficient.

Activities desirable in themselves are those from which nothing is sought beyond the activity itself. Virtuous actions are thought to be of this nature, for doing noble and good deeds is something desirable for its own sake.
D: Elegant: Having treated virtue, friendship, and pleasure, we now turn to happiness itself. Happiness cannot be a mere disposition — otherwise the sleeping, the vegetative, and the wretched could be called happy. It must be an activity, and specifically one desirable in itself, not as a means to anything else. It is self-sufficient, lacking nothing. Virtuous actions qualify as such activities, since doing noble deeds is desirable for its own sake.
D: Summary: Aristotle recapitulates his earlier conclusions to begin his final account of happiness. Happiness cannot be a passive state or disposition but must be an activity desirable in itself, self-sufficient and complete. Virtuous actions qualify because they are pursued for their own sake.
D: Distilled: Happiness is not a mere disposition but a self-sufficient activity desirable for its own sake, of which virtuous action is the clearest example.
Notes: This passage opens the climactic argument of the Ethics (X.6-8) by recalling conclusions from Book I, especially I.7 (the function argument) and I.8-10 (happiness as activity, not disposition). -- The "sleeping throughout life" and "life of a plant" thought experiments appeared in I.5 and I.10. -- The criterion of self-sufficiency (autarkeia) was established in I.7. -- The summary method here is characteristic of Aristotle's dialectical approach: he reviews endoxa (reputable opinions) and established conclusions before advancing to new ground. -- The argument that follows in X.7-8, identifying contemplation as the highest happiness, has been among the most debated passages in the history of philosophy.

D: Original: Pleasant amusements also are thought to be of this nature; we choose them not for the sake of other things; for we are injured rather than benefited by them, since we are led to neglect our bodies and our property. But most of the people who are deemed happy take refuge in such pastimes, which is the reason why those who are ready-witted at them are highly esteemed at the courts of tyrants; they make themselves pleasant companions in the tyrants' favourite pursuits, and that is the sort of man they want. Now these things are thought to be of the nature of happiness because people in despotic positions spend their leisure in them, but perhaps such people prove nothing; for virtue and reason, from which good activities flow, do not depend on despotic position; nor, if these people, who have never tasted pure and generous pleasure, take refuge in the bodily pleasures, should these for that reason be thought more desirable; for boys, too, think the things that are valued among themselves are the best. It is to be expected, then, that, as different things seem valuable to boys and to men, so they should to bad men and to good. Now, as we have often maintained, those things are both valuable and pleasant which are such to the good man; and to each man the activity in accordance with his own disposition is most desirable, and, therefore, to the good man that which is in accordance with virtue. Happiness, therefore, does not lie in amusement; it would, indeed, be strange if the end were amusement, and one were to take trouble and suffer hardship all one's life in order to amuse oneself. For, in a word, everything that we choose we choose for the sake of something else-except happiness, which is an end. Now to exert oneself and work for the sake of amusement seems silly and utterly childish. But to amuse oneself in order that one may exert oneself, as Anacharsis puts it, seems right; for amusement is a sort of relaxation, and we need relaxation because we cannot work continuously. Relaxation, then, is not an end; for it is taken for the sake of activity.
D: Refashioned:
Pleasant amusements might also seem to be activities desirable in themselves, since we do not choose them for the sake of other things. In fact, we are harmed rather than helped by them, since they lead us to neglect our bodies and our property. Yet most people who are reputed to be happy take refuge in such pastimes, which is why those who are witty and entertaining are highly valued at the courts of tyrants: they make themselves agreeable companions in the tyrants' favored diversions, and that is the sort of person tyrants want. These amusements seem to constitute happiness because people in positions of power spend their leisure on them.

But such people prove nothing. Virtue and reason, the sources of good activities, do not depend on holding power. And if these people, who have never experienced pure and noble pleasure, resort to bodily pleasures, we should not on that account think bodily pleasures more desirable. Children, too, think the things valued among children are the best. It is to be expected, then, that just as different things seem valuable to children and to adults, so they should to bad people and to good people.

As we have often maintained, the things that are truly valuable and pleasant are those that appear so to the good person. For each person, the most desirable activity is the one that accords with their own character, and for the good person, this means the activity that accords with virtue. Happiness, therefore, does not consist in amusement. It would indeed be absurd if the end of life were amusement, and one labored and suffered hardship all one's life in order to amuse oneself. Everything we choose, we choose for the sake of something else — except happiness, which is an end. To exert oneself and toil for the sake of amusement seems foolish and utterly childish. But to amuse oneself in order to exert oneself afterward, as Anacharsis puts it, seems right. For amusement is a form of relaxation, and we need relaxation because we cannot work continuously. Relaxation, then, is not an end; it is pursued for the sake of activity.
D: Elegant:
Amusement might seem to be an end in itself, since we do not pursue it for anything further, and powerful people spend their leisure on it. But this proves nothing: power does not bring virtue, and those who have never tasted noble pleasure naturally gravitate toward bodily pleasure, just as children value childish things.

Happiness cannot consist in amusement. It would be absurd to toil all one's life for the sake of play. Amusement is relaxation, and relaxation exists for the sake of renewed activity, not the reverse. As Anacharsis said, we play in order to work, not the other way around. Everything is chosen for the sake of something else — except happiness, which is the end.
D: Summary: Amusement cannot constitute happiness, even though powerful people spend their leisure on it, because power does not confer virtue or discernment. It would be absurd to labor all one's life merely to amuse oneself. Amusement is relaxation, which serves activity rather than being an end in itself.
D: Distilled: We amuse ourselves in order to work, not the other way around, so amusement cannot be what happiness consists in.
Notes: Anacharsis was a legendary Scythian sage (6th century BCE), one of the Seven Sages in some ancient lists, known for pithy observations about Greek customs. -- The critique of tyrants' courts reflects Aristotle's political experience; he spent time at the Macedonian court and was tutor to Alexander the Great. -- The argument that powerful people's choices do not establish what is truly good is a pointed social observation: wealth and power do not guarantee wisdom about the good life. -- The children analogy recurs from I.3, where Aristotle warns that the young are not proper students of ethics. -- The principle that relaxation serves activity (and not vice versa) is important for the Politics (VIII.3-5), where Aristotle discusses the proper use of leisure (schole) in education.

D: Original: The happy life is thought to be virtuous; now a virtuous life requires exertion, and does not consist in amusement. And we say that serious things are better than laughable things and those connected with amusement, and that the activity of the better of any two things-whether it be two elements of our being or two men-is the more serious; but the activity of the better is ipso facto superior and more of the nature of happiness. And any chance person-even a slave-can enjoy the bodily pleasures no less than the best man; but no one assigns to a slave a share in happiness-unless he assigns to him also a share in human life. For happiness does not lie in such occupations, but, as we have said before, in virtuous activities.
D: Refashioned:
The happy life is thought to be a life of virtue, and a virtuous life requires serious effort rather than consisting in amusement. We hold that serious things are better than laughable things and those connected with amusement, and that the activity of the better part — whether we are comparing two parts of our being or two people — is the more serious. But the activity of what is better is by that very fact superior and more characteristic of happiness.

Anyone at all — even a slave — can enjoy bodily pleasures no less than the finest person. But no one assigns a share in happiness to a slave — unless they also assign to a slave a share in truly human life. For happiness does not consist in such pastimes but, as we have said before, in activities of virtue.
D: Elegant: The happy life requires virtue and serious effort, not amusement. Serious things surpass laughable ones, and the activity of the better part of our nature is more characteristic of happiness. Anyone, even a slave, can enjoy bodily pleasures equally — but no one attributes happiness to a slave who does not share in a truly human life. Happiness lies in virtuous activity, not in pastimes.
D: Summary: Happiness requires the serious exercise of virtue, not mere amusement. While anyone can enjoy bodily pleasures, happiness is not attributed to those who lack a share in genuinely human life. The activity of our higher nature is inherently more connected to happiness than passive enjoyment.
D: Distilled: Happiness requires the serious exercise of virtue, not amusement, since even a slave can enjoy bodily pleasures but is not thereby called happy.
Notes: The slave example reflects ancient Greek social reality but carries a philosophical point: Aristotle is arguing that bodily pleasure is not sufficient for happiness because it does not engage what is distinctively human. -- Aristotle's view of slaves is complex and controversial; Politics I.4-7 contains his problematic defense of natural slavery, though some scholars argue his criteria would exclude most actual slaves. -- The distinction between serious (spoudaios) and laughable or trivial anticipates the hierarchy of activities in X.7. -- The claim that the better part's activity is more characteristic of happiness prepares for the argument that contemplation, as the activity of the highest part (nous), is the supreme form of happiness. -- This passage bridges the negative argument (happiness is not amusement) to the positive argument (happiness is contemplation) that follows.

D: Original: If happiness is activity in accordance with virtue, it is reasonable that it should be in accordance with the highest virtue; and this will be that of the best thing in us. Whether it be reason or something else that is this element which is thought to be our natural ruler and guide and to take thought of things noble and divine, whether it be itself also divine or only the most divine element in us, the activity of this in accordance with its proper virtue will be perfect happiness. That this activity is contemplative we have already said.
D: Refashioned: If happiness is activity in accordance with virtue, it is reasonable that it should be in accordance with the highest virtue, and that will be the virtue of the best thing in us. Whether this element is reason or something else — whatever it is that is thought to be our natural ruler and guide, that takes thought of things noble and divine — and whether it is itself divine or merely the most divine element in us, the activity of this element in accordance with its proper virtue will be perfect happiness. That this activity is contemplation we have already said.
D: Elegant: If happiness is activity in accordance with virtue, it must accord with the highest virtue — that of the best thing in us. Whatever this ruling element is, whether reason or something beyond it, whether itself divine or merely the most divine part of our nature, its proper activity is contemplation, and this constitutes perfect happiness.
D: Summary: Since happiness is activity in accordance with virtue, the highest happiness must consist in the activity of our highest faculty — reason, our natural ruler, which grasps what is noble and divine. This supreme activity is contemplation.
D: Distilled: Perfect happiness is the contemplative activity of reason, the highest and most divine element in human nature.
Notes: This pivotal passage announces the thesis of X.7-8: perfect happiness (teleia eudaimonia) is contemplation (theoria). -- The identification of reason (nous) as the ruling element connects to De Anima III.4-5 and the Protrepticus (a lost work partially reconstructable from quotations). -- The tentative phrasing ("whether reason or something else") may reflect genuine philosophical uncertainty or dialectical caution about identifying nous with the divine. -- The reference to having "already said" this points back to I.7 (the function argument) and possibly to the discussion of sophia (theoretical wisdom) in VI.7. -- This identification of happiness with contemplation is the most controversial claim in Aristotle's ethics, leading to the so-called "dominant" vs. "inclusive" debate about eudaimonia: does Aristotle mean that contemplation alone constitutes happiness, or that it is the highest component of a life that also includes practical virtue?

D: Original: Now this would seem to be in agreement both with what we said before and with the truth. For, firstly, this activity is the best (since not only is reason the best thing in us, but the objects of reason are the best of knowable objects); and secondly, it is the most continuous, since we can contemplate truth more continuously than we can do anything. And we think happiness has pleasure mingled with it, but the activity of philosophic wisdom is admittedly the pleasantest of virtuous activities; at all events the pursuit of it is thought to offer pleasures marvellous for their purity and their enduringness, and it is to be expected that those who know will pass their time more pleasantly than those who inquire. And the self-sufficiency that is spoken of must belong most to the contemplative activity.
D: Refashioned: This would seem to agree with what we said before and with the truth. First, this activity is the best, since reason is the best thing in us, and the objects of reason are the best among knowable things. Second, it is the most continuous, since we can sustain contemplation of truth more continuously than we can sustain any other activity. Third, we consider happiness to have pleasure mixed in with it, and the activity of philosophic wisdom is acknowledged to be the most pleasant of virtuous activities. The pursuit of philosophy is thought to offer pleasures of extraordinary purity and endurance, and it is to be expected that those who possess knowledge spend their time more pleasantly than those who are still seeking it. Furthermore, the self-sufficiency we speak of must belong most of all to the contemplative activity.
D: Elegant: Contemplation satisfies all the criteria for perfect happiness. It is the best activity, since reason and its objects are the best in us. It is the most continuous, since we can sustain contemplation longer than any other pursuit. It is the most pleasant, for philosophy offers pleasures unmatched in purity and endurance. And it is the most self-sufficient of all activities.
D: Summary: Contemplation meets all the requirements for the highest happiness: it exercises the best faculty (reason) on the best objects, it is the most continuous human activity, it provides the purest and most enduring pleasures, and it is the most self-sufficient pursuit.
D: Distilled: Contemplation is the highest happiness because it is the best, most continuous, most pleasant, and most self-sufficient of all human activities.
Notes: The four criteria applied here — best, most continuous, most pleasant, most self-sufficient — recall the criteria for happiness established in Book I.7. Aristotle is showing that contemplation satisfies all of them. -- The claim that contemplation is the most continuous activity connects to X.4's discussion of why pleasure fades (because activity tires), suggesting that contemplation tires least. -- The distinction between those who know and those who inquire (echontes vs. zetountes) reflects Aristotle's view that possessing knowledge is more pleasant than acquiring it, connecting to the energeia/kinesis distinction. -- The pleasure of philosophy "marvellous for purity and enduringness" is a rare moment of personal testimony from Aristotle. -- The self-sufficiency criterion becomes important in the next passage, where Aristotle compares contemplation with practical virtue.

D: Original: For while a philosopher, as well as a just man or one possessing any other virtue, needs the necessaries of life, when they are sufficiently equipped with things of that sort the just man needs people towards whom and with whom he shall act justly, and the temperate man, the brave man, and each of the others is in the same case, but the philosopher, even when by himself, can contemplate truth, and the better the wiser he is; he can perhaps do so better if he has fellow-workers, but still he is the most self-sufficient. And this activity alone would seem to be loved for its own sake; for nothing arises from it apart from the contemplating, while from practical activities we gain more or less apart from the action. And happiness is thought to depend on leisure; for we are busy that we may have leisure, and make war that we may live in peace.
D: Refashioned:
For while a philosopher, like a just person or anyone possessing any other virtue, needs the necessities of life, once sufficiently equipped in that regard, the just person additionally needs other people toward whom and with whom to act justly, and the same is true of the temperate person, the brave person, and each of the others. The philosopher, however, can contemplate truth even when entirely alone, and the wiser one is, the better one can do so. It may be that having fellow inquirers makes it easier, but the philosopher is still the most self-sufficient of all.

Moreover, this activity alone seems to be loved purely for its own sake. Nothing comes from it beyond the contemplation itself, while from practical activities we always gain something more or less beyond the action. And happiness is thought to depend on leisure. We are busy so that we may have leisure, and we make war so that we may live in peace.
D: Elegant: The philosopher, unlike the just, brave, or temperate person, does not need other people to exercise their highest activity. The philosopher can contemplate truth alone, and the wiser they are, the better they can do so — making contemplation the most self-sufficient activity. It is also loved purely for its own sake, since nothing results from it beyond the contemplating itself. And since happiness requires leisure, and we are busy precisely in order to have leisure, contemplation fits the requirement perfectly.
D: Summary: Contemplation is the most self-sufficient activity because the philosopher can pursue it alone, unlike the practically virtuous person who needs others. It is also pursued purely for its own sake, producing nothing beyond itself. Since happiness requires leisure and all our busyness aims at leisure, contemplation — the supreme leisure activity — is best suited to be happiness.
D: Distilled: Contemplation is the most self-sufficient activity because it requires no one else, produces nothing beyond itself, and is the supreme use of the leisure that all our busyness aims to secure.
Notes: The argument about self-sufficiency marks a crucial contrast between practical and theoretical virtue. The just person needs others as objects and partners in justice; the contemplative needs no one, though companions help. -- The claim that happiness depends on leisure (schole) is foundational for Aristotle's political philosophy. In Politics VII-VIII, the well-ordered state exists to provide citizens with leisure for noble activities. -- The famous line "we are busy that we may have leisure, and make war that we may live in peace" (ascholoumetha hina scholazomen) inverts common priorities. -- This passage has been central to the contemplation-vs.-action debate in Western philosophy, influencing Hannah Arendt's distinction between vita activa and vita contemplativa in The Human Condition (1958). -- The Stoics would later disagree, arguing that virtue in practical life is sufficient for happiness.

D: Original: Now the activity of the practical virtues is exhibited in political or military affairs, but the actions concerned with these seem to be unleisurely. Warlike actions are completely so (for no one chooses to be at war, or provokes war, for the sake of being at war; any one would seem absolutely murderous if he were to make enemies of his friends in order to bring about battle and slaughter); but the action of the statesman is also unleisurely, and-apart from the political action itself-aims at despotic power and honours, or at all events happiness, for him and his fellow citizens-a happiness different from political action, and evidently sought as being different. So if among virtuous actions political and military actions are distinguished by nobility and greatness, and these are unleisurely and aim at an end and are not desirable for their own sake, but the activity of reason, which is contemplative, seems both to be superior in serious worth and to aim at no end beyond itself, and to have its pleasure proper to itself (and this augments the activity), and the self-sufficiency, leisureliness, unweariedness (so far as this is possible for man), and all the other attributes ascribed to the supremely happy man are evidently those connected with this activity, it follows that this will be the complete happiness of man, if it be allowed a complete term of life (for none of the attributes of happiness is incomplete).
D: Refashioned:
The practical virtues find their exercise in political and military affairs, but actions in these domains are inherently unleisurely. Military actions are completely so — no one chooses to wage war or provokes it for the sake of being at war. Anyone who made enemies of friends in order to bring about battles and slaughter would seem absolutely murderous. The statesman's activity is also unleisurely and, beyond the political action itself, aims at something further — power, honors, or at least a happiness for the statesman and fellow citizens that is different from the political action and is evidently sought as something distinct from it.

So if among virtuous actions political and military deeds are distinguished by nobility and greatness, yet are unleisurely, aim at ends beyond themselves, and are not desirable for their own sake, while the activity of reason — which is contemplative — seems both superior in worth and to aim at no end beyond itself, and to have its own proper pleasure (which augments the activity), and to possess self-sufficiency, leisureliness, and freedom from weariness (so far as this is possible for a human being), and all the other attributes ascribed to the supremely happy person — then this must be the complete happiness of a human being, provided it is allowed a complete span of life. For none of the attributes of happiness is incomplete.
D: Elegant:
Political and military action, though noble, are unleisurely and aim at ends beyond themselves — no one makes war for its own sake, and statesmanship seeks results distinct from the political activity itself. Contemplation, by contrast, is superior in worth, aims at nothing beyond itself, carries its own pleasure, and possesses self-sufficiency, leisureliness, and unwearied persistence.

Since contemplation alone exhibits all the attributes of complete happiness — given a full span of life — it must be the complete happiness of a human being.
D: Summary: Political and military activities, though noble, are not pursued for their own sake and are inherently lacking in leisure. Contemplation alone combines superiority of worth, self-sufficiency, leisureliness, its own proper pleasure, and freedom from weariness — all the marks of the supremely happy life. It is therefore the complete happiness of human beings, given a full lifespan.
D: Distilled: Contemplation alone unites all the attributes of complete happiness — self-sufficiency, leisureliness, intrinsic worth, proper pleasure, and unwearied persistence — that political and military action, however noble, lack.
