Notes: The contrast between contemplative and political life is one of the most consequential arguments in Western philosophy, shaping debates from Cicero's De Officiis through the medieval contemplative tradition to modern political theory. -- The proviso "if it be allowed a complete term of life" recalls I.7 and the Solon story (I.10): happiness requires a full lifespan. -- Hannah Arendt argued in The Human Condition that Aristotle's privileging of contemplation over political action distorted the Western tradition's understanding of the political. -- The observation that no one makes war for its own sake connects to Politics I.8 and VII.14, where Aristotle argues that war serves peace and work serves leisure. -- The claim that contemplation carries its "own proper pleasure" connects back to the analysis in X.4-5.

D: Original: But such a life would be too high for man; for it is not in so far as he is man that he will live so, but in so far as something divine is present in him; and by so much as this is superior to our composite nature is its activity superior to that which is the exercise of the other kind of virtue. If reason is divine, then, in comparison with man, the life according to it is divine in comparison with human life. But we must not follow those who advise us, being men, to think of human things, and, being mortal, of mortal things, but must, so far as we can, make ourselves immortal, and strain every nerve to live in accordance with the best thing in us; for even if it be small in bulk, much more does it in power and worth surpass everything. This would seem, too, to be each man himself, since it is the authoritative and better part of him. It would be strange, then, if he were to choose not the life of his self but that of something else. And what we said before' will apply now; that which is proper to each thing is by nature best and most pleasant for each thing; for man, therefore, the life according to reason is best and pleasantest, since reason more than anything else is man. This life therefore is also the happiest.
D: Refashioned:
But such a life would be too high for a mere human being. One would live this way not insofar as one is human, but insofar as something divine is present within. And to the extent that this divine element surpasses our composite nature, its activity surpasses the exercise of the other kind of virtue. If reason is divine, then in comparison with human life, the life lived according to reason is divine.

But we must not follow those who counsel us, being human, to think only of human things, or being mortal, to think only of mortal things. Rather, we must, as far as we can, make ourselves immortal and strain every nerve to live in accordance with the best thing in us. For even though it is small in bulk, it far surpasses everything else in power and worth.

This element would also seem to be each person's true self, since it is the authoritative and better part. It would be strange, then, if someone were to choose not the life of their own true self but the life of something other than themselves. And what we said before applies here as well: what is proper to each thing is by nature best and most pleasant for it. For a human being, therefore, the life according to reason is best and most pleasant, since reason more than anything else is the human being. This life is therefore also the happiest.
D: Elegant:
The contemplative life surpasses ordinary human existence: we live it not as mere humans but insofar as something divine is in us. We should not heed advice to think only mortal thoughts. Instead, we must strain to live by the best thing in us — for though reason may be small in bulk, it surpasses all else in power and worth.

This divine element is each person's true self, since it is the ruling and better part. It would be strange to choose the life of something other than one's own deepest self. Since what is proper to each thing is naturally best and most pleasant, and reason is what a human being most truly is, the life of reason is the happiest.
D: Summary: The contemplative life transcends ordinary human existence by exercising the divine element within us. Though reason is small in physical scale, it surpasses everything in power and worth and constitutes our true self. We should not resign ourselves to merely mortal concerns but strive to live according to this highest part. Since reason is what we most truly are, the life of reason is the best, most pleasant, and happiest life.
D: Distilled: Reason is our truest self, and though the contemplative life seems superhuman, we must strain toward it because living according to what we most truly are is the happiest life possible.
Notes: The claim that "something divine" is present in humans connects to De Anima III.5 (the active intellect, which some interpret as divine) and to Metaphysics XII.7-9 (God as pure thought thinking itself). -- The exhortation to "make ourselves immortal" (athanatizein) is one of Aristotle's most striking phrases, apparently contradicting the conventional Greek wisdom that mortals should think mortal thoughts (a view expressed by Pindar and others). -- The identification of the true self with nous (reason/intellect) is philosophically radical: the body and even the moral character are secondary to the intellect as constituting who we really are. -- This passage profoundly influenced Neoplatonism (Plotinus), Islamic philosophy (Al-Farabi, Averroes on the intellect), and Christian theology (Aquinas's beatific vision as intellectual contemplation of God). -- The tension between this passage and X.8's acknowledgment of a "secondary" happiness in practical virtue remains one of the central interpretive problems of the Ethics.

D: Original: But in a secondary degree the life in accordance with the other kind of virtue is happy; for the activities in accordance with this befit our human estate. Just and brave acts, and other virtuous acts, we do in relation to each other, observing our respective duties with regard to contracts and services and all manner of actions and with regard to passions; and all of these seem to be typically human. Some of them seem even to arise from the body, and virtue of character to be in many ways bound up with the passions. Practical wisdom, too, is linked to virtue of character, and this to practical wisdom, since the principles of practical wisdom are in accordance with the moral virtues and rightness in morals is in accordance with practical wisdom. Being connected with the passions also, the moral virtues must belong to our composite nature; and the virtues of our composite nature are human; so, therefore, are the life and the happiness which correspond to these. The excellence of the reason is a thing apart; we must be content to say this much about it, for to describe it precisely is a task greater than our purpose requires. It would seem, however, also to need external equipment but little, or less than moral virtue does. Grant that both need the necessaries, and do so equally, even if the statesman's work is the more concerned with the body and things of that sort; for there will be little difference there; but in what they need for the exercise of their activities there will be much difference. The liberal man will need money for the doing of his liberal deeds, and the just man too will need it for the returning of services (for wishes are hard to discern, and even people who are not just pretend to wish to act justly); and the brave man will need power if he is to accomplish any of the acts that correspond to his virtue, and the temperate man will need opportunity; for how else is either he or any of the others to be recognized? It is debated, too, whether the will or the deed is more essential to virtue, which is assumed to involve both; it is surely clear that its perfection involves both; but for deeds many things are needed, and more, the greater and nobler the deeds are.
D: Refashioned:
But in a secondary degree, the life lived in accordance with the other kind of virtue — practical virtue — is happy. For the activities of practical virtue befit our human condition. We perform just and brave acts and other virtuous deeds in relation to one another, observing our duties concerning contracts, services, and all manner of actions, as well as concerning our passions. All of these seem characteristically human. Some virtues of character even seem to arise from the body, and virtue of character appears in many ways to be bound up with the passions.

Practical wisdom, too, is linked to virtue of character, and virtue of character to practical wisdom, since the starting points of practical wisdom accord with the moral virtues, and moral rightness accords with practical wisdom. Being connected with the passions as well, the moral virtues belong to our composite nature, and the virtues of our composite nature are human. So, therefore, are the life and the happiness that correspond to them.

The excellence of reason, by contrast, stands apart. We must be content to say just this much about it, for a more precise description exceeds our present purpose. It would seem, however, to need little external equipment — or at least less than moral virtue does. Grant that both need the necessities of life equally, even if the statesman's work is more concerned with the body and similar things — for the difference there will be slight. But in what they need for the exercise of their activities, the difference will be great. The generous person will need money to perform generous deeds, and so will the just person for returning favors (since intentions are hard to discern, and even unjust people pretend to wish to act justly). The brave person will need power to accomplish deeds befitting their virtue, and the temperate person will need opportunities — for how else will they or any of the others be recognized? It is debated whether the will or the deed matters more for virtue, which is assumed to involve both. Its perfection clearly requires both; but for deeds, many things are needed — and the greater and nobler the deeds, the more things are needed.
D: Elegant:
The life of practical virtue is happy in a secondary sense. Just and brave acts, performed in our dealings with one another regarding contracts, duties, and passions, are characteristically human. Practical wisdom and moral virtue are mutually dependent, and both are bound up with our composite bodily-and-rational nature.

Contemplative excellence stands apart and needs little external equipment. Practical virtue, by contrast, requires substantial external resources: the generous need money, the just need means to reciprocate, the brave need power, the temperate need opportunity. The greater and nobler the deed, the more such resources are required.
D: Summary: Practical virtue provides a secondary form of happiness, suited to our composite human nature. Moral virtue and practical wisdom are intertwined and inseparable. But practical virtue requires far more external resources than contemplation — the generous need wealth, the brave need power, and so on — making it more dependent on circumstances.
D: Distilled: The life of practical virtue is genuinely happy but secondary to contemplation, and far more dependent on external resources like wealth, power, and social opportunity.
Notes: The distinction between primary (contemplative) and secondary (practical) happiness is the famous "two-tier" account that has generated enormous scholarly debate. Inclusivists argue both forms are components of a single happy life; dominantists argue contemplation alone is true happiness. -- The mutual dependence of practical wisdom (phronesis) and moral virtue is established in Book VI.13. -- The observation that moral virtue belongs to our "composite nature" (suntheton) — the body-soul compound — while intellectual virtue belongs to reason alone reflects the hylomorphic psychology of De Anima. -- The list of resources needed for practical virtue (money for generosity, power for courage) recalls I.8-10 on external goods. -- The debate about will vs. deed in virtue connects to II.4 on the conditions for virtuous action.

D: Original: But the man who is contemplating the truth needs no such thing, at least with a view to the exercise of his activity; indeed they are, one may say, even hindrances, at all events to his contemplation; but in so far as he is a man and lives with a number of people, he chooses to do virtuous acts; he will therefore need such aids to living a human life.
D: Refashioned: But the person engaged in contemplating truth needs none of these things, at least not for the exercise of that activity. Indeed, such things might even be hindrances to contemplation. Yet insofar as this person is a human being and lives among other people, they will choose to perform virtuous acts and will therefore need such external aids for living a human life.
D: Elegant: The contemplative person needs no external resources for contemplation itself — they may even be hindrances. But as a human being living among others, one still chooses to act virtuously and therefore needs the means of human life.
D: Summary: Contemplation requires no external resources and may even be hindered by them. However, since the contemplative person is still a human being living in society, they will still engage in practical virtue and need the corresponding resources for that purpose.
D: Distilled: Contemplation itself needs nothing external and may be hindered by it, but the contemplative person still lives a human life requiring practical virtue and its resources.
Notes: This brief passage is important for the inclusivism-vs.-dominantism debate: Aristotle clearly states that even the contemplative person will choose (proaireitai) to do virtuous acts, suggesting that practical virtue is not abandoned but remains part of the good life. -- The concession that external goods can hinder contemplation connects to the discussion of leisure in X.7 and the Politics's concern with structuring civic life to enable contemplation. -- The idea that worldly engagement is necessary but secondary to contemplation profoundly shaped the Christian monastic tradition, especially through Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelian and Augustinian thought on the contemplative and active lives.

D: Original: But that perfect happiness is a contemplative activity will appear from the following consideration as well. We assume the gods to be above all other beings blessed and happy; but what sort of actions must we assign to them? Acts of justice? Will not the gods seem absurd if they make contracts and return deposits, and so on? Acts of a brave man, then, confronting dangers and running risks because it is noble to do so? Or liberal acts? To whom will they give? It will be strange if they are really to have money or anything of the kind. And what would their temperate acts be? Is not such praise tasteless, since they have no bad appetites? If we were to run through them all, the circumstances of action would be found trivial and unworthy of gods. Still, every one supposes that they live and therefore that they are active; we cannot suppose them to sleep like Endymion. Now if you take away from a living being action, and still more production, what is left but contemplation? Therefore the activity of God, which surpasses all others in blessedness, must be contemplative; and of human activities, therefore, that which is most akin to this must be most of the nature of happiness.
D: Refashioned:
That perfect happiness is a contemplative activity will also appear from the following consideration. We assume the gods to be above all other beings in blessedness and happiness. But what kinds of actions should we attribute to them? Acts of justice? Surely it would be absurd to imagine the gods making contracts and returning deposits. Acts of courage, then — confronting dangers and running risks because it is noble? Or generous acts? To whom would they give? It would be strange if they actually had money or anything of the sort. And what would their temperate acts be? Surely such praise would be tasteless, since they have no base appetites.

If we run through all the practical virtues, the circumstances of action would turn out to be trivial and unworthy of gods. Still, everyone supposes that the gods live and are therefore active — we can hardly suppose them to sleep like Endymion. Now if you take away action from a living being, and still more production, what remains but contemplation? Therefore the activity of God, which surpasses all others in blessedness, must be contemplative. And of human activities, that which is most akin to this must be most of the nature of happiness.
D: Elegant:
The gods are supremely blessed, yet none of the practical virtues — justice, courage, generosity, temperance — can sensibly be attributed to them. Justice requires contracts, courage requires danger, generosity requires recipients, temperance requires base appetites to resist. All such circumstances are trivial and unworthy of divine beings.

Yet the gods must be active, since they live — they do not sleep like Endymion. If neither action nor production belongs to them, only contemplation remains. Therefore God's activity is contemplation, and the human activity most akin to it must be most characteristic of happiness.
D: Summary: Since the gods are supremely happy yet none of the practical virtues can meaningfully be attributed to them — justice, courage, generosity, and temperance all require circumstances beneath divinity — their activity must be contemplation. Therefore, the human activity most like divine contemplation is the truest form of happiness.
D: Distilled: Since the gods are supremely happy and their only conceivable activity is contemplation, human contemplation must be the highest form of happiness.
Notes: The argument from divine activity is one of the most famous in Aristotle's ethics. It connects directly to Metaphysics XII.7-9, where God is described as noesis noeseos — thought thinking itself. -- Endymion was a mythological figure whom Zeus (or Selene) put into eternal sleep; he serves here as the absurd limiting case of a living being without activity. -- The systematic elimination of practical virtues as applicable to the gods is a reductio argument showing that theoria alone suits divine beings. -- This argument was enormously influential in medieval theology: Aquinas used it to argue that the beatific vision (direct intellectual contemplation of God) is the highest human happiness (Summa Theologiae I-II, Q.3, a.8). -- Critics note a tension: Aristotle elsewhere attributes to God properties (like being a cause of cosmic motion) that seem more than purely contemplative.

D: Original: This is indicated, too, by the fact that the other animals have no share in happiness, being completely deprived of such activity. For while the whole life of the gods is blessed, and that of men too in so far as some likeness of such activity belongs to them, none of the other animals is happy, since they in no way share in contemplation. Happiness extends, then, just so far as contemplation does, and those to whom contemplation more fully belongs are more truly happy, not as a mere concomitant but in virtue of the contemplation; for this is in itself precious. Happiness, therefore, must be some form of contemplation.
D: Refashioned:
This is further indicated by the fact that no other animals share in happiness, since they are entirely deprived of contemplative activity. The whole life of the gods is blessed, and the life of human beings is blessed insofar as it contains something resembling such activity. But none of the other animals is happy, since none of them shares in contemplation at all.

Happiness extends, then, exactly as far as contemplation does. Those to whom contemplation more fully belongs are more truly happy — not merely incidentally, but by virtue of the contemplation itself, for contemplation is intrinsically precious. Happiness, therefore, must be some form of contemplation.
D: Elegant: Animals have no share in happiness because they are entirely incapable of contemplation. Gods are wholly blessed because their life is pure contemplation. Humans are happy to the extent that they participate in contemplative activity. Happiness extends exactly as far as contemplation does, and those who contemplate more are happier — not incidentally, but because contemplation is intrinsically precious.
D: Summary: The scope of happiness maps exactly onto the capacity for contemplation: gods are fully blessed because they contemplate perfectly, humans are happy to the degree they contemplate, and animals are excluded from happiness entirely because they cannot contemplate at all.
D: Distilled: Happiness extends exactly as far as contemplation does: the gods have it fully, humans partially, and other animals not at all.
Notes: The exclusion of animals from happiness because they lack contemplation is consistent with Aristotle's biological works, where he attributes perception and desire to animals but not rational thought (De Anima III.3, History of Animals VIII.1). -- The claim that contemplation is "in itself precious" (kath' hauto timion) restates the self-sufficiency criterion from I.7. -- The graduated scale (gods → humans → animals) reflects Aristotle's scala naturae, the hierarchy of living beings organized by soul-capacities. -- The phrase "not as a mere concomitant but in virtue of the contemplation" is crucial: it says that contemplation does not merely happen to coincide with happiness but is constitutive of it. -- This argument influenced the Great Chain of Being tradition in medieval and early modern thought.

D: Original: But, being a man, one will also need external prosperity; for our nature is not self-sufficient for the purpose of contemplation, but our body also must be healthy and must have food and other attention. Still, we must not think that the man who is to be happy will need many things or great things, merely because he cannot be supremely happy without external goods; for self-sufficiency and action do not involve excess, and we can do noble acts without ruling earth and sea; for even with moderate advantages one can act virtuously (this is manifest enough; for private persons are thought to do worthy acts no less than despots-indeed even more); and it is enough that we should have so much as that; for the life of the man who is active in accordance with virtue will be happy. Solon, too, was perhaps sketching well the happy man when he described him as moderately furnished with externals but as having done (as Solon thought) the noblest acts, and lived temperately; for one can with but moderate possessions do what one ought. Anaxagoras also seems to have supposed the happy man not to be rich nor a despot, when he said that he would not be surprised if the happy man were to seem to most people a strange person; for they judge by externals, since these are all they perceive. The opinions of the wise seem, then, to harmonize with our arguments.
D: Refashioned:
But being human, one will also need external prosperity. Our nature is not self-sufficient for the purpose of contemplation: the body must also be healthy and must have food and other provisions. Yet we must not think that happiness requires many or great things merely because supreme happiness is impossible without external goods. Self-sufficiency and action do not require excess, and we can perform noble acts without ruling over land and sea. Even with moderate means one can act virtuously — this is clear enough, since private individuals are thought to perform worthy deeds no less than rulers, and perhaps even more. It is sufficient to have just so much; the life of someone who acts in accordance with virtue will be happy.

Solon was perhaps sketching the happy person well when he described them as moderately furnished with external goods but as having performed the noblest acts and lived temperately. For one can do what one ought with only moderate possessions. Anaxagoras too seems to have supposed the happy person to be neither rich nor a despot, when he said he would not be surprised if the happy person seemed strange to most people, since they judge by externals alone, perceiving nothing else. The opinions of the wise, then, seem to harmonize with our arguments.
D: Elegant:
Human beings need external prosperity for contemplation — health, food, and basic provisions — but not great wealth or power. Self-sufficiency does not require excess; one can act virtuously with moderate means, and private citizens may do so more readily than rulers.

Solon described the happy person as moderately equipped with externals but having done the noblest deeds and lived temperately. Anaxagoras expected the happy person to seem strange to the many, who judge only by visible wealth. The testimony of the wise agrees with our argument: happiness needs enough, not abundance.
D: Summary: Although contemplation requires basic external goods like health and sustenance, it does not require wealth or power. One can act virtuously and live happily with moderate means. Both Solon and Anaxagoras described the happy person as someone with enough resources to live well, not someone with great riches — and the happy person might look strange to those who judge only by externals.
D: Distilled: Happiness requires moderate external goods for health and sustenance, not wealth or power, and the truly happy person may seem strange to those who judge only by appearances.
Notes: Solon (c. 640-558 BCE) was the Athenian lawmaker and one of the Seven Sages. His view that one should call no person happy until they are dead was discussed in I.10. Here Aristotle draws on a different aspect of Solon's thought: the emphasis on moderation. -- Anaxagoras of Clazomenae (c. 500-428 BCE) was the pre-Socratic philosopher who introduced nous (mind) as a cosmic principle. His reported remark about the happy person seeming strange to the many aligns with his known intellectualism. -- The claim that private individuals may do more worthy deeds than despots subverts Greek aristocratic assumptions and connects to the Politics's preference for the rule of law over personal rule. -- The moderate-externals thesis distinguishes Aristotle from both Stoic indifference to externals and Peripatetic thinkers who gave greater weight to fortune.

D: Original: But while even such things carry some conviction, the truth in practical matters is discerned from the facts of life; for these are the decisive factor. We must therefore survey what we have already said, bringing it to the test of the facts of life, and if it harmonizes with the facts we must accept it, but if it clashes with them we must suppose it to be mere theory. Now he who exercises his reason and cultivates it seems to be both in the best state of mind and most dear to the gods. For if the gods have any care for human affairs, as they are thought to have, it would be reasonable both that they should delight in that which was best and most akin to them (i.e. reason) and that they should reward those who love and honour this most, as caring for the things that are dear to them and acting both rightly and nobly. And that all these attributes belong most of all to the philosopher is manifest. He, therefore, is the dearest to the gods. And he who is that will presumably be also the happiest; so that in this way too the philosopher will more than any other be happy.
D: Refashioned:
But while such testimonies carry some conviction, the truth in practical matters is discerned from the facts of life, since these are the decisive factor. We must therefore test what we have said against the facts of life, and if our account harmonizes with them, we must accept it; but if it clashes, we must suppose it to be mere theory.

Now the person who exercises reason and cultivates it seems to be in the best state of mind and most dear to the gods. For if the gods have any care for human affairs, as they are thought to have, it would be reasonable that they should delight in what is best and most akin to themselves — that is, in reason — and that they should reward those who love and honor reason most, as caring for the things dear to the gods and acting both rightly and nobly. That all these attributes belong most of all to the philosopher is clear. The philosopher is therefore dearest to the gods, and the person dearest to the gods will presumably also be the happiest. In this way too, the philosopher will be happy above all others.
D: Elegant: In practical matters, theory must answer to the facts of life. The person who cultivates reason is in the best state of mind and most beloved by the gods, who would naturally delight in what is most akin to themselves and reward those who honor it. Since the philosopher most fully embodies these qualities, the philosopher is dearest to the gods and therefore happiest of all.
D: Summary: Practical truth must be verified against the facts of life. If the gods care about human affairs, they would naturally favor those who cultivate reason, which is most akin to the divine. Since the philosopher does this most fully, the philosopher is dearest to the gods and consequently the happiest person.
D: Distilled: The philosopher, who cultivates what is most akin to the divine, is dearest to the gods and therefore happiest of all human beings.
Notes: The methodological principle that practical truth must answer to the facts of life (erga tou biou) restates the empirical commitment from X.1 and I.3. -- The argument that the gods love the philosopher because reason is most akin to them completes the theological dimension of Aristotle's ethics, connecting human happiness to cosmic order. -- This contrasts with conventional Greek piety, which emphasized ritual and sacrifice; Aristotle effectively argues that intellectual activity is the highest form of devotion. -- The passage has been important in philosophical theology: Aquinas drew on it for his account of how the intellectual virtues relate us to God. -- The entire X.6-8 argument concludes here, and X.9 will make the transition from ethics to politics.

D: Original: If these matters and the virtues, and also friendship and pleasure, have been dealt with sufficiently in outline, are we to suppose that our programme has reached its end? Surely, as the saying goes, where there are things to be done the end is not to survey and recognize the various things, but rather to do them; with regard to virtue, then, it is not enough to know, but we must try to have and use it, or try any other way there may be of becoming good. Now if arguments were in themselves enough to make men good, they would justly, as Theognis says, have won very great rewards, and such rewards should have been provided; but as things are, while they seem to have power to encourage and stimulate the generous-minded among our youth, and to make a character which is gently born, and a true lover of what is noble, ready to be possessed by virtue, they are not able to encourage the many to nobility and goodness. For these do not by nature obey the sense of shame, but only fear, and do not abstain from bad acts because of their baseness but through fear of punishment; living by passion they pursue their own pleasures and the means to them, and and the opposite pains, and have not even a conception of what is noble and truly pleasant, since they have never tasted it. What argument would remould such people? It is hard, if not impossible, to remove by argument the traits that have long since been incorporated in the character; and perhaps we must be content if, when all the influences by which we are thought to become good are present, we get some tincture of virtue.
D: Refashioned:
If these matters — the virtues, friendship, and pleasure — have been dealt with sufficiently in outline, should we consider our program complete? Surely not. As the saying goes, where there are things to be done, the end is not merely to contemplate and understand them but to do them. With regard to virtue, then, it is not enough to know what it is; we must try to possess and exercise it, or pursue whatever other path there may be to becoming good.

Now if arguments by themselves were enough to make people good, they would rightly have earned great rewards, as Theognis says, and such rewards would need to be provided. But as things stand, while arguments seem to have the power to encourage and stimulate those among the young who are generous-spirited, and to prepare a character that is well-born and genuinely loves what is noble to be receptive to virtue, they are unable to turn the majority of people toward nobility and goodness. For most people obey not a sense of shame but only fear, and abstain from bad actions not because of their baseness but through fear of punishment. Living by passion, they pursue their own pleasures and the means to them, and avoid the corresponding pains, and have not even a conception of what is noble and truly pleasant, since they have never experienced it.

What argument could reshape such people? It is hard, if not impossible, to dislodge by argument traits that have long been built into someone's character. Perhaps we must be content if, when all the influences thought to make us good are present, we manage to acquire some measure of virtue.
D: Elegant:
Our inquiry's end is not merely to understand virtue but to become good. Arguments alone can inspire the generous-spirited young and those born with a love of nobility, but they cannot redirect the majority, who are ruled by passion, guided by fear rather than shame, and motivated by punishment rather than the sense of what is noble. Having never tasted true pleasure, they have no conception of it.

Since argument cannot reshape deeply ingrained character, we must be content if, given all favorable influences, we achieve some measure of virtue. The real question becomes: what can make people good?
D: Summary: The purpose of ethics is not just to understand virtue but to become good. While philosophical arguments can inspire the naturally noble-minded young, they cannot reform the majority, who are ruled by passion and fear rather than reason and shame. Deeply ingrained character traits resist argument, so we must look beyond philosophy for what can actually make people good.
D: Distilled: Philosophical argument can inspire the noble-minded but cannot reform the many, who are ruled by passion and fear, so becoming good requires something beyond knowledge.
Notes: This passage marks the crucial transition from the theoretical culmination of the Ethics (X.6-8) to its practical and political conclusion (X.9). -- Theognis of Megara (6th century BCE) was an elegiac poet whose verses on nobility and virtue were used in aristocratic education. The reference to "great rewards" may paraphrase Theognis 432-434. -- The limitation of argument in moral formation echoes II.1-4 (virtue is acquired by habituation, not teaching alone) and connects to Plato's similar concerns in the Republic and Laws. -- The observation that most people are ruled by passion, not reason, anticipates the political argument that follows: law and civic institutions must supply the formative pressure that argument cannot. -- This passage influenced Kant's distinction between autonomy and heteronomy, and Mill's concern with moral education in On Liberty.

D: Original: Now some think that we are made good by nature, others by habituation, others by teaching. Nature's part evidently does not depend on us, but as a result of some divine causes is present in those who are truly fortunate; while argument and teaching, we may suspect, are not powerful with all men, but the soul of the student must first have been cultivated by means of habits for noble joy and noble hatred, like earth which is to nourish the seed. For he who lives as passion directs will not hear argument that dissuades him, nor understand it if he does; and how can we persuade one in such a state to change his ways? And in general passion seems to yield not to argument but to force. The character, then, must somehow be there already with a kinship to virtue, loving what is noble and hating what is base.
D: Refashioned:
Now some think we are made good by nature, others by habituation, and others by teaching. The contribution of nature clearly does not depend on us but is present in those who are truly fortunate, as a result of some divine cause. Argument and teaching, we may suspect, are not effective with everyone. The soul of the student must first have been cultivated by means of habits to take noble joy and noble hatred, like earth that must be prepared before it can nourish the seed.

For the person who lives as passion directs will not listen to reasoning that tries to dissuade them, nor even understand it if they do hear it. How can we persuade someone in such a state to change their ways? Passion seems to yield not to argument but only to force. The character, then, must already be there in some form, already possessing a kinship with virtue — already loving what is noble and hating what is base.
D: Elegant:
Nature, habituation, and teaching each contribute to making us good. Nature's contribution is beyond our control, a matter of fortune. Teaching and argument require prepared ground: the student's soul must first be cultivated through habits of noble joy and noble aversion, like soil prepared for seed.

The person ruled by passion will not hear argument, let alone heed it. Passion yields not to reason but to force. Character must already exist with a kinship to virtue before argument can take hold.
D: Summary: Becoming good involves nature, habituation, and teaching. Nature is a matter of fortune; teaching requires a soul already shaped by good habits to love the noble and hate the base. Those ruled by passion cannot be reached by argument alone — passion yields only to force. The ground must be prepared before the seed of reason can take root.
D: Distilled: Teaching virtue requires a soul already cultivated by habit to love what is noble, because passion yields to force, not argument.
Notes: The three sources of virtue — nature (phusis), habit (ethos), and teaching (didache) — recall II.1 and are developed further in Politics VII.13. -- The agricultural metaphor (preparing earth for seed) is ancient; Plato uses similar imagery in the Phaedrus (276b-277a) for planting arguments in receptive souls. -- The claim that passion yields to force (bia), not argument (logos), provides the philosophical justification for turning to law and politics. -- This passage directly motivates the transition to the Politics: if most people cannot be made good by argument, then political institutions and laws must do the work of habituation. -- The Aristotelian emphasis on habituation as prior to intellectual instruction profoundly influenced educational theory, from classical rhetoric through Renaissance humanism to modern character education.

D: Original: But it is difficult to get from youth up a right training for virtue if one has not been brought up under right laws; for to live temperately and hardily is not pleasant to most people, especially when they are young. For this reason their nurture and occupations should be fixed by law; for they will not be painful when they have become customary. But it is surely not enough that when they are young they should get the right nurture and attention; since they must, even when they are grown up, practise and be habituated to them, we shall need laws for this as well, and generally speaking to cover the whole of life; for most people obey necessity rather than argument, and punishments rather than the sense of what is noble.
D: Refashioned:
But it is difficult to receive a proper training in virtue from youth up if one has not been brought up under the right laws. Living temperately and with endurance is not pleasant to most people, especially the young. For this reason, their upbringing and occupations should be regulated by law, since these things will not be painful once they have become habitual.

But it is surely not enough that people receive the right nurture and care only when they are young. Since they must also practice and be habituated to virtue when they are grown up, we need laws for this as well — and indeed, generally speaking, laws covering the whole of life. For most people obey necessity rather than argument, and punishments rather than a sense of what is noble.
D: Elegant: Right training in virtue from youth is difficult without good laws, since temperate and hardy living is unpleasant to the young. Law must regulate upbringing so that good habits become second nature. But laws are needed not just for the young — they must cover the whole of life, since most people respond to necessity and punishment rather than to argument and nobility.
D: Summary: Virtuous training from youth requires the support of good laws, since temperate living is unpleasant to the young until it becomes habitual. Laws must regulate not just childhood upbringing but the whole of life, because most people respond to compulsion and punishment rather than to reason and the sense of what is noble.
D: Distilled: Good laws must shape virtue from youth through old age, because most people respond to compulsion rather than argument.
Notes: The emphasis on law as necessary for virtue links the Ethics directly to the Politics. Aristotle is arguing that ethical theory is incomplete without political implementation. -- The observation that virtuous habits are painful at first but become natural with practice restates the central thesis of II.1-3. -- The claim that laws must cover "the whole of life" anticipates the comprehensive legislative program Aristotle develops in Politics VII-VIII, covering education, marriage, childrearing, and leisure. -- The contrast between necessity/punishment and argument/nobility maps onto the distinction between the many and the noble-natured from X.37. -- Sparta's comprehensive legal regulation of citizens' lives from birth to death (discussed in X.42) serves as the historical model, though Aristotle will criticize its limitations.

D: Original: This is why some think that legislators ought to stimulate men to virtue and urge them forward by the motive of the noble, on the assumption that those who have been well advanced by the formation of habits will attend to such influences; and that punishments and penalties should be imposed on those who disobey and are of inferior nature, while the incurably bad should be completely banished. A good man (they think), since he lives with his mind fixed on what is noble, will submit to argument, while a bad man, whose desire is for pleasure, is corrected by pain like a beast of burden. This is, too, why they say the pains inflicted should be those that are most opposed to the pleasures such men love.
D: Refashioned:
This is why some thinkers hold that legislators should urge people toward virtue by appealing to what is noble, on the assumption that those who have been well formed by habituation will respond to such appeals. Punishments and penalties should then be imposed on those who disobey and are of an inferior nature, while the incurably bad should be banished altogether.

The reasoning is that a good person, since they live with their mind fixed on what is noble, will respond to argument, whereas a bad person, whose desire is for pleasure, must be corrected by pain, like a beast of burden. For this reason, they say, the pains inflicted should be those most opposed to the pleasures such people love.
D: Elegant: Legislators should appeal to nobility for those already well-habituated, impose penalties on the disobedient and inferior, and banish the incurably bad. The good person responds to argument about what is noble; the bad person, driven by pleasure, can be corrected only by opposing pains, like a beast of burden.
D: Summary: Legislators should use different approaches for different characters: appeals to nobility for the well-habituated, punishments for the disobedient, and exile for the incorrigible. Good people respond to reason and the noble; bad people, driven by pleasure, can only be corrected through pain that opposes their favored pleasures.
D: Distilled: Good legislation uses nobility to guide the virtuous, punishment to correct the wayward, and banishment for the incurable, matching the remedy to the character.
Notes: The tripartite classification (responsive to nobility, responsive to punishment, incurable) reflects the practical realism of Aristotle's political thought. -- The comparison of correcting the vicious with training beasts of burden is harsh but consistent with Aristotle's view that those ruled entirely by passion lack the rational capacity that distinguishes human moral agency. -- The principle that punishments should be opposed to the offender's favored pleasures shows sophisticated thinking about deterrence and rehabilitation. -- This connects to the Politics's discussion of different types of citizens and the appropriate form of governance for each. -- The idea of banishing the incurably bad resonates with Plato's Laws (IX.862d-863a), where incurable criminals face the death penalty.

D: Original: However that may be, if (as we have said) the man who is to be good must be well trained and habituated, and go on to spend his time in worthy occupations and neither willingly nor unwillingly do bad actions, and if this can be brought about if men live in accordance with a sort of reason and right order, provided this has force,-if this be so, the paternal command indeed has not the required force or compulsive power (nor in general has the command of one man, unless he be a king or something similar), but the law has compulsive power, while it is at the same time a rule proceeding from a sort of practical wisdom and reason. And while people hate men who oppose their impulses, even if they oppose them rightly, the law in its ordaining of what is good is not burdensome.
D: Refashioned:
However that may be, if — as we have said — the person who is to become good must be well trained and habituated, and then must spend their time in worthy occupations, doing nothing bad either willingly or unwillingly, and if this can be achieved only when people live in accordance with a kind of reason and right order that has binding force, then the following becomes clear.

A father's command does not have the required force or compulsive power. Nor, in general, does the command of any one individual, unless they are a king or hold some comparable authority. But the law does have compulsive power, and it is at the same time a rule proceeding from a kind of practical wisdom and reason. Moreover, while people resent individuals who oppose their impulses, even when those individuals oppose them rightly, the law in ordaining what is good is not perceived as burdensome.
D: Elegant: Becoming good requires living under reason and right order that has binding force. A father's authority lacks sufficient compulsive power, as does any private individual's. But law combines compulsive power with practical wisdom and reason. People resent individuals who thwart their impulses, even justly, but they do not resent the law's prescriptions of what is good.
D: Summary: Becoming good requires living under rules that combine rational guidance with binding force. Individual authority, even a father's, lacks sufficient power. Law uniquely combines compulsion with practical wisdom, and people accept its requirements without the resentment they feel toward individuals who correct them.
D: Distilled: Law alone combines the compulsive force needed to shape behavior with the rational guidance of practical wisdom, which is why it can accomplish what individual authority cannot.
Notes: The argument that law combines force with reason is foundational for Western legal philosophy and natural law theory. -- The observation about psychology — that people resent personal correction but accept legal requirements — shows Aristotle's acute understanding of human nature. -- The insufficiency of paternal authority to produce virtue motivates the need for political institutions, connecting the Ethics to the Politics. -- This passage influenced Aquinas's definition of law as "an ordinance of reason for the common good, made by one who has care of the community" (Summa Theologiae I-II, Q.90, a.4). -- The claim that law is a form of practical wisdom (phronesis) connects to Book VI's account of political science as the architectonic form of practical wisdom.

D: Original: In the Spartan state alone, or almost alone, the legislator seems to have paid attention to questions of nurture and occupations; in most states such matters have been neglected, and each man lives as he pleases, Cyclops-fashion, 'to his own wife and children dealing law'. Now it is best that there should be a public and proper care for such matters; but if they are neglected by the community it would seem right for each man to help his children and friends towards virtue, and that they should have the power, or at least the will, to do this.
D: Refashioned:
In the Spartan state alone, or virtually alone, the legislator seems to have given proper attention to questions of upbringing and occupations. In most states, such matters have been neglected, and each person lives as they please, in the manner of the Cyclopes, "laying down the law for his own wife and children."

Now it is best that there should be a proper public system of care for such matters. But if they are neglected by the community, it would seem right for each individual to help their children and friends toward virtue, and to have the ability, or at least the will, to do this.
D: Elegant: Only Sparta has given serious legislative attention to upbringing and character formation. In most states, each person lives as they please, Cyclops-fashion, ruling only their own household. Ideally, the state would provide public education in virtue. Where it does not, individuals should take responsibility for guiding their children and friends toward virtue.
D: Summary: Sparta alone among Greek states has legislated comprehensively about education and character. Most states neglect these matters, leaving each person to govern their household like Homer's Cyclopes. Ideally the state should provide public moral education, but where it fails, individuals must take on this responsibility themselves.
D: Distilled: Where the state neglects moral education — as nearly all states except Sparta do — individuals must take responsibility for guiding their children and friends toward virtue.
Notes: The Cyclopes reference is from Homer's Odyssey (IX.114-115), where the Cyclopes are described as having no laws or assemblies, each ruling his own family independently. -- Sparta's educational system (agoge) was famous throughout the Greek world for its comprehensive regulation of citizens' lives from age seven onward, including physical training, communal dining, and moral formation. -- Aristotle discusses Spartan institutions at length in Politics II.9, where he both praises their educational intent and criticizes their execution. -- The fallback to individual responsibility when the state fails prefigures the discussion of private vs. public education in X.43. -- This passage makes clear that ethics without politics is incomplete: moral formation requires institutional support.

D: Original: It would seem from what has been said that he can do this better if he makes himself capable of legislating. For public control is plainly effected by laws, and good control by good laws; whether written or unwritten would seem to make no difference, nor whether they are laws providing for the education of individuals or of groups-any more than it does in the case of music or gymnastics and other such pursuits. For as in cities laws and prevailing types of character have force, so in households do the injunctions and the habits of the father, and these have even more because of the tie of blood and the benefits he confers; for the children start with a natural affection and disposition to obey. Further, private education has an advantage over public, as private medical treatment has; for while in general rest and abstinence from food are good for a man in a fever, for a particular man they may not be; and a boxer presumably does not prescribe the same style of fighting to all his pupils. It would seem, then, that the detail is worked out with more precision if the control is private; for each person is more likely to get what suits his case.
D: Refashioned:
It would seem from what has been said that a person can do this better if they make themselves capable of legislating. Public management is clearly accomplished through laws, and good management through good laws — whether written or unwritten makes no difference, nor whether they apply to the education of individuals or of groups, just as it makes no difference in the case of music, physical training, or other such pursuits.

For just as in cities laws and prevailing customs have force, so in households the father's instructions and habits carry force — and even more so, because of the bonds of kinship and the benefits the father confers. Children start with a natural affection and disposition to obey.

Furthermore, private education has an advantage over public, just as private medical treatment does. In general, rest and fasting are good for a person with a fever, but for a particular patient they may not be. A boxing trainer presumably does not teach the same style of fighting to all his pupils. It would seem, then, that details are worked out with more precision when education is private, since each person is more likely to receive what suits their individual case.
D: Elegant:
To help others toward virtue, one should learn the art of legislating. Good governance works through good laws, whether written or unwritten, public or private. In households, the father's instructions carry even more force than civic law, thanks to natural affection and bonds of kinship.

Private education has a key advantage over public, much as private medical treatment does: it can be tailored to the individual. A fever patient may need different treatment than the general rule suggests, and a boxing trainer does not prescribe identical techniques to every pupil.
D: Summary: Those who wish to help others toward virtue should become capable of legislating, since good laws — whether public or private, written or unwritten — are the instruments of moral formation. Private education has an advantage over public in that it can be tailored to individual needs, just as private medical treatment can be customized for particular patients.
D: Distilled: Private moral education, like private medicine, has the advantage of being tailored to the individual, but it requires the knowledge of a legislator to be done well.
Notes: The analogy between legislation and medicine is one of Aristotle's most characteristic comparisons, appearing throughout the Ethics and Politics. -- The advantage of private education (individual tailoring) must be balanced against the advantage of public education (systematic knowledge), as the next passage will argue. -- The boxing trainer example is typical of Aristotle's use of craft analogies in ethics. -- The claim that household authority derives its force from natural affection connects to Politics I.1-2 on the natural development from household to city-state. -- Written vs. unwritten law was a major topic in Greek legal thought; Aristotle discusses it further in Rhetoric I.13 and Politics III.16.

D: Original: But the details can be best looked after, one by one, by a doctor or gymnastic instructor or any one else who has the general knowledge of what is good for every one or for people of a certain kind (for the sciences both are said to be, and are, concerned with what is universal); not but what some particular detail may perhaps be well looked after by an unscientific person, if he has studied accurately in the light of experience what happens in each case, just as some people seem to be their own best doctors, though they could give no help to any one else. None the less, it will perhaps be agreed that if a man does wish to become master of an art or science he must go to the universal, and come to know it as well as possible; for, as we have said, it is with this that the sciences are concerned.
D: Refashioned:
But details are best attended to by a doctor, a gymnastics instructor, or anyone else who possesses general knowledge of what is good for everyone or for people of a certain kind — for the sciences are said to be, and indeed are, concerned with what is universal. That said, some particular case may perhaps be well handled by someone without scientific knowledge, if they have studied accurately through experience what happens in each situation — just as some people seem to be their own best doctors, though they could not help anyone else.

Nonetheless, it will perhaps be agreed that anyone who wishes to become a master of a craft or science must proceed to the universal and come to know it as fully as possible. For, as we have said, it is with universals that the sciences are concerned.
D: Elegant: The best individual care comes from those with general scientific knowledge — doctors, trainers, and the like — since the sciences deal with universals. Experience alone may suffice for one's own case, as some people are their own best doctors, but they cannot help others. Anyone who wishes to master a craft must ultimately learn its universal principles.
D: Summary: While experience can guide individuals in their own cases, the best care — whether in medicine, training, or moral education — comes from those who possess universal scientific knowledge. Anyone who wishes to master the art of making people good must learn the universal principles, not rely on experience alone.
D: Distilled: Effective moral education, like effective medicine, requires knowledge of universal principles, not merely personal experience with particular cases.
Notes: The relationship between universal knowledge (episteme) and particular experience (empeiria) is a central theme in Aristotle's epistemology, discussed at length in Metaphysics I.1 and Posterior Analytics II.19. -- The example of self-doctoring echoes the practical wisdom discussion in Book VI: the phronimos must be able to deliberate about particulars, but this is best done with knowledge of universals. -- The craft analogy for ethics is both illuminating and limited: Aristotle recognizes that moral formation is not purely technical, but the analogy supports his argument that political science (the art of legislating) requires systematic knowledge. -- This passage provides the philosophical justification for the study of politics and constitutions that Aristotle announces in X.48.

D: Original: And surely he who wants to make men, whether many or few, better by his care must try to become capable of legislating, if it is through laws that we can become good. For to get any one whatever-any one who is put before us-into the right condition is not for the first chance comer; if any one can do it, it is the man who knows, just as in medicine and all other matters which give scope for care and prudence.
D: Refashioned: And surely anyone who wants to make people better through their care — whether many or few — must try to become capable of legislating, if it is through laws that we can become good. Getting any person who comes before us into the right condition is not a task for just anyone. If anyone can do it, it is the person who has knowledge — just as in medicine and all other fields that involve care and judgment.
D: Elegant: Anyone who wishes to make others better must learn the art of legislating, since laws are the instruments of moral formation. Shaping a person's character is a task for the knowledgeable, not the amateur — just as in medicine and every other field requiring care and expertise.
D: Summary: Making people better requires the art of legislation, since laws are the primary instruments of moral formation. Like medicine, moral education is a skill requiring knowledge, not something any well-meaning amateur can accomplish.
D: Distilled: Making people good through laws requires the expertise of a legislator, just as healing requires the expertise of a physician.
Notes: This passage reinforces the practical conclusion of the Ethics: the moralist must become a political scientist. -- The medicine analogy recurs: throughout the Ethics, Aristotle compares the moral educator to the physician (cf. II.2, III.3, V.9). -- The claim that laws are the instruments of moral formation connects to the opening of the Politics, where political science is described as the architectonic practical science. -- The phrase "any person who comes before us" (ton tuchonta) emphasizes the universality of the legislator's task: they must be able to shape any character, not just favorable ones.

D: Original: Must we not, then, next examine whence or how one can learn how to legislate? Is it, as in all other cases, from statesmen? Certainly it was thought to be a part of statesmanship. Or is a difference apparent between statesmanship and the other sciences and arts? In the others the same people are found offering to teach the arts and practising them, e.g. doctors or painters; but while the sophists profess to teach politics, it is practised not by any of them but by the politicians, who would seem to do so by dint of a certain skill and experience rather than of thought; for they are not found either writing or speaking about such matters (though it were a nobler occupation perhaps than composing speeches for the law-courts and the assembly), nor again are they found to have made statesmen of their own sons or any other of their friends. But it was to be expected that they should if they could; for there is nothing better than such a skill that they could have left to their cities, or could prefer to have for themselves, or, therefore, for those dearest to them. Still, experience seems to contribute not a little; else they could not have become politicians by familiarity with politics; and so it seems that those who aim at knowing about the art of politics need experience as well.
D: Refashioned:
Must we not, then, examine next where and how one can learn the art of legislation? Is it, as in all other cases, from those who practice the art — that is, from statesmen? It was certainly thought to be a part of statesmanship. But is there an apparent difference between political science and the other sciences and arts?

In other fields, the same people both teach the art and practice it — doctors and painters, for example. But while the sophists claim to teach politics, it is actually practiced not by any of them but by the politicians, who seem to operate through a kind of skill and experience rather than through systematic thought. They are not found writing or lecturing about political matters (though this would perhaps be a nobler occupation than composing speeches for the law-courts and the assembly), nor have they managed to make statesmen of their own sons or any other of their friends.

Yet one would expect them to have done so if they could, for there is nothing better they could have bequeathed to their cities, or could prefer to have for themselves, or therefore for those closest to them. Still, experience seems to contribute considerably — otherwise, people could not have become politicians merely through familiarity with political life. So it seems that those who aim at understanding the science of politics need experience as well.
D: Elegant:
Where does one learn legislation? In other fields, the same people teach and practice the art. But in politics, the sophists claim to teach while politicians actually practice — relying on experience and skill rather than systematic knowledge. Politicians neither write about their art nor succeed in making statesmen of their own sons, though they would if they could.

Yet experience clearly matters: no one becomes a politician without it. The aspiring political scientist therefore needs both theoretical knowledge and practical experience.
D: Summary: Learning the art of legislation poses a unique problem: unlike other fields where practitioners also teach, in politics the teachers (sophists) do not practice and the practitioners (politicians) do not teach or theorize. Politicians rely on experience rather than systematic thought, and cannot even transmit their skill to their own children. Both theoretical knowledge and practical experience are needed.
D: Distilled: Political science uniquely suffers from a gap between its theorists, who do not practice, and its practitioners, who do not theorize, making both knowledge and experience essential.
Notes: The critique of sophists who claim to teach politics targets figures like Isocrates (436-338 BCE), whose school rivaled Plato's Academy, and possibly Protagoras and Gorgias. -- The observation that politicians cannot transmit their skill to their sons echoes Socrates' argument in Plato's Meno (93b-94e) and Protagoras (319d-320b). -- The gap between political theory and practice is a perennial theme: Aristotle is arguing for a new kind of political science that bridges both. -- The mention of composing speeches for courts and assemblies refers to the profession of logography (speechwriting), practiced by figures like Lysias and Isocrates. -- This passage explains why Aristotle's own Politics combines theoretical analysis with empirical study of constitutions.

D: Original: But those of the sophists who profess the art seem to be very far from teaching it. For, to put the matter generally, they do not even know what kind of thing it is nor what kinds of things it is about; otherwise they would not have classed it as identical with rhetoric or even inferior to it, nor have thought it easy to legislate by collecting the laws that are thought well of; they say it is possible to select the best laws, as though even the selection did not demand intelligence and as though right judgement were not the greatest thing, as in matters of music. For while people experienced in any department judge rightly the works produced in it, and understand by what means or how they are achieved, and what harmonizes with what, the inexperienced must be content if they do not fail to see whether the work has been well or ill made-as in the case of painting. Now laws are as it were the' works' of the political art; how then can one learn from them to be a legislator, or judge which are best? Even medical men do not seem to be made by a study of text-books. Yet people try, at any rate, to state not only the treatments, but also how particular classes of people can be cured and should be treated-distinguishing the various habits of body; but while this seems useful to experienced people, to the inexperienced it is valueless. Surely, then, while collections of laws, and of constitutions also, may be serviceable to those who can study them and judge what is good or bad and what enactments suit what circumstances, those who go through such collections without a practised faculty will not have right judgement (unless it be as a spontaneous gift of nature), though they may perhaps become more intelligent in such matters.
D: Refashioned:
But those sophists who profess to teach the political art seem to be very far from actually teaching it. To put the matter generally, they do not even know what kind of thing political science is or what it is concerned with. Otherwise, they would not have classified it as identical with rhetoric, or even as inferior to it, nor would they have thought it easy to legislate simply by collecting well-regarded laws. They say one can select the best laws, as though the selection itself did not demand intelligence, and as though sound judgment were not the most important thing — just as it is in music.

For people experienced in any field judge its products rightly, understanding by what means and how they are achieved, and what harmonizes with what. The inexperienced must be content if they can at least tell whether a work has been done well or badly — as in the case of painting.

Now laws are, so to speak, the products of the political art. How, then, can one learn to be a legislator from them, or judge which are best? Even doctors do not seem to be trained merely by studying textbooks. Yet medical writers do try to describe not only treatments but also how particular types of patients can be cured and should be treated, distinguishing various bodily constitutions. While this seems useful to the experienced, it is worthless to the inexperienced.

So while collections of laws and constitutions may be serviceable to those who can study them with a practiced judgment for what is good or bad and what enactments suit what circumstances, those who go through such collections without a trained faculty will not arrive at sound judgment — unless perhaps as a spontaneous gift of nature — though they may become somewhat more discerning in such matters.
D: Elegant:
The sophists who claim to teach politics do not even understand what it is. They equate it with rhetoric, think legislation is merely a matter of collecting good laws, and assume selection requires no judgment — as though discernment were not paramount, as it is in music.

Laws are the products of political art. One cannot learn to legislate merely by studying collections of laws, any more than one can become a doctor by reading medical texts. Such collections are useful to the experienced and the discerning, but worthless to those lacking trained judgment. Without the practiced faculty of a political scientist, one will not know which laws are good, which are bad, or which suit which circumstances.
D: Summary: The sophists fail to teach politics because they misunderstand its nature, equating it with rhetoric and thinking legislation is simply a matter of collecting good laws. But selecting good laws requires trained judgment, just as appreciating music or painting requires experience. Collections of laws and constitutions are useful only to those with practiced political judgment, not to beginners.
D: Distilled: Studying collections of laws without trained political judgment is as useless as studying medical textbooks without clinical experience.
Notes: The critique of reducing politics to rhetoric targets Isocrates especially, whose educational program centered on rhetorical training as sufficient for political leadership. -- The analogy with music appreciation and painting judgment reflects Aristotle's general view that each domain of knowledge has its own standard of precision (I.3). -- The reference to collections of laws and constitutions alludes to the massive research project Aristotle and his school undertook: they collected and analyzed 158 constitutions of Greek city-states, of which only the Constitution of the Athenians survives. -- The medical textbook analogy is apt: the Hippocratic corpus was well known in Aristotle's time, and his father Nicomachus was a physician. -- The phrase "spontaneous gift of nature" (autophuēs) acknowledges that some individuals have natural political insight, but this is unreliable as a basis for education.

D: Original: Now our predecessors have left the subject of legislation to us unexamined; it is perhaps best, therefore, that we should ourselves study it, and in general study the question of the constitution, in order to complete to the best of our ability our philosophy of human nature. First, then, if anything has been said well in detail by earlier thinkers, let us try to review it; then in the light of the constitutions we have collected let us study what sorts of influence preserve and destroy states, and what sorts preserve or destroy the particular kinds of constitution, and to what causes it is due that some are well and others ill administered. When these have been studied we shall perhaps be more likely to see with a comprehensive view, which constitution is best, and how each must be ordered, and what laws and customs it must use, if it is to be at its best. Let us make a beginning of our discussion.
D: Refashioned:
Now our predecessors have left the subject of legislation largely unexamined. It is perhaps best, therefore, that we should study it ourselves, and with it the question of constitutions generally, in order to complete our philosophy of human nature to the best of our ability.

First, then, if anything has been said well in detail by earlier thinkers, let us try to review it. Then, in light of the constitutions we have collected, let us examine what kinds of influences preserve and destroy states, what kinds preserve or destroy particular types of constitution, and what causes some states to be well governed and others badly. When we have studied these questions, we shall perhaps be better able to see comprehensively which constitution is best, how each must be ordered, and what laws and customs it must employ to be at its best.

Let us make a beginning of our discussion.
D: Elegant: Our predecessors have left the science of legislation largely unexamined. We must therefore study it ourselves, along with the question of constitutions, to complete our philosophy of human nature. We shall review what earlier thinkers have said well, examine our collection of constitutions to discover what preserves and destroys states, and seek to understand which constitution is best and how each must be ordered to achieve its highest form. Let us begin.
D: Summary: Aristotle announces the transition from ethics to political science, proposing to study legislation and constitutions in order to complete the philosophy of human nature. He will review earlier thought, analyze the collected constitutions, investigate what preserves and destroys states, and determine which constitution is best.
D: Distilled: The Ethics concludes by announcing the Politics: a systematic study of legislation and constitutions needed to complete the philosophy of human nature.
Notes: This final passage of the Nicomachean Ethics serves as the direct introduction to Aristotle's Politics, making the two works a continuous inquiry into human flourishing from individual character to political order. -- The reference to "constitutions we have collected" points to the famous collection of 158 constitutions gathered by Aristotle's school at the Lyceum, of which only the Athenaion Politeia (Constitution of the Athenians) survives, rediscovered on papyrus in 1890. -- The program announced here — reviewing predecessors, analyzing constitutions, determining causes of political stability and decline — corresponds closely to the actual structure of the Politics, especially Books II-VI. -- The phrase "philosophy of human nature" (peri ta anthrōpeia philosophia) frames the entire Ethics-Politics project as a single discipline studying the human good at both individual and communal levels. -- This passage has been central to debates about the unity of Aristotle's practical philosophy and the relationship between ethics and politics, from Hellenistic commentators through Aquinas to modern scholars like Alasdair MacIntyre (After Virtue, 1981).
